Although it would be very interesting to know who supplies them with such weapons, and at the same time with smokes. Interesting sponsors.

An incredibly mysterious and secretive community. Usually it is enough to comb through a couple of palmtops from corpses and a fairly comprehensive picture will emerge. Here, there is zero information about the base and numbers, about the leaders, as well as about the scope of activities, in terms of what cases they take on and what they reject. In fact, nothing is known about them! But this only fuels interest and curiosity. The only thing that is clear is that there are some intermediaries between them and the customers, through whom all deals are concluded. But how to find these informants or dealers is also encrypted. One gets the impression that respectable traders like Sidorovich and the leaders of the groups have this information, but keep their mouths shut due to some non-disclosure conditions. It would be interesting to get acquainted with these conditions. In general, everything looks very much like a real guild, and not just a crowd of idiots who united into a group for fun. Most likely, even the people are tracked and selected secretly, and not simply forced to read the oath. You can't just worm your way into the ranks. You live and have fun, and one day you come across a note in your own backpack with an invitation to meet and discuss the possibilities of cooperation. Although this is speculation and fairy tales, in reality I don't know anything.

There are many rumors and horror stories about these crazy freaks, this de facto personal guard of the Monolith, if such a thing exists at all. There were only sectarians wandering around here, praying to a stone from the other world, which had materialized or fallen from the sky into the very center of the Zone. They worshiped it and believed that the boulder rewarded true believers with the fulfillment of their cherished desires. They hung around and around the Burner, and one day they all rushed through the radiation to the station. It was like they had cooked up a mass suicide. However, a few months later the schizos returned alive and well! Only their roofs were completely blown off, they went completely crazy and now mow down all the infidels indiscriminately. Starting from the radar forests and up to the station itself, they control every path, protect their stone from encroachment at the cost of their own lives. Nobody knows how they manage to survive with the extreme levels of psi-radiation and radiation there, but it was after the return of these idolaters that the stalkers began to seriously talk about the Monolith as a reality. It seems that if there really wasn't this stone, the pilgrims would have died, but since they returned, it turns out that there really is something mystical in the center. Maybe so, but a bullet in the forehead still calms these enlightened ones, which suits me just fine.

The most ordinary military personnel are mostly just serving here, and accordingly there is no enthusiasm to risk their skins for the sake of exterminating undesirable elements in the form of our brother stalkers. In other words, they do not voluntarily drag themselves deep into the Zone in platoons on their own initiative, they periodically organize helicopter raids solely for reporting purposes and mow down whoever comes their way. Extremely rarely, I have not observed it myself, but people said that the soldiers deploy serious operations with the aim of capturing certain objects. Probably, they are looking for the causes of the disaster. But in such operations, it is not ordinary conscripts who act, but ruffled special forces with appropriate weapons and equipment. Whatever one may say, this does not happen often, so you can not be afraid of such antics. Usually, they sit at checkpoints around the clock and shoot at pedestrians with a machine gun.

The most profitable and harmless orders can be had from them. They are interested in mutants, preferably dead and chopped up into parts. They constantly master different properties and the working material is constantly needed. As far as I know, I did not live in this time, before they focused on artifacts, people floated these boulders to them by the hundreds. However, these are not organic tissues, accordingly, you can pick around much longer before the stones become unusable. Although they still buy now, but the prices are ordinary. They have nothing to do with local squabbles and wars, the only reason this intelligentsia is profitable. In general, they are very cynical and indifferent scoundrels. People are considered expendable material, the achievement of the most incomprehensible goal justifies any sacrifice.

(12.04.11 16:48) Holy shit, I've been stuck in this anomaly for 6 days already, the stalker warren that gets into the anomaly saves me, I kill everyone without exception, I've already collected a decent amount of loot, and to sell... I need to urgently find a dealer or a way out of this crap. I need to walk around and look around, maybe I'll find something interesting. (12.04.11 19:43) Wow, what was I looking for, I went up to the barn, and there was a stalker warren, I didn't kill them, there were too many suckers there, I said that I was a freelancer, if only they knew who I really was. I'll live with them for now, share my loot, gain their trust, and then we'll see what to do with them.

(06/23/11 11:23) In general, I got along with these suckers, but this Lyuty, he really pisses me off... I'm holding on with all my might not to shoot him once or twice. Oh well, let him live for now. I hid the most valuable loot that I took from those dead men. I haven't had such stuff for a long time, there's no one to brag to, it's even offensive. Oh well, I'll go check the stash again, is everything in place.

(07/14/11 18:53) Well, this anomaly has changed me after all, I've already forgotten what it's like to be a bandit, the only good thing is that I've learned to shoot well. Oh well, I killed three suckers today, two mercenaries and a scientist. There was some kind of crap in the egghead's things, after looking at the documents, I found out that it was a "Decoder" and that with the help of this crap you can get out of here, but how? I'll hide it for now, and in the meantime I'll study these documents better.
We need to get out of the Zone
What a great guy I am
(yesterday, 19:23) Well, that's it, now we can get out of this damned Zone, Lame ram, he told me about his and Lefty's stash, all that's left is to somehow find out where exactly it is.

(yesterday, 21:57) I know where the container is. Well, idiots, they themselves, drunk, told me everything. All that's left is to figure out how to steal the container so that no one would think it was me.

(yesterday, 23:19) The deed is done, I hid the loot, Lame is dead. Stavrid and the other morons thought that Lefty killed him. All that's left is to get rid of him, out of harm's way, and get out of here without any problems.

(today, two hours ago) Damn mercenary, he's meddling in things that aren't his business. How he pisses me off. I need to get rid of him immediately, he's becoming a real threat. And it wouldn't hurt to cut out Shaman's tongue for his chatter. If it weren't for him, the mercenary would have done all the work for me. He would have gotten rid of Lefty himself. Damn, he also lost his knife, I need to find it urgently before someone finds it before me.

(08/14/2008, 13:42) Now I understand how it all happened, and why these guys call themselves that. After all, they really did provoke the last day. The last quiet day of humanity. I remember how after that meeting with the bloodsucker, they gave me shelter and protection. And then, they dedicated me to their secrets. At first I thought they were crazy, but now I understand how wrong I was.

(03.10.2008, 16:11) This Sakharov is not as simple as he seems. Well, to hell with him, the main thing is that so far he hasn't suspected anything. It's strange, why did he suddenly become so interested in psi-radiation? According to him, a prototype of a device that can weaken the radiation will be ready very soon. As soon as I find out all the details, I'll immediately report to the base.

(14.10.2008, 19:34) The prototype tests failed miserably, and Sakharov is now responsible for the deaths of three military personnel who signed up for this hopeless undertaking. I wonder what will happen to the professor?

(02/15/2009, 13:42) Mark told me about "Clear Sky". It turns out he worked with Lebedev and Kalancha. They are smart, and it would seem they are on the right path, but they do not see the obvious. It is strange that they did not join "O-consciousness" at all. You can hardly call the guys from "Clear Sky" allies. Mark says that their base is located somewhere in the south of the zone.

(02/23/2009, 13:42) We have big problems. Information has appeared that some stalkers are going to go on a hike to the center of the Zone, for the "scorcher". It would seem, what's wrong with that? There were several dozen such groups, but it's not that simple. These guys are being helped by the Swamp Doctor. Which means they have a real chance of carrying out their plans. Damn, don't they understand what events will follow because of their idiotic antics? So far we only know one name - Ghost. We need to get to this stalker urgently, and then finish him off.

(04.08.2009, 13:42) More than half a year has passed since the Ghost killed Mark, thus breaking up our group. The remnants have scattered in all directions. The Cold Squad, together with him, decided to join the Clear Sky. Fools. And me? It's time for me to get out of this cesspool. Alone, I'm powerless anyway.

(10/17/2009, 1:42 PM) It shouldn't have turned out like this. The fanatics from "Clear Sky" most likely all perished at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant. I wonder if it weren't for that mercenary, would they have succeeded? I don't think so. Ugh, what a lot to think about before dying. It's a shame. There was just a little bit left, and I would have already been on the mainland. Damn it all, this damned Zone, Ghost and Strelok, "O-consciousness" and all the rest. This is the end. That very "Last Day" has come. Mine. My, last day.

- Kolya the Grumpy… - drawled the interrogator - a young lieutenant, clean-shaven, fit, stern. He tapped the table with the tip of his pencil and looked at the prisoner with slight contempt. - Why did you get such a nickname? You probably like to complain about life, like all your stalker brothers. Nikolai glanced at the lieutenant from under his brows. "It's not a nickname," he said quietly. "It's my last name." The investigator smiled, then immediately became serious. - Well, Kolya... hm... Grumpy, so as not to waste time, let's get to the point of our conversation. I need to know when and where to find the stalker who calls himself Brave. That's why I'm here, and that's why you're in this room. The lieutenant nodded to someone behind Grumpy. Stalker realized what was about to happen and instinctively shrank as much as his hands, cuffed behind his back, would allow. A strong blow to the back of his head made his vision go dark for a few seconds. Kolya shook his head, clearing the haze that was clouding his vision, then turned his head towards the soldier who had hit him and said over his shoulder: - Thanks, bro, you've earned my respect. The stalker's ear went numb and a trickle of blood began to flow down his neck. “This is to make it clear,” the investigator spoke again, “I’m not going to joke.” - What jokes are there, - Nikolai looked at him. - People usually laugh at jokes, but I don't really feel like it right now. The lieutenant leaned back in his chair in a relaxed pose, clasping his fingers. - I see you're a smart guy. Let's do it this way: I'll tell you what I know, and then you'll tell me what I want to know, - he looked at Grumpy for a few seconds, waiting for an answer, and, taking the stalker's silence for consent, continued: - So, I know that you and Brave went on raids together several times, which in itself is an interesting fact, considering that your friend is a hardened loner and rarely takes on assistants. There is no doubt that you were his assistant, and not the other way around, otherwise I would be interrogating him now, not you. There is also information that the so-called "gang war" did not happen without your participation, and that it was you two who destroyed Sutuly's gang and got your hands on the loot they stole. But I don't care about marauders and your stalker quirks. And so it was until last week, when you bastards attacked the checkpoint, shooting five young guys... The lieutenant could not restrain himself and his face distorted into a grimace of anger. The stalker looked down and muttered: - I didn’t give them machine guns and I didn’t drag them into the Zone. - Shut your mouth! - growled the interrogator and slapped his palm on the table. - You didn't steal it and I didn't steal it! They came themselves to protect the rest of the world from all this filth in which you crawl like beetles! You vultures, all you know is how to kill each other and line your pockets... The lieutenant suddenly fell silent, as if ashamed of having given free rein to his emotions, quickly pulled himself together, leaned back in his chair again and continued with his former calm: - Now it's your turn. Well... I'm listening. - You can listen, of course, - Grumpy smiled sadly, - but I have nothing to say. I don't know shit. - Uh-huh, - said the interrogator. He was silent for a while, then nodded, as if in response to some thoughts of his own, and said: - So it's the hard way. Well, what then... He looked up at the soldier standing behind the stalker and motioned for him to begin. It's strange, but today I didn't find Grumpy in his usual place. The last three times I visited the Zone, we always met him, and always in this very place. But today the stalker was gone. Just in case, I walked around the half-collapsed hut. You never know! Maybe his mortal body is lying around the corner, torn apart by blind dogs or some other mutants. He's not my friend, of course, but we've made a couple of trips together, and he helped me out at the checkpoint last time. The merchant, however, never said what kind of device we took from the soldiers. He silently took the suitcase, counted out the money, and instead of "thank you" told me to get lost and not show up for a while. But I'm not offended - the raid was good, I made some money and got a Kalashnikov with optics. It's a pity that Vorchun is not here today, he provided good cover... Oh well. No need to grieve! So, what should I do today? I remember Brown said he had a task for me. It would be nice to earn some extra money, because I had my eye on a suit from the Trader, reinforced, with an air recirculation system, but the price was too high. And Brown promised to pay well… Throwing the machine gun over my shoulder, I took out my binoculars, felt the bolts in my pocket, adjusted my duffel bag and headed towards Brown's lair. The cold ground chilled his body, even through the thick fabric of his overalls. But the lieutenant did not pay attention to such trifles. "Kolya Vorchun didn't lie... the bastard is coming, he's coming..." - sprawled on the top of the hill, the soldier looked at the stalker through powerful binoculars with an anti-glare coating. - "Well, that's it, you're finished, you bastard!" He turned to the sergeant standing next to him. - Get ready! And tell the boys not to be heroes, let's get him before he goes behind the trees... Suddenly, shots rang out from the direction where Brave was walking. The lieutenant immediately looked at his binoculars again. - ...your leg! - he couldn’t help but say. From above, it was clearly visible how a pack of wild dogs attacked the stalker. Smely killed two animals with a Makarov, the rest scattered to the sides, hiding in the tall grass. Then the dogs turned around and rushed to attack again. The stalker's actions aroused involuntary admiration in the lieutenant: precise, sparse movements, accurate shots. The brave man, slowly retreating and trying not to let them come at him from behind, effectively discharged the PM into the nearest predators, slashed the dog that jumped on him with a knife, immediately raised the Kalashnikov to his shoulder, stopped and felled several more animals with short bursts. “It works beautifully,” muttered the sergeant, who was watching what was happening together with the lieutenant. - Beautiful?! He killed our guys just as "beautifully"! - the officer growled. - Begin the operation! “Yes,” the sergeant replied and slowly went downstairs, where a squad of fighters was waiting for him. The lieutenant looked at the stalker again and cursed again - Brave ran towards the trees until the retreating pack decided to launch a new attack. Here comes the day! I ran into a pack! These creatures, it's hard to see in the grass, you can't even tell how many dogs are whining and barking around you. I should have run right away, but I thought: I'll slap a couple as a warning - the rest will run away. Aha! No way! I shot the first two, and the rest almost tore them apart. I barely escaped! The adrenaline from the fight was still burning his veins, but strangely enough, he was in a good mood... Although, why was it strange?.. After all, he remained alive - you could say he emerged victorious from this fight. By the way, I need to reload my weapon, because the PM is empty, and the Kalashnikov has less than half a magazine, and I still have a long way to go to Bury. I listened: the pack decided not to pursue. That's good! Slowing down, I caught my breath. Going around the trees and stepping over snags, I slowly changed the magazine of the pistol and habitually finished off the machine gun magazine with cartridges. The pursuit was dragging on, and this irritated the lieutenant. Brave was slowly but surely getting away from them. No! We can't let him escape! Otherwise we'll have to track him down again and wait for him to deign to appear in the Zone. - Faster, guys, faster! - he urged his men. A bright lightning flashed across the sky. A few seconds later, loud peals of thunder rolled over the soldiers' heads. Heavy drops splashed on the ground, forcing the tall grass to bend down, and the rain gradually dispersed, creating a gray curtain, hiding the stalker from his pursuers. “I just hope I don’t miss it, I just hope I don’t miss it…” the lieutenant muttered to himself. But luck was with them: having rounded another hill, the soldiers came to the edge of an abandoned village. Most of the houses were just rotten fragments, sticking out like grey-black teeth in the midst of the vegetation that was swallowing them up. And only a few huts were more or less preserved. Near one of them, the lieutenant walking ahead noticed people and immediately ordered the soldiers to hide. The sergeant approached him and whispered: - Well? - Eight people... - Well, and that one... there? The lieutenant, looking at the stalker through binoculars for the first time, noted his unremarkable appearance. He was practically no different from other stalkers - a similar jacket and backpack on his back, a hood thrown over his head, standard ammunition... but you can't confuse him with anyone - something in his movements, habits, manner of holding himself, distinguished the Brave One from the others. There. Third from the left, speaks with a high voice. - I see. Shall we take it? But the lieutenant did not answer right away; he was cursing to himself again: "Well, damn you! We missed a good moment! And now this bastard is not alone! He joined some gang. They are agreeing on something, planning something... If you push through, you can even get a bullet." - Sergeant, do you have a marksman in your group? - We'll find it. - Do you see that house where half the roof has collapsed? Direct him there. "It's not a very convenient position," the sergeant estimated appraisingly. "The trees will get in the way. And the distance is long - we're not SVDs." - The most important thing is to keep him down. His target is Brave. Don't pay attention to the others. In the meantime, we'll lure him out. “Understood,” the sergeant nodded and crawled back to give orders. Time dragged on like melted caramel, the lieutenant was overcome with a tremor of impatience, but he waited for the signal from the sniper. Finally, the long-awaited words came from the radio: “In position.” Through binoculars, the officer saw how Brave perked up. He began to look around, grabbed his machine gun, and the other stalkers became alarmed along with him. “They’re listening to our wave!” the lieutenant guessed. - Come on, sergeant, let's start, let's start! - he raised his machine gun and fired at the enemy. In position" It took me a while to realize that it was my radio that was giving me this. I looked questioningly at Brown, who responded with the same surprised look. - Warriors! - I realized, looking around. The guys also started looking around, and then from behind the houses at the end of the village they opened fire on us. I ran for cover. The fence boards behind me shattered into splinters. Crouching down, I ran into the house after Bury and hid by the window. Bullets whistled and squealed, piercing the boards, breaking the window frames, and scattering the remains of the furniture… Putting the machine gun to my shoulder, I began to look for targets. The soldiers were attacking from the thickets of an overgrown hedgerow, and their location could only be determined by the flashes of gunfire. I aimed the sight slightly to the side, where one of the shooters might presumably be, and fired a short burst. Then another. There were no more shots from that place, but a hail of bullets fell on the window that served me as a kind of embrasure. Without getting up, I moved to another window and again pressed my face to the sight. I found another shooter and was just about to pull the trigger when someone's back blocked my line of fire. Bury's guys decided not to sit back, but to go on the offensive. Bad tactics! - Where are you going! What idiots! - I yelled. The fight moved to close range. The stalker sawed-off shotguns thundered several times until they fell silent. They were shot one after another, only Brown himself remained, who was smart enough not to go into hell. Where did he find such idiots?! Youngsters who had just gotten into the Zone and now are stuck in it forever! I looked out cautiously. The soldiers, dispersed and pressing against the fences, advanced through the village in our direction. I counted five, led by some officer. This rain is not at all convenient! You can hardly see anything at all. The soldiers were keeping to the right side, where they were covered by trees. We had to change our disposition. I was already running through the plan in my head: I'll get out of the house, hide behind the fence, take out a couple, go back into the house, come out on the other side, find another place to hide and carefully shoot them one by one. It's like a game of tag - the main thing is to run away in time. There was no point in waiting any longer. Crouching low and trying not to make any noise, I got out of the house. At that moment, Brown played into my hands. The stalker hid in the bushes of wild raspberries and opened fire, thereby drawing attention to himself. Excellent! I looked through the crack between the boards – two soldiers were exposed. I put the Kalashnikov to my shoulder and aimed at the officer. Through the optics, I could see the lieutenant's stars on his shoulder straps. The center of the crosshairs was exactly above the bridge of his nose. "Now you, my dear, will catch an 'olive' from me..." The enemy bullet came from somewhere on the right. The blunt blow knocked me to the side, there was no pain, only the sensation of being hit. My finger pulled the trigger, but I was no longer shooting at the target, but upward. "What a bastard! He flanked me!" flashed through my mind. At that very moment, two more bullets found me after the first one. I stretched out on the ground, feeling my life drain away. Everything swam before my eyes, the shadows deepened, the sky turned crimson… “The target has been destroyed,” the sniper’s voice came from the radio. - Well done! - the lieutenant sincerely admired his work. - Sergeant, deal with this stalker already. There is no one brave, you don't have to be so careful. - Got it. We'll finish now. The officer waited until the shooting died down and the sergeant reported the end of the operation. Then he straightened up and headed towards where Brave was lying. The lieutenant was no coward, but he approached cautiously - you never know, maybe the stalker was only wounded, no need for extra risk. But Brave was dead. He lay on his back, his arms spread out, his frozen gaze fixed on the grey sky.

The calendar winter had entered its second decade, but was behaving like an inveterate lazybones. No frost or even obvious cold, not a single snowflake or gusts of northern wind. Low gray clouds hung over the Zone as usual, it was drizzling, sometimes in certain sectors the tree branches and grass were covered with a thick layer of frost. That's probably all. Numerous bodies of water remained free of ice, and the ground squelched with greasy mud. That is, there were no particular differences from autumn yet. “The days have become shorter,” Major Brazhnikov finally found a significant difference. “And the rest… an orphan winter, of poor quality. It’s a different story at home, in Siberia. There, it’s been white for a month now. Well, almost. Not counting the city landscapes, especially in the Kuzbass, near the coal mines and opencasts. But even there, it’s freezing like crazy. It’s December tenth, no joke. It’s time for the first wave of real frosts.” The major turned and motioned for his men to disperse. Behind the line of special forces soldiers, the grey whale-like carcasses of two helicopters could be seen. The resemblance was further enhanced by the "fountains" of rotating blades, which shone silver over the humped backs of the rotary-wing machines. The only thing missing was the heavy sighs and splashing. There was little sound at all. The hum of the engines and the whistle of the air cut by the blades were not typical for these models of helicopters. How the designers managed to achieve such an effect was always a mystery to Brazhnikov, but the fact remained a fact - the helicopters were considered almost silent, just right for secret operations. The "boards" were not going to go to base, they were simply following orders not to turn off the engines, just in case. The Zone is the Zone, here you have to be constantly on the alert. Especially after arriving at the operational square on such an alarming "introduction". The thought of the danger that permeated the Zone made the major take another look around. Previously, he had only come to this area as part of a foot group. Sometimes he had managed to drive part of the way by car, but on the southwestern outskirts of Chernobyl he had to dismount and trudge through the eternal mud with his boots. Once he had managed to penetrate the adjacent Lelevo area by water, but that had been an exceptional case. Almost the same as now, when the special forces had risked landing on the shore of the former Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant cooling pond in the Kopachi sector. Even in the dim light of a gloomy day, almost in twilight, the exhaust pipe of the station was clearly visible to the north, and to the west, among the foliated but still dense thickets, the hills above the ruins of the settlement could be discerned. Most of the houses of the dead settlement were covered with earth after the first catastrophe, when they unsuccessfully tried to defend the zone (not yet with a capital letter) in the fight against radiation. That battle was lost completely and almost immediately, and therefore the liquidators did not completely bury the settlement of Kopachi, as much as they could. To this day, near the Chernobyl-Pripyat highway, among the trees that have grown tall in less than three decades, a dozen slanted wooden buildings and the skeletons of a couple of stone buildings have been preserved. In one of them, a former kindergarten, military raiding parties often set up an operational headquarters, and in general it was conventionally considered the center of the sector, a kind of gathering point in unforeseen situations. Today, the special forces group landed far from the road, but even from this angle, Kopachi was nothing new to the major. He immediately spotted familiar landmarks and determined the direction of the search. Strictly north, along the coast, about three hundred meters. It was there, according to intelligence, that illegal artifact dealers had set up a temporary transshipment warehouse, near which the military had problems during the cleanup. As the duty officer said, the penultimate message from the "cleaners" commander left no doubt - the unit had reached its target. But the next report was not so cheerful. Even more - chaotic and unclear. "He was shouting that we had ended up in a big meat grinder, asking for a helicopter," the duty officer added, handing over a piece of paper with a short instruction. "Watch out there, work carefully, don't get into trouble either..." Brazhnikov tried to clarify what the commander of the "cleaners" meant by the word "meat grinder" and whether he mentioned superior enemy forces, but the duty officer only shrugged his shoulders. The chief of staff of the MIS, who unexpectedly showed up in his own person as the special forces departed, did not clarify the situation either. He only repeated the duty officer's request to work without bravado and wished them luck. The major, in principle, did not object to such a formulation of the task. In the current situation, it was more important to arrive at the square as quickly as possible, and then sort things out on the spot. That is why rapid reaction forces exist, to respond to any irritants and sort out problems with lightning speed. And yet, it would have been better to have more specific information before the operation began. At least a fresh report on anomalous activity in the sector. It was easy to get into some kind of crap (even the simplest "Carousel" - that was already a problem) on approach or at the moment of landing. The eternal twilight due to low clouds and the lousy weather were quite conducive to this. Fortunately for the group, the helicopters landed successfully, neither the detectors nor professional intuition detected any anomalies, and with the mutants in the sector everything was fine, that is, not at all. On the one hand, such sterile cleanliness of the territory was pleasing, but on the other hand, it was alarming. In any case, it did not relax. From his own experience, Brazhnikov knew that “cleanliness” in the Zone is the first sign of approaching major troubles. And the cleaner it is now, the bigger the problems will be a little later. Animals and anomalies completely disappeared from the sector only in two cases: if the Zone was going to close it for conservation, or if another anomaly was brewing, more powerful and larger than all the “Tramplets”, “Funnels” and “Carousels” that existed here, taken together. The major glanced at the detector screen. The device had not yet detected any serious disturbances in the anomalous energy, but that didn't mean anything. Electronics worked strictly according to programs, and they were written by people. And people had not yet fully understood the Zone, that is, they were not able to take everything into account. All that was left was to rely on intuition, which told Brazhnikov that problems were certainly brewing, no matter how you looked at them, but there was still time. Brazhnikov checked the airwaves for the kit - silence - and gave the signal to the fighters. The group immediately moved towards the warehouse. The object was carefully camouflaged, from the side - a hill overgrown with bushes, of which there are plenty here, but two obvious things gave it away: a compacted road leading to the main gates hidden in the thickets, and a small, but clearly working pier at the auxiliary gates, overlooking the pond. The thick mud underfoot made it difficult to move quickly and stealthily, sticking in lumps to boots and making a treacherous squelching sound, but the moans of the weak wind entangling itself in the branches and the measured splash of cold waves drowned out all the tell-tale sounds, and so it was possible to approach the warehouse almost silently. It was a pity that it was only possible to approach it, but not to penetrate it. At the last moment, when there were no more than fifty meters left to the gate, one of the fighters on the left flank stumbled and fell to one knee in the mud. The major turned to the violator of the sound masking and froze, peering tensely into the semi-darkness. No, the fighter was fine, he quickly got up, but for some reason did not move further. Brazhnikov glanced at the area in front of the warrior frozen in indecision. The essence of the fighter's doubts became clear at first glance. His path was blocked by a real blockage of human bodies. About two dozen dead people were lying side by side, in various poses, on an area of thirty square meters. The major gestured for the group to stop and slowly approached the scene of the massacre. The people had died recently, and a light steam could be seen over some of the bodies. It was smoking from the jagged wounds, most likely left by animals, but Brazhnikov was somehow sure that the mutants had galloped to the ready-made. The fact was proven at least by the fact that the scavengers had gotten scared and ran away when they heard a new group of people approaching. Real predators would not have abandoned their prey. The major crouched down next to the nearest corpse. Unlike the others, it had no visible injuries. Brazhnikov tried to feel for a pulse, but found none. However, the skin was pliable and still warm. It turned out that the special forces were a few minutes late. It was a shame. Especially because all the dead, to a man, were their own. The major stood up and looked around the area again. He noticed another cluster of corpses about twenty meters to the south, and a soldier on the right flank signaled him about a third. It suddenly dawned on Brazhnikov that the bodies were lying at an equal distance from the place where the major had stood a minute ago, when the left flank soldier had stumbled. Brazhnikov returned to his previous position and saw several more bodies from there. These dead were also armed, but not dressed in camouflage, most likely they were warehouse guards. And they were not lying in a heap, but forming a distinct semicircle. The major turned half a turn, then again, completing the panoramic view, and looked down at his feet. It seemed that all these people died in an instant from some explosion that thundered in the very place where Brazhnikov was now standing. However, the special forces commander could not find any crater or other signs of an explosion, no matter how hard he tried. "An anomaly?" Brazhnikov thought, finally stumbling upon some suspicious dark spot two steps away from him. It was only an iota, but still different in color saturation from the surrounding dirt. "If it is an anomaly, then it is previously unknown. It would be interesting to know if it produces artifacts?" Brazhnikov looked again at the black spot in the middle of the conventional circle and felt a slight prick of intuition. The spot had grown. Not much, but still. Whatever it was - the sought-after artifact of the unknown anomaly's production, residual phenomena, or another phase of the anomalous process, he shouldn't linger in the danger zone. The major gave the signal, and the group moved toward the gate. There was no need to linger, that was true, but the combat mission had to be completed without fail. The order to save the survivors had not been cancelled. Perhaps all the "cleaners" were lying here, around the circumference of the strange, black-marked clearing, but that was just a theorem that needed to be proven. The warehouse gates were slightly open, and inside, apart from the ubiquitous rats, there was not a single living soul. The group quickly walked along the entire length of the room, checking every nook and cranny and looking under each of the dozens of shelves piled high with goods, but they still found no one. In the back wall of the warehouse, there were three open doors. The central, widest, double-leafed one opened into a corridor that ended at the emergency gate, the other two led to stairs. The left one led to the stairs up, which in turn led to a small warehouse office, the right one - down. As Brazhnikov had assumed, the "temporary" warehouse turned out to be very solid, with three underground levels. On the "ground" one, food and things were stored, and on the first from the surface, a small arsenal was discovered. And also no one, no guards, no loaders. On the "minus second" boxes with artifacts were stored in a working mess, and the third was completely empty, only shelves. After a more thorough examination, Brazhnikov found traces of a recent renovation. Apparently, this level was going to be loaded with goods any day now, but they didn’t have time. But they clearly had time to fight here, and very seriously. In the far corner of the room, not far from the slightly open grate above the sewer collector, lay three “drunk dry” bodies and six non-human corpses: a controller and five bloodsuckers. Considering the species characteristics of these mutants, it was strange to see them nearby even dead, but the fact, as they say, took place, and there was no getting away from it. All the mutants were killed in a single scuffle - the major bent down and picked up one of the many shell casings lying nearby - and from the same type of weapon. Judging by the arrangement of the shell casings, four or five barrels were used, and all special ones, “Val” or “Vintorez”. "The Cleaners" sent a special group through the sewer? Possibly. But where are these specialists? Three "dry"? No, they died first, and only then the mutants. And there are fighters with conventional weapons lying at the gate. The specialists couldn't have left through the sewer, like they came in? Although why couldn't they? If a serious anomaly raged in front of the main gate, they could have retreated by a backup route." Brazhnikov knelt down and peered into the collector. The night vision device's illumination allowed him to see only a few meters into the tunnel, effectively an arm's length from the grate. To check the new version, he had to go down into the tunnel. The major didn't want to make life difficult for himself and his subordinates, so he decided to limit himself to a signal "flare." He lit the torch and, leaning into the tunnel's opening, threw it as far as he could. Apart from the hissing of the "fire", no sounds came from the tunnel. Not even the rats rustled. This meant that if the fighters had left this way, they had done so quite a long time ago and had probably already come out to the surface somewhere. Having reassured himself with this conclusion, Brazhnikov rose from his knees and motioned for his fighters to move in the opposite direction. There was no one to save at the illegal warehouse near Kopachi. Fact. Brazhnikov was the last to leave the gloomy warehouse bunker, like the captain of a sinking ship. The distant resemblance was reinforced, on the one hand, by the proximity of the vast water area of the cooling pond, and on the other... by the huge and unnaturally round puddle in front of the gates of the warehouse territory, lazily swaying like an oil slick. The major did not immediately realize where so much spilled oil had suddenly come from, but he immediately understood that it was not worth stepping into this strange puddle. He caught up with the soldiers shifting at the gate and rummaged in his pocket. The best friend of a stalker, and any other walker in the Zone, a bolt was found right away. Brazhnikov threw it almost to the middle of the puddle, watched for a minute as a rainbow bubble swelled and burst above the place of impact, and then raised a sad gaze to the sky, which had brightened a little under the onslaught of the increasing wind. It was unknown whether the black muck would spill into the warehouse, but further actions had to be planned based on the worst-case scenario. However, there was nothing to plan here. There was only one escape route, not counting the collector - through the spare gate. As before, without saying a word, Brazhnikov pointed to the far end of the warehouse. The pier, thank God, was free of anomalies. The fighters quickly descended to the shore, walked along the water's edge to a small beach littered with garbage and headed for the helicopters. The major, out of habit, brought up the rear of the column, and even lagged behind the group a little. To be honest, he didn’t want to leave empty-handed. It’s clear that he didn’t feel any guilt. The special forces had arrived as quickly as possible, exceeding all the standards, and if this had been an ordinary brawl, it would have been possible to save at least half of the “cleaners”. But… in the Zone, everything unusual is ordinary. It’s rare to manage to rescue people who have fallen into an anomaly, even if you’re a hundred meters away from them, and what can we say about half an hour of flying? So it wasn’t a matter of guilt, but of a high-quality attitude to work. Formally, Brazhnikov was required to carry out the order and get out of there, and if the mission was impossible, then just get out, without any unnecessary movements. But Brazhnikov, as a thoughtful and inquisitive officer, could not afford such straightforwardness. And it was not welcomed in the special forces unit. Especially after the squad was led by a former military guide, now Colonel Bibik. According to his unofficial order, every soldier, and especially the group commander, upon encountering a new anomaly, was simply obliged to see and remember as much as possible. And even better - to get something connected with it: an artifact or at least evidence characterizing it. Brazhnikov completely agreed with this approach to service, but in this particular case, there was a problem with both artifacts and evidence. It was hardly realistic to scoop up "oil" from the black spot, and the major did not see any other "material evidence" nearby. The first fighters had already started loading into the helicopters, and Brazhnikov was still halfway from the pond to the helicopters, because he could not resist the temptation and turned slightly to the right to take one last look at the black spot. It turned out that he was walking to the warehouse gates along a new path, on which several more corpses were unexpectedly discovered. This time there was no doubt, the fighters were killed with firearms. All the dead were well equipped and armed, but, judging by the number of cartridges around, they died in an unequal battle rather quickly. The major leaned over and examined one corpse more closely. On the sleeve was a chevron of the "Duty" group, a special weapon, night vision devices, an insulating mask... clearly a saboteur or a scout. Brazhnikov straightened up and rubbed his temple in confusion. The Dolgovtsy's bodies lay as if they had been shooting back at someone who had come from the direction of Kopachi. That is, the military had been in their rear. This fact completely confused all the tracks and covered the picture of what had happened with a thick layer of dark substance. For example, the kind that was splashing in the unnaturally round puddle in front of the warehouse gates. The major turned around and trudged along the path. The "oil spill" was still there, but it seemed to have stopped growing in width. Brazhnikov once again probed this "anomaly rollback" with a bolt, watched the inflated bubble again, and finally gave up. There was no hope of bringing back any survivors, artifacts, or evidence from the raid, only impressions. "Then so be it." Brazhnikov shook himself and trotted to the nearest "side". He managed to run only to the edge of the clearing where the helicopters had landed. Here he could already feel a strong wind raised by the blades of the flying machines, and at first Brazhnikov did not pay much attention to the sharp gust. However, after two steps he was forced to stop, because the wind suddenly became so strong that it became impossible to overcome its onslaught. The major bent down and lowered the armored glass of his helmet. This helped to reduce the noise level a little, now the terrible howl did not block his ears, but that was all. Brazhnikov backed away under the onslaught of the wind, tripped over a hummock and sat down right in the mud. He had never been caught in such a hurricane on level ground. "And for no reason! It was quiet, almost dead calm, and suddenly, here you go, get it. Another anomaly? Isn't that too much? The third time in one square kilometer in one hour. What's really going on here?!" There was no panic. The major had gotten into worse scrapes than that. But the feeling of helplessness – the wind literally pressed him into the mud, not even allowing him to move – Brazhnikov categorically did not like. The role of an outside observer always weighed on him. So far, nothing terrible had happened, and the major could calmly wait out the cataclysm, lying between the hummocks, like a snake that had eaten its fill of frogs. The guys, as far as he could see through the cloud of wet leaves and branches raised by the hurricane, were fine. Almost all of them had already loaded into the helicopters and were now looking out anxiously, trying to spot the commander. But this uneven distribution of troubles worried the major. It seemed as if the hurricane was raging exclusively over Brazhnikov’s head, and some hundred meters to the east there was the same dead calm. “An anomaly is an anomaly... but where is the conscience?” The major felt that the persistent wind was trying to lift the prey and carry it away through the air to the ends of the earth, like the sorcerer Chernomor. Brazhnikov grabbed the withered grass and tried to press himself even closer to the ground. In order to completely deprive the hurricane of a chance, the major even sacrificed the cleanliness of his armored glass and, bending his head lower, literally hit his face (or rather the visor of his helmet) in the mud. The wind reluctantly retreated. Not immediately, but still weakened its mad onslaught, and soon died down completely, as if it had never been there. The major raised his head, tried to wipe the glass, but only smeared the dirt. He had to raise the visor. Having done this, Brazhnikov realized two things: that his premonitions had not deceived him, and that everything that had happened in this godforsaken place had been a warm-up, the worst was looming ahead. Or rather, around. The anomalous wind had not died down at all. It was just that now it was blowing not from the east or from any other direction, but was dancing in a circle, specifically around Brazhnikov. The major, of course, was not under any illusions, it was unlikely that the anomaly was dancing its jig especially for him, it just so happened that today Brazhnikov was destined to find himself in the center of events every time. First in the center of a strange "circle of the dead", now in the center of an irregular tornado. Moreover, the new circle was approximately the same diameter as the previous one - about thirty forty meters. The major looked back. The whirlwind was no longer playing with leaves and broken branches. Now it was building something like a translucent wall from tiny water drops and liquid mud, only this wall was not gray or brown, but reddish. Brazhnikov once again assessed the power of the raging elements and involuntarily shuddered. The show was impressive, to be sure. The major suddenly wanted to go closer to the swaying wall. He took a step, but then someone touched him on the shoulder. The major turned around abruptly. Behind him stood a man, smeared with dirt no worse than himself, in a uniform, presumably a mercenary. It was impossible to determine exactly how dirty this man was. But the filter mask on the guy's face remained relatively clean. "A competent walker," the major concluded. "How come I didn't notice him right away? Where was he hiding? And who does he remind me of? Someone familiar, perhaps? I can't tell, only his eyes are visible, and even that is conditional, I can't even determine his color." It was indeed difficult to see the stranger's eyes through the tinted glass, and yet it seemed to Brazhnikov that he had met this man before. And then there was that gesture... the stranger clapped the major on the shoulder in a very familiar way. It would have been logical to simply ask "Who are you?", but it was impossible to hear any words through the howling wind. The wind was quickly picking up speed and was already roaring louder than a jetliner taking off. Try talking while standing thirty meters from the runway. The only thing left was sign language. Brazhnikov nodded and looked questioningly at the man. He pointed at the mud wall, shook his head negatively and seemed to beckon the wall towards him. The major looked around once again and understood the hidden meaning of the pantomime. The ring of the mad whirlwind was slowly shrinking. And again an association with the black spot at the warehouse gate arose. The movement of the anomaly in this case was in the opposite direction, but the speed was the same. Brazhnikov involuntarily backed up to where, according to his feelings, the center of the circle outlined by the whirlwind was located. The stranger again shook his head negatively and delicately pushed the major aside. In his opinion, the center should not be occupied. Brazhnikov soon understood why. When the whirlwind compressed the howling ring to a diameter of only ten meters, a bright scarlet flash flashed right in the center of the circle, about thirty centimeters from the ground, and an artifact that literally appeared out of nowhere fell into the dirt. The size of a fist, it strongly resembled a carelessly processed diamond of not the purest water - with a clear crimson tint. Unlike the "oil puddles" and "ring vortices", this Zone creation was familiar to the major. He had seen such artifacts during a recent excursion to a military laboratory. Scientists claimed that this was the rarest and most expensive - the market price was up to a million euros! - artifact, produced by no less than the "Black Angel" itself, an anomaly that was poorly studied and extremely dangerous, and which also appeared exclusively in the center of the Zone. That is, where only three people out of several tens of thousands of inhabitants of the Zone and the Borderland dared to go. But the artifact's dubious origin and outrageous price were not the strongest jokes about the reddish piece of fused glass. The funniest were the rumors that "Joker", as stalkers simply dubbed the artifact, provides absolute protection from all misfortunes, from radiation to any anomaly, including the mother one, that is, the "Black Angel" itself, and also allows you to control mutants and heals even severe wounds. A panacea, in a word. Plus the magic flute and Prince Julian's armor. Brazhnikov did not believe in the outright nonsense about the super-artifact, and therefore was not surprised to see that the "Joker" was in the game, but it was not the "Angel" that was "giving him up", but only a relatively harmless anomalous vortex. Actually, that is why the major did not even raise an eyebrow when the artifact was quickly picked up and put into the pocket of a nimble stranger. The major was much more concerned about the operational situation than about the dubious artifact. It was, to put it mildly, shitty and was getting worse with each passing second. The people who had fallen into the trap stood shoulder to shoulder, and soon they would have to decide whether they would stand back to back or embrace, as if merging in a final dance. The circle howling at the top of its voices shrank to a minimum. "But the dirt flying at such speed can grind us down no worse than a sandblasting machine. It will polish us to the bone, that's a fact. Damn it, I managed to get myself into trouble!" It was unknown what the man standing behind the major was thinking, but he seemed to be preoccupied with other problems. Or the same ones, but from a different perspective. He suddenly pulled the hidden artifact out of his pocket and thrust it into Major A. Brazhnikov's hand. He looked at the man in surprise. He explained with a gesture: "We're breaking through, you're first." The major grinned and tried to return the "Joker," but the stranger blocked his hand and nodded confidently. It was strange, but for some reason Brazhnikov believed him. There was no chance of coming up with another way to survive anyway, so why not rely on the experience of a fellow sufferer? He seemed to know what he was talking about, or rather gesturing. Why did he give up the artifact to the major? There was no time to think about that either. So it had to be that way. The stranger seemed to confirm the major's conclusion, with a new gesture indicating the last point of the agreement: "We'll break through - you'll return it." Brazhnikov nodded, closed his visor and prepared for a fight that was obviously lost. He didn't have to jump into the roaring wall with a desperate cry. Vikhr himself closed his heavy embrace and prepared to sharpen "under the pencil" the people who had fallen into his "sandblasting". The stranger suddenly took something out of his pocket again, stretched his hand out in front of him, stepped forward and tried to stand in front of the major, as if covering him with himself, but swayed and fell back, into Brazhnikov's arms... …The first thought was simple and clear: “Alive!” The major was lying on his back in the same cold mud, but the hurricane was no longer howling around him, and his arms, legs, and head were in place, and not at all worn down to the bones. And in general, he felt normal. A second later, slowly but surely, the next thought came: “It’s good that he’s alive, but it’s better not to lie around.” Brazhnikov carefully sat up and raised his “visor.” Directly ahead, a meter-high parapet rose, on which a stranger sat with his back to the major. "Break through?" The major quickly looked over his uniform. Everything was in place. The sandblasting had done almost no damage. The stranger, at least from behind, also seemed normal. "Maybe I imagined it?" Shadows of approaching people flashed over the parapet. Brazhnikov did not hear their steps, his ears were still ringing, but he felt the vibration of the ground. It seemed that everyone was rushing to the rescue, including the helicopter pilots. The major stood up and took a step toward the sitting man, but suddenly froze in place. The reason for this was the faces of the special forces who ran up to their "partners" who had escaped from the anomaly. More precisely, the expressions on those very faces. Having seen a lot both in the Zone and beyond, the specialists stood rooted to the spot and stared at the sitting stranger, as if they had seen a living ghost. A mixture of mistrust and slight fear was read in the guys' looks. It was as if they could not believe that a person in such a state was able to sit as if alive and sway rhythmically, as if lulling the pain that had gripped his body. Brazhnikov walked around the stranger in a circle and joined the fighters who had gathered in indecision. It was really rare to see something like this. Unlike the back and the back of the head, which were untouched by the sandblasting, the man's face, arms and chest were very, very seriously damaged. Neither the Kevlar combat suit, nor the bulletproof vest, nor the helmet, nor the mask helped. The lower part of the body was more or less intact (a "Freudian" thought flashed through Brazhnikov's mind: "Everything is intact between the belt and the knees, and that's good"), but the face, chest and arms were transformed into huge wounds, completely devoid of skin. Muscles trembled under whitish fascia, bare bones showed white, and the surviving vessels pulsated heavily. But the most terrible thing was the man's gaze. He stared at one point with eyes that had lost their eyelids and seemed surprised that he was still alive. Combined with the gaping hole of his nose and the grin of his uncovered jaws, the look of the living dead man was doubly eerie. - What are you standing there for? Move over! - The group's doctor pushed his way to the parapet. - Get the stretcher out of the helicopter! Mr. Major, are you okay? - Huh? - Brazhnikov turned his gaze to the medic. - I'm... fine. Do you think we'll get him there? - It's a tough case, but there's a chance. - The doctor sat down and placed the bag with the medical kit on his lap. - If he doesn't die from pain shock along the way, he might pull through. We need to give him an injection as soon as possible, and then... The medic shrugged. - Well then! - Brazhnikov looked around and surveyed the clearing where he had almost died a few minutes ago. Or almost turned into a living skeleton, which is hardly better. The parapet extended around the entire clearing and seemed to mark the initial boundaries of the vortex. But the major was not interested in this. He was concerned about the place where the artifact had appeared. No, not that he was really concerned, it was just... his intuition had woken up. And not in vain. Right in the center of the circle, an "oil" spot was clearly visible. For now, it was the size of a saucer. The major turned back to his subordinates and gestured for them to hurry. The "cleaners" were killed by the same "sandblasting", that's a fact. Not by grinding them down like this guy, but by some other method, but it was exactly the same anomaly as the one that had recently raged around the major. "It's good that my guys got into the helicopter," the thought flashed. "If they had been closer, they would have died for some unknown reason. And it looks like a 'rollback' is brewing here, similar to the one at the warehouse gates." The prospect of getting stuck in an "oil puddle" for the sake of completeness of sensations did not warm Brazhnikov. For today he had exhausted his personal limit of thrills. When the group had settled back into the choppers, the major jumped aboard the second, "ambulance," car and sat down next to the stranger. The bandages the doctor had applied were soaked through almost immediately, and now his unknown savior looked like a blood-soaked mummy. Brazhnikov caught himself involuntarily calling the stranger a savior. His hand slipped into his pocket. The artifact was in place. It was warm to the touch and seemed electrified, a slight tingling sensation spread through his fingers. The major took the thing out and turned it over in front of his eyes. It looked like a Christmas tree decoration, not an artifact. But if not that, then what helped him survive the sandblasting? Brazhnikov caught the doctor's gaze out of the corner of his eye. - What? - Nothing. - The medic nodded at the artifact. - If the scientists are not lying, it is a valuable thing. The victim could have used this thing. – Do you believe that this artifact is... omnipotent? - I don't know, there are few of them yet, they haven't been studied properly, but there's no smoke without fire. You, for example, got out without a single scratch. It wasn't a coincidence, was it? “Not by chance.” Brazhnikov carefully placed the artifact on the stretcher, next to the victim’s head. - Better here, so it doesn't roll down. - The doctor busily hid the thing under the wounded man's arm. - Now we'll definitely get it there. And in general. - Do you believe so much in the power of artifacts, doctor? - The major glanced at the medic from under his brows. - Medicine is a science, but the Zone, mutants, anomalies, artifacts - it's almost mysticism... do they go together? - Mysticism is everything we can't understand. - The doctor shrugged again. - For me, for example, mathematics is pure mysticism. What is it now, not science? The Zone and its products are the same old story... I'll tell the team that this "Joker" is yours. The guy will get better, the doctors will return him to you. - Not mine, his. But pass it on when commanded. To be on the safe side, so that no one steals it... for the sake of mysticism. - They can. - The doctor nodded. - In Dymer they can. - Leave Dymer alone. - The major shook his head. - We'll take him to our hospital. “The patient is a civilian,” the medic doubted, “it’s not allowed.” "That's an order," Brazhnikov said firmly. "I'll sort it out with the hospital authorities. And one more thing, doctor... take three guys and organize round-the-clock security." - In the hospital? They have plenty of their own security there. “I understand that you are a military man only formally, Captain of the Medical Service Fomin,” Brazhnikov condescendingly patted the medic on the shoulder, “but... if you discuss orders, I’ll give you a punch in the face, okay?” - I see... that is, yes, sir. - Fomin sighed resignedly. - Should I guard the artifact or the patient? - Together. You said he wouldn't survive without the Joker. But the patient first and foremost. I don't like this whole oil painting, Fomin. I don't like it. Brazhnikov, as if to emphasize that the concerns were serious, looked out the porthole. - I don’t understand why we’re not taking off? The major asked the question quietly, and the pilots did not answer it, but the "answer" in the form of a small missile fired from a MANPADS flew in from the nearest forest belt. Brazhnikov and Fomin were lucky only in that the missile was intended for the other side. Then the events completely broke loose from their leash and rushed at a gallop. The major jumped back from the glass and grabbed Fomin by the sleeve. - Hold on! The helicopter shook noticeably at that very moment. The doctor didn't have time to grab onto something like a handrail and fell on the wounded man. Brazhnikov meanwhile jumped to the door and pushed it aside. - Let's jump! Fomin was, of course, a "semi-military", but in critical situations he was no slower than a special forces soldier. He grabbed the stretcher with the wounded man and turned it over sharply. It turned out quite successfully. The major, who jumped to the ground, caught the wounded man who fell out of the helicopter and fell on his back. The patient turned out to be quite heavy. The doctor threw out the stretcher and also jumped out. The second missile was not long in coming and hit the tail of the "ambulance" helicopter. For some unknown reason, the machine roared with its engines, which had been "silent" until then, and began to bury its nose in the ground, simultaneously turning around its vertical axis clockwise. Brazhnikov briefly imagined how the heavy gray side of the helicopter crushed the lingering people, but the image did not linger in his imagination. There was no time for fantasies. The major grabbed the wounded man by the shoulders and dragged him away from the rumbling, ironing ground helicopter. Fomin also quickly left the danger zone and not empty-handed, he dragged a stretcher. “Jump!” the major turned and shouted to the pilots. The helicopter pilots had almost gotten out of the dying machine without a team, but at that moment another missile hit the engines, and the helicopter burst into flames, settled down, and began to fall apart. The blast wave lifted the three survivors slightly, and then slammed them hard into the ground. Brazhnikov remained conscious, and did not lose control for a moment, but he clicked his teeth with all his heart, which was also not very pleasant. However, the wounded man got it the hardest. The "Joker" was still with him, his pulse seemed to be palpable, but it is unlikely that a new portion of slaps did the poor guy any good. Fomin did not get off as easy as the major, a piece of some helicopter part caught his shoulder, but the doctor was combat-ready and even thought soberly, without panic. - We need to get to the warehouse, otherwise we’ll get lost in the field! - Agreed. - Brazhnikov pointed to the copse. - They were firing from there, so we need to go the other way. We need to go down to the water. We'll get up there, put the wounded man on a stretcher and walk along the shore. We'll enter the warehouse through the gates on the pier. The main ones are blocked by the anomaly. - Got it. Let's crawl? - Exactly. - Brazhnikov glanced sideways at the burning wreckage of the helicopters and chuckled sadly. - He who is born to crawl flies... not very well. Grab the right shoulder, and I'll grab the left. Forward!..

It only seems that night is a time of complete silence and serene peace. The night is full of sounds: the noise of raindrops, footsteps, rustling, howling wind, indistinct echoes of someone's groans, and filled with life, even if not always normal. And it is also saturated with fear, heavy smells and moisture. Especially moisture. At the end of October, the nights here are extremely damp. Moisture hangs in the air as small drops of rain, settles on clothes as a thin film of oily condensation, runs down the face in small drops, mixes with sweat and leaves a salty taste on the lips. Walking through this damp disgrace on an autumn night is terribly tiring and doubly dangerous. The most reasonable thing to do is to climb into the cabin of a rusty truck abandoned many years ago or into some ruins and settle down for the night, but here in the Zone life has its own logic. Tired eyes close by themselves, damp palms slide off the wet butt of the machine gun, and legs slide apart in the slippery mud, but the frightening sounds, smells and shadows in the gray night darkness do not allow you to stop to rest and wait until the rain stops or at least until dawn comes. They urge you on no worse than a long shepherd's whip, they make you count new steps and kilometers, stubbornly moving towards the goal or walking in a circle, everything depends on the tactics and personal preferences of the stalker. But the common tactic of all prospectors was and remains one rule: always move. Only those who are constantly moving have more or less decent chances to return. And if you consider that most people come here not for fun, but for business, constant movement also becomes commercially profitable. And yet, a person cannot walk forever. A regular raid into the Zone takes, of course, less time than eternity lasts, but it is unrealistic to stay on your feet for a week - from Emission to Emission. Halts are necessary, no matter how frightening the stops in the dank night are, no matter how it seems that if you sit down for a moment and relax, you will immediately become prey to hungry mutants prowling everywhere or a victim of competitors. And a fire, to at least dry your clothes and equipment a little, is also simply necessary. And here you have to choose between fear and health. However, for experienced stalkers the choice is obvious. Mutants, who cares! And competitors are more likely to pass by or sit down by the fire; offer to buy or sell some things, exchange found artifacts, simply tell news from the mainland. A night fire in the Zone is a kind of truce territory. It happened that on particularly cold winter nights even fighters from warring groups got along quite peacefully by the fires, not to mention ordinary stalkers, who, by and large, have nothing to quarrel about. There is no hostility between them. Healthy competition, and that's all. Of course, it doesn't always work out that way, but that's only because there are a lot of freaks wandering around the Zone who don't give a damn about all the laws and traditions, and there are also a lot of crazy people or people who are simply exhausted by endless tests of strength and despair. But most stalkers still follow the unwritten rules. For example, don't walk alone, don't get involved with groups, don't put artifacts in a backpack but carry them in several belt pouches, don't take personal tokens as trophies, and only bring "passengers" into the Zone for a specific purpose. The Zone doesn't like those who come here on an excursion or a "safari", that's a fact. They get it in the neck. And the guides get it in the neck too. There are rules about the fire too. And it seems that they are approved by the Zone. Those who break them do not live long. Perhaps this is just a superstition, but no one risks testing the veracity of the sign on themselves. "Even rats avoid fires. They seem to be creatures, and mutants at that, but they sense that the Zone has a special opinion on this matter," one of the stalkers sitting by the fire smoothed out the coals and placed an opened can of meat on them. “But I don’t believe in omens,” said the other, lazily adjusting the hood of his rubberized raincoat. “And in general, I don’t give a damn about all these superstitions. I just do what common sense tells me, and that’s it. It works without fail. Are you saying that the Zone is to blame for this too?” - The Zone is not to blame for anything. Just as it is not your fault that you were born so stubborn. The first stared for a second into the impenetrable darkness beyond the edge of the light spot, and then began to blow on the coals barely smoldering in the drizzling rain. The second adjusted his hood again and fidgeted, settling himself more comfortably. - I see you don't understand me, Phil. We've been together for so long, but you still haven't gotten into my attitude to life. You keep clogging your brain with some incomprehensible theories. You keep trying to find a common language with the Zone, like some kind of egghead. That's why you can't get your hands on artifacts. You're thinking about the wrong thing. "It's not about theories." Phil blinked rapidly and rubbed his eyes with his fist. "And it's not about a common language with the Zone. I just want to understand if there's a place for people in it or not?" - Exactly. And there's no time left for action. Mark my words, you'll get nothing but hemorrhoids from your thoughts. You certainly won't make any money. Follow my example! I live easily and in complete harmony with my body. If I want to sleep, I sleep, if I want to drink, I drink, and I don't bother my head with nonsense. - Come on, Motya, I've been imbued with your philosophy for a long time. Lately I've been thinking about almost nothing, but the loot still floats past. It's probably not my thing. I'm trying in vain. I've turned out to be a lousy stalker. - But you express yourself smoothly, and your head is full of all sorts of ideas. You could open a business in the borderland. Well, like paid courses for beginning stalkers. - Who needs these courses? Everyone is smart these days, everyone has long known where artifacts come from and how much they cost. And how many newbies, according to statistics, do not return from their first raid, does not matter to anyone. The hunt is worse than captivity. Plus the damned greed. A gold rush, an epidemic broke out, you can't call it anything else. - That's true, the Zone has stirred up the people, straightened out many people's brains. But not everyone is coming here out of greed. Take Monolith or Freedom. Did these lunatics come here for money? Their ideas brought them here. And Duty? They drag everything they collect to scientists. They're losing so much money! They're also crazy idealists. What does greed have to do with it? "In this case, it has nothing to do with it," Phil shrugged and threw a couple of damp branches onto the fire. "But this is another option, a clinical one. In the Zone, everything is not like with people." There was more smoke from the branches than heat, but the rain had not stopped for a minute in the last two days, and no one would have been able to find dry deadwood. Perhaps in the forest, between the boars' beds. But there it was more likely to find death than decent firewood. - A profound thought, - the partner chuckled. - I, with my average mind, wouldn't be able to dive to the bottom. It's obvious that you're a former philosopher. - And there are plenty of military here, too, - Phil continued thoughtfully. - And quite a few scientists. No, you've gone too far with the gold rush. If you take it as a whole, less than half of the Zone are prospectors. The rest earn money in other ways or don't earn it at all. - Okay, I agree, - Motya lazily turned from side to side. - How's the stew going, is it warmed up? - You should take the "Soul" off your belt, - Phil glanced jealously at his comrade's pouch, - Otherwise you'll just have to gobble and gobble. You'll soon burst. - I don't have to eat everything, - the partner yawned loudly. - I'll have a little drink and go to sleep. And there's nothing to envy. I invited you, you refused. — I never get involved with the Carousel. I'm not afraid of anything, I can even get very close to the Funnel... well, almost... and this one... as soon as I see it, my heart sinks. Maybe I have a premonition of something? - Your death? That's unlikely. Unless, of course, you're slowly turning into a "dwarf," - Motya laughed shortly. - They sense everything long before. The experts said that some of them set up ambushes a week in advance. They foresee everything, the bastards, and they're not afraid of anyone. Except maybe blind dogs, and even then, when they attack in a pack. How are things with our X-rays? He yawned again and shivered. Either from the autumn chill, or from the mention of the creepy, half-blind dwarfs from the dungeons of Chernobyl and Pripyat. - Half a bottle left, if that's what you're hinting at. It's not much for fun, it'll only make you sleepy, and it'll help against radiation somehow. But for such purposes it should be consumed little by little, evenly. - Well, let's... finish her off evenly, and let her feel sleepy, we were going to take a nap for half an hour anyway. - No. There's still a day before the Emission. There's a lot to do. - I've already stocked up pretty well. I can slack off. - You are an insensitive person, Motya. You are fine, and that's okay? If a boar bites you, you will crawl out as best you can. You don't want to help me, and I won't help you. "The Soul" will help," Motya lazily patted his bag. "With it, all wounds heal before our eyes, you know." - And your legs are like cotton wool, - Phil grinned crookedly. - And you can only escape from a boar on your legs, you don't really have anything to shoot with. And you don't have anything. How many bullets are left? Ten? - One and a half. And, by the way, all with explosives, - the partner stretched and yawned. - Very rare, hand-made, you could say. Taken from a dead man. Looks like he was a mercenary. “A mercenary with a Kalashnikov?” Phil doubted. - What? Do you think they all have advanced guns? By the way, the Kalashnikov, if seven-sixty-two and with special cartridges, is the most versatile machine. "A hay mower," Phil chuckled. "No precision, especially with yours, the shortened one. And the cartridges, both homemade and factory-made, are rubbish on vegetable oil, every tenth one misfires." - Well, yeah, how can we compete with your Italy! - Motya snorted resentfully. - Anyway, Phil, don't push it. I'll help, so be it. In the morning. Okay? And now let me take a nap, since you're not pouring me any. Motya stuck his hand behind his collar and happily scratched his shoulder, chafed by the strap of his backpack. Phil wanted to answer his partner, but suddenly stood up abruptly and reached for his weapon. Motya also became wary and froze in an awkward pose - his hand behind his collar. Both slowly turned in approximately the same direction and stared into the darkness. They could not see anything, but that was not necessary. The stalkers were listening. From the southwest, approximately from the direction of Old Krasnitsa or Buryakovka, a wave of new sounds rolled in, uncharacteristic for a rainy night. The rustling and whispering gave way to distinct stomping and growling, and the sound of rain and the rustling of leaves drowned in an indistinct muttering and snorting, clearly not made by people, but not by animals either. Motya with difficulty freed his damp limb from under the hood, stood up and, rummaging in his backpack, took out an army night vision device. The almost extinguished fire did not interfere with the device's work, and the stalker perfectly saw the source of the cacophony that had disrupted the standard night "soundtrack". Skirting the dying fire and following strictly in one direction, dozens, and maybe even hundreds of creatures were walking past the frozen prospectors. Among them were quite a few people (or they were zombies, but in any case, bipedal), although the main mass of the living wave moving somewhere towards the center of the Zone consisted of all sorts of mutants. The sight was eerie and incomprehensible even to an experienced walker in the Zone, and there were two reasons for this. Firstly, the creatures walked as if enchanted, looking straight ahead and not paying attention even to their sworn enemies, who were often walking nearby, at a distance of one jump. For example, literally a meter from the fire, a snork hobbled on all fours, which, contrary to its usual behavior, did not sniff the ground or jump up like a cat under an electric shock. Motya somehow fastened the strap of the noctoscope, fixing the device on his head, and raised his favorite (albeit heavily "used") "AKSU", preparing for a fight, but the mutant did not even twitch an ear, as if the stalker did not exist. The boar, following almost on the heels of the snork, reacted to the stunned prospectors in exactly the same way, that is, in no way, and a whole pack of blind dogs trotted a little to the left - creatures usually very aggressive, but now somehow stunned and whining, like newborn puppies. All this did not fit into the usual framework, but the second circumstance looked even stranger. None of the migrating creatures even tried to go around the anomalies, which seemed to have been arranged in large numbers along the invisible "migration path" by order. Motya clearly saw how several blind dogs, who usually sensed any traps, were sucked into the "funnel", and the terrible pseudo-giant, trudging along on unsteady legs, was spun and torn into small pieces by the "carousel". In complete bewilderment, Motya turned to his comrade and discovered that he had also put on “night glasses,” but was looking not in the direction of the Zone’s border, but after the wandering mutants. “Do you understand anything?” his partner asked hoarsely, feeling Motan’s gaze. - I... well, sort of... no, - admitted Motya. - But actually... The release is soon. It's like clockwork now, once a week. Maybe that's the point, they're rushing to their holes. “Then we should wander to the periphery,” Phil shook his head. “These ones are heading to Starye Shepelichi, and maybe to Kosharovka or straight to Pripyat.” - Maybe it's the Call? - the partner put forward a new version. - Remember, they said at the base?
