I don't remember who... I think it was Ryaboy. Well, a new anomaly has appeared. The Call. Who is calling and why - no one knows, but even the controllers are losing their will and are following this damn call, like those rats to a pipe. “It was Ryaboy’s idea,” Phil objected quietly, finally turning to Mota. “And Pal Palych said that this Call is being issued by a particularly powerful controller. It has infiltrated Pripyat, settled in the Energetik there and is trumpeting ‘general assembly’. And the Zone amplifies this call. Or perhaps the controllers have learned to cloud our consciousness ‘in chorus’ and…” Phil faltered, breaking off his sentence and staring into the darkness again. - Us? - Motya shook his head. - The call doesn't only affect people. Look, even rats and wolves are drawn to it. I think it's the call of the Monolith. "Sectarian nonsense," Phil muttered, squatting and feeling for his backpack. "These Monolithists are ready to blame everything on their brick. Any phenomenon anywhere on the globe. - What earthly? - Motya pulled off his glasses and wiped the sweat from his forehead. “Shara,” the partner repeated almost without intonation. - Ah, - Motya felt himself going numb, as if after a large dose of tranquilizer. Trying to drive away the obsession, he shook his head, but the numbness did not go away. - I don't feel right, Phil. Maybe we should get out of here before it's too late? - Although, it is possible that the sectarians are right, and the Monolith really does have special properties, - Phil muttered under his breath. - Or it is the Zone itself... calling. One way or another, something else is important - those who have gone to the Call do not return. Yes, Motya, let's get out of here before it's too late. Life for a stalker is movement... or vice versa... it doesn't matter. Let's go. He threw his backpack over his shoulder, put the shotgun on the crook of his elbow and resolutely took a step against the movement of the strange wave of migrants. He took only one step. Then he turned sharply around and wandered off with everyone else. Motya opened his mouth to call out to his comrade, but for some reason he couldn’t squeeze out a sound – a lump suddenly rose in his throat, as if from resentment or an attack of unaccountable fear. The next second, an electric discharge seemed to run through his body, the stalker shook thoroughly, and his legs carried him after his partner. - Stop, Phil! - Motya finally managed to cope with the nervous spasm. - We can't go there, we'll be lost! “If we don’t get sucked into the river, we’ll get out,” the comrade said through gritted teeth without turning around. - Wait! - Motya grabbed his friend by the sleeve. - Follow me! I'm not so smashed, I have a full set of artifacts on my belt! There are two "sea urchins". They will protect us both from any misfortunes, if we walk next to each other. From the Call too... probably. Together we will get out! "It's a futile undertaking, Motya," Phil turned his glassy gaze to his friend and smiled with just his lips, without eyes. It looked worse than a zombie's grin. "We're screwed. Fact." "We didn't hit anything!" Motya looked around in a futile attempt to find something like an anchor, something that could dispel the spell and give his partners the strength to overcome the Call. At first he didn't find anything, but after a couple of seconds his gaze fell upon a figure in a dark robe, confidently walking "against the current." The unknown man passed about fifty meters from the stalkers, now they could only see his back, but that didn't stop them from seeing how seriously equipped the Call-defying walker was. Behind the man in the long coat hung a backpack of an old American flamethrower, not as powerful as the Soviet "LPO-50", but also not as heavy and more economical. Its ammunition consisted of seven one-and-a-half-liter shots, against three, three liters with a hook, for the "LPO-50". In his hands, the flamethrower operator was clutching a pair of "silent" pistols, which he alternately used when the slow-moving mutants walking towards him did not want to give way to him. - Look, - Motya made his friend turn around. - A man is going his own way - and nothing! And we will go too! Just shake yourself, Phil! “I can’t,” Phil dropped his hands limply and stared at the ground. “I can’t.” - Shake yourself! - Motya slapped him hard on the cheek, grabbed his chin and forced him to look again after the man walking his way, - That's where we need to go, to Rechitsa, not to the center. There! The stranger, meanwhile, drew level with the skeleton of the truck, about a hundred meters from the stalkers’ extinguished fire, and stopped, clearly intending to “light up” his flamethrower. "We'll follow him like an icebreaker!" whispered Motya. "Just try, Phil!" - No, let go! - The partner tried to break free, but Motya held on tightly. - Do you see? - Motya wanted to use the man with the flamethrower as an example again, but stopped short. The darkness of the night was torn apart by a prolonged horizontal lightning. The fiery stream filled the remains of the truck's cabin with hellish flames, in which writhing human silhouettes could be discerned. The stranger stopped firing, bent down and picked up some equipment from the ground. From afar it was difficult to understand what it was, but Mota thought it was bags for artifacts. And full of swag. In principle, this barbaric massacre in the Zone looked almost normal. The flamethrower killed several stalkers, or maybe bandits, and took their loot. In the Zone, such behavior was the order of the day. But as soon as Motya decided so, the situation changed dramatically. The stranger tied all the pouches into one knot and threw them into the burning cabin. There was no doubt that the trophies would burn to ashes. Mota didn't want to use him as an example or call him a "rescue icebreaker" any more. The crazy ones are worse than mutants, it's better not to mess with them, this is one of the main laws of the Zone. Stalker let go of his comrade and sighed heavily. There was nowhere to retreat, and there was almost no strength left to resist the Call. The remnants of will were rapidly evaporating, despite the support and protection of the artifacts. - What is he doing... the barbarian... - Motya stared with a glassy gaze at the silhouette of the stranger, swaying in the reflections of the chain of small fires that had formed after the fire "show". - He's losing so much money... "The Stalkers are losing," the comrade added with a noticeable delay. "And not for the first day. I know who that flamethrower is. It's Smoker. After Strelok, he's the shadiest guy in the Zone. The traders have set a good price for his skin." - Well, go ahead and try to get this skin, - Motya said sadly. - You'll lose yours in no time. Have you seen his lighter? - I could handle it, but... - Phil shook his head. - I can't. No time. This... Call. Pulls. Let's go, or I'll turn inside out. "I'm feeling the same way," admitted his partner. "Damn it, Phil, we're going to die if we go any further!" “I know,” the partner answered indifferently, like a robot. “I don’t care. I’ll go.” - And this... Smoker... doesn't care about anything, - Motya sighed. - The crazy ones are lucky. "It doesn't matter." Phil stumbled on level ground, fell to one knee, got up using an expensive Italian SPAS-12 shotgun like a regular crutch, and again wandered in the direction of the distant ruins of Novo-Shepelichi, the northwestern outskirts of the dead city of Pripyat - the center of the Zone. “Maybe it doesn’t matter, but I’ll go first,” said Mo-tya, and, taking off his backpack, he walked three steps ahead of his comrade. "It's all pointless," Phil whispered barely audibly. "Who goes first doesn't matter. You can't cheat either the Call or death with this." After walking a hundred meters, he unhooked and threw down the pouch with the meager haul of artifacts, threw off his backpack, and then, absolutely without regret, threw down his gun. Bass, on the edge of perception, the Call simultaneously frightened and beckoned. The combination of unaccountable horror and inappropriate delight, akin to the euphoria of a premonition of great success, created a sense of unreality of what was happening. It could not be so, but it was. The remnants of common sense tried to sober up the prospector who was losing control of himself, but all was in vain. With every step, with every meter that brought Phil closer to the city ruins, the mysterious Call became more insistent and, it seemed, more voluminous. It filled not only all the space around, but also penetrated every cell of the body, touched every nerve, every neuron, which made his movements constrained and uncertain, like those of a zombie, and his will and mind finally dissolved in the eerie, but melodic sea of low sound. The prospector walked, no longer thinking about the mortal danger waiting ahead, not experiencing any feelings except for fear, heavy as a concrete slab, and a fatal desire to quickly reach the unknown source of the Call. And nothing could interfere with this desire. Not even Phil's long-standing fear of the "Carousel", monotonously circling in the pre-dawn twilight. Phil panicked for a moment, but the damned indifference mercilessly trampled the panic that would have been saving in this case, and the Call continued to pull him forward, straight into the deadly anomaly. Phil looked sadly at the hunched back of his partner, who was walking ahead and slightly to the right. Motya's route lay literally three meters from the conventional line, stepping over which a person risked instant death. An invisible but powerful whirlwind spun and tore the victims apart, scattering only small scraps of the unfortunate around. If Motya had moved slightly to the left, he would definitely not have been up to the damned Call. However, his partner was walking where he was walking. In addition, he had more than one valuable artifact in his pouch. In addition to the "Hedgehogs" and "Soul", there was also the "Night Star", which seriously weakened the attraction of anomalies, like this very "Carousel". So now Phil should have worried not about his comrade, but about himself. His route lay exactly "where it should". That is, on the contrary, where it shouldn’t be, exactly through the center of the vortex. Phil tried to stop, but his body wouldn't listen. His legs stubbornly carried him toward death. The same death he had sensed, like an ugly mutant "dwarf", from the very beginning, from his first trip to the Zone. The prospector gathered all his will into a fist, clenched his teeth and tried to change the route, to go at least a few meters to the right, but his will turned into imaginary water, seeped through his fingers, and Phil continued on his way strictly in a straight line. Very soon the gloomy melody of the Call sounded new to Phil. Now it clearly sounded like variations on the theme of a funeral march. But the stalker no longer cared. Everything that was happening seemed like a dream to him. There were only a few steps left to the Carousel when the walker came to his senses for the last time, involuntarily closed his eyes and tried to scream, but horror squeezed his throat, and Phil only wheezed barely audibly... ... Motya still didn't understand what made him emerge for a moment from the Call's melody, dissolving like acid. Either it was someone's hoarse groan, or a distinct sense of trouble, as if transmitted by a strong psionic. After walking about ten steps, he turned around and looked around the area with an indifferent gaze. Phil was not behind him. There was only twilight, the shadows of mutants enchanted by the Call wandering in parallel courses, and almost nearby, behind and slightly to the left, a barely noticeable dust vortex, marking the "Carousel". "Unfortunately, my premonition was right." Motya shook some bloody scraps off his shoulder. "I feel sorry for Phil. Although, perhaps, this is the best option. After all, who knows what awaits me there, at the source of the Call. Maybe something ten times more terrible! And the Carousel is quick and painless. Once - and done. An easy death." He looked forward again and involuntarily raised his eyes above the horizon, to the gray morning sky. In the leaden clouds a small gap appeared for a few seconds, and the stalker saw a piece of blue sky with a reddish dawn edge. "The last dawn," the stalker lowered his gaze to the ground. "The morning of the last day. Was it worth it? Probably it was. And if not, it's too late to guess now. The Call will explain everything... tell everything... and then kill. And it will be right."

Olga was awakened by a drawn-out sound, as if someone had sighed heavily on the floor above. The girl opened her eyes wide and stared into the darkness. She lay there for a few minutes, listening, but she didn’t hear anything else. However, the sound had not been a dream, Olga was sure of that. She dreamed of completely different sounds. Mostly, they were screams, growls, and the crackle of machine guns. Every night for several years in a row. And no matter how many times a night Olga woke up, each new immersion in sleep started the eternal “roller” from the beginning. Even if Olga didn’t remember her dream the next morning, she could swear on a polygraph that she had dreamed the same thing as always. During the first six months, waking up after yet another nightmare, Olga cried, even had a quiet fit of hysterics, but then she got used to it, and after a year she stopped paying attention to her dreams altogether. Or rather, her dream. The same one every night. A person gets used to everything. Especially a person who has been tested by the Zone. Olga closed her eyes and almost immediately found herself back on that fine April day...
...Spring landscapes, some buildings and road junctions flashed past the window of the tour bus. The people in the bus were a little excited, Olga heard the steady hum of conversations and laughter. Olga's eyes were suddenly caught by a road sign. "CHORNOBIL". Against the backdrop of seemingly normal, blooming spring nature and a piercingly blue sky, the sign with the ominous inscription did not look scary at all. Normal. Harmless. The bus pulled into the parking lot in front of the institute, where all excursions to the Exclusion Zone began. The tourists had been warned about this in Kyiv, but someone still asked the young tour guide: "Where have we arrived?" The tour guide, his name was Sasha, answered all questions patiently and kindly. The guy, by the way, was cute. However, Olga was fifteen at the time, and she was extremely attracted to all guys from seventeen to twenty. Sasha got off the bus and suggested that the tourists also go out into the fresh air. Someone, having left the salon, began to look around cautiously, like her mother, someone looked around with curiosity - this was Olga, and, for example, her father did not look around, he stared with hidden sadness at the sign to the right of the entrance. In this institution a long time ago, just after graduating from the institute, he worked. That is why, when Olga suggested going on an excursion to the Zone, he did not twirl his finger at his temple, as his mother did, but agreed. Not right away, after a formal pause, but he agreed. Olga suspected that he had wanted to do this for a long time, but for some reason he could not bring himself to come to the place where he had once been happy. A motley crowd of tourists mingled with another crowd pouring out of a bus that had arrived a minute later. The late bus was already the sixth and did not fit into the cramped parking lot. Exclusion Zone tours were popular, no doubt about it. The crowd around Olga was buzzing, gasping and showering foreign “wows” as they looked at the generally unpresentable picture. There was barely a glimmer of life in the city: scientists and power engineers worked here, workers at small auxiliary enterprises, like the canteen or laundry, sluggishly pulled the strap, but there was not a single local resident. Everyone came here from the mainland and, having worked, returned there. No one lived in Chernobyl permanently. So the city evoked despondency rather than delight. Despite the blue sky stretching above it and the sunlight flooding the unkempt streets. The tour guide seemed to read Olga’s thoughts and smiled encouragingly at her. - Compared to Pripyat, this is still a living city. Well, you'll see for yourself... what's your name? Olga's heart suddenly began to beat faster, and her tongue went dry for a moment. - Olga. - It will be cool, Olya, I promise! Sasha smiled again and turned his attention to the other tourists. - Now you will all go through the instructions, get dosimeters and masks! Don't forget to buy spare batteries! “How long are we going to stay here?” someone from the crowd asked. - An hour in Chernobyl. Then we go to the observation deck next to the sarcophagus and then to Pripyat. There are three hours and then we come back here. We go through security, have lunch - and then to Kyiv. - And what dose of radiation will we receive? - Minimal. - Sasha found Olga with his gaze again and winked. - Don't worry, you're not the first excursion in the Exclusion Zone (he nodded at the other buses). We've been bringing people here for five years now, everything is worked out. He no longer looked at Olga, but she was not offended. Work is work. No time to flirt. After all, the main thing is that he himself paid attention to her! Not to the curly-haired, like a lapdog, blonde with curvy shapes and a wasp waist, but to the plain, big-eyed teenage girl. And also constrained by the presence of her parents. Cool! And not only will there be, but there already is! Olga glanced sideways at her parents. Mom pressed herself against her father's shoulder and muttered something. Olga listened. - I don't feel comfortable here. “Relax,” the father said calmly. “We agreed, no panic! It’s just a little adventure. Right, Olya?” Olga forced a smile and answered with exaggerated cheerfulness: - Yes, Dad! Mom, relax! My classmates have all been here already. Everything will be fine. - You see. - Father put his arm around Mother's shoulders. - Out of the mouths of babes... - Dad! - Olga raised her eyebrows indignantly. The late bus backed up and pulled out onto the roadway. It drove forward and rolled across a small square, preparing to turn around. At this time, the tourists, smiling carefree, trudged toward the entrance to the institute. And at that moment... everything around was filled with a blinding white light from a bright flash. Then the dream usually simply switched to a new episode – in contrast to the previous one, dark and gloomy, but sometimes a narrow terminator of crumpled memories still appeared between the light and the darkness. Olga saw a bus flying into the air, saw how the monstrous force of the blast wave threw a heavy car onto a monument in the center of the square and tore it in two, how then the building collapsed, and a heavy rain of stones fell on the crowd of tourists. In this part of the memory, obscured by reason, another terrible and very important episode was hidden, but Olga could not remember it either in a dream or in reality. Each time she tried to do so, it was as if a fuse was triggered: the dream abruptly returned to the moment of the flash, and Olga was forced to be content with something like a splice. Immediately after the flash came a cold, frightening darkness. Olga sat on the spiky concrete rubble, hugging her knees and sobbing in quiet hysteria. She felt the air moving – there was no roof over the ruins she had huddled in. She smelled a strong burning smell, coughed every now and then from the dust, but Olga was unable to move from her spot to find a less smoky and dusty place. She heard the crackling of burning embers, an incessant roar, a drawn-out howl of the wind and distant echoes of some eerie sounds. They were either groans, or growls, or maybe insane laughter. It was impossible to tell from a distance. And then at some point Olga heard new sounds. They were heavy steps. Several people were carefully walking along the rubble. They kept exchanging remarks, and in the pauses between them something hissed and squeaked. The footsteps grew louder. Then they died down. Olga looked up. A man in military uniform, wearing a filter mask and holding a weapon, stood before her. He shone a flashlight into Olga's face, and the girl closed her eyes. The man slowly approached, sat down and removed his mask. “Don’t be afraid,” he said quietly. “It’s all over. Everything will be fine now. Shall we go?” He smelled of tobacco and sweat, but Olga was not irritated by it, it even made her happy. This was a living person! After the cold, viscous eternity in the dead ruins, meeting a living person was pure happiness, even more than happiness. Olga almost calmed down and leaned forward, fully prepared to stand up and go with the soldier. The soldier also rose from his haunches and extended his hand to Olga... And at that moment Olga saw something she would never be able to forget, even if the nightmares stopped. A terrible monster emerged from the semi-darkness behind the soldier, only vaguely resembling a human, sank its sharp teeth into the soldier's neck and literally bit off his head in two bites. Olga screamed wildly and tried to crawl away, but it did not help her. Blood and warm pieces of human flesh flew into her face, and she choked on her scream. Most often the dream ended there, but occasionally Olga lingered in the terrifying "roller" a little longer. She dreamed that she was pressed against the wall and closing her eyes in anticipation of imminent death. The monster loudly chomped, devouring the remains of the soldier, and Olga was forced to listen to this disgusting chomping, as well as the remarks that came from the headset that flew off the soldier's head. - Berkut, answer Topaz! Berkut! Report the situation, what's going on with you?! Are there any surviving civilians? - Topaz, I'm Berkut, there's heavy smoke, I can't see a thing! Some movement... - What movement, Berkut, speak clearly! Are these civilians? Survivors? - Who can figure them out? Maybe they are survivors. They are behaving strangely. They don't come near us, they shy away, but they don't go far. Damn, it looks like they are surrounding us! - I don't understand you, Berkut. Do you see the enemy?! - No! Damn it, what kind of freaks are these?! Where did they come from?! The on-air dialogue was briefly replaced by the crackling of interference, growling, distant shouts, the noisy breathing of a running man, more growling, and gunshots. - They are everywhere! - Berkut suddenly screamed. - There are thousands of them! - I don't understand, Berkut, who are there thousands of?! Is this an enemy or not? - These creatures are everywhere! - There was panic in Berkut's voice. - They are... NOT PEOPLE! Take that, freaks! Take... The crackle of machine gun fire was heard again, then the scream stopped, replaced by a wheeze and again interference. - Berkut! - Topaz screamed, breaking his voice. - What's going on?! There was no answer. The airwaves were filled with screams, growls, and the same crackling static. The only articulate sounds left on the airwaves were Topaz's cries: - Hold on, Berkut! Help is coming! Gradually, the number of shots became fewer and fewer, and the eerie howling and growling became more distant... and at some point, the sounds disappeared altogether. Even the longest dream ended here. Which is not surprising. In reality, by this moment Olga had lost consciousness... .

Happiness is the same for all stalkers. You made a trip to the Zone, successfully avoiding the traps - happiness. You didn't get bitten by a completely crazy monster - happiness again. You filled a container with trinkets, for which the dealers who settled in the Zone are ready to fill your pockets with tugriks - happiness is doubly good. Well, and if after that you got out of the Zone in one piece, with arms and legs, and a working mind - great happiness. The Zone does not accept everyone, and lets them go - even more rarely. But each person has their own misfortune. Because the Zone is inventive in all sorts of nastiness and mischief. And it has its own surprise for everyone. Special. Not like the others. The Zone - it doesn't like to repeat itself. It will scare one to death, but then let go anyway. And it will kill another where dozens, or even hundreds have passed before him without even stumbling. And after that they will walk and marvel - what can you do, out of the blue... The stalker nicknamed Gupi knew what happiness was. He had been going to the Zone for five years already, and he was still alive and well. But he didn't believe in luck. He knew many young men who believed in their luck, but the Zone chewed them up, swallowed them, and didn't even spit out their bones. Gupi knew for sure that if you wanted to stay alive in the Zone, you shouldn't rely on luck or fortune, but on precise knowledge of the terrain and the habits of the creatures that inhabit it. And most importantly, trust only yourself. After all, no matter how you look at it, the greatest value for every person is their own skin. They can sincerely swear eternal friendship and dog-like loyalty, but when death grabs them by the throat, they will instantly forget about everything. And only one thought will guide all their actions: I want to live! Oh, how I want to live! I want it so much that I'll rip anyone's throat out! With this in mind, Gupi always went to the Zone alone. Maybe that's why he's still alive. He did not join any of the stalker clans - although he had acquaintances among the veterans and even received offers - and never took "master keys" with him - novice stalkers, who can be sent ahead of you on a slippery path. Gupi also refused to work as a guide for extreme tourism enthusiasts who climbed into the Zone to tickle their nerves. Even if the route was not difficult, and the money offered was good. He did not take orders from dealers either. He simply went to the Zone immediately after another emission, climbed into places where not everyone would go, filled a full container with trinkets, and, upon returning, sold the goods to whoever he could. At the same time, Gupi knew the price of his trinkets and did not like to haggle. If a dealer refused to pay what the stalker considered to be the right amount, he silently took the goods and went to another. And having sold the trinkets, he disappeared somewhere for a month or two. Gupi was never seen drunk or debauched. He had no habit of throwing money around. He never gambled, even for small amounts. If he did go into a bar favored by stalkers, it was only for business - to buy weapons, ammunition, or a flash drive with a fresh map. …Where he came from, no one knows. And there are few left who remember how Gupi first stumbled into Krys’s bar. In worn-out tarpaulin boots, wide soldier’s trousers and a shabby army pea coat, belted with a belt with a star-shaped buckle. On his head is a baseball cap with a greasy visor and an incomprehensible inscription GSC. In his belt is a sawed-off sixteenth-caliber Izhevsk over-and-under shotgun. In his hands is a huge shopping bag made of blue-green fiberglass - the kind that “shuttle traders” from China and Turkey used to carry clothes in in the old days. Gupi silently walked through the room, past tables with stalkers staring in amazement at such a wonder, stopped at the counter, put the bag on the floor, looked soulfully at the bartender and quietly asked: - Who can I offer artifacts to here? Hearing this, the Rat - and at that moment the owner himself was standing behind the counter - only smirked contemptuously. What could such a weakling bring? A shock of "Rusty Hair"? "Witch's Tears"? Five empty empty bottles? But when Gupi put his trunk on the table and began to take out the trinkets from it, the Rat's eyes bulged out of his head. And soon, sensing that something unusual was happening, the stalkers who were in the bar began to pull themselves up to the counter. And Gupi, meanwhile, calmly, as if there was no one around, laid out the trinkets he had brought in front of the stunned Rat. First, two "Paradise Apples" hung over the counter without touching it. - How did he get them without a container? - one of the stalkers said in amazement. And out of the bag came three "Sad Whistles", simply stuffed into condoms so they wouldn't whistle for no reason. Then - two briquettes of doom-mumiyo, each the size of a brick. A handful of "Mobius Rings", five "Blunt Lighters", about ten "Shards of Sky" - a couple almost the size of a fist! - one "Dragon's Eye" and a strange object resembling a semi-abstract figurine with smooth lines, reminiscent of a person sitting in thought, as if cast from a material similar to stone, glass and metal at the same time. Before the then unknown Goopy appeared in this bar, only five such figurines existed. He became the sixth. Later, he dragged in three more pieces. Having passed through numerous hands of resellers and intermediaries, one of the figurines was bought by the British Museum. The second was acquired by the Louvre. The rest ended up in private collections. It is unclear why these figurines were called "Crake Gods". Although, what does a corncrake have to do with it? No one could explain what they actually were. Scientists agreed on only one thing - the material from which the "Crake Gods" were made is impossible to obtain in terrestrial conditions. The figurines emitted a black radiation, so they were displayed under caps made of thick leaded glass. It is understandable, the "Crake Gods" were found only behind the Rusty Forest, in the zone of the second radar, where not every experienced stalker will wander. Because if you do not manage to get out of there in time and get caught in psychotropic radiation, then this woodpecker will hit you on the head so hard that your brain will turn into a fricassee. Gupi could not get there without an anomaly detector and special protective equipment on his first trip. It turns out that he found another place. But he kept silent about it, no matter how much he was questioned. But that wasn't all! At the end, Gupi took out a three-liter jar covered with a plastic lid from his bag, and in it were two homunculi! Here, for sure, everyone's mouths gaped. And Krys immediately went for the tugriks - lest the newcomer offer his trinkets to anyone else. And, it must be said, he paid Gupi fairly. He paid most of it in tugriks, and the rest in weapons and equipment. Although later, when Gupi had drunk a cup of coffee and left the bar, Krys, looking after him, shook his head sympathetically. - He's a lucky bastard, but he won't last long. The Zone doesn't like the lucky ones. That time, not only Krys, but probably everyone in the bar decided that the newcomer was simply incredibly lucky. This happens sometimes. A newcomer goes into the Zone, climbs into a place where no one has been, brings back some expensive biryuli, gets drunk in joy, has a blast, and then disappears without a trace on his second trip. Besides, even dressed as befits a stalker, Gupi hardly resembled a stalker. He was almost two meters tall, but skinny, like a Stalinist prisoner, with shoulders that looked so feeble that it was strange how the straps of his backpack did not fall off them. Gupi's face was narrow - someone even joked that all he had was a profile - with a sharp chin, a long, hooked nose, slightly slanted, close-set eyes and large ears that seemed glued to his skull. And to top it all off, a thin thread of black moustache on his lip. Not a stalker, seasoned by life, but a Mediterranean pimp. However, Gupi went to the Zone time after time and returned safe and sound. And for the trinkets he brought, the traders almost threw themselves at each other with knives. Everyone knew that any such trinket, although expensive, would pay for itself three times over. That's the minimum. Gupi also had strange habits. Strange for a stalker. Firstly, he didn't smoke. Secondly, he didn't drink alcohol. That is, absolutely! He didn't touch a drop. Not even for company. Maybe that's why he didn't have company with anyone. Thirdly, he didn't gamble or place money bets on anything. However, he had his weakness. Gupi couldn't live without coffee. And he needed real, freshly brewed coffee, not half-cold slop from a thermos, and certainly not instant powder. Therefore, wherever Gupi went, he always had a bag of ground coffee and an old tin coffee pot with him, which he could put directly on the coals. The mosquito warned Gupi more than once: - Stop this nonsense, old man! It won't lead to anything good for you! When you make coffee for yourself, the smell goes all over the Zone! - So what? - Gupi pretended not to understand what they were talking about. - And the fact that tracking you down by it is a piece of cake! In response to this, Gupi curled his lips in bewilderment. - When you smoke, tobacco smells really good too. - Well, that's tobacco. Have you seen many non-smoking stalkers? And you're the only one who brews coffee over a fire. - And the bloodsucker doesn't care whether it's coffee or tobacco. He'll smell it and come to you. - Am I talking to you about bloodsuckers? - Komar grumbled indignantly. - About whom then? - Gupi raised his eyebrows in surprise. - There is a creature in the Zone that is more terrible than a bloodsucker, - at these words Komar lowered his voice slightly. - It is called a human. And everyone around knows that you are lucky with trinkets like no one else. - I'm not lucky, I just know how to search. - What difference does it make! Watch out, Gupi, you'll run into marauders. Or even some of the younger ones will want your property. “I’ll be careful,” Gupi promised Komaru. And he was, indeed, careful. Damn careful. But the coffee continued to brew. Either out of principle, or he really couldn’t refuse it. After all, without weaknesses, it's not even a person. And the one that Gupi has is not the worst... Standing up, Gupi removed the lid from the coffee pot and stirred the boiling coffee with a spoon. The bitter aroma that escaped from under the lid immediately covered the smell of swamp rot with a metallic taste that was coming from the shore of the lake. Somewhere in the distance a night bird hooted loudly. As if in response to it, the Chernobyl dog howled, drawn out and sad, so that it turned the soul inside out. Gupi smiled contentedly, pulled a thick cook's mitten with a goldfish on it over his hand and took the coffee pot off the fire. The cache where he was going to while away the night was reliable. Almost like the safe where Krys kept his tugriks. Having first discovered this place a couple of years ago, Gupi had been using it regularly since then. The cache was located not far from Lake Yantar, and in such a good place that it was bypassed by the wave of psychotropic radiation that covered the shores of the lake every time the radar started working under its bottom. A nasty thing - a psychotropic radar. Nasty, because it is incomprehensible. It knocks your brains out in no time. Gupi had seen people stupidly stomping along the echoing roofs of cars sunken in the lake to its very center. Why? Who knows! No one ever comes back. That's why even zombies try to avoid these places. To stay away from sin, as they say. The cache that Gupi had once chosen was, in all likelihood, the entrance to a system of military bunkers, the passages between which, as they say, stretch under Yantar. Something must have exploded in this bunker so hard that the walls and ceiling collapsed. If you go deeper into the cache, then after about seven meters you will run into a blockage. A good blockage, a solid one. You can not be afraid that some creature will crawl out from under the ground. Those same burers in the underground communications abandoned by the military are swarming, breeding and multiplying, in full accordance with God's command. And in front of the entrance to the cache, a fire is burning. It is not visible from the outside - Gupi checked - but if someone pokes their nose, a silhouette will immediately light up against the fire. And Gupi has a machine gun with an underbarrel grenade launcher at hand - if you fire at point-blank range, the bloodsucker will come out. The place was also convenient because it was located near a village of eleven yards, of which not even the name remained. But after the ejection of artifacts, it was as crowded as mushrooms in July after rain. True, anomalies also crawled out everywhere, like fly agarics. But that's what the Zone is for, to show teeth. And that's what he was a stalker for, to knock out these teeth. Or to go around - it depends. Gupi usually timed it so that he would end up near this wonderful village just before the ejection. Having waited out the ejection in the cache, he was the first to find himself in the berry place and long before the main group of artifact hunters arrived, he collected all the most expensive and interesting things. And what is it called? Luck? No-o-o! A competent, qualified approach to business - that's what it's called! Luck has nothing to do with it. And having collected the trinkets, Gupi would go to the scientists' camp, which was located at the other end of the lake. Not only did the "nerds" pay well not only for rare trinkets, but also for quite ordinary ones that they urgently needed for experiments, but you could also get useful things from them, the same capsules and containers for artifacts or a software update for the anomaly detector. And even learn something new about why the Zone was so distorted. In fact, the "nerds" themselves did not really understand anything, but they loved to talk with a smart look about things that ordinary people would never understand. Even after a hundred grams. Gupi took a large enamel mug out of his backpack, carefully wiped the inside with a clean handkerchief and only then poured the steaming coffee into it. First, he closed his eyes and carefully inhaled the aroma hovering above the mug. The smell of coffee is a very important component of the pleasure that a true connoisseur gets from this magnificent drink. If coffee had no smell, it would probably be tasteless to drink. Gupi pursed his lips and carefully sipped the scalding hot drink. Divine! Gupi threw his head back, a blissful smile spreading across his face from ear to ear. It was for this smile that one of the green stalkers, who perished on his fourth trip, called him Gwynplaine behind his back. He must have been an educated man. Everyone liked the nickname, especially when the guy explained what it meant. But it turned out to be too long for everyday use. A stalker's nickname should be short, one breath long enough to easily call out. Or one shot long enough to quickly put him down. That's how Gwynplaine became Goopy. However, now this story has also moved into the realm of stalker mythology, and most neophytes sincerely believe that the nickname Goopy has its roots in the name of a fish known to every novice aquarist. Let them think what they want! Gupi himself didn't care. Leaning sideways against an old tractor tire, he slowly and with feeling sipped his wonderful coffee and received colossal pleasure from it. However, it was not only coffee that was the reason for the stalker's blissful state of mind. There were two containers nearby, one of which was loaded with a dozen and a half "singing springs" and two "Chains", each about a meter long. Actually, initially it was one "Chains", but while trying to push it into the container, Gupi carelessly hooked the "Chains" with a stick, and it fell apart in two. It is clear that one long "Chains" is better than two short ones. But nothing can be done about it, "Chains" are like that, they tear easily. In the second container there were two "Dragon Eyes" and a medium-sized doom-mumiyo briquette. And in the backpack, carefully wrapped in a flannel rag, lay a capsule with dead water. It was because of it that Gupi stayed in the village longer than he had planned. Usually he reached the scientists' camp before dark, where he spent the night in a small trailer for technical personnel. But when he came across a bush whose leaves were covered with droplets of dead water, as if with black dew, Gupi realized that he could not leave such a wonderful find to anyone else. He spent a long time carefully and diligently collecting droplets of dead water with an automatic dispenser. Three times Gupi was distracted from this task, which required absolute attention and full concentration, in order to scare away the flesh rustling in the bushes. And already in the evening he had to take up his machine gun, when a zombie came out to him, quite fresh, with a huge bazooka on his shoulder. Judging by the weapon and uniform, it was a soldier from the German corps of the international armed forces guarding the perimeter of the Zone. Fortunately, the former soldier had apparently managed to forget how to use the weapon, which he still carried around with him out of habit. Gupi first broke his legs at the knees with a short burst from a machine gun, and then chopped off his head with a machete. Overall, the day was calm. The release occurred just before dawn, and the bulk of the creatures had not yet managed to get here from the center of the Zone. Tomorrow, and especially the day after tomorrow, the stalkers who come to get what Gupi left behind will have someone to shoot at here. The bazooka that the German zombie was carrying turned out to be damaged. And there were no charges for it. But Gupi dragged it to his cache anyway. Who knows, maybe it will come in handy someday. He decided to sit out the night in the cache. Not to sleep - it is impossible to sleep! - but only maybe take a little nap. And in the early morning, as they say, in the dew, but certainly not barefoot, he could run to the scientists' camp, throw them some of the spillikins and sleep for a few hours. If you leave Yantar at about two o'clock, you can still make it to the Stalker bar before dark. If, of course, nothing happens on the way. And it could very well happen. And anything. This is the Zone, not Disneyland. And not even the Alexey Peshkov Park of Culture and Recreation. The "botanists" were supposed to get what was in the first container - the "Singing Springs" and the "Taper" that had split in two. Gupi was going to dump the "Dragon Eyes" and the doom-mumiyo on Draga, a serious dealer who didn't take everything, but only what went up in price tenfold outside the Zone gates. The "Dragon Eyes" were popular with mystics, occultists, spiritualists and psychics, who considered this artifact a significantly improved version of the classic crystal ball. And the doom-mumiyo was valued for its supposed ability to cure any disease, including the Peruvian trench fever, which had plunged the entire civilized world into fear and trembling a year and a half ago, just like AIDS had once done. The "botanists" from Yantar didn't really say anything about these two trinkets. Although they were willing to buy them. But they paid significantly less than Draghi. That's why they rarely got anything. And Gupi himself believed that if the "Eyes of the Dragon" could predict the future, and Doom-Mumiyo could save from any illness, then stalkers would be the happiest, healthiest and most informed men in the whole wide world. Gupi grinned and took a big sip from his mug of coffee. - Hey, stalker!.. - a long groan came from the lake. - Stalker! Brother!.. Help! What a disaster! Gupi almost cursed. The dead stalker had started his song again! So now he would walk around half the night, howling and moaning like the ghost of Hamlet's father, until he himself disappeared in the lake. - Stalker!.. Quickly, stalker!.. I can't stand it anymore!.. Help, brother! What nasty little people there are! Not only did they kick the bucket themselves, but they also want to drag others down with them!.. Unfinished scum! Gupi spat contemptuously to the side and drank the rest of the coffee in one gulp. Having placed the empty mug on a small flat stone, the stalker reached for the coffee pot standing by the fire to fill it again. And at the moment when he took hold of the hot metal handle, a shadow flashed in the open opening of the cache. Gupi did not even have time to understand who it was, as a solid, elastic blow to the chest threw him against the wall. Fortunately, not far. Falling sideways, Gupi reached for the machine. The coffee spilled into the fire hissed. The light dimmed, and an intoxicating aroma floated under the vaults of the cache. Gupi turned on the under-barrel flashlight. A ray of light, sliding along the stones at the entrance, caught on something grey and shapeless that had not been there before. Without thinking twice, Gupi pulled the trigger. A short, dry burst broke the silence of the night. The burer, who had been hiding among the rocks, jumped to his feet and, comically and terribly raising his short, crooked little arms with spread fingers above his head, rushed at the stalker. Despite their funny appearance, the telekinetic dwarfs were among the most dangerous creatures in the Zone. Mainly because, unlike other creatures, they were intelligent. But their intelligence was of a different order than that of humans - that's what "nerds" said about the burers - perverted to the limit, so much so that it was never possible to understand what was on the burer's mind. The burer who had penetrated the cache spread his arms out to the sides, trying to lift a piece of concrete beam lying in front of him. Usually burers can easily perform such tricks, but for some reason this one couldn’t cope with the block. Having barely risen, the concrete block fell to the ground again. Having squeaked something discontentedly, the burer directed his hands with spread fingers at the stalker. Gupi felt himself pressed against the wall. But not too hard. If he wanted, he could even get up and go towards the burer. But why do that when the stalker had a machine gun in his hands. Gupi took good aim, pulled the trigger and held it until the dry click of the bolt indicated that the clip was empty. Quickly unlatching the empty magazine, Gupi replaced it with a new one, rose to his feet and, keeping his sights on the motionless body of the burer, which resembled a lump of old rags, approached him. Unlike those bloodsuckers, the burers were not particularly resilient. Gupi carefully poked the little freak in the side with the toe of his boot. The dwarf groaned quietly. The fingers of his outstretched hand clenched into a fist. What a number! Burer was still alive! Actually, the most reasonable thing to do in this situation would be to finish off the burer and throw him into the lake. But Gupi suddenly wanted to understand what was going on? Sometimes such a misfortune happens - you want it so much that it's unbearable. As if you can't reach the itchy place. Something strange was happening, indeed. Or, better yet, unusual. To begin with, the burers had never looked into Gupi's cache before. They never showed up on the shore, although they were swarming around in old army bunkers nearby. And this half-dead burer was behaving strangely. Why the hell did he crawl into the light? As if there was nowhere else to hide. Maybe he was sick? Even his first kinetic blow knocked Gupi off his feet only because of the surprise. And then the dwarf couldn't even lift a stone. Hooking his toes under his feet, Gupi turned the burer's body over onto its back. The ugly dwarf stared at him with wide eyes. His lips were moving as if he was trying and unable to say something, only blood was bubbling at the corners of his mouth. In the burer's left temple there was a gaping bullet hole the size of a finger, surrounded by a crust of dried blood. So he was shot before me, Gupi realized. And he really did crawl into the cache to hide. Or… Burer hugged himself with both hands, and tucked his left one under his tattered robe. As if he was trying to hide something. Exactly! Gupi got it. Burer got into the cache to hide something here. No one knows about this place except Gupi, and most of the time it remains empty. What can I say, it's a good place. But what should Burer hide? And who was hunting him? Gupi squatted down next to the dying burer, put his machine gun on his knees and stretched the wound on his head with two fingers. Three hours at most had passed since the bullet hit the burer's forehead. How far could a seriously wounded burer go?.. That's it - his pursuer was somewhere nearby... What nonsense! Who would chase a mortally wounded burer around the Zone? - Come on, show me what you have there? - Gupi tried to move the burer's left hand to the side. But he still tried to resist. Gupi raised his machine gun and, with almost no swing, hit the burer in the temple with the butt. The dwarf twitched his whole body and fell silent. Maybe he's finally dead. Gupi spread the burer's arms to the sides, took out a knife and carefully rummaged through his clothes. Although, what the hell kind of clothes were they - rags, old and torn, God knows where they were found and worn out to the point of "I can't stand it". There were as many rags on the burer as there are leaves on a cabbage. And with each new layer the stench from him became more and more unbearable. Gupi had already decided that enough was enough, let this stinking thing go to hell along with all his stinking secrets. He had to grab him by the legs and drag him out of the cache. When suddenly the tip of the knife hit something hard. - Brother!.. - the dead stalker began his mournful song in the darkness again. - Save me, brother-stalker!.. I'm lost!.. “Yeah, wait, I’m already running,” Gupi muttered discontentedly under his breath. Having ripped open yet another rag that replaced the burer's clothes, Gupi hooked it with a knife and pulled out a PDA from underneath. Not the kind that stalkers wear, but an army one. And it didn't work. The inscription was stuck to the cracked display forever and ever: "18-07/13:32. Swamp 2-12. Stalker Semetsky died. Cause of death: Venom bug bite." Yesterday's news. Gupi turned the PDA over. Made, as expected, in China. Judging by the inventory number, it was the property of the Belgian corps from a unit of international armed forces. Well, now everything is more or less clear. Burers are well-known kleptomaniacs. They steal, like magpies, not what is needed, but what is shiny. Apparently, this one also stole a PDA from a soldier, but the soldier still managed to put a bullet in his head. The burer, scared to death, rushed to hide, and at the same time decided to bury his junk... Could it be that, even after getting a bullet in the forehead, he did not understand that he was dying?.. Gupi turned the PDA over again. A burer with a hole in his head could not have gone far from the scene of the crime. And soldiers do not just wander around the zone. Therefore, it is safe to assume that the PDA belonged to one of the soldiers guarding the scientific camp. There is no other military location nearby. - Stalker!.. Brother!.. I can’t wait any longer!.. - the dead stalker howled again. “Shut up, you bastard,” Gupi muttered discontentedly. He flicked his fingernail across the display. Semetsky died. What a surprise! It's not the first time. But the burer, an unfinished idiot, gave his beastly life for a broken PDA. All PDAs used in the Zone, both stalker and military, have a nice program called "Kamikaze". As soon as the mini-computer loses contact with its owner, "Kamikaze" nimbly, like a samurai sword, chops up all the information on it. How could it be otherwise? Information is sometimes worth more than biryuli. So there was no point in trying to steal something from the built-in data storage device of the PDA stolen by the burer. Instead of information, the only thing you can do is pick up a spy virus, which will immediately transfer all the data from your own mini-computer to someone else's address. Looking at the unlucky, and therefore very dead, burer, Gupi smiled almost sympathetically and threw the PDA into the fire. Having fallen with one edge on the coals, the other PDA hit a stone. A soft click was heard, and the edge of the flash drive appeared from the opened receiving cell. Now this could have been interesting! Gupi quickly snatched the flash drive from its casing, which had begun to warp from the heat of the coals. He turned it over in his fingers. It seemed to be in order. At least, without any visible defects. Having blown on the other flash drive just in case and waved it in the air as if it were hot, Gupi stuck it into the receiver of his PDA and immediately launched the antivirus program. The flash drive turned out to be clean. I mean, uninfected. And there was only one graphic file written on it. Before opening the unknown file, Gupi placed a mug on a stone, picked up the overturned coffee pot and poured out the rest of the coffee. It turned out to be a little less than half a mug. And that's good. Gupi took a sip. The coffee had cooled down. But, unlike other gourmets, Gupi liked both hot and cold coffee. The main thing was that it was brewed properly. And without a single grain of sugar. Then everything was fine. But with sugar, cold coffee was slop. Gupi finished his coffee and put the mug on the stone. Okay, Gupi decided, the file from the flash drive won't go anywhere, but he needs to get rid of the dead burer. Otherwise it stinks as if it's been dead for a week. Gupi rose to his feet, threw his machine gun over his shoulder and, grabbing the burer by the edge of the cloak in which he was wrapped, dragged him towards the exit. Once under the open sky, the first thing Gupi did was listen. The night was quiet. The stars were reflected in the black water of the lake. Somewhere far away, in the direction of the swamp, some creature was hooting loudly. Either it was hungry, or it just didn’t know what to do with itself. Gupi turned on the life-form detector. Within a radius of ten meters around the cache, no one was hiding, waiting for prey. - Brother!.. - was heard again from the shore. - Stalker!.. I can't stand it anymore! Help! Gupi pulled the machine gun out from under his elbow and shone the under-barrel flashlight in the direction where the voice was coming from. A vague, blurry figure stood not far from the water's edge, swaying like a leaf in the wind. If a dead stalker was walking along the shore, it was better not to go there now - he would be sucked into the sewer like an ant. Gupi lowered his machine gun, grabbed the burer's rags again and dragged him away from the shore. He had already checked this road during the day, so there was no risk of running into an anomaly. Actually, it would have been nice to stick the dead burer in the "Zharka" so that the stinking corpse would not attract nasty animals. But the closest "Zharka" marked by Gupi was about a hundred meters away. And a hundred meters in the dark is not a he-he in poppy smoke. You might not get there. Or you might get there, but not return. No, it would be better to let the rats gnaw the burer and the crows peck him to pieces. With an effort, Gupi threw the burer further into the bushes, wiped his hands on the damp grass and went back. - Stalker!.. Buddy!.. Help!.. I lost my own!.. “You’re annoying, the bastard,” Gupi fired a short burst at the dead stalker, and he disappeared. Although, most likely, not for long. Having climbed into the cache, Gupi threw some wood on the fire, poured water from the flask into the coffee pot and put it by the fire to warm up. Pulling the sleeve of his camouflage jacket up to his elbow so that the entire display was visible, Goopy looked thoughtfully at the menu window that had popped up in the lower right corner. Is it even worth looking at what's on the flash drive, a thought flashed through the stalker's mind. There are secrets that are better left unsaid. Although, on the other hand, well, what could a soldier have put on a flash drive, whose mini-computer the burer stole? Most likely, porn... Or family photos... No, ninety-nine percent - porn! Gupi grinned and pointed his finger at the glowing "Open" strip. What appeared on the screen did not look like pornography. It was a plan, in which intersecting lines intertwined into a bizarre pattern. Individual places on the plan were marked with red or green dots. At five nodes, where three or even four lines intersected, there were question marks. Approximately in the center of this entire web there was a black square. At first glance, the picture greatly resembled a children's puzzle in which it was necessary, bypassing dangerous places, to find a way to the center of the labyrinth. The entrances to the labyrinth were marked with numbers - three, eighteen, forty-four, sixty-nine and one hundred and two. Goopy, for fun, tried to follow one of the paths with his gaze, which soon led him to a dead end. The second path was also lost among the red dots, which Goopy regarded as prohibitory signs. But the third, after wandering for some time, led the stalker to a black square. Gupi smiled contentedly, switched off the image and pulled the flash drive out of the receiver. Apparently, the military has nothing better to do if they play around with such nonsense. A stupid toy - it doesn't even have a runner. Damn bourgeois... They want a scented pipifax in the Zone. That's why they're going crazy because they don't know what else to want. They say that a couple of years ago in the West, for some unknown reason, rubber Chinese sneakers suddenly became fashionable, which in Soviet times, almost the entire country wore. Not because they were stylish, but because there were no other sports shoes. And why, one wonders, do they need these sneakers?.. The thing is that none of them has a big, real dream. One for which they wouldn't mind giving their life. And without a dream - what kind of life is that! Twirling the flash drive between his fingers like a prestidigitator would a coin that could disappear at any moment right before the eyes of an astonished audience, Gupi threw it into the fire. The dead stalker, who had risen from oblivion, howled again by the lake. Only now he started singing a different song to the same tune. - Buddy!.. Ah, buddy!.. Give me some water to drink!.. I'm dying, I'm so thirsty!.. Buddy!.. I have two more wounded here!.. Help me, brother stalker!.. - Go to hell! - Gupi couldn't help but bark back. - You piss-stained stump!

A tall, stocky man, dressed in worn jeans and a black leather jacket, appeared on the outskirts of the settlement N shortly before the curfew. It was early autumn outside, weightless cobwebs were floating in the clear, chilly air, and the stranger's sunglasses, hiding his facial features, looked somewhat out of place. However, he did not linger long on the street, making the short journey from the bus stop to the roadside cafe with an energetic, sweeping stride, not arousing the interest of the rare passers-by, who were also hurrying about their business. Seeing a military patrol appear at the nearest crossroads, he quickly climbed the steps of a shabby porch, above which a faded sign with an illegible name was hanging. Entering, he looked around, went to the bar, waited patiently for a minute until the bartender deigned to come out of the back room, then exchanged a few words with him, ordered a beer and, having immediately paid, headed to the only occupied table at which two stalkers were having dinner. The stranger's unerring conclusion about their occupation was based on several simple observations. First, despite the empty hall, these two occupied the farthest table - in such a way that there was a blank wall behind them, and the display windows and the entrance to the cafe were constantly in sight. Second, their sparse gestures and attentive, tenacious glances betrayed their constant internal readiness for immediate action, indicating that they spent most of their time among the deadly dangerous spaces of the Exclusion Zone. "Lone stalkers," the stranger mentally corrected himself, deliberately crossing the room slowly. An interesting type. Outwardly, they are the complete opposite of each other. One has a deep scar across his cheek. His dark hair is combed back, his nose is straight, his eyes are brown, his features are rough, his chin is massive. It is clear that in business he often works on reflexes, completely trusting his instincts. The second stalker is very different from his comrade, at least in appearance. Short light hair, eyes with a cold, gray-greenish tint of the pupils, facial features are thin, aristocratic, shaven surprisingly cleanly, in battle he is probably a leader, calculating the situation one move ahead. “Aren’t you in the way?” The stranger stopped, took off his dark glasses, which should have been regarded as a gesture of goodwill, and, without waiting for an invitation, moved his chair, settling himself to the side of the table so as not to block the view. - Didn't get anything mixed up? - One of the stalkers, the one with the scar, looked at the stranger sullenly and without interest. - I need a guide. “We don’t take tourists,” the blond man said calmly but categorically. - I'm not a tourist. I haven't been to the Zone for two years. I decided to go back, but they say everything has changed a lot here. The stalkers looked at each other silently, then one of them took his PDA out of the inside pocket of his jacket. - Speak. “Corkscrew,” the stranger introduced himself, perfectly understanding what was being discussed. His identity could only be verified through the local stalker network, and if the result was positive, all questions to him were automatically removed – he could count on help, within reasonable limits and for a reasonable fee, of course. The miniature display lit up dimly. - A corkscrew, huh? - The fair-haired stalker carefully compared the archive photo he had received from the Internet with the original sitting next to him, while his face showed no emotion. - When was the last time you were in the Zone? - In July of the tenth year. - There are rumors that at that time a group of stalkers were looking for a path leading to Pripyat, bypassing the Radar. Is that true? Corkscrew nodded. - Who else went with you? - Broker, Karas and Globus. They died. The stalker entered a request, checked the received data, then turned off the PDA and said: - Let's consider that we have met. I am Monk. “Huron,” the second one introduced himself without much enthusiasm. - So what? - Shtopor leaned forward slightly. - Will you take me to the Cordon? “We were going to leave in an hour.” The monk clasped his fingers together, glanced sideways at Huron, caught the doubt in his answering gaze and added: “You won’t have time to get equipped.” “No problem,” answered Shtopor. “I have a hiding place on the Cordon.” - You haven't been to the Zone for a long time, - the monk grinned. - The perimeter used to be like a sieve. Now it's tightly closed. You won't get through without special equipment. - He thought for a second, then nodded: - Okay. We have a third set of equipment. But I'll warn you right away: we're going without weapons, we'll pass the military line quietly, in the place I've indicated. No shooting. You'll have to obey completely until we get to the Cordon, and ten thousand for the set of special equipment. Corkscrew nodded, almost without thinking. It seemed that money was not of much interest to him at the moment. “Why without a weapon?” he tensed up. - Less risk. We have a rule: if the military takes you, don't fight back, pretend to be "tourists". They'll hold you for a day and then let you go. And if they find a gun on you, they'll kill you right there. - Seriously? Is everything so cool now? - Shtopor was genuinely surprised. - Six months ago, they introduced a strict blockade of the alienated territories, - explained Inok. - The groups have their own corridors, and free stalkers have a hard time. - So, without weapons... - Shtopor looked puzzled. - And what about in the Zone? Will we run into mutants? Or bandits? "We have hiding places just beyond the border," the monk reassured him. "We'll share with you from our personal reserves. We won't leave you naked." “Out of respect for your elders?” Shtopor narrowed his eyes. “In memory of the dead,” Huron, who had not said a word up until that moment, suddenly became gloomy. The monk glanced at his comrade in surprise, but did not comment on his remark. He just made a mental note to ask him, if necessary, why such a sudden reaction. - If you agree to the terms, then let's go, - he said, turning to Shkorpor. - We can't linger, it will soon be dark.

October 18. …Around midnight, we – a group of scientists captured or recruited at different times and in different parts of the world – were brought to a certain transit point. The journey was long. Everyone is treated the same. Both those who work for money and those who are intimidated, whose families are still hostages. I am afraid. The veneer of civility was too thin. First, we, each one of us, were brought to an animal state by constant threats, public torture and abuse. Some were unable to bear the physical pain, others, who at first were brave, talking about their own importance in the scientific world, about the special services that would certainly rescue them, broke down and lost hope. In Central Africa, at the temporary staging area where we first saw each other, many of us were revived. After a long period of isolation, everyone felt much better: we, broken individually, dreamed that together we could figure out a way to escape, or at least to make known the dire situation in which we found ourselves. The camp is guarded by mercenaries. They are completely unsociable, but are subject to strict discipline. For any violation - death. I look at them and think: our governments could pursue a different policy. Instead of leading the world to collapse and chaos, spending billions on political intrigues and obvious adventures, they should take care of the general level of well-being and education of the peoples inhabiting the Earth. After what was done to me, such thoughts do not seem naive. The senseless abuse I was subjected to not only destroyed my will, but also erased many illusions. We are doomed. The world is doomed. I do not know what goals the non-humans who captured us have set for themselves, but they feel like absolute masters of the situation. Using the technical achievements of civilization, they do not give a damn about any of the laws of the civilized world. I am afraid. Brute, animal force breaks the thin partitions of consciousness, destroys illusions, giving rise to a persistent feeling of hopelessness. I ask myself: why and how did this become possible? The answer is simple and lies on the surface: half a century of double standards and outright duplicity have created the illusion of impunity among many groups operating under the wing of governments in a number of countries. We ourselves have nurtured a new strain of plague. A moral plague. This is especially clear from here – from the very bottom of the abyss where we had to fall. October 25th. I keep a diary. My past horror is nothing compared to what I just experienced. Our escape plan was exposed. It turned out that there were provocateurs among us. Everyone was driven out of the barracks, lined up on a dusty parade ground, then the completely brutalized mercenaries, on the orders of a pale, thin "gentleman" of European appearance, drove every fifth one out of the line. They were shot before our eyes. My hands are shaking. I can barely hold the pencil stub in my fingers. They were killed without any pity, brutally, with pleasure. The bodies were left lying in the dust, a minute later flies were already swarming over them. I can no longer bear the constant moral pressure, the debilitating fear. The only form of protest I am capable of is these notes. If they are found, I will certainly be killed. It is terrifying, sometimes I am ashamed of myself. I do not have the strength to die with dignity, throwing myself at the guard - the thirst to live that torments my mind is too great. I do not understand why they needed a geneticist? I have dedicated my entire life to the development of genetically modified plants, and have only just begun experiments on animals, instilling in representatives of rare, endangered species some qualities that are not inherent to them, which should help them survive in the conditions of the rapidly changing biosphere of the Earth. My resistance is finally broken. It's been a year now since the continuous nightmare has continued. There is no more hope. November 14th (approximately) We are being taken somewhere. Transported in the dirty hold of an old, dying ship. The conditions are inhumane. The constant pitching is driving you crazy. Continuous torment brings to mind self-torture. How much deprivation can a person endure? Where is the line beyond which fear will disappear, and the hope for mythical salvation will cease to glimmer? Among those locked in the cesspool-like hold, I don't see many familiar faces. Gradually, I come to understand: there, in the camp, we were filtered. Apparently, the number of specialists from different fields of knowledge was initially excessive. In the process of selection, the strongest in spirit were eliminated, as a lesson to the weak. A win-win tactic. No one is thinking about resistance any more. Many have fallen into complete depression. It would seem that if our tormentors are going to use the captives to conduct scientific research or experiments, they cannot bring us to a state of complete apathy, indifference to our own fate. I'm afraid that upon arrival we will be given illegal drugs. Just not drugs. December. Our suffering is over. The ship reached its destination. Where we were and where they would take us next was a complete mystery. Fire hoses were lowered into the hold, we were stripped naked, our clothes were taken away and turned into rags, we were thrown bars of soap and the water was turned on, and we were ordered to tidy ourselves up. After the wash, several mercenaries acted as barbers. They shaved our heads, again treating us like animals. I am gradually beginning to get used to the state of existence. My entire past life has turned into a dream, it is the delirium of an inflamed consciousness. Bundles of clothes were waiting for us on deck. Everyone chose their own things. A piercing, cold wind was blowing, so no one had to be urged. The clothes are clean, worn, but good quality. I tried to count how many of us were left. It turned out that there were about a hundred people. Consciousness, sobered by the icy wind, began to work again, emerging from a state of fatal slumber. I try to talk to others, to find out at least something. The information is very scanty and absolutely joyless. There is a persistent rumor that we were brought to one of the countries of the former Soviet Union. I have heard the opinion expressed several times that our captivity and all the tortures we endured were the machinations of the KGB. The latter seemed dubious to me. Each of us indulges in the illusion of our own genius, but if you look at it sensibly and impartially, we are no more than middling specialists. There are no geniuses among us. Good performers with a high level of highly specialized training, capable of picking up and successfully developing a certain idea. Nothing more. Considering the scientific potential scattered by the whirlwind of perestroika and the hard times of the nineties throughout the post-Soviet space, I come to the conclusion that the local special services definitely do not need us. It's scary to think, but the only advantage of all countries in the region without exception is almost complete anarchy. It's easy to hide traces of any adventure here. Soon it will be the new year 2000. The millennium is coming. Is the predicted end of the world really coming? December 25th (exactly). They put us on buses. The mercenaries do not hide their weapons. The road, initially paved, now resembles a country road. We silently look out the windows. Behind them are dull, gray landscapes. Winter without snow. Wretched villages float by. For a while I fell asleep. I woke up from the feeling of shaking. Outside the bus windows in the twilight I could see a terrible, chilling, unreal landscape: a dead city abandoned by people. Our column was slowly moving along one of the streets. My knowledge of Russian was enough to make out the letters on one of the signs. Street of Heroes of Stalingrad. Rare snowflakes are falling from the sky, slowly circling. The mud has frozen, turning into potholes. I look around and see signs of life that has suddenly stopped everywhere, as if someone is projecting similar black-and-white photographs onto the window: gloomy houses with the lights out, street lamps that don’t work, cars with flat tires that are already pretty rusty, shop windows with half-empty shelves with the remains of goods visible in the light of bus headlights, playgrounds covered in snow, swings creaking in the wind, a doll forgotten by someone in the sandbox… The bus climbs a hill. The outlines of some fundamental buildings are dimly visible on the horizon. My neighbor, a cybernetics specialist, suddenly leans over and whispers in my ear: - This is the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant. I visited it before the accident. We are now moving through Pripyat - a dead, abandoned city of power engineers. His whisper burns. Buses suddenly turn and suddenly... We are driving along an inclined ramp leading underground. A sticky horror grips me. Timelessness… I don't know how many months or years have passed since we were brought to the old, Soviet-era underground missile complex. The bunker zone, stretching for many kilometers, has become a prison for the mind and body, but I have already lost track of time and have no more hope for anything. My hopes of rescue or escape faded as the meaning of the experiments being conducted here became clear. Those who captured us and keep us underground hate Humanity, despise the civilization that nurtured them. They are preparing the apocalypse. The types of weapons developed here surpass the most delirious and fantastic ideas. We are truly used as diligent craftsmen. Each is given a narrow front of work. Now I know that I am developing other people's ideas, research, stopped somewhere on the edge of a foul. Genetic changes introduced by viruses created in laboratories will spare no one. They serve for the total destruction of the enemy, but before they die, people and some types of domestic animals, for which strains of genetic viruses are designed, will be endowed with extraordinary abilities and will serve as weapons. The world will perish or change beyond recognition. Take dogs, for example. Here, in the dungeons of the bunker zone, two types of viruses have already been brought to the stage of combat readiness, changing the sensory system of "man's friends", endowing dogs with extraordinary vitality and hypertrophied rage. Due to the changes introduced into the brain structure, pseudo-dogs exhibit the ability to mentally influence a lower subspecies - the so-called blind dogs. They can control them. I'm not afraid anymore. I became an accomplice not just to a crime, but to the preparation of a mass genocide of humanity. I don't understand what those behind the experiments are counting on. Are they really so stupid or insane that they expect to survive? In other labs, I saw terrible creatures created on the basis of people. The most terrible creation of the experiments are the controllers. They are able to influence the psyche of other mutants, gathering them into combat groups. This is MADNESS in its most horrific and cynical form. Timelessness. Recently I was receiving reagents from the warehouse. It turns out that a few kilometers from the biological laboratories there is a sector for physicists. There I met an old man. He seemed vaguely familiar to me, and when we exchanged a few phrases, I recognized with horror a young and undoubtedly talented scientist whom I had met in that past life at one of the conferences devoted to the problems of synergetics and the formation of related branches of knowledge existing at the junction of various sciences. He did not seem to remember me. He has no personality left. Only the eerie fanaticism of a researcher. He speaks indistinctly, often and inappropriately sprinkles incomprehensible terms. It is surprising that I had the courage and patience to communicate with him. The most terrifying thing is that I managed to understand that he has almost completed work on the installation that suppresses the human personality. Only primitive reflexes remain. He claims that with the help of another device, which is being developed by "subcontractors", enemy soldiers whose minds will be suppressed can be controlled - however, for now within a radius of ten kilometers, no more. They become obedient, but not to the will of a person or a controller. They fall under the influence of a second emitter, controlled by a cybernetic system. Timelessness… I am not afraid of anything anymore. My life is over. I am morally dead, and physical suffering does not frighten me anymore. I remember the dead city we once drove through. Will the entire Earth really turn into a huge cemetery where mutants and undead controlled by cybernetic complexes will reign? I suffered, I was afraid, I clung to life, I squirmed like a worm. Now I am dead. My last wish is to do something to prevent the coming catastrophe. Until all the research is completed, until the strains of genetic viruses are brought to such a degree of stability that they can be put into use, counting on a 100% result, until the last tests of the Burner and its mysterious component that controls the behavior of people who have lost their own minds have passed, I must do something. I was unable to live with dignity, but I finally found the strength to die. I'm releasing all the pseudo-dogs and blind dogs in the lab. Maybe I can get to the neighboring sector and free some controllers. I heard that the exclusion zone around the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant is controlled by the isolation forces of one of the countries. I hope they are not in cahoots with the mercenaries guarding us and their owners. Dogs have broken out of their cages before - at first no one will be particularly alarmed, and when the mercenaries understand the scale of what happened, it will be too late. I hope...

-Would you like some coffee? -Have you fixed the machine yet? - The girl was clearly flirting with the guy, a smile that looked more like a smirk appeared on her face. The young man opposite was smiling with his whole face, and with his hand he invited her to follow him. They both walked along a white corridor, in which every now and then there were branches, tightly closed doors. Some of them had not been opened for several years, while others had a brand new seal. Pipes ran along the walls. They beautifully described the arc of doorways, some instrument racks and generators. Some went somewhere into the floor, others into the ceiling. Representatives of the station were often encountered - people in respirators and white coats. They were constantly fussing.
