Apparently, the work was stressful and that precious minute, which they went out to drink coffee or smoke in a special room, was comparable to torment. People dependent on work, people living at work. The young people themselves were also dressed in robes, on which badges were proudly displayed: "Senior Assistant to the Chief of the Third Control Panel. Strizhenova Lyudmila Aleksandrovna" and "Security Officer Ustenko Dmitry Vladimirovich". -People, what do you like? - Let's have a cappuccino instead. Damn, I'm so tired! - Luxurious black curls were gathered in a bun. The girl herself was at that age when they talk about the last chance to get married. But at a fleeting meeting, it was easy to guess her age, a working girl who recently graduated from college. First impressions often fail. -Yes, everything is fine, it’s Thursday already. -And the number? -The twelfth. Soon we will have a professional "holiday". It's April, after all... - Yes, it's getting worse... Twenty-two years ago, life here stopped. As if someone just turned off the lights, strange. You know, sometimes I think that humanity itself did it. Deliberately, laughing at nature, and now we just don't care what we've done. All we do is sit and try to do something we don't know what. And who are we laughing at? At ourselves. -You are telling the truth. After all, it is not for us to decide all this, much less judge. There are big guys in offices, they sit on luxurious leather-covered chairs. They sit and do nothing but decide how we should live. And there, too - Dima raised his index finger to the ceiling - there are people who manage everything. To build "templates" by which we live, and to make decisions about our future life. Man has nothing to do with it... -Since when did you become a believer? -When there is little left, the way out is faith. - Interesting, by the way, today we have a scheduled inspection, the management is coming. It seems like someone said that we are violating safety standards. So they are coming to "pat us on the head". Thank God, I am only an assistant. -Senior assistant. - Dmitry took Ludina's "badge" and turned it over in his hands with a smile. - Oh, come on! They should harness me now. Give them tours of the station, and look into their mouths fearfully, like, what do they want to ask... It's sickening. - Yes, you are all informers there. Well, look at them, Lyud! Seriously, quit your job, damn it. What are we doing at this station? We have been trying to get something going since 1986. And what is the result? Only the first power unit is running, supplying us with electricity, and the security system of the other “units”. As they say, we only dreamed of production capacity. It is sad to realize that with two higher educations, good nuclear specialists and physicists are rotting. In the natural sense of the word... And the management decided to increase the “permissible radiation level”. They have to stuff us with antibiotics, or whatever they call it?! The point is that they think it is cheaper than upgrading the equipment, the sarcophagus, and properly reburying the fuel, reprocessing it. The “UN” has been promising to provide funds for this for ten years now... Because of these “standards”, more and more people are dying from radiation. Look, Vovochka got cancer... The whole shift buried him last week. - Dim, well, it's not because of radiation. Yes, the background is growing, but everything is within the norm. Of course, our peculiar norm, but still. We all go through the procedures. There's something different here. This place is cursed. In general, there shouldn't be a station here, remember? And the old legends about the black forest. Well, they told us about them as children. -Yes, yes. How can I forget that? This place is cursed, you're right. Why don't you just leave? And then you see, I'll move over, to you... closer. - Dima smiled and leaned towards the girl. They kissed tenderly, but then timidly stopped. -Dim. I told you many times already. My family is buried here, where would I go? Everyone died in Pripyat. When the crush at the buses started. That's it... not another word about it. Hug me. Two people, kindred spirits, stood hugging each other near the wall not far from the coffee machine. They felt what is usually called love, but they were afraid of it. Work, responsible work for both of them, warned the mind, but the heart took over every now and then. Only a sharp push cut short the sweet moments of their meeting, which they so often lacked. -All personnel, take your posts! - the siren was piercing. - This is not a drill! All personnel, take your posts! - What's this? - Lyudmila was scared, Dima was just surprised, military training and many years of experience did not allow him to panic. He helped Lyudmila get up from the floor. Having shaken off his girlfriend, Ustenko took her hands and looked into her eyes. He probably wanted to make sure that everything was okay, or maybe he was just calming her down. - No idea. Quickly, to your posts! Lyuda! Pull yourself together! - Several people ran along the corridor. One of them was holding his nose, blood was coming out of it. The second one was constantly switching from running to walking, and from walking to running. After all, the station personnel had not passed a single sports standard. They didn’t even really know how to shoot, although the regulations had several points devoted to this. Everything depended on funding and the money that Kyiv gave out, it was barely enough to publish a book on safety rules at the station, for the personnel, and even for a couple of seminars a year. Personnel retraining and advanced training courses were generally rare. The station itself worked only on its own, internal resources. It was not profitable for someone to bury it and everything connected with it. This place was chewed over, tormented and studied. Intently. Dissecting every centimeter of land, air. Greedily absorbing the knowledge received, at the cost of the lives of ordinary people... workers. Lyudmila nodded to Dmitry in fear, and, gathering the rest of her thoughts, ran forward along the corridor... Dima "flashed" a few meters behind her and went out onto the landing, went down a couple of floors and ran along another corridor, he found himself in a room that looked like a computer lab. On the far wall there were about seven LCD monitors. Some showed important readings from devices, others showed videos from surveillance cameras. Along with this, there were about ten more people in the room, who were already in place and waiting for their "bosses". -What do we have? - Dmitry was all ears. His question was not directed at anyone in particular, but at everyone in the room. Today he was running the show. - Dmitry Vladimirovich. Tremors were recorded, four and a half points. The background level increased twofold in a minute and continues to grow. - Damage to the sarcophagus body? - Not recorded! What was that? - The professional incompetence of the personnel was evident in the everyday life of the station. Somewhere they would forget to salute, because the station is under the jurisdiction of the military. Somewhere they would address by name, to a senior in rank, not according to the regulations. Even the usual order of dialogue "superior - subordinate" was violated, almost every minute. The practical knowledge of the rank and file also left much to be desired. In Kyiv, they had long ago gotten used to this "garbage dump" that needed to be fed and did not particularly worry about the training of the personnel. They let a couple of scientists into the station, so let them teach the people. - I don't know myself. - Dima, standing near his computer, ran diagnostics of some systems. A positive result allowed him to breathe easy. Only the aftershocks interrupted the momentary relief. Everyone fell to the floor. Things fell from the tables, and plaster rained down from the ceiling on people's heads. One of the monitors on the wall tilted and, falling, caught three more. The screeching of metal, plastic and screams were heard. - The level has increased eightfold. - A man of about thirty, who had already managed to approach the control panels after the tremors had ended, concluded with excitement. He was gray-haired, but it seemed that the gray hair had managed to form during the tremors. It was scary. "-To all employees" - Turn off the alarm! Everyone is already at their posts. - Ustenko began to lose his temper. - Sashka and Marina. Diagnostics of the entire station from the consoles. Go through the cameras. Sergey, contact Kiev. We have an accident... A bustling scurry began in the room, no one was standing idle, and the instrument readings continued to grow. Dmitry was not exactly surprised. He was in a slight shock, which was not allowed given his position. The tremors, especially in Chernobyl, were the first in the entire history of his work. And most likely in the entire history of the Chernobyl region. -Volodya, sarcophagus monitoring. Constant! Just try to get distracted, I'll strangle you right here. Okay. We're lowering the rods on the first one, what's wrong with you? You were supposed to lower them two hours ago according to the plan! Vit, signal to the third control panel. Let them lower the rods of the first power unit. Urgently! - Yes sir! - The boy leaned over and with a new breath began his message addressed to the third control panel. The work of these people, just like Dmitry's, forced them to be cold-blooded at any moment. But apparently they hadn't had time to prepare them for all the wonders of nature. - Dmitry Vladimirovich. The order is denied. They're panicking there, I think. -What's going on?! Lower the reactor rods right now! We'll blow ourselves to hell, quickly cool them down. Quickly! Sergey, where's the connection with Kiev? -The phones don't work! -The first monitor to the third remote control! Quickly! - Yes sir. - A picture appeared on one of the surviving monitors. Several people lying on the floor were barely moving. The rest were standing right under the camera and actively waving their hands, shouting something. After a few seconds, all four stopped waving and turned their heads to the right. A strange light illuminated their faces. Dmitry only managed to see how Lyuba turned to the camera and said something before the flash disabled the camera. -Lyuba? - he said barely audibly. - Where is the image? Quickly return me the image of the third remote control! - In response, he received only uncomprehending looks from his colleagues. -Dmitry Vladimirovich! The sarcophagus is completely hermetic. -Radiation level? - Dmitry was laconic. He needed to pull himself together. -Ten times higher than the permissible limit. - What the hell?! - Ustenko rushed to one of the tables, - He has to work, he has to! - he picked up the red phone and waited for an answer..

It is not for nothing that the cordon is considered the Zone's calling card, which it brazenly pokes under the nose of every green stalker who crosses the perimeter. Like, this is what the Zone is like, and here is what it can offer you to get acquainted with from its samples. Be careful: right in front of you is an insidious anomaly. (Don't see it? Believe me, it's definitely there. And if you don't believe me, then check it out. Or are you too weak?) In the nearest hollow there are a couple of not very valuable artifacts, and over there, near the abandoned farm, there are evil, stupid and eternally hungry mutants. But this is far from the entire assortment of the Zone. Do you want to get acquainted with it in more detail? No problem, let's move on. And if you've lost your desire, get lost while you still have the chance. However, if you accept the offer, you will certainly be satisfied. A full range of extreme entertainment awaits you as soon as you pass the last checkpoint and, having left the Cordon, find yourself in real "business". From this moment on, it will be much more difficult to break the contract, and as a penalty under it, the Zone may demand your life. These are the rules. No one invited you here, but since you have come, please obey... Major Konstantin Kupriyanov, aka the Department's operative intruder, working under the code name Kalter, had been beaten by the bitch of life more than once, and, having glanced at the Zone's "business card", he immediately understood what a vile creature he would have to deal with. However, unlike other adventurers who signed a contract with it on a voluntary basis, the major had no choice. In this deal, he was merely an executor of orders from his command. And it did not give Kalter the right to reject cooperation with the Zone based on personal hostility towards it. And so the intruder, having examined the Cordon through binoculars from the first hill he came across, only gritted his teeth in annoyance and headed southwest, to the meeting place with the informant. Heavy grey clouds were crawling over Kalter's head so low that it seemed he could reach out and touch them. The eternally gloomy, leaden sky of the Zone resembled an additional sarcophagus, erected by the mighty forces of nature on top of the main one, over the Fourth Reactor of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant and the surrounding area. Mother Nature clearly did not trust her careless child, the builder of the first Sarcophagus, a man, in this matter, so she played it safe. And how could she trust him, since it was none other than a man who built a nuclear power plant in these places and allowed one of its reactors to explode a quarter of a century ago. The consequences of that explosion still excite not only Europe, but the entire world, which is closely watching what is happening in the Zone. Somewhere behind the clouds the sun was hiding – a welcome but rare guest in these parts. It definitely wouldn’t appear today. Quite the contrary, it would probably start raining by midday. Dry grass and yellowing leaves on the trees, ready to fall at any moment, rustled in the wind, and for some reason Kalter couldn’t believe that they had once been green. The major would rather believe that the local flora was already growing dry and withered, since he couldn’t imagine how buds swelled on the trees in the Zone, and how emerald young grass grew from the ground. If anything was born in abundance on this damned radioactive land, it was only anomalies. And who even thought of assigning them this term? Flowers, lush meadows, ordinary animals and birds, not those mutated into monsters – they are the ones that are anomalous for the Zone. And deadly dangerous centers of paranormal energy are the norm by local standards. Just like the total desolation, destruction and the “constantly changeable”, cold wind, sometimes changing its direction several times a minute. The major did not want to admit it to himself, but he was afraid of the local wind, because it was not just unfamiliar - it was alien. Even the dry and hot winds of the Middle East and Central Asia, where Kalter often had to go on duty, were much more familiar to him than the air element that was now breathing in the intruder's face. And if it were only a matter of the specific, mostly alarming smells of the Zone. No, in reality everything was much more complicated. Kalter could not explain what caused this “anti-wind” reaction of rejection in him. Probably, along with the usual smells in the local air, there were fluids of those very anomalies and mutants who were preparing to embrace the intruder who had invaded here in their deadly embrace. A disgusting cocktail of terrible aromas: the constant fear of death, hatred, uncertainty and general decay. The Zone presented Kalter with a dirty, rusty cup filled to the brim with this foul-smelling drink, and the major had to drain it to the bottom. What can you do – fate, and the major had no power over it. The mighty Department decided everything for him, and if it ordered Kalter to “Drink!”, he choked, but drank the swill presented to him to the last drop. So it was and so it is. And whether it will be in the future, no one knows. We will live to find out… A quarter of a century ago, on the site of the southern sector of today's Cordon, through which Kalter entered the Zone, there was a Soviet collective farm, and, judging by everything, a rather rich one. Vast lands overgrown with grass long ago; half-ruined farms, dryers, grain threshing floors, machine shops and garages; abandoned, rusted-through equipment everywhere... Having walked a couple of kilometers through the copses, the major even came across a small factory, the purpose of which was no longer possible to determine today. At first, Kalter wanted to turn there to climb onto the roof of a two-story workshop and study the surroundings better, but, having come closer, he discovered that the factory was inhabited. A dozen armed guys in worn leather jackets had settled down there for a rest or were guarding some valuable cargo. But clearly not the one that interested the major who had invaded the Zone. The intruder hid in the bushes and, turning on a directional microphone, aimed it at the company hiding behind the peeling walls. But he soon stopped reconnaissance and, going around the factory, continued on his way. A short radio listening was enough for Kalter to understand who he had stumbled upon. The specific jargon that the bandit gangs operating in the Zone spoke made it possible to unmask these people even in the dark - also, so to speak, a calling card of one of the local pack predators. To get to the village where the informant wanted by Kalter lived, the major had to cross the only narrow asphalt road in the area, along which army patrols periodically traveled. Unlike the bandits, the intruder could do this openly - the military observer documents issued to him were genuine. However, by showing himself to the soldiers of the local peacekeeping contingent ahead of time, Kalter could attract the attention of people whom the Strategist had marked him as "obstacles". Which would force them to be wary and postpone the illegal arms deal planned somewhere in the Zone. Therefore, the intruder decided to cross the road the way he had been taught as a child: before stepping onto the roadway, carefully look left and right to avoid excesses. However, in these places they could lie in wait for a pedestrian even on a completely deserted road. Kalter had to move across open terrain for the last hundred meters to the obstacle, and when he had already sneaked half of that distance, the damned patrol suddenly appeared on the road. The soldiers were leisurely moving in the direction of the second, internal checkpoint, which was located a kilometer to the north, not far from the collapsed railway bridge that had once spanned the same road. The soldiers were accompanied by three civilians in protective windbreakers with hoods and simple respirators pulled over their faces. A mixed team of fledgling stalkers and army men might have seemed strange to the intruder, if he had not been informed that such things were the order of the day in the Cordon. Almost every inhabitant of the Zone, be he a hardened bandit or an ordinary first-year soldier, had his own small business here. Even military stalkers, career army special forces fighters, spat on instructions and quietly traded in the artifacts they had collected. Well, the simple motorized infantry, which was forbidden to go deep into the infected territories, earned their demobilization, as they say, without leaving the barracks. For example, by escorting newly minted stalkers through the Cordon, so that they would not run into bandits or mutants prematurely, and at the same time giving the greenhorns a short briefing on how to behave in the Zone. The intruder, approaching the road, came across exactly such a group. There was still quite a decent distance between him and the patrol, but the major preferred not to take any risks. Noticing unwanted witnesses, he darted to the first shelter he found: a rusty Belarus tractor standing in a ditch, its tires long since rotted and turned into rags. There could well have been an anomaly underneath it, but fortunately, Kalter was lucky, and even the radiation from the tractor was not too strong. Having darted under the tractor's bottom, the major hid behind the massive hub of the rear wheel and practically became part of the pile of iron junk hanging over him. Fortunately, the color scheme of the major's camouflage overalls was developed by the Department's laboratory specifically for the conditions of the Zone and allowed him to hide unnoticed at garbage dumps, in a field, or on bare rocks. Moreover, the intruder had been playing such hide-and-seek for two decades, and the fact that he was still alive was direct evidence of his ability to deceive the "drivers". And sometimes, the same masters of their craft as Kalter himself played their role. Just look at the Pakistani "black storks" that the major encountered last year near Peshawar... The major had been in the Zone for a few hours and was just getting the hang of the rules of life there. "Take your time and look around!" one of them said. A veteran of the special department "Misanthrope" known in the Department, field operative Kalter had been in places no less disgusting. However, there, at least, he had been confronted by ordinary, not "anomalous" enemies, whose behavior was generally predictable. Here, it was impossible to predict even your next move, not to mention other local quirks. Therefore, Kalter tried not to fuss, even though he was pressed for time. The irrational rules of the Zone required the major to thoughtfully comprehend them, since, sacredly observing secrecy, the Department had not provided a guide for him. It assumed that the "misanthrope" would figure everything out on his own. But even after walking around the Cordon for a short time, Kalter began to doubt it. But what could one do – an order was an order, and this rule prevailed over those that the intruder had yet to comprehend. So far, the only enemy he had to fight to the death was an overly aggressive fauna. Like any of the "misanthropes", the major preferred to study an unfamiliar enemy in battle, although Kalter naturally did not get into a fight unless absolutely necessary. The intruder did not intend to fight mutants, but sometimes the sensitive creatures themselves revealed his disguise and wanted to get to know him better. The easiest thing to get rid of was a pack of blind dogs that Kalter encountered right at the outer barbed wire fence. Half a dozen ugly beasts jumped to the side as soon as they heard the click of a rifle bolt being cocked. The major didn't even have to shoot. The train of thought of the eyeless dogs worked in the right direction - one of the stalkers clearly taught this pack of mangy predators some sense, once again confirming Grandfather Pavlov's postulate about conditioned reflexes. However, this short warm-up ended, giving way to an endless tournament, where the main and only prize for Kalter was his own life, valued by the Department, to tell the truth, much more highly than by the “misanthrope” himself. The fat, barrel-shaped creature on long, thin legs, like those of a giant grasshopper, nicknamed pseudo-flesh by the stalkers, was not going to feast on the major, but he had to shoot it anyway. Something or someone had startled the mutant, and it was galloping straight toward the intruder hiding in the tall grass. And if he had not laid the monster out with his compact VMK-3.0, equipped with an integrated silencer and a multifunctional underbarrel rocket launcher, the pseudo-flesh would have flown into Kalter, breaking his ribs and trampling his kidneys. And with the pseudo-dogs that were hanging around the factory occupied by bandits and smelled a man sneaking in the bushes, there was an embarrassment that almost ended badly for the major. Without a second thought, he decided to get rid of the pseudo-dogs with the help of "Paniker" - a special repellent developed by the Department's laboratory for the quick and effective scaring off of predatory animals. Kalter did not check whether these ferocious, fanged and, unlike the first pack he encountered, big-eyed creatures would be scared by the clicking of the bolt. Just as the intruder initially refused to shoot at them. Fire from an automatic rifle, even with a good silencer, near the bandit camp would certainly reach the ears of the two-legged predators with small arms. Seeing the running pseudo-dog pack turn around and head towards him, Kalter grabbed the necessary express sprayer from his unloading vest and "sneezed" from it towards the approaching beast. After which he raised his "VMK" as a warning - what if the chemicals the major had taken on his business trip didn't work? It was as if he had seen into the future. At first, everything seemed to be going smoothly. Having sensed the Panic-monger, which was elusive to the human nose, the pseudo-dogs shied away and darted around the area. “Excellent,” the major mentally summed up the field testing of the experimental repellent. “At least one of our ‘bigheads’ new developments has paid off the budget money spent on it.” But before Kalter could put the sprayer back into the "unloading" vest, the pseudo-dogs had once again formed a friendly pack and rushed towards the man who was already celebrating his victory. If it had not been for the tree lying nearby, uprooted from the ground, the major would hardly have emerged from the fight with the enraged pack of four-legged mutants without a single scratch. Running away, he jumped onto a fallen tree trunk and, hastily climbing to a height inaccessible to the pseudo-dogs, mentally thanked the elements that had dropped this mighty ash tree here. And also himself - for always being conscientious about running and rock climbing training (however, there were no unscrupulous runners or rock climbers in "Misanthrope"). Intruder looked closely to see if the chase had attracted the attention of the bandits at the factory. And then, having made sure that it had not, he methodically shot the mutants running around under the tree and drooling with single shots. After which he swore to forget about non-lethal means of self-defense and not to let the anomalous inhabitants of the Zone get close to him. Although a minute later he realized that he had given himself a stupid oath - was it possible to swear off anything in the Zone? The intruder seemed to have no problems detecting areas of increased radioactivity and anomalies – these malicious bursts of unknown energy. The dangerous radiation was detected by an active dosimeter. And the anomaly detector that came with the stalker’s PDA – a hybrid of a shockproof PDA and a portable radar – recorded the nearest areas, notifying about them with an annoying vibration signal (the major immediately switched off the beeper for obvious reasons). In addition, Kalter soon learned to detect some energy traps by eye – they gave themselves away either by a haze emanating from them, or by electrical flashes, or by a phosphorescent glow. But no matter how precisely the detector was set up and how keen his vision was, Kalter still nearly missed this threat a couple of times. Even before meeting the pseudo-dogs, the major nearly flew into the "whirlwind," mistaking it for its harmless atmospheric variety. It's just that at that moment, Kalter was looking at another nearby anomaly - a more noticeable "springboard" than the "whirlwind" - and believed that the detector was signaling about it. Having nearly gotten into trouble (and that's putting it mildly!), the intruder learned two important lessons from the incident. The first: he began to rely less on the detector - like any electronics, it, too, was not immune to failures. And the second: he put the stalker rule "Don't rush and look around" into the tune of a catchy song and chewed it over in his head until it, figuratively speaking, was imprinted on the major's subcortex. However, even this constant vigilance did not always help. Having shot the pseudo-dogs, Kalter jumped down from the tree and almost burned the top of his head with the "stinging fluff" that hung from the branch of a fallen ash tree. If the intruder had not learned in advance what this anomaly, mimicking ordinary weeping vegetation, looked like, his victory over the pack of mutants would have been greatly marred. To say the least - completely annulled. Only now did the major truly regret the mistake he had made. Namely, the failure to take with him his proven stalker tool for exploring the path ahead – a bag of iron bolts. The blunder was one of those that intruders of Kalter’s level would simply be shameful to make. “I’ve never carried all sorts of junk in my pockets before!” he thought contemptuously, having studied the operational materials about the Zone that the Strategist had collected for him. “I’ll somehow get by without bolts, I wasn’t born yesterday! What the hell do I need eyes, ears and a bunch of expensive electronics for, one wonders?” The Zone could only laugh at Kalter's self-confidence. Sometimes it seemed to him that he even heard the malicious giggling of this creature, whose invariably gloomy face did not seem to suggest that it had a sense of humor. Or maybe it was not a giggling at all, but an alien wind howling in the skeletons of wrecked equipment and the ruins of the surrounding farms?.. After this series of unfortunate incidents, each of which could easily have ended fatally, Kalter became doubly cautious. From personal experience, the intruder knew that sooner or later he would inevitably encounter a problem that he would not be able to solve by force. The Zone is generous with surprises, and for every pleasant one, such as a precious artifact found, there are always fifty deadly ones. The army patrol that Kalter encountered was not such a danger in itself, of course. But it inevitably caused the major a whole host of other difficulties. Having passed the rusty Belarus and the Intruder hiding in the ditch under it, the soldiers and stalkers walked another fifty meters and came to a bus stop booth that had been preserved here since the old days. A similar building on the opposite side of the road had been destroyed to the ground, and the surviving booth was the only convenient shelter in the area from the rain that was just beginning to drizzle over the southern Cordon. The rain was light and harmless, so the patrol commander glanced at the sky and ordered the soldiers to continue moving along the route. However, the stalkers did not want to get wet and, after a short consultation, decided to sit out the bad weather in the booth. Especially since it was only a short walk to the second checkpoint, and the newcomers did not need guides in principle. On that note, they parted ways: the soldiers moved north, and the stalker trio sat down under the canopy and, having lit a tablet of dry fuel, began to heat up cans of food on it in turn. Kalter noticed that all the newcomers were armed with pistols, which the guys prudently took out of their pockets in order to keep the weapons at the ready at all times. The major, who had crouched close to the ground, cursed silently: oh shit – now, in order to get out of the stalkers’ sight and bypass the ill-fated road, he would have to crawl along a fairly large distance. Of course, he could abandon the conspiracy and overcome the obstacle right here, in the open. And he could simply ignore the curious questions of these three, which she would probably want to ask the first stalker she met. However, he should not forget that the newcomers sitting in the booth would soon have to cross the second checkpoint, where they could blab to the soldiers about the uncommunicative wanderer in the strange jumpsuit. So the intruder had no right to reveal his presence. Kalter had already started to crawl out from under the tractor when he suddenly noticed some movement nearby. Wanting to find out who else besides the three stalkers was wandering around the area, the major hid again and, carefully peering out from behind the wheel, took a closer look. At first, the intruder thought that the man walking along the ditch parallel to the road had also spotted him and squatted down for greater invisibility. But as soon as he resumed moving, Kalter immediately realized that if this subject had once been a man, he was no longer one, even formally, since he had long been included in online stalker reference books under the name of snork. With the obligatory note: intelligent and extremely dangerous mutant. The strong and incredibly agile snorks were more like chimpanzees in their habits: they moved in zigzag leaps, leaning on all fours, and when they attacked their prey, they did not bite it, but beat it to death with crushing blows. At the same time, they themselves did not feel any pain, which is why they did not worry about self-preservation and were always covered from head to toe with unhealed wounds. But the most curious feature of the snorks, which the researchers of the Zone are still puzzling over, is hidden in something else. For some reason, this evil mutant is terribly embarrassed by his face, or rather, his muzzle, and hides it in every possible way, loving to use old gas masks for this, which can be easily found in all corners of the Zone. That is why from afar or in the semi-darkness it seems that the snork's head is completely bald, his eyes are the size of an Olympic medal, and instead of a nose he has a half-meter trunk; in reality, it is a piece of a gas mask hose. In other words, he is a mutant, both physically and mentally. At the sight of the jumping monster, Kalter did not fall into such a delusion, because before being thrown into the Zone, he had taken a course in theoretical "mutant studies." And so the major decided that if he lay in the shelter and did not move, the snork would not notice him and would soon leave. Or would not leave, but would decide to hunt the three inexperienced stalkers sitting in the booth. They had no idea of the threat hanging over them, gobbling up warmed-up canned food and washing it down with canned beer. What carelessness, the intruder mentally cursed them, having already learned from bitter experience what excessive self-confidence is fraught with in the Zone. Didn't they really realize where they were, or were they hoping for the proximity of the checkpoint and thinking that it was safe here? They should have first posted an observer before sitting down to dinner. The snork didn't even think about hiding, so it was clearly visible from the road. But the guys didn't look around, although the soldiers had probably instructed them about elementary precautions in these wild places. The major had no intention of rescuing a group of careless stalkers. He had his own orders, for the sake of which he was ready to sacrifice all the stalkers in the Zone taken together. If Kalter had suffered from bouts of nobility, not only would he not have been assigned this job, but he would not have been accepted into the Department at all. The intruders of the special department "Misanthrope" are not a bargaining chip, like most field operatives, whom the command was ready to sacrifice without regret if the situation required it. Enormous funds are spent on training each "misanthrope", and the degree of risk the intruders face before the upcoming mission is calculated to the percent. And if Kalter starts to pretend to be a selfless hero every time in such cases, he will endanger not only his own precious life, but also, no matter how pompous it may sound, the security of his country. A sense of responsibility for it was instilled in the major as firmly as survival skills. And therefore the intruder always and everywhere took only the risk that was sanctioned by his immediate commander – the Strategist. Kalter did not recognize any other risk. Holding his breath, the major kept a close eye on the snork. And the snork, in turn, froze and became wary, listening to the voices coming from the booth. Kalter was already mentally wishing the careless stalkers good luck, when suddenly the damned mutant turned its head in his direction and stared at the tractor through the glass circles of the gas mask. The creature definitely could not have heard the major. The intruder had not made a sound since its discovery, and the snork's ears were hidden under a rubber mask. Seeing him was also doubtful. Even if you had eagle eyesight, looking at the world through the dirty and cracked glass of a gas mask, you would hardly be able to see anything further than your outstretched hand. This meant that the snork had sensed the lurking "misanthrope" either by smelling him through a corrugated hose, or by some other sixth, seventh or eighth sense unknown to humans. And the intelligent mutant had enough brains to figure out that it was much easier to deal with a lone stalker than with a whole group of these bipedal creatures. What Snorks were like in combat, Kalter knew only from the unclear video recordings in the Department's archives, shot in the Zone by amateur stalkers. However, the major could not even imagine how agile these ugly wearers of torn gas masks really were. Before he had time to pick up the rifle lying nearby, the Snork cut the distance between them in half with one jump, and with the second jumped onto the hood of the tractor. The rusty springs creaked with strain, and the hood lid dented with a crash, which made Kalter momentarily think that the old Belarus was about to fall over on its side. But, fortunately, the tractor stood firm, only receiving a couple of extra dents from the jumper. Despite the snork's skinny appearance, it weighed at least a hundred kilograms, and was probably as angry as a whole pack of pseudo-dogs. The major grabbed the rifle, but the cunning monster had a good reason to jump onto the tractor. The snork had clearly foreseen that they would shoot at it - it also apparently had some combat experience - so before pouncing on its victim, it chose an ideal platform for an attack. Kalter couldn't hit the monster sitting on the tractor from underneath, and he would have had to stick his head out anyway. But which side was safer to do it from? The intruder would have been able to shoot back in all four directions, but not from such a lightning-fast enemy. It was extremely difficult to change firing positions while lying in a confined space, and the major, having switched the safety of the "VMK" to burst fire, decided to play a preemptive game with the enemy. Having rolled out into the gap between the axles, Kalter raised his rifle, intending to put a dozen bullets into the snork sitting on the hood. But, as it turned out, he had already left his position and jumped off the "Belarus" onto the ground. And while the intruder was aiming at the rainy sky, the mutant managed to duck under the tractor in a second, grabbed Kalter by the scruff of the neck and jerked him back to where the man was constrained in his movements. In addition, the "VMK" caught its belt on the tractor step and broke out of the major's hands, almost breaking his fingers. Kalter made no attempt to retrieve the weapon he had lost. Dragging the snork like a sack of potatoes, the major snatched a light double-edged dagger from the sheath he wore on his left forearm. And when the creature dragged its victim out from under the bottom on the opposite side, the intruder twisted and plunged the blade into the enemy's thigh up to the hilt. And then he forcefully spread the mutant's thigh as if he were slicing open a huge zucchini. Thick and stinking blood mixed with pus stained Kalter's hands, but the snork was unfazed. "He really doesn't feel pain," the intruder noted quickly, after which he inflicted several more deep wounds on the mutant in the crotch and lower abdomen. And again, to no avail. A human would have died from such wounds in a matter of seconds. But the snork seemed in no danger of dying from exsanguination, for life in him was maintained by otherworldly forces unknown to science. Perhaps, if Kalter had managed to cut the monster's tendons, it would have ended up immobilized. But the intruder could not get his dagger into the hellish jumper's flailing ankles. He could not grab them either: covered in sticky ichor, they constantly slipped out of the major's hands. And then he grabbed what he could - a corrugated gas mask hose dangling in front of his face. The same trunk that many overly impressionable stalkers see on a snork... The intruder did not cut off the mutant's oxygen supply, since he doubted that the creature was breathing air at all. Instead, Kalter yanked the hose with all his might and tore the gas mask off the humanoid monster's face. The rotten rubber snapped, and the mask, bursting in half, turned into useless shreds. The major did this without thinking at all - he simply got carried away or subconsciously remembered the desire that had arisen in him during the briefing to find out what exactly the "shy" snorks were hiding under their masks. But be that as it may, this counterattack by the intruder turned out to be much more effective than the senseless flaying of the mutant's flesh with a knife. Like most predators, the snork preferred to hunt without making a sound. But when the kicking victim forced him to look at the world not through the glass of the gas mask, but directly, the creature roared so loudly that in comparison to its roar, the roar of a tiger would have sounded simply pathetic. As it turned out, the enemy was completely unprepared to fight "with an open visor." The snork threw the major aside and covered his face with his sore-ridden, but quite human, palms. It was hard to believe, but the snork really did have a "complex" about his appearance; after all, it wasn't the pale daylight that blinded him, was it? Especially since today's rainy weather did not contribute to this at all. Roaring at the top of his lungs and continuing to hide his ugly face from his victim, the snork began to quickly back away in the direction from which he had come. However, Kalter had no intention of letting the mutant go, especially now that he had won the advantage from him. Not because the major had gone berserk and was eager to kill the enemy at any cost. Sparing the snork would have been a strategic mistake, which the intruder had no right to make. Who knows what would pop into the head of the intelligent creature after the snork ran away and found a new gas mask. What if the mutant decided to track down the offender and get even with him for the humiliation he had suffered, when Kalter had managed to safely forget about it? No, he would not allow such a sword of Damocles to loom over his head. The major had enough other concerns, and he certainly did not need an extra threat in the person of a vengeful monster. Jumping to his feet, Kalter caught up with the snork, grabbed him by his shabby hair and with a powerful blow cut the hunched monster's throat all the way to the spine. Then he grabbed the enemy's head with his hands and broke it to the side with such force that the mutant's neck cartilage could not withstand it and burst. Without releasing his grip, the intruder threw himself on the ground and, dragging his opponent along with him, performed a classic wrestling coup. From the jerk intensified by the fall, the snork's already almost severed head was torn off completely. Therefore, when Kalter completed the move, the body of the agonizing monster lay on the major's right, and the head, thrown to the side, on the left. Whatever one might say, it had been a long time since Kalter had seen his hands shake with excitement. For the umpteenth time that morning, the Zone had demonstrated how much the major had underestimated it. He thought that foreign business trips were real work for a "misanthrope," but here, practically in his own backyard, everything would go smoothly, in the style of "I came, I saw, I conquered"? No way! If even Kordon had almost finished off the experienced intruder, what would happen next when he delved into the real wilderness? After all, Kalter had to not only adapt to local realities in the shortest possible time, but also complete the task assigned by the Department. No, this time the Strategist had definitely miscalculated, and this business trip would be much more difficult than the smart departmental analysts had assured him. But it was no time to be upset about it. It was time to get away from the field of the fleeting battle before the stalkers holed up in the booth realized what was going on. Kalter picked up his rifle and crawled away from this place, using the path trodden by the snork, so that the movement of the grass would not give away the creeping intruder. And the alien wind blowing at the back of his head seemed to mockingly laugh at him and, dragging low leaden clouds across the sky, hinted to Kalter that he had every chance of never admiring the sun again... - Did you see?! No, tell me, did you see this?! - the overexcited Sanek Brynza started shouting, pointing the barrel of his gun at the place where either a fight or some other abnormal crap had just taken place. The stalkers didn't have time to really see who had started the fuss in the dry grass behind the bus stop. They ran out to the side of the road with their weapons at the ready only when the passions that had flared up in the ditch had died down. - I saw it, I saw it! - Kolyan Patlaty snapped, aiming his PM in the same direction his comrade was pointing. - And stop yelling, or all the pseudo-creatures will come running here! - After which he turned to the third, oldest member of their newborn stalker alliance: - Kesha, did you spot anything? - Nope, - Kesha, nicknamed Lesovoz, said laconically. Then, looking closer, he added: - But I heard everything. It seems like the dogs near the tractor mauled someone. But definitely not a stalker. Did you hear how that creature roared? “Let’s go and have a look?” Shaggy suggested uncertainly. - Come on, are you nuts? - Brynza took his partner's initiative with hostility. - What if those mutants are still there, in the grass, hiding? They're just waiting for us! And anyway, it was a shame we lagged behind the patrol. I told you right away that it would be better to trudge to the checkpoint with the soldiers! - Then why the hell did you even come to the Zone if you're afraid of any noise? - Lesovoz threw at him contemptuously. Then he waved his hand in the direction of the Belarus: - Let's go, Kolyan, let's check out the situation. Did you hear what the sergeant said? There's a scientist living somewhere near Amber Lake who buys up dog tails, boar hooves, and pseudo-flesh eyes for the price of good artifacts. Let's go see, maybe there really is something lying around for him. Just don't forget to keep an eye on the sides, okay? Holding their pistols at the ready, Kolyan and Kesha began to slowly descend into the ditch. Sanek timidly stamped his feet, but nevertheless chose to join his comrades. Trying to step quietly, the stalker trio made their way to the tractor and began to look around warily. Having quickly discovered the trampled grass and the headless body lying on it, the hunters for tails and hooves immediately surrounded the remains, still continuing to aim at them. Well, how else? According to local tales, a living dead person is not uncommon in the Zone. So where is the guarantee that this dead man will not jump up and attack the gullible stalkers? - My God, there's so much blood, so much blood! - Brynza grimaced with disgust, wiping his boot, stained with the monster's blood, on the grass. - And we, fools, missed such a fight! - Guys, it's a snork! - Lesovoz whistled in surprise. - I bet it's him! Everything matches: there's a head lying there, and there's a torn gas mask! - Holy shit! - croaked Shaggy, who also recognized one of the most odious mutants of the Zone by his characteristic features. - And they say that there are no snorks in the Cordon! So it turns out that it's all a lie? "It turns out it's a lie," Kesha summed up, looking around and checking if another such creature was lurking nearby. "I'm more worried about something else: what kind of monster tore our snork apart along with the gas mask and where that bastard ran off to afterwards." “A bloodsucker?” Kolyan suggested uncertainly. The timber truck shook his head: it was doubtful, as if something bigger and more toothy had definitely been here. No one else suggested any other versions. The company sank into an oppressive silence; the only sounds were the rustling of the wind in the withered grass, the barking of a pack of dogs beyond the copse, the crackling of an electrical anomaly nearby, and the patter of raindrops on the rusty cabin of the Belarus. - Guys, this... - the cowardly Brynza was the first to break the silence. - Well, in short... I wanted to ask: does anyone know what needs to be cut off from a snork to sell? It has this... no tail, no horns, no hooves... And it seems like it doesn't have any eyes either. - What dolts like you usually cut off from a snork, and where they then stuff this stuff, I'll whisper to you later, Brynza, in private, - Kesha smiled wryly. - Although by that time you'll figure it out yourself... But for now, guys, I'll tell you this: we've stocked up on the wrong guns. We need to urgently find something more powerful than these cheap scarecrows. Much more powerful... .

Two short but furious battles ended almost simultaneously. The body of the machine gunner, cut down by Kalter, rolled down the stairs at the same moment when a cumulative grenade, fired by the already dead Corsair, exploded on the construction site below. And if the sectarians who finished him off were still guessing about the fate of their brothers who had gone upstairs, Kalter and Mrakobes realized the unenviable fate of their partner as soon as the shooting died down. It was unrealistic for the Dolgovtsy to kill ten opponents in such a short time. The most he could do was hold them back with fire while his partners got out of the building. But the barrage died down, and clouds of dust were pouring out of the windows of the two-story building and were carried away by the wind. Everything indicated that cover could be safely forgotten. No matter how many Monoliths survived, they would soon all arrive at the site of the failed exchange. The Dolgovtsy and the intruder urgently needed to throw the ropes they had brought with them to the ground and climb down them along the windowless southern wall of the multi-story building. However, only Kalter and Mrakobes could make an emergency retreat. Things were bad for Bulba, who had been shot by a machine gun. He couldn't even move on his own, let alone perform high-altitude stunts. The Duty soldier who had been shot was bleeding profusely, and as soon as the commotion on the platform had died down, Mrakobes immediately rushed to his wounded comrade to provide him with first aid. One glance was enough for Kalter to understand: Bulba would be lucky if he survived until lunch with such injuries. It was unknown whether there was a qualified surgeon in the Bar and how quickly the legendary Aesculapius of the Zone, the Swamp Doctor, could arrive there on call. But now half of the Wild Lands lay between Bulba and the only place where he could receive help, and God knows how many surviving sectarians were hanging around at the foot of the building. Obscurantist and Kalter themselves had practically no chance of surviving - what care for the wounded man? If an intruder had been in his place, he would have found the strength to swallow an ampoule of cyanide, but Bulba, although he was on the brink of life and death, clearly did not think about suicide. Obscurantist did not intend to relieve his comrade of suffering either. On the contrary, he quickly began to inject him with morphine and apply tourniquets from whatever came to hand. The major tore off the protective mask and helmet from the sectarian he had finished off first, lifted his head by the hair, wiped the dead man's blood-stained face and called out to the Obscurantist who was busy with the wounded man: - Is this Huron? Dolgovets took a break from his chores for a moment, looked closely at the dead enemy, shook his head, and replied: - No. It's definitely not him. That one looks like an Indian, and our stiff has a mug like that actor who acts in Russian TV series... That one, the blond one... Oh, what the hell difference does it make! We screwed up Huron, it's clear as day! Either he's somewhere down there, or he never came to the Wild Lands! Better go help Bulba stop the bleeding while those goats are still hanging around at the entrance! - Bulba is not long for this world, and you can see it, - Kalter responded. - Leave him and let's go down, otherwise another minute and it will be too late. We need to hide in time. The sect will think that mercenaries wanted to "ditch" them, that's all. And you will lead me to the Military Warehouses, and tonight we will correct our mistake. - I won't leave here without Bulba! - Mrakobes objected. - He needs to be taken to the base. There's a smart doctor there, he'll help him! And you'll help me and Bulba! “I don’t have time for this,” the intruder snapped, taking his half-mask from the fingers of a dead enemy and taking the rifle and unloading vest hidden behind a column. “I have to be at the Military Depots tonight. And you too! You have orders: to kill Huron. And your mission is not yet complete.” - To hell with your orders! - the debtor exploded. - My friend is dying, don't you get it?! And I'll drag him to the Bar at any cost, with or without your help! "I'm heading for the Warehouses, and you do as you wish," Kalter told him before heading for the stairs leading down. "But in that case, I don't care about your Huron. The contract is broken." - Go to hell! - Mrakobes snapped and began to tighten another tourniquet on his bleeding comrade. - Bulba is my best friend, and if we have to die together, then so be it! And you are nothing to me, and I don’t give a damn about you, got it?! Kalter did not answer. He was already descending the stairs and listening to what was happening at the construction site. Apparently, the sectarians intended to comb the building. Short, abrupt commands were heard from outside, exchanged by the enemies approaching the entrance. Running lower was dangerous, since the major did not know what forces he would have to face on the first floor. Jumping out the window was also not the best option for retreat. Over the past quarter of a century, piles of construction debris had accumulated under the walls of the high-rise, and jumping onto them from a second-floor window, the intruder risked breaking his legs. There was only one way out: to use a mountaineering winch. Which is what the major did, hooking his carabiner onto a ledge on the windowsill and jumping out of the window opening on the northern wall of the high-rise. The descent and winding of the rope took only a few seconds for the trained climber Kalter, but he still couldn't get away unnoticed. When the major touched the ground with his boots, the Monolithists had just burst onto the first floor. But, noticing some movement in the windows, they quickly figured out what was going on and immediately jumped out. The cultists had clearly figured out that the mercenaries did not want to end up in a trap, and as soon as they realized that their gamble had failed, they grabbed the most valuable artifacts and left the building via the ropes. And although the ranks of the cultists had noticeably thinned, they were still determined to catch up with the fugitives, who had also probably suffered significant losses. At least one pimp was definitely lying decapitated on a pile of broken bricks at the edge of the construction site. Like a cat being chased by a pack of dogs, the intruder raced along a familiar route from the high-rise to the corner of the wooden warehouse and, taking cover behind an overturned garbage container, paused for a moment to figure out what forces were pursuing him. Six or seven opponents, half of whom were wearing exoskeletons... It was unknown how many of them were "Gauss", but it had to be assumed that the major was in the sights of four electromagnetic guns (and that was the case - the "Gauss" of the sectarian killed by Korsar was picked up by one of his surviving brothers). How many Monolithists remained in the building could only be guessed at, but in any case, Mrakobes would now have to fight a much smaller enemy group. However, this would soon become clear from the bursts of machine gun fire that would inevitably be heard from the high-rise when the sectarians climbed to the third floor... A terrible blow shook the container behind which the major was hiding, and instantly a huge hole with torn, outwardly gaping edges appeared in the side of the iron box, half a meter from Kalter. Then this swift something also pierced the transformer booth standing a hundred meters behind Kalter, and tore something else apart in the distance - only the roar and the ringing of broken glass reached the intruder. Without thinking twice, the major rushed around the corner of the warehouse, and then something began that could only be compared to a raging tornado - a rare phenomenon even for the Zone, rich in all sorts of cataclysms. The much more powerful blow that followed the first one threw the container away like a matchbox, smashed the transformer into pieces and made a significant hole in the end of the railway tank car that had rusted to the rails. After that, the wooden, tin-lined warehouse, along the wall of which the major was running, literally began to tear itself apart. This violent destructive process was accompanied, in addition to the cracking, by a series of methodical sounds, similar to the whistle of a huge whip, and rolling blows on the tin. There were no explosions either inside or outside the warehouse. And yet, the structure that stretched across the entire site began to systematically fly apart from one end to the other, like a long string of New Year's firecrackers. Casting a quick glance over his shoulder, Kalter was horrified to see that not only was the warehouse behind him turning into a chaotic jumble of slate, tin, and boards. A freight train, stopped on the nearest track, had suffered the same fate. The whistling, thundering hurricane was tearing apart the wooden carriages, leaving only bare platforms that only a nuclear explosion could move. It didn't take long to guess what kind of elements had caused all this chaos. Four Gauss cannons were firing in turn, one after another, not giving Kalter a second's respite. The cultists clearly weren't going to bother themselves with a long chase through the Wild Lands. It would have been much easier to first raze the area to the ground and then slowly search the rubble for the body of the nimble fugitive. The meticulousness with which the Gauss guns approached the task at hand was seriously frightening. Not sparing the shells, they methodically covered sector after sector with fire, forcing the intruder to move in one direction - forward and only forward. Behind Kalter, a whirlwind of motley debris raged, into which the Gauss shell turned everything that came its way. And in front of the major there was a brick wall, which served either as the border of the marshalling yard, or simply as a dividing barrier between its vast areas. Kalter could have overcome the wall at full speed if he wanted, especially since he was already running at breakneck speed. But the wire fence stretched over the fence would not have allowed the intruder to jump over the wall in one dashing leap. The fugitive found himself between an impregnable barrier and the chaos that was catching up with him, in which it seemed that not even a fly could survive. The warehouse and the carriages were disappearing like houses of cards in gusts of wind, and the Gauss missiles tearing off fragment after fragment did not allow this storm to subside for a moment. The major saw that it would catch up with him when he reached the fence. And then, even if Kalter dodged the lightning-fast projectile, the fragments of the wall blown apart by the explosion would bury the man under a brick mound, the clearing of which would take a good half day. Although for the wearer of an exoskeleton such work is unlikely to seem like a burden and any of the “armored ships” will dig a fugitive out from under the ruins without even straining themselves. This was the main advantage of the Gauss cannons. The initial velocity of their shells allowed them to hit the target with direct fire at a great distance, and not every shelter could withstand their penetrating power. Now the enemy was shooting at Kalter with special blunt-ended shells, whose penetrating power was reduced in favor of destructive ability. When such a shell hit an obstacle, it did not just pierce it, but also blew it to pieces; when hitting a metal surface, it left huge jagged holes. Due to the flying debris, the area of destruction of the "Gauss" increased many times. And firing from four electromagnetic cannons at once, the sectarians did not leave Kalter a chance of survival at all. It seemed as if there was no barrier in the entire visible space that would protect the major from the all-penetrating shells. Except perhaps for one single shelter: the steep edge of the unloading platform, along which Kalter was currently running away. For the convenience of unloading freight trains, the platform was built at the level of the wagon platforms. In order to get inside the wagon, it was possible to do without a ramp - the train approached the edge of the concrete elevation almost right up to the edge. Between them there was only a narrow gap, which could be squeezed through only by taking off the stalker's overalls. Kalter had no time to undress, but he didn't need to. Having reached the coupling of the last and penultimate cars, the intruder jumped between them into a fairly wide gap. After which, having fallen onto the sleepers, he dove under the wheel pair and froze, covered on all sides by the multi-ton iron structure. A second later, a barrage of gauss fire passed over the major's head, sweeping the remains of the warehouse from the platform and in the blink of an eye destroying the wooden carriage as if it had been glued together from cardboard. Dirt, nails and wood chips rained down on Kalter from above, but large debris could not fly through the cracks in the platform. Boards, fragments of carriage frames and sheets of broken slate that had flown here, together with bent tin, formed impassable blockages on the railway tracks. To top it all off, shells that hit the fence seasoned this whole vinaigrette with brick fragments and scraps of barbed wire... Having retained a more or less civilized appearance until now, from now on this area of the station, more than others, began to fall under the definition of "Wild Lands". If Kalter had been sure that the enemy would limit themselves to shelling, he would have lain under the destroyed train car for at least another couple of hours before he would have risked popping his head out into the light of day. But the sectarians would hardly consider the fugitive mercenary dead without concrete evidence. The Gauss, of course, would not poke their nose into the rubble they had created, but the battleships would certainly not fail to walk back and forth over it to make sure that their brothers had not wasted so much ammunition in vain. Getting out from under the car was not so easy. In fact, the major was now at the bottom of a large pile of bulky garbage. Before Kalter managed to find a way out, he had to crawl along the sleepers to the very end of the car. The departmental overalls protected the major from cuts that would inevitably have been left on him by sharp splinters and torn edges of tin sheets. But still, pushing through the trash constantly clinging to his clothes was extremely tedious. In addition, it created unnecessary noise and could attract the attention of enemies, who were probably already studying the destroyed area through binoculars. However, apart from the creaking, crackling and grinding of the settling mountains of garbage, no sounds had reached Kalter yet. Including the expected bursts of machine gun fire from the high-rise building. Of course, it was too early to draw any firm conclusions based on this observation, but, apparently, all the surviving sectarians had set off in pursuit of the major. Who, in turn, apparently decided that the fugitive was also the only surviving enemy. If this were not so, more mercenaries would certainly have come down from the top floor of the high-rise. For why would some of them suddenly run away, while others remained in the building? Mercenaries are a close-knit clan, and they do not fight according to the principle of “every man for himself.” The climber’s escape also did not look like a diversionary maneuver – he clearly did not want to catch the eye of the sectarians who noticed him… In general, whether Kalter wanted it or not, but by drawing the rest of the enemy forces to himself, he still helped Mrakobes in his noble aspiration. However, it is difficult to say whether this will save the seriously wounded Bulba. Unless Mrakobes calls for reinforcements from Bar. But even this did not guarantee that the debtor who had lost so much blood would live to receive qualified medical care. There was only one way to check whether the Monolithists had noticed Kalter, who had emerged from under the rubble. Although the major himself would hardly have had time to find out the result of this check - the enemy "gauss" would have taken off the intruder's head before he heard the whistle of the flying shell. But, finding himself in the open air again, the fugitive realized that his appearance at liberty had not caused a stir in the enemy camp. But he himself soon appeared before Kalter's eyes. Three sectarians in exoskeletons confidently approached the rubble, intending to begin searching any minute. It was not possible to determine where the "gauss" were at that moment. But they had all certainly moved to more advantageous positions in order to continue observing the nearby area and, upon discovering the dead fugitive, immediately direct the "battleships" at him. If he was found alive, the Gauss tactics were to differ from the previous ones in only one way: first, the fugitive had to be killed, and only then would his coordinates be reported to the searchers. The major needed to get out of the rubble as quickly as possible and run to the cluster of wagons, where the Monolithists themselves had recently appeared. There, on a multi-track railway line, stood mostly freight trains carrying fuel and bulk cargo. The former, of course, had long since been dumped, but the latter – coal, gravel, fertilizers and similar materials – were still mostly in containers. Despite the deadly power of the Gauss cannons, they were definitely not capable of shooting through a wagon with gravel. In addition, the labyrinth with high walls, which is essentially what that vast territory cluttered with trains was, was an ideal place to cover their tracks. And besides, it led in the direction of the Military Warehouses. That's where Kalter headed as soon as he was sure that he had not attracted the attention of the sectarians. The intruder covered the first thirty meters at a snail's pace. It was simply impossible to get around the rampart of wagon and warehouse debris that had grown up near the unloading area. Kalter had no choice but to creep right over the piles of garbage, freezing and holding his breath every time the precarious support under his feet shuddered and settled. Unlike the major, the exoskeleton wearers moved forward much more confidently, simply kicking and throwing aside everything that blocked their path. The rubble under their onslaught flew apart as if by magic, and a unit of regular fighters could now follow each "battleship" without hindrance. Kalter figured that if he and his pursuers continued to move at the same speed, in a couple of minutes the cultists would completely cut him off from the road to the northeast. He had to press on before the situation went from bad to extremely bad. Running away to the trains under fire from four "gauss" would be as hard as swimming out of a raging whirlpool. The further Kalter went from the destroyed site, the less rubble there was on his way. Soon he no longer needed to climb over piles of rubble, since they could be safely bypassed. However, at the same time, the major began to appear more and more often in open spaces, looming in sight of enemy observers. Now each of Kalter's runs resembled a toss of lots: it was impossible to predict whether the sharp-sighted "Gauss" would spot the fugitive this time or whether he would get lucky again. It goes without saying that playing with a coin for a long time will sooner or later make it fall on your palm on the wrong side. The intruder's play with his pursuers ended in the same predictable way. True, it was not the Gauss that first noticed Kalter, but the battleship with a machine gun. Having broken through the jams and looked around better, the machine gunner soon discovered a suspicious flickering right ahead, after which he immediately opened fire on the target looming ahead. This served as a signal for the other Monolithists: the Gauss quickly figured out where their comrade was shooting and began shooting in the same direction, while the remaining battleships rushed straight through the rubble with redoubled agility, intending to quickly join their comrade. For Kalter, retreating by dashes was not only unprofitable, but also dangerous. He had no idea how clearly the shooters with electromagnetic guns could see the target, but he knew that the faster and more chaotically he ran, the harder it would be for the Gauss to hit him. There were no more than a hundred steps left to the nearest freight train. But to cover this hundred meters straight and stay alive would be an absolutely unattainable result for the intruder. Piles of debris scattered everywhere rose into the air again from the shells that hit them, and a reinforced concrete support, broken at the base, collapsed right in the major's path. Without stopping, Kalter jumped over it and immediately darted to the left. Just in time: the asphalt behind the major rose up with torn-out layers, as if a small meteorite had crashed into it. The ominous whistle of invisible whips began to cut the air again and stimulate increased adrenaline releases in their potential victim. The shooting at the zigzagging target continued until one of the Gauss rifles fired a shell at the Electra that had turned up on Kaltera's path. It was an order of magnitude larger than its sisters that the intruder had already encountered in the Zone. Having spread its lightning tentacles, this anomaly was the size of a low, spreading tree. It did not bother the Major, since it was located off his route. The Monolith shooter could not have hit the Electra by accident. Therefore, he fired at it in such a way that the anomaly would strike and cover the fugitive with an electrical storm. And once again the Zone proved that making any predictions about it is a futile exercise and fraught with trouble for those who decide to rely on these predictions. Even Kalter, whose stay in the Zone did not exceed a week, was aware that the anomaly pulsating to his right was not as simple as it might seem at first glance. There was something atypical in its appearance, if not warning. And the size of the enormous Electra had nothing to do with it, although it also suggested such thoughts. This mystery defied explanation, as did the sensations that the alien wind caused in the major. The "Gauss" who shot at the anomaly thought practically, but the tactics he had devised were of no use. Pierced by the projectile, the lightning-fast - in the literal sense - anomaly did not give birth to an electrical storm, as he expected. Instead, the sparkling "tree" suddenly grew into a tumbleweed the height of a good hot air balloon. The gigantic sphere, which consisted entirely of a complex web of pulsating lightning, jumped up like a ball, and then with the same small (relative to the hot air balloon, of course) jumps galloped to where the projectile that had hit it had come from. The Gauss cannons fired at the fugitive a couple more times, smashing the cabin of the nearest electric locomotive and blowing the roof off the wooden boxcar. However, the cultists' priority target then became not Kalter, but this strange Electra. The anomaly that had suddenly come to life had distracted all the attention of the Monolithists, especially since it was rolling in their direction. The whistle of the Gauss cannons did not stop, but now all four shooters were firing exclusively at the electric sphere, wanting to "discharge" the anomaly before it could jump to its offenders. Kalter had just reached the last car in the long coal train and threw himself under the protection of the eighty-ton colossus, like a man jumping out of a hot steam room into a snowdrift. Not yet guessing what was happening behind him, the major expected that enemy shells would start to bite into the side of the car at any moment. But, contrary to such predictions, the car continued to stand motionless, as it had for many years before. Which was doubly surprising, since the shots of the Gauss rifles whipping the air did not subside for a moment. Intrigued, the intruder leaned out from behind the massive wheel, wanting to find out what had happened. The tumbleweed, molded from lightning, was slowly approaching the rubble, from which Kalter had a hard time getting out. Seeing the danger, the three pursuers did not tempt fate. Having stopped the chase, the "battleships" hurried to give way to the enraged "Electra" and hid wherever they could. They did not risk shooting at it. But the "gauss" did not spare shells, trying to deal with the ominous sphere, an encounter with which did not promise anything good for the Monoliths. It seemed that the shooters did not care what would happen if it exploded near their brothers hiding behind the rubble. The "Battleships" acted extremely wisely, preferring to play hide-and-seek with the tumbleweed instead of fighting it. "Electra" passed by the non-aggressive sectarians as if they were not there. The anomaly was only interested in those stalkers who continued to fill it with steel shells. However, they did not pierce the sphere and did not cause any disturbance in it, but simply disappeared in clots of lightning, either melting in them or falling into another dimension altogether. Finally, the Gaussians realized why the anomaly had not touched the people closest to it, but had gone straight to those who had settled in the distance. But the solution to this secret dawned on the shooters too late. Each of them had received a curse on their heads as soon as they fired the first projectile into the electric sphere. After that, it was already impossible to correct the situation, as well as to get rid of the Electra. Although there were rudiments of primitive reason in it, it was unlikely that this monster was capable of mercy - a quality alien to all the creations of the Zone. In the last moments before the electric predator struck back, there was no longer any shooting at it. The Gauss left their positions and tried to escape the curse that was catching up with them. Naturally, they failed - the punishment caught up with all four careless sectarians. Soaring above the unloading platform, the anomaly first turned from a ball into a huge flat circle, which then curved into a translucent dome and covered an area the size of a stadium. The impressive anomalous structure existed for only a few seconds. But during this time, such a monstrous lightning storm had managed to erupt and subside under the dome that when Electra was reborn into a sphere, only a gigantic scorched space remained in the place of its rampage. There was nothing to be seen on it, not even charred embers. Even the smoke had disappeared without a trace - no doubt the anomaly had sucked it in like an exhaust hood. Only flakes of gray ash hung in the air; for some reason Electra refused to dispose of them. They were all immediately carried away by a gust of wind, which did not allow them to fall on the smooth and black, like a freshly plowed field, circle - the place from which just a minute ago four Monoliths were firing at Kalter. And the agitated Electra calmed down, after which it remained hanging over the fire it had caused, as if enjoying the results of the revenge that had been accomplished. How long this would continue no longer mattered to Kalter. The enemy wanted to use the power of the Zone against him, but it, however, did not want to serve the sectarians, even though they stood guard over its main artifact, the "Wish Fulfiller". A strange choice... Although, one cannot say that it was too unexpected. After all, it was not the major who awakened the gigantic anomaly, but his pursuers. Therefore, they had to reap the fruits of their own short-sightedness. Having caught his breath a little, Kalter jumped up from the rubble embankment and, casting one last glance back, trotted along the train to the northeast. Intuition told the major that the three surviving armoured ships would not get rid of him so easily. Even if they had abandoned their intention to get even with the fugitive, they would still have to leave the Wild Lands along the same road. The intruder had no intention of engaging the Monoliths in battle – let them leave. Surely one of them had already contacted the command at the Military Warehouses and reported how this false deal had ended. Therefore, there was no point in eliminating the surviving enemies. But there was a risk that, having attacked the “armoured ships”, Kalter would be wounded or killed and, therefore, fail the mission. Now it could be complicated by the fact that Huron - of course, if he did not die incognito in the Wild Lands today - would order the helicopter with the Purga-D to be driven back to its previous parking place for safety reasons. But most likely, this would not happen. Flying the captured Mi-24 over the Zone once again, which had now become absolutely priceless for the Monolith, would be too risky. Kalter was sure that until the sect decided to strike at the Sky Spider, the helicopter would not leave its hangar at the Military Warehouses. From there, the target was practically a stone's throw away - five minutes' flight. At the same time, returning to the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant, Huron had every chance of running into army aviation patrolling over the Zone. Therefore, it was much easier for him to strengthen the outpost's security than to relocate the PRK to another point. Kalter did not know how quickly Huron would pull up additional forces to the Warehouses. But he believed that this would not happen in the next ten to twelve hours. In theory, the intruder should have reached the Monolith base before reinforcements from Pripyat. Here the major had not lied to Mrakobes at all: it was better not to delay in penetrating the enemy outpost. The favorable time period for this was limited and was shrinking with each hour.
