And since Mrakobes preferred to carry out Voronin's order to save his comrade, it meant that the intruder would carry out this operation without the support of the Duty. The general ordered that Kalter be given not only reinforcements, but also plastic explosives and ammunition. Plus a two-day dry ration, which the guest from the Department did not refuse either. The intruder, who had lost his support, nevertheless had everything he needed to complete the suddenly complicated work. The “misanthrope” in enemy territory could easily do without everything else… .

To the Chief of Staff of the Joint Command, Colonel...
transcript of radio communications between a group of military stalkers from the 2nd special forces detachment and aircraft #403: 05:47 "...I'm Four Hundred and Three, meet me at the point in two minutes..." ".understood, Four hundred and third, we are waiting..." "...commander, the movement is straight fifty, it seems like a stalker... or... I don't understand yet..." "...Taras, clarify who you see?.." 05:48 "...commander, this is Taras, the object is not responding to warnings, opening fire to kill..." "...Beaver, this is the commander, cover us with fire... Taras is wounded... the enemy has changed direction, heading south..." "...I am Four Hundred and Three, to the commander: what is going on with you?.." 05:49 "...commander, Four hundred to the third: entered the battle, two two-hundredths, one three-hundredth..." "...Chatterbox, to the commander: now this bitch will come to me, I will... it's Davydov!" "...Chatterbox, repeat..." "...Chatterbox to the commander: Davydov, it's him! What's wrong with his face?!" "...Chatterbox, answer the commander... why are you silent?.. Are you sure you identified Davyvod?.." "...The Chatterbox is dead, it's the Cat, the commander..." 05:50 "...I'm Four Hundred and Three, can anyone answer me? I'm Four Hundred and Three, can anyone hear me..." 05:51 "...I am Four Hundred and Third, Fifty-Fifth: I am observing seven motionless bodies along the edge of the clearing, signals from the PDAs of the military, only ours... all dead... I am not observing anyone else, requesting instructions..." 05:53 "...Fifty-fifth, Four-hundred-third: burn the clearing along with the bodies... return to base... end of communication...".

To the Chief of the Special Department of the Joint Command Headquarters, Colonel... ... based on the materials of investigation No. 3231-1/7. In connection with the revealed circumstances: develop a list of rules for commanders of military stalker groups, including paragraph nineteen. The group commander encrypts the request for confirmation of the "liquidation" code with a personal key. The response is duplicated to the group commander...
The commander slowly fell on his back, Davydov turned the pistol towards Zakhar. I was already sitting on my right heel, my left leg bent at the knee and extended forward, and in the sight, in the hologram circle, I could see the figure of the grenadier and the flashes from the flash suppressor of his M4. The bullets spattered against the stump. My shots knocked over the figure looming in the sights. Bolshoi emptied a sniper rifle magazine in Davydov's direction, but he managed to hide behind a tree. Several more barrels hit our group. Having fallen behind a stump, I looked around, not understanding anything yet, but automatically noting: the position is bad, we are too close, we are in each other's way, the enemy has pinned us to the ground and trees, not allowing us to properly fight. The enemy! I never thought I would call the special forces that. What is going on, controller take you all?! Why are they shooting at us? Not a single sensible thought in my head, no guesses... The burst crossed the stump, I cringed, then abruptly moved to the left, sticking the barrel out to the side, fired through the thick grass and immediately recoiled. We had to retreat, but where to? The clearing we had reached was about three kilometers from the swamps, and behind us was an area full of anomalies. If we went southeast and moved about a kilometer away from the clearing, judging by the map, we would end up at an abandoned military unit. And there is a third group. What are they going to do, who are they going to shoot at? I didn't have to wait long for an answer - in the south, to my left, there was a rumble. Bullets flew at us from the edge of the clearing. What a funny fire card it turns out to be! Clinch, fire bag, now our only way is back, to the anomalies. Labus had a good position, he was lying in a hollow between two pine trees growing in the shape of the letter "V". The roots there were washed away, nature itself created a mini-trench. Having settled comfortably, Kostya fired at the fighters attacking us. Bullets flying from the south fell on the pine tree to the left of him, Labus did not pay attention to them, although he could not shoot back. But Kostya held off Davydov's men well, but they were still approaching us, albeit slowly thanks to Labus's fire. In short dashes, from tree to tree - they would soon be within grenade throwing range. Zakhar was wounded, but Davydov still managed to shoot him. From this position I saw the signalman sitting, leaning against the thick trunk. He was tightening a tourniquet on his right hand - he clamped one end with his teeth, and with his left he was winding the rubber band around and around right over his jacket. Zakhar looked up from his work, reached into his vest and pulled out a grenade. Further on there was a shallow ravine, where Marat was hiding. It was hard to shoot him there, but he could hardly fire, risking getting a bullet right in the head as soon as he looked out. I didn't notice Aidar, and Bolshoi was ahead, behind a snag, the fighters from the third group didn't let him stick his head out. Khmury was positioned behind, closest to the far edge of the clearing, his machine gun was working in short bursts. This was the line of defense, more like a cross in shape. I didn't see Maly alone - where was he? Portable communications equipment went dead, something in this place was interfering with their work, maybe the proximity of the anomalous field. I had to shout out the command: - Step by step, in a serpentine pattern, we retreat! Direction - east, along the border of the clearing! It was a big risk, but I didn’t want to go back into the anomalies. Labus repeated the command, followed by Marat. Gloomy did not respond, and I suddenly realized that his machine gun had been silent for several seconds. And then I saw the fighters of the third group far to the left - they were running diagonally across the clearing, shooting as they went, a white dog was racing between them in long leaps. Marat was closest to them, but he couldn't stick his head out, he was lying there, rolling his eyes, probably realizing that the pear trees were approaching him, a little more - and it would be the end. Now they would squash us from the flank, and me - second after Marat, it was not easy to hide behind a stump ... I turned to cut them down with a burst, but other people's shots rang out very often, I had to press myself into the ground, lowering my head. It was as if I had gotten into an air meat grinder. Space shook, bubbled, bullets pierced it - to the left, to the right, above me - there was a dull knocking nearby, they hit the stump, tearing out splinters. The fighters were closing in on us from both sides, squeezing us as if with pliers. My shoulder was burned, a bullet flew by tangentially, tore the sleeve of my jacket. Damn it all! I stood up and raised my rifle. The bullets were whining, the stump was shaking from the blows. I aimed the barrel at the special forces running across the clearing, but then the first of them, waving his arms, fell. The dry sound of gunshots was heard - someone opened fire from the eastern, far end of the clearing. The second fighter fell, and then the shooter hit something that no one had noticed before - a transparent cloud of hot air hanging low above the ground. The third group of P-3rs should have run past, but when shots were heard from the east, they rushed around, jerking their barrels from side to side, looking for the shooter, and found themselves dangerously close to the anomaly. The heat worked. The fire shot upward, a concentric wave of hot air rolled, bending the grass - it turned black, shooting out smoky streams. Three fighters were scattered in different directions, two flared up like torches, the third's back caught fire. The bullets mowed down those who were set on fire by the anomaly, and the fiery fountain shrank and went out. The surviving pear trees lay down, we did not see them - the grass did not catch fire, but smoke spread over the edge of the clearing, making it difficult to see. A white dog with a singed bottom flew out from somewhere to the side and rushed towards the ravine, barking furiously. Marat jumped to his knees. Aydar jumped out of the bushes to the side and rushed to help his master. The white beast jumped, its mouth opened wide - its fangs were almost sunk into Marat's neck, when the blade of an army knife flashed in the air. Marat stretched out his hand forward, grabbing the thick fur on the dog's chest, and struck from below with his second. The dog finished itself off: when the blade sank into its belly, it ripped it open in a jump. A squeal was heard, the muzzle hit Marat's shoulder, he turned around and threw the dog further over the ravine. A moment later, Aydar, growling angrily, was nearby. Marat, clasping him across the body, fell back. When the heat worked and the shots died down for a while, Labus was able to stick his head out from behind the pine tree. Davydov's men jumped up to attack us, pinned down by the crossfire. Realizing too late that the third group was being fired upon, they came under Kostya's machine gun. The big one, running from the snag to the squat oak, stuck his head out from behind the trunk and took out one pear tree with a precise shot. Zakhar, having pulled the wound on his hand, threw a grenade. It exploded, one of Davydov's men fell, someone screamed - and at that moment the general flow of the battle changed imperceptibly, as if the scales had tipped. Now the tactical advantage had shifted to our side, we began to gain time. The big one ran back, Zakhar, on the contrary, ran forward, we found ourselves on the same line. Kostya shouted: - Cover it! I reloaded the rifle. Pointing the barrel above the torn-up stump, I fired a burst over the ground. Labus, bending down, rushed to the stretcher, hoisted the scientist onto his shoulder and hurried to the eastern edge of the clearing. Zakhar ran after him, clutching the weapon with his healthy hand, looking back every now and then. Kostya fell, leaving the scientist behind, turned around and raised the machine gun. Zakhar immediately dropped to one knee next to him. - Go! - shouted Big. Holding the rifle to my chest, I rushed towards them so as not to block the line of fire. Aidar's bark was heard from the side, he jumped out of the ravine and rushed along a parallel course. A shot rang out from behind, then a second one, a bullet whizzed over my shoulder; Zakhar's rifle crackled rapidly, the Minimi began to rattle. The figure of Marat, running, flashed at the edge of my vision. Zakhar's magazine was empty, and soon Labus's machine gun fell silent, but I had already fallen between them, turned over my shoulder, and jumped to my knees. The barrel of the M4 turned towards the enemy. - Go!!! - I shouted across the clearing. That's what a "snake with a ledge" is. The big one ran towards us, I saw movement far behind him, a blurred outline appeared in the sight's panorama - the helmet of a fighter sprawled in the grass - a shot, the helmet jerked and disappeared. Marat crouched down next to him, aiming, Aidar hid behind his back and barked angrily. Zakhar reloaded and started shooting, Labus was cursing nearby, something jammed in his machine gun. The one who had settled in on the eastern edge of the clearing fired frequently at the fighters of the third group, not allowing them to stick their heads out of the smoking grass. The big one appeared nearby, turned around, and crouched down. - Go! - Lekha, come on! - Labus barked. - Zakhar! We rushed on, and immediately "Minimi" spoke from behind. I noticed Gloomy by a fallen tree, the barrel of his machine gun was raised up and smoking slightly. Gloomy was lying with his face buried in the grass, an exit bullet hole gaping in the back of his helmet - the sniper had done his job, the bastards! I clenched my teeth so hard that my jaws clenched. If I get to the one who gave that order, I'll shoot him on the spot without any further ado! Who could force our special forces to work on their own?! Beyond the clearing we ran in a long arc. The big one covered the retreat. As I expected, the shooter who stopped the specialists turned out to be Maloy. He didn't lose his head when he heard the shooting in the forest, realized what was happening, took a risk: he went around the clearing along the eastern side, which is about two hundred meters, and returned back - just in time. “Where is the commander?” he asked as he ran. - Killed, - I said, without looking at him. - Gloomy too. Zakhar was wounded in the arm. What's ahead? The second number of the sniper pair reported: - The edge of the clearing is clear. To the left, two hundred meters, is jelly, and ahead, a hundred, is a carousel. Further on there are thickets. There is no way to get through them now, there is no time to fight our way through. If we go to the jelly, we will see an animal trail, I think wild boars have trampled it. There are no fresh tracks, but animals are definitely walking along it. The only thing we lacked was local boars. Although if the animals use the trail, there are no anomalies there, that's not bad. Most likely, it will lead us to a military unit or some other object in the Zone. Maybe to an abandoned village, to a permanent camp of one of the groups or to the ruins of a collective farm... Any buildings for us are a shelter, which the group really needs right now. - Marat! - Me! - the scout responded. - Let's go "head" with Aidar. Zakhar, how are you? - It’s okay, I can go. - Labus? "We're panting," Kostya answered easily. It wasn't noticeable that the scientist's body on his shoulder was somehow bending him to the ground. “Don’t slow down,” I said. He sat down, put his hand in the side pocket of his backpack, pulled out a coil of steel wire, and took the “EF” out of his vest. The others ran on. The Big One, who had caught up with me, also stopped and said: - Let me help you. He was breathing heavily, whistling. Big lived up to his nickname: plump, tall, calm. In general, he and Zakhar are a bit similar - not in appearance, but in behavior. - Here you go, - I handed the grenade to the sniper and stuck the end of the unwound wire in. Jumping across the path, I made a loop around the trunk of a young aspen and nodded to the Big One. "Take off the centimeter," he said. I loosened the loop on the tree, released a short piece of wire and tightened it again. - And I left a surprise for them there, - Big One grinned angrily. - I wove such a macrame... They'll definitely copy one, but the other two will catch on! Lyokha, what kind of nastiness is this? Why is that? The commander, Karl, then Gloomy - three of ours, huh?! - Later, Big One, - I answered with difficulty. - We'll talk about everything later, now we're running away. “Done,” he said, finishing fiddling with the grenade. We sprinkled the "efka" and the loop with moss and fallen leaves. I did not hear the pursuers, after all, they were professionals, but I felt that they were nearby and quickly approaching. Having finished with the tripwire, we started to catch up with the group. Not even thirty seconds had passed when an explosion thundered behind us - the GAR men had run into the Bolshoi's tripwire. This gave us five extra minutes - now they will be walking slowly, peering into the thickets and trees in front of them. If only we knew how many of them were left... I figured: I took out one myself, two or three by Maloy, one by Bolshoi. Total: minus four, maybe five. Three caught fire in the anomaly, two of them were definitely killed by Maloy... And there were eighteen GAR men, it turns out that there are already twelve or even eleven, plus one dog. Well, there are six of us and the "cargo three hundred". We need to urgently find shelter, some walls, they will catch up with us in ten minutes. You can't really run around the Zone, it's not that kind of territory. We ran on in the same order, rustling leaves, looking back every now and then. I remembered how they once chased a stalker into Agroprom, and he managed to hide somewhere in the basements. They led him for about a kilometer through the Zone, dogs were not yet part of the patrol groups. And the stalker, the bastard, was lucky - there was an emission, one of the corridors was blocked by an anomaly. We almost stuck our noses in there, there was clearly a trace showing that the fugitive had passed through there. Gloomy, the Zone rests in peace, he smelled the ozone in time, and then the detector confirmed: the carousel is ahead. In general, we waited out the aftermath of the emission for another day. The mutants then seemed to go crazy - the emission affects them differently, the Zone is unpredictable. No one really wanted to stick their heads out. Later, when we went back, we looked - the anomaly in the corridor had shifted, crawled towards the wall. We checked the corridor, and there was a dead end and an empty can of stew lying around. There was a sign on the wall, made with charcoal: "Hemul was here." The stalker, it seems, was trapped then, and when he managed to get out, he decided to have one last laugh at his pursuers. He was lucky that we were forced to wait it out like marmots in a hole, he got away from us, lucky guy. That's how it is. And here's the moral: luck is the first thing in the Zone, it's even more important than any skills or abilities. They won't save us now, because people with the same skills are chasing us, and there are twice as many of them. And we really need luck. We went deeper into the forest when I realized that the group was following the very path that Maly had mentioned. A real animal path - wide, the ground well-trodden by hooves and paws, the bushes along the edges broken. Animals probably often run along it, I would also like to know why here, where from and where to? We had walked about half a kilometer when the forest began to thin out, and a wide gap between the trees loomed ahead. Soon we found ourselves at the edge of a clearing, above which towered two high-voltage power line masts on widely spaced "legs". Torn wires swayed in the wind, clumps of rusty hair fluttered. It smelled of ozone. “Oh, you...” said Zakhar. - What’s wrong with him? The others were silent, looking at the clearing. It was overgrown with tall grass - unnaturally green and thick for this time of year. It was late autumn all around, the whole forest was yellow-red, and here it was the end of spring! Labus muttered something and spat - he didn't like such incomprehensible things in the Zone, they drove him crazy. "The detector is going off the scale," Marat said nervously. "There's too much electricity in the air." "Can we cross the clearing?" I asked, looking at the path that crossed the once-cut-down section of the forest. "It's long, the devil knows how long we'll have to go around it." - I don't know, I have a solid spot on the detector. Maybe Aidar will conduct... The dog was also noticeably nervous. “Big one, move back and come back when I whistle,” I ordered. The sniper disappeared behind the trees. The scientist hung motionless on Kostya's shoulder; Kostya did not lower him into the grass, but merely hunched over to make it easier to support his body weight. - Aidar, look. We need a passage, Aidar. - Marat patted the dog on the scruff of the neck and unfastened the leash. Aidar first tried to get out onto the path, but changed his mind and began to scurry along the edge of the clearing. We stood and waited, shifting from foot to foot. The special forces were following in our footsteps, the advantage we had gained was melting away with every second. Finally the dog stopped, stretched out his head as if trying to see something on the other side, and jumped to the base of the mast - once, twice, in long leaps. Only his grey head flashed above the lush grass. He ran between the supports of the mast, reached the edge of the forest on the other side without incident, turned and wagged his tail. - Forward, step by step! - I commanded. - Marat, Maloy, Labus behind me, then Zakhar, Bolshoy is keeping watch behind. Let's run. We need to cross the clearing as quickly as possible, we are a very good target for the shooters from the forest. If, while we are here, the pursuers reach the border and open fire, hiding behind the tree trunks... It is better not to think about it. I whistled for Big to come to us. - Stop! - shouted Maloy and grabbed Marat by the backpack. Aidar's whining was heard - he began to dart about, his tongue hanging out. The dog did not take his eyes off his master, but did not dare approach him without a command. Marat froze with his leg raised, leaning far back. The kid was holding him by the backpack - if it weren't for that, Marat would have fallen on his back. The shadow from the mast covered them, the wires swung over their heads, one often hit the long tongue of rusty hair, an unpleasant creaking sound reached their ears. “What’s there?” I asked quietly. "It seems we have a problem," Maloy answered without turning around. "Look above." I looked closer and finally understood what he was talking about: in front of Marat's head, almost right next to the mast, there was a cloud hanging, as if strung on a pole. It resembled the phenomenon I had seen near the electric arc near the helicopter - like the air trembling on a hot day over a heated roof or asphalt. What was it? A new kind of anomaly? Without turning my head, I looked around. A light breeze bent the grass in waves, and nothing else was moving around. The pursuers were getting closer, hanging around in the middle of this clearing was certain death. I looked at the anomaly again. It was billowing at head height. Aydar slipped under it calmly, and we, fools, looked at him and did not notice the danger. - Little one, put Marat on his back. Marat, relax. Big one, what's behind you? - It's clear for now, commander, - this word, "commander", slightly cut me... and probably the others too. After all, just recently they addressed another person like that. And not just a person - our friend. Who is no longer alive, and we were not even able to bury him properly. Now command of the group has passed to me, although I did not want it. While Maloy was busy with Marat, Labus was controlling the forest ahead, and Zakhar, I and Bolshoi were looking in the opposite direction. Finally, Maloy's whisper reached us: - Okay, let's continue. Just crawl. I turned around - Marat was crawling towards the opposite edge of the forest between the long "legs" of the mast, diverging from the trunk at an angle of forty-five degrees. Maloy squatted down, preparing to follow him. In principle, the effect of the anomaly could extend all the way to the ground, so I raised my hand, showing the others: stay put. It turns out that Marat became a stalker "master key" for us - we deliberately allowed him to risk his life instead of us. But there was no other way out, either he exposes himself to danger, or we all end up under the bullets of the pursuers. Marat is not a newbie, on whom we studied the seriousness of the threat, he is our equal and just happened to be ahead of everyone now. If I were there, I would crawl instead of him. He stood up when the mast with the trembling cloud was left behind. Only now did I realize that I hadn't breathed for probably half a minute - and I sighed with relief. Normal! He lowered his raised hand, showing the others that they could follow, and hissed at Maly's back: - Let's! Aidar wagged his tail: the owner was saved! He tried to lick him, but got a punch in the nose for wasting the anomaly. When Maloy passed the mast, I looked back at Labus. And I remembered: he has a scientist. We must somehow drag the wounded man under this cloud... He ordered: - Kostya, drag him by the legs, and I'll pull him up under the shoulders. Labus sat down on all fours and laid the "three hundred load" in the grass. I lay down in front. - Well? - he asked. - One, two... come on. They began to drag the body under the anomaly. "One, two, three!" I repeated, and we jerked it further. When we approached the mast, a hum was heard. We covered another meter - it became louder, heavier. The grass around moved in tiny waves, it resembled ripples on water. Heaviness fell upon us, a stabbing pain appeared in the back of my head. - Labus, crawl, - I said hoarsely. - Don't stop... “I’m crawling, I’m crawling,” he responded. We were already right under the pillar, from below it seemed that it towered up to the sky, furrowing the low-floating clouds with its top. The roar grew louder, it became really hard to breathe. - Come on! Come on! - I repeated like a clockwork toy. But we could have gone to the side, crossed the clearing along an arc, passing the air anomaly... But for some reason Aidar did not want to go the other way along the strange grass, he ran almost straight. So, something is wrong with this clearing, there are still some dangers on it that we do not see. And now, since Marat and Maloy remained safe, it is unlikely to hit down. The hum began to die down, the heaviness diminished. Time dragged on slowly, it seemed to me that it took fifteen minutes to drag the scientist, but according to the chronometer it was only two. Labus and I had exceeded the standard for transporting a wounded person to the battlefield with interest. Marat and Maloy were waiting for us by the trees, their weapons raised, watching to see if anyone would appear on the other side. Having reached the edge of the forest, I immediately sent Marat ahead along the path, and I and Maloy began to cover Zakhar and Bolshoi, who were crawling towards us. Labus at this time dragged the scientist to the nearest tree and laid him down under it. The trees near the high-voltage line looked even more bizarre than where the helicopter had fallen, the trunks twisted into spirals - some thick and squat, others long and narrow. It was good if this forest was passable at all. When I went on my first field trip during my military service as part of a regimental reconnaissance company, I understood very well what kind of forests we have, what kind of windfalls there are. And what it was like to fight your way through the forest not like some Indian, with a knife in your hand and a bow on your back, but when you have equipment and weapons on you - I also understood. Although back then there were normal, ordinary territories, in the Zone mutated plants sometimes create completely impassable barriers. Trees often emit poisonous juice, the stems of some types of grass can cut even the dense synthetic fabric of a vest or uniform. There are many legends among stalkers, including that there are man-eating trees in the Zone, but I have not met any yet, and no one on Yantar knows anything about this either. More tales, probably. Although I have heard about a strange anomaly called a puppeteer tree several times, and from people I trust. The big one was already crawling under the anomaly when on the other side, at the very edge of the clearing, figures began to flash. - Faster! - I hissed. - Here, come on! Having reached us, Big jumped up, and we rushed into the forest. - In the same order! - I commanded as I ran. Marat's figure loomed ahead. When we found ourselves among the trees, shots rang out from behind. The pear-pickers jumped out into the clearing, and we left them a very good landmark for passing through it, having plowed a wide path in the grass. As if we had marked the route on the map with a red dotted line. I was hoping that they would send the second dog ahead, it would run along the path, like our Aydar, and then the people would fly into the anomaly at full speed. That would buy us some time... But Davydov is an experienced commander, what if he notices that we were crawling under the mast? Although he might not realize it in the heat of the moment, they are not just walking there - they are in a hurry, pursuing us. I wonder who is commanding the third group? Nikolaev or Bobrov? It would be better if it was Nikolaev, he hasn't been in the Zone for long, less than a year, little experience, and there are rumors that he is someone's protege. If he is the one following us with his group, then most likely he won't work his ass off for the British flag, why would he be so zealous? But if it is Bobrov, then everything is much worse. The Bobrovites have been together for two years already, they have only had two losses in that time - professionals, may they all get the hiccups! How did they convince them to organize a hunt for their own? Of course - an order. It can't be a mistake, if the code for liquidation came, Davydov was obliged to send a request, the code should have been confirmed in OK... And after all, the special forces drank more than one canister of alcohol with us, we more than once backed each other up, carrying out joint tasks... The thoughts were interrupted by a bang, similar to the explosion of an offensive grenade. And then a scream came from behind. Aha - the pear guys are in trouble! Now, while they figure out what's what, while they find a new path or crawl along the old one - if that's even possible after the anomaly turned on for the first time - we'll have time to get to some shelter. I wonder what that cloud was hanging on the mast? How did it work, what just happened in the clearing? I'll have to describe it later in the report... What report?! - I immediately shouted at myself in my mind. What are you babbling about? The command betrayed you, set your former comrades on you! What can we do now, we're all the same as dead men, our lives aren't worth a penny, if the OKA decided to give us up. That's it, you're finished, Kurortnik, do you understand? You won't go on any vacation, and you won't see your mother and sister, and you won't get out of the Zone at all: they'll kill you here, and that's it. Because - where are you going now? To become a stalker? That's how they accepted you - you, a former military man. Both the OKA and they are your enemies now. And yet I could not believe that there was no way out. In the depths of my consciousness there lived a thought: a mistake, a misunderstanding, the main thing is to figure it all out, contact the command, explain something... On the road we started to come across gnawed bones of mutants - they were lying around the path and on it. What the hell is that? Soon the path ended, Marat and Maly, who had gone ahead, were waiting for us. “What’s the matter?” I asked, running up. Aidar sat quietly, his tongue hanging out. The instruments were clean. Marat pointed to the clearing ahead, and Maloy commented: - To the left of the pine tree, there’s something like an arch, do you see? I took a closer look, even pushed my glasses onto my helmet. Aha, there it is, far ahead: a squat caponier with a narrow embrasure, overgrown with moss. So... So we reached the military unit's farm. But what do these bones lying around mean? “What are your thoughts?” I asked. The boy pulled back the collar of his jacket and scratched his neck. - None yet. All these bones... it looks like someone was feasting here. Marat added: - And this caponier is not at all pleasing. “Do you think someone has settled here?” I asked skeptically. “We don’t think anything, commander, decide,” answered Maloy. I looked back, looked at the caponier again. This concrete thing is clearly a defensive fortification, as they are called in army circulars and orders. In principle, just what we need. There may be some passages there, possibly leading to a military unit... Although it is not a fact. What if someone is sitting inside and, like in a shooting gallery, shooting mutants running along the path? Then it is better for us not to go there, of course. However, the caponier seemed abandoned, and if the impression is correct, you can’t think of a better shelter for the group. - Marat, what's to the right? Marat had a good, powerful detector with a long range. He looked at the tablet, frowning. - You can't go any further to the right, there's something not very clear there. I see a strip of electromagnetic fields, in some places the temperature is pretty high... like an anomaly, but for some reason they're all hanging in one front. If we start looking for a passage, we'll lose our time advantage, even with Aidar. - On the left? - Three hundred meters free, and then the same thing. - So, to the left. Run! After my command, Marat sent Aidar ahead, and he trotted in front of us. Soon an interesting picture opened up: many broken young trees, as if they had been mown down by a giant. Older and stronger plants survived, but deep chips were visible on the trunks. I stopped, looking closely. All this led to unpleasant thoughts - boars could not rub against trees like that. What kind of animals did this here? Or was this some kind of special anomaly? I liked this place less and less, but two groups of special forces were catching up with us and there was no other way. Marat, who was running ahead, gave a sign to stop, Aidar crouched down, waiting for the command. Marat waved his hand and went on, now much more slowly. Kostya, who had been dragging the scientist until now, was breathing heavily, and I decided it was time to order Bolshoi to take the scientist. I had just managed to look back at Labus when a click was heard from the right. Everyone froze, the barrels turned towards the source of the sound. I saw a trench, overgrown with tall grass at the edges - its parapet seemed to me at first like a long hummock covered with bushes. The trench was about five meters from us, Marat was standing closest to it. Having moved slightly to the side, I made out part of the far wall, filled with concrete cracked by time. Not a bad shelter. Reminds me of the kind of crevice that is equipped for personnel to wait out a bomber raid... A slight buzzing sound was heard, similar to the work of servos. And then, above the caponier, the barrels in a pair climbed out of a hidden hatch. They stood upright, but as soon as the platform that raised them was fixed, they fell with a clang into the grips installed on the swivel. The little one exhaled: - SHVAK! That's right - it was a twin ShVAK, an aircraft cannon. Its twenty-millimeter shells shot down dozens of German planes during the Great Patriotic War - but how did it end up here, in our time? Before I could finish my thought, I heard a characteristic clanking sound that you can't confuse with anything - the belt being pulled. During my army youth, I heard enough of them in the turret of a combat vehicle to last a lifetime... - Atas! - shouted Maloy, and Marat jumped to the trench. - Big, Labus, follow me! - I shouted. Everyone rushed to the shelter. The trench wasn't that big, about three by one and a half meters, but it was almost as deep as a full height. The soldiers fell on top of each other, squashing each other's fingers and loudly banging their rifle butts on their helmets. From the outside, we probably looked like we had decided to all get into one bathtub to wash ourselves at the same time, fully clothed and with our gear. When the Big One fell on the wounded Zakhar, who was groaning in pain, a crash was heard from above. The rate of fire of this fool is about eight hundred rounds per minute. Such guns were installed on defensive turrets along the line of fortified areas during the Second World War. How many shells are in the belt? I did not remember, but, apparently, a lot. Surely boars come here to feed - and maybe not only them. Sit and wait until some animal comes under fire from ShVAKs. Scattered gnawed bones are better than any bait - the mutated creature follows the path, anticipating an imminent meal, runs into a motion sensor and ends up under fire from an automatic installation. Shells from such a gun will tear anyone to pieces - all the better for the predator. Already cut up pieces of meat fly around the area, as if on a platter, only have time to pick them up, drag them to the path and devour them there. And soon new creatures will appear at the smell, or one of their relatives will accidentally get into trouble, the pack will not disdain such things. Everything around was shaking, clods of earth were rolling across the concrete. Someone was twitching under me, someone's elbow was pressed into my back between the shoulder blades, and I was twitching too, pushing someone with my elbows and knees, and the space was vibrating, the roar of gunshots filled it - as if we were inside a drum, which was being pounded by a crazy drummer. The shots stopped as suddenly as they had started. My ears were ringing, and dim circles were spreading before my eyes. I asked hoarsely: - Is everyone alive? - I think that's it, - Labus groaned from somewhere to the side and below. - Don't crush my scientist! Why the hell did I drag him for so long? He's skinny as it is, and you're huge moose... Marat was in the worst situation, he found himself at the very bottom. Aidar was pressed against the wall, the dog was hanging between the bottom of the trench and Zakhar, whining. - Big... - I began, but he had already figured out what he needed to do. He pulled off his helmet, put it on the rifle barrel and raised it up. Silence. "We're in the gun's dead zone," he said. "Shall I look out, Commander?" - Don't rush, - I stopped him. - It's better to free Aidar and help him get up. We pushed the dog to the surface in several stages, and Big carefully stuck his head out of the trench. - So... run over there, - the sniper waved to Aidar several times, indicating the direction. The dog shifted from paw to paw, tried to stick his muzzle back into the trench, and threw earth from the parapet onto us. Labus, raising his head, tried to say something, and clods of earth got into his mouth. Kostya got angry and promised to execute our four-legged friend via mapupa. Aidar got scared and trotted towards the turret. Everyone held their breath. About fifteen seconds passed, and we heard a calling bark. The big one looked out of the trench again, took a closer look, and crawled out onto the parapet. Zakhar followed him, I helped Labus lift the scientist, then Maloy and Marat climbed out. No one dared to get up on their feet, they crawled to the guns. They looked around and quickly found a hatch covered with branches. Just as Big wanted to pull the bolt handle, the sound of servos was heard. We froze, Big grunted, Small cursed, Aidar barked briefly. The gun mount moved its barrels from side to side, raised them vertically and went back underground. The locks of the armor plates slid over them clicked. Everyone breathed a sigh of relief. All that was left was to hope that the hatch was not locked from the inside - it was unlikely to be taken out with a simple grenade, and we had very little plastic explosive. The big one took hold of the lever again and said: - Well, God bless you. He pulled. There was a grinding noise, the lever turned. The sniper pulled the hatch towards himself, and it creaked back onto the ground. I came closer and looked in. It was dark below. The big one looked at me, and the others followed suit. I hesitated. Finally Labus said: - Well, commander? The specialists are probably very close, decide. - Down, - I said. - Kid, go first. Extreme caution. The nimble, agile Little was better suited than the others to move ahead of everyone else in such a place. He slipped past Big, took a flashlight from his vest and disappeared into a well with brackets in the concrete wall.

Bear's group disappeared somewhere in the Chernobyl area. But they were well equipped, they took guns, supplies and equipment, and all seven of them were seasoned guys, they had been in the Zone for years. But they disappeared together with Bear himself, who was considered to be almost the best stalker on this side of Pripyat. - Well, check again, - I said. - Are there really two signals? We were lying in the bushes on a slope, below were ruins, a slanted one-story house and a water tower. Further on was Chernobyl, and beyond it the wide expanse of Pripyat. The city stood on a steep bank. The opposite bank, flat, was overgrown with lush greenery in the summer, but now everything there is brown, reddish and yellow-red: fallen leaves, earth and bare trees. And the small rivers that originate from Pripyat are blue. Peschanka, Rachya, Starik, Kamyshovaya... It's a pity you can't see the barge from here. There is a good barge there, big, powerful - I heard they started using it instead of a ferry even before the second accident, when they needed to transport multi-ton trucks, parts of tower cranes and armored vehicles across the river. Prigorshnya, my partner, pulled back his sleeve again. I hate to carry a PDA on my wrist, because a portable computer included in the local positioning system is obviously much larger and heavier than a regular watch. It irritates me, it always seems like something is stuck to my wrist, so I almost always carry the PDA in my backpack pocket. The partner turned on the map, looked closely at the small rectangular screen and said: - That's right, two are down there. And the motion sensor is silent. They're right there, Khimik, under that hill. Why can't you see them? And indeed - why? There is no one at the foot of the hill. And the silence is like in a morgue at night: deaf, dead. I raised myself up and moved my binoculars from side to side. A water tower, an almost intact one-story house, trees, ruins, an old military truck that has sagged onto its left side... Soldiers used to be transported in such trucks, but lately I haven't seen any - at least not on the move. Maybe it still works, maybe there's even gas in the tank? Although no, it's unlikely... I should probably light a cigarette. I was about to unbutton the flap of my breast pocket, but at the last moment I changed my mind. No, later, the situation was too uncertain. You should light a cigarette slowly, with pleasure, after you have finished some important task - and now it is just beginning. - Well, Chemist? - asked Prigorshnya. - How are things there? And then his gaze caught something unusual in the grass. More precisely, for the Zone - just normal, or rather, habitual. - Okay, I see one, - I said. - To the right of the tower. His clothes are dark green, so it's hard to see. But something's wrong with him. He tore himself away from the binoculars and glanced sideways at Prigorshnya. He nodded indifferently. - "Not so" - dead, then? Well, yes, that's what the marks show... Two motionless red marks appeared on the screens of our PDAs about fifteen minutes ago, when we were already approaching the hill. The Chernobyl area is one of the nastiest, stalkers do not like to wander here, because the computers work terribly here. It seems that the entire area is covered by some kind of anomaly, fundamentally different from others: it seems harmless to people, but it disrupts the work of electronics and jams communications. You can’t always understand what the computer is showing, the color of the mark, which denotes higher organics like a person, can change from green to red and back, as if the PDA carrier is constantly dying and then resurrecting; or it will suddenly go out, and a minute later it will appear again, but a kilometer away… - But it's not the Bear, that's for sure, - said the partner. - A regular signal, without any of these... additives. I nodded. The Smoker, our regular buyer, had supplied Bear with equipment for the expedition and had matched up seven of his guys. For this, Bear had promised half of the loot. But the Smoker didn't trust the stalker enough to let him go so easily, even if his guys were to accompany Bear. Equipment, weapons, supplies - all of this cost money. After all, Bear, after he got out of that scrape with the zombies - a large group, according to him, attacked him in the center of Chernobyl - crawled to the bar in only pants and a torn T-shirt, wounded... The girls who worked in the bar nursed him back to health, and that also meant expenses: food, medicine, bandages. Now it turned out that even the clothes in which Bear went on the job belonged to the buyer. In general, the Smoker was interested in the expedition, firstly, returning, and secondly, with loot. Besides the fact that he had invested a decent amount of money, there was also a very big jackpot in sight. After all, the Bear was going to find not just anything, but a field of artifacts, about which rumors had been circulating in the Zone for a long time. He swore, he swore that he had seen it when he was escaping from zombies, that the field was somewhere there, near Pripyat, and he would definitely find it again... The Smoker, of course, grabbed the bull by the horns and bargained with the Bear for half of the expected profit, although the stalker initially offered only ten percent. Prigorshnya and I were doing something else, so we didn’t go with them. Artifacts are my specialty, I would have definitely forced myself on the Bear as a fellow traveler, having brushed off one of the Smoker’s people, and the partner would have followed me, where would he go… But it didn’t work out. When we found ourselves in the bar, the buyer was already exhausted: The Bear isn’t responding, his guys aren’t either, go, Chemist and Prigorshnya, find them by all means, I trust you, you’re a real team, seasoned, you’ll go where those seven couldn’t go… And how he groaned, how he grieved when I said that for less than half of his half we wouldn’t budge. Then he started swearing, calling us bad names, but there was nothing he could do, he gave in. According to the most conservative estimates, the artifacts obtained from the field could be sold for a quarter of a million, and therefore, in the PDA that the Smoker gave to the Bear personally, a special microcircuit, a beacon, was secretly added to the stalker, which introduced an additional harmonic into the mark. An ordinary PDA does not inform others who its owner is, it only signals that someone is nearby, plus, if the owner dies, it sends a corresponding impulse. But now it was possible to determine from the Bear's computer that it was him. Although we didn't see the signals from his PDA. But we did see the other two... Taking off his cowboy hat, Prigorshnya rolled over onto his back and began to look at the low autumn sky. His partner was blond, with a scar on his forehead from a poorly treated wound, with six reddish dots - the marks of staples. - Well? - he asked for the umpteenth time, but I didn't react. He was still young and therefore fussy, overly energetic. And in the Zone you have to know when to run as fast as you can, shooting at random, and when to freeze and hold your breath, observing your surroundings. Now is the second case: is there anyone there, in the ruins, or not? In these, motherfucking ruins? - There's one body, but two signals. So, look for the second one. I nodded. The motion sensor was silent, and I didn't see anyone moving below either. I needed to get up and go down, but something was holding me back. - Let's go already, Chemist. I'm tired of lying here. - Well, my God, you're so restless! - I muttered angrily. - Be quiet for a bit, I'm doing some reconnaissance. - What? - he asked with interest, raising his head. - What are you producing? I just winced in response. He clearly lacks education. I started thinking more simply in the Zone, and my speech changed, became coarser. But still, I am, so to speak, an intellectual, I studied at the chemistry department, and my partner was drafted into the army right after school, where he ended up in the paratroopers. They trained him there, of course. He said he had even been in hot spots. Since then, the skill has not gone anywhere and has saved our lives more than once. We need to go, it seems calm. Although why are there two signals, but only one body? It's not clear... What the hell is this? I rose to my knees, so abruptly that Prigorshnya became worried. - What? What is it, Chemist? At a height of one and a half meters, a man was hanging with his back to the tower. I wouldn't have been so surprised if, say, a crowbar had been sticking out of his belly, with which some strongman had pierced both his body and the brickwork - but no, he was simply hanging there, like an insect stuck to a flytrap on a chandelier. Lowering the binoculars, I glanced at Prigorshnya. He was already kneeling next to me, leaning forward slightly, his head bowed and the butt resting on his shoulder. The barrel moved smoothly from side to side. My partner had not just a simple weapon, but a whole “machine-grenade launcher system” called “Groza”, which could be turned into an assault rifle, a carbine, and a grenade launcher, and from the first to the last - with just a simple switch. On his left side under his arm hung a compact “Kiparis”, though with a broken laser designator, and on his belt on the right - a “Beretta”. “What’s there?” he asked tensely, not lowering the Thunderstorm. “There’s a man hanging from the tower,” I explained. - Ah! - Prigorshnya exhaled after a pause. - I see. So it's a dead man! - Well, yes, - I agreed. - A dead man, however. - Wow! You said "hanging." I thought he was hanging, holding onto something, but he... What did they nail him with, I can't figure it out? He lowered his machine gun and sat down, tucking his legs under him. He looked at me questioningly. Corpses are a common thing for the Zone, there is nothing surprising about that. I understood why my partner was in a hurry: not only because of his natural fussiness, but also because he needed to examine everything before dark and get out of here as quickly as possible. - Okay, - I said. - Screw you, let's go. He immediately jumped up. I stood up too; we began to descend, carefully, without taking our eyes off the house, the ruins and the tower. It was not as badly damaged as the buildings nearby, only the roof was blown off, the twisted beams were visible. But, in general, it was almost whole, and the doors were closed, perhaps even locked. - Why are you so nervous all the way? Taking a few more steps, I answered: - This is some kind of rotten business. - Why suddenly? People got into trouble, we need to help... And earn a little money ourselves... "A little"... We were counting on raising about a hundred thousand from this operation if everything worked out. By my standards and my partner's, it was a huge sum, we had never seen anything like it in our lives. It would have been enough for the "all-terrain armored car universal" that Prigorshnya had long dreamed of, and there would have been some left over. “So what’s rotten?” he repeated. We walked slowly, weapons raised. At the foot of the hill, nothing moved, no movement at all. - Why did the Bear come to the bar from the Dump instead of from Chernobyl? - I asked. - Well, he probably got lost... He could barely stand on his feet and was thinking poorly because of the poison from his rusty hair. - Lost... Okay, but you still haven't asked yourself this question: why didn't the Bear really remember where the artifact field was located? - It arose. And the Smoker arose. He told me that he asked the Bear. - What are you talking about? What a perceptive man our Smoker is... And what did the Bear answer? - He said: he was half-delirious when he was running away from the zombies, they managed to tangle up the Bear, and he also got caught in a thicket of rusty hair, and they bit him. The girls at the bar told me that he really was all red, in a rash and blisters. That's not counting the wounds and abrasions. He suffered a lot during the first few days, and only then did he recover. We went down to the middle of the slope. I am of average height, and Prigorshnya is almost a head taller than me, so he had a better view. But I didn’t notice any dangers either. A flat meadow below, the remains of barns, a tower with a house and a truck. And that’s it. And no problems. However, when we had already passed two-thirds of the distance to the goal, I suddenly felt a chill - as if invisible fingers with long claws were running over my body, from top to bottom. I almost screamed. “What’s wrong with you?” whispered Prigorshnya when I stopped abruptly, blindly waving the barrel of my machine gun in front of me. - Don't wait! - I almost barked. - There's something there. - Come on, come on. There's nothing. Without asking permission, he took the binoculars from my belt and looked around. “Yeah, it’s empty,” Prigorshnya concluded after a while. I have a lot of bags on my belt and two special containers, a bigger one and a smaller one. While I was getting the nut with a piece of bandage on it, the binoculars rose higher: my partner was looking over the Chernobyl houses at Pripyat. “The barge is standing there,” he muttered. “Explain to me, Andryukha, why is it standing there?” Prigorshnia is the only one in the Zone who knows my real name: Andrey Nechayev. Earlier, Ivan Pistolet and Vitya Crazy Fist knew, they were the first buyers who settled here. But they are long gone, one shot himself, and the other went crazy. "Stop, be quiet," I ordered, took aim and threw the nut to the foot of the tower. And then, to the left, the second, and then the third - but to the right, towards the motionless body in the grass. No, there was nothing there. The nuts fell normally, and the trajectory was normal for everyone. So what's the matter? I even squatted down because I started to feel sick, my insides twisted. And I was shivering, my fingers on the machine gun were shaking a little. - What's wrong with you, Chemist? - asked Prigorshnya, finally becoming serious. - Is it really that bad? But it's empty there, I can tell you for sure! These corpses... so what - corpses? There's a skeleton hanging under the chandelier in the Smoker's bar. That's not an indicator. I straightened up carefully, keeping my eyes on the area below. Sometimes my premonitions deceived me. Or was I getting sick? I caught a cold, wandering through the autumn forest and crawling on my belly on the bare ground... And yet I stamped my feet in one place, continuing to dart my gaze around the area, until my partner pleaded: - Listen, Chemist, we'll be going down like this for three days. How long can this go on? We'd have found that Bear or that field by now, we'd be sitting at the Smoker's stash and drinking vodka... He was, of course, exaggerating: it would be a long way to the bar, and we wouldn't make it by tomorrow morning. Not to mention that it's still not clear where the Bear is, we might have to look for him here for two days. But perhaps I'm being too cautious. We need to go, especially since it seems to have eased, I don't feel sick and the chills have gone. I said: - You know, I don’t believe in any artifact fields. - What? How can you not believe me? - he became worried. - No, they don't exist, most likely, they're legends, you understand? Any such... such a place, - I waved my hand, - like the Zone - quickly becomes overgrown with legends and all sorts of superstitions. Because our life depends not only on skill and caution, but also on luck. That's where all the tales about the field of artifacts, the Masters of the Zone, the Black Stalker, the Monolith come from... - The Monolith is not a fairy tale, - he objected offendedly. - You're saying the same. What, and the Doctor, in your opinion, is a fairy tale? And the Masters of the Zone - they definitely exist! - Of course, the Doctor is no fairy tale, he has cured so many. And I only saw him once. But the rest... Oh, well, what's the point of arguing with you, an ignoramus! The truck was parked with its back to us and its front to the ruins. Green awning, green cabin. The paint was peeling, all in cracks and reddish spots of rust, the driver's door was slightly open. We passed by, looking inside just in case. No one, of course, although I would not have been surprised to find a zombie or just the skeleton of a driver in a cap and with a holster on his side, clutching the steering wheel with bony fingers. But near the front wheel lay human bones and two skulls, one crumpled, almost crushed. Even the birds did not chirp here and the flies did not buzz. Having reached the tower, we examined the hanging body. "Coyote," said Handful. It was indeed him - a skinny, reddish stalker with a freckled face. He hung like Buratino in Karabas Barabas's room: his head drooped on his chest, his limbs hanging limply. His face was distorted, as if something had scared Coyote just before his death... scared him to death. A PDA was visible on his right wrist, and there was a wet puddle on the ground under his body. Nikita stepped closer, carefully grabbed his boot and pulled. And then he jumped back: with a dull smack, Coyote peeled himself off the masonry and collapsed under the tower. The partner snorted and spat into the grass. The dead man's back began to flow like butter in the heat - something was glistening and bubbling there, trembling softly... We quickly backed away from the body, because as soon as it fell, a specific sweetish smell began to spread around. “Have you seen anything like this before?” Nikita asked, for some reason whispering. I shook my head: - No. But tissues shouldn't decompose like that, that's nonsense. Still backing away, we passed the closed door of the building and only then turned our backs to the dead man. I hardly knew the coyote, I had met him several times at the Smoker's - a stalker like any other stalker, a regular vagabond of the Zone. But my soul still felt uneasy, somehow dreary. Here was a man - and then he was gone, and he died a terrible death, judging by his distorted face. His PDA would give a signal until it discharged, and then the mark would go out along with the human life. “I don’t even feel like looking at anyone else now,” admitted the partner. Nevertheless, we approached the second body and began to examine the corpse in silence. It was impossible to say whether the deceased had experienced the same horror before his death as Coyote or not. Because his face, like the rest of his body, was either eaten away or half dissolved by some kind of acid. The skin had peeled off in shreds, scraps of clothing had eaten into the flesh, as if mixing with it. Only the belt with the buckle, which shone as if it had just been polished, the boots, the holster and the PDA on his wrist, which resembled a reddish-brown stick, remained intact. "Do you recognize him?" asked Prigorshnya. - No. - Probably one of the Smoker's guys too. Okay, you wait, and I'll have a look around the house. Slightly bending over, he walked towards the one-story house. His partner's movements changed, became ingratiating, restrained and precise. His feet in army boots silently touched the ground - and it was hard to believe that this big guy could move like that. Nikita hung the Groza behind his back, took out the Cypress and held it out in front of him, squeezing it with both hands. I myself only have a PEM in a holster, and also a Kalashnikov, I need to finally buy something cooler... This Groza is a good weapon after all. And expensive, but I did not spare the money, because now I feel safe next to my partner. Well, relatively, as much as it is generally possible in the Zone. He paused in front of the half-open door, listening, then pushed it with the barrel, crouched down, and swung forward. He glanced at me, waved his hand, and disappeared inside. I waited, moving away from the corpse, studying the ruins and the truck. Prigorshnya appeared soon - "Cypress" hanging under his arm, long arms swinging relaxed along his body. He smiled with the corner of his lips. Well, that's good, then. While he was walking towards me, I lowered the barrel and squatted down. Still, despite his fussiness, I couldn't find a better partner. Prigorshnya and I have been walking around the Zone for over a year now and during this time we have become friends, and our friendship is the kind where I can rely on him, and he on me, in everything. I know he will cover for me. He knows I won't ditch him, I will share the profits in a brotherly way. He is a marksman, and I... I am a chemist. No, of course, I can shoot, too, but, in general, I am not the coolest fighter. Glory to the Black Stalker, I have other advantages. - The eye is there, - he said, passing by. - And there's nothing else. I'm afraid to go near it, to hell with it. You go ahead, I'll take a look around some more. And he headed around the tower, rustling the grass. And I asked after him, confused: -Eye? He didn't turn around - he must have been secretly enjoying the effect it produced. An eye! A rare thing. And very expensive. But I still won't sell it, it will be useful for other things... Stop. Don't share the skin of an unobtained artifact, it's a bad omen. I entered the house. Probably some guard used to live here. Like a lighthouse keeper, only he kept an eye on the water tower. Now, of course, there is no one for a long time: the places around are remote, wild, only zombies and controllers wander around here alone. The room is completely empty, only the remains of a chair and a rusty bucket in the corner. There are still pieces of wallpaper on the walls, and all the plaster has fallen from the ceiling, the laths are visible. In the middle of the room, about a meter above the floor, hung a pale green-blue oval the size of a fist, the shape of which actually slightly resembled the eye. Semi-transparent, dense in the center, and sparse at the edges, so that the opposite wall was visible through it. It consisted of something semi-material: it seemed to be both substance and energy. I walked around it, almost touching the walls with my shoulder. The Geiger counter was silent. The air trembled slightly, the artifact was surrounded by a ball of barely noticeable shimmer, in which sparks sometimes danced. Squinting, I squatted down. Well, I'll be damned: the fluffy cloud that enveloped my eye had a "leg", resembling a huge dandelion that had sprouted from the floor. As I approached the artifact, I heard a barely perceptible buzzing - not even a buzzing, but a humming, like a whistle with a thin rattling. Taking the container off my belt, I stepped closer, then even closer, and finally found myself right next to the eye. It hung at waist height, I bent down, looking into the center. And I saw there, quite clearly, a picture: a dugout surrounded by dense thickets, a small fire burning nearby, a man in a camouflage jacket squatting in front of it. Two short spears, an iron rod, a pot... The stranger was stirring it with a stick. I saw all this from a height of about three meters. The head suddenly rose, turned sharply - left, right, and then the man stared up, straight at me, so that I even recoiled, recognizing Hemul, a stalker known in our area. Hemul also recoiled, fell on his back, kicking his legs and catching the pot. I couldn’t make out what happened next, but it seemed that one end of the rod had come loose from the fork and the pot had tipped over into the fire. There were no sounds; the picture inside the artifact had faded, as if it had been covered in gray steam. The last thing I saw was Hemul sticking his head out from somewhere to the side with a pistol, from which he began to fire into the sky. I recoiled: for a moment it seemed that he was about to hit me right in the forehead. But nothing happened, the bullets did not fly out of the eye into the ceiling. But when I looked again, the picture had disappeared. That’s how it was. I wonder if there was an eye hanging above him, too, which Hemul hadn’t noticed before? Surely there was something, it wasn’t empty air that scared him… When we meet, I must remind him of this incident and ask what he saw. In any case, the eye was not a threat to me, and I, having become bolder, unfastened the container lock from the belt. The tightly fitted lid shifted heavily in the grooves, revealing the metal inside. I moved the container under the eye and lifted it up so that it was inside. Holding it from below with one hand, I closed the lid with the other. I stepped back. The eye floated through the wall. Damn! I stood there, staring at it in confusion. No, it wasn't really floating anywhere, it was just hanging there in the same place... Or had it moved a little? That's right - it had moved a little, and now it was coming back, swaying like a flower head on a stem... What should I do? I stepped back a little and squatted down again. I narrowed my eyes. There it was, the "stem". It had bent when I moved the container, but now it had straightened up. What if I used the crystal? Would it be able to chop off that leg, cut it? Yes, perhaps... Although - it itself is rare and very expensive, is it worth losing another artifact for the sake of one? And then, if the crystal and the eye start to interact somehow... I haven't tried combining them yet and I don't know what might happen. But if you think about it, the eye is more valuable. A smoker, for example, would pay about one and a half times more for it than for a crystal. I pressed my palm to the second, small container hanging on my belt and then remembered that the artifact we had recently obtained near the abandoned farm, having previously gutted two zombies, was now with Prigrshnya. Where is it, by the way, why is it so quiet outside? Worried, I stood up, backed away from the eye, experiencing an irrational reluctance to turn my back on it, and left the house, raising my machine gun. And he lowered it with relief, seeing his partner, who must have managed to go around the tower during this time and was now trying to unlock the door. He bent down, picking at the crack with an army knife, which he had traded with a soldier for a case of vodka and five cans of condensed milk, which we had gotten last winter from an abandoned warehouse not far from Agroprom. I headed towards Prigorshnya, and in the meantime he, moving the blade, yanked the handle and broke something with a crunch - either a bolt or a lock tongue. The door opened. Prigorshnya stuck his head inside - and screamed. The scream was so wild that at first it stunned me: I had never heard a person scream like that before. My partner jerked back, slipped, fell on his back and crawled, still screaming. And I was confused - I froze, instead of grabbing the machine gun and opening fire over Prigrshnya, into the dark doorway. After all, something was going to jump out from there now... And then I was overcome again, and more powerfully than on the slope: as if an invisible hand had reached into my chest, squeezed, compressed my heart. I gasped, fell to my knees, and seemed to scream too, although I myself did not hear the scream. It was climbing out of the tower. And Prigorshnya was already crawling on all fours, not looking back, and screaming, opening his mouth like a big O. When he and I were separated by several meters, I was finally able to cope with the horror that seemed to have flooded my entire body. My arms were shaking, my legs were buckling, but I somehow stood up, pressed the Kalashnikov to my left side, and opened fire. Everything around me rumbled and rattled. Prigorshnya suddenly appeared very close. - Don't shoot, it's no use! - he yelled right into my ear. - I'm moving! I stopped shooting; he grabbed my shoulder, jerking me so hard I almost fell, and ran away. We ran, looking back every now and then, and it was clear that there was no point in running: it was moving much faster, spreading from the tower in a wave, swelling, catching up... Horror rolled ahead, like a shock wave, filling the brain with blind panic. - We won't make it! - I yelled. - No, stop, where are you going... But Nikita had already jumped into the cabin, pulling me in with him, and rolled over to the seat next to the driver’s. - The key! There is a key… The key was indeed sticking out of the ignition. While I was getting in, Prigorshnya turned it. The engine rumbled. So it was on the move, this truck - our salvation, because it was - already very close, already covered the dead man in the grass, and his body trembled, foamed red, dissolving, spreading over the ground... I shook, huddled on the seat, stamping my feet under the steering wheel, missing the pedals: finally I got there, jerked the gear stick and stepped on the gas so that the truck jerked from its place at the very moment when it rolled up to the rear wheels.

The forester was coordinating the buckshot. He carefully laid the buckshot into the cartridge case in even columns and pushed matches between them, sprinkling them with starch to be sure. Good starch, yesterday the forester had cooked blueberry jelly with it. Methodically collecting cartridge after cartridge, the stalker listened. It was quiet in the evening forest. High above the bait - the carcass of a young elk tied to a branch - a crow cawed. The carcass lay on a sloping platform at the bottom of the slope, a sagging rope stretched from it to a tree, there was a hole below. The sun had set, the shadows of the trees faded. Twilight was thickening. Soon the pseudo-dogs would go hunting. The hut made of spruce branches, the Forester's ambush, stood to the side of the "wolf reel", the steep slope and the bait were clearly visible from the shelter. The carcass smelled noticeably, but this did not bother the stalker. He inserted two cartridges into the barrels, the rest were laid out on the shelf in front of him. Everything was ready for a successful hunt. The Forester set up an ambush near the line he had calculated, risking his own skin. A large pack of mutants lived further on, but all but the largest would be afraid to cross the border of the territory. How many of them would there be, brave or hungry? Having found a place free of anomalies, he dug a hole for a couple of creatures, right under the slope. Having finished, he set up a dense palisade along the earthen walls. The knots on the stakes stuck out downwards to prevent the beast from getting out of the hole. Of course, a mutant is not a wolf, what do some knots matter to him? But that is why the Forester is sitting here with his trusty double-barreled shotgun. The inclined flooring was carefully rubbed with lard, a tied carcass lay on it, the bottom of the pit was studded with thorns. The animal, having sensed the elk, would climb down the steep slope and roll down the flooring. Everything seemed to be in order, and even the wind was favorable, but something was bothering the stalker. In the Zone, it is impossible to foresee everything, hunting is a dangerous business, but the skins of rare spotted pseudo-dogs with red stripes on their tails are very well paid. But money is needed. The forester touched the right side of his chest with his fingertips. It already hurt to hold the double-barreled shotgun to his shoulder, aiming... It was getting dark quickly. The shelter smelled strongly and spicy of pine needles, fallen leaves and damp earth, moss rustled under his knees. The forester smoothed his disheveled dark beard with threads of gray, thoughtfully shook the barrel of an old double-barreled shotgun... And then a howl was heard in the distance - the pack was calling to each other before the hunt. He knelt down, thrusting his gun through a crack in the prickly branches, and stood motionless, looking along the trunks. He could sit motionless like that for hours, waiting for game. The pack was getting closer. It moved left and right, but finally the howling and barking merged into a hungry hubbub that quickly grew louder: the pseudo-dogs had scented the bait and were racing straight for the Forester. He aimed his gun at the top of the hill, his finger on the trigger tensed. The metal under his skin was already warm, the Forester felt its pleasant warmth. The branches crackled, the young fir tree twitched, and animals appeared between the trees. The heart began to pound in the chest, sharply and angrily. No matter how experienced a hunter you are, adrenaline is released into the blood at the sight of prey. The stalker silently inhaled the cold evening air through his nostrils. The hill was the border. The first, the largest mutant stopped abruptly at the slope, paws dug up the earth. Ropes of saliva hung from the bared mouth, eyes burned with hunger and rage. The aroma of stale meat hit the nose, the carcass was very close - but outside the pack's territory! "Come on!" the Forester said mentally. "Jump! Take him!" The beast's shoulder blade was in the sights, but the stalker did not want to scare off the pack. Next to the first pseudo-dog, a second one stopped, then a third... The mutants began to rush about and howl from impotence. Here one of the young ones, unable to stop, rolled down the hill. The claws scraped the ground, the beast snapped its teeth, trying to grab the prey, but caught only air - and with a confused cry fell from the flooring into the pit. The forester blinked. This greenhorn ruined everything! The pack stamped their feet in confusion, listening to the offended squeal coming from the trap. They were scared, damn them! Now they won't go near the bait. The stalker could see the mutants well, they were circling between the trees on the top of the hill. The smell of carrion attracted them, but their instinct didn't allow them to leave their territory, and then there was their brother, who had disappeared into who knows where... He waited a minute, then another, and realized that the hunt had been ruined. Because of some stupid wolf cub! The forester rose heavily, resting his head on the spruce branches, and then one of the mutants jumped. It seemed to be a leader. The pseudo-dogs retained something of the wolf's habits - they began to eat carrion from the belly, and this was the principle of the trap called "wolf reel". A deep hole was dug under the hill, and the carcass was hung on a steep slope. The animal jumped over the bait to pounce on the food from the belly, and rolled into the hole on a slippery, lard-rubbed flooring. The forester bared his teeth when he saw the mutant fall through a gap between the spruce branches. But several more followed the leader. The pack is small, but it should be enough to fill the hole and crush the first beast - a large and very expensive one. The stalker jumped out of the hut, raising his double-barreled shotgun to his shoulder. Down, flailing his paws, flew a young pseudo-dog, followed by a large, broad-headed female sliding down the flooring, scratching the ground and howling. The whining of two mutants caught in a trap poured out towards them. Almost without aiming, the Forester fired from one barrel and immediately from the other - a pair of pseudo-dogs fell at the top. The young one and the female plopped into the trap. Cursing, the stalker quickly reloaded the gun and both charges of buckshot, which he had been laying for so long, were fired into the chest of the seasoned beast. It pressed itself to the ground, preparing to jump, the iron hair on its withers stood on end... It never jumped, it fell on its side. The pack began to scurry about. Some runt, whining, lay down on his belly on the grass and rode head down to the moose carcass, carefully moving his paws. The slope was steep, the mutant began to twitch almost immediately, tried to crawl back, but couldn’t and rolled down the hill. The forester loaded the double-barreled shotgun again and raised it to his shoulder. This is the last one – in the trap, not counting the young animals, there will be three large animals, for which he can get enough to... The stalker pressed his cheek to the butt, pressed one trigger. And then he was twisted. The pain flared like fire, burned through his chest. He was a healthy man, albeit an old man, with the powerful sloping shoulders of a wrestler, a sinewy neck, a broad chest - and yet he was twisted into a knot. There was a thunderclap, a shot went into the air. His hand was cramped, the finger on the trigger was twisted by the falling gun, and the Forester fell sideways into the moss. The runt fell into the pit, and an offended whine came from there. A juicy blow with a paw, a short, almost human cry - and there was a rustling sound at the edge of the pit. The forester raised his head with an effort, looked around the trap with a dull look. The first attack passed, the pain weakened, and the stalker felt the grass, wet with dew, tickling his neck. It was almost dark, in the twilight he saw a black silhouette with red spots in front of him. A healthy mutant, too big even for a leader, climbed out over the bodies of his fellows and crouched down, ready to pounce. The stalker squinted. The double-barreled shotgun was lying only a meter away, but to get it, he had to move – and provoke the pseudo-dog. And if he lay still, the beast might not attack. After all, he was now on foreign territory... The leader growled. The forester felt rather than saw how his paws tensed, how the muscles flowed under his spotted skin... He jumped. The stalker rolled away, picked up the gun and jumped to his knees, taking aim. The beast, having lost its victim, rolled along the ground, leaving deep furrows. The huge head turned, the jaws opened. The fangs flashed in the light of the rising moon. Without a single sound, the mutant turned and rushed at the man. The forester fired. The pseudo-dog howled and fell three steps away from him and immediately rose. The forester grabbed the rifle by the butt, straightened up, raising it like a club. Blood was flowing from the pseudo-dog's neck. Its teeth snapped at the man's throat, the stench from its mouth hit his nostrils. The forester barely managed to retreat and brought the butt down on the shaggy head with a wide swing. The mutant fell, twitching its paws. The forester stepped toward a spreading spruce, leaned his back against the trunk, inhaled and exhaled several times, wincing from the pain in his chest. He quickly loaded his gun. There were six cartridges with matched buckshot and a dozen regular ones left in the cartridge belt. The stalker shot the pseudo-dog in the head as it tried to rise, and it went quiet. The pack on the hill, which had been squealing cowardly during the fight, began to howl. - So you, - said the Forester, turned his back on the dead leader and wandered towards the trap. In the pit the captured animals were whining, scraping their sides, two young ones and a large bitch. They would give for the spotted female as for the leader - after all, she was a little smaller than him, probably a mate - and for the runts they would give... The howling grew louder, and the Forester turned to listen. Damn it! He had spent too long with the pseudo-dogs in neutral territory - a pack from the neighboring area, hearing the noise, was running here. The mutants were close, they would be here in a couple of minutes. He looked around - Moonlight illuminated the forest, long shadows of trees stretched across the grayish grass.
