It became colder, wisps of steam flew out of his nostrils. It smelled of fallen leaves and dog hair. The pack on the hill came to life and barked loudly. How many of them were left? The forester didn't even try to make out in the darkness. He quickly returned to the grove where the hut stood and the dead leader lay. An ambush is an unreliable shelter; the angry animals will tear it down and not notice it, and the person inside will be trampled and torn to pieces. Therefore, the forester didn't even stop near the hut, but went straight to the tall, spreading spruce, throwing his gun over his shoulder. The branches bent and cracked, but he climbed quickly, and in a minute he settled in a fork about two meters above the ground. He did not climb higher, so that it would be easier to shoot the mutants. Although the Forester was expecting them, the pseudo-dogs that ruled this area still appeared unexpectedly. Emerging from the bushes into the moonlit clearing, they surrounded the animal's corpse. Two young ones, growling, pounced on the still warm body. The second pack, left without a leader, circled around the hill. These animals were ordinary, not spotted. Gray skins and tails cost pennies - no point in bothering. The largest mutant bit one of the yellow-mouthed ones, it howled, the spotted ones on the hill responded in a discordant chorus. They no longer tried to get the bait, but the owners of the area smelled it. The leader gave a signal, the young ones dropped their dead brother, and the mutants turned their muzzles to the carcass of the moose. "Just don't fall into the trap!" growled the Forester, raised his gun and fired twice. The largest mutant fell into a puddle of moonlight. Both packs howled. The wounded beast picked up its hind legs, trying to stand up, but fell face down in the grass. The forester pressed the butt to his shoulder, aiming for the next victim. The animals on the hill raged, growling, scurrying along the invisible boundary of the site. And then the second attack began. The stalker pressed himself against the barrel, which smelled acridly of resin, and grabbed the dropped gun with his left hand. His right hand was seized by a cramp, his fingers curled into a fist, his nails dug into his skin. Blood began to flow down his wrist. Somehow throwing the strap over his shoulder, he grabbed the barrel with his healthy arm and legs. His head was spinning, his chest was eating away as if with acid, and the blood was pulsing in his temples. Everything went dark before his eyes. The grays had smelled him and were now scurrying below, jumping up and down. Their teeth were clicking right next to their boots. The pseudo-dogs on the hill couldn't hold out - first one, then another slid down the slope, followed by the remaining six or seven. Before the Forester could get scared for the trap, the first beast jumped onto the moose carcass, pushed off - and in a long jump swung over the hole. The others followed his example, one after another. Pseudodogs viciously attack anyone who intrudes on their territory, but rarely wander into each other's territories; all their wrath usually falls on stalkers - that is probably why the packs have been hesitant until now. Now instinct has prevailed, and the animals have rushed at the strangers. The owners of the plot, their grey fur bristling and their teeth bared, rushed into battle. The forester froze in an awkward position. Beneath him, the pseudo-dogs tore at each other's throats, rolled on the ground, scattering leaves, growled and howled. Leaning against a rough tree trunk, he cradled his cramped right hand. The pain slowly receded. It seemed that the attacks were becoming more frequent. The disease would take him to the grave before he had saved up the money needed for the operation... When the Forester grabbed a thick branch, his palm became sticky from the smelly resin. After the second attack, he always felt weak, now he could fall before the creatures could gnaw each other. And it was unknown what was worse - to break his neck or to end up in the thick of enraged mutants. The cramp finally passed, although his trembling fingers still wouldn't bend. Overcoming the pain, the Forester wound the rifle strap around his right hand, somehow reloaded, lowered the barrels and fired without aiming. His vision was clouded by a murky haze, but it was hard to miss the jumble of bodies below. A loud squeal was his answer - the stalker had hit. He took a deep breath and settled down more comfortably. Weakness rolled over him, but the Forester could still hold on. With shaking hands he reloaded the gun and fired, then again. The moon brightly illuminated the clearing, and when the veil before his eyes cleared, the stalker began to aim more carefully. The coordinated buckshot, which gave the best accuracy of the battle, was gone, only the usual charges remained. The animals were killing each other, and he was sitting there, his legs wrapped around the trunk, slowly loading the double-barreled gun and shooting. The wind blew, a pine cone flew in from above, flew past his face, scratching his cheek. The forester swung on the branch, almost letting go of the gun. Another cartridge twisted out of his trembling fingers and flew away after the pine cone. The last pseudo-dog from the spotted pack fell dead. The fight was over, and the owners of the territory froze around the tree, their muzzles raised. The forester reached for the next cartridge. His chances of staying alive were minimal – the animals would tear him to pieces, he couldn’t shoot them all. He ran his fingers over the cartridge belt. Five cartridges. And the animals? One, two... eight mutants. True, most of them were young, but the closest to the spruce were three seasoned males who could even jump... Without wasting any more time on thinking, the Forester raised his gun and took careful aim. You can't take out such a mutant with regular buckshot. It would be good if it worked with two shots. He pulled both triggers. The forester swayed, the branch cracked under him. A huge pseudo-dog jumped under the spruce – and silently fell to the ground. Ready. He singled out and killed the second one. One last bullet remained... The mutants jumped, howling loudly. The stalker picked up his legs, found the youngest one and shot. The beast fell, whining, twitching its paws. The forester hoped that he had broken its spine, and the greenhorn was not dangerous, would not rush as soon as he landed on the ground. The branch was cracking, there was no point in climbing to another one – he wouldn’t be able to sit here all night, and the animals would easily lie in wait until the morning. There were four of them left, all males. Now he could only rely on the strength of his arms and agility. Although the weakness still hadn’t passed... The forester threw his gun hard at the nearest pseudo-dog and slid to the ground. His vision went dark. He moaned through his teeth and leaned against the tree trunk. Two of them jumped on him at once, the stalker threw his hand with a knife in front of him and plunged the blade up to the hilt into the neck of one, dodging the teeth of the other. The effort was too great - he fell on his side. Teeth snapped where a human throat had just been, and the mutant crashed his muzzle into the trunk. Sliding down it, he rolled away and whined, shaking his head. And the stalker found himself face to face with the last member of the pack. No, not the last one – on the other side, growling, another one stood up, blood flowing from his muzzle. The forester groped at his belt, not finding the second knife. Where is it... nowhere, but there, literally a step away, lies a gun... The two mutants jumped at the same time. The stalker rolled onto his side, stretched out his hand. The beasts collided above him and fell, locked in a clash. Paws squirmed across his body, tearing at his rough leather jacket with their claws. Gritting his teeth, the Forester raised his gun and struck without looking. The butt hit the ridge, it crunched, the powerful heavy male went limp on the stalker, pressing him to the ground. The second pseudo-dog growled, the Forester pushed the corpse off himself and sat down with a double-barreled shotgun in his hands. Right in front of him was a gaping dark mouth. The enraged beast jumped... Later, the Forester could not explain how and why he did it. Everything happened by itself. The blued barrels of the old double-barreled shotgun went deep into the throat. Surprised eyes, a heavy wheeze... The beast fell on its side, the stalker stood up, pressing down, pinned it to the ground. The male wheezed, trying to wriggle free, but the Forester pressed so hard that his ears began to buzz and circles began to crawl before his eyes. Something smacked in the mutant's throat, tore, crunched... He thrashed - and gave up the ghost - the Forester rose, resting his foot on the corpse, pulled out the double-barreled shotgun, found a knife and went to finish off the wounded pseudo-dogs. Only after making sure that everyone was dead, he allowed himself to sit down near the spruce, lean against the trunk - and briefly lose consciousness.

It was not possible to pass between Yantar and the Wild Territories without losses. Mazai quickly escaped the pursuit thanks to secret paths and Zharka, who turned up at the right time, but in the Wild Territories he was attacked by two mercenaries. Either they coveted the weapon and equipment of a lone stalker, or they got bored... As a result, both were forever left in a ravine overgrown with nettles, and Mazai received a bullet in his sore leg. At first, he wanted to go further north, towards Radar and Limansk, but now he decided to turn indeserted quiet places to the northwest of Yantar, find a cache and sit it out, heal the wound. The bullet entered the fleshy part of the thigh shallowly, Mazai easily picked it out with a knife, bandaged the wound. There was little blood, but the wound affected his general well-being. Although the stalker was accustomed to limping, his speed of advancement had noticeably decreased; in the last hour he had covered no more than a couple of kilometers - and yet the terrain was flat and almost without anomalies. He passed a freight railway station overgrown with weeds, grass and bushes, walked around a diesel locomotive standing on the rails in a wide arc with a single carriage full of sand - the machine was humming, the engine was running, it was unclear why - and went deeper into the forest. Mazai dragged himself along the edge of the meadow, keeping in the shade of the trees, dragging his wounded leg. His thigh ached more and more, the blood did not stop, weakly, but it flowed - no wonder, he needed to give himself at least a few hours to rest. The bandage was soaked and slipping, and the painkiller that Mazai had injected into himself no longer worked. But there is not a single secluded spot around - not a torn-up tree, between the roots of which you can take a nap, not a blind ravine, not an abandoned house. Quiet, calm, deserted. There are few anomalies with artifacts, and if so, then stalkers are almost never encountered. There are no military patrols at all. You can live here for a year, two - and not meet anyone. The wilderness, the back streets of the Zone - but not such an ominous, gloomy wilderness as the one that stretches beyond the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant, and less dangerous. The sun touched the forest, cold pinkish rays broke through the sparse clouds. The trees cast long shadows on the flooded meadow ahead. Mazai stopped at the edge of the forest, looked at the open space from under his palm. The wind weakly stirred the tall, lush grass. Not a rut, not a black bald spot from a fire... Untrodden, protected lands. Closer to the trees grew sedge and marsh wild rosemary - that meant a river or a swamp nearby. Perhaps he should go deeper into the forest, hide under some spreading spruce and rest, have a snack, change the bandage. Mazai felt his strength flowing away with every drop of blood soaking the bandage. There was one bad thing: after meeting the mercenaries and other troubles like a blind dog suddenly jumping out onto the trail, there was half a magazine of ammo left for the machine gun, and only three in the TT. And the can in the backpack was empty. He had barely entered the canopy of the forest when he heard the distant splash of water, and sure enough - a river. Mazai limped toward the sound, avoiding the hummocks on which tall blueberry bushes grew. His boots sank into the moss, and there was a squelching sound under his soles. The banks of the river were marshy - how could he get to the water? He walked along the river, hearing the gurgle and rustle of water, but not seeing it, hidden by willow thickets. The bank gradually became higher, and the soil drier. When the squelching underfoot completely stopped, Mazai was about to turn towards the river - it seemed that the bushes had become thinner, and hobbling with a wound on his thigh was already completely unbearable - but he saw a lopsided hut ahead, sunken into the ground. The window was smeared with dirt, thick fireweed grew on the roof, pale pink petals fluttering in the wind. Mazai looked around, squinting. What a sacred, ringing silence all around... The hut was hidden in the center of abandoned, forgotten lands. Grass, forests, fields - it seemed that the whole world was made of them, and no people, no weapons, helicopters, shooting. He had no idea that such places remained in the Zone. And this house was an excellent hiding place, as if on order, as if the invisible forces that ruled the Zone had taken pity on the escaped stalker and given him a gift. Hidden from prying eyes, no animals to be seen around, no anomalies - Mazai rarely encountered them after the Wild Territories, when he turned northwest - but there was water, and he could hunt. Here he would bandage his wound, eat, and get some sleep. Dragging his leg, the stalker hurried to the hut with all his might. The door faced the river, three steps cut into the ground led to the threshold. Having already thrown off his backpack, Mazai frowned and looked at them. The rolled-off clay lay nearby, long hidden by grass, the steps were also overgrown, but the stems were flattened - someone had walked here recently. Deep in his soul, a worm of doubt was nagging: something was wrong, there was no such thing as a free lunch except in a rat-wolf trap. Why had the Zone been so generous with such a gift? The Zone was cunning, sly, bloodthirsty, it may seem obliging, it may pity, but this is a deceptive pity, behind which lies a treacherous, treacherous nature. Mazai turned and took a long look at the forest. Trees, bushes on the shore, ferns and moss... No one anywhere, not a twig cracking, not a branch moving, the wind had died down, serene silence all around. But a familiar sense of threat hung in the air, and waves of weak, tickling shivers ran down his spine, from his shoulders to his tailbone. And that smell... Bloodsucker! It was as if he had been hit in the back of the head, the word echoed loudly in his head. Mazai stuck his hands into the straps of his backpack and hobbled away to get away from this place as quickly as possible. A bloodsucker had taken a fancy to the hut - old, seasoned males exude a barely perceptible smell of sour beer, there is no mistaking it. Having looked back several times, the stalker began to hurriedly climb the slope. The hill is not very high, but steep, there are almost no trees here, but the top is overgrown... He stopped and sniffed the air. Well, I really got it! Mazai cursed in dismay. The smooth, knife-cut top of the hill was thickly overgrown with blood grass. He had only dealt with it once before and hoped that he would never have to repeat that experience. A very characteristic pungent aroma emanated from the plant with narrow, long leaves that looked like flattened, blood-stained fingers - they were covered with a dense network of the finest red veins. You can't stay near the bloodroot for long. It was the Forester who once showed it to Mazai and told him the little he knew - the herb is very rare, few people have heard of it. The main thing is that it releases poisonous gas. Suddenly, it was as if he had been poked in the back with a rusty nail. Mazai shuddered, turned around, and met the bloodsucker's gaze. A bunch of tentacles were moving behind the dirty window of the hut. A decrepit ancient bloodsucker was watching the man with a sad, hungry look. He was disgusting, and Mazai, raising his machine gun, gave a short burst. Too late he realized that he had very few bullets... and now there were even fewer. He still hadn't shot the bloodsucker: the bullets hit the log above the window, the mutant disappeared. Either he became invisible, or he retreated into the depths of the house. Now he would come out and chase the completely exhausted stalker, whose wound was bleeding and who practically had no bullets left. Mazai cursed and went on. As far as he knew, the fumes of blood were even more poisonous for a bloodsucker than for a human, and the mutant wouldn't go to the top of the hill. But how long will he last? How big are the thickets, will he have time to cross them before the poison reaches a critical concentration in the blood? At the top of the hill, Mazai stopped and cursed under his breath. The bloodroot stretched far away, the rising wind stirred the narrow leaves - a whole sea of reddish greenery. He turned and, limping heavily, hurried along the thickets, feeling the acrid, intoxicating smell. Death, according to the Forester, would be excruciating. Slow suffocation, when gradually, almost imperceptibly, spasms of the larynx begin - it is increasingly difficult for air to enter the lungs, the throat seems to narrow, after twenty or thirty minutes you begin to fight for every breath and understand that you are losing in this fight... Don't think about it - think about how to get away from the bloodsucker! Mazai was barely hobbling - fatigue was taking its toll. Was the mutant chasing him? He looked around: the hut, the trees and bushes near it - everything was floating. Well, yes, the effect of the bloodsucker begins with the fact that vision deteriorates, the surroundings are covered in haze. However, the mutant, of course, became invisible, it was impossible to determine whether he followed. Although what doubts could there be? Mazai had not seen such a hungry look for a long time! Taking a few steps down the slope to get out of the toxic fumes, he took a deep breath of clean air. His vision cleared up a bit, and the stalker noticed that the grass in front of the hut was swaying - not all of it, as if from the wind, but only in one place, and these swayings were moving in his direction. Mazai shook his head and limped on, deciding that he would enter the bloodroot thicket only when the mutant was already very close. Soon he turned around to check how fast the bloodsucker was moving, and almost screamed. Tentacles were floating above the trampled grass. This bloodsucker was as old as a worn-out sole! He didn't have enough strength to disappear completely... Mazai raised his machine gun and pulled the trigger. But the mutant noticed the man's movement - and disappeared completely. Bullets flew over the grass, knocked splinters out of the roof of the hut. No sound, no falling body - the bloodsucker, although an ancient old man, managed to jump away, Mazai missed. Cursing himself for using up his last bullets, the stapker hobbled on. Now it was the end - this was a bloodsucker, not some rat! Even such a wreck could easily kill a wounded, exhausted man. The leg hurt more with each step, it was swollen and made it difficult to walk. Mazai turned into the bloodroot thickets and sat down, trying to breathe shallowly. Leaves with red veins swayed around, rustled, emitting a deadly aroma. From the top, the stalker had a good look at the surroundings and was finally convinced: the situation was hopeless. The thickets covered the entire slope and stretched further between the trees. The hill extended forward for another twenty meters, bending to the left, meeting the river. And where the thickets of bloodroot came close to the water, the puppeteer tree towered. Mazai recognized it immediately, even through the haze of toxic fumes, and shuddered. The puppeteer always disgusted him. It seemed that the tree even caught someone, but at such a distance, and with his blurred vision, he could not really see. This mutant hybrid seemed to the stalker to be the product of a sick fantasy, something completely unreal. How does it catch people, how does it understand that a victim has appeared under the crown? In what devilish way do the thinnest shoots-twigs grow together with nerve endings? Mazai shuddered. No, you can’t get through there. And he can’t cross the river now – it’s quite wide, the current is fast... And nowhere – not a bridge, not even a log thrown over the dark water. He finally realized that he was trapped. Behind him was a hungry bloodsucker, in front of him was a puppeteer tree, on the right were thickets of bloodweed, on the left was a river. The worst thing was that he was chained to a poisonous plant - if he left it, the mutant would pounce. But what was the point of sitting in the thickets? The bloodsucker wouldn't come here, but Mazai would die here within half an hour... His throat was sore, Mazai started coughing. Now it was hard to breathe - this was the beginning of the end. Stalker checked the bullets - only one. Was this also a gift from the Zone? One bullet, the last one to shoot himself... - Bite me! - Straightening up, Mazai bent his right hand and slapped his left hand on his bicep. - Only cowards shoot themselves. I won't get the last bullet! He threw the backpack into the grass with a decisive movement, rummaged through his pockets, in the pouch - and found another cartridge. If the bloodsucker was so old that it was difficult for him to remain invisible, he simply had to lie in wait for the mutant and shoot him, there was no other way. Having inserted a cartridge into the magazine, Mazai took a step out of the tall grass onto the slope of the hill, stood there, breathing in the air less saturated with toxic fumes, and looked through the haze at the triangular hollow between the hill, the river and the bloodsucker's hut. The hollow was trembling, floating, as if he were looking at it through the hot air above the fire. A blurry shadow flashed among the trees near the slope, tentacles floated and disappeared. Mazai raised his machine gun, waited a couple of seconds, carefully aiming, and pulled the trigger. The bullet whizzed along the twisting trunk of the aspen, a quiet half-moan, half-sigh was heard... He hit! No, you can't kill a bloodsucker with one shot, even this ruin. A clearly visible silhouette appeared in the air, filled with colors, darkened, then dissolved again - a vague shadow darted forward and disappeared, leaving splashes of blood on the grass. Mazai stepped back, under the cover of the acrid smell of blood sausage. He hit, but what's the point? He only angered the bloodsucker, now he can try to grab him and drag him down to a safe place. Mazai helplessly gritted his teeth, blinking frequently from the pain in his eyes - and saw how fern leaves were crushed right in front of him down the slope. Not believing in his luck, Mazai fired almost point-blank. ...And this time it didn't work. The bullet whizzed over the head of the mutant, who was crawling on all fours. For a moment he appeared - and slowly melted away, his image blurred in the air, trembling at the edges, until he disappeared completely. What bad luck?! Mazai mentally sent Mother Zona to hell, and then cursed out loud. His head was spinning, he could hardly stand on his feet. His throat was sore, it was hard to breathe. Mazai held back his cough, because he knew that if he coughed, it would only get worse, the spasm would squeeze his airways faster. He saw no way out. He could either hang around in the bloodsuckers or go after the bloodsucker with a machine gun as a club. Both were a sure thing. The stalker stood, staggering, with difficulty sucking in the air filled with a deadly aroma. When he lowered his trembling hands, the useless machine gun slipped from his fingers and fell into the tall grass. And then a bloodsucker appeared at the edge of the thicket - it appeared in the air, the tentacles appeared first, then the head, the sunken chest... Red dripped right from the empty space under it, as if the air itself was bleeding. A deathly, sullen and somehow very tired look stared at the face, Mazai was doused with damp cold, as if the stalker was instantly transported to a rainy late autumn, under a piercing wind. The bloodsucker appeared entirely and immediately began to disappear. The body was covered with ripples, like a reflection in water, disappeared - only the tentacles remained. They swayed and began to move away from the thicket of bloodsucker, until they dissolved in the haze. Now nothing testified to the presence of a mutant nearby, Mazai did not feel its sour smell either behind the sharp smell of bloodsucker, but he sensed with his sixth sense: the bloodsucker was nearby and waiting. Mazai touched his sweaty forehead and swallowed. The blood saturate his blood and consciousness with poison, his head was buzzing, strange hallucinations began: it seemed to him that he heard a quiet whisper coming from the direction of the hut - the whisper of death. But he did not want to die. Mazai had never thought about his death before, although he was ready to meet it every day. Because he was threatened with a quick death - either in an anomaly, or on the fangs of a boar, in the paws of a pseudo-giant, under the claws of pseudo-flesh ... before you even have time to understand, you are already gone. But Mazai never thought that he would have a painful, long death, and he would be so weak and helpless that he would not be able to avoid it. The world shook, went in waves. Squinting, Mazai rubbed his forehead, cheeks, scratching his palms on the stubble, touched his cheekbone. He looked around. He was standing in the thicket itself - what a place he had ended up in! The bloodweed here was almost waist-deep, and the smell was such that any man would give up the ghost in a few minutes. Swaying, the stalker climbed back to the edge of the thicket. Thoughts in his head moved sluggishly, intertwining like sleepy snakes, slippery and seemingly alien. There is one option. He just needs to remember - which one? Someone told him, some bearded hunter... Oh yes, the Forester! The Forester said there was one way... But that also means almost certain death... Stop, we can't go any further! - He stopped at the very edge of the thicket. The bloodsucker was somewhere there, although he was not visible now. Breathing heavily, wheezing, Mazai somehow bent down, almost falling, tore off a long blade of grass, picked it up in his palm and examined it closely. Almost certain death. The forester told... No, no! It's better to die either from the fumes of the bloodsuckers or from the tentacles of the bloodsucker, and not from both at once! Or does it make no difference? There is a river there, which under normal circumstances would not be an obstacle, but now he cannot swim across it, he will go to the bottom, like Chapayev. Here is a tree-puppeteer, next to it is a bloodsucker, and here is a bloodsucker. Everywhere there is death, but... A painful cough tore at his throat, he barely had the strength to hold it in. Mazai coughed, spat at his feet and saw a backpack lying in the grass. And I made a decision. Moving like a zombie, slowly and hesitantly, he took the small pot off the fastener on his backpack. How much time was left? No more than fifteen minutes, probably less, before the blood squirt would knock him down. Mazai moved forward along the top of the hill, collecting dry twigs. You don't need much to make an infusion, just heat the water to body temperature, no need to boil it. Having trampled a small clearing about a meter and a half from the edge of the thicket, he folded the twigs into a tent and clicked a lighter. The dry twigs caught fire quickly, tongues of flame ran along the branches. They melted as if they were plastic, bending in the fire. Mazai, who had crouched down next to him, raised his head - the bloodsucker stood nearby, like a sculpture carved from mossy dark wood, and watched the man with a sad look. Everything was swimming before his eyes, the campfire had turned into a yellow spot in the middle of a red-green canvas of grass. The trees on the hillside had become black stripes, the world had merged into a single gray background. The kettle had suddenly turned into the face of the Snakeling, and Mazai almost screamed, reaching out to it. The face had blurred, turning into the red mug of an angry Elephant. Voices were heard again - many, from all sides, painfully echoing, as if from a well, the voices of all the stalkers Mazai had known in life, they vied with each other to condemn him, shouting that he was wrong, that he had abandoned the boy alone to be torn apart by these beasts in human form, all these Sharpeners, Bolts, Elephants, Mar-goshams... Mazai froze in the midst of the discordant muttering, biting his lip. A drop of blood trickled down his chin. Then a twig cracked loudly in the fire, and the voices suddenly fell silent, as if a door had slammed shut. He sobbed, wiping the blood from his chin with his sleeve. The hardest part was yet to come - to scoop up water. To do this, he had to go down to the river, past the bloodsucker or the puppeteer tree. Mazai took the pot and headed along the top of the hill toward the tree, shuffling like an old man, hunched over and grimacing. The fumes from the blood slush swirled around him like an invisible tornado, bending him toward the ground. He glanced sideways to the side—the bloodsucker was sadly dragging himself along behind him. The mutant was no longer hiding, the bastard realized that he had nothing to shoot with, but he kept lower down the slope to avoid the poisonous fumes. Finding himself three meters from the tree, the stalker froze in indecision. At what distance does the puppeteer become dangerous, how far can he stretch his branches? No one knows for sure - and the one who happened to find out will not tell. What surprised and disgusted Mazai most was the senselessness of the puppeteer's actions. Mutants attack because they are hungry and angry. Controllers take people under their mental cap to protect themselves or use them in their place for some task. And why does a tree need people-dolls? After all, it is a thoughtless plant, roots and a trunk with branches, what does it need except water, soil and sunlight? What benefit or pleasure does it give to a puppeteer to make people dance? One unfortunate fellow was hanging on the branches. He resembled a mummy, his clothes hung like on a hanger - but he was alive. Mazai turned away, looked at the bloodsucker. It did not take its painfully hungry gaze off the man, waiting for Mazai to leave the area too dangerous for the mutant. The stalker slipped his arm under the handle of the bowler hat, hanging it on the crook of his elbow, pulled on the hood, pulled the sleeves over his fists and squeezed the edges to hide any area of skin. He bowed his head to his chest - and hobbled under the tree as fast as he could. His breathing was interrupted, a painful cough was trying to get out, his eyes were swimming. The bloodsucker slid forward, but stopped a meter from the tree and raised its tentacles, trying to reach its victim. Flexible, elastic branches groped along the hood, along the shoulders, along the back, greedily searching for bare skin to suck on it, pierce the flesh and merge with the nerve endings. Mazai hobbled, staggering. The hardest part was ahead - he had to collect water under a tree so that the bloodsucker couldn't get to it - and for this he would have to bare his palm and wrist, the most delicious place for the puppeteer... ...He didn't remember how he got to the fire. The fire was almost out, ashes covered the crushed grass. Mazai put the pot in the center of the fire, threw in some dry wood he had collected along the way, and fanned the flames. The water on the pot's walls hissed, evaporating. The bloodsucker howled – long and mournfully. He felt that his victim was trying to escape, but he couldn’t understand what she was up to. Mazai saw the sagging skin on his muzzle, covered with pigment spots – a real patriarch of the bloodsucker tribe, an aksakal! The stalker began to tear red leaves and throw them into the pot. The water there had managed to warm up a little. The worried bloodsucker crawled out of the bushes and crawled closer, almost to the very edge of the thicket. Separated by a strip of grass, the mutant and the man looked at each other. The creature moved its tentacles, sniffing, took a hesitant step forward, but then retreated further. Mazai could not stand it and began to cough. His throat was torn as if by sandpaper, and his lungs felt as if iron filings had been poured into them. Sparks exploded before his eyes, Mazai, opening his mouth, stepped to the edge of the thicket, hoping to take a breath of air less saturated with poison, blood dripped from his lip. The bloodsucker came to life and hurried forward, and Mazai backed away towards the fire. The disappointed mutant stopped, devouring his victim with his gaze, and moved away a little again. The stalker put his hand into the pot to feel immediately when the brew would be warm enough. He was shaking with chills, the voices of the dead and the living were again heard from all sides, the trees on the edge of the forest were calling to each other, someone was muttering terribly in the hut, the waves of the river were making a deathly whisper. Mazai shook his head, bent down lower over the pot. The leaves had colored the water brown. The forester said that under the influence of the brew a person completely loses control, scratches his face, convulses, and if the brew is too strong, the spasm takes over the whole body, and you die of suffocation. But if you brew it not strong enough, the brew will not work... Finally, Mazai decided: it was time. Grabbing the hot handle with his sleeve, he lifted the pot, tilted it and began pouring brown water into his mouth, swallowing greedily. The warm broth softened his throat, torn apart by coughing. The bloodsucker stirred, came closer, looked at the drinking man in bewilderment, could not resist - and crawled into the grass. Mazai threw back his empty pot and wiped the wet leaves from his face. Straightening his legs with difficulty, like an athlete doing a weightlifting, he trudged away from the mutant, down the hill. He had to get out of the bloodroot thicket, or else it would be the end... The bloodsucker dragged itself along behind him, whispering and moaning. It moved with difficulty, but it was still catching up, because Mazai was barely hobbling. When the stalker realized that the mutant was very close, he fell on his side and rolled down the slope. The creature, stretching out its tentacles and wailing like a woman, hurried after him. Having rolled into the lowland, the stalker froze, lying on his back. His eyes darkened, and the last thing Mazai saw was an inhuman face above him, and the last thing he remembered was the cold tentacles clinging to his neck..

When the wet sticky tendrils covered the body, the Chemist closed his eyes and clenched his teeth, not removing his hand from the knife. He really didn’t like how things were going, he couldn’t stand such situations - if he didn’t control the situation, he would getuneasy. Now the Chemist was not sure that he would be able to cut the flexible appendages. Despite the soft, pliable surface, the tentacles possessed considerable strength. It was a little reassuring that the Lame One had safely overcome the entrance - the satellite loomed in the semi-darkness, three meters separated him from the border of light and shadow. The Lame One looked at the Chemist, ready to come to the rescue. The tentacles squeezed the stalker, rubbed against his clothes, stained with a yellowish secretion, and recoiled. The Chemist stepped into the dark womb of the hill. He stood next to the Lame One. "Do you think I can turn on the flashlight here?" - Well, now we'll find out. Click, the flashlight on the hood frame flashed, the white beam slid across the cave. Several dozen furry little animals were scurrying underfoot. When the light fell on them, they squeaked, squinted, tried to move into the shadows or froze in stupor. It was difficult to determine the size of the underground cavity because of the tentacles that hung from the vault, sluggishly moving. Unlike those hiding at the entrance, the stalactite tentacles behaved passively, only occasionally moving - unfolding, descending to the earthen floor, touching the backs of the mutants. They sprayed their secret, and the tentacles rose again. But the powerful shoots hanging at the entrance, curled into thick loops, were waiting for new guests and did not try to poke into the depths of the dungeon. On the floor between the gatekeeper tentacles, scattered small crystals glittered. “Looks like roots,” the Chemist noted. “I’ll go up to the hill later and check what’s up there.” The furry creature slipped between the stalkers' legs, jumped up to the curled guardian tentacles, grabbed the crystal with its front paws and emerged into the light with it. "Look, this octopus doesn't touch those who come out," the Chemist noted. "Okay, I need to find my blockhead." The stalkers walked between the appendages hanging from the cave's roof. The Lame One carefully stepped over the mutants frozen in the beam of light, the Chemist threw the little animals out of the way a couple of times. He was already convinced that they were harmless, and did not stand on ceremony. The furry creatures squealed when his boot touched them and rolled away. The lame man looked around curiously, interested in the life of the underground colony: the scurrying little animals often collided, sprayed a fragrant secretion, sniffed each other and ran away. There was a sense of order in the mutants' movements, like the scurrying of ants in an anthill. The chemist also turned on the flashlight and looked up - there, the pale shoots that overgrown the entire vault swayed languidly. The floor was a dark sticky mass trampled by the paws of the mutants, apparently consisting of garbage and waste products of the colony. Here and there, skulls, ribs, shin and hip bones, small fragments were visible pressed into it... Parts of the skeletons belonged to both people and mutants. Making their way between the tentacle-stalactites, both stalkers kept silent. It was stuffy and humid under the moving canopy, the cave was filled with the specific smell of the secretion secreted by the mutants. Outside, in the wind, the aroma even seemed pleasant, but in the cave it became sharp, cloyingly bitter, it made the nose itch and the eyes water. The cave was not very big - about thirty paces long. Then the lanterns illuminated the far wall, overgrown with tentacles. Here they stuck out especially thickly, the ceiling between them was not visible, the floor was hidden under a solid carpet of disparate fragments of skeletons. The stalkers saw a blind dog - probably the same one that had been caught just recently. The mutant was lying on its back right under the wall, barely visible under a pile of swarming cave inhabitants. - What are they doing with the dog? Huh? Are they really... copulating? - There was surprise in the Lame One's voice. The dog showed no activity, and the little mutants were squirming around on him very vigorously. Looking closely, Lame noticed that several tentacles were running under the pile of furry little creatures - it looked like they were holding the dog or perhaps somehow paralyzing him. The Chemist bent down, grabbed the furry one running under his feet, turned it over... then threw it away, grabbed another, a third... "This is a real stunt, I'll die," he muttered. "They're all females." “So where is the Handful?” Lame One responded. “Oh!” - What? — The PDA is vibrating… I forgot that the sound is off. There is an anomaly somewhere nearby. The beams of the flashlights, sliding along the languidly moving tentacles, illuminated the motionless body of the Handful. Even further away, tiny lightning flashed rhythmically, flew up, twisted into spirals and went out in the darkness of the cave. Crystals glittered next to the anomaly - the same ones that the furry creatures carried with them when they got outside. The stalkers were not interested in the unknown anomaly now, they rushed to Nikita. He lay with his arms spread out, little animals were crawling over him, fiddling with his soft paws, tugging at his belt, the fasteners of his overalls, pulling at his boot laces... A soft, round mass was moving above the stalker, like a swollen bubble. A swollen, shapeless head could be discerned above the bubble, with skinny limbs sticking out to the sides. A humanoid creature with a swollen belly was hanging, held in place by tentacle-roots that had grown through it, its mouth was drowning in folds of pale, shiny skin, its wide-open eyes had no pupils - round white spots, and around the creature the walls and tentacles were thickly splattered with a yellow substance secreted by small, furry mutants. Under the fresh, wet streaks, brown layers, dried out by time, could be discerned. “What is this?” Lame Man whispered, dumbfounded. - I'll explain it to you later! - the Chemist barked, - Grab this blockhead and take him out! And hurry up, Lame One, damn you... When the stalkers kicked the indignantly squealing mutants aside, it became clear that Prigorshnya was lying on top of a pile of torn overalls, shirts and quilted jackets - many poor fellows had been on this bed before him. Nikita's arms and legs were entangled in snake-like appendages. The lame one cut the tentacles with a knife and threw away the loosened loops. The creature with a swollen belly shuddered, its thin limbs trembled, its eyeballs slowly turned in their swollen folds, cloudy, blurry pupils appeared, fat pieces of flesh in place of a mouth began to move... The pale tentacles swayed, intertwining and untwisting their coils, groping blindly beneath them. The Lame One kicked them away and pulled the Handful toward the exit. The chemist raised his rifle and fired several bullets into the monster's head. The head exploded, the swollen belly began to convulse, and immediately the forest of tentacles began to move - the cave was filled with rustling and squelching sighs, small mutants ran around among the waving appendages with a squeal. They splashed a yellow substance, which made the tentacles even more agitated. Dark, branched veins appeared on the fat bubble. The bubble began to contract and tremble, a stream of transparent oily liquid splashed out of it... and a wet purple lump fell out. It turned around, twitched its tiny limbs, and squealed. This mutant was born premature - its fur was stuck together in clumps, and underneath it, thin skin, covered in ulcers, pulsated. The skin cracked and liquid oozed out from underneath it. The Lame One fired a long burst, the bullets hit the tentacles, ripping open the elastic serpentine bodies, and wet shreds flew. But the shooting did not produce a significant effect. While the Lame One was shooting, the Chemist reloaded his rifle. The Lame One threw the machine gun over his back, grabbed Nikita under the arms and dragged him to the exit. The Chemist ran alongside, chopping with his knife, pushing away the tentacles with the barrel when they tried to block the Lame One’s path with his load. The stalactite tentacles were not a serious obstacle, but those guarding the entrance were dangerous. The stalkers stopped, raised their weapons and released everything that was left in the magazines, crushing the forest of waving bundles. The Chemist rushed forward first. Some of the guards remained motionless – their program of not touching those leaving was still in effect, but a pair of serpentine appendages rushed towards the stalker. Wielding a knife, the Chemist shouted: “Lame, faster!” The lame man grabbed the unconscious body again, dragged it out, stepping on blue crystals, staggering, dragged it along the path. Outside, the rain was pouring down hard, streams were hitting the ground; hissing and gurgling, the water flowed down the clay slope. The lame man slid down, dragging Prigorshny along with him, looked back as he went - the Chemist, looming in the dark opening of the cave, reached with his free hand for the container, took something out of it... The bushes hid him from view, and then an explosion thundered behind him. The Limpy One stopped and unclenched his numb fingers. Streams were running down his face and into his overalls, washing away a layer of yellow secretion that had already begun to dry and form a sticky crust. Leaving the Handful lying face up, the Limpy One took a few steps up the slope and saw smoke billowing out of the cave, and the Chemist almost freed himself from the tentacles. The pale tendrils, having lost control, became sluggish and attacked hesitantly. The Chemist bent down, grabbed a handful of crystals and tore away. — Anomaly? — shouted Lame. — Be careful! The chemist also noticed the acid widow, darted to the side, Several artifacts fell out from between clenched fingers, bounced along the gentle slope, along the path, one fell into the anomaly. It boiled, fiery splashes flew up, streams of rain hissed, meeting lightning... The chemist fell into the bushes, rolled to the side, and where he had just been, the bushes stung by lightning turned black, disintegrating into dust. The rain quickly washed away what was left of them, a dark bald spot formed around the anomaly. - Chemist! - called Lame. Thunder rumbled and the rain began to pour down with renewed force. - Chemist! - Don't shout... I'm here... - The stalker rose heavily from the thicket, took the rifle at the ready and, trampling the bushes, walked towards the calmed down anomaly. - I dropped it somewhere around here... Aha. In the grass, among the bubbles boiling under the blows of large drops, a crystal flashed. The Chemist looked at it closely - and cursed in surprise. The artifact that had been in the acid widow had changed, turned green and even changed shape. It seemed larger, smoother and harder than the crystal that the Chemist had dropped in the grass. - Interesting... - the stalker drawled. - This thing was made by an anomaly in the cave, but if you bathe it in the acid widow, it turns green... Hey, Lame? Green and completely different! Have you ever seen something like this before? - Chemist! Get out of here! Prigorshnya still hadn't come to his senses, but at least he was breathing, his arms and legs occasionally trembling, his muscles contracting. The Chemist joined Lame, and together they dragged Nikita away from the cave. Having reached the oak tree under which Lame had buried the loot, the exhausted stalkers sank down into the grass next to the prostrate body. The storm had moved away somewhere to the south, it had become lighter, the rain had become lighter, but had not yet stopped. Drops were drumming on the leaves, soaking into the moisture-soaked earth. “Ugh…” Lame One wheezed, wiping his forehead with his sleeve. “You have a tough partner, hero…” Prigorshnya called: - Katya, what's wrong with you?.. Do you hear me, Katya... - Then he opened his eyes, looked at the stalkers who were catching their breath heavily and asked in surprise: - Where's Katya? Where did they put Katya? Hey, I'm talking to you! Are you deaf? The chemist grimaced and whispered something. - What? - Prigorshnya tried to get up and groaned, clutching his temples with his palms. - Damn, my head hurts so much... Well, why are you silent? - Okay. - The chemist stood up. - Wait here, I'm going to get the "Little One." He glanced angrily at Nikita and left. Nikita watched his partner with a puzzled look, and when the silhouette melted into the veil of rain, he asked Lame: - What's wrong with him? He jumped out of nowhere and left again? - Don't you remember anything? Nikita rubbed his temples. — Actually, I have a feeling that I missed something interesting... For example, where is our swag? I don't remember where we took it... But it's all Katya's fault! If she hadn't invited me, I would never have gone. Lame, you're looking at me strangely too. What's the matter? It's not my fault that women are sticking to me!.

I'll tell you honestly, guys: when your closest friend, whose life you saved more than once or twice in the Zone, with whom you repeatedly fought back against military stalkers and marauders shoulder to shoulder, with whom you always shared your last can of stew and with whom you drank more than one tank of fuel and lubricants in the "Shti" bar, aims a pistol at you from forty paces away, then the pleasure is significantly below average. This is extremely disgusting, if anyone hasn’t realized it yet. As an ostrich from one cartoon said in a similar situation, I have never found myself in such a stupid situation even in Canada. “Don’t move, Hemul,” Pathogenych said with concentration, squeezing the pistol handle with both hands. His voice was shaking slightly from the tension, and I didn’t like that at all. “Stay still. Don’t move at all, dog, or I’ll make a hole this big in your forehead. You’ll walk around with a hole like the last idiot.” I licked my lips convulsively. I measured the distance between us with my gaze again and again. Damn. Damn. “Okay, brother,” I said, trying to sound dry and independent. I’m not sure I succeeded, but at least I tried. “If you’ve decided to shoot, then shoot, no point in talking. Not in the Verkhovna Rada.” “Hemul, don’t tell me what to do with children,” Pathogenych chuckled sullenly. The black muzzle of the pistol hypnotized me, did not allow me to look away. I must have been looking at it like a rabbit at a boa constrictor. Catching myself doing this, I forced myself to look away, sliding my gaze past Patogenych, frozen at the gate of a half-ruined farm. Out of the corner of my eye I caught a glimpse of the forest in the distance visible through a hole in the wall, a half-ruined iron barrier, the rusted remains of an ancient ZIL, which had once been parked in the farmyard and remained there forever, and the bizarre clouds swirling into a huge foamy spiral in the piercingly blue sky. Subconsciously expecting a shot every second, I meticulously examined the gray walls of the room. Everything inside was either gray, or black, or cobwebs. General devastation, the abomination of desolation. Entering this damn cowshed, I noticed a carnivorous invisible fungus on the wall even from the entrance, swollen like an ugly growth in the middle of sloppy damp spots on the plaster darkened by age, but then I was distracted by the steep troubles that had begun, and now I could not remember where exactly it was sitting: the nasty predatory plant perfectly merged with the grayness, blackness and cobwebs that surrounded it. However, what do I care about it now, if the stopwatch of my life seems to be counting down the last sweet moments? What kind of trifles always fills my head in moments of mortal danger... Don't believe those bookish romantics who claim that a person's whole life flashes before their eyes the moment before their death. Nonsense, I can say with authority as an expert in this matter. At that moment, only a couple of lonely thoughts about a hidden invisible mushroom and a shoulder burned by stinging fluff are dangling in a completely empty head, as well as a stupid fleeting regret about the fact that a lot of money for a precious artifact, because of which you will almost certainly remain lying here with a bullet in the head, will safely march past you. And also hysterically flickering on the edge of consciousness, like a green man at a traffic light: "Not fair! Not fair!" All sorts of smart-aleck newbies naively believe that a real cool stalker should think exclusively in manly obscenities, especially in critical situations, and the word "unfair" is good only for kindergarten or teenage stories; go to hell, smart-alecks. For some reason, all the surrounding lands are strewn with your bones, and not mine. However, what difference does it make if the end is the same anyway? I have trampled the Zone many times longer than you, smart guys, and what's the point? I closed my eyes tiredly, unable to bear to see the years of dust and cobwebs around me. There was still no shot. It is wrong when the last thing you see before death is dust and cobwebs. There is some kind of supreme meanness in it. As soon as vision stopped transmitting its eighty percent of information to the brain, all other senses immediately became more acute. Sometimes I used this technique when I could not understand what exactly was bothering me on the route, why I could not take the next step along the path, although my eyes did not discern the slightest danger. Often it worked, and I quickly discovered the source of anxiety. The distant hum of propellers immediately became audible on the edge of hearing – a peacekeeper patrol helicopter was patrolling over the Dump. It was unlikely to head in our direction, the analytical center in my head automatically clicked: it was making a turn over the Dog Village towards the Perimeter. It smelled piercingly burnt and somehow sour from the corpse of pseudo-flesh lying in the neighboring stall. The ribbed surface of the bolt head in my fingers. Why do I need a bolt now? Okay, let it be. Dying with a bolt in my hands is the dream of any stalker! A wonderful heroic phrase, the level of idiocy of which is fully deserving of being inserted into a cartoon about an ostrich. The heavy intermittent breathing of Pathogenych, clearly audible even from such a distance. It would be nice for him to finally quit smoking, otherwise lung cancer will send him to the grave in the next few years. And it’s not so easy to shoot your old comrade in arms. It breaks, brother, doesn't it? There was still no shot. I wonder if I'll have time to hear the shot or if I'll die faster than its roar catches up with the bullet that ran ahead?.. And another thin, disgusting sound in the impenetrable darkness that surrounded me – right in front of me. Barely perceptible, on the edge of audibility, drowned out even by the whisper of the grass in the gusts of a lazy wind, but no less ominous and unpleasant – as if someone was striking the back of a two-handed saw with a bow again and again, and its bending blade was vibrating thinly, crying and singing in an unnatural voice, choking and sobbing, like an unearthly creature with four bullet wounds in its chest. Having decided that waiting for the shot with my eyes closed was not constructive, I again stared straight into the muzzle of the Beretta. Hello, my dear. This nasty day had started off quite well. Having spent the night in the Stalker bar, Pathogenych and I set out for Military early in the morning. The sun was scorching like crazy, it was hard to believe that it was already deep autumn. However, it is always deep autumn in the Zone, even in late spring. The last emission was a long time ago, so before Military our clan already had a more or less marked path - not exactly safe, but at least minimally marked on the ground. Another question is that further on, in the north of the level, where we were now heading, the anomalies always stood like a wall, and even the most seasoned veterans preferred not to climb here alone or in pairs. Good artifacts are often born on Military, but such a risk is still not justified. For a sortie, you need five or six minimally trained master keys, just in case. For your own peace of mind. And an experienced assistant to the leader. Again, so that it would be possible to fight off marauders and wild boars without much difficulty, if necessary. However, when you are carrying a terribly expensive artifact in a container, and there are five or six unpredictable guns around you, the situation becomes rather ambiguous. And then you have to divide the loot between everyone if you are carrying half a dozen lock picks. It’s not that Pathogenych and I were pathologically greedy in life, but when he plopped down next to me on a high stool at the Shti bar and put a printout of a broken file from the PDA of a dead tramp whose body he found not far from an abandoned military base on the counter, I immediately realized that we didn’t need lock picks in this matter. Apparently, the stalker from the Clear Sky clan went to Military alone. Otherwise, it would be unclear why his partners abandoned his body in the Zone, chained like a greedy man to a concrete slab, with all his weapons and equipment, as well as such valuable information. By the way, Patogenych received some money for his portable computer from the system genius Che; he probably raked in twice as much when he sold it to Clear Sky, but that's not our business. But beforehand, my colleague rummaged through the dead man's PDA and downloaded all the interesting information that was found in the electronic memory. And he deleted one broken file from the trash bin - just in case. Having carefully studied the printout of this very file, I looked up at Pathogenych: - Is this exactly what I think it is? "The dog knows what you're thinking," Patogenych responded reasonably, lifting a glass of clear beer with three fingers. After drinking, he grunted thoughtfully, put the glass on the counter and tapped his fingernail on the sheet of paper in front of me: "But this should be terribly interesting, brother." I already figured it out myself. I asked, just in case. The printout showed pictures of areas and readings from the anomaly detector. Very blurry pictures and very rough readings from the anomaly detector. However, that was enough to take a risk. Boil your milk!.. The guy from Clear Sky turned out to be experienced enough to figure out what exactly such a location of anomaly fields could mean, but not experienced enough not to make any notes on his PDA about it. Or maybe he himself did not understand anything and specially wrote down the data to show it to some veteran and find out more precisely what such an amazing picture meant. And it meant one simple, but important thing: wealth. Or instant death - well, it depends on how the cards fall... “An egg,” I said, busily moving my portion of the transparent one towards me. “Exactly,” said Pathogenych, taking the printout from me and carefully hiding it in the inside pocket of his denim jacket. “Scream even louder, dog, or maybe not everyone in the bar has heard you yet.” “It’s a dangerous business,” I said, lowering my voice out of politeness, although I was already speaking almost in a whisper. “A barrel of gunpowder.” "A pile of money," Patogenych responded immediately. "Two piles of money. The last egg was discovered three years ago. So, three piles of money." “For two,” I clarified just in case, so that some tragic misunderstanding wouldn’t arise between the companions later. - It's obvious. Four piles of money for two. You can't send the youngsters inside, they'll all die on the first steps, and if they don't go inside, what the hell is the point of taking them with you? Nowhere to put the money? And an extra veteran won't make a difference, he'll just get in the way. "We need to come to an agreement with Bubna first," I said worriedly. "He might not have that many karbovanets on him when we bring him this thing. Let him prepare the required amount of money in advance." - And we need to immediately calculate and agree on how we will leave the bar with such a sum, - added Patogenych. - All sorts of surprises are possible here. And how to dispose of this sum later. Because we will need to dispose of it extremely quickly, rumors will spread instantly... At this point the preliminary negotiations ended, because Faze landed on the next stool at the counter. Faze is a good man, but why should he know about the devil's egg? That's what I'm saying: there's absolutely no reason for a good man like Faze to know about the devil's egg. Pathogenych and I passed through the Perimeter like a knife through butter. Beautifully. After the memorable grandiose breakthrough, the military has not yet managed to fully restore the integrity of the first line of defense, so we did not have any particular problems. We carefully opened the barbed wire fence, carefully patched the hole, quickly overcame the minefield and managed to disappear into the forest before the clatter of patrol helicopters was heard from the direction of Chernobyl 4 - either flying for our souls or making a planned flight around the territory. Covering each other, we crossed the Dump along a short diagonal, successfully passed Agroprom, without any special adventures went around the shore of Yantarnoye Lake and got to the Stalker bar. We spent the night here, because we entered the Zone in the evening, having quickly gathered right after the military council in Shtyakh. Climbing into the labyrinth of anomalous fields at night, in pitch darkness - no, thank you, I know more elegant ways of committing suicide. We did not encounter many animals on the route: the Big Breakthrough took too much biomass, which the military burned on the approaches to Kiev, and now the creatures were intensively breeding and multiplying somewhere in the depths of the Zone, near the Fourth Power Unit, trying to restore their former numbers. During the entire sortie, we shot only pseudo-flesh, a boar, three blind dogs near the Dog Village and three marauders. In general, we did not use up even a clip of cartridges until the Military itself. It's a laughing matter. For some reason, the marauders attacked us on the outskirts of the Dump, although it was obvious that we were going to the Zone empty-handed, and not returning with loot. However, there was enough to take from us anyway: at first glance, it was clear that the people were experienced, thorough, and equipped according to all the rules. A couple of our PDAs would have been enough to sit modestly in the "Shti" bar. Plus anomaly sensors, plus weapons, plus strong boots that are comfortable for trampling the Zone. However, at first glance it should have been obvious that getting involved with such serious people would be more expensive for us. Frankly speaking, I would not have been surprised if I had learned that we had discovered the marauders a few moments before they had discovered us. After all, Pathogenych and I thoroughly know all the places in the Dump where you can set up a proper ambush. In short, as soon as the shootout started, we immediately pinned two of them to the trees behind which they were hiding with short bursts. The third one tried to get away, but the target designator of my "hopful" did not fail this time either. It was not for nothing that I took this toy from He He before he left the Zone forever. The machine eagerly ate the standard .45 cartridge from the "M 16", so I had no problems with ammunition. We found almost nothing interesting on the looters' corpses and requisitioned only cartridges, half a bottle of vodka and an open pack of cigarettes. The penny artifacts we shook out of their containers would not have been worth the effort of bending over to get them, especially on a day like this, when we were heading for a damn egg. Naturally, we also disdained the moldy half-loaf of smoked sausage, the half-empty first aid kit and the soggy, falling apart loaf of bread. The Military level, as if on cue, was not completely covered. Over the last three days, part of the almost continuous anomalous front that had been cutting its territory almost in half for the past week had shifted to the northeast. Half of the contact pairs were completely discharged. This was all to our advantage, but there was a risk that with such a scenario, the rarest intersection of disparate anomalous fields at the point of interest to us would also disintegrate. Since we were going at random, not even fifty percent sure that a lucky coincidence had produced a precious artifact, such news did not add to our optimism. Ahead of them was a long-abandoned livestock complex of a large farm. At the time of the first explosion, they were called something funny – either kibbutzim or klokhozy. In short, some Jewish word. Three buildings for the animals were located on the ground in the shape of a giant letter P. In the farmyard, fenced with a low concrete fence, abandoned trucks towered in crooked, shapeless heaps, rusted to the ground, in the corner a Belarus tractor was visible, fallen on its side. And very close by – a brand new Niva, shining in the sun, as if it had just rolled off the assembly line. Only this model had not been produced since the last century. We lay down behind a hillock and carefully studied the cowsheds with Patogenych's army binoculars. Here it is, this place. Everything matches. It was this concrete letter P that the late stalker captured, and almost from this very point. Fact. Here is an electrical substation, here is a small house with former office premises, here is a stream, here is a huge ugly tree, as if twisted by giant hands. And here is an anomalous field. The sensor seemed to have gone crazy: according to its readings, on the territory of the livestock complex, meat grinders climbed onto bird carousels, trampolines collided with gravitational bald spots, and roasts hovered above all this disgrace. Of course, this does not happen, heterogeneous anomalies never touch each other, there is always a passage between them. Only identical traps merge into continuous impassable strips, and even then not always. Now it only remained to find out how wide these passages between the anomalies were - let's say, whether a stalker slightly larger than average in size could squeeze through them. While we were getting to the place, I mostly walked ahead. This is the stalker law: if you are taken into a partnership for a sortie for rich loot, be kind enough to work it off. No questions. But when we reached the goal, the work was over. Now the most difficult part of the mission was ahead, so Patogenych and I were currently on equal terms. Otherwise, it would have turned out that he took me as a master key to rake in the heat with someone else's hands; but I know how to earn money in less risky ways. That is, I am ready to take a fairly serious risk, but when I work for myself, and not for some stranger. Even if this stranger is Patogenych. - So what? - the colleague asked quietly, not taking his eyes off the half-ruined cowshed. - Rock-and-scissors? - Coming. A game everyone knows from childhood: opponents simultaneously throw their right hands forward. The hand is either clenched into a fist - "rock", or open - "paper", or two fingers are extended - "scissors". Scissors cut paper, paper wraps rock, rock dulls scissors. Everything is extremely simple. A fool will most likely throw out a "rock" in the first round. He naively believes that a "rock" is something unshakable, solid, the strongest position in the game, clearly superior to both fragile "scissors" and thin, fragile "paper". However, this is an illusion, all signs in this game are equal: each of them beats one sign and is beaten by another. None of them has any advantage at all - but even if a fool is able to comprehend this with his mind, on a subconscious level he will still strive for a "rock" as a symbol of power and steadfastness. Therefore, a smart but inexperienced person will certainly throw out a "paper" first. This is the simplest, intuitively understandable logical problem - checkmate in one move. Taking this into account, a smart and experienced person will throw out "paper" in a game with a fool or a child, and "scissors" in a game with a smart but inexperienced person. Checkmate in two moves. The master of the game, calculating a combination several moves ahead, must very subtly and accurately determine the degree of intelligence, analytical ability and experience of the opponent. In a game with a smart and experienced opponent, he will certainly throw out a "stone" at first - but only if he is convinced by indirect signs that the opponent is capable of calculating the course of the game no more than two moves ahead. For most people, this is the limit of calculating a multi-move combination in everyday conditions - after that they begin to get confused and confused - so the master does not take much risk. At least his chances of winning are much higher than those of the opponent. In a game between a master and a master, there are no rules for the first move. On the first move, everything is decided by blind chance. However, it should still be borne in mind that the probability of getting a "stone" in such a situation is slightly lower than the other two signs - for the same reasons that a fool or a child is likely to immediately throw out a "stone": subconsciously, the master tries not to repeat this primitive mistake. However, this is also a mistake, since such behavior from the opposite can be calculated by another master and used. The main task in a game with a master is to not let him catch himself on some behavioral pattern that he will be able to turn to his advantage. Masters of the game are subtle psychologists and are sometimes able to predict up to ninety percent of the symbols that an inexperienced opponent will throw out. In scientific studies, such a result would certainly be recognized as experimentally proven clairvoyance. In short, on the first move, Pathogenych and I threw out two “scissors”. So; now here's another matter. The first move is like "e2 - e4" in chess: before it, no strategy development is possible, but after it - the widest field of combinations. However, unlike chess, long openings, rallies and defenses are not applicable here: each next move may be the last. This is super blitz, the winner is not the one who can painstakingly build complex and cunning traps, but the one who can more accurately and quickly calculate the opponent's train of thought. And it is reasonable to take risks in accordance with your calculations. In the next round I threw scissors again, and Pathogenych threw rock. One - zero. So. It is unlikely that the third time in a row "scissors" will fall out, this will already be a clear pattern; however, there is a small chance that Patogenych, who wants to confuse me with his unpredictability, will decide to throw them out. I looked closely at my opponent. No, it is unlikely; he clearly understands that I am now on guard. The possibility of "scissors" is negligible, so I do not take it into account. So, "rock" or "paper". "Rock" or "paper"? If, in his opinion, I expect a third "scissors", there will be "paper" to wrap up my "rock". If not, and I, the insidious beast, am myself going to throw "scissors" to confuse my opponent, then "rock" will fall out. Looking straight into the impenetrable face of my opponent, I throw out the "paper". Patogenych has a "stone". Well, of course, you old fox. Who were you trying to fool? The game continues until three victories. We rack our brains, intensively calculating options, trying to act as unpredictably as possible, but at the same time catching the opponent on his own miscalculations and not falling for the enemy's tricks. A delightful work for the analytical centers of the brain, as if you are sparring with two swinging makiwaras at the same time - you need to hit one and simultaneously dodge the second. Great for developing abstract thinking. Then two more "scissors" fall out, then Patogenych returns the slap to me, wrapping up my "rock". Two - one is not in my favor. Then two "rocks" fall out. Two "papers". Two "rocks". Then I even the score, blunting Patogenych's "scissors". It is also necessary to keep in mind all the moves, the entire sequence of symbols that fell out before - from this, too, one can identify the patterns in the opponent's behavior necessary for victory. The tension is rising, the spectators in the stands are frozen in awe. Two "scissors". Two "stones". Two "scissors". Two "papers". - A smoke break? - I suggest. Pathogenych shakes his beard, looks at me from under his brows. Calculates combinations in his mind. With my remark, I am not simply breaking the rhythm of the game and distracting him from the analysis. There are many secondary associations associated with a smoke break. Cigarette paper, a cardboard pack of cigarettes, paper advertising posters, a scrap of newspaper for lighting. I wipe my sweaty forehead, stall for time, stare intently at Patogenych. In a few seconds, the association should be firmly embedded in the opponent's subconscious. You know how it happens when you accidentally hear the name of a popular song or group, and a few minutes later you suddenly catch yourself humming a familiar tune. You start to spin the associative series - and you understand where it came from. But this is later, at the level of consciousness. At first, the subconscious obediently extracts this very tune from its dusty archives and slips it to you - here, master! You were talking about this, right?.. If everything goes right, Patogenych won't even realize at first why he suddenly threw away the "paper." He'll decide that it was the result of his own clever analysis. I confidently throw out the "scissors" - and get the same in return. No, it's a dead end to catch an old experienced pike on a bare hook. Pathogenych is not a Raccoon, which can be manipulated as you like. If, of course, you know its weak points well. And yet, we must try again. Two "scissors" for the third time with a step of one round is already a stable logical sequence, and the first task of the master in this game is to destroy any external logic of his actions, so that the opponent cannot calculate them. And now Patogenych will probably try to do just that. Most likely, the necessary association has already firmly nested in the subconscious of my colleague, reinforced by the last "scissors" that fell out - an object closely associated in real life with that very paper. Again, without thinking, I throw out the “scissors” and run into Pathogenych’s “stone” with them. Oh, how. Well, then it turns out I outsmarted myself. “You’re a bit of a small boy compared to your dad, you dog,” the colleague commented phlegmatically, putting his hands in his pockets. “Don’t burst from the realization of your own importance,” I muttered. We slowly walked down the hill to the barn, like those three bulls from the joke – true, there were only two of us, but we were definitely worth three. We stopped fifty meters from the nearest building, meticulously examining its rough, weather-beaten walls covered with bizarre lilac lichens. You don’t come across such lichens outside the Perimeter – perfectly round, with a spongy surface and a halo of sharp teeth along the edges. It seems like it’s about to bite your hand. - Well, what? - asked Pathogenych. - Will you go to the instruments? “Any other suggestions?” I snapped back weakly. There were no other proposals. I took off the Hopful I had slung over my shoulder and carefully placed it on the grass. I threw the backpack straps off my shoulders and put it next to me: the smaller my overall dimensions, the better. It was unknown how narrow the gaps between the anomalies I would have to crawl through would be. I gave Pathogenych all the sensors I had and my PDA: the last thing I needed was for them to attract a stray discharge when I started squeezing right up to the meat grinders. The only weapon I kept for myself was a bayonet knife in a dielectric sheath. “Good job, tramp,” Patogenych admonished me, taking a position that would make it easier to record the anomalous fields surrounding the barn using sensors. This is what is called “going by the instruments”: the follower moves extremely carefully in a particularly dangerous place, relying only on his senses, trying to see, hear and feel the danger lurking in the surrounding space. Because moving forward, looking at the sensors and around at the same time, is not the best idea - you will certainly miss something, if not there, then here. To prevent such a disaster, the leader carefully watches the light mark of his friend moving across the screen of the anomaly sensor, and gives the follower commands by voice - where to move, where to slow down, where to turn. The follower completely opens up to the Zone and adjusts the data transmitted by the leader in accordance with his own feelings. Naturally, this trick won't work with a regular newbie: he has a pinch of experience, so he can't rely on his instincts and must strictly follow all the leader's commands. In order to be able to confidently and relatively safely follow the instruments, the follower must trample the Zone for at least two years. And I won't even mention the leader. I moved slowly toward the half-ruined building, taking short, barely noticeable steps. Step. Step. Step. A strip of burnt, yellowed grass on the right, clearly standing out against the background of the brown-green, rain-saturated turf. Nonsense, far away; and Pathogenych is silent. For ten hours, a small meat grinder crackles, biting with tiny purple lightning bolts a blade of grass swaying in the wind, covered in drops of dew. Meat grinders are a strange name for electrical anomalies; in fact, this was once the name given to the now completely disappeared traps that slowly and busily ground people into mincemeat with bone fragments. Why their name was transferred to these half-spheres of lightning that appeared a little later - the devil knows. The tramps from "Clear Sky" call them "electro", the "debtors" - "sockets", the "free" - "batteries". Also not much. Step. Step. In the wide doorway, long tangled icicles of rusty hair, reminiscent of a bloodsucker's beard, sway in the draft: this means that upon entering, you will have to bow to the Masters of the Zone so as not to burn your head, and at the same time, you must carefully watch your feet so as not to step on the pieces of red icicles that have come off and fallen down. The boots are brand new, it will be a shame. They will eat through them by evening. Step. Step. Step. To the left, an impressive circle of flattened and pressed grass floats by, and nearby, within reach, is a generous scattering of "wolf tears". Of course, I do not bend over for them. One of the main stalker laws: when you go on a big job, do not get distracted by trifles, do not waste your time on trifles, do not be greedy - you will be safer. The Black Stalker does not like greedy people. Step. Step. According to my calculations, I was already moving through invisible anomalous fields, but Pathogenych was still silent. I was even going to call out to him – had he fallen asleep – when he finally spoke up: - Left three. Carousel. I obediently moved to the right, although I did not discern any signs of a bird carousel on the left. But it is unlikely that Pathogenych simply decided to test my compliance. - Two twelve, - Pathogenych's voice reached me. - Circles. Three nine. Waiting.
