Obeying the leader’s commands, I turned away from the entrance to the barn, although there were only three meters of seemingly completely clear space left. “A coup,” Patogenych continued to command, using the terminology accepted in our clan to denote the slightest turns and movements on rough terrain. “Eleven. Wait. Right five. Trampoline. Right two. Stop! Where are you going, dog! Eight, right two!.. As a result, I described an impressive arc in front of the entrance with tiny steps, going around the meat grinder crackling from the morning dampness and something else invisible - probably another bird carousel, because on a free patch of space by the wall there were some untidy lumps lying around, similar to a torn to shreds corpse of a crow. Then I turned again to the gates of the barn. From the outside, my crazy route probably looked like a slalom track. But there was nothing to be done about it. It was good if there was a passage inside in the continuous anomalous field, otherwise we would have to return with nothing. Pathogenych continued to lead me along an invisible corridor between deadly dangerous traps. I squeezed right up to the dozing bird carousel, feeling its gentle breath on my face. An icy needle was pulsing in the back of my head, my fingertips were tingling, the hair on the back of my neck was standing on end, my eyes felt like someone had poured dry sand into them - my body was sensitively reacting to the too close presence of various anomalies. I would hardly have made it here alone. Now any step to the side, any wrong move could be my last. I was focused, collected and attentive. I listened carefully to my own feelings, trying not to miss the slightest rustle, the slightest change in the surrounding space. - Right seven. Waiting, - Patogenych commanded in a businesslike manner. - Waiting. Jump. Two ten. Circles. Left eight... I had already started to carry out his last command, but froze in my tracks, having taken only half a step. I didn't even put my foot on the ground. “Left eight,” repeated Patogenych just in case, although he already understood that I was slowing down for a reason. I lowered my foot very slowly and carefully. Nothing terrible had happened. I peered intently at the patch of ground directly in front of me, covered in tall, ugly, twisted timothy grass. It was absolutely no different from the neighboring ones. Not finding anything remarkable, I began to turn my head from side to side, trying to detect a close contact pair or any sign of another anomaly. To no avail. But where does this terrible, almost physiological reluctance to go forward come from? To the right – please. To the left… no, I won’t go to the left, there’s a powerful gravity concentrate there: it will drag you to itself and flatten you on the ground, you won’t have time to squeak. Back – it’s also undesirable: a stalker can’t go back the same way he came, you’ll almost certainly die. In short, go forward and don’t worry… No, I can't. I took a bolt out of my breast pocket and threw it in front of me. The bolt stuck straight up into the wet clay. No, of course, it wasn't a gravity concentrate, not even a greedy one - just damp clay. There it was, the dog, stuck in... “Are you sure there’s nothing there?” I asked in a strangled voice. - Three meters clean, - Patogenych responded confidently. - What, brother, is it flattening? The old veteran understood better than anyone that stalker's premonitions should not be ignored. Maybe it was just the subconscious playing tricks, or maybe it was actually protecting from very real dangers that could not be determined otherwise. “And it’s shaking,” I responded in a strangled voice. “Nine twelve,” Patogenych immediately plotted a new route without hesitation. I moved to the right, cursing my intuition. If it weren't for it, I would have found myself at the farm gate in just a dozen steps. Or maybe he would be lying right now in this very place, pierced through by a powerful electric discharge. No, to hell with him, better quietly, by a roundabout way, but more reliable. At one time, stalker clans tried to use special sapper robots in the Zone - such remote-controlled toys successfully defuse unexploded ordnance in hot spots and terrorist mines. But this fashion quickly passed when several outrageously expensive robots were lost out of nowhere. An operator located at a safe distance sees the same thing as the machine through a special camera and telemetry sensors, but too many parameters remain behind the scenes for him - a sudden gust of hot wind, a strange tingling sensation in the fingertips, an icy needle piercing the back of the head, the subtlest stalker premonition that does not extend to a piece of iron stuffed with electronics. I wandered around the labyrinth of anomalies for another five minutes, until finally a new safe route led me straight to the wide-open gates of the barn. Cautiously peering inside, I carefully examined the room, mechanically noting that not far from the entrance on the right wall sat a carnivorous invisible mushroom. The barn resembled a long hangar or an empty warehouse, divided by wooden and metal barriers into rows of animal pens. The feeders in the stalls had long since rusted to the ground, the silage in them had turned into a lumpy stone mass. The wind lazily walked around the room, penetrating through broken windows, a leaky roof and an impressive gap in the wall. It was light enough inside for me to see several bald spots of gravity concentrate, which had pulled the remains of rotten straw towards themselves and pressed them into the concrete floor. The nearest fences were noticeably bent in their direction. Well, there you go. A quarter of the job is done. The combination of anomalous fields we were interested in was located at the far end of the barn. Nothing to be done about it. If a valuable artifact has even the slightest chance of ending up in the most inconvenient and dangerous place, then that's where it will end up, rest assured. I didn't even get upset about it, because I knew from the very beginning that this was how it would be. At the very entrance I had to perform an almost circus trick. The doorway was blocked by a small gravity concentrate and a meat grinder, leaving a passage about thirty centimeters wide between them. Pathogenych led me to the starting position, I marked the boundaries of the anomalies with bolts, and then, sideways, sucking in my stomach, I moved between them in tiny side steps. The situation was further aggravated by the long locks of rusty hair hanging from the lintel: I had to bend down slightly so as not to catch my head in them. The meat grinder sparked behind my back when I squeezed dangerously close to it, but I apparently did not get close enough for a full discharge. It took me about twenty minutes to get to the far end of the room, although the distance there was only about forty steps. I had to circle around in one place for a long time, obeying Pathogenych's commands, climb over barriers, squeeze through narrow gaps between deadly dangerous areas. In one place I even covered a couple of meters on my hands, clinging to a low-hanging ceiling beam and tucking my legs in - the horizontally oriented frying pan could hit me with a stream of flame below. In the far corner, a dried-up, almost mummified corpse of pseudoflesh was discovered, in front of which lay a pile of huge, cleanly gnawed bones - cow bones, I think, because the bones of large mutants have a much more bizarre shape. One bone was sticking out of the wrinkled mouth of the pseudoflesh. I don’t know if the animals were evacuated from here after the first accident. It’s unlikely - there was barely enough transport to take people out. So, the unfortunate cows remained here. It’s unlikely that their decomposing carcasses lay around until the second explosion to become prey for mutants, after all, twenty years had passed since then, and up to now, all forty - so they fell into the hands of stray dogs back in the last century. Or even more likely, they were removed after the first accident by special liquidation squads, who built a large cattle burial ground not far from the abandoned livestock complex, which was dug up and destroyed by blind dogs four decades later - that’s where these bones came from. For the pseudoflesh, the barn became a deadly trap. You can't feed on bare hard bones. She probably got here during another emission - maybe by accident, or maybe she was trying to hide. I don't know if mutants hide from emissions like people, or if for them it's like a light summer rain. I'm afraid no one knows. At least, there are no fools to crawl outside during this local cataclysm and take a look, and those who still dare to crawl out will never tell anyone anything again. In any case, after the emission, the anomalies changed their location, new traps appeared in previously clean places - and the pseudoflesh found itself locked in this damned barn. Pseudoflesh are very sensitive and cautious creatures, unlike dogs, they extremely rarely get into anomalies, and if this one did not sense a way back in an almost continuous anomaly field, then there was none for her. Where I squeezed sideways right up to the deadly anomalies, the pseudo-flesh, twice as wide as me, would not have been able to pass. The creature waited a long time for the passage to finally open, until it finally died of hunger. I carefully turned my head. There it is. Two large meat grinders, fused together at the sides, visible even to the naked eye. To the side, a bird carousel lazily spins a dusty spiral. A little further away is a springboard, closer to me is a medium-sized gravity concentrate. All this is concentrated in a tiny patch of space, all this breathes, pulsates and waits for an idiot to come closer like a multi-headed hydra. And it generates a unique anomalous field capable of generating a precious artifact. “We’ve arrived!” Pathogenych’s cry came from outside, confirming my suspicions. I raised my head and, squinting, began to carefully study the roof and ceiling beams. The devil's egg has negative weight, so you should look for it under the roofs of half-ruined buildings, under the ceilings of basements, in the dense crowns of trees that prevent it from soaring into the sky. Perhaps this explains the extreme rarity of devil's eggs: most of them, after they appear, simply fall upward and disappear forever into space. No, nothing is visible. However, from this point I could only see part of the roof, crossed by diagonal beams. I told my partner that I needed to circle around the area, took a few more steps, obeying the invisible Pathogenych’s commands coming through the window: “Corner seven, we’re waiting, right three, four twelve!” – and approached the corpse of pseudo-flesh, meticulously studying the wavy slate surface above my head. After which the dead mutant's right eye, seemingly permanently bulging in death throes, jerked around and stared at me..

Soon I regained the ability to move. But now I had nowhere to go. I had lost Dinka again, lost her for the third time in the last twenty-four hours, and it seemed like forever. The Marked One was going to turn her into the same creature he was, and I was sitting powerless in the Red Forest with only a knife and could do nothing about it. Unlessto cut my throat so that this monstrous grief would no longer be screwed into my brain like a jagged, hot screw. No. There are no hopeless situations in principle. There are no, motherfucker, Dima Shukhov, you damn traitor, hopeless situations! Your immortal aphorism always saved me, even then, four years ago on the Hollow Hills, even when the "tourists" and I were buried in the catacombs of the Dark Valley after the explosion of a powerful deep-vacuum munition, even when Mukha and I ran into a bandit ambush in the Wild Lands and it seemed that we were in complete and utter ruin. Why is it that now, for the first time in my entire stalker career, I have finally found myself in an absolutely hopeless situation - and precisely when my life is not in danger?! Why are you doing this to me, Dima, Dima... By the way, what were you saying about corn, you black bastard?.. I rose slowly from the ground, not taking my eyes off the dark green island that suddenly caught my eye in the midst of the red and brown surroundings. Black Stalker, by the way, acted quite logically. He had saved me more than once before – and this time he saved me too. Because if Dinka and I hadn’t been able to enter the bunker, we would have started looking for another way to get out. Maybe we would have decided to sit it out in the well for a while, so as not to get caught by the Monolithists who had occupied the building. In short, they would have finished me off either right there, in the well, or already at the top, if we had managed to get out. Entering the bunker was my only chance to survive, and this chance worked. Black Stalker tried to save my life – and as a result, he made it so that I myself brought Dinka to the Marked One and handed her over to her loving daddy. Now it’s clear why Dima behaved so strangely when he came out to our fire with Goose at night – he already knew that either Dinka was destined to join the Masters of the Zone, or I was destined to die. Shukhov probably understood that the first option would be worse than death for me, and that was why he was so reluctant to share the code to the bunker's electronic lock. However, he apparently saw no other way out. In a word, the Black Stalker betrayed me - meanly and completely. Burn in your icy hell, scum. However, he dropped something about corn. That I shouldn't, he said, poke my nose into something a blind dog wouldn't stick his nose into. Curious, curious... A small patch of tall, ugly corn was located at the very edge of the Red Forest. I don’t know where it came from – maybe some time ago, some Pripyat resident arranged a garden plot for himself outside the city. It seems that at that time it was terribly fashionable and was called either a squatting or a hacienda. It’s not that the gardener chose a suitable place for this, but the Soviet people, they say, were generally distinguished by a rare indifference to their own health and future. A whole nation of stalkers, then. Or maybe everything is much simpler and the half-digested seeds were brought in the stomachs of rats or crows. Over the years, the corn on the plot mutated and grew into an impenetrable thicket. That’s where Red Shukhov advised me not to go. And that means that’s where I’ll go. Because he already screwed me over once, and now I’ll do everything the other way around. No, maybe he did leave me some chance to try to change everything. "Will you talk me out of it?" - that's what he said. There's something in that corn that will still allow me to fight. An underground passage leading back to the Marked One's bunker? A single-seat helicopter? A Monolith cache with a set of protective equipment that allows me to safely cross the Radar's kill zone? The Black Stalker made it clear to me that using this thing would be extremely risky, but he still left me one last tiny chance. Because otherwise it's unfair. Because otherwise there is no freedom of choice and I'm just a puppet in his hands, and I won't forgive even the Black Stalker for that. He still gave me a choice - to leave without Dinka or to die trying to save her. I was frantically breaking through the tangled branches and leaves of the giant, ugly corn, furiously chopping them with a knife, pushing aside the unyielding trunks, pushing my way through the thicket like a wounded bear through old windfalls, frantically looking around - where? Where is this damned thing, because of which I should not have climbed into the corn? Where is it?.. And then I suddenly saw it - when, in the heat of the moment, I had almost flown right into its middle. And I stopped dead in my tracks. It turns out that the Black Stalker's sense of humor is also black. Blacker than anything. Black as the guardian devil. I slowly approached this crap, almost point-blank, and stopped, silently examining the sparkling anthracite shimmers on the huge green stalk. It looked as if someone had splashed tar from a half-liter mug into the corn thickets, the viscous liquid slowly stretched downwards and soon solidified into thick, greasy streaks and weightless web threads, glittering in the light like Dinky's black eyes. A treacherous thing: anyone who rushes headlong, without looking back, through the thickets will certainly fall into the set nets. It is especially dangerous at night. In the dark, it is better not to touch anything at all - if the invisible mushroom does not bite off your fingers, then you will fly into the web of the guardian devil or into blue tin. Fortunately, this black sticky abomination has become very rare lately, but once upon a time, according to the recollections of old-timers, all the trees on Military and in the Dark Valley were hung with it. At first, many tramps picked up a mental parasite through it, some even liked it at first, but only Stalker Ghost managed to survive with it. If you can call it life, of course. I wonder if anyone else, besides the Ghost, has ever thought of voluntarily getting into a guardian devil to resolve a hopeless situation? They say it's much worse than death. As far as I understand, it's roughly the same as selling your soul to a real devil. At least, I've never heard of such cases. Of course, a person who has hooked a guardian devil gains incredible advantages, he starts getting lucky in literally everything - from finding artifacts to choosing a safe road, but all this luck is leveled by the fact that you can't get any benefit from it at all. Such a lucky person immediately begins to leave a trail of many accidental deaths, and he becomes an outcast. Everyone avoids him like he has the plague, like he is infected with the brown stuff, because anyone who exchanges even a few phrases with him, who touches him, who he winks at, or who he addresses by name, will die within a few hours – from an accident, from a sudden incurable disease, from a bullet, from poisoning with bootleg vodka… He won’t be able to sell his swag to anyone, because anyone who touches the artifacts he has obtained or tries to resell them will die. He won’t be able to buy himself a weapon or equipment, because anyone who sells him anything will die. He won’t be able to get food or medicine, he won’t be able to spend the night in the Stalker bar, he won’t be able to get vital information – no fools will help him. He won’t be able to leave the Perimeter either – and it’s probably not by chance, the guardian devil simply doesn’t let his victims leave the contaminated territory, from which he receives energy nourishment… And after some time, he will go crazy and disappear without a trace in the Zone. The military has probably already tried to use the mental parasite for their own purposes, but since so many years have passed and no military developments in this direction have been heard of, I think the experiments were unsuccessful. The Guardian Devil indiscriminately destroys everyone with whom his protege even indirectly contacts, be it casual acquaintances, lab assistants or the top generals of the superpower. I suppose the price seemed excessive to them. And good for that. I slowly lowered myself to the ground, crossing my legs Turkish style. Squinting, I looked up at the black blob with a fringe of cobwebs swaying in the wind above my head. I clasped my throbbing temples with my palms. Here it is, your chance, stalker. Here it is, the real alternative. Get into the crap, and the insane physical laws of the Zone will be on your side. You will pass through the Radar coverage area without any protective devices. You will throw away a squad of Monoliths in exoskeletons and with Gauss rifles with your bare hands. You will take the Marked One by the throat and save your beloved. However, you will not be able to leave the Zone. And in a couple of days you will go crazy from continuous luck and disappear forever somewhere in the Wild Lands or Death Valley, like dozens of such poor fellows before you... I had my own theory about where the lucky ones go. After all, if they continued to have such fabulous luck as before, they could wander around the Zone forever, successfully finding food and first aid kits in other people's caches or taking them out of the backpacks of dead stalkers. However, tramps with a mental hanger-on behind them disappear because such happiness cannot last forever. It is quite possible that, having pumped over a certain amount of someone else's luck and redirected it to his master, the guardian devil at some point changes the sign to the opposite and begins to attract misfortunes with terrible force. And then yesterday's lucky guy slips on level ground, breaks his leg, runs into a sharp branch with his eye, trying to get up, hits a springboard, breaks his back, stubbornly crawls forward, feeling the sharp teeth of rats that have crawled out of the ruins dig into his legs, heading straight into the heat invisible in the sun... That's why there are not even corpses left of them - because pathological bad luck continues to haunt the owner of the guardian devil even after death. The tortured corpse is twisted by lightning, torn to pieces by hungry dogs, ground up by wandering anomalies... And who knows, maybe when you are incredibly lucky once again, when you escape alive from marauders, when a chimera runs past without showing any interest in you, when a devil's egg, instead of killing you on the spot, obediently dives into a container for artifacts - is it because somewhere nearby in the bushes lies the hidden corpse of a former lucky guy, whose posthumous luck the guardian devil sitting on his shoulders stubbornly continues to redirect to everyone who happens to be nearby?.. I slowly rose from the ground. Rubbed my instantly frozen palms together. Stepped toward the black blot of the guardian devil, almost pressing my face into it. No, nothing special: mold and mold. Black, greasy, disgusting to look at. Slowly and carefully, I stretched out my hand toward it. For a moment, it seemed to me that the black muck swayed, moved toward my fingers. But it was probably just the wind… Lowering my hand, I stepped forward with determination, tearing a black spot of hellish mold with my chest with a quiet crack. Untidy pieces of cobweb hung on my jacket. My body desperately resisted the mind, pushing it to suicidal actions. Blood rushed to my face, my legs refused to obey, an icy stream of liquid nitrogen suddenly tore through my spine from the bottom up when I turned around and, for good measure, plunged into the thickets of corn, glued together by the deadly mold of luck, with my back. It was like in that movie where one submariner had to descend to an extremely high depth: his lungs would inevitably be flattened under such pressure, so he had to fill them with an incompressible liquid supersaturated with oxygen. It couldn't cause any harm, but the body definitely regarded the water in the lungs as a mortal threat, and for some time after the liquid entered the diver would suffer excruciating agony until his subconscious came to the conclusion that everything was fine and calmed down. Something similar was happening to me now - I didn't feel any external influence, pain or discomfort, but the body was simply going crazy, demanding that this abuse be stopped immediately. Cold sweat appeared all over my body, my stomach was convulsively contracting, my throat was desperately sore, as if from an excess of adrenaline. The body protested, refusing to die a horrible death, even though it was supposed to happen only a few days later. But I was killing myself right now, in these terrible moments. I turned back to face the torn-to-pieces guardian devil, falling from the corn stalks to the ground. My breathing was gradually returning to normal, my ears were no longer burning hotly, and the nausea was no longer rising in spurts in my throat. I wonder if that's enough? Does this stuff get hooked through my clothes and protective suit?.. Just in case, I scooped up some black mold and rubbed it between my palms with quick movements, as if I were washing my hands with soap. With disgust, I forcefully smeared the edge of my palm on my neck. Well, now for sure. The tarry muck stuck to my palms like fuel oil, and both hands immediately began to itch terribly, although this was most likely just a psychosomatic reaction of the body. From the stories of the old people, I knew that the guardian devil himself is not felt at all. I burst out of the corn thickets with a bang, climbed a hillock, and looked towards the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant. Closing my eyes, I threw my head back, and exposed my face to the autumn wind. I wonder if some time needs to pass for the guardian devil to take effect, or if he is already firmly seated behind my shoulders? However, I don’t have time to wait anyway, every minute counts now. If he hasn’t taken effect yet, then so be it with the Black Stalker. I’ll have to suffer less. I descended the hill and moved towards the border of the space scorched by the Radar. The guardian devil had already saddled me. At least, it seemed so, because Radar was silent - I did not feel the slightest discomfort, nor the slightest signs of a brain disorder. Maybe the brain-crusher suddenly broke down, or overheated, or another routine ten-minute period for cooling the system or its preventive maintenance had just arrived. My body was silent too - apparently, for today it was already tired of reacting in vain to mortal danger. I quickly walked along the black earth, raising miniature clouds of gray ash, and I absolutely did not care about anything. I was following Dinka, and not a single freak could stop me now. A sniper's bullet hit the ground right in front of me, scattering the wet turf far and wide. My body immediately woke up and screamed that I needed to fall backwards immediately, but I gritted my teeth and stubbornly continued to walk. Either the guardian devil would fix this problem on his own, without my participation, or I would get a bullet in the head and this crazy day would finally end. Both options suited me just fine. The second bullet whined in front of me, after which the sniper fell silent. There could have been any number of reasons for this, from an unexpected weapon failure to a sudden heart attack. There are so many absurd accidents in life. I moved forward resolutely. I had no thoughts at all, no plans in my head – for example, about how I would break through the triple barbed wire fence, how to find the entrance to the Marked One’s bunker, how to break into this fortress with its iron hermetic doors. Let the guardian devil worry about all this. I sacrificed myself to him, and that’s not so little, so let him work it off. I crossed the scorched space and headed for the barbed wire fence. There were several Monoliths wandering around the area on the other side, and several more sentries were positioned on towers at the corners of the barbed wire perimeter. Excellent. Without hiding, I walked at full height toward the gate. The Monolith men did not waste time on menacing shouts and warning shots in the air. The machine gunners on the towers immediately turned their monsters in my direction. However, two towers did not open fire - one of them was blocked from firing by the roof of a two-story administrative building, and something happened to the shooter on the second - at least, while I was moving towards the fence, he did not move, did not change his position. I think he could really use a doctor. The machine gun on the watchtower closest to me managed to fire only one short burst, which passed well above my head, after which its barrel exploded with a roar, maiming the gunner with shrapnel. The other machine gunner fired twice, after which he also fell silent and began to fuss around his dead weapon: I would not have been very surprised to learn that he had carelessly taken an almost empty magazine of cartridges to the guardhouse. Meanwhile, the patrolmen on the grounds were getting ready to fire their gauss rifles. One of them apparently couldn't see me very well through the triple row of barbed wire, so he stepped off the beaten path along the fence and stuck the long barrel of his rifle between the wires. It was a mistake, because his aiming device caught the tripwire of an anti-personnel mine on the outer row of the fence. The explosion knocked him onto his back, and the barbed wire in all three rows fell away at once, creating a passage large enough for a man to crawl through: the fragments of the mine flew so well that they cut the barbed wire in several places. It's understandable, because today is my day. I took the direction of the resulting passage and moved towards it straight through the minefield in front of the security perimeter, absolutely sure that wherever I stepped, there would be a mine-free space. One of the Monolith soldiers tried to shoot at me, but at the last moment before the shot he stumbled, jerked the barrel of his rifle and slammed an armor-piercing bullet into the polarized visor of his colleague's helmet. Another fighter dropped his gauss and fell to his knees, helplessly scraping his gloved fingers across his chest and helmet - I don't know what happened to him there. In short, I penetrated the barbed wire fence unhindered. Beyond the fence, the usual chaos of an abandoned construction site reigned, typical of the Zone's upper levels. Concrete channels lay in stacks, like those used to build the checkpoint in the scorched territory. Steel pipes of various diameters lay everywhere. Concrete air vents emerged from the ground, and cables and radio masts stuck out from unnaturally regular-shaped mounds covered with steel sheets and rectangular hatches. It was completely impossible to find the entrance to the bunker in this mess. As if in response to my thoughts, a hatch in the side of one of the hills swung open, and a young SSP fighter without a helmet or weapon cautiously peered out. I immediately walked towards him. Seeing me, he quickly slammed the hatch shut, but it did not close completely: something hard had gotten between the hinges. When I approached the entrance to the bunker and, having swung the hatch back, climbed inside, the boy was no longer there: he had run off somewhere into the inner rooms. Well, sit quietly, brother, and most likely you will not suffer. And pray, if you raised the alarm. I walked down the concrete stairs past the fast-asleep guard with indifference. The other one was nowhere to be seen – he was probably suffering from diarrhea in the toilet. There was no need to tell me the way any further; I remembered the corridor through which the two Monolith bastards were dragging me quite well. All the doors I passed in the corridor were tightly sealed or closed so that the Monoliths in the bunker would not notice me ahead of time. Surely each of them had some super-urgent and super-important matter to attend to. Well, that was wise. As an ostrich says in such cases, do what you must and come what may, and then we'll see who has more aces up their sleeve. A sudden, disturbing thought struck me. When exactly would the devil reverse course and rain down numerous misfortunes on my head? Could it be that the amount of luck he transfers to his master is always the same, so if I spend it quickly, generously, on some global scale that significantly exceeds what the theory of probability allows - say, forcing him to turn off the Radar unexpectedly or helping me penetrate a fortified bunker - could it be that the reserve of luck allocated to me would be depleted much faster than if I used it to search for some trivial artifacts? Just in case, I had to assume that I had not days, but hours, maybe even minutes. Near the door to the room with the reservoirs, one of those Monoliths who had dragged me from here half an hour ago suddenly stepped out of the side corridor and blocked my way. It seemed that the Marked One had spoken to me through him. The devil knows, it seemed to be him – he was wearing a helmet then, and I could have been mistaken. “Stay put,” he ordered, pointing the Fort at me. Instead of answering, I showed him my hands, smeared with black mold, and he seemed to understand what was going on. At least something changed in his eyes, and without wasting any more time on conversation, he quickly pulled the trigger five times. And five times in a row, completely by accident, there was a misfire. A good pistol, the Fort, but too unreliable compared to its analogues. The cartridges in the barrel sometimes warp. I did not wait to see how the guardian devil would rid me of the pest this time. The amount of luck allotted to me could indeed be limited, and it was not worth spending it carelessly on such trifles. Therefore, simultaneously with the sixth misfire, my Bowie, lightning-fast snatched from my pocket, cut the enemy's throat. Desperately wheezing, the Monolith stepped back, and I carefully took the pistol from his raised hand. Putting the knife in my pocket and pushing the fresh corpse aside, I unscrewed the door handle and pulled it towards me. “Hello again,” I said, stepping over the high threshold and raising the pistol. Both metal cylinders were slightly open. In one of them my Dinka was lying and looking at me with wide open eyes. Eduard Borisovich was standing near the metal cabinet and clicking the switches. “Get away,” I said. “Keep your hands at your sides, bitch. Quickly!” - Ah, he's back already. Promptly. - The Marked One glanced at me over his shoulder. - I see, I see. Got himself into trouble with the Guardian Devil, idiot. Good boy, smart. Just forgot one little thing: I'm one of the Masters of the Zone! - He turned to me, shrugged with a guilty smile: like, did you see how it all turned out? - Here, I determine how everything should happen. I determine how the Guardian Devil should work, do you understand? In my presence, the physical laws of your world do not apply. Or rather, I determine whether they apply or not, this is my choice and my sacred desire. Got it, vagabond? "You don't know a damn thing, you clown," I said. "Well, let's bet ten: will it fire now or not, when the universal law of meanness works against your telekinesis? It's high time for one beauty to get out of the box and say goodbye to her daddy. "You're a fool, Hemul," Dinka's dad said regretfully. "You could have had a lot. As a result, you'll lose even the little you had." “I think you read too many American detective stories as a child,” I said, not taking the gun off him. Moving sideways to the cylinder with Dinka, I leaned over and began to carefully rip the wires out of its headboard, so that Baichurin wouldn’t get the idea at the last moment to start his infernal machine at the cost of his own life. “You’re talking in some Hollywood cliches…” The heavy glass cover of the tank suddenly fell from above, hitting me hard on the head and shoulders. After all, daddy was not at all sure whether he could stop the bullet fired by the owner of the guardian devil, so he tried to protect himself in another way. The "fort" was knocked out of my hand and fell inside the glass and metal sarcophagus. I felt my heart being squeezed by a merciless hand again. Only this time the grip was not gentle, but deadly. I gasped for air with my mouth wide open, bloody circles flashed in my eyes, crossed by a wavy line of fire, but at that moment something loudly cracked and flared in the metal cabinet behind my opponent. He rushed there in panic and began clicking the switches again, leaving me alone. The guardian devil continued to protect me. I got up from the floor and grabbed the knife. My keeper and I weren't going to leave our enemy alone. I rushed at him from behind, breaking all notions of a noble duel and a fair fight. However, he caught my movement and met me with a crushing mental blow. I again crouched on the floor, but he was again unable to finish me off - the metal mount of one of the ceiling lamps burst with a roar, and pieces of iron reinforcement and shards of glass rained down on the Marked One, who barely had time to cover his head with his hands. I immediately rolled to the side, trying to come to my senses and at the same time find a position for a new attack. It seemed the forces were equal. I couldn't reach the Marked One with a knife, and the guardian devil didn't let him kill me. Pat. We stood in different corners of the room, breathing heavily, looking askance at each other. Apparently, the realization of the impasse came to us at the same time. “I think we should discuss this,” Borisych said quietly. “Perhaps I really do want too much, demand too much. What if we come to a reasonable, mutually beneficial compromise that would allow each side…” He talked and talked, and with each word he uttered I felt more and more acutely that he was pulling my leg. That he needed to buy some time for some reason. But what he was trying to achieve, I could not understand. I only began to understand this when I moved forward again and an invisible force lifted me off the floor and pinned me against the opposite wall. - Well, that's it, - Marked One smiled kindly. - All I had to do was take control of your hanger-on. Half a minute. That's it, you've got no more luck. - He pointed his little finger at me, and I felt a heavy slap, from which my head jerked sharply to the right. - Puppy. Well, what was all that for? - Another slap in the distance, from the other side. A warm stream flowed cheerfully from my nose. - Do you need this, son-in-law? I certainly don't need it. It doesn't give me any pleasure... - My left arm began to twist out of the joint capsule, and I hissed in pain. The damned sorcerer crucified me on the wall and slowly, with relish, twisted my limbs, pinched arteries and capillaries, thrust thin needles of pain into nerve nodes. He forgot about everything in the world: that he was a higher being, that his daughter was in the room, that he was savagely torturing his own, so to speak, son-in-law. I passed out several times from the blinding pain, but the Marked One immediately brought me back to my senses. He was saying something, but I could not hear anything through the growing roar in my ears. He stood below, his head thrown back and looking at me with huge Dinka eyes, enjoying my helplessness and my torment. I tried with all my might to wriggle out of his invisible grip, but the forces were too unequal: it seemed as if I was pressed against the wall by a giant piece of clay, so that I was barely able to breathe. This went on for a long, long time. Perhaps several millennia. It went on until suddenly there was a pistol shot, my executioner fell backwards, and I fell off the wall. While Marked One was torturing me, Dinka picked up the Monolith's pistol that was lying in her crystal coffin and decided the outcome of the duel in her own way. The "Fort" is a good thing after all: it can easily work even after six misfires in a row. Borisych lay motionless on the floor in a pool of his own blood and brains. He shouldn't have spread his fingers like that. He decided that he didn't need to ask Dinka's opinion, that he could decide everything for her. In vain. In the fifteen years they hadn't seen each other, his Worm had grown sharp claws and teeth. And his own opinion the size of a three-story building. The gun fell out of her hand and I heard it hit the cement floor with a dull thud.

...Olga emerged from the cold depths of sleep again, but did not open her eyes. What's the point? She would fall asleep again anyway. And dream again on the given topic. Given six years ago, but still "uncovered" by no one. Not even by Olga herself. Although she knew the answer to the main question better than anyone else: where did the mutants in the Zone come from? From there. From Chernobyl and the settlements closest to the Zone. The outbreak of the Second Catastrophe was special. All subsequent outbreaks had and have a different nature. Now it is simply a radiation and light show accompanying the gravitational Emission. Dead noon, as stalkers say. But then the noon was Abnormal. The outbreak easily and casually (as if it were just repainting the fence pickets) turned people into monsters, into zombies, and those who were lucky - into hidden mutants, like Olga herself. “Lucky?” Olga sighed. “I doubt it. I certainly got off easy, all I got was a strong aversion to both the Zone and the outside world. Now I’m forced to suffer, unable to live in the Zone or leave it. And as compensation for such suffering – resistance to hypnosis and anomalies, but these are only proven, proven deviations. How many of them do I actually have? And how terrible are they? What if I just haven’t found myself in a situation yet where the monster inside me wants to break out?” Olga involuntarily opened her eyes again and sat up abruptly in bed. The drawn-out sound was repeated. Now she knew exactly what that sound was. A floorboard in the hallway had given way under someone's foot. Olga hurriedly felt for her overalls, quickly pulled on her clothes, shoved her bare feet into her boots and fished out her machine gun from under the bed. Carefully, trying not to make any noise, she approached the door and stood to the left of the opening. She managed to move the worn fire selector lever almost without a click. “I’ll shoot you,” someone boomed behind the door. The voice was vaguely familiar. Olga had heard it recently, but probably not more than once or twice. The girl tried to remember, but she couldn't. “Who’s there?” Olga couldn’t think of anything better to ask. - Major Brazhnikov, special detachment of the D-3 base. General Ostapenko invites you, madam, to his place. There is business. Refusal will not be accepted. So put on your ladylike things and go out. Leave the machine gun at home. - I... - Olga was a little confused. - Wait... I... will get myself in order. - That's what I'm talking about, - Brazhnikov said in a deep voice. - Just in case, Olya, keep in mind: my fighters are under the window. On the floor above and at the back door too. You have five minutes for everything. Time is running out. There was a short squeak behind the door. It seemed that the punctual Brazhnikov had indeed started the stopwatch. Olga went to the window and slightly pulled the curtain aside. Two special forces soldiers were smoking under the window. One of them managed to notice the centimeter-long movement of the curtain and waved his hand at Olga. Olga threw the machine gun on the bed, took off her overalls and began to dress like a human being. If General Ostapenko had sent a group for Olga, then there was a serious matter ahead. Most likely, an exit to the Zone. And she had to pack for it with all due care. Olga glanced sideways at the window. She desperately did not want to get involved with the military again. Nothing good could be expected from the head of counterintelligence of the International Isolation Force. Even if everything worked out and General Ostapenko really did simply give Olga a task, and did not exchange some exposed spy for her, either the task would be hopeless, or the prize for it would be "copper". - One minute left! - Brazhnikov warned. “Yes, I’m coming, I’m coming,” Olga said irritably. Besides one minute, she had at least one more chance. It was worth trying. She quickly rolled up the rug by the bed and opened a small hatch in the floor. Perhaps someone was really on duty at Brazhnikov's on the floor above, but it was unlikely in the basement. The entrance to this basement opened in only two places: in Olga's bedroom and in a small room in an inconspicuous house almost a kilometer to the south. Olga hung her machine gun on her back, leaned against the edge of the opening and jumped into the dungeon... where she was instantly laid face down in the damp earth and tied with wire ties by two fighters. A minute later, Olga found herself back in her bedroom, only her hands and feet were tied. - You don't respect me, young lady. - Brazhnikov broke off a splinter where the lock had been knocked out, entered and stopped opposite Olga. - What are you afraid of? “Ostapenko promised to hand me over to Interpol,” Olga answered sullenly. “Do I need that?” - Put your fears aside. - The major nodded to the soldiers, and Olga was untied. - There is a matter in your profile. You need to go to the Zone and, most importantly, return back. The rate is decent and the company is not bad. - What do you mean "not bad"? - Olga looked at the major with suspicion. - Yours? - You'll see there. - Brazhnikov grinned. - Get up, let's go... You'll like it. Fact..

Nobody likes night calls. The sharp sound is frightening, makes the body waste adrenaline and generally confuses. And if you have weak nerves or have serious sins, then night calls can even lead to a heart attack. Former mercenary and now free walker Andrey Lunev, aka the Old One, was not one of the timid ten, but for some time now he also did not like calls. Both night and day. After a serious skirmish with the "Black Angel" anomaly, which almost cost the lives of all the inhabitants of the Zone, the mercenary syndicate canceled the hunt for Andrey. For two months now, Lunev had been feeling relatively at ease. But his professional intuition and rich life experience did not allow him to calm down completely. Mercenaries forgave nothing and no one. Even if you were a hero three times over and a savior of the Zone from hostile intrigues, you would have to answer. At one time, Lunev betrayed the syndicate and for this offense he would have to be punished sooner or later. In a year or two, when the wave of Stary's popularity subsided and the favor of high-ranking military men became less obvious, Lunev would have to answer for everything. In addition, in addition to the syndicate, Andrei had many enemies from among "private individuals." For example, Max Kruger, nicknamed the Mechanic, who led the hunt for Stary. For failing to complete the syndicate's mission, he too flew out of the glorious ranks of mercenaries, like a cork from champagne. Or take the members of Kazakevich's underground enterprise, destroyed with the assistance of Stary. They are unlikely to forgive Stary for being left without a very profitable job. Or former members of the Black Angel group... Who knows who else wanted to put the Old One's head on a pike and throw it deeper into the Zone? And yet Andrey fell out of love with calls for another reason. When you are a lone wolf, experienced and skilled, there is nothing to worry about in principle. Will the syndicate start a new hunt? Weigh anchor, go to the Zone, and look for them there, citizens, don’t wear out your feet. As for “private individuals”, it’s really simple. If someone takes a risk... so much the worse for them. Andrey did not consider himself the toughest fighter in the Zone, but he was not inclined to underestimate his own capabilities either. If Strelok or Marked One were alive, the retired mercenary Stary would be much more worried, but in their absence the only real danger was the collective power of the syndicate and, perhaps, an attack by the state machine. Everything else was something like the threat of a runny nose. It exists, but it is stupid to take it seriously. However, this was the case until about Christmas. And then suddenly it changed. Tatyana appeared in Andrey's life again, this time seriously and for a long time, and it turned out that Lunev had someone to worry about. The Old Man felt for the phone, rejected the call and carefully freed the hand on which Tanya was sleeping. The picture that appeared on the screen told Andrey many things, but first of all that the subscriber would definitely call back. So that a new call would not wake up his friend, the Old Man got up silently and went into the kitchen. The subscriber was in no hurry to call back. Apparently, he was giving him time to wake up. Andrey was under no illusions. It was not a matter of the caller's tact, but of his prudence. It was better to wait five minutes and then talk to a normally thinking person than to waste time on repetitions and listen to a half-asleep mooing in response. The old man turned on the kettle and took instant coffee from the cupboard. Andrey could bet a million that there would be no time to make normal coffee. The "night caller" was General Ostapenko. This fact automatically meant that "sleep was out of the question tonight." The general's sophistication knew no bounds. He called back, as Andrey had expected, exactly five minutes later, but not to Stary, but to Tatyana. Lunev did not like this trick for two reasons. Firstly, it was too insidious, and secondly, it meant that Ostapenko intended to involve Tanya, a freelance employee of the scientific department of counterintelligence of MIS, in his new adventure. Okay, if she was an expert who would analyze the facts and evidence collected by Stary in the Zone "on the shore". But what if the general ordered her to go to the Zone with Stary? This monster would do anything. He did not care at all that Tatyana Sergeyevna, a tall, green-eyed, brown-haired woman, beautiful and, surprisingly, smart, was not suited to work in the Zone. The general believed that any means would do to achieve lofty goals (and Ostapenko did not set any others for himself). He sent people to certain death without blinking an eye, composed intricate combinations in which he cold-bloodedly sacrificed “pawns,” and forced his subordinates to spit on everything: from conscience to safety, just to complete the task. In general, Tatyana could not expect anything good from the duplicate call. The kettle switch clicked, and almost simultaneously the tongue of the bedroom door clicked. A sleepy Tatyana appeared in the kitchen, phone in hand. Squinting in the light, she glanced around and sighed. - Did they call you too? - She stifled a yawn. - Five minutes ago. Would you like some coffee? - The boss's manner. - Tanya nodded. - He didn't say what he wanted? - And you? – I didn’t have time to ask, he hung up. - Hooligan. - The old man grinned. At that moment the phone rang again, and Andrey pressed the answer button. “Get ready, both of you, I’m waiting in the hangar,” Ostapenko ordered briefly and sternly. - And "hello" to you too, good man, - the Old Man grumbled discontentedly. - Why now? The morning is wiser than the evening, don't you know? - Stop being so insolent. - The general softened his tone a little. - Assemble at zero thirty. Field uniform. Over and out. Andrey turned off the phone and thoughtfully turned it over in his hand. – The general switched to military jargon. “He’s a general,” Tatyana shrugged. - No, he usually expresses himself in a human way. And now, apparently, he is getting ready. Something will happen. By the way, he knows that we are... together now. - Scout. - Tanya took a sip of coffee and put the cup down. - Go alone anyway. I'll be there in about ten minutes. - Are you embarrassed? - Andrey looked at her from under his brows. - What? My past? "It's yours, and it's the past." She came up and pressed herself against the Old Man. "And it's not as terrible as you think." “I killed for money,” the old man shook his head. - No one has the right to judge you, Andrey. No one. At least not here, in the Zone. Go, meet me in the hangar...
...The last month of winter in the Zone differed little from the first. And it was also almost indistinguishable from late autumn or early spring. Except that the wind blew stronger, and the eternal rain did not fall around the clock, but only from morning until midday. But every day. As for the temperature, it did not fall below zero and did not rise above ten from December to early March. For lovers of coolness - simply paradise. The Old Man was not one of those fans. He preferred September weather. Almost summer, but not so hot. Or rather, not so much like a bathhouse. A warm shower, and that's it. From the Old Man's point of view, if it weren't for the anomalies and mutants, the Zone at this time could be considered the most comfortable place on the planet. True, in this case, hardly anyone would appreciate its charm. After all, without anomalies there are no artifacts, and without them - stalkers and traders, who, like sheep, were constantly tracked by wolves of all stripes: military, bandits and members of numerous groups. Stalkers obtained artifacts, brought clients to the Zone, earned money in other relatively honest ways, the military tried to control them, and bandits and others - to pinch them. Understandably, not everyone liked this, and conflicts often arose between the inhabitants of the Zone. And now these conflicts were closely monitored by mercenaries. He willingly helped everyone who could pay for the expensive services of the syndicate to eliminate competitors and especially annoying opponents. In general, gradually an almost natural ecosystem formed in the Zone, with its own weather, flora, fauna, population and anomalous specifics. If you get used to it, or rather, merge with it, or even better, dissolve in it, the Zone becomes, indeed, not such a hopeless place. Far from charming, but at least suitable for life. The old one had long since joined in and was firmly stuck, but had not yet dissolved. He still remembered what life was like outside the Zone, and was determined to return to it someday. Someday. And now... Andrey turned the corner onto the path leading to the hangar and almost collided with two subjects in paramilitary uniform. The Old Man knew one of them, the larger one, quite well. The other, the smaller one, he knew only in absentia and had only seen through a telescopic sight. However, in the Zone it was not customary to pretend that you were completely unaware of who was in front of you, if you had at least heard about the person from acquaintances. Upon seeing Andrey, the large acquaintance exhaled loudly and grumbled discontentedly: - How the bloodsucker appeared out of nowhere, motherfucker! “And what about the scoreboard?” the Old Man asked calmly. - No, well, no offense, but you can give yourself a heart attack like that! And I still have plans! Hi, Old Man. - Hello, Bibik. - Andrey shook the big guy's hand and looked at his companion. - Hello, Scout. - Hello, Old Man. - Scout stared at Andrey with interest. - How do you know me? - I saw you in the Zone. I thought you stayed there. In the ruins, not far from the sarcophagus. - It happened. - Scout grinned. - The Monoliths tried hard back then, but I survived, as you can see. I had to lie in the hospital twice as long as he did (Scout nodded at Bibik), but no matter, I'm ready for work and defense again. - Nice. - The old man pointed to the entrance to the hangar. - Are you coming here? - Where else? - Bibik nodded. - An alert. Ostapenko called personally. It looks like something serious is brewing. The wall of the hangar to the right of the entrance was suddenly illuminated by the headlights of an approaching car, from which two soldiers and one civilian quickly unloaded. This "face" was well known to both Stary and Bibik. The old man watched as the fighters, holding him by the arms, led the subject to the door, then watched the car drive away and grinned. - Oh my! I don't know how serious the matter is, but it's definitely murky. “It’s always like this with Ostapenko,” Scout noted. “Either speak well of the boss or say nothing,” Bibik said sternly. “Let’s go before you tell on the disciplinary battalion.” “And who is this young lady?” Scout asked, trotting after his comrades. “Olga,” Bibik answered briefly. - And that's all? - And that's all. The hangar was light, dry and clean, as is always the case at a military facility by the time the high command arrives. The two guards who had brought Olga were standing by the door, and the young lady was sitting at the far wall of the room at a folding table. Sitting opposite her, absorbed in reading some papers, was that very high command: General Ostapenko himself. Seeing the trio, the general glanced at his watch and nodded toward the plastic chairs arranged around the table. When Stary took one of the chairs, it became clear that in addition to the previously noticed characters, there was someone in the hangar who preferred to remain in the shadows for the time being. The blinds were lowered on the windows of the glass duty room fenced off in the left front corner, and someone was clearly hiding behind them. Stary moved the chair slightly so that Bibik was between him and the duty room. Not because he was afraid of a sniper, but simply out of habit. Ostapenko noticed Stary's movement, but said nothing. The general's face remained, as usual, impenetrable, and his gaze was cold and slightly distant. He looked at the guests like a chess player at his chess pieces. The only thing that could be guessed from the general's gaze was that in the new game Stary was assigned the role of the queen, no less. Why not Special Forces Colonel Bibik was still a mystery, but Andrei suspected that the answer would be simple. “Are we waiting for someone else?” the Old Man broke the prolonged silence. “We’re waiting,” Ostapenko replied and again became engrossed in reading the papers. Andrey shrugged his shoulders slightly and turned his gaze to Olga. She tried to look at the table, but every now and then she glanced sideways at the Old Man. - Mister General, - Scout tried to pick up the banner, - maybe some coffee? There should be a kettle in the duty room. “No,” Ostapenko said without looking at the stalker. “We’ll start in a minute.” Olga suddenly looked up, but not at the Old Man, but at the disappointed Scout. Her eyes showed clear interest. Andrey was not exactly hurt by the girl's change of priorities, but still slightly intrigued. Olga looked at Scout as if she was trying to remember where she had seen him. All these staring games ended, as the general had promised, exactly one minute later. Ostapenko carefully folded the papers into a leather folder, looked at his watch again and cast a displeased glance at the hangar's entrance door. At exactly the same second, the door opened and Tatyana entered the room. Of course, everyone paid attention to her. The old man simply watched her go, Ostapenko and Bibik nodded in greeting, and Olga narrowed her eyes suspiciously. But Scout studied the lady from head to toe. “Well, well,” the stalker said quietly, “even something good comes from Moscow.” “Close the window,” Bibik advised quietly, glancing at the Old Man. The scout's eyes widened in understanding and he fell silent. “So,” Ostapenko began, not waiting for Tatyana to take one of the two free seats, “you all understand that it was not without reason that I gathered you in such a strange combination. “And there was a reason they left the empty space,” Andrey noted. “That’s right.” The General stared at the Old Man with a searching gaze. “But before another man takes over, I must ask each of you if you agree to sign a contract for a raid into the Zone?” “On a deadly raid, you mean?” the Old Man clarified. - Yes. Does this scare you, Mr. Lunev? - No, Mr. Ostapenko. - Andrey easily withstood the general's inquisitive gaze. - I just want us to do without feints, omissions and empty formalities. You don't need to ask Colonel Bibik and the military guide Scout. Give them the order, and that's it. As for me... state the combat mission, and I'll think about it. Well, Olga will speak for herself. “I don’t agree,” Olga said quietly but firmly. "You have a simple choice, madam," Ostapenko retorted no less firmly. "Either to the Zone or behind bars. You are wanted on serious charges. I warned you at one time not to show up in the Borderlands, but you didn't listen." “I don’t agree!” Olga repeated stubbornly, staring at the tabletop. "The matter is closed." The general glanced at Tatyana. "I cannot order you, but I kindly ask you..." - Yes, of course. - Tatyana looked guiltily at the Old Man. - I'm ready. “I object!” Andrey frowned. - You haven't heard the introduction yet. - Ostapenko shook his head. - And anyway, you have no right to object, you're not a soldier. If you refuse, that's your right. But everyone else will go to the Zone. With or without you! - I wonder who will lead them? You've clearly decided to hold back Bibik. Then who? Scout? Andrey grinned. “What, am I second-rate?!” Scout was indignant, but quickly cooled down after receiving an elbow in the side from Bibik. “I’ll drive,” came from the duty room. The voice was distorted by a booming echo, but the Old Man still recognized it and tensed up, preparing for trouble. - Let's meet those who don't know each other, - Ostapenko suggested, carefully watching Andrey's reaction. - Max Kruger, nicknamed the Mechanic. A mercenary who was recently excommunicated from the syndicate for failing a mission. He failed to destroy the Old One. “What a statement,” Scout grinned and also looked at Andrey with interest. - And why do you need me in that case? - The Old Man managed to hide his emotions and looked unkindly at Ostapenko. - You can go get some sleep, I told you. - The general pointed to the door. - The exit is there. The Old Man did not move and did not answer. The pause lasted longer than a minute. Much longer. But even when, according to all theatrical canons, it was time for it to end, Andrei still did not voice his decision. However, Ostapenko's nerves were no weaker than the Old Man's. He did not show any impatience and calmly waited for the former mercenary to mature. The general knew that Andrei would agree. He was one hundred percent sure. And he was not mistaken. - I agree. - Excellent, - Ostapenko said in a businesslike tone. - Kruger, state your case. Tatyana Sergeyevna, listen carefully, your comments as a knowledgeable scientist will be needed. And yours, Lunev, as the most experienced walker. - Experienced! - Scout snorted quietly. - And bloodthirsty! “You should go with him,” Bibik whispered in the stalker’s ear. “Are you tired of living?” - Yes, I'm in the Zone... there was no Zone yet, and I already... - Scout stopped short. - Okay, let's forget about it! The mechanic stared at Andrey for a while, and Andrey responded to the mercenary in roughly the same way. In principle, the silent duel of the former comrades could have continued until the morning, but Kruger managed to control his emotions and began to tell his story. - Not long ago I went to the Zone and came across a strange sector. I won't describe it in detail, you'll see for yourself. In short, it's the worst mess I've ever seen in the Zone. The mutants are such that it's like they're pumped full of anabolics and angry, the bastards, as if their tails were smeared with turpentine. The anomalies are three times more powerful than anywhere else, and the artifacts are more serious. The ones in other sectors are roaches compared to those. In short, I brought a couple... He turned to Ostapenko. The general, as if reluctantly, took out of his pocket a rather large artifact, of the "Stone Flower" type, produced by the "Springboard" anomaly. The thing looked really large and had a special shade of inclusions. Usually the dots on the mirror surface of the artifact were yellow-red, but here the yellow color looked almost golden. The red one shimmered like a juicy garnet. "They studied it in the laboratory," Ostapenko reported. "The protective properties against gravitational anomalies such as "Funnel" and "Carousel" have been increased almost twofold. The protective field's effect against small arms is also higher than normal." “What about radioactivity?” asked Tatyana. - Almost the same as in ordinary specimens. “It’s interesting,” said Bibik. “Such an Eldorado, and no one has gotten their hands on it yet?” - Could you please give a little more detail? - asked Tatyana. - What anomalies are predominantly found in the sector where it is located, and what other oddities have you discovered? - I didn't go deep. - The mechanic shrugged. - Life is more important. But there are many strange things there, I bet. Flying creatures, for example. I haven't seen anything like that in other places. "Crows, or what?" Scout asked. - You're a crow yourself. - The mechanic looked down on the stalker. - Mutants. Like controllers, clumsy, toothy, but they also fly. They fly without wings. Levitate, if you want to call it that smart. “Plague!” Scout exclaimed. “I saw some like that not far from Lelev,” Stary noted. “And Tatyana Sergeyevna even used the services of this airline.” - Yes. - Tatyana winced at the unpleasant memory. - Four of them grabbed me, almost carried me away... somewhere. “Maybe to this strange sector?” Bibik suggested. “No,” the Mechanic replied confidently. “You can only get there on foot. It won’t work by air, I watched. Some rooks flew by – right past, but I chased the flesh along the same route – it entered calmly. And they ate it there, too calmly. The jerboas swooped down like piranhas, and gnawed it off in a flash. They’re the size of dogs and running around there… pure cheetahs, damn it. I barely escaped.” “Are there any settlements there?” the Old Man asked doubtfully. - Some kind of ruins. - The mechanic looked at the Old Man from under his brows. - Jerboas live in their cellars. What, you don't believe me? - I believe you, Max, - Andrey answered calmly. - But so far you haven't said anything special. I've seen plump mutants in Chernobyl, and flying creatures in a field near Lelevo. As for unusually powerful anomalies... there are plenty of them in the Novoshepelichi and greenhouse areas. The only thing I haven't seen yet is such large "Stone Flowers", but I've seen something cooler than any usual artifacts. - "Joker", for example, - Bibik said, nodding in agreement. - The old man is right: enough water, give me the meat. Is there anything really special in this sector? Andrey and the colonel's questions clearly stumped the mercenary. The mechanic looked questioningly at the general, who redirected the silent question to Tatyana Sergeyevna. - You didn't answer where the sector is located? - Tatyana came to the aid of the Mechanic. - Ah... well... in Chernobyl-2. - Kruger waved his hand vaguely. - That is, next to it. But you can't go straight to it, you have to make a couple of turns. In short, there's like an invisible path trodden there. While you're walking along it, you see the entrance to the sector, but if you step to the side, you don't see it. I wandered for a long time before I figured out this path. I spent almost a month walking in circles. - So much for the oddities. - Tatyana looked meaningfully at Bibik, and then at Andrey. - What does the entrance look like? - The old one hasn't given up yet. - Almost nothing. In short, there's a clearing, about forty meters in diameter, and a glow around it, like a greenish fog spreading in a circle. It's not high, a meter from the ground, and it's so transparent... on a sunny day, you probably can't even see it. - Where do you get them, sunny days? - Scout muttered. - And how can you see the path? - No way. - The mechanic became a fully confident mercenary again. - Here she is. He tapped his finger to his temple. “Really, did it all fit?” Scout asked ironically. Kruger remained silent. Instead of answering, he looked pointedly at the stalker and ran his hand across his throat. - Gentlemen! - Tatyana intervened. - Stop it! Like little children, honestly! Max, do you remember any other oddities? I'm not interested in mutants or anomalies, but in the strange properties of the area, the sector itself. Does it look like it's migrating or mothballed? - No, nothing like that. - Kruger paused for a second. - But actually... Anyway, when I went in there, I went north, and when I came out... also north. I went there five times, once I tried to enter from the east, but it was always the same. The way you looked when you entered, that's the way you looked when you left. Is that weird? - You bet. - Tatyana thoughtfully drummed her fingers on the table. - Oh, crap, - Scout suddenly said condescendingly. - It's not strange at all, it's a typical amoebi... or whatever it's called... a loop, to put it simply. - Mobius sector? - Tatyana perked up. - What, it's possible! - Typical! - Bibik chuckled. - Have you seen many of them? - Not a single one. - Scout spread his hands. - But I've heard of them a hundred times! Many have wandered into such places. And the boys told me about fat mutants, and about super-duper anomalies. But no one has ever brought such large artifacts. - Because your boys were telling stories. - Bibik raised his hand, forestalling Scout's indignant remark. - The Zone has been chattering about this mythical "Mobius Sector" for a long time, but to no avail. No one can pinpoint exactly where it is located. “Now it can,” Andrey said reluctantly. “Mr. General, is this what you wanted to hear?” - Yes, Andrey. - Ostapenko nodded. - You made the right conclusions, which means I was not mistaken, you will succeed. - What exactly should happen? Or are you suggesting that I figure that out on my own? If so, I'm out. “Me too.” Scout folded his arms across his chest and, sprawled in his chair, slid a little under the table. - Olga, maybe you can guess? - The old man looked at the girl. - Otherwise you're silent like a fish all the time. “I don’t understand what you’re talking about at all,” Olga muttered. “And it’s equally important to me.” “I can explain briefly,” Tatyana suggested. There was a friendly note in her voice, but Olga seemed unmoved. Olga was in no hurry to accept the helping hand extended by Tatyana Sergeyevna out of a sense of female solidarity. However, Tatyana was not at all embarrassed by the cool attitude of her future sister in arms. With the general's tacit consent, she tore off a narrow strip of paper and constructed a simple Mobius strip. - I think everyone knows what it is. The sector that Max discovered has approximately the same curvature of space. Simply put, the strange sector is located on the side of the tape that is invisible to us. But if we go forward, we will pass through the invisible sector and... return to its visible part, to the starting point. “Face in the same direction,” Bibik added. - Exactly. As easy as pie! “Simple,” Olga reluctantly agreed. “What’s the point? Well, sector, well, Mobius, what next?” – The thing is that such a sector cannot be compact. Do you understand? - No. – Specialists in the so-called “territorial anomalies” conditionally divide the Zone into twelve sectors, each thirty degrees. Conditionally because in reality some sectors are slightly larger, others slightly smaller, but deviations from the template are insignificant. All changes, such as the migration of individual territories, the formation of new anomalous areas of the terrain, the opening or closing of some areas for so-called conservation, occur strictly within the boundaries of their own, that is, one of these twelve, sectors. Even the famous migrating sector of Limansk actually moves exclusively within the boundaries of its “large” sector, one twelfth of the entire area of the Zone. It’s just that others shift five kilometers, no more, and this one drifts a distance of up to twenty. “I’ll break your brains now,” Olga said through her teeth. “Can’t you make it simpler?” - I'm trying. The Mobius sector, by all calculations, cannot be part of any of the twelve large sectors of the Zone. In order for mutants to be able to live there fully, for anomalies to rage, and most importantly, for a serious amount of anomalous energy to accumulate there, this sector must completely occupy one of the twelve parts of the Zone... - But all these parts have been studied and do not look like a strange sector, right? - Olga finally went to meet him. - Exactly! - Tatyana hinted at a smile. - And where is it located, in that case? Underground, like the secret city under Pripyat? - Unlikely. - That's a question for citizen Mobius, - Bibik intervened. - Somewhere. We don't care about the details. What's important to us? To mark the entrance and exit, to study as best we can what kind of "lost world" is there, and to find out why the inhabitants of the outer sector have been appearing on the screen more often than "chocolate-clad blondes" lately. Am I right, Mr. General? - Everything is correct, Bibik. - And there's something else to keep in mind, - Bibik became inspired. - This sector is essentially another anomaly. Which means that, like all decent anomalies, it may have artifacts. I suggest taking this into account when studying the sector. “I don’t mind,” Ostapenko agreed again. - Well, yeah, what's there to argue about? - Scout grumbled. - It's not you who's going to fall into an anomaly with your ass. - Did you say something, military guide? - Ostapenko pretended not to hear. - Always ready, I say. - Scout sighed. - Where haven't I gone... “I went wherever I was sent,” Bibik added. - Exactly. But I've definitely not been sent to another dimension yet. “It’s not a completely different dimension...” Tatyana tried to get back on her favorite hobbyhorse. - Got it, got it! - Scout raised his hands again. - Hidden sector! - Sector thirteen, - Andrey suggested his option. - Hard to reach and extremely anomalous. We need to find out why. Unlike Scout, who was mainly interested in security issues, or Tatyana, who was mostly interested in the unusualness of the sector, Lunev understood that in the Thirteenth, the group of scouts would face many more mysteries and problems than the walkers could imagine now, sitting "on the shore", in a warm hangar. The General, as usual, voiced barely half of what he knew himself. So all the guesses of Stary, Bibik and the others did not clarify the real situation at all. In order to at least understand a little what Ostapenko expected from the raid, Stary had to think over all the details, and at the same time remember all the conversations with Ostapenko, in which the Zone and the general's main headaches were discussed. For example, the conversation in the Som bar, which took place immediately after the defeat of the Black Angel group, when the general admitted that without Andrei, most likely, he would not be able to identify a particularly cunning and dangerous enemy, who had long been opposing the MIS headquarters and the general personally, as the head of counterintelligence. Lunev concentrated and recalled almost word for word what Ostapenko had said then... "I have a suspicion that the black angels were not financed from the mainland at all," Ostapenko said, thoughtfully examining the glass. "Do you remember Colonel Dorogin? When he came to, he told me that Kazakevich mentioned a whole network of underground laboratories. I think that Kazakevich was not in charge of this network, just as the "Black Angel" group was not commanded by Angels Alpha and Beta. But this mysterious puppeteer is not a millionaire from outside the Zone. In addition to the general approach to the matter, there is much in both stories that people from the mainland do not know and do not want to know. This is not without reason. - Both adventures were conceived and planned by one person, and this was one of the inhabitants of the Zone, did I understand correctly? - Andrey clarified. - A person or an initiative group, - it is not yet clear. I can only repeat with complete certainty: the adventures have a single style, and they were invented directly in the Zone. - And what are these adventurers trying to achieve? - I don't know. Maybe they want to take control of the Zone, or maybe they just want to make money, quickly and in large quantities. Actually, I need you, Lunev, to understand this. Colonel Bibik says that your brain is not in the right place, but I believe that even if it is shifted, it is in the right direction. And exactly as much as is required for successful work in the Zone. Do you get it? - You speak in a complicated way, but I get it. Do you have any clues? Besides the approach to the matter. - None. - Ostapenko spread his hands. - If they were available, I would have developed the topic myself, without you. Naked theory and logic are all we have...
...Now Andrey understood that Ostapenko had not forgotten that conversation either. Especially the phrase about the brains being shifted "as much as necessary". Otherwise, he would not have assembled such a motley team and would not have appointed Stary as its leader. "A non-standard approach to non-standard matters, that's all right." These thoughts did not at all imply that Ostapenko considered the Thirteenth Sector to be the source of all problems. Most likely, the general expected to find in it those very clues, hints that would help in the investigation of the complicated case of the secret patron of all local adventurers. “If, of course, Ostapenko is not mistaken, and this patron really exists.” - Don't rack your brains, - Bibik said, distracting Andrey from his thoughts. - You'll get your bearings on the spot, it'll be safer that way. It's not your first time going to the Zone. Stary looked around the group and stopped at Tatyana. She was the weakest link, and this worried Andrey the most. Stary did not hope to convince Ostapenko, but a compromise was still possible. “I need another experienced fighter,” Andrey stated firmly. “I knew you’d say that,” Ostapenko said a little tiredly. “Don’t count on Bibik. I need the colonel here at the base. He’ll keep his finger on the pulse and, if anything happens, he’ll bring the ‘cavalry’, I promise.” - I need a real fighter, not a virtual one! “Take your pick,” the general nodded toward two special forces soldiers at the entrance to the hangar. “The base is full of eagles like that.” - Mister General, - the Old Man winced, - we agreed, no tricks! - You agreed on that, - said Ostapenko. - Okay, let it be "no tricks". The sixth member of the group will join you a little later. He is busy now. And don't look at me like a policeman looks at a thimblerigger. The fighter will be real, as you like to say. Realer than anything. - How will he find us? - The route is written down from the words of the Mechanic. Do not deviate, and everything will be fine. Any other questions? – Are there any restrictions on the choice of weapons? - None. The arsenal is yours entirely. And also, Andrey... The general fished a briefcase out from under the table and carefully placed it on the table. He hadn't even touched the locks of the case yet, but Lunev already knew exactly what was inside. The general's expression was too solemn and at the same time deliberately displeased. He was clearly violating some staff instruction, but he was doing it with hidden pleasure. As if he was secretly taking revenge on his staff colleagues who issued stupid orders and scribbled useless, and sometimes harmful, instructions in the Zone. - There are five copies here, the sixth will have his own. - Has your conscience awakened? - Lunev grinned and took one of the five "Joker" artifacts from the case. - Oh! - Scout began to fuss. - This is... him, right? "The Joker"? I heard a lot about him in the hospital, but I've never seen him! Does he really protect against any anomalies and scare away mutants? “And it also protects you from bullets and heals wounds in no time,” added Bibik. “Even fatal ones.” - Class! “You should believe more in all these tales,” the Colonel smiled indulgently and patted Scout on the shoulder. - What, a lie?
