Then what are these glass things for? I'd rather take an extra pack of cartridges! - That's right, - said Andrey, - it protects. And from anomalies, and from animals, and even from psi-blows. I tested it on myself. Just don't relax and it's better not to deliberately climb into anomalies. And it will deflect bullets if they come from far away. If someone fires at point-blank range, it will hit easily, keep that in mind. “And the Joker won’t save you from cold weapons either,” added the Mechanic, also trying on the artifact. “From a shuriken, for example.” He raised an inquisitive look at the Old Man. He just spread his hands. There was nothing like that in his arsenal today. Andrey intended to use only the standard weapons. Although, if you dig around well, you can find anything in the basements of Ostapenko's weapons room. From a bow to a MANPADS, from a fusee to an advanced assault rifle and from a Swiss folding knife to a broadsword. Katana, shurikens, nunchucks and other oriental exotica too. And this in the usual three dimensions, without any hidden Mobius sectors. - Shall we go to the bins? - Andrey looked at Ostapenko. - You know the way. From now on, you are in command, Lunev. - The general took the documents out of the folder again. - Bibik is coordinating. Departure at six in the morning. Everyone is free..

Nobody really knows what telepathy is. A direct connection between two minds or a mental entry into a special information space and a connection with the subscriber's mind through this astral, like through the Internet. Perhaps the mechanism is fundamentally different, and the ordinary mind is unable to imagine it. Be that as it may, the essence of the matter does not change. Especially in the Zone. Here, the existence of telepathy is not questioned even by inveterate skeptics. In the Zone, this anomalous phenomenon, from the point of view of traditional science, is not only widespread, it also exists in several modifications. The most popular is mental control, with the help of which mutant controllers control people who have fallen under their influence, certain types of monsters, and even those who are theoretically impossible to control – the dead. That is, zombies. Telepathic vision is also often found, for example, in blind dogs, although many scientists believe that dogs disfigured by the Zone simply rely on heightened sense of smell and hearing, and telepathic vision has nothing to do with it. The third option, even two - the third and fourth, are common among small, but especially evil and insidious mutants of the yellow-white dwarf species. Firstly, these underground creatures have the gift of foresight. They often, without any reconnaissance, set up ambushes where people are supposed to pass in a few hours. And secondly, dwarves have such a talent as telekinesis - essentially an aria from the same opera as telepathy. With a mental effort, lifting a weighty stone and throwing it at an enemy is a piece of cake for dwarves. But this is what concerns mutants. People in the Zone do not have such a familiar relationship with telepathy. Among them there are good hypnotists and beginning telekinetics, but there are very few real telepaths, almost none. And those few who are part of this “almost” will never admit their talent. If only because their best friends will immediately shoot them. With this, the inhabitants of the Zone have it simple and strict. There is a deviation - a mutant, and that means a bullet in the forehead. But who in their right mind (even taking into account their unusual talent) would admit that they are a telepath? What for? It is much more profitable and safer to just live with it and use it to the fullest. Although to the fullest is a strong word. The coil is so-so, so you rarely want to use it. Here's the tricky part: it's practically impossible to establish telepathic contact with normal people. You can hypnotize them, scare them, make them move against their will, but you can't read their thoughts or instill your own in them. This only works with telepaths, who... see above... are extremely rare among people and numerous among mutants. And exchanging thoughts with a controller is even more stupid than admitting to people that you're a latent mutant. And it's also mortally dangerous. “Your longing for communication with your own kind is close and understandable to me,” a mental voice rang out. The beginning of the telepathic contact was not accompanied by any preliminary special effects. No calls, no pictures in your mind's eye. A voice simply began to sound in your head. Like a madman's. - I'm not sad. Sometimes I just want... not to be alone. - And I understand that. I'm with you, which means you're not alone. You have information. What kind? Just try to think concretely and briefly, I don't have much time. - Me too. Ostapenko's men have discovered the trail. The general has assembled a special group. At this very moment, it is preparing for a raid. "A group of suicides." The thought phrase had a hint of disdain. "The general can send his entire army down the trail, it won't change anything. It's too late." - There are only five in the group. A sixth one should join them a little later. But the small size of the team does not mean that it is not dangerous. Ostapenko is very cunning. He carefully selects personnel. - You're in the group too, aren't you? - Yes. And this could also be a military trick. It is unlikely that the general knows about my abilities, but he can guess. And this is probably not the only ace up his sleeve. I suspect that at least two more walkers have hidden abilities. - It's funny. But I still don't see any reason to worry. So far, Ostapenko has only won isolated skirmishes on our invisible front. He won't win the entire campaign. - The group is headed by the Old One. Personally, I don't like this man, I have a feeling that there will definitely be problems with him. - You're exaggerating. This mercenary is no better than other people. - On his own, yes. Under Ostapenko's leadership, no. That mercenary ruined two major operations for you, didn't he? First with the Call, then with the Black Angel. Where's the guarantee that he won't ruin a third? - The new operation is fundamentally different from the previous ones, - the interlocutor paused. - But, perhaps, I agree with your concerns. Will the group be able to follow the path to the very entrance? - Yes. The group's guide knew the route well. - Can they be detained? - Not for long. But for that you will have to come up with something new. The old one cannot be stopped by simple barriers in the way. - Okay, I'll send a few psionics to meet the group. They'll cause the Old One serious problems along the route. - And if the group does reach the hole? What will you do? - You know very well what the Thirteenth Sector is. People cannot survive in it. Even the Old One will not walk more than a hundred steps through the sector, our wild brothers will eat him alive right at the gates. I will not have to do anything. - The old one can simply mark the entrance and not stick his nose in. He'll ring on the satellite and wait for the cavalry at the threshold. - No, if the group gets there, it will definitely enter the sector, believe me. The Old One won't call the "Cavalry". - I can’t see your thoughts – why? - Consider that I have studied Ostapenko well. If the general really expects to play some secret trump cards at the last moment, his people will definitely enter the sector. - Are you going to let them in? - Let them get there first, and then we'll see. Forewarned is forearmed. Whatever Ostapenko has planned, we now have the advantage: we know his secret team is coming to us. – If one of the walkers drags a nuclear bomb in his backpack, the imaginary advantage will turn into defeat. - This will become clear long before the group approaches the hole. You will find out. And my psionics will help you. - I can't imagine how I'll find out. It's clear that if someone's load is too heavy, eventually it will become clear what's in it. But a landmine could bring a sixth one, who will follow in our footsteps. - Think of something. It doesn't matter who carries the bomb: one of the walkers or the sixth. The old one will definitely know the mission objective and the activation code. I don't think he'll tell you the code, but he'll at least let you know the objective for sure. You just need to build the right relationship with him. Hardships experienced together bring people closer together, make them more open, remember that. Psionics will provide the group with these hardships. All you have to do is ask the right question at the right moment. Is that a feasible task? - Yes. - Keep me updated. - I'll try....

The tradition of going on a raid after dark was, in Andrey's opinion, quite reasonable. Usually, while the groups were moving along the Borderland and the relatively safe outskirts of the Zone, noctoscopes provided normal visibility. And by the time the groups entered the Zone deep enough and it became unsafe to rely on night vision devices, the sky, covered with eternal clouds, brightened, and the problem solved itself. Usually. But today Lunev felt that the Zone was preparing some kind of trick right outside the base gates. Or even on its territory. So the darkness could work against the walkers. The group had just loaded up their gear and lined up, and the ghost of impending trouble was already looming in their field of vision. Almost at the gates of the headquarters hangar, only about forty meters away, on a small parade ground, a strange greenish glow had somehow quietly appeared and quickly gained strength. At first, Andrey decided that the night vision device was faulty, but, looking with the naked eye, he realized that the “malfunction” had a different origin. A ring of greenish fog, about twenty meters in diameter, had drawn a perfect circle in the middle of the parade ground. Stary did not try to guess what kind of anomalous phenomenon it was. It was not his problem. He needed to concentrate on the raid, and other people should deal with minor deviations on the base’s territory. At least that same Bibik. The colonel stood with his back to the parade ground, carefully helping the group check whether all the straps were fastened, and did not see the emerging problem. Andrei nudged Bibik in the shoulder and pointed with his gaze at the ring of fog. - What? Oh, that, - the colonel waved his hand, - sometimes happens in the morning. “I haven’t noticed,” Andrey said doubtfully. “Always in the same place?” - No. This is the first time I've seen it at the base. But I've seen it in other places many times. In the Zone and in the Borderlands. I even saw it once in Dymer. It hangs for an hour and then melts away. A harmless thing. - And they didn’t conduct a chemical analysis? - Tatyana intervened. - No. - Bibik reached out to Olga's harness strap. - This is where you need to tighten it. - Hands! - Olga pulled Bibik's claw away from her chest. "It was a shame they didn't do it," said Tatyana. "What if it's poisonous gas? Marsh fumes can sometimes be very harmful." - And why did you decide that it was fumes? - Bibik took a step back and looked around the formation with an attentive gaze. - It's just fog. - Green? - Tatyana grinned. - Tell me you were too lazy to bother! “Stand up,” Andrey ordered quietly, interrupting their argument. “We don’t mind making an effort, if only we had someone to do it with,” Scout remarked cheerfully and unexpectedly slapped Olga below the back. The reaction was instantaneous. The girl elbowed the merry fellow in the stomach, so hard that Scout almost fell on his bottom. If Scout hadn't been wearing a bulletproof vest, he would have spent the rest of the inspection bent over, trying to catch his breath. When the discouraged stalker took his place in the line again, he got it from Bibik as well. The colonel lightly slapped his friend on the forehead with his palm and shook his head reproachfully. - Think with this place, not with your pants, you non-combatant moth! - And what about me? - Scout grinned. - Nothing, just to establish friendly contact. “Next time I’ll break my nose,” Olga promised calmly. “And I’ll help,” the Mechanic spat and added unkindly. - Thank you, I can handle it. - You're mean, - Scout feigned offence. - You can't go to the Zone with such a mood. You need a positive attitude, and you have... emptiness. If we fail the mission with such an attitude, we'll definitely fail. "Is this positive?" The mechanic gave Scout a withering look. - Old man, take this gang away. - Bibik waved his hand. - Before they tear each other into puzzle pieces. Good luck. - Thank you, Colonel. - Andrey shook Bibik's hand and turned to the group. - Mechanic first, Scout last. Quick march! The group formed a column of one and moved towards the northern gate of the base. Olga went second, just in case. Andrey paused for a second and once again cast an anxious glance at the greenish ring of fog. “You should wait until this trouble goes away and watch,” he said to Bibik. - Don't worry. - The colonel smiled indulgently. - I told you, it's a familiar thing. Get it out of your head. You have so many problems ahead of you, your brain will be full to the brim without this fog. The old man shrugged and walked after the group. Most likely, the colonel was right. Andrey should have concentrated as much as possible on his problems, both external, awaiting the group in the Zone, and internal. For example, Stary could bet a hundred to one that the Mechanic volunteered to go to the Thirteenth Sector only because he was sure that Ostapenko would send Andrey with him. That is, the mercenary would have a real chance to get even with his former colleague. Andrey measured the Mechanic stomping ahead with his gaze. No, Max would hardly dare to attack before the group reached the "inner circle", the territory between Chernobyl and Pripyat, but he should have kept his ears open right away - it was safer that way. Besides Mechanic's revenge, there were other problems to be feared. For example, quarrels between Olga and Scout. It was unclear what kind of cat ran between them, but the sparks between them were so intense that you could just take it and light a cigarette. Stary was not worried about Tatyana's loyalty, but she remained a weak link in the combat plan, which also promised problems. In general, yes, the colonel is right. All unnecessary things should simply be thrown out of one's head. The old man looked around again, worried. The base had already disappeared behind the forest, and this fact finally removed all questions. Now everyone relied on themselves and their closest comrades: Andrey on the group, Bibik on the base garrison. The old man quickened his pace and caught up with the Mechanic. “We’ve turned east.” The old man nodded toward the surface of the river glistening between the trees ahead. “What are you planning?” - Nothing... - Max moved the branch aside and also looked at the river. - The path starts there, on the outskirts of Opachichi. If we go straight northwest, we won't find the entrance to the sector. “That’s strange.” Andrey looked at Kruger with suspicion. - You may not believe it. - The mechanic shrugged. - I tried about twenty times. I went directly, and through Stechanka, and tried to enter from the north. It was useless. The entrance opens only if you start from here and go through Opachichi, Yampol, Cherevach. And then, before reaching Novosyolki, turn northwest and go past Lake Krinka and the eastern outskirts of Korogod. - Through the swamp? - Yes. - And then? - And then strictly north, about three kilometers. - The mechanic grinned. - You know it yourself. What, you still don't believe it? - No, Mechanic, I don't believe it. - Andrey tried to catch Kruger's eye. - Why did you suddenly volunteer to help Ostapenko? - Left without work, I have to live somehow. - The mechanic hid his eyes. - You yourself were in such a situation. And still are. - But the syndicate is not hunting you. Why? - In short, what do you want? - the Mechanic could not resist. - To expose me? No way! I have no special intentions. I earn money as best I can. And you should thank the general that we ended up in the same group. I didn't really want to meet you either, let alone go to the Zone! - You didn't answer about the syndicate. - Go to hell! - The mechanic resolutely walked forward. - Listen, Old Man, why are you so suspicious, - Scout said quietly, catching up with Andrey. - Why the hell would the syndicate need him? Hunting him... isn't worth the candle. - Did someone ask you? - Andrey looked at the stalker sullenly. - Ah. - The scout waved his hand. - The general has selected a group! All individuals, all with character, with pride... We'll fail the mission! One hundred percent! “Shut up!” Olga suddenly demanded. “Do you hear?” - What? - Scout turned his head. - Just the wind. - Cannonade. - Olga raised her index finger up and then confidently pointed in the direction where the base remained. “Maybe it was just thunder?” Scout listened. - No, these are explosions, - Andrey confirmed Olga's guess. - After all, I was not mistaken. There were problems at the base. - So what now, go back? “Now we have to get there even more.” The old man shook his head. “Do you think there’s a connection?” Olga pointed first to the south, then to the northwest. “I don’t know yet.” Andrey glanced at Tatyana, but she just shrugged. “And I know that if we stand there, we won’t find out anything,” the Mechanic growled from the thicket. “Move!” Scout and Olga looked questioningly at Lunev. He was silent for a few seconds and nodded. The mechanic was right. There was no point in standing still and gossiping. The group formed a semblance of a line again and moved through the oak grove towards the dirt road, long overgrown with young trees, which once connected the Oranoye-Chernobyl-Pripyat highway (aka R-10) with the village of Kamenka, lost in the woods. The mechanic had a good sense of the terrain and led the group to the old road exactly where they should have. If Stary had gone first, he would have come out of the grove right here too. Behind and in front of the group, to the right of the road, two black clusters of ruins loomed, a sluggish stream gurgled in a concrete pipe under the road, and to the left stretched a vast swamp with numerous islands on which skinny birches huddled. This picturesque place near Kamenka, at the junction of the Bolshoy Krug and Cheremetovskaya Plot, was considered by the walkers to be a kind of border point between the relatively safe periphery of the Zone and its main territory, where dangers and problems began to grow like a snowball. But that was there, ahead. And here, in a kind of neutral zone, for some reason nothing ever happened. No anomalies formed, mutants did not settle in the ruins, and groups, even such irreconcilable ones as "Duty" and "Freedom", put their weapons on safety and continued the interrupted fight only when they left the two-hundred-meter stretch of road between the mossy ruins. The most interesting thing is that no one in the Zone agreed on exactly these rules of conduct in a strange place not far from the abandoned village of Kamenka, as well as in other similar places. There were at most five or six of them in the entire Zone, and they occupied no more than a hectare in total, but perhaps this was their special value. The old man noticed how warily Tatyana was looking at the nearby ruins and hastened to smile encouragingly at her. - Everything is fine, animals don’t live here. - Why? – Nobody knows, but the fact is proven. - A clean island, - Scout butted in. - It's good to spend the night in places like this. You sleep like a log. And here... The scout did not finish his thought, because there was no longer any need for it. The lulling tale of the "clean island" suddenly melted away, like morning fog under the rays of the sun. Four of the five unlucky guests of the "island", as if on command, closed their eyes and clenched their teeth. At the same time, something like that very morning sun flashed before everyone's inner vision, and at the same time, a sharp pain pierced the heads of the four. If it weren't for Olga, it would have been hard to say how this "sunbath for the brain" would end. Olga was the only one who didn't react to the obvious psi-attack. Lunev managed to notice this and, despite the pain, managed to remember Olga's immunity to hypnotic influence. “Kill... this... creature,” Andrey ordered through clenched teeth, trying to open his eyes. “Find... - I'm searching! - Olga raised her machine gun and turned on the night vision device sensitivity to maximum. Finding the controller who was hiding somewhere nearby or the one who had attacked the group with telepathic suggestion turned out to be very difficult. Olga couldn't see him, no matter how hard she tried. Meanwhile, Scout and Tatyana had already fallen to their knees and were starting to sag, almost losing consciousness from the pain. Andrey and Mechanic were holding on, but they were also at the limit of their strength. Another minute and the group could have shrunk to just Olga. The girl got nervous, irritably tore off her night vision glasses and suddenly saw the enemy. Or rather, she didn’t see him, she just realized where he was hiding. She felt him. Without thinking for another second, Olga turned towards the ruins, raised the sight higher and emptied the entire magazine into the upper branches of the old oak tree hanging over the ruins like a huge umbrella. The mutant's body fell like a stone and landed with a loud slap on the mossy stones of the ruins. At that very moment, Kruger fell down. Only Andrey and Olga remained on their feet. “Check it!” the Old Man demanded, wincing in pain. Olga, changing the magazine on the go, approached the enemy's corpse and fired a short control burst into the mutant's head. "A strange creature," she told Lunev when she returned. "It looks like a controller, only bigger and has a different face. Almost human." “Almost?” Andrey asked, vigorously massaging his temples. It was clear that he was still in pain, the effects of the psi-blow were wearing off slowly. - Eyes without whites, black as night. - I've seen one. - Lunev ran his hand over his face with effort, then wiped his watery eyes with his sleeve. - I saw one in Chernobyl. They're not only telepaths, but also telekinetics. That time, a similar creature lifted three tons of iron into the air and almost dropped it on our heads. Look, Tatyana was there too. “I remember,” Tatyana quietly confirmed, sitting down on the ground. “But I don’t understand... we have ‘Jokers’... why didn’t they help?” “Because the Jokers,” Scout muttered hoarsely, “work wherever they want.” “Maybe it’s this clean island?” Tatyana suggested. “Do other artifacts work here?” - Who checked them? - Scout spread his hands. - Before, no one attacked anyone here and there were never any anomalies. Maybe other artifacts lost their power, but no one noticed. Tatyana rummaged around in her pouch and pulled out a dosimeter. - The background is almost zero, strange. Even on the mainland you rarely see this. - Well then! - Scout waved his hand. - What, "here"? - Olga pushed the stalker aside. - What, what... an anomaly in reverse, that's what! - Here you have everything "upside down." - Olga twirled her finger at her temple and turned to the commander. - Old man, someone is sneaking up on us. When the controller had you pinned down, I heard someone moving in the forest. She pointed her rifle at the hazel bushes to the east of the ruins. "People," the Mechanic added confidently, nodding in the same direction as Olga. "Lots of people. But they won't attack now. Clean Island is still their authority." - Bye? - Scout came to life again. - And for how long is this "bye"? "We won't find out," Andrey decided. "We're leaving. Do we need to go to Kamenka?" - No. - The mechanic backed away. - Let's go further north. We'll go along the old clearing to the cowsheds near Opachichi. That's where the trail starts. “Let’s go!” Andrey ordered. The group somehow formed up and moved past the second cluster of ruins. The old man was bringing up the rear, looking back every now and then. The oak grove soon ended, the old gnarled trees were replaced by tall, slender pines, and if this was advantageous in terms of clean air, then as a cover – no, the pines were not such a reliable shield. And there was less undergrowth here to camouflage the walkers. The pursuers did not reveal their presence for some time, but, realizing that they were falling behind, they also quickened their pace. After about twenty minutes of energetic marching, Andrey heard not only footsteps behind him, but also noisy breathing and even quiet voices from somewhere on the left flank. The people who had followed the group were clearly trying to take the walkers in a pincer movement. The mechanic looked around, noticed how intensely Andrey was turning his head, slowed down and waited for the commander. - They won't get through from the left, - Max said quietly. - Kamenka has been on the black list for a month now. In short, some kind of beast has made a den there. Four stalkers disappeared there last week alone. - And on the right? - It seems to be clean there. But these ones won't go around. They'll try to take it by storm, attack right on the clearing. - Why did you decide so? - Bandits. - The mechanic shrugged. - Do you hear how they're talking? All over the forest. Pros don't do that. And since they're bandits, that means they'll go for it with a bang, as usual. Anyway, bring the guys, I'll plant the mines. I had a feeling I brought three of them with me. Directional, with radio fuses. The bandits won't be satisfied with that. - Stop it. - Andrey pushed the Mechanic forward. - You won't make it in time. And your mines might come in handy. Let's try to break away. - Madam, your breath is knocked out of you, you'll soon start stumbling. “When it starts, then we’ll decide.” The old man didn’t react at all to the hidden irony in Kruger’s voice. They quickly caught up with the group and with a few gentle but persistent jabs in the back forced their comrades to increase the pace to the limit. Tatyana, contrary to Kruger's predictions, managed to even out her breathing and maintained the acceleration. Scout was the first to fall behind, not one of the women. “My lung is shot through!” he pleaded, breathing heavily, and broke into a quick walk. "Do you want to get shot in the head too?" Olga hissed, grabbing him by the belt of his unloading vest. "Run!" - Let go! - Scout shook her hand off. - Run, I'll cover you, I'm used to it! - Walk! - Andrey ordered. - Scout, don't fall behind. You'll cover us when there's dire need. - And it's already there. - The stalker suddenly raised his machine gun and fired a short burst, aiming somewhere to the right, into the gap between the pines. - They've gone around it! - There's no one there! - Olga shouted, trying to lower the barrel of his machine gun. - You're giving us away! On purpose, or what? - What, are you blind? - Scout pushed the girl away and raised the machine gun to his shoulder again. - There they are! Bullets whistled over the heads of the walkers, but they clearly did not come from where Scout was aiming. They were shooting from the clearing. Andrey and Mechanic immediately pushed their comrades back under the cover of the pine trees to the left of the clearing and opened economical fire, single shots from their relatively silent "shafts". Olga meanwhile tore off a rawhide strap from her neck with a leather pouch in which her "Joker" lay, and shoved the thing into Scout's bosom. The stalker stared at Olga in surprise, then shook his head, as if shaking off a spell, and also turned to face the clearing. - It flattened again, damn it! What the hell is going on?! - The controller wasn't alone. - Andrey nodded at Olga. - Now cover her with whatever you want. Without the "Joker", she's target number one for the shooters. - Let him take it! - No. You have no resistance to psi-attacks at all, without the second "Joker" the controllers will take you like a puppy with their bare hands and brainwash you. You'll even mistake us for mutants. “Do we need it?” Olga added. “Well, stick with me,” the stalker reluctantly suggested to her. “As always, I’m the last one.” The shooting from the clearing ceased, but the sounds of footsteps only grew louder. The pursuers rushed through the young animals like moose in a rut, paying no attention to the heavy bullets buzzing around them. - We need to retreat, - said the Mechanic. - In three hundred meters there will be a real a... it's... in short, a clearing, there's an anomaly after an anomaly. But we'll get through it normally with the Jokers, at a good pace, and the bandits will definitely slow down. “That’s why they’re in a hurry,” Andrey concluded. “They know about this clearing, too.” "It's no secret." The mechanic fired a short burst at the particularly loudly crackling bushes. "Go ahead, I'll hold them back after all!" He took a couple of grenades out of his pouch and tore off the rings. Two explosions thundered when the group had already jumped out onto the edge of the forest and slowed down, trying to determine where and what anomalies were here. Tatyana, as always, was the first to grab the devices, took out the detector, and Scout, wiping away the sweat, reached into his pocket for nuts. Andrey and Olga simply glanced at the clearing and almost simultaneously pointed to a barely noticeable path laid along the right edge of the forest. “Past the Funnel,” Andrey said. “There’s also the Trampoline further on, but you can squeeze through.” “One step to the left and that’s it,” Olga doubted. - Take the Joker for a while. - No. - She winced. - Artifacts make me sick, you know that. Hold my belt if anything happens. "I'll hold you," Scout said. "Don't worry, no nonsense." - Hey, stop! - A sweaty Mechanic tumbled out of the bushes. - You can't go there. There are three holes in a row further on, you just can't see them, "Mousetraps". Follow me! He confidently stepped forward and walked across the clearing... in a straight line. Tatyana's eyes widened and she looked at Andrey in confusion. - There... anomalies... all over the place! A real minefield! “Mechanic!” Andrey called out to the guide. - Move! - Kruger said irritably. - The bandits are catching up, there's no time to hesitate! - You can't have two wives, you can't avoid one. - Scout sighed and grabbed Olga tightly by the belt. - Shall we go down the aisle, my dear? “You promised no nonsense,” Olga reminded. - Forward, - Andrey finally decided. - Tanya, follow them. If anything happens, just sit down and don't move. Several bursts crackled dryly in the forest, and lead woodpeckers began to knock on the nearest pines. Several bullets whizzed to the left of the walkers. Stary suspected that if the group had not had "Jokers" at its disposal, the bullets could have flown a couple of meters to the right, and the walkers would have suffered their first losses, but everything worked out, and Andrey threw unnecessary worries out of his head. Now it was time to worry about the Mechanic. More precisely, about the infallibility of his choice. The characteristic signs of the presence of anomalies could already be seen with the naked eye. In the pre-dawn twilight, it was hard to make out the dark spot and light swirl above the Funnel, which was located closest to the edge of the forest. As for the Electra, which occupied the legal ten-meter-diameter section of the terrain slightly to the left of the route, or the Springboard, to the right of the path trodden by the Mechanic, they could be tracked without the help of detectors. There was a barely noticeable spark above the Electra – the wind was constantly blowing withered blades of grass and leaves into the anomaly. And the Springboard was kindly marked by Scout. He threw a nut into the anomaly, which, having gained speed there, whistled forward and dully hit the trunk of a pine tree on the opposite edge of the forest. However, everything worked out with the "Funnel" too. The walkers passed literally three meters from the danger zone, even felt the pull - Tatyana slipped in the mud, and Andrey had to support her - but this time the "Jokers" flawlessly performed the role of universal protection. - There's something else there, - Tatyana said, nervously clutching the detector in her hands. - Ahead. The mechanic is heading straight into the anomaly! - Calm down. - Andrey grabbed her elbow more tightly. - There is nothing ahead, this is a suggestion. The bandits are accompanied by some controller, he is trying to confuse us. - The one who made Scout shoot at empty light? - That's him. - Andrey looked back. - Faster! A hundred meters behind, at the edge of the forest, dark figures began to flicker. The Old Man estimated the number of pursuers. There were at least two dozen of them, and there really was someone alien with them. One of the figures was dressed in a robe, like an oversized cloak with a hood. The alien did not aim at the walkers' backs, as the others did, but he seemed much more threatening. Andrey involuntarily touched the pouch with the Joker, and then put his hand inside his bosom and felt another artifact that he never parted with - the Amulet hanging on a chain, bought more than a year ago in one of the secret laboratories. Compared to the Joker, the artificial artifact was a primitive trinket, it only protected against psi-effects, but now that was exactly what was needed. The old man was sure that only thanks to the joint work of the two artifacts he was able to withstand the onslaught of the controller on the clean island and felt nothing but a frightening psi-wave now. Tatyana also turned around and shuddered. - They... are aiming right at us! “Calm down,” Andrey repeated. “They won’t shoot. We’re covered by the Funnel. Bullets won’t fly through it.” - It looks like they're going to go around the clearing. - That's what we need. We'll gain time. - Five people are moving after us! “Step wider,” the Old Man ordered. “We can do it.” Lunev was right. There was no trap right ahead. The mechanic, followed by Scout and Olga, entered the forest unhindered from the other side of the clearing and slowed down, waiting for the lagging couple. Andrey and Tatyana had only about twenty meters to go when the three comrades suddenly raised their weapons and opened fire. The bullets whistled very close, and Tatyana was literally paralyzed by this music. "Duck and go left!" Scout shouted. "Go left!" He waved to the right. But the Old Man understood him correctly. He grabbed Tatyana in his arms and jumped away from the line of fire to where the stalker was pointing. That is, to the right. Finding himself under the cover of the trees, Andrey carefully leaned his frightened friend against a pine tree, and he himself joined the shooters. The Mechanic who ordered the opening fire was right. The five bandits who were hot on the bandits' heels were indeed dangerous. As soon as the pursuers passed the "Funnel", nothing could stop Andrey and his companion from shooting in the back, and the bandits almost started shooting from the hip. Only the return fire could stop them. The five reflexively scattered into a chain and tried to lie down, but only three succeeded. The extreme fighter on the left flank rolled too far and hit the Electra. The anomaly immediately flared up with a sheaf of small white-blue lightning, which illuminated the clearing no worse than a powerful mercury lamp. The man arched and began to convulse. Gradually, his face and hands turned black, and blue smoke poured from his clothes. The bandit closest to the unfortunate man jerked to the rescue, but at that very second the Mechanic fired a shot, and the would-be rescuer buried his shot-through forehead in the ground. The two bandits, pressed into the mud in the center of the path, began to slowly crawl away, but the oncoming fire did not frighten the one on the right flank. The fighter rolled, fired an aimless burst, rolled again, jumped to his feet and rushed forward. After taking a few steps, he plopped down on the ground to take a new position, but did not reach the ground. It was as if the shock anomaly "Springboard" was in his path. The bandit hung for a moment a meter from the ground, and then, having received a powerful blow from an invisible fist, rushed forward as if on an air cushion. On the third day of the weekly cycle of anomalous activity, "Springboards" are not as dangerous in principle as they were immediately before the Emission. They could only kick a person with lethal force over a short distance, but in this case, a palisade of trees was located at exactly that distance. The fighter crashed into a pine tree slightly to the right of Tatyana, and the walkers heard a distinct crunch of bones. Andrey, just in case, took a step to the side and aimed at the bandit, but almost immediately returned to his previous position. A living person could not lie in such a pose. The fighter's head was twisted at an unrealistic angle to his body. The two surviving bandits stopped backing up and pressed themselves into the ground. The mechanic, just in case, put a few bullets in front of their noses and backed up into the forest. Scout backed up after him, and Andrey dragged Tatyana away. This time, Olga acted as the last one. She lingered at the edge of the forest, trying to see someone on the other side of the clearing. - Did you see someone you knew? - Andrey asked when the group reunited and again headed for Opachichi. “The same as the one on the tree,” Olga stated. “And this is definitely not an ordinary controller.” “You can kill him, and that’s the main thing,” Andrey said. “Mechanic, will there be more anomalies?” - On the edge of the village. Right before the path there will be a large "Carousel", but we will notice it right away. In short, it has been there for the fourth cycle already. So many entrails around - a blind man will see it. And if he doesn't see it, he will hear it. Crows are circling there, as if over a cattle burial ground. - Are there any animals? - I can't speak for them. They are not anomalies, they wander where their instincts lead them. In short, I only saw a couple of boars. But it looks like there are dwarfs in the village. And bloodsuckers, where would we be without them? “What a nightmare!” Tatyana said quietly, horrified. - Don't worry, sister. - The mechanic grinned. - We won't go into the village, but on the outskirts, in the open space, we can defeat any creature. - Thank you, brother, you calmed me down. - Tatyana instantly concentrated from the familiar address and became almost her former self. In any case, she hid her fear deeper and cheered up. Andrey smiled faintly. That's where it all started. In the first hours of their acquaintance - two months ago - he also perceived Tatyana as a science-obsessed metropolitan thing. But less than a day had passed before he had completely revised his attitude towards her. And a month later, even... But it all started with Andrey being surprised by Tatyana Sergeyevna's character. She could get scared, even panic at first, but after two or three shocks she seemed to harden and then acted almost on par with experienced walkers. With the same composure, and without falling apart even in an extremely difficult situation. "Stop chatting," Olga suddenly hissed. "I feel the controller. He's already close." The group quickened their pace, but this did not prevent the conversation from continuing. Only the phrases became shorter, such that they fit into one exhalation. It is clear that this way of conducting a conversation was invented by the most restless one – Scout. - So, Olya, you don’t completely ignore hypnosis? - I won't give in. Shut up. - But do you feel the controllers? How? - In my gut. Shut up, I said! - And if there are many of them? - It doesn't matter. Shut up! - And if... Scout didn't finish his sentence again, because he suddenly tripped, fell on his stomach and skidded about three meters. Olga slowed down and leaned over to help him up, but received a strong push in the back and fell sprawled next to the stalker. The Old Man realized what was going on faster than anyone else. He pushed Tatyana aside and pressed her back against the nearest pine tree. He raised his machine gun and fired several times into the air. Hearing the shots, Olga did not get up, but rolled onto her back and also began firing at the creatures diving from the gray sky. It was a dozen relatively small flying mutants that caused Scout, and then Olga, to end up on the ground. The mechanic cast a quick glance at the brightening sky and shook his head in displeasure. "They know where we're going." Kruger looked at Olga suspiciously. "I didn't warn them." - Me too, - the girl said, having fought off the mutants' attack. - And don't stare at me. Scout, are you alive? “I bit my tongue,” the stalker complained, getting up. - Very fortunate. - Very funny! - Scout spat. - You should do that! “Now we’ll have to keep an eye on the air too,” Max concluded. “They’ve surrounded it tightly.” “Do you think they’ll be waiting for us at the trail?” Lunev asked the Mechanic. - The main thing is that the mortars don't shoot us, and we'll deal with the ambush. - Kruger grinned. - And who promised it would be easy?

When the walkers disappeared into the darkness, Colonel Bibik swaggered up to the ring of glowing fog and, folding his arms across his chest, stared thoughtfully at the strange phenomenon. Bibik had indeed seen something similar two or three times in the Zone and the Borderland, but, to be honest, it had simply never occurred to him to study this phenomenon. And, apparently, he was not the only one. Bibik had never heard military guides or free stalkers gossiping about rings of greenish fog. In fact, that was why the colonel had told Stary that this short-term anomaly was not dangerous. If problems had arisen with it even once, the rumor would have spread throughout the base instantly. But nothing like that had happened, so there was no reason. Bibik stood by the swaying ring for five minutes, dug into his pocket, found a coin, threw it into the circle, listened to the copper ringing as it bounced along the parade ground asphalt, and with a clear conscience moved towards his detachment. The old man was an experienced walker, of course, and his sense of danger was something that God would grant to anyone, but in this case the retired mercenary was clearly playing it safe. "Morning, fog, what's strange about that?" Bibik yawned and shivered. "It's not hot, but it's not frosty either. Plus it's so damp. What else should there be, frost, or something?" Something suddenly crunched distinctly behind him, and the colonel, stumbling on level ground, froze. In fact, it crunched as if instead of fog there really was frost on the parade ground and someone big and heavy was slowly walking on it. Bibik put his hand on the holster with the antediluvian, but dearly beloved Stechkin pistol and turned around. A dark figure towered in the center of the circle. It was impossible to tell whether it was a man or a mutant. The figure wavered like a tongue of black flame. Bibik quickly pulled a lightweight NATO night vision device in the form of glasses from his pocket and put it on his nose. The image enhanced by the device was little different from what was visible without electronic processing. In the greenish ring of fog, slowly shifting to the left, the figure of a creature vaguely resembling a man was swaying. When the dark figure smoothly moved to the side, freeing the center of the circle, a second one immediately appeared in it. That's right - it appeared. It didn't crawl out of some dungeon or descend on a parachute. It just appeared. Out of nowhere. A few seconds later, the second figure also shifted to the periphery of the circle, and the center was occupied by a third. By this point, Bibik had already overcome his stupor and began to understand what was happening. He feverishly fumbled for his communicator and pressed the emergency button. The duty officer at the base responded immediately. “Alarm,” Bibik said quietly. “Code red. Base under attack!” “Repeat,” the duty officer asked dryly. The creatures inside the foggy ring suddenly moved towards Bibik and literally a second later found themselves halfway from their point of appearance to the colonel's position. Bibik suddenly pulled his pistol out of his holster, assumed a standing shooting position and opened rapid fire. Apparently, the duty officer arranged such a "repeat". A drawn-out, slowly gaining momentum howl of an alarm howler spread throughout the base. The bullets stopped the trio of creatures, but none of them fell dead. The black shadows simply froze in place, allowing Bibik to waste all twenty rounds, and then slowly moved on. The creatures' behavior was strange, but Bibik could easily decipher the meaning of their movements. Their first dash was something like a statement: "This is how we can do it." Then they clearly showed that they could not be taken with personal firearms, and finally politely asked him to move. The colonel backed away at about the same pace as the creatures approached, trying to reload the Stechkin and contact the duty officer again at the same time. With the pistol everything went quickly and clearly, but the connection just didn't want to be established. There were no beeps or interference in the receiver, only deathly silence. For a short time, Bibik decided that everything that was happening was an illusion. The creatures were moving too smoothly, unnaturally smoothly. And the siren was roaring in a voice that was not its own, and apart from it, there were no other signs of an alarm being raised at the base. No one was running, lining up, unpacking equipment, or hurrying to the place where several bursts from an automatic pistol had just been heard. But it only seemed that way to Bibik for a moment. Actually, that's why the siren was false and no one was rushing to help. Time stretched out like a rubber band for the colonel, and a second turned into almost an eternity. However, it really only seemed that way to Bibik. The false eternity ended abruptly, time found its normal pace, and the colonel forgot about illusions. The attack on the base was real. More and more creatures were emerging from the glowing circle, and now these were not only shapeless black shadows, but also familiar, but unusually large, mutants of the bloodsucker, snork, and pseudo-giant species. It was strange to see these mutants in one formation, but Bibik easily recalled several situations when mutants united into even stranger brigades. And through all these episodes, the main thing ran like a red thread - the unification of mutants foreshadowed serious troubles for people. "And then there are these... in robes. They seem to be hypnotists no weaker than controllers. Look how they drove through my brain, time even curled up into a tube. We have a problem, though. The old man turned out to be right, as usual. The man's instinct is... simply animal-like. He'll give any mutants a run for their money." The thought of Andrey's special sense stirred up some vague doubts in his soul, but a second later Bibik had no time for them. The creatures crawling out of the misty circle quickly crawled across the base's territory. And if it was still easy to track the large mutants, then the second wave, consisting mainly of toothy "jerboas" and rats, was almost impossible to stop or track. The small monsters scattered like peas into the nearest nooks and crannies and began actively looking for the smallest cracks in order to penetrate the basements of buildings and all sorts of utility rooms. Bibik didn't like this rapid development of events at all. The sound of many feet was heard behind him. The colonel looked back and waved his hand, hurrying the approaching fighters. - Pashkov, come to me! The commander of one of the special forces combat groups ran up to the colonel and covered him from the approaching crowd of mutants. - Step back, Colonel! - Stop it! Go with your group to the arsenal, which is next to the fuel and lubricants warehouse, there are flamethrowers there. Bring them here! And grab gasoline, oil, in short, anything that burns! - Eat! The fighter sped away, and Bibik, hiding behind a UAZ parked nearby, gave the signal to the rest of the soldiers. - Take positions! Machine gunners, to the points! The rest, fire grenades! Something thundered loudly on the hood of the car. Bibik saw a soldier with a heavy machine gun out of the corner of his eye. A second later, a second soldier appeared next to the first, holding two boxes of ammunition. It took the machine gunner no more than three seconds to load the gun, but during that time the mutants managed to get very close. “What cartridges?!” Bibik shouted, turning to the second fighter. - Regular and tracers! - Leave it and go after the "BZ"! - Eat! The fighter left the boxes and rushed towards the ammunition depot, but managed to take only ten steps. From the center of the foggy circle, a whole flock of large, human-sized creatures suddenly soared. They flew up to a height of about fifty meters, circled over the parade ground and quickly dived down on the people's positions. The first victim of the flying mutants was the very fighter whom Bibik had sent for armor-piercing incendiary cartridges. The damn "batmen" lifted the guy high into the air and staged something like a short set of air volleyball. Only instead of a ball, they had a man, and after each pass, less and less living mass remained of the fighter. Body parts and shreds of uniform flew in all directions, and very soon there was practically nothing left of the guy. His severed head fell not far from the UAZ and, having bounced, rolled under the car. - Well, bitches! - the machine gunner next to Bibik gritted his teeth and began mowing down the approaching mutants with long tracer bursts. “Air!” Bibik belatedly shouted and began firing at the flying creatures with his Stechkin. To the left of the colonel's position, another machine gun started firing, then from the second floor of the nearest barracks, machine guns crackled and under-barrel grenade launchers slammed. Most of the grenades landed right in the circle, the explosions tore several dozen mutants to shreds, but that didn't solve the problem. The monsters crawled out of the greenish ring, completely ignoring the defenders' fire. - How many of them are there?! - the soldier next to Bibik muttered, hastily changing the machine gun belt. - I didn't take a spare barrel... “You shouldn’t have done that,” Bibik said while reloading his pistol. Behind him, the clatter and roar of engines was heard again. The colonel turned around, hoping to see flamethrowers rushing to help, but all he saw was a platoon of soldiers running at full speed and three Hummers with heavy machine guns on the roofs. The machine gunners in the Hummers were not asleep and were trying to fire anti-aircraft fire on the move. In principle, they were doing everything correctly. The situation did not require special precision. What was required was a tight grouping of fire. The flying creatures, like a black cloud, blocked the entire sky. Large-caliber bullets easily coped with the task, "batmen" fell to the ground in packs, but at the same time new flocks rose from the ominous circle, and there was no end to them. Several dozen especially agile flying mutants managed to break through the dense fire and attacked the car, which was driving on the left flank. Their ugly bodies, wrapped in some rags, completely covered the "Hummer" for a second, and when visibility was restored, Bibik saw that all that remained of the machine gunner was his right hand, tightly clutching the trigger. - Armored personnel carriers here! - Bibik shouted to no one in particular. - Where are the flamethrowers, damn them! The machine gun next to him fell silent, and the colonel looked around anxiously. The machine gunner was fine, just loading the last belt. “We need to retreat,” he muttered, not looking at the commander. “The last box. And it will overheat soon.” The ground monsters took advantage of the pause and rushed forward. The machine gun started working again and slowed the advancing crowd, but a couple of bloodsuckers still managed to jump up to Bibik's position. The colonel gave one monster a hearty kick in the stomach, which sent the bloodsucker flying back about three meters, where it was instantly cut in half by a burst of machine gun fire. Bibik stopped the second one with a rather deft undercut, and when it fell on its back, the colonel, without any fear or disgust, stuck his hand with the pistol practically into the monster's mouth. The tentacles around the bloodsucker's mouth immediately began to move and wrapped around the colonel's hand, but a short burst blew the bloodsucker's ugly head off - and the tentacles went limp. Several dozen grenades fell into the foggy ring again, but now, judging by the density of fire and the features of the explosions noticeable to a professional eye, it was not the "under-barrel grenade launchers" that were at work. From somewhere in the southern direction, from behind the hangars, "Plamya" grenade launchers opened fire. Bibik nodded approvingly. The creatures still managed to get out of the circle, but now at least not in a continuous stream. For a while, the colonel even had hope that this "breach of the sewer pipe" could be eliminated. However, as soon as the grenade launchers fell silent for a short time, a new flock of "batmen" broke out of the ring, which rushed at low altitude right in the direction of the grenade launchers' positions. Behind the hangars, there was random shooting, several explosions, then more shooting, not as dense, and finally, a final, especially powerful explosion was heard. The nearest hangar was enveloped in a cloud of black smoke, from which a few seconds later a dozen monsters flew out. Several shots hit them, but no one opened heavy fire. The machine gun nearby fell silent. The soldier looked questioningly at the colonel. - Yes, we're retreating, - Bibik decided. - We'll secure ourselves at the warehouses. There's ammunition nearby, and anti-aircraft guns with flamethrowers, and communications. And most importantly, the boxes - BMPs. The colonel gave a signal to retreat, and the surviving soldiers of the screen rose from their positions. It turned out that the losses, despite all the efforts of the machine gunners, were quite significant. There were no human bodies left in the abandoned positions, but only because the monsters tore people to shreds. In fact, half as many soldiers left this line of defense as arrived to help Bibik. The colonel ran up to the nearest Hummer and, jumping onto the running board, assessed the situation from a relative elevation. The dense fire of the retreating fighters held back the onslaught of the monsters according to the scheme "one step back - two steps forward". Slowly but surely the hellish army advanced towards the center of the base. As for its northern part, closer to which the breakthrough ring was formed, it was lost to people forever. Right up to the northern wall of the outer perimeter, the territory was covered with a swarming gray mass of mutants. Bibik jumped off the running board and quickly, no longer trying to shoot back, walked towards the brick buildings of the armories. When designing the warehouse complex, the engineers took into account the possibility of breaking through the outer perimeter of the base, and therefore the warehouses were built in the form of an octagon. Eight two-story brick buildings with meter-thick walls, no windows on the first floor and narrow loopholes on the second floor, eight gates in the gaps between the ends of the buildings and sixteen towers on the sides of the gates turned the warehouse complex into a real citadel. In addition, all the buildings were connected by underground passages, which allowed for the even distribution of ammunition and human resources along the entire perimeter of the defense. And Bibik no longer doubted that they would have to distribute it exactly like that. The northern part of the base was lost, from the east, the scattered remnants of the commandant's company and several groups of military guides were retreating under the onslaught of mutants. In the west, the fighters of the "cleaners" battalion were still desperately fighting, but there was no reason to expect a breakthrough there either. Moreover, the backs of the fighters retreating to the center were already flashing between the barracks and other buildings of the western sector. It was relatively quiet to the south of the warehouse citadel, in the trade and scientific sectors, but rare shots were heard there, which meant that small groups of mutants had already infiltrated there too. Bibik ran. He needed to get to the new positions before the fighters retreated there, so that he could immediately assign everyone to their numbers. A major, the head of the warehouse complex, ran out of the nearest gate to meet the colonel. He was in a belligerent mood, although the sweat that had appeared on his face was clearly not from the long run, but from fear. - What luck! - Bibik waved to the major. - Back! Prepare for defense! Machine gunners to the towers! The major nodded and backed away. - Stop! - Bibik shouted. - Have you seen Pashkov? - There. - The major pointed to the nearest warehouse. - Flamethrowers... trying to start. - Are they gramophones, so that they can be wound up?! - Well, I mean... load. There's not enough incendiary mixture, - the major fiddled. - Almost none. We weren't thinking... Bibik became furious. - Sold it or something?! Rear-line rat! When this mess ends, I'll personally skin you alive! Run to your place! Bibik followed the major through the half-open gate and almost ran into a soldier loaded with two machine guns. The colonel grabbed one and pointed the soldier to the tower to the right of the gate. Without hesitation, he himself stomped up the spiral staircase of the left tower. The colonel had barely installed the machine gun in its nest when the communicator showed signs of life. The audibility was terrible, but Bibik easily recognized the subscriber. - Bibik, where are you? - I'm securing a position in the warehouse complex, Mr. General! - Send people to the southern and southwestern gates, let them cover. I'm leading a large group of civilians. - It will be done! - Bibik noticed the approaching group of failed flamethrowers and leaned down. - Pashkov! One fighter here! "They sold all the fire mixture on the market!" Pashkov shouted from below. "They probably leaked it to Smoker or someone else!" - I know! Go to the southern gate, meet the civilians! - Eat! “On the line, Mr. General,” Bibik said into the headset again. “Any other orders?” - Send equipment inside the citadel, support the gates. - We’re already rolling it out, this is a standard plan, we worked on it during the exercises. - Then that's all for now. No, not all. Be kind enough to wait for me alive. - Will try! - Answer according to the regulations! - I hope you'll be alive! - That's another matter. End of communication. Bibik gave way to the machine gunner who had climbed up the tower and slid down the fire pole. - Do you dance in your spare time? - Major Brazhnikov, the commander of the special forces reconnaissance detachment, suddenly appeared next to him. - In which bar? - Have a joke with me. - Bibik pulled the major by the sleeve, dragging him out of the way of the BMP driving into the citadel. - What are you doing here? I think Ostapenko sent you on a special mission. - At seven o'clock. - Brazhnikov raised his hand with the watch. - And now it's twenty to. - We've been smashing for forty minutes already? - Bibik shook his head in surprise. - As if it was just a moment. So what do you plan to do now? - It depends on the situation. - The major shrugged. - If the general confirms the order, I'll get through somehow - it's not the first time. And if not... there's work for me here, too. Brazhnikov suddenly raised his machine gun and fired a short burst into the air. A shot-down "Batman" fell from the dark sky. The monster was still alive and even dangerous, but he didn't have time to do anything. A fighter with a sapper shovel in his hand ran up to the mutant from behind. One swing - and the creature lost its head. Brazhnikov gave the fighter a thumbs up. - That's right, Dan, the cartridges will still come in handy, and if the shovel gets dull, you can always sharpen it! “Do you think the dancing will last long?” Bibik asked without looking at the major. - I think so. But I'm more interested in something else: where are they crawling out from? Did they dig an underground passage? - No, I saw this place, there's something different there. And there are so many of them coming out that it's unbelievable. As if they're stamped out there. Without counting. - It's some kind of mysticism. - Brazhnikov grinned. - Or else! - Bibik pointed to the southern gate, through which the first refugees began to enter the citadel. - Go meet him, talk to Ostapenko. Otherwise, you might not get out of here later. The beasts will surround you, there will be nowhere to spit. - Bye. - The major clapped Bibik on the shoulder and trotted off to meet the new arrivals. The colonel waved after the scout and walked toward the northern gate. The last of the fighters were just entering. Bibik glanced at the area in front of the gate, the gray mass of advancing enemies swarming on the other side of the square, took a few steps to the side and gave the signal to the BMP commander. The heavy vehicle roared its engine, backed up and stopped, pressing its stern against the gate. Almost simultaneously, five more gates were locked in the same way. The southern gate remained open the longest. But when all the refugees and the soldiers accompanying them entered through it, it too was closed. The military base of the International Isolation Force "Dityatki-3" itself found itself in complete isolation..

Up until the outskirts of Opachichi, the pursuers did not bother the group in any way, except for the slight but persistent pressure on the psyche. According to Olga, at least one more monster of the same type joined the controller who led the gang, but they were not going to attack yet. Apparently, they were waiting for reinforcements. When Lunev asked from which direction these reinforcements should come, Olga confidently pointed to the northeast. That is, straight ahead. - There is an ambush, and there are two more controllers in it. - How do you know all this? - Scout was surprised. - You feel it in your gut, I understand that, a convincing explanation. When they trample on your brain, I can sense it myself. But how do you know how many of them there are and where they are hiding? - It sounds like a loud whisper. - Olga shrugged. - Why are you bothering me? I don't know how to explain it more simply! "I didn't ask for it to be simpler." Scout aimed his finger at her. "Admit it, you're a little... yourself, aren't you?" - What "that"? - Olga visibly tensed up and put her finger on the safety catch of the machine gun. - What are you hinting at? - You can read minds, can't you? - Scout became bolder. - Aren't you a planted Cossack, Olya?! - Stop! - Andrey intervened. - You, stalker, will come to an agreement now! If we dig deeper, we can all be registered as mutants. - And some of them are even moral monsters! - Olga snorted and flashed an angry look. - Why are you protecting her? - Scout narrowed his eyes suspiciously. - What, the general confided some secret to you, huh? You are the main characters, and the rest are extras? - Are you going crazy again? - Andrey asked calmly. - Should I hang the third artifact around your neck? - No, what, am I wrong or something?! “You’re wrong,” the Mechanic said quietly, clicking the safety catch of his machine gun over Scout’s ear. “And shut up, you’ll give us away again, we’re getting close.” - Well, of course, everyone is a hero, I'm the only burden! - Scout threw the machine gun over his shoulder. - I can wait here while you chop up the ambush. I'm no use anyway! “You’re wrong again,” Tatyana intervened now. “You’re counting on sympathy, but no one will try to persuade you.” - No need! In the bushes, right behind Scout, a deadwood crackled loudly, and the stalker suddenly changed his mind about staying in the rear. He threw the machine gun off his shoulder, turned around and, looking warily at the bushes, began to back away after the group. "Hey, wait a minute!" Scout called over his shoulder. "Can't you hear me? Or is this a hallucination again?" “There’s flesh there,” Andrey said. “Let it live. Catch up.” - Yes, it stinks like flesh, - Scout gave in, wincing. - Okay, I'll go with you. But if you come at me again, I'll go back to the clean island and go to bed! - Eternal sleep, - Olga muttered. - Old man, slow down. There are no bandits up ahead. The controllers have brought someone more serious. I can feel how tense the mutants are, they can barely control their charges. - It's "Dolg", - Mechanic supported Olga, pressing his gaze to the eyepiece of the optical sight. - Look, over there, on the hill... That's it, they're hiding. "Dolg" people, for sure. We'll have to improvise. “There’s open space on the left, and an old stream bed on the right, which is the most convenient way to walk,” Olga suggested. “They’re waiting for us there,” Andrey objected. “Let’s go along the edge of the forest. At least the left flank will be safe.” “There won’t be any room to maneuver,” the Mechanic objected. - Not negotiable. Forward! - As you say... - Kruger grinned. - Wherever we go, they'll find out in advance. I'll bet you a hundred. Andrey gestured that the conversation was over and pointed to the east. The group moved through the lightening forest at the same pace and managed to cover about a kilometer without incident. When the road went uphill - the walkers reached the hill where the Mechanic had noticed the Dolgovites' patrol - Andrey, looking back, managed to make out several figures of the pursuers. No matter how hard the fugitives tried to break away, the bandits were hot on their heels and stubbornly refused to lag behind. Olga appeared next to Lunev. She held the commander by the sleeve and, when they fell a little behind the group, whispered: - There is a plan! The old man nodded. - Go ahead. - If we let the bandits drive us to "Duty", we'll end up in a circle. But if the ambush or the beaters don't have the support of the controllers, they'll start shooting at someone other than us, you understand? For example, if the "Duty" guys wake up and see an armed gang rushing towards them... what will they do? - They'll chop the Arkharovtsy into cabbage, - Andrey caught the thought. - And the strangers won't help the bandits. They simply won't have time. All we'll have to do is get out of the line of fire in time. - That's exactly it. - But it’s easier to eliminate strangers in the pursuit group. - Agree. - What if telepaths read our plan? - And we won't let anyone. - Olga looked forward meaningfully. - I'll tell you a secret: mutants can't read minds. They can easily subjugate someone to their will, iron out their brains, even burn them to hell, but they can't catch what a person is thinking without their consent. They'll only be able to find out about our plans if someone whispers to them mentally. Voluntarily. - So what if there is a telepath among us, and he is an enemy? Do you understand what you are talking about now? - All about the same. But, unlike some overly energetic stalkers, I do not declare anyone a mutant. And there are telepaths among people. Even on the mainland. - It can't be one of us. - Andrey shook his head. - Everyone's been checked a hundred times. Everyone's one hundred percent normal. "I'm not a hundred," Olga reminded calmly. "But I'm not a telepath. I'm sure you are, too. So... if the others are unaware of the plan, there is no risk." - Did you catch the suspiciousness virus from Scout? - The old man shook his head disapprovingly. “Strictly speaking, the Mechanic was the first to suspect everyone,” Olga answered offendedly. “I’m just being on the safe side.” - The general promised a sixth one, - Andrey remembered, - who can go on a parallel course. True, he was probably also checked and rechecked personally by the general... but... someone is giving us up, this can be considered a proven fact, you and the Mechanic are right. - All the more reason not to discuss the plan in public. - Olga's face showed that she was skeptical about Stary's version of the sixth man, but she understood how much he did not want to suspect his subordinates of betrayal, and sympathized with the commander. - When we get close to the ambush, I'll fall behind, go into the forest and wait for the bandits. When they pass by, I'll shoot the brains of the mutants leading the gang and run away. In the meantime, you shoot at all the white light and go to the right. We'll meet there. - Sound plan, just two questions: are you sure the mutants won't kill you first? And second... for this to work, you need to calculate the timing very carefully, how are you going to do it? - The controllers don't sense me, I know for sure. And time... a fight will start, I'll shoot in the confusion. – So it turns out that we will end up in the crossfire in any case? - It works. But this way we will have a chance, and if we get surrounded, there is zero chance. “Okay,” the Old Man agreed after thinking for a moment. “Just don’t climb trees. Snipers only sit like that in movies. The more awkward the position, the greater the chance of remaining unnoticed.” "Don't teach a lady how to fasten her bra." Olga winced, but not irritably, just a little tiredly. Something like a smile even appeared on her lips. "We'll meet." “No problem,” Andrey nodded and went to catch up with the group. Olga's plan was not bad, but still a bit raw, and adjustments had to be made to it from the very first second of the escalation of the situation. The ambush by the Dolgovites was set up competently, and the creatures controlling them did not give away their presence in any way, so, despite the full combat readiness of the walkers, the skirmish began unexpectedly. Crossfire forced the group to scatter into the bushes and lie down. None of the four could open aimed return fire, crawl to the right, or even raise their heads. Therefore, when the clatter of running bandits was heard behind him, Andrey had an unpleasant thought that someone had overheard his conversation with Olga. Whether it was the mysterious sixth one or the girl was mistaken about the mutant telepaths not being able to read people's thoughts, it did not matter now. The plan had clearly failed, and the walkers had only one option left - to stand up to their full height and engage in a fight, relying on their own training and the protection of the "Jokers". Andrey had already started to rise when the oncoming wave of pursuers suddenly slowed down and opened fire on the move... at the positions of the Duty members. In the ensuing shootout, the chances of catching a stray bullet were no less, but the situation had improved. The fighters of the Duty group had also escaped the control of the strangers - at least partially - and were busy with their usual business: eliminating bandits, and not shooting harmless walkers, who also had official passes to the Zone. "In theory, if you destroy the controllers who are sitting in the second line of ambush, you can use the "Duty" fighters as an escort. The group is paramilitary, and prefers not to quarrel with the official authorities... But is it worth experimenting? Our best ally is stealth." Andrey crawled up to Tatyana, pointed out the direction in which to move, and then found Scout and Mechanic with his eyes. They didn't need any advice, they figured out what to do on their own. Less than ten minutes later, the group found itself quite far from the scene of the battle, in a shallow gully, the bed of a long-dried-up stream on the eastern slope of a wooded hill. - "We're finishing our plot, he's letting us go already," - Scout quoted, getting up and shaking his clothes somehow. - I don't get it. You said that there were telepaths both in front and behind. Why did they pit their charges against each other? - Ask them, - muttered the Mechanic. - Old man, where's Olga? What, the first losses? Andrey didn’t have time to answer. "You'll lose her, of course!" Scout snorted. "There she goes." He pointed down the slope. Olga was walking quite briskly and quickly, although the dry riverbed was thoroughly littered with pine needles, branches and stones. Seeing that the brave girl was in perfect order, Andrey, unnoticeably, as it seemed to him, breathed a sigh of relief. Tatyana noticed this and raised an unfriendly gaze to her comrade in arms. Olga looked at the Old Man in surprise, and then at everyone else in turn. - What? “It’s okay,” Andrey said. “We were worried about you. Did everything work out?” - We got out of it. - Olga shrugged. - The bandits didn't understand anything. As soon as the psi-control disappeared, they decided that they came here to fight "Duty", and rushed to attack. The controllers accompanying the "Duty" smelled something fishy and ran away. I saw it myself. But I doubt they went far. They're afraid to come back without our scalps. “They told you that themselves?” Scout became wary again. - Don't start this nonsense again, okay? - Olga waved her hand. - I just assumed. Those advanced controllers are still nearby, I can smell them. And since they haven't left, that means they want to get us after all. - But they don't attack themselves, because they're afraid, - Andrey supported Olga. - After the incident on the clean island, it's not surprising. And they don't go back home, because they're afraid of punishment from their relatives or the owner. I don't know if there's someone in charge there or if it's just the collective mind that's in charge. - In short, I understand, - Kruger intervened. - The main thing is that the way is clear. We'll get almost to the goal along the stream. Are we going? “Yes, go ahead,” Lunev agreed. “Tatiana, stay.” The friend reluctantly slowed down, but did not look at the Old Man. - What? “Tell me,” Andrey took her hand, “why are you so upset?” “Have you known her for a long time?” Tatyana asked, still reluctantly. “Your ex?” - You're crazy. - Andrey grinned. - We've known each other... for less than a day of pure time. We ran into each other in the Zone in the fall, and it turned out that we both worked for Ostapenko. That's how we met. And then we parted ways, like two bullets in a duel. We haven't met until today. Do you believe me? - I believe you. Sorry. - It's okay. It's even nice. You're jealous, which means things aren't that simple with us. Right? - Andrey, I don't know... - Tatyana lowered her gaze to the ground. - Don't rush things, please. And as for this girl... just don't think that I'm jealous again... there's something wrong with her. - It's not news. - Andrey nodded. - But there's no need to be afraid of it. Don't listen to Scout, he's just a troublemaker. Olga is not like everyone else, but she is a human being and her soul will always be on the side of people. - It's not about Scout, who he is, I understood right away. But I think he's right in some ways. You say that Olga's soul will be on the side of people... but I'm not sure about that. - What? That she has a soul? - The old man chuckled again. - You speak of the soul in the literal sense, not as I do. Right? And these are the words of a scientist? Aren't you supposed to be an atheist? - No, it is not allowed. I understand that I am butting in where it is none of my business, but... believe me, science is, first and foremost, close observation of nature, whether in general or in some particulars. First and foremost, scientists know how to observe and notice what others do not see or do not want to see. I cannot yet clearly explain what exactly I see, but it scares me. That is why I am talking to you. - Okay, I'll take that into account. Of course, I don't believe in Olga's heartlessness, but I'll try to find out in more detail what's wrong with her. Although... for our mission, only one thing matters - she's not susceptible to psi-influence under any circumstances. “Perhaps there is a connection between the reason for my concern and this talent of hers. - We'll figure it out, I promise. - Andrey put his arm around Tatyana's shoulders. - Only later. Now we need to focus on the tasks of the near future. Not so mysterious, but no less important. - Get on the trail? - Tatyana shook her head. - We've been lucky so far. - And stay alive. We've been lucky in this too, but luck may run out. “A rather terrifying forecast,” Tatyana involuntarily shuddered. – You shouldn’t rely entirely on luck, otherwise the forecast won’t come true. The further journey seemed like real compensation for all the troubles we had experienced earlier. It became much lighter in the forest: the gloomy winter morning had finally arrived, and the trees, as they say, parted. True, a fine, annoying rain began to drizzle, but the walkers were used to that. In part, it was even pleasant. The measured patter of small drops on the hoods calmed the nerves perfectly. Plus, we were lucky enough to walk downhill, and no one was breathing down our necks. After half an hour of a calm, relatively unhurried march, an almost peaceful expression appeared on the faces of Scout, Tatyana and Olga. Andrei remained thoughtful, but also not as tense as on the stretch of the path from the clean island to the ambush. The only one who did not allow himself to relax was the Mechanic. As if he had heard the commander's wish "not to rely entirely on luck", he walked with extreme caution to the outskirts of the village drowned in the thickets. Only seeing the "Carousel" ahead, Max also relaxed a little. He threw his machine gun over his shoulder and nodded forward. - There's a path over there. You can't see it from here, so take my word for it, the road to Yampol starts in the thickets to the left of the sheds. We'll follow it. There's almost no asphalt left on it, but it's still nicer to walk than through the gullies. - All that's left is to go around the Carousel. - Lunev looked ahead through the optics. - Big bastard. “This is exactly the case when size matters,” said the Mechanic. “You can’t go around to the left, through the forest. I mean, you can, but it’s no use. You won’t get to the path. Coming in from the north is the same old story. There’s the eastern access, but if you walk along the edge of the village, it can get tight, and if you go too far, the mutants can eat you. You’ll have to walk like a knife.” “We have ‘Jokers’,” Andrey reminded. - I've taken that into account. - Kruger nodded. - Without them, there's no point in going anywhere. I think there's a reason this "Carousel" has been here for a month already. And how long it lasted before that is unclear. It's like a gate here. Those who have particularly large and powerful artifacts with them get through.
