The rest are just mincemeat. - Artifacts from the Thirteenth Sector? - They are the very ones. - Let's hope that ours are no worse. The old man gestured for them to move on. The group descended to the village and slowly moved tangentially towards the Carousel. It really wasn't hard to pinpoint the location of the anomaly. The circle within which all living things perished, caught in the brutal spin, was drawn with utmost clarity. Piles of rotting remains and scraps of equipment looked like a disgusting meat-packing plant dump covered with writhing gray-black foam from a distance of a couple hundred meters. An incredible number of rats and crows were constantly fighting for the brown remains of people and animals with greenish mold spots. Both of them simply couldn't be counted. The noise in the small clearing was deafening. Even a tropical storm couldn't drown out the indignant squeak of rodents and guttural cries of birds, let alone the whispering rain and light wind. The situation with smells was about as bad. The outskirts of Opachichi stank so much that the group, without orders from the Old One, hastily pulled on filter masks, although in these places there was practically no danger of inhaling a hot particle or being poisoned by some kind of swamp gas. The walkers slowly, trying not to attract the attention of the small, but no less dangerous creatures, covered a third of the way and almost encountered a new problem. From the very thickets where the beginning of the treasured path was located, several pseudo-dogs waddled out, mutants, more like large wolves than dogs. Seeing people on the way, the creatures froze for a moment, then fell to the ground for another moment and suddenly took off sharply in the direction of the group. The sight of a dozen toothy, dirty and drooling monsters rushing towards them confused the walkers for a while, but by the time the pseudo-dogs approached the distance of an effective jump, all five barrels of the group were aimed right at the pack. Rapid fire laid half of the monsters in the mud, and forced the rest to retreat, but the shots scared off the crows and attracted the attention of the rats. The sky instantly turned black from the outstretched wings, and the ground became uniformly gray and shiny with wet rat skins. A gray wave rolled from the edge of the anomaly towards the walkers, and the group was forced to flee again. Running at top speed one after another was not easy, but there was no other way. If they deviated even a meter to the left, even the almighty Joker would not be able to cope with the force of gravity of the Carousel, so powerful was this anomaly. Two high, slippery earthen ramparts and a deep ditch between them did not allow them to deviate to the right. And even if they had managed to go to the right, nothing good would have come of such a maneuver, that’s for sure. There were no fewer rats on the other side of the parallel parapets than around the Carousel. The coolest members of the group, Lunev and Mechanic, tried to shoot several of the so-called rat wolves, particularly large creatures that led many small packs that together made up the gray horde, on the move, but the shooters' efforts were in vain. Either the rats were led into battle by someone other than their usual leaders, or they didn't need any leaders in this situation. Realizing the futility of their efforts, the former mercenaries briefly exchanged glances and rushed to catch up with the group. The rats caught up with the walkers at the very moment when the leader of the race, Scout, opened fire from the waist, and burst into the treasured bushes. The long burst did not bring results, no one was waiting for the group in the bushes, the pseudo-dogs disappeared, as if they had never been there. But as for the rats... Several dozen large gray creatures grabbed the legs of the fugitives at the rear of the column with sharp teeth and claws and began to quickly climb over the equipment in the hope of reaching vulnerable or completely exposed parts of the bodies. Kevlar combat suits and light armored vests with metal-ceramic inserts were too tough for the rats, but the humans still experienced a few unpleasant seconds. The mechanic didn't have time to shake off all the rodents, and one of the creatures, clinging to the edge of his mask, deeply scratched his cheekbone. Having thrown the rat off, Kruger also managed to kick it into the bushes, but it was too late. Upon returning to the base, Max was facing a course of injections, like those given for rabies. However, this prospect was unlikely to upset the former mercenary. It wasn't like his leg was chopped off. And injections, pills, hospital boredom after every second raid... this is a standard situation in the Zone. Everyone else was a little luckier, they got off with what is called a light fright. Finding themselves on the path, the walkers immediately figured out where to run and rushed, squeezing the last of their strength out of themselves. The rats would certainly have been able to develop the necessary speed and maintain the pace suggested by the people, but for some reason they fell behind almost immediately. Strictly speaking, they had only gotten onto the trail by inertia. Literally a hundred meters later, on the chipped, cracked road behind the fugitives, there was not a single gray creature left. The Old Man was the first to be convinced of this. Risking his breathlessness, Andrey looked back and immediately slowed to a walk. It seemed that the path for the local mutants was some kind of forbidden place, like a clean island. That's why the pseudo-dogs crossed it in transit (both there and back), and the rats didn't linger there. Andrey didn't think about what the trick was. "Who knows? In the Zone, if you dig deep, there are so many snags and subtleties, thinking about them all can break your brain. And your brain can still come in handy..." As soon as Lunev thought this, it turned out that there was at least one person in the group who did not value his brain at all. It was easy to guess who. - Left behind, huh? - Scout stopped and, trying to catch his breath, leaned on his knees. In this position he waited for the commander. - Isn't this a dining area? Hello, people, whoa! “Stop... commanding,” Andrey said, breathing heavily. - Yes, I... purely... - Scout shook his head and pulled off his mask. - Okay, I won't do it anymore. Oh, and it doesn't stink here, although the wind from the garbage seems to be blowing in our direction. "This is a path," the returning Mechanic stated meaningfully. "Consider it the beginning of the Thirteenth Sector." - In what sense? “Its visible part,” Tatyana prompted. “Remember, I explained?” - Ah, well... no, I kind of remember, but I don't understand. You said it takes up an entire sector, but doesn't affect the others. - It doesn't affect you, you idiot! - Olga was the last to approach the place of a short rest stop. - Get off the path and you'll find yourself in a normal sector. - And you won't be able to return, even if you take a step back right away. Only again through the "Carousel" in Opachichi, - Mechanic finished his thought. - Fershteyn? - Yes, naturlich. - The scout nodded. - And how wide is this path? - It varies. Here it's about this road, in Yampol it's already half a kilometer, and where Cherevach is in the normal Zone, it's all of one and a half. Then it narrows, but near Korogod it starts to widen again, and the closer to the hidden part of the sector, the wider it gets. Well, and after the entrance I told you about, there it is... Wherever you go, in short, it's the Thirteenth. There's only one outlet into the outside world, well, this entrance. It's also the exit. - Which, by the way, is not entirely clear, - Tatyana noted. - From a scientific point of view. If we are already in the Thirteenth Sector, the entrance, theoretically, remains there, in Opachichi. But if the sector begins in the area of Chernobyl-2, then where are we now? - I'm selling it for what I bought it for. - Kruger spread his hands. - You are our science, so you can make guesses. - Hey, Mechanic, wait a minute, - Scout grabbed hold. - You say: "Where in the normal Zone is Cherevach." But in the Thirteenth... well, or where we are now... there is no such village, or what? - If there were a path through it, maybe there would be. But the path goes to the left. In short, after the bridge over the Uzh, it immediately turns to the forestry and then through the collective farm yard, the field, strictly along the power line, straight to Lake Krinka. - What a geographer, bitch! - Scout expressed his admiration in a dubious manner. - You speak so well, as if you were singing a song. “We should have studied at school,” Olga noted ironically. “Old man, we’ve caught our breath.” - Then let's go ahead. - Andrey turned his gaze to the Mechanic. - Tell me right away where problems might arise? “Everywhere, this is the Zone,” Kruger answered briefly and moved forward. “But it’s not simple, it’s cunningly twisted,” Scout noted and tried to use his gesture to depict some particularly complex squiggle. Unexpectedly for everyone, the stalker's cunningly twisted gesture led to strange consequences. The scout, for no apparent reason, lost his footing, but did not fall, but soared into the air about two meters and hung upside down. - As I said, everywhere... - the Mechanic said through clenched teeth. - Now everyone hold on. Looks like we've hit the "Screw"..

The soft milky-white glow of the large crystal seemed to flow along the mossy stone walls and the dirt floor, with the only difference being that the shimmering "moon milk" moved not from top to bottom, but vice versa. The glow flowed from a flat stone in the center of the spacious hall and, having reached the walls, rose along them to the vaulted ceiling with a huge hole in the middle. The strange glow refused to crawl along the ceiling, no matter how brightly the crystal glowed. The owner of the milky-white artifact called it the "Glass Effect". The owner did not know the reason for such strange behavior of the glow. He also did not know why the crystal, as soon as he took it in his hands, changed shape, turning into a strict parallelepiped, then a cube, and then a strongly elongated pyramid - almost like a bayonet. The third mystery was the crystal's reaction to the gravitational Emission, which shook the entire Zone to its foundations every week. During the hours when the precursors and then the Emission itself raged in the outer sectors of the Zone, the crystal stopped glowing and became like a large diamond, intricately crafted and unusually transparent. When the outer Zone was flooded with the brightest light of the Dead Noon, the crystal became cloudy again, and its usual glow began to gain strength. And along with the intensity of the milky-white glow, the ability of the artifact to serve as a key between the sectors also gained strength. The owner did not know why all this was happening. But he knew something else: in any state – transparent crystal or white artifact – the “Key” fulfilled the will of its owner. And this was much more important than all the mysteries taken together. The artifact easily opened the transition (in the state of the crystal – one, and at the peak of the white glow – up to a dozen) where the owner wished, and maintained it in the state of entering or exiting the Thirteenth Sector for as long as was required. Usually it was necessary to open for a short time. Just to go out or come in. Therefore, the owner did not experience any special problems with the crystal. He did not even think about the fact that the energy charge of the "Key" could be spent. No, he suspected that this was happening, but the owner never bothered to find out how much the almighty "Key" was losing in power. Not once until the moment when preparations for the main operation began. The new "holes" were supposed to be made very wide and held for as long as possible. In addition, a lot of them could be needed. Until now, the "Key" allowed to create no more than a dozen transitions from the inverted world to the normal one and keep them in exit mode for about a day. Perhaps the artifact was capable of more, but it lacked something for this. No matter how hard the owner tried, he could not open the thirteenth corridor. Formally, there were exactly thirteen transitions - the "hole" in Chernobyl-2 was the first of them, but in fact this transition should not be counted. It was not created by the crystal, and therefore the owner could not close or limit, for example, the entrance to the sector through this gate. The gate to the Thirteenth Sector in that place always existed, and what controlled them, if not the "Key", was still unclear. It all started with these uncontrollable gates. Animal instinct and chance helped the future owner of the crystal to come to them first of all psionics. To come, find the crystal - the only artifact of the great Flash - and become a real master in the hidden sector. Well, and then... there was a lot. Finding new relatives, a new name, or rather a call sign - Engineer, a new meaning of existence... But it all started with the gates. “Strictly speaking, they were the beginning of the rebirth from the ashes. And before that, there were long days and nights of painful transformation: both physical and moral. Perhaps, if I had found the gate earlier, everything would have been less painful. And if I hadn’t found it at all, I wouldn’t have become who I became. I would have degraded to a simple controller and ended my life, having fallen under the purge of the military or under the hot hand of “Debt”. And, by the way, it’s still a big question: wouldn’t such a development of events have been the best option? From a human point of view, I lost too much during the catastrophe. So much that further life simply lost its meaning. But, unfortunately or fortunately, I became a creature for whom all human motives are an empty phrase. Unfortunately or fortunately... it’s impossible to understand now. It’s not even worth trying. It’s the same as arguing whether it would have been better to stay in the womb of the mother. After birth, thinking about this is killing time. A very stupid occupation... however, it’s just as tempting.” The owner of the "Key" touched the crystal and seemed to be transported back several years, to that difficult time when he did not yet fully understand what had happened and who he had become. Or, to be more precise, to the moment that became a turning point for him...
...At first he remembered nothing but the flash. A blinding white ball that rose rapidly above the horizon and then turned the surrounding world into a black-and-white frame "lit up" the memory of his entire previous life. The engineer remembered nothing but the flash, but somewhere in the depths of his consciousness, fragments of memories still remained. These were memories of things so important to him from his past that he could not forget them. He had a family, he lived somewhere nearby, and he was happy. Nothing concrete, but while these embers smoldered in his memory, one could consider that not all was lost. It was this thought that pulled him out of the semi-oblivion he had fallen into immediately after the shock wave that followed the flash swept away everything around him and, picking him up, threw the helpless man to the bottom of some cold and damp pit. It is difficult to say how long he lay in that pit without moving, without water or food, without even the desire to live. But in the end the Engineer opened his eyes and stood up. And standing up to his full height, he looked around and was horrified. The world around him, so recently so warm and welcoming, had turned into a gloomy, gray squalor. There were mountains of rubble all around, with clouds of dust and ash billowing above them, and the sky was covered with leaden clouds. And it was terribly cold, as if there was not a drop of warmth left in the world. The cold blew from everywhere: from the ground, from the sky, from the ruins, and from the creatures that tried to climb over the rubble. The Engineer looked up at the nearest pile of rubble, where two people were crawling with difficulty. The curtain of dust prevented him from getting a good look at the pair, but the Engineer didn't need to. He somehow knew who these people were. Or rather, who they were former people. The strange flash had changed them beyond recognition. Their bodies had become more flexible and strong, but there were almost no thoughts left in their heads. All that controlled these creatures now were instincts. For example, right now they were guided by a sense of hunger. They sensed prey and climbed onto the pile of rubble to descend the opposite slope to where that prey was hiding. It was hard for the engineer to understand who the crazy creatures had targeted as their victims – people or similar creatures, but, by and large, it didn’t matter. He didn’t care about the fights between the crazy people. All he wanted was to get away from this terrible place as quickly as possible and try to restore his memory. He really hoped that the amnesia caused by the flash in him, or the clouding of the mind of the couple crawling down the slope, and similar problems in many other people flickering in the gaps in the dust curtain, was a temporary phenomenon. He was almost certain that his personal condition would return to normal much faster than others. After all, he had only lost his memory, not his mind as a whole. He could guarantee that everything would end well. It simply couldn't be otherwise! But it seems that the Exclusion Zone, having played a strange joke on people, did not care about all the hopes, confidence and guarantees of one of the people who fell into its trap. It had its own plans for the creatures who survived the catastrophe. The Engineer felt the case with the spare glasses in his pocket, but did not take it out. He slowly lowered his hand and, without knowing why, moved after the couple of crazy hunters. He walked carefully, trying not to stumble, every now and then convulsively waving his arms to maintain his balance. No, his head was not spinning, his arms and legs obeyed, but his own body seemed alien, and the unusual sharpening of all his senses made the Engineer feel out of place. However, this state did not last long. With each step he walked more confidently, and his heightened senses became more and more familiar. As they say, it is easy to get used to good things. Even before reaching the top of the rubble, the Engineer realized that his “renewed” body was in excellent shape, and his hearing, sight, and smell were sharper than ever. He took out his unnecessary glasses and threw them aside. His hearing caught the crack of the case and the ringing of broken glass, barely audible to a normal ear. Now it sounded to the Engineer like the crash of a collapsed display case. His hearing even told him exactly which of the glasses had broken. The Engineer grinned. So far, he still did not understand what the mysterious flash had done to him, but he was beginning to like it. "Perhaps this will really pass soon," the Engineer thought, "for example, if the flash only temporarily activated the body's hidden capabilities. But until it passes, it should be used to the fullest!" The next signal was given by the heightened sense of smell. The enthusiasm that had surged up immediately evaporated. The smell that reached the Engineer was heavy, disgusting and... so attractive! The Engineer suddenly realized that he had been following this smell from the very beginning, he just realized it only now. He was following the smell of fresh blood. He was drawn to the same place as those two madmen and... for the same reason! The Engineer felt very scared for a moment. He felt a wild, overwhelming hunger. In an instant, his entire perception of the world was turned upside down. Now, for the Engineer, there was nothing left in the world except hunger - a beast that had settled inside a man. This terrible beast was tearing itself out, gnawing its way out, devouring the insides, and howling wildly, using the empty stomach of its owner as a megaphone. The Engineer's gaze was covered by a murky veil, through which, as if through a filter, only one color penetrated - red. However, besides it, only the gray color of dust and ash remained in the world, so the "filter" did not have to bother much. Finding himself on top of the rubble, the Engineer looked around at the view. The ruins of houses towered ahead, so he couldn't see the horizon, which was still covered in a milky white glow. But he did see what excited his new being the most. The loot. In a narrow hollow between two rubble heaps, a hunt was underway. Two women, trembling with fear, backed away and turned their heads, feverishly trying to find a way out of the trap, but they clearly had no chance of escaping. Two more madmen joined the two, with sooty faces and some strange gloves, with fingers that looked like they were five sizes too big. These clowns behaved almost exactly like the madmen. They bared their rotten teeth, growled gutturally and stretched out their hands to their victims. It all looked like a scene from a bad horror movie, and that's all. The four hunters, as the Engineer noticed, were reaching out to only one of the women. Why – became clear when the Engineer looked more closely. This woman was wounded. Her right shoulder, side and thigh were covered in blood. The heady smell of blood hit his nostrils again. The engineer barely restrained the desire to throw himself down and, pushing aside his competitors, sink his teeth into the victim's throat. Only one thing stopped him - calculation. Trying to take the prey from four madmen could be a dangerous undertaking. It was much easier to attack the second woman... The Engineer did not think that these thoughts were wild and crazy. Moreover, as soon as they came into his head, they immediately became something like a new set of life rules. No, the Engineer had not gone crazy. He knew that for sure. He had simply become different. Who? At the moment, it did not matter to him. All that worried him was the desire to satisfy his hunger. He tore off the top of the rubble and rushed down, growling menacingly and waving his fists. At first he was running straight at the second woman, but at the last moment it was as if a "filter" had fallen over his eyes again, and his legs themselves stopped next to the bleeding victim of four mad hunters who had already begun their feast, although the woman was still alive. The pain shock did not allow her to scream, she only wheezed and thrashed in agony, her eyes wide with horror and pain. The engineer suddenly met the gaze of the victim and froze for a moment. No, it was not what he read in the gaze of the man being devoured alive that stopped him. Such sentimental nuances no longer bothered him. The second-long pause was caused by something else. The engineer wrinkled his forehead, as if trying to remember something, but he remembered nothing and abandoned this stupid activity. While he hesitated, competitors could gobble up the most delicious morsels. Feeling that a rival had appeared behind him, one of the "smoked" ones turned around and extended his hand with an open palm towards the Engineer. The long fingers of the hand covered in dirt and blood were not the fingers of the clown gloves at all. These were the natural fingers of the gnarled hand of a strange creature. Also gnarled, and with very long sharp claws. It is unlikely that this guy was born such a freak; most likely, the same flash was to blame for this change. “But then there may be some changes in other people too!” The engineer forgot about his hunger for a second, looked at his hands, and then quickly felt his shoulders and face. Apart from the waxy pallor of his skin, everything seemed normal. The second "smoked" also raised his gnarled hand in a strange greeting, and the Engineer suddenly felt weak. He took a couple of uncertain steps back and, stumbling, awkwardly fell on his butt. He wouldn't be able to clearly explain what had happened, but he understood very well that he wasn't afraid of the "smoked ones" or their mysterious weapons. Because he himself wielded these weapons. And even better than these clumsy freaks. When the opponents returned to their wild feast, the Engineer rose from the ground and stepped forward resolutely. He was not angry, did not intend to throw his opponents aside or engage in a fight using secret weapons. He simply walked up and took what he wanted. And no one stopped him. The madmen, whining like beaten curs, crawled away, and the "smoked" ones simply ducked, stepped aside and left the best pieces for the Engineer. As the Engineer became more satiated, his thinking became more productive, but the new basic rules did not change. From now on, everything that had previously been unnatural became the norm. This did not fit with the concepts of human behavior, but the Engineer did not care about such subtleties. "A person is free to set boundaries and norms. For special people, they should also be special. I am special now, no doubt about it. And it doesn't matter why and for what purpose I became like this! What matters is that I am now a different person, I am a representative of a new species of humanity! And the fact that ordinary people are now just food for me does not contradict this at all. The strong have always devoured the weak. Now, in the literal sense too. A normal situation." One of the madmen tried to crawl towards the gnawed remains, but the Engineer noticed his advance and casually kicked the creature in the side. The creature whined and jumped to the side, hitting some little thing in the process. The Engineer looked at the thing and suddenly realized that it interested him. It was as if he had seen this thing a long time ago, in a past life. The Engineer picked it up and wiped the dust off it. It was a small powder compact, made in the shape of an oyster shell. Most likely, it fell out of the victim's pocket. The Engineer pressed a button, and the plastic shell folded open. As the Engineer expected, there was a mirror glued to the inside of the lid. The Engineer looked at his reflection in it and suddenly realized why this thing seemed so familiar to him. No, he had never looked in this mirror. But he knew the woman who did it well. He himself had bought this powder compact for that woman. The Engineer brought the mirror closer to his face and stared blankly at his reflection. The terrible discovery should have excited him, caused at least some reaction: disgust, anger, shame... anything! But the Engineer felt nothing. No human emotions boiled in his soul. And what did they not boil - they did not even stir. "Human?" The engineer finally realized what he was seeing in the mirror. "Who, that... monster?" The engineer threw the compact on the ground and stepped on it with his heel. At that moment he did not yet know what the future held, did not know what world he would have to live in, and did not even know if he would live to see tomorrow. But he knew one thing for sure - there was no turning back. All talk of a new species of humanity turned out to be empty fantasies. The flash did not create any new species. It only disfigured the old one. “And those whom she was unable to disfigure, she killed.” The engineer turned his gaze to the place where the second woman had sat before the wild feast began. She was not there, and that was good. The engineer could not remember exactly, but he suspected that he had known this woman in his past life. To devour her as well would probably be too much. Even for the monster he had become... .

... – Engineer, – came a mental call. – Are you in the castle? The gloomy scenery of the vision flared up with a milky-white fire, moved away and crumbled into weightless ash in the distance. In the foreground there remained only a pale face with bottomless black eyes without whites and a madly distorted mouth, in the corners of which human blood was caked. The face (or should I say, the muzzle?) of a mutant. At that moment, just a mutant, a monster who only much later would receive a species name - psionic and a personal nickname - Engineer. Yes, the face was exactly as he saw his reflection in the cracked mirror. The owner of the "Klyuch" almost howled like a wolf, exactly as he did then, in Chernobyl. A guttural growl burst from his throat, and the waxen face with black eye sockets immediately moved away sharply. The Engineer finally realized that what was around him was no longer an illusion, and he cut off his growling. The pale face in front of him was not a figment of his imagination or a reflection in a mirror. The creature that looked like the Engineer (as well as three dozen other psionics) was real. It was the Ghost, the master's closest assistant. Where such a strange nickname came from, no one knew, not even the master himself. Perhaps it was the Ghost's talent to appear silently, as if using the notorious teleportation. Although who would understand this Zone? It could well have endowed him with such a talent. In the past, the Ghost, like the Engineer, was a fairly educated and smart person, and therefore, by the will of the Zone, he turned not into a cunning and evil controller, but into something more. Why shouldn't the Zone give him a bonus? After all, it gave the Engineer access to the Thirteenth Sector and handed him the crystal. During the outbreak, the Ghost turned into a creature of the same species, which means he also had the right to a prize. For example, to further development, to turn into something even more advanced. “Exactly... into something,” the Engineer measured his assistant with a glance. “Into a creature without a face, feelings or personality. Into something capable of controlling the living and the dead, but without the slightest idea why it needs it. After all, manipulating creatures and people in the Zone is a pure game, and we are not capable of going beyond this damned territory. Perhaps into an additional dimension. But that is also part of the Zone. We are alive only in the Zone. One step beyond it - and that’s the end. And here, by the way, a reasonable question arises. Maybe Prizak’s ability to teleport is a hint that I am on the wrong path? Maybe the future belongs to people like him, and not to my experiments with the crystal?” The psionic, frozen opposite, bent down slightly and lowered his black gaze. He caught the thoughts of the master and, probably, agreed with them, but he was in no hurry to admit that the future was his. Just as he was in no hurry to develop his talent for teleportation. The Engineer, with or without the crystal, was stronger, and the Ghost intended to serve the master faithfully and truly. All this was easily read in the assistant's thoughts. He did not hide anything from the boss. Otherwise, he would not have become the first assistant. When you and your master are telepaths, you can't hide bad thoughts. Either you are sincerely devoted, or you are dead. There is no third option. Especially in the service of such a tough and tenacious master. The Engineer, indeed, became the master because he thought more rigidly, clearly and logically than all the "converted", as they called themselves. And he also made decisions faster than anyone else. And most often - the right decisions. There were misfires, of course, but there were more successes than failures. Therefore, the psionics unconditionally trusted the Engineer, respected him and feared him. After all, decisions were often final. All those who disagreed with the Engineer (and persisted in their delusions) instantly became dinner for a dozen Cerberus dogs guarding the master's residence, or disappeared without a trace in the milky glow of the crystal. What happened when the Engineer led the obstinate psionics or lower creatures into the glow, no one knew. It was impossible to see anything through the walls of the light glass, and no one dared to catch the thoughts of the owner. Personally, the Ghost believed that the guilty became dinner for the Engineer himself. And he did not find anything in this version that contradicted the laws of the Zone. The laws of survival of creatures among creatures and people. Partly, such loyalty of the Ghost was due to loyalty to the owner, and partly because the assistant himself often preferred to eat, as they say, right there at the cash register. The Thirteenth Sector was full of biomass. Such busy creatures as the Engineer and his first assistant often simply did not have time to go hunting in the regular Zone in order to observe the unwritten rule - to kill only people. In fact, this rule is long outdated. The myth that mutant meat is poisonous to everyone except the master's guard dogs (whom he allegedly periodically injects with an antidote) turned out to be a myth. The Engineer had proven this long ago, but shared the results of his research only with the Ghost. Other psionics and creatures had to be kept in the dark for the time being. Once they had completed the main task... let them eat each other as much as they wanted. It was not easy to hide such thoughts from the psionics, but for now both the Ghost and the Engineer were coping. Yes, to be honest, they had no time to think about such trifles. The new operation was in full swing. The Engineer personally supervised the testing of the access points to the outside world opened by the "Key", but the Ghost also had no time for extraneous thoughts. The small but important operation entrusted to him was not going very well. That was why the assistant lowered his gaze. So far, nothing catastrophic had happened, but in general, the "vector of developments", as the Engineer himself liked to say, was clearly not directed in the direction that the psionics wanted. “Two of our brothers are out of the game,” the assistant reported mentally. "Unacceptable losses," the Engineer thought. "Four more died in the Borderlands. There are too few of us to lose brothers and sisters in local skirmishes." - Two of the walkers are resistant to hypnosis, and one is completely immune to our influence. In addition, all five are protected by the Red Death artifacts. Only the Black Angel was able to neutralize these artifacts, but... “This anomaly has not existed for a long time,” the owner finished his assistant’s thought. “The Key is also capable of this,” the Ghost hinted. - Now I can't use the "key" for other purposes. You should figure out how to hack their defense. Let the brothers involve the controllers and dwarves in the matter or force the people to get into some kind of psi-anomaly. Think, Ghost, think. I have no time to waste time. These people are dangerous, it is important to stop them, but capturing the Borderland is more important now. If we fail to cope with this task within 24 hours, we will have to lead the creatures deep into the Zone and wait for the beginning of a new cycle. And it will begin only in three days. During this time, the people can destroy our army. "It's not easy to hack the Red Death's defenses," the Ghost doubted. "Almost impossible. The cunning artifact easily bypasses all energy traps. No wonder people call it the Joker." - So, find a way to destroy the artifacts along with the people. - I will try. “Don’t try, find!” demanded the Engineer. “I’m terribly sorry...” a mind unfamiliar to the Ghost suddenly intervened in the mental conversation. “Leave us, Ghost,” the owner ordered. The assistant bowed again and stepped outside the light glass. If he wanted, he could have quietly listened to the conversation between the master and the unfamiliar psionic, clearly a newbie. But it was not worth risking it out of pure curiosity, and the Ghost had no other motives. The master trusted the assistant with enough secrets, and the Ghost did not need those he did not trust in his work. - Am I in the way? The thoughts that came through the telepathic channel had a pronounced emotional coloring. The palette included shades of pain, frustration, and fear of anomalies. Such coloring was not typical for full-fledged psionics. However, psionics did not end up in anomalies out of stupidity. - Not out of stupidity, but for conspiracy! What else could I do? Everyone would end up in "Auger", but I wouldn't? - You flatter yourself. - The engineer chuckled. - Your gift is akin to ours, but you are still far from a real psionic. Perhaps you will never become one. - But I don’t differ from other people in appearance! - That's what I'm thinking about. Everyone should use what the Zone has given them, without forgetting what it left them from their previous life. - And about what she took away. - A hint of disappointment flashed through the thoughts that came from outside. “It seems we’ve already discussed this issue,” the Engineer commented sharply on the last thought. “Back during our first mental conversation. I don’t see any reason to repeat what we’ve already covered. The less time you take up from me, the sooner I’ll start discovering new points of penetration that will automatically expand the boundaries of our world. And that means they’ll allow us to live where we want. Do you finally understand, or should I repeat it again and more slowly?” - I understand. Then very briefly. Your psionics are too arrogant. That's why they are easy to kill. - Your friends aren't the best Zone walkers either. How did you end up in "Auger"? - Something like that. However, notice that all the walkers reached the path safe and sound, and the psionics suffered irreparable losses. I don’t know how many people will get out of the “Screw”, but the survivors will definitely continue the raid. Yampol is ahead of them. A little more, and half the way will be passed. You are clearly losing this round. - Stop being cheeky! - the Engineer thought irritably. - I have no time to deal with trifles! My assistant will take measures. If you try, you will read in my thoughts the outline of the plan according to which the psionics will act in the future. I give my permission. - It's not easy to read. - Your problem. If you're afraid of dying with everyone else, then strain yourself. But I don't have time to explain it to you. And I don't really want to. - Who was that woman? Why can't you forget her? There were hundreds more victims after her. So why? Because she was the first? Or is there another reason? “You’re getting into the wrong thoughts, you two-legged leech!” the Engineer was indignant. - You said it yourself - "Try harder." A smile appeared in the telepathic voice from the outside world. - One more trick like that and I'll burn your brains out! - Very scary. Okay, I'll limit myself to current affairs. So what have your psionics prepared for us? Ah, I see. Not much. - That's enough for you. It would be better for you to "accidentally" lag behind the group at the last moment and carefully escape from the danger zone. - I'll figure it out. Well, that's it, I have to go. The group is starting to come to its senses..

Raindrops fell from the grey sky slowly, almost like snowflakes on a windless New Year's Eve. Having fallen on the cracked glass of the filter mask, they broke into thousands of tiny splashes, which a little later again united into large drops and flowed into a grey puddle. From the "parterre", from the ground itself, or rather, from the old chipped asphalt, the puddle seemed like a natural sea. Real waves with foamy breakers were moving along it, and where the raindrops fell, the grey sea threw out intricate fountains resembling crowns and swirled small whirlpools. The only thing that let us down was the angle. Lying with our right cheek in such a “sea,” it was difficult to appreciate all its monochrome beauty. The left eye saw the “sea” at an angle of ninety degrees, and the right one contemplated the murky depths. However, if it weren’t for the rising water level, one could admire this dull but hypnotizing micro-landscape forever. Andrey tried to come out of his stupor, water had already started to pour into his nose, but willpower alone was not enough. Andrey involuntarily inhaled a portion of the muddy water, coughed, but was unable to turn over: his body did not obey. "It's funny and a sin to drown in a puddle!" Lunev concentrated and tried to pull his legs to his stomach and his hands to his chest. The attempt was successful, but it did not improve the situation. Andrey's face was still lying in the gray sea. Suddenly, some external force came to the rescue. It easily pulled Andrey away from the puddle, turned him over on his back and pulled the mask off his face. Lunev's eyesight darkened from a sharp headache, and his ears felt like thick cotton wool. - No, after "Sandblasting" you were, of course, even more handsome, - someone's voice broke through the cotton wool in my ears, ringing like a bell. - But even now you're still a handsome man. You've been pretty battered. Are you alive, no? The buzzing in his ears began to subside, and his vision began to clear. At the same time, the stranger's voice lost its metallic tint and became almost recognizable. A little later, Andrei felt his body. At first, he felt a slight tingling and warmth in his stomach, then the warmth spread across his chest, arms, legs, and finally reached his head. As soon as the warm wave reached the top of his head, the Old Man realized that he was in control of his body again. He lay there for another minute, watching silvery drops fall from the gloomy sky, then gathered his strength and first rolled over onto his side, and then slowly sat up on his knees. True, the attempt to raise his head to look at the person standing opposite him ended in failure. A sharp pain in the back of his head forced him to drop his head onto his chest. Andrei massaged his neck and tried again. This time he managed to raise his head, although the pain still interfered. Having somehow coped with task number one, Lunev tried to fix his floating gaze, but again failed. He had to close his eyes briefly, then blink and close them again. After several exercises, the dizziness almost passed, and Andrei finally understood who was trying to establish contact with him. - How long have I been lying here? - Half an hour for sure. - The man squatted down. - What have you gotten yourself into? You look like a cafeteria cutlet. There are probably no flies hovering over you. – At least I don’t stink? “Not yet.” The man grinned and extended his hand. “Hello, Old Man.” - Hello, Major. Andrei grabbed Major Brazhnikov's hand like a drowning man grabbing a lifeline. - Yes, healthier than you, that's a fact! Major Brazhnikov helped Andrei to stand up, drag himself to the rusty skeleton of a tractor, sunken into the mud near the road, and sit down on the running board. “We got into the ‘Screw,’” Andrey said, carefully touching his face. “It felt like I was riding down a steep hill inside an iron box.” - "Auger"? - The major thought. - Never heard of it. Something new? – Haven’t you noticed that everything in Sector Thirteen is new? - In the sector? - Brazhnikov looked around. - It's Yampol, wake up! - How did you get here? - Just like you. - Well, here it is... the path is both the Zone and the Thirteenth Sector, that’s what the Mechanic says. - And what about near Chistogalovka, in Chernobyl-2, then? - And there is the Thirteenth in its pure form. Its hidden part. - It's too difficult for me. Brazhnikov picked up some rusty fragment and threw it into the roadside bushes. There was a rustling sound in the bushes. The major raised his machine gun and took aim. - Hey, listen, soldier, ours! - they groaned from the bushes. - Looks like I broke my leg! Help! - Scout? - the major, without lowering his machine gun, took a couple of steps towards the thicket. - Yes, it's true. - The stalker groaned. - It hurts, damn it! - I'll help you now. Brazhnikov went deeper into the bare, but still dense thickets and soon pulled out the incessantly cursing Scout. - Everything hurts, I want to eat compote! - Scout groaned, gritting his teeth. - My whole body is one big bruise! Your "Jokers", damn them, didn't help at all! - Without the "Jokers" you would be silent as a fish now. - The major sat the stalker down next to Andrey and clarified: - Like a dead fish. - Olga! - the Old Man perked up. - She was left without an artifact! - Well, may she rest in peace, - the major said calmly. - I don't know what kind of "Screw" it is, but judging by your condition, it's unlikely you'll survive in it without a "Joker." “It’s my fault,” Scout sighed, hugging himself by the shoulders. “I feel sorry for the girl. Even though she was a bitch... a sight to behold.” “Goat,” someone in the ditch just to the left of the tractor groaned barely audibly. “Did you hear?” Scout asked, alarmed. “Or did I imagine it?” - We heard, - said Brazhnikov. - The mermaids are whispering from the puddle. I'll go fishing. The major quickly descended into the ditch and almost as quickly climbed out. The "catch" turned out to be more impressive than one could have expected. Brazhnikov fished out two "mermaids" at once in the mud. One, Tatyana Sergeyevna, climbed out almost on her own, the other had to be dragged and then carried. It was Olga, and she really got it much harder than the others. She didn't seem to have any serious fractures, but there were bruises and probably a lot of internal injuries. “Scout, give her back the Joker,” Andrey ordered. - Isn't it too late? “I’m telling you... you’re a goat,” Olga said through clenched teeth. - Take it, take it, - the stalker hung the bag with the artifact on Olga's neck. Tatyana helped tuck the thing into Olga's bosom. - Get well soon. - The Mechanic is left. - Andrey tried to get up. - Major, don't you see him? - I wish I never saw him again. - Brazhnikov shook his head. - But it wasn't meant to be. Look, he's hobbling. He was thrown the furthest. You were really "lucky". Like copper pots. I passed by just fine. In just half an hour. “Why would that be?” Scout muttered, glancing sideways at Lunev. Andrey remained silent. He didn't have the strength to stop the stalker again. - So, Slavs, are you alive? - the Mechanic hobbled over, sat down right on the side of the road and looked at the group with a tired look. - And the major arrived in time. Good. - Is it not customary for Germans to greet each other? - Brazhnikov measured Kruger with a hostile look. - The costs of a barbaric upbringing, - Max calmly retorted. - I was born in Kazakhstan. In 1941, my grandmother was exiled there from the Volga region. Did you get through without any problems? - Well, so. - The major illustrated his words with a vague gesture. - I did a little shooting. But the trail was clear. And what kind of "Auger" did you end up in? - Like this. - The mechanic drew a spiral in the air with his finger. - It lifts you two meters into the air and starts screwing you in like a screw. With constant acceleration. If you don't group yourself together, it can spin you around so much that your arms and legs will be torn off. In short, it's like the Carousel, only with a horizontal axis. - The way you describe it, it looks more like the barrel of a rifled weapon, - Brazhnikov noted. - And you had bullets in it. - You could say that. We were lucky that the barrel was short, we only had to drag it a hundred meters. - What hundred meters?! - Scout was indignant. - We almost flew a kilometer! - By inertia. - Kruger nodded. - But without the "Jokers" this would have been enough. – Olga was without the artifact – and nothing happened, she survived. - She landed in the mud. And if, like the Old Man, her face was in the asphalt, they would have gotten tired of scraping it off. “It’s all clear,” Brazhnikov concluded. “Shall we move on?” “Let me wake up,” Andrey said. “I need to take an energy drink.” “And about a hundred grams...” Scout winced and rubbed his leg, “painkiller.” - You'll be patient. - Brazhnikov crouched down and felt the stalker's leg. - The bones are intact, you were afraid for nothing. “I’ll stay,” Olga said quietly, “you won’t go far with me.” - Refuse. - The old man shook his head. - It's time for a rest. If you don't get up, Scout will drag you to the base, and we'll go on. - He'll get up. - Major Brazhnikov took a first aid kit out of his pouch, and then his "Joker". - We'll cure him now. The entire hour allotted for rest, the major worked his magic on Olga, Andrei and Tatyana dozed, leaning against each other, and the Mechanic and Scout quietly quarreled, simultaneously observing the area. Several times mutants appeared in their field of vision, mostly boars and pseudo-dogs; once a group of some walkers flashed by on the edge of the forest to the south of the road, and twice on the horizon, presumably over Novosyolki, a flock of unusually large birds soared into the sky. When the flock soared into the gray skies again, the Mechanic managed to raise his machine gun and examine it through the optical sight. It turned out that these were not birds, but humanoid, but at the same time quite good flying mutants, nicknamed by the people "batmen". In general, everything in the Zone was as in the Zone. Anomalous life went on as usual, practically not interested in the rather vulnerable group of walkers at the moment, licking their wounds on the outskirts of the village of Yampol, which had long since turned into ruins. It cannot be said that this arrangement did not suit the group, but towards the end of the halt, the Old Man still tried to understand why the Zone had suddenly given the walkers such a long respite. “We’re on the path, but we can still be seen,” Andrey said, finishing outlining the essence of his doubts, turning to the Mechanic. - And why do you want to warm your head? - Kruger grinned. - No one is attacking, so be glad. “There’s really nothing to be happy about,” Brazhnikov said, looking closely at the edge of the nearest forest. “It’s a fact.” The old man and Kruger also stared at the dark gray thicket. Five seconds later, Andrey stood up abruptly and checked his weapon. - The rest stop is over. Wake up. Major, how is Olga? “I’m fine.” Olga limped toward the trio. “If I don’t run... I’ll get there.” - That's great, get moving! - Lunev cast another anxious glance at the edge of the forest. - Brazhnikov, bring up the rear. Let's go! It was impossible to say for sure what was happening at the edge of the forest, but it was not necessary. There was a ghost of danger hovering over the edge, or some clear threat was hiding in the forest – there was no particular difference. In both cases, it was wiser to avoid meeting it. Shadows flickered among the trees, and the bushes and young trees swayed every now and then. Several times, the crackling of deadwood and indistinct sounds were heard from the thicket: either echoes of words, or the growling of animals. All this could indicate the movement of, for example, a large family of wild boars or a couple of pseudo-giants, but Andrey did not rule out the worst option. A raiding party of some group could be moving parallel to the group. If it was again led by controllers, then sooner or later the pursuers would attack. This type of danger could not be avoided even if you wanted to, but by pressing on, the walkers could at least find themselves in a more advantageous position than the one they occupied now. For some time, while the invisible path coincided with the highway, it was easy to control the terrain on the left. The group simply kept "alignment to the left" and at the first sign from the Mechanic leading the column, they scattered along the right shoulder, aiming towards the forest. When the highway brought the walkers to the main zonal route "R-10" and the path turned sharply to the right, it turned out that the source of the probable danger remained in the rear and the entire burden fell on the rearguard. Brazhnikov and Lunev had to almost back away, tensely peering into the thickets on the sides of the highway and into the silhouettes of the ruins of the old car wash, which darkened half a kilometer to the left of the highway. Closer to the half-destroyed bridge over the Uzh River, it became even more difficult to monitor the situation. The floodplain of the river on the left, overgrown with tall bushes, and the dense forest on the right provided the enemy with an excellent chance to stealthily get close to the road. And what convenient targets the walkers directly on the bridge became, there is nothing to say. A dream of a novice sniper. It's good that by this time the group had completely forgotten about the consequences of the encounter with "Screw" and entered the working rhythm, and therefore passed the bridge in a matter of seconds. Even Olga, who was still limping and generally moved, as athletes say, "on moral and volitional strength", did not prevent the group from passing the dangerous section at a good pace. The remains of houses and buildings of the village of Cherevach were dark ahead, but Mechanic, as promised, immediately led the group to the left after the bridge. Now the path ran along a long, almost devoid of vegetation, from which both the opposite bank of the river and the bridge were clearly visible. Walking on dense sand was no more difficult than on asphalt, and a good view allowed one to relieve tension a little, although, on the other hand, stomping through open terrain, the group clearly gave itself away. In response to Brazhnikov’s remark on this matter, Mechanic threw up his hands. - There are no options, but it's even good. We'll get to the forestry, get into a small grove, but then we'll come out into the field again. We'll have to walk about a kilometer and a half as if in the palm of our hand. So, in short, train. Whether the major was satisfied with Mechanic's arguments or not remains a mystery. Brazhnikov turned towards the river and stood there, watching the bridge and the opposite bank, until the moment when the group disappeared into the copse. Then the major caught up with his comrades and again went at the rear. It turned out to be not that far to the "forestry", and they managed to pass it without any obstacles. The group passed through the non-existent forestry, without even tripping over the remains of the buildings. The vast ashes on the site of the office and outbuildings had long been overgrown with bushes and practically drowned under many years of layers of pine needles, rotten grass and river silt brought in by floods. - Now to the collective farm yard and across the field. - The mechanic pointed straight ahead and then to the left. - Roughly - heading for the lake. Or Novoselki, as you like. "Wait," Olga said, alert. "There, ahead... something's wrong. Tatyana, you have the best detector, could you...?" - Yes, yes. - Tatyana quickly took an anomaly detector out of her pouch. - You're right. The anomalous background is slightly elevated ahead, but I can't say anything specific. - I can, - Brazhnikov declared. - We can't get to the collective farm yard in a straight line. There's a huge hole there, the "Mousetrap". We'll have to look into Cherevach. Mechanic, do you think it'll work? - The path takes in the outer street and the entire collective farm MTS. You can go there. It's dangerous, of course, the ruins were always full of dwarfs, and between the combines and all sorts of seeders at the station you can get lost and run into poltergeists - there is such a chance, albeit minimal, but it's better than falling into a hole. "A normal choice," Scout hissed sarcastically. "If they don't salt you in the pit with the silage, they'll cover you with a copper basin. And so that you can get through without any problems at all, can't you?" - No. “So let’s go,” Andrey decided. “Lead the way...” ...What the Mechanic called a street was more of a clearing. The leaning, partially destroyed houses were buried in dense thickets, and in the summer they were probably not visible, and what used to be the street itself was overgrown with sparse young trees and weeds that stubbornly stuck out even after drying out and then getting soaked a thousand times by the endless rain. The less tough grass had long since fallen and rotted, which made it even more difficult to push through the stubborn weeds. Every now and then, your feet slipped in the mixture of rot and dirt. - In the summer there are vipers here... - Scout ran his hand over the top of his head. - Like this. And all fat, with big faces. Yellow ones, with a black zigzag. Or the other way around, depending on how you look at it. - Large, with a zigzag? - Tatyana shuddered. - These are Caucasian vipers. They shouldn't be found in these places. I mean all of Polesie. - You can have and enter whatever you want, but I'm talking about what I saw. Sometimes you walk like on bicycle tires, there's a continuous hissing sound. “Maybe I’m confusing something?” Tatyana shrugged. “The Zone,” Brazhnikov muttered. - A convenient excuse. - Tatyana waved her hand. - Anomalies, mutants, mysteries... all this did not arise out of nowhere. For example, mutants appeared only as modifications of traditional local inhabitants. Wolves, pigs, rats, dogs... and so on. There is not a single mutant based on a reindeer, for example, or a walrus. “I wonder what the pseudo-giants were in their past lives?” The major chuckled. “Or bloodsuckers.” “People,” Tatyana answered confidently. “Just like controllers, snorks, and other bipeds.” – What about poltergeists? - Same. - No, - Andrey intervened. - With the latter, everything is more complicated. This is precisely from the category of riddles. As for snakes, they really do exist, and not only in the ruins. There are many of them in the swamps and in the forest. - Achtung, citizen scientists. - Kruger turned sharply and raised his hand. - Quiet! We are approaching. No more was left of the former collective farm yard and machine and tractor station than of the entire village, with the only difference being that the thickets here were not so dense. The bushes grew only near the fence and around the repair shop, in which not only the roof and rusty gates were miraculously preserved, but also several panes of glass in small windows. Scout stuck his nose into the half-open gates, clicked his tongue in surprise and reported that there were even machines and workbenches with vices inside. – It’s unclear how they didn’t steal it. - Who needs them? - The mechanic grinned. - Tools, I understand, but the vice... and the background radiation here is considerable. They won't even accept this radioactive junk for scrap metal. The group rounded the workshop and, as if on command, stopped. Ahead, in the distance, stretched a large field, brown with mud and withered grass, but to get to it, it was necessary to pass between dozens of scattered carcasses of machines, tractors, combines and all sorts of trailers to these rusty masterpieces of agricultural engineering. - Damn, it's really a labyrinth! - Scout blurted out. - Follow me, - said the Mechanic and looked meaningfully at the women. - And not a step to the side! But if you do get lost, don't even think of shouting. Stand and wait, I'll find you. Otherwise you'll call out more... we'll get tired of fighting back. “Don’t get lost yourself,” Olga answered. “I walked here too.” - For a long time? - Last year. “You’ll be surprised,” Kruger promised and grinned mysteriously. - What for? - You'll see. "The labyrinth changes after each Emission," Brazhnikov suggested. "The equipment is the same, but it's in new places." “How do you know?” Kruger, unhappy that the intrigue had died as soon as it was born, turned to the major. “I know.” Brazhnikov nodded forward: “Lead the way, pathfinder.” The Mechanic's advice was useful, but those who listen to smart advice do not go to the Zone. Only stubborn people, convinced that they know the highest truth and do not need anyone's advice, climb into it. That is why the same Olga, "just" having decided to go around the skeleton of an ancient caterpillar tractor not on the left, like the rest of the group, but on the right, found herself in a dead end and was unable to return. She wound around the thick sticky mud about ten times around the ill-fated "ChTZ", but was still unable to find the passage in which her comrades had hidden. All paths led to a dead end, from which there was only one way out - back to the rusty tractor. In the end, Olga, exhausted, sat down on some beam next to the iron giant and began to clean lumps of mud from her shoes. Having cleaned one boot, Olga paused for a moment in her exciting occupation to catch her breath, and suddenly felt someone's gaze on her. Someone was looking from the gray depths of the labyrinth and was unlikely to simply admire. Rather, they were assessing, estimating how tasty the dinner would be today. Olga felt the safety catch of her machine gun and tried to find the enemy, using all her senses, including her abnormally acute sense of smell. The result was not encouraging. The enemy was nearby, but Olga could not figure out where he was hiding, no matter how hard she tried. The situation was even worse than on the clean island. For some reason, the enemy was slow, but the girl had no doubt that he would attack sooner or later. Olga slowly got up and took a couple of steps along the path trodden in the mud around the tractor. No, she did not hope that at the last moment she would find a way out of the trap; simply sitting idly by and waiting for an attack was beyond her strength. Besides, maneuvering in battle was always a good help. Finding herself on the other side of the tractor, Olga stopped and listened. Her senses still couldn't pinpoint the enemy's exact location, but her hearing seemed to have picked up something. A rustling sound broke through the faint drizzle and splash of puddles. The sound came straight from the pile of peeling metal that blocked the view of the repair shop. Although, perhaps the rustling was coming from someone hiding not inside, but on the other side of the pile of scrap metal. Olga pointed the weapon at the pile and slowly moved forward. After the October brawl, Olga had never gone out into the Zone with her beloved flamethrower; the legendary Smoker had disappeared the very moment General Ostapenko gave Olga a chance to start a new life. And yet, Olga often regretted that now her passionate love for fiery toys was limited to making Molotov cocktails and using standard incendiary ammunition. There were times when a flamethrower would have come in handy. For example, now. One shot - and the enemy would have jumped out of his hole like a cutie. Olga even sighed imperceptibly, imagining how good a mountain of metal would look doused with flaming incendiary mixture. And how the mutant would have squealed, set on fire along with it. Olga gritted her teeth. An old mental wound made itself felt. An almost irresistible desire to burn this whole damned Zone to the ground flared up in her soul like a giant torch, which instantly smoked her mind. Olga raised her machine gun to her shoulder and fired a long burst into the center of the pile. The heavy Val bullets did not ricochet, piercing the tin and iron and getting lost somewhere in the depths of the pile, but they were unlikely to pass through. Olga realized that she was only squandering ammunition, but the relapse of pyromania was stronger than common sense. Only when the machine gun magazine was empty, the girl came to her senses and abruptly lowered the weapon, almost dropping it from her suddenly weakened hands. Feeling this strange weakness, Olga realized what was happening. There was no enemy nearby. And no one was trying to attack her from a distance using telepathy. She hadn't lied to the Old One; the controllers really didn't feel her, and when you can't feel your enemy, you can't harm him. So it wasn't the mutants. It was the anomalous energy of the labyrinth itself. Olga remembered Brazhnikov's words about the changes that occurred in the labyrinth after each Emission. "This is a typical sign of an anomaly! Rare, perhaps, but essentially the same as all the others! And what do anomalies put pressure on, if they don't destroy physically? That's right, on the psyche! On weaknesses, all sorts of quirks and fears! And even though my psyche is special, "corrected" by the Zone, it is ninety percent the same as everyone else's! So I got myself into trouble." Olga raised her hand, shaking from weakness and nervous tension, with difficulty and slapped herself loudly on the cheek. It helped to some extent. The surrounding anomaly immediately became more understandable and almost not scary. After the second slap, strength returned to her body, and when Olga mentally (and very firmly) declared to herself that it was time to get out, the labyrinth gave in and opened before her the very passage that she had been unsuccessfully searching for. Olga, quite proud of herself, had only taken a dozen steps down the aisle when the Mechanic suddenly turned around the nearest corner. Seeing the girl, Kruger wiped the expression of concern from his face and glued on his usual mask – a mixture of arrogance and condescension. True, other feelings could still be read in Max’s gaze. First of all, relief that everything had worked out. It was unknown for what reason, but he did not want to lose Olga. It was unlikely that it was a matter of hidden sympathy, rather such was Ostapenko's order, and Olga was still flattered by the special treatment. Maybe not the brutal handsome Kruger, but at least the seasoned practitioner and cynic Ostapenko had some mysterious plans for the strange girl. Perhaps there was nothing good in this, and the general needed the underage pyromaniac criminal only for such raids to the ends of the earth, but for Olga this was an achievement. “You won’t have to exist all your life due to hatred for the Zone. It will pass someday, and you will have to look for another incentive. Professional value is quite suitable for this role. You couldn’t come up with a better incentive. A striking example of professional value is the Old Man. He himself probably doesn’t remember who he was before and why he came to the Zone. And it doesn’t matter. And what trials he had to go through in the Zone are also unimportant. Now the only thing that matters is who he has become. And he has become one of those who are usually called living legends and extra-class professionals. Ostapenko himself seems surprised at how he managed to find, and even get his hands on such a gem for his own use. So, maybe I will too... someday...” “Well,” Max interrupted her thoughts, “are you convinced that you shouldn’t relax here?” - I didn't relax. - Olga looked at the Mechanic defiantly. - I checked something. “I wonder what?” Kruger chuckled. - This labyrinth is a psi-anomaly, right? - And to find out, you spent a whole store? - The Mechanic's gaze became cunning. - You could have asked, I would have told you for free. - I know what you would say! - Olga said, blushing slightly. - "You'll see for yourself." I've already heard! - Okay, don't be so stern, - Kruger asked in a conciliatory tone. - It was not for nothing that I said that you can't go here alone. I have proven it from my own experience. I once got into trouble no worse than you. I spent a day making eights around two seeders. - Yes? - The Mechanic's unexpected frankness melted the ice a little, and Olga softened. - Yes. - The Mechanic's grimace of superiority slipped for a second. - Such an anomaly. It doesn't seem to be lethal, but it straightens out the convolutions no worse than an ironing press. And you're a good girl. You did it in half an hour. A record. Will you share the secret? “I can’t.” Olga looked away. - It's your right. Let's go, the people are waiting. The people shuffling about at the edge of the field were not only tired of waiting, but also worried, and therefore, seeing Olga, they were happy. At least, that's what Olga thought. Even Tatyana, who had recently been looking sideways at her young companion, smiled, sincerely glad that Olga was okay. Only the Old One looked at her with condemnation, but did not give her a dressing down. Unlike the Mechanic, he knew for sure that the psi-anomaly, like the mutants' hypnosis, was not a problem for Olga, but a temporary difficulty. Perhaps, if there had been no comrades nearby, the Old One would have said a few kind words to Olga, but only about the girl's sluggishness. Olga herself understood that out of the half hour spent in the labyrinth, she had wasted twenty-nine minutes on stupidity. The psi-anomaly did not so much deprive her of the ability to think as it touched delicate emotional strings, and Olga succumbed to the hypnosis not of the anomaly, but of the “music” it played. Olga waved to her comrades and quickened her pace, but literally three meters from the group she suddenly tripped and froze, almost falling face down in the mud. In the literal sense. In the collective farm yard plowed up by all sorts of cataclysms, any traps could be hidden in the mud, so it was not surprising to stumble here, but Olga lost her step not because she caught on an obstacle. She was stopped by a sudden surge of mortal danger that threatened the entire group at once. Now this was not a stupid concession to hypnotic pressure, but something more useful in a practical sense. The signal was sent by intuition, unmistakably determining that the controllers were dangerously close (but, by the way, shamefully missed the psi-anomaly). Olga suddenly raised her frightened eyes to Andrey and whispered just one word: “Run!” - To battle! - the Old Man immediately got his bearings. - Brazhnikov, forward, run, march! The rest of you, follow him! Mechanic, bring up the rear with me! Gather in the forest by the power line support! Run! The major and Tatyana, as the most disciplined members of the group, did not ask a single question and did not linger in place for a second. “The controller is leading the dwarfs,” Olga warned Stary and also rushed after Brazhnikov. Kruger, hearing these words, immediately took up a position behind the skeleton of a wheeled tractor to the right of Andrey and took aim at the nearest exit from the labyrinth. As usual, only Scout remained in a special pose. He glanced after his fleeing companions, then assessed the situation in the rear and finally looked suspiciously at Lunev. - I didn’t understand, what just happened? - Don't be stupid. - Andrey jerked his head to the right. - Run into the forest! - What, again? Everyone is a champion, and I'm a dumbass, right?! Why are you so excited, can you explain to me? "Olga has sensed the controller," the Old Man explained patiently, though with some effort. "This time he is not leading bandits, but dwarves." - Where did he dig them up? - I don't know, maybe in the local basements. Do you care? Run, don't hold me up! - I'll stay. I can shoot no worse than you. "Your lung is shot through," the Mechanic reminded him with a grin. "Run, you dead thing, while you still have the chance." - Listen, mercenary. - Scout narrowed his eyes angrily. - It doesn't take much strength to pull the trigger. Fershteyn, a hint? - Sent... Ostapenko a group. - Andrey spat and barked, to the envy of any sergeant: - Scout! - Got it! - The scout threw the machine gun over his shoulder and backed into the field. - Jumped up once, ran away twice. You should have said so right away! He trotted off in the same direction as the first half of the group, but didn't get very far. Something thundered loudly in front of him, and suddenly a huge fountain of mud appeared. Scout involuntarily recoiled, fell on his bottom, and shook his head in disbelief. When the mud and the gray water dust that had risen up with it settled, it turned out that a rusty bulldozer blade was sticking out of the ground in front of Scout's nose. There was no doubt that it hadn't suddenly grown from the rain. Scout looked around and tried to estimate the trajectory along which the ton-weight piece of iron had flown. It turned out - from the center of the labyrinth. - Hey, comrades! - bleated Scout. - That son of a bitch, the controller, he didn't bring dwarfs, but some kind of giants! Andrey and the Mechanic quickly caught up with Scout and helped him to his feet. "They won't get into the sight," Krueger concluded. "But they won't be able to aim themselves either. They'll be firing at areas." - It's a lottery anyway, - the Old Man noted. - But there's no escape. Let's run! - And me?! - South began to fuss. - I have... a lung! - A kick? - suggested the Mechanic. - To speed things up. - Danke. - The scout ran towards the forest and soon overtook the Mechanic by fifty meters. At first, the "artillery bombardment" with agricultural machinery parts was sluggish. The telekinetic mutants, invisible behind the wall of the labyrinth, were clearly trying their hand at throwing what weight and how far they could throw it. But when the group had covered half the distance, the enemies got a taste for it and iron shells of all shapes, sizes and weights began to fall from the sky like hail. The most disgusting thing was that neither running in zigzags nor other tricks could add to their chances. The dwarves' "mortar" fire was not aimed. It simply covered a certain area. Exactly the one the walkers were rushing through. The rollers of heavy tractors, the links of tracks, the parts of frames, the suspensions of bodies, the augers of combines, the buckets of excavators and even the whole cabins flew by with an ominous whistle and, crashing into the ground at different distances from the walkers, raised not just fountains and waves, but whole geysers and tsunamis of thick mud, which knocked them off their feet and almost deprived them of the ability to move. Twice Lunev and once Scout had to literally dig each other out from under a thick layer of mud, and when they reached the forest, the "final shot" almost covered them both. The support roller of the tractor, like a razor, cut off the nearest pine tree, and the tree fell with a groan right on the walkers. Thanks to Andrey's reaction, who pulled his companion out of the danger zone, everything ended with a couple of splinters stuck into Scout's forehead and a reddened cheek - Lunev was lashed by a branch of a fallen tree. Having barely risen, the walkers took their hands and legs again and very soon retreated into the forest to a relatively safe distance. Right to that power transmission line support that the Old Man had mentioned. The unique structure, rusting here without being used since time immemorial (all the wires were stolen by the first stalkers who hunted in the exclusion zone after the Chernobyl disaster), by some whim of the Zone had not yet collapsed and served as an excellent landmark for many walkers. There was complete silence near the support. The deafening splash of mud waves, the dull blows of clods of earth and the whistle of "shells" were reliably cut off by the forest. - I... will run around today... from a heart attack... and in a hundred years... he won't catch up, - Scout said, barely breathing. - You... don't have any spare pants? - Wash yourself. - The old man turned his head, trying to find the others. - Have you seen the mechanic? - I thought you... would ask about Tatyana first of all... - Scout showed a grin. - Kruger and I ran. - No, I didn't see it. Oh! But I see yours! There they go, all three of them! - She's not mine. - Well, yes. - Scout grinned. - Everyone says that. It's not clear who they're testing. Either their friends, or their fiancees, or themselves. Like, to see how strong their feelings are. It won't help, mind you. Your friends will try to talk you out of it, your fiancee will make eyes at them, but you'll get married anyway. - I'll take that into account. - It was clear from the Old Man's face that he wasn't going to pay attention to Scout in general or his fabrications in particular. Andrey didn't want to offend the man who had just helped him get out of the mud captivity. - Now shut up. Or I wanted to, but in such a way that he wouldn’t be too offended. “I was dirtier only once, and that was in my thoughts,” Olga said as the trio approached the gathering point. “We were going to the lake, right?” Tatyana asked with hope in her trembling voice. "Ladies!" Scout hissed dismissively. "If only they could wash themselves." - Andrey, are we on the trail? - Brazhnikov asked his question. - Have we left? Where is the mechanic? - I would like to know myself where this guide is... to a bright future. - Lunev glanced at the surrounding thickets. - And we definitely haven't left the trail. The trail is almost a kilometer and a half wide in this place. Max said so. “He said it was getting narrower,” Scout reminded. - In any case, we are still on it, I'm sure. “What’s the point of arguing? Let’s wait for the Mechanic, and he’ll decide,” said Brazhnikov, but noticing the sour expressions on Andrey and Scout’s faces, he stopped short: “Or what... we won’t wait any longer?” “Where will he go?” Andrey said, not too confidently. “He’ll come!” "Listen, Old Man," Scout said quietly. "I'm not sure, but... I think I know where the Mechanic went." - Are you going to develop a conspiracy theory again? - Andrey looked sternly at the stalker. He waved his hand and winced. - No, come on... I'm not completely crazy! I'm talking about something else. I think I saw Max get hit by a huge spare part. - Did you really see it? - Lunev frowned. - I'm not sure, I'm telling you! You remember how awful it was. A tractor wheel whistled past my ear... just like that... - Scout almost folded two fingers, leaving a centimeter gap. - A little lower - and half my skull would have been blown off. - Not a wheel, a roller. - No way! In general, I didn't have time to look back, and I didn't really want to. But I did turn around a couple of times. That's when I noticed that the Mechanic was almost running after me at first, and then suddenly a frame with a square cabin fell from the sky, and that was it. I never saw Max again. No, that frame... from a combine, probably... could have fallen not on the Mechanic, but simply between us! If he suddenly slowed down - easy! But I'm afraid it fell on him anyway. He didn't brake, he raced like a real horse, no gullies bothered him. That's the thing. "It's really crap," Andrey agreed sullenly. "He's been through so much, he's crawled out of so many situations, and he died so absurdly. He deserved more." - What more? - The scout looked skeptically at the commander. - So that they would drop a pyramid on him? - At least don't be sarcastic now. - The old man flashed his eyes. - He deserved to live, I'm sure, even though we weren't friends. A long and happy life. And you "pyramid"... You're an unfinished clown, Scout. Cheap! The scout remained silent, only sighed and made a sympathetic face. "In war, as in war," Brazhnikov, who had been silent until now, summed up. "We'll come back and remember. But for now..." - Let's move on, - Lunev finished for him. - Even if everything is not as bad as Scout described, we can't wait here for the Mechanic any longer. We need to move. And Kruger, if he's alive, will get there himself. He'll even get there before us. Let's go!

The grey mass of mutants turned out to be a fairly organised army, but only when following two basic commands: "Forward" and "Back".
