Waves of disgusting monsters ran up the walls of the citadel, climbed up, clinging with their claws to any unevenness, lost their attacking mood along with hundreds of "heads" and rolled back under the cover of the buildings surrounding the warehouse complex. The defenders of the citadel celebrated the first two or three repelled attacks with a joyful "Hurray!", but after the fourth and especially numerous fifth wave, it became obvious to people that the mutants did not intend to abandon what they had started and sooner or later would climb onto the roofs of the warehouses, and then inside the citadel. More and more "batmen" and controllers appeared among the attackers, forcing the creatures of other species to turn on hidden reserves and jump almost without a run straight onto the roof. Those monsters that could not fly or jump had a chance to climb over the walls and gates on the corpses of their relatives. By dawn, mountains of ugly bodies formed a real rampart around the warehouse complex, three meters high. Whoever was driving the mutants forward clearly did not consider the losses. With the dawn, it became easier to fire accurately at the monsters, but this could not solve the problem. Reinforcements were constantly arriving for the infernal army. And not simple ones. Several hundred boars and almost a hundred pseudo-giants made up the main striking force of the new assault wave, which moved forward a little later than the other creatures and attacked all eight gates at once. The heavy, thick-skinned boars picked up decent speed and crashed into the gates by the dozens at once, leaving deep dents in the solid iron. The machine gunners on the towers were forced to spend a lot of ammunition to stop the boars, but as a result, the northern and northwestern gates still collapsed, and if it were not for the infantry fighting vehicles propping them up from the inside, the mutants' breakthrough into the complex would have been inevitable. The "cleaners" hiding behind the vehicles opened massive fire from under-barrel grenade launchers and managed to push back the monsters. However, not for long. Very soon the boars returned, but then the soldiers from the commandant's company came to the rescue of the barrier groups, who ran around the perimeter in soap and foam until the last moment and brought the fighters cartridges. Now the "commandants" had the opportunity to prove themselves in a real case. They dragged several boxes of hand grenades to the gates and arranged a competition for the longest throw. Considering the limited supply of grenades in the warehouses, the measure was forced, but the situation required such measures. Having made sure that the next wave had subsided, General Ostapenko, who was personally monitoring the situation in the places of a probable breakthrough, ordered that the remaining grenades be saved for the next attack and switched attention to another front. Inside the citadel, right on the parade ground in the middle of the warehouse complex, under the watchful eye of Bibik, a mortar battery was deployed. Moreover, it had been deployed quite a long time ago, but it had only begun to fire a quarter of an hour ago, when the anti-aircraft gunners on the roofs of the warehouses and in the turrets of the machine guns mounted on the Hummers had more or less settled the problem with the attacks of the "batmen". Ostapenko climbed over the rubble of flying monsters, torn to pieces by large-caliber bullets, with some difficulty, and reached a relatively free parade ground. Seeing the general, Bibik tried to give the command "Attention!", but Ostapenko prevented the colonel's statutory impulse with a gesture. - How do they deliver mines to you? - The general pointed to the piles of stinking bodies around the perimeter of the parade ground. - Why don't you clean up this crap? “Everyone is working!” Bibik glanced at the crews bustling around the mortars. - Colonel! - shouted the artillery officer. - Ready! - Fire! - Bibik waved his hand and covered his ears. The light mortars barked out a "dog waltz" in disarray and a second later repeated the play, but more harmoniously. The third salvo almost merged into a single, slightly howling roar. "All on target," Bibik's communicator came to life. "One is hitting the right edge, adjust it." - Got it. - The colonel motioned to the artillery officer and handed him a communicator. - The spotter says one barrel is mowed down. Maintain contact, correct. - Yes! - The officer took the typewriter and rushed to the position. “Don’t forget to return the phone!” Bibik shouted after him. “Do you think it will help?” the general asked quietly. - We'll hold it until the mines run out. - Bibik waved his hand, pointing to the west. - At the very beginning of the brawl, the "cleaners" from three "AGS" were processing the "hole". The asphalt is in dust, instead of a parade ground there is a crater, and the hole is still there. And the animals crawl out of it without any problems. No, it is unrealistic to fill the "hole", because... there is no "hole" there. - What does it mean? “You know it yourself,” Bibik answered, slightly irritated. “My fault! My nerves are giving way.” "I don't know, but I can guess," Ostapenko said calmly. "And you should take valerian if your nerves are giving out. Do you think this 'hole' is an access point to the Thirteenth Sector?" - Well, it's a strange coincidence, of course. - Bibik shrugged. - It seems like they just sent a group, and then, please, get this - the mountain itself came to Magomed. Everything coincided too precisely, but, on the other hand, maybe we guessed right, maybe the situation was just ripe? “Or someone reported to the Thirteenth about our guesses and they decided that there was no point in delaying,” Ostapenko added, nodding thoughtfully. The battery began to howl and bark again, and Bibik had time to think about the general’s words. “I even know who could have reported it,” the colonel said when the time came for a new adjustment. - I'm not sure about that. - The general shook his head. - And it doesn't matter. For now. What's more important now is to get out of this cauldron. “I understand that.” Bibik winced and snapped his fingers, as if trying to catch an elusive thought. “Only... if an exit from the Thirteenth opened up here and the Old One’s group is heading to the Thirteenth...” Ostapenko stared at the colonel with interest and even nodded slightly, as if encouraging Bibik: “Come on, think about it.” - Maybe we should have started the raid from here? - Bibik suggested this not too confidently. - Maybe we should try it ourselves? Mow down the bastards with massive fire, throw a ton of explosives into the "hole", and move to the Thirteenth. To counterattack. - No. - A grimace of mild disappointment appeared on Ostapenko's face. - You didn't understand the main thing, Bibik. There's only an exit here. You can't enter the sector through it. - Yes? - Bibik thought again. - Actually... that's right. No matter how much we hammer, it's no use. All the mines and grenades explode on the surface. I mean, in our... sector... or whatever you call it... in our dimension, or something? "Now you're thinking correctly," the general approved. "That's why Stary's mission is becoming vitally important for us. If Lunev can figure out what's going on and find a way to fix the situation, we'll survive. If not, then no." “He will find it,” Bibik said confidently. “He must find it.” - I think so too. And also... The general did not finish his thought, because Bibik suddenly leaned forward and with all his enormous mass fell on the commander. Ostapenko lost his footing, and the officers found themselves on the ground. There was no malicious intent in the colonel's actions; on the contrary, if it had not been for his throw, the D-3 base could have been left without its only general. It turned out that a large flock of "batmen", about forty heads, still managed to break through the barrage of improvised air defense and rushed at low altitude, just a couple of meters from the ground, straight to the position of the mortar battery. Ostapenko and Bibik found themselves right in the path of the flying creatures. As the pack passed, the colonel jumped up and tried to open fire, but he only fired a couple of shots and lowered his Kalashnikov. The monsters grappled with the fighters, and it became too dangerous to shoot. Bibik tried to fumble for his communicator, but remembered that he had given the device to the battery commander, and spat in frustration. “Duty officer,” Ostapenko boomed from the stalls. “Special group to the parade ground!” “Yes!” the general’s communicator speaker responded in a toy voice. “I’ll warm up.” Bibik attached a bayonet-knife to his machine gun and resolutely moved toward the place of the fight. - Colonel Bibik! - Ostapenko stood up. “I am.” Bibik turned around and looked at the general in such a way that he changed his mind about stopping him. - Carefully! - Eat! Bibik got off to a flying start and began to accelerate like a locomotive. By the time he crashed into the thick of the fight, all eight pounds of his live weight were rushing at the speed of a cannonball. It is not surprising that Bibik knocked two mutants out of the melee like bowling pins, simply crashing into them with his shoulder and chest. The colonel casually hit another one with the butt of his rifle, so hard that, having fallen, the monster no longer moved, well, and the last one, who had finally extinguished the kinetic energy of the living express, Bibik pierced almost through with a bayonet. The bayonet entered the creature's skinny belly like butter, and also, almost without effort, severed its spine. The "Batmen" sensed what a formidable reinforcement had come to the rescue of the mortar men and instantly redistributed their forces. Six or seven mutants immediately soared into the sky and, having performed a somersault, synchronously dived down on the colonel. Bibik was not frightened by their acrobatic stunt. The colonel took a couple of steps to the left, then abruptly leaned forward and spun his machine gun over his head with his arms outstretched. The maneuver allowed him to get rid of one of them – it was flying straight at the colonel, but after Bibik stepped forward, the beast was forced to go up again, almost scraping its nose along the asphalt. And the “marching techniques with weapons” pretty much spoiled the mood of the others. The mutants probably expected the man to start shooting, and were quite prepared for this – they flew head first, with their clawed paws outstretched (the word hands would have been more appropriate, but hands are not clawed), and even “corkscrewed,” to use flight terms. Shooting at such small and moving targets would really have been necessary, carefully aiming, which meant wasting time. But Bibik, contrary to all the assumptions and wishes of the mutants, acted unconventionally. He was not afraid to let the monsters get close enough and engage in hand-to-hand combat with them. Having fallen into the mill, which was represented by the machine gun rotating above Bibik's head, two mutants suffered fractures of both arms, and one almost lost his head - the bayonet-knife slashed the monster across the throat and cut it to the spine. The fourth got a glancing blow from the butt, but also quite hard. The creature squealed and, having plopped down on the ground, hastily crawled away as far as possible. The two mutants remaining "on the wing" made a turn following the example of the first relative and again rose to a height of ten meters. They probably expected that the very first one would be waiting for them there, and therefore, not finding the leader, they hesitated for a moment, looking around. This turned into a shameful, and most importantly - fatal fall. The special group that arrived in time took down the creatures hovering above Bibik's head with just two accurate shots. Both "batmen" fell a meter away from the colonel, in the same place where the notorious first one fell like a stone. A few seconds earlier, General Ostapenko personally shot him down from an almost silent "Vintorez". Bibik gave way to the special forces and, putting the car into reverse, walked towards the general. - Good shot. For the boss. "Stop being ironic, Colonel." Ostapenko's voice lacked its usual severity. "Not all generals are fat careerists." “I’m not being ironic,” Bibik shrugged. The exchange of pleasantries was interrupted by a new cannonade. As soon as the special forces cleared the position of the remnants of the hellish squadron, the surviving mortar men continued firing. They did it in good time. It was noticeable how the fighters on the roofs of the warehouses and the machine gunners on the towers came to life. Most likely, taking advantage of the pause in the battery's work, the enemy brought fresh forces out of cover and went on another assault. The guess was confirmed exactly one minute later, when the drawn-out song of the mortar choir was joined by numerous rattles of machine guns, the bass, measured rumble of large-caliber weapons, and the pops of underbarrel grenade launchers. Ostapenko nodded toward the roof of the nearest warehouse. Bibik moved first. They managed to climb onto the roof without any problems, but what the officers saw from the observation post promised very big problems in the future. Base D-3 no longer existed in its original boundaries. All its sectors had been reduced to smoking ruins: from the airfield and combat units to scientific laboratories and a trade sector, which adjoined a vast tent city, where stalkers and guests from the mainland lived in numerous trailers, campers and tents. Smoke hung over the ruins, blocking most of the view, but in the gaps between the smoldering ashes in the drizzling rain, one could see not only what was happening on the former territory of the base. Visibility to the south and east was more or less decent, almost to the gray horizon. To the east, beyond the sparse forest and the ravine-covered field, at about the level of the settlement of Gubin, which remained only on maps, a red glow was blazing. What could be blazing there and how the mutants managed to set this "something" on fire, one could only guess. However, the answer to the second question could be simple. The arsonists were not the creatures. The forest or the remains of the settlement's buildings were set on fire by people in order to stop the countless army of monsters spreading from D-3 in all directions. The fact that the mutants were gradually spreading along the outskirts of the Zone was confirmed by other "species". First of all, the closest approaches to the citadel. The mutants were slowly approaching, using a smoke screen as camouflage, and they were approaching not only from the "hole", but also from other directions. Another confirmation that taking the mutant warehouse complex was an important, but not a primary task, was the southern view. A fairly decent group of mutants was concentrating in the wagon-tent city, and it was clearly aiming at the citadel, but even more creatures were showing their backs and tails to the defenders of D-3, slowly moving towards the outer edge of the three-kilometer-deep border strip. Bibik raised a powerful pair of marine binoculars, confiscated from the thrifty chief of the warehouse complex, and tried to examine the vanguard of the army advancing on the mainland. As the colonel had expected, those same human-looking controllers in oversized robes were moving ahead, under cover of pseudo-giants and several strike groups of boars (ambiguously lined up in a wedge). They were walking slowly, even more so - gradually slowing down, but they were not going to stop yet. Bibik mentally calculated where it would be most convenient for the new owners of the Borderland to draw a new border, and came to the conclusion that it would be the bank of the Teterev River. And apparently, Bibik guessed the intentions of the leaders of the hellish army. As soon as the vanguard disappeared into the coastal sparse forest between the villages of Oranoye and Zorin, the bulk of the horde advancing south stopped and began to disperse through the numerous copses, ravines, lakes and buildings. It was unlikely that they were doing this for the purpose of clearing out the enemy, but most likely they were looking for food, but in this case the former did not interfere with the latter. The mortars fell silent again – the commandant’s company was completely exhausted, and the process of bringing ammunition to the battery had failed. Bibik was distracted from studying the situation. Having convinced himself that the difficulties were temporary and purely technical, the colonel continued to survey the surroundings, but now he tried to see through the smoke the source of all the troubles – the “hole”, the supposed exit from the Thirteenth Sector. For some time the smoke was too thick, and all Bibik could see was the roof of the nearest barracks, but soon a light but persistent breeze blew, and for a few seconds the “hole” became visible as if in the palm of his hand. - Colonel, your connection! - The commander of the special group appeared on the roof. - The mortarman transmitted. Bibik silently took his communicator from Pashkov and put it in his pocket. - Thank you. - We gave them a light! - the lieutenant exclaimed, his eyes sparkling with excitement. - And will you teach us this "mill"? - Later, Pashkov. - Bibik again pressed his gaze to the binoculars. - We'll exchange impressions later, too, - Ostapenko added coldly. - Go through the positions, lieutenant, collect data on losses. And warn everyone. There will be a new assault soon. - Eat! - That's for sure, they'll start crawling now, - Bibik said, having finished studying the situation around the "hole". - They want to catch us by surprise. They're quietly gathering their strength. - They are secretly assembling a strike force? Where? - In the barracks. - Bibik pointed to the two-story barracks nearby. - The "Batmen" are leaving the circle on foot and dispersing to the barracks. “This is bad,” the general became alarmed. - That's right. They'll take off unexpectedly in a flock, an anti-aircraft regiment won't be enough to fight them off. And when they land in the courtyard, things will be really tough. We can cover our own with crossfire. We'll have to fight hand-to-hand. The most we can use are pistols and shotguns. "It's bad," repeated Ostapenko. "Hand-to-hand combat is half the trouble. The 'Batmen' aren't the most toothy creatures, and they don't use weapons..." - Their claws are five centimeters long and sharp as knives. The special forces are fine, they are equipped with Kevlar, and the "cleaners" only have armor. They can get hurt... like the mortar men. The mutants tore three of them to shreds while I was juggling a machine gun. - Yes, losses are inevitable, - Ostapenko firmly agreed. - But I think most of the regular units will still have enough training to dodge claws and teeth. I'm talking about something else. I'm worried about the mutants' intelligence and the beginnings of tactics in their actions. It feels like they're being commanded by people. "I've seen a few," Bibik agreed. "They look like commanders. I'm not sure they're human, but they look and act... well, not like mutants, that's for sure." - The missing link? - Ostapenko grinned. - Or vice versa. - Bibik did not understand the hidden meaning of the general's phrase. - An advanced version of controllers. You wouldn't even guess at first that they are mutants. They behave almost like humans, and they can brainwash you - much better than controllers. Such spies will infiltrate the trade sector, and you won't immediately be able to distinguish them from the local public. Kazakevich's dream, in short. Only the other way around: not people with mutant abilities, but mutants disguised as people. If they mentally command the landing force, the "batmen" can put up serious resistance. We can't handle them with knives and shovels. “There are quite a few stalkers among civilians,” Ostapenko said thoughtfully. “They have guns.” - They won't give it back. - We won't take it away. Let them go into the yard and destroy the flying landing force with us. I don't think they'll refuse to help. - Well, yes, they are helping themselves, - agreed Bibik. - I will talk to them myself, they will not refuse me. - Yes, go, organize this militia before it's too late, and take command. I can handle this myself. At the same time, I'll try to contact the group. Maybe the connection will be better from here. “If it doesn’t exist, then it won’t exist at all,” Bibik shook his head. “It’s a satellite line.” “It’s a strange situation.” The general took out his communicator and turned it over, as if hoping to find a button on the body that could be used to increase the signal or do something like that. “If they’ve already entered Sector Thirteen, that’s not strange,” said the colonel. “You see, nothing gets in, only... all sorts of nasty stuff comes out.” - Too early. - Ostapenko shook his head. - Okay, go, scourge of the "batmen", get to work. “We’ll push them against the southern wall,” Bibik warned. “Don’t send ours there, so they don’t get shot.” - Good. Good luck! Bibik saluted and hurried down the echoing stairs. The general, perhaps without realizing it, said the main thing. When you fight an enemy for whom there are no rules other than "kill the enemy or die", but who is led by quite reasonable commanders, training, powerful weapons, physical strength and courage are not enough to win. You also need at least a little luck. "On the other hand, fortune favors not only the lucky, but also the stubborn. Judging by the fact that we are stuck in this mess, we cannot be considered the first, which means we will fight until this capricious lady decides that we are worthy of being among the second. Should we get used to it?"

The forest to the northwest of the dangerous settlement of Cherevach was boring. Especially in contrast to the mysterious village and the "cheerful" field beyond its outskirts. Pines and pine trees. Occasional bushes. Underfoot, a springy coniferous litter, branches and roots, over which everyone had tripped at least once. Above, between the crowns of the pines, the sky was gray, from which small drops of rain continued to fall. And that was all. No one attacked, did not annoy, did not even snore somewhere in the bushes. Animals, people, birds - they all disappeared somewhere, as if they had never been in these places. It seemed that the Zone took a time-out to observe how the walkers would behave, left without a guide. Would they lose their way and abandon their hopeless undertaking before it was too late. Andrey had no intention of losing his way. He had already figured out the route the Mechanic was taking the group along, and he could complete the raid without his former comrade's help. The only thing the Old Man lacked was a sense of direction, which the Mechanic had managed to hone in a month of wandering along the route. On the other hand, Andrey was still a more experienced walker and knew the Zone very well. Where the Mechanic sensed the path, Lunev relied on basic logic and knowledge of the terrain. Let's say the group was now moving towards Lake Krinka. The mechanic said that the trail goes past the lake, to the left of it, but after a kilometer it turns left, and before the village of Korogod it again leads north. Lunev didn't even have to strain his brain to understand that there was simply no other secretive, and at the same time quite convenient, path along this section of the Zone. Yes, from the point of view of ordinary stalkers, this was a rather unsafe route, but that's the point. Those who walked along the trail were not afraid of mutants and anomalies. They were only worried about not giving away the location of the entrance to the Thirteenth Sector to strangers. Well, and not to strain themselves too much. For example, like in this forest. “Not a march, but a walk...” Andrey chuckled, “from one anomaly to another.” Lunev had no doubt that they would soon encounter some new anomaly ahead, and so he was in no hurry. The comrades needed to rest and come to their senses. For about half a kilometer, the group walked slowly, gradually accumulating strength and trying to scrape off the lumps of dirt. Although the clay stuck to their shoes was removed fairly quickly, the "camouflage" stains from their clothes simply did not want to be removed. "It'll dry out and fall off on its own," Brazhnikov said, commenting on Tatyana's fruitless efforts. "Don't waste your energy, you'll need it." - I can't... so dirty... - But we blend in with the terrain. - The major grinned and caught up with Andrey. - Not in a hurry? - Is it necessary? - How can I tell you? - Brazhnikov shrugged. - You left before dawn, but I only left at seven, and even then... I didn't leave, I crawled away. “We heard explosions,” Andrey nodded. “Something serious?” - Oh, nothing special. About a hundred thousand mutants infiltrated the base and started doing weird things there. Well, and the garrison, of course, started shooting back. - Why didn't you say anything?! - And when was there time to talk? We also have diarrhea and scrofula. - Tell me more, - demanded Andrey. - Seriously, up to a hundred thousand... mugs showed up? And from what direction did they attack? How did they manage to break through the perimeter? There's electric barbed wire and machine guns there. - Well, maybe I was wrong about the hundred thousand, who counted them? But if I lied, then it was on the smaller side, that's a fact. And they broke through... they didn't break through from nowhere. They shot out like foam from warm champagne, right in the middle of the base, and began to crawl away into the back streets. - In the middle of the base? - Well, almost. On the parade ground next to the airfield. Where our weapons hangar and detachment headquarters are. "We started from there," Andrey said thoughtfully. "Was there fog? A greenish one, in the shape of a ring. - I don't know. I didn't see it myself. Bibik enlightened me when we had already moved to the warehouse complex and started barricading ourselves. - Where did they go?! - Lunev stared at the major. - Is it really that bad? - Even worse. If our guys are still holding out in the warehouses, then this isle is all that's left of the base. And it's not clear for how long. There's enough ammunition and food there for a month, at least, but that's if you just sit there and half-heartedly shoot through the loopholes. That won't work with mutants. They'll try to storm the place every half hour. They'll drag two or three fighters away at a time, and after a hundred attempts there won't be enough shooters for all the walls - and then it's all over. Without support or evacuation, there's no chance, that's a fact. But those arrogant bosses at headquarters, you know, only think quickly if they need to steal something. While they're consulting with NATO, while they're coordinating with the Belarusians, and they're coordinating with the Muscovites... – We can’t evacuate with helicopters, the weather is lousy, and the “batmen”... - Exactly. So what? They'll plan either a breakthrough or... a bombing. - What about the garrison? Not all of it died while you were digging in the warehouses. - Up to a third of the personnel were lost. The rest are on the walls. Not only that, there are up to a thousand civilians there. But you understand, the headquarters are afraid of the Zone like the devil. It's easier for them to bomb D-3 to smithereens and blame it all on the same Zone. - Is there any way we can intervene? - Lunev switched to a businesslike tone. - Tell me, - Brazhnikov looked closely at Andrey, - the general didn't send you for no reason. On purpose. - Well, yes. - Andrey smiled sadly. - I just forgot to say what the goal was. He has this habit. First he sends it where it would be impolite to say it in front of the ladies, and it is unclear why, and then he waits for the results. - He only does that with some people. - Brazhnikov rubbed his cheekbone and sighed. - He sends you and Bibik like that. He only sends me on specific assignments. - Don't be jealous. - Andrey shook his head. - It's easier for you. - You're more interested. - Well, yes, especially when you feel that a lot depends on you, but what exactly... no idea. - I would be glad to help you. - The major spread his hands. - But this is all too complicated for me, you know. I know how to fight, and I know how to conduct investigations at the same time, to unravel all sorts of enemy tactical schemes... - Strategic ones too. - All the more so... it's not mine. But if you want... I'll express some thoughts. They may not be worth anything... “Speak, of course,” Andrey encouraged him. “An outside perspective is always worth a lot.” - Bibik called the place where the mutants broke through "an access point to the sector", and the scientists, whom the general managed to bring to the citadel, argued about some "contact spot". At first I thought they were discussing tires, but then I realized: they were talking about the same trouble as Bibik. Do you get it? - Sector thirteen? - Well. - Why are we going to an unknown place if access to the sector opened right at the base? - What's the point of it being open? The beasts are rushing through it - if you try, they'll trample you in a moment. No, we're going the right way. We'll get to the Thirteenth from the rear, far from the front line, and what we'll find there... that's a question for your brain. My helmet hanger doesn't have much imagination. Fact. - Yes, that's a problem. - Lunev looked thoughtfully at his feet and suddenly stopped. Brazhnikov looked at Andrey in surprise, then turned his gaze to the pine forest floor and squatted down to get a better look at the object that had stopped Stary. It wasn't a mine or a tripwire, but it was definitely worth stopping. The major threw his head back and carefully examined the bare branches of the nearest pine trees. He didn't find anything unusual, and yet the thing that had interested Stary was none other than the "Thorn" - an artifact produced by the "Burning Fluff" anomaly. Usually, this vegetation, altered by the Zone, hung from low trees and bushes in long whitish strands that were easy to notice and avoid, but now there was nothing similar nearby. However, just as there is no smoke without fire, so there were no artifacts in the Zone without anomalies. If there is a "Thorn", then there should be "Burning Fluff" nearby, the only question is - where? Overall, the anomaly was not the most dangerous. Lunev, for example, had to pass through entire groves of such a disaster. If you move slowly and smoothly, the vegetation did not react in any way, even allowing you to admire it in a sense. The sight, to be sure, was exotic and beautiful in its own way. But as soon as you quickened your pace or made a sharp movement, the insidious mutated trees and bushes immediately shot out small burning spores, which, having hit open areas of the body, burned no weaker than drops of concentrated acid. Below average pleasure. Brazhnikov gave Andrey a sign to stay put and slowly moved forward to reconnoiter. He returned a minute later. - There, behind the bushes, there is a strip of young animals, all in shaggy hair. It stretches for about three hundred meters to the left and right, we'll get tired of going around it. – We may or may not get tired, but we will stray from the path. “It’s a fact,” the major agreed. “We’ll have to take a risk.” Both of them, without saying a word, put on masks, hoods and gloves. - Listen, Old Man, someone's hot on our heels! - Scout hurried up to the vanguard. - They're sneaking around like cats, but I still heard them. Oh, why are you covering your faces? “I advise you to do the same,” Andrey said. “And don’t twitch. Further on is the grove of “Burning Fuzz.” - Ah, I see. - Scout took hold of the mask. - Wait, what about those... in the back? - We'll warn them too, don't worry. - No, I'm not talking about the women, but about those who sneak. If they attack, what, we won't be able to run away? - If they attack, we will have to fight. - How? Among the "Pooh" you can't quickly change your position, or give a hearty punch if it comes to a fist fight! A dozen spores will land on one spot, they'll burn through both clothes and Kevlar! The guys told me how spores burned through one's armor! - They won't burn through, - Brazhnikov replied confidently. - It's all talk. Fear has big eyes. If you're afraid, go over there, behind the pine tree, take a leak beforehand, so you don't wet yourself later. - Oh, what a joke! - Scout grimaced, snorted contemptuously and pulled on his filter mask. - Why don't you joke any more? I'm wearing a gas mask, you can command: "I allow smoking." That's your humor, military man, isn't it? "You should get a job at the mill," Brazhnikov suggested calmly. "You can easily grind a sack of flour a day with your tongue." - There's someone there! - Tatyana and Olga declared almost in one voice, drawing level with the men. - They're catching up! - Is the controller leading? - Andrey asked Olga. “I don’t feel it,” she answered and pursed her lips. - What difference does it make to us? - Scout intervened. - Let's leave while there's still time! They'll catch up in a hurry, but we'll have to crawl. Ladies, fart! - What do you mean? - Tatyana was surprised. - Are you giving orders again?! - Olga was indignant. - Put on masks, hoods and gloves, there is an anomaly ahead, - Andrey resolved their doubts. - And no sudden movements. - "Burning fluff"? - Olga guessed. - Oh, if only there was a flamethrower here... "That's true," Brazhnikov agreed. "A forest fire would be handy now. At the same time, it would shake off all the extra people from the tail." - Fire? - Tatyana looked at the major with distrust. - Are you serious? - Are you against it? - This is barbarism! – Barbarity is when people kill people. And a forest fire is a forced measure, a way of survival. - Outrageous! - Tatyana looked at Olga for support. "Fire is not always an enemy." Olga looked away. "Especially in the Zone." - Olya knows what she's talking about, - Scout chuckled. - I remember there was a stalker wandering around the Zone with a flamethrower, his name was Smoker... "The wind is blowing the wrong way," Andrey interrupted him. "Major and Scout, bring up the rear. Ladies, follow me." The time gap that Scout had talked about disappeared as soon as the group entered the gray kingdom of anomalous vegetation. The "burning fluff," similar to gray tow, hung from the branches of trees, mostly young pines, everywhere and left rather narrow corridors for passage. In some places, gray strands intertwined, forming a solid wall, and then they had to make intricate turns or lie down on the ground and crawl. And all this had to be done extremely slowly, as if they were portraying the Baltic rapid response team at a KVN festival. Meanwhile, the pursuers had come very close to the burning grove and even poked their noses into it, but they did it too quickly. It was as if a light breeze had run through the outer trees, and the "Burning fluff" on their branches began to move. The abnormal vegetation shot out spores almost silently, but after this volley, noise was guaranteed. In this case, these were painful screams and swearing, by which it was easy to determine not only the distance to the pursuers, but also their numbers, and also to guess what kind of uniform they were wearing. Andrey waited until Olga caught up with him and looked questioningly at the girl. "The telepath is not with them," she whispered. "He is somewhere nearby, but these fighters are walking on their own." - Take Tatyana away, - Andrey ordered. - Don't turn anywhere. In fifty meters there will be an old clearing, go along it to the north to the fallen pole and wait for us. - And you? - And we'll have some shooting. “Pooh doesn’t like that,” Olga warned. - I know, but if we don’t close the issue now, these bandits will follow us all the way to Chistogalovka. - Are you sure these are bandits? - Nine to one. They are the easiest for the controllers to deal with, so they forced "Duty" to let the Arkharovtsy onto the trail. - What if you're wrong? What if it's someone more serious? - You're not mistaken. I recognized several voices. They were in the forest near Kamenka. That's it, crawl away from here. Andrey repeated the order with a gesture to be sure, waited for the ladies to continue their difficult journey through the dangerous thickets, and then slowly moved back. Seeing the commander, Scout and Brazhnikov understood everything without further ado. They parted and lay down in line with Lunev, each about fifteen meters from the leader. The bandits appeared in sight about five minutes later. They also tried to move slowly and smoothly, but this did not add to their vigilance. The Arkharovtsy clearly did not expect that the walkers would dare to do such a trick as shoot out in a forest full of "Burning fluff". Apparently, the bandits believed in the tales about the special burning properties of anomalous spores and could not imagine that someone would dare to disturb the peace of the anomalous forest, bringing down its burning wrath on their head. Andrei signaled his comrades not to shoot until the entire gang had entered the thicket, and when that happened, he was the first to open fire. What began in the ranks of the bandits when Lunev's comrades took up the initiative could not be called panic, that was too weak a word. Those of the Arkharovtsy who fell to protect themselves from bullets received a powerful volley of stinging spores, and those who froze in place still fell to the ground, but already dead. Only three tried to return fire, but only one of them risked falling and rolling. The other two fired on the move, backing up to the edge of the grove. It is clear: in the thickets of "Burning fluff" this was not the best way to move and conduct combat operations. Both fighters soon found themselves in the thick of gray strands hanging from the nearest tree, and here neither clothes nor masks really helped them. Even from afar, it was clear that people's skin was swelling into large blisters, their faces were swelling, their eyes were swollen, and their hands were swollen and covered in purple sores. But these two did not die from pain or sores, but from suffocation. Following the face and hands, the larynx swelled, and the edema blocked the flow of oxygen to the lungs. If there had been a doctor in Lunev's group, he would have confidently said that these people actually died not from burns, but from a severe allergy caused by substances penetrating through the affected skin. In a matter of seconds, only one fighter remained from the group of pursuers, which initially numbered about a dozen bayonets, the same one who was not afraid to fall and opened return fire. According to all the rules of survival in the Zone, it would have been better to finish him off, but Andrei ordered to retreat. Not because he felt sorry for the enemy. The fighter simply acted competently, and even three of them could have spent a lot of time to destroy him. And time, as it turned out in a conversation with Brazhnikov, was in short supply. The trio of walkers slowly rose, moved back, and soon emerged from the dangerous thickets into the normal forest. "It's brutal," Scout said, examining his gear. "I picked up these spores like a dog picks at burrs. How do I clean them off now?" - With a knife, - advised the major. - Are you convinced that all the stories about the special acidity of spores are pure show-off? - No, what about those... that swelled and burst? - He didn't even have time to come out of the anomaly, and he's already making up new stories, - Brazhnikov grinned, turning to Andrey. - What kind of breed is this?! Who burst, what are you talking about? “Well, they didn’t burst,” they wheezed and fell down like dead men. - They've crawled right into the tow - feel the difference. These hairs, as if alive, can crawl under your clothes. Even into your pants, if the fly isn't zipped. Well, and there... - Hello, Horror, if in pants. - Scout took out a knife and began to clean the spores from his clothes. "Let's move," Andrey said, pointing the direction. "We'll clean up as we go. There'll be something like an automatic car wash ahead, with brushes." - Where, what? - Scout turned his head. - In the clearing. Look at the young growth. We'll have to fight our way through it like through a jungle. - Ah, well, that's usual. - The stalker grabbed the knife more comfortably and waved it, as if warming up his hand before a saber fight. - And where are these? - Brazhnikov looked around. - Did you send them here or somewhere further away? “These ones are here,” came from the bushes on the right, “as agreed, we’ll wait by the fallen pole.” The ladies emerged from the thickets. - Well, that's great. - Brazhnikov hid his grin. “That we’re not at a broken trough,” Scout did not fail to add. “We’ve finished the chatter,” Andrey ordered. “Knives ready, we’ll fight our way through.” “Barbarically destroying the young growth,” the restless Scout nevertheless had the last word. The group's formation changed again. Olga moved in behind. It took all of his manly strength to transform the virtual path into a real one. Andrey suspected that in a fairly wide area - here the path was certainly not any narrower than the hundred-meter-wide clearing - there would definitely be a path trodden by someone else. For example, by the inhabitants of the Thirteenth Sector or the same Mechanic. But it could take more time to find the old path than to cut a new one. In addition, there was a risk of running into someone stomping from the hidden sector on the old one, or... who knows... Andrey didn't try to invent anything else, but simply got down to work. After all, the lake wasn't that far, about half a kilometer, and after Krinka the trail should have gone through a relatively light forest again. When the exhausted group reached the lake, the mud on their clothes had finally dried and started to fall off in chunks. But the mud that covered their faces and hands and had penetrated under their combat suits was again dissolved by sweat, and now it stung unbearably, getting on numerous abrasions and scratches. Seeing water in the gap between the trees, the ladies squealed quietly but in unison and rushed towards the lake. It was impossible to stop them with a simple shout, and Lunev trotted after them. Scout and Brazhnikov got out onto the shore a few meters to the left of the trio. None of the men shared the ladies' delight. Lake Krinka was considered a bad body of water. No one was in a hurry to assign it the high status of an anomaly, but still, stalkers preferred not to approach this round, as if by compass, and deep, like an artesian well, lake without extreme need. The water here was crystal clear, no doubt, the air was also, there were never any radioactive tails nearby, and the view around the lake was simply fabulous. But still, stalkers did not like this place. A tall, dense pine forest surrounded the lake without gaps, even in those places where a clearing had once been cut for a power line. The same one, one of whose supports still remained near Cherevach, and near another woman Lunev and his comrades were waiting while they fought with bandits in the burning forest. The pines around Krinka looked calibrated, grew almost at an equal distance from each other and exactly three meters from the water's edge. But the most unusual thing was the shore. It was covered with thick, silky and absolutely alive (despite February) grass, which looked just as alive not only in the air, but also under water. The grass carpet began under the pines and went into the water to an unknown depth. In any case, as far as the transparency of the water allowed, the same grass could be seen on the bottom as on the shore. The gray clouds hanging over the lake and the drizzling rain spoiled the fairytale picture a little, but, on the other hand, the fairytale could have been about a gray wolf. "Even, most likely, about him," Andrey thought. "Nobody spreads rumors for nothing. If stalkers say that Krinka is a bad body of water, then there are reasons for that." Lunev exchanged glances with Brazhnikov. The major was also not thrilled about the forced stop on the shore. Moreover, he looked with the greatest anxiety not at the forest, but at the lake. "It's kind of... creepy here," Scout said. "I heard there are water sprites here. Old man, have you heard of such things?" - No. - Well, damn, you're something! Don't you go to bars, don't you listen to the news? Water ones, these huge ones, like newts, only two meters long. They see you from under the water, come up and, like, show you their reflections. Well, like a double from the water is staring at you. You, of course, are in shock and for now you just stupidly stare at "yourself" in the water... - The water spirits grab you by the nose and drag you to the bottom, - finished Brazhnikov. - Sneeze. They'll make up such tales for you in bulk at the market, a ruble a bunch. - Not at all, not... nonsense, - Tatyana objected, not stopping washing herself. - Andrey once... well, we saw these water spirits. In Pripyat. “They couldn’t have moved here,” Andrey said, nodding reluctantly. “It’s far from Pripyat. Not even close to Uzh. And there are no streams flowing in or out. If there are water spirits here, they’re our own. Or maybe it’s just a fairy tale.” - Old man, - Olga called, - to hell with them, with the water spirits. I have no strength, give me five minutes... for personal hygiene. “You washed your face, what else?” Scout interjected. - Did they ask you? - Olga looked meaningfully at the stalker and then turned her gaze back to Lunev. - Dirt under the clothes... rubs everywhere. We have leather... not like your tarpaulin. The old man hesitated for a couple of seconds, but, noticing Brazhnikov’s nod out of the corner of his eye, he made a positive decision. "Scout, go into the forest, watch the rear," he ordered. "Brazhnikov, check the route. I'll remain... in charge of the bathhouse." “Will you at least turn away?” Olga asked half-affirmatively. - No. - What do you mean, no? - Olga was surprised. - Why?! - No - that means no, I'll look at you and the water at the same time. - Andrey nodded at Tatyana. - Tanya just told me why. - You saw water spirits in Pripyat, not here! - The conversation is over. If you want to wash up, go ahead, if not, let's move on. “I wish,” Tatyana intervened in their squabble. “Andrey is right.” - Well, yes, it's easier for you! - Olga looked sullenly at Tatyana, and then at Lunev. - But you are still a pervert, Old Man. - Why “still”? - Tatyana asked with interest. - Ask him some night, he will tell you. Tatyana looked questioningly at Lunev, but he didn't even raise an eyebrow. As if Olga hadn't said anything. Tatyana seemed to be satisfied with this reaction. She took off her shoes, unloading vest, bulletproof vest and pulled off the top part of her overalls. The dirt from the bombing in the field near Cherevach and the crawl exercises in the burning forest had really gotten into the most hidden folds of her clothes. Tatyana carefully walked along the soft grass and fearlessly stepped into the cold water. She stood there for a few seconds, getting used to it, then pulled off her T-shirt, bent down and began to splash. “Is it cold?” Olga asked. "I like contrast showers," Tatyana smiled with difficulty. "I set the regulator to forty-five, and then to four degrees with an interval of ten seconds, and so on for five minutes. It's even warmer here, about ten degrees. - Does she really have a shower with an automatic thermostat? - Olga asked in surprise, turning to Andrey. - Scientists live in a lush environment. “Wash yourself,” Andrey answered briefly. “You could have just said, ‘I’m tolerating it,’” Olga muttered quietly, clearly addressing the remark to Lunev, “but... ‘I love contrasts.’ What a bimbo!” The old man saw perfectly well that Olga was simply embarrassed, so she was trying to talk her way out of it, first of all to herself. Finally, Olga took off her unloading, began to unfasten her bulletproof vest... and suddenly, against her will, she flew away from the water's edge, as if she had been sharply pulled toward the forest on a cable. Andrey acted as a cable, and at the same time a winch. Grabbing the girl by her waist belt, he pulled her back and to the side with all his might, removing her from the dangerous coastal zone, and at the same time from the line of fire. In fact, there was no reason to open fire yet, but there was already a reason for serious concern. Andrey stepped ankle-deep into the water, called out to Tatyana and extended his hand to her. At the same time, he did not take his eyes off the center of the lake. No, Andrey did not see dark silhouettes under the surface of the water or other signs of approaching creatures. He was not at all sure that Tatyana was threatened by any creatures. Perhaps it was the anomalous nature of the lake itself, but the danger was obvious, that was indisputable. In the center of the lake, the water, contrary to all laws of physics, was slowly rising in a huge dome. If this were not a lake, but a swamp full of viscous mud, the formation of a giant bubble on the surface would be understandable. But the water definitely could not rise in this way, unless... Lunev looked around restlessly... unless a gravitational anomaly was trying to manifest itself in the center of the lake. Tatyana hurriedly pulled on a damp T-shirt and overalls, and only then wandered to the shore. The lost seconds cost both her and Andrey dearly. The bubble that had swelled in the middle of the lake suddenly exploded, and a tall fountain shot up into the gray sky. A tight stream of water seemed to lash right at the low clouds, but the most amazing thing was not the height of the fountain, but its diameter. The water column was at least three meters in cross-section. What an unimaginable mass of water rose to a height of almost a hundred meters, it was possible to realize only when all these ten railway tanks of clean and cold liquid opened like a crystal umbrella and fell back into the lake. The terrible roar blocked up their ears, and the icy wind almost knocked them off their feet. However, what the air that had started moving could not do, the water did. A five-meter wave raised by the fountain instantly rolled to the shore and, having subsided, easily, like two weightless pine needles, washed Andrey and Tatyana into the lake. The water also reached Olga, who had managed to move further into the forest, but these were already tears. The wave doused the girl only up to her waist and did not even manage to knock her off her feet. Andrey couldn't see how Olga was doing, but he assumed that she was much more comfortable than the couple struggling in the icy water, and so he focused exclusively on finding and rescuing Tatyana. For some reason, his companion had been carried much closer to the center, and now she was floundering on the slope of a new wave, not as high, but still dangerous. The wave was carrying Tatyana straight to the pines, which had temporarily become a parapet for the lake that had overflowed its banks. Lunev remembered very well how an encounter with trees ended after a sliding with acceleration. The episode with the bandit who got into the "Tramplin" was quite fresh in his memory. Andrey tried to rush across, but the wave was both faster and stronger than the man. The old man was already being carried back, and therefore, in order to make it to Tatyana's rescue, he had to swim against the flow of the water. Not straight, at an angle, but still against. There was no chance of success. And yet Lunev swam. He rowed desperately until he himself was slammed against a tree, so much so that Andrey passed out for a while. However, the slight knockdown did not prevent him from grabbing the pine tree with his arms and legs and firmly fixing his grip. The next wave that returned, this time a "childish" one, about two meters high, brought Andrey to his senses. He shook his head, blinked and tried to find Tatyana with his eyes. At first he saw no one. His heart sank, but a second later Andrey discovered that Tatyana was still afloat, although again quite far from the shore. And she, most likely, was also “lucky” to have firmly touched the pine “parapet”. The girl did not panic, she struggled and did not seem to be going to drown, but her movements were not very coordinated. In such a state, it would not take long to lose her bearings. The lake is small, but, no matter how you look at it, the shore where Lunev was standing was much closer than the opposite one, to which the confused Tatyana could have headed. It is especially important to consider that the swim took place in icy water, and even a small extra distance could have been fatal. Lunev's fears were confirmed just as he plunged into the water again. Tatyana began to paddle towards the opposite bank. A little more, and Andrey would have had to swim not to the rescue, but straight to the other bank and meet his companion there - Lunev was rather cool about this prospect - but then Major Brazhnikov intervened in the development of the situation. He emerged from the forest, instantly assessed the situation and rushed to intercept. Lunev's heart immediately felt lighter. From Brazhnikov's position, Tatyana was twice as close, and the major had more strength than Andrey, which meant that the chances of rescue were seriously increasing. Andrey stopped and, turning over on his back, swam back. A new wave came to his aid, this time quite light, not stopping Brazhnikov, but only slightly slowing him down, but it was enough for the tired Lunev to reach the shore. The old man froze for a moment, stretched out on the grass in the pose of a starfish, then somehow got up, looked for Tatyana and the major with his eyes and... threw himself into the water again. Low waves began to run towards Andrey again, but this time they were raised not by the anomaly in the center of the lake, but by those very creatures whose appearance the Old Man had feared from the very beginning. Foaming the water with blows of their long flattened tails, two large monsters were spiraling tighter and tighter around the swimmers. It was difficult to see them in detail from afar, but Lunev immediately noticed that the creatures had no paws, and their large flat heads with wide mouths and round, unblinking eyes were most like those of a snake. In general, the mutants looked like giant vipers. One difference was their flat tails, which perfectly served as a monofin. However, a little later it became clear that there were other differences. When Andrey swam closer, he saw that the snakes were covered with large scales, more reminiscent of a fish than a snake, and had a different color. Along the body there were not zigzags, but chains of almost perfect diamonds of purple and dirty yellow color. But it finally became clear that the creatures only resembled large vipers from a distance when one of the monsters opened its mouth. The front teeth, inside which in ordinary snakes there are channels from poisonous glands, were not much longer than the others. A palisade of fifteen-twenty centimeter teeth stretched along the entire mouth, both above and below. Andrey had no idea what he could do when he got close to the creatures, but he stubbornly swam, trying to make as much noise as possible. Brazhnikov behaved in approximately the same way. Neither of them had any serious weapons; when they jumped into the water, they both left their machine guns on the shore, but the major managed to pull out a pistol. Looking at it, Andrey pulled out his Makarov. It was unlikely that these firecrackers could pierce the monsters' scales, but they could certainly distract them. And, in fact, that was what was needed now. Lunev stopped, "sat down" using the water polo swimming technique, and, holding the pistol with both hands so that the violent shivering from the cold would not interfere, took aim at the monster hanging over Tatyana. The Makarov may not be the most powerful or accurate pistol, but it is unrealistic to expect it to fail, or even delay, after just a swim in clean water. In terms of reliability, this weapon has always been in an honorable second place after the Kalashnikov, even in comparison with the best world models. The pistol did not fail this time either. Andrey emptied the magazine in a matter of seconds and hit the target at least three times. The bullets did not cause any harm to the monster, but they served as a sufficient irritant for the giant snake to hiss with displeasure and sharply turn towards Lunev. Andrey changed his position and, throwing the gun, swam back on his back. Competing with the water snake in speed was a sure-fire lose-lose proposition, but the Old Man was not used to giving up. When the monster was already two meters away, Andrey "sat down" in the water again and pulled out his two favorite Finnish knives from their sheaths. These blades, one a replica and the other a real one, with a rich history (the blade still had the engraving "NKVD" and serial number), had ripped open the bellies of more than a dozen mutants and could well have done what bullets could not do. The giant reptile raised its flat head above the surface of the water and opened its terrible mouth again. The snake suddenly stuck out its forked tongue, which almost touched the face of its future victim. Andrey estimated the distance and prepared to hit the snake hanging over him right in the vertical pupil. There was one chance in a thousand for the success of such an adventure, but Lunev believed in himself and his luck. There was nothing else left for him: just hope and believe. - Down! - suddenly came from the shore. Andrey, who was about to jump out of the water up to his waist to strike, suddenly relaxed his legs and plopped down on his back. At least a dozen bullets flew into the snake's mouth, breaking off teeth and knocking fountains of blood mixed with scraps of mucus and meat out of the sky. The monster's head jerked back sharply, and its thick, one and a half girth body jerked convulsively, and the creature, having plopped down on its back, began to rapidly dive. Another burst from the shore clicked on the scales of the gray-yellow belly, but these bullets did not frighten the snake. It continued to dive, sticking its belly out above the water. It seemed to Andrey that a huge wheel was spinning a few meters away in the depths, part of the tire of which protruded above the surface of the water. Finally, the snake effectively slapped the water with its flat tail, and this blow almost took off Andrey's head. It was good that a moment before this he had shaken off the stupor caused by the mesmerizing spectacle of the giant reptile diving, and made a couple of strokes back to the shore. The creature had barely disappeared into the depths when shots began to blare from the shore again. Andrey turned around sharply and saw that the cover was now firing at the snake that was attacking Brazhnikov. The distance was twice as great, and this creature had not exposed its vulnerable spots to bullets as recklessly as the first bastard, so the major had much less of a chance. Brazhnikov's pistol was more powerful, the "Gyurza" was popular in the special forces unit, but in the confrontation that unfolded, the "snake" in Brazhnikov's hands was clearly losing to a real snake. The huge monster froze for a second, waiting for all the bullets to hit its scaly face, then opened its mouth and with an elusive, fast movement rushed at the major. Andrey even thought he heard the crunch of breaking bones, but the Old Man couldn't swear to it. All sounds were drowned out by Tatyana's desperate scream, floundering ten meters from the scene of the fight. Lunev put the knives back in place, gathered his last strength and swam toward the center of the lake again, but then loud splashes were heard from the left, and Olga's new cry came from behind: - Old man, come back! Scout will help! Andrey stretched his neck and made sure that Olga was right. Scout swam much faster. He very soon reached Tatyana, skillfully, like a real lifeguard, grabbed her from behind and pulled her to the shore. Lunev turned his gaze to the place where the major had been fighting seconds earlier. Bubbles were bursting on the surface of the water and a red spot was slowly spreading. What this meant was clear as day. Andrey gritted his teeth and swam to the shore. Olga helped him get out onto the slippery grass, his own strength was not enough. Almost simultaneously, Scout and Tatyana crawled out onto the shore. Without saying a word, without even exchanging gestures, both, barely alive from the cold and overexertion, couples hobbled into the forest and met in a tiny clearing about twenty meters from the lake. The old man fell face down and lay there for ten long minutes. During this time, Scout managed to somehow wring out his clothes, wipe down his machine gun and take his place as a sentry, while Olga went ashore and collected her weapons and equipment. Tatyana sat next to Andrey the whole time. He didn't see her, but he heard and felt her. - And you say... there are no water spirits, and snakes... don't live here, - Scout said, catching his breath, not looking at Tatyana, but clearly in her direction. - And what kind of ones do live here. I've never seen such teeth in my life. Tatyana did not answer because she could not. She was shaking violently, and her jaws were clenched as if in a spasm. The lady sat, hugging her knees pressed to her chest, and was barely audible in howling from the horror she had experienced. Olga brought her equipment and tried to help Tatyana get dressed, but for now it was impossible. Tatyana could not unclasp her hands, it was beyond her moral strength. Andrey, on the contrary, finally found the strength to sit down. He was also terribly cold, he could barely move his lips, but he still managed to answer: - In Pripyat... the water spirits looked like newts, but here... - To the vipers, I told you. - Scout sighed sadly. - Those fucking Nessies... they ate such a man! May they choke on themselves, the filthy creatures! May this whole Zone choke on itself! “We’d better keep quiet.” Andrey ran his hand over his face with effort, as if wiping away the remaining water. - I can't keep silent! Well, tell me, Old Man, why is there such injustice? Why are you and I still alive, but the major is no more? How are we any better than him?! - Scout! - Andrey seemed to raise his voice with the last of his strength. - This is the Zone! If you want to cry, go home and watch TV series. The major knew that every minute here could be your last! And that every action in the Zone has only two consequences - you survived or you died. He knew that too! Do you think I don’t feel sorry for him? We’ve been through such scrapes together, you couldn’t even dream of it with a hangover! But now unnecessary thoughts and torments will only demoralize us! I’m sure that Brazhnikov would say exactly the same thing in my place. So I ask you, as a human being, to shut up! Scout looked sullenly at Lunev, shook his head disapprovingly, but – just a miracle! – did not say a word in response. To prevent him from changing his mind and starting again with his correct, but absolutely inappropriate in the current situation, reasoning, Olga entered the conversation. Unlike Scout, she spoke briefly and to the point: “A geyser...” Olga said hoarsely, sitting down next to Tatyana. “There definitely haven’t been any geysers in the Zone yet.” “It’s not a geyser – the water is cold,” the Old Man objected. “And anyway... water doesn’t rise in such a column under normal conditions. It’s as if it was pushed by a piston from below. Or by something else.” - Do you think it's telekinesis? - Most likely. And the bastard who organized this trick is still somewhere nearby. - Then we need to get out of here! - Scout became worried again. - If he was able to swing and throw up five hundred tons of water, then throwing pine trees at us would be nothing at all. "No, he left," Olga objected to both her companions at once. "I don't feel anyone. Such experiments probably take a lot of energy." "All the more reason to hurry," Scout said. "While he hasn't had a rest. Maybe we can break away." - He'll find it anyway. - Olga looked sympathetically at Tatyana. - And we're not ready to go right now. Let us at least dry off and rest a little. It's all good for you, you swam there and back, stretched your legs, and that's all. And how long they floundered! - As if you were overworked! - Scout started to get worked up again. - And why is it "he'll find us anyway"? Are you going to tell him where to look for us? - You're getting on my nerves. - Olga waved her hand wearily. - You're a paranoid lunatic. - No, you answer! - Scout! - Andrey said sternly. - The topic is closed once and for all, understand? If you mention traitors in the group again, I'll unscrew your head! "I'll get to the bottom of it even with my head unscrewed," Scout muttered. "Olga still hasn't answered! You can unscrew it. Here, please!" He leaned towards Andrey and stretched his neck. - Sit down and rest, - the Old One ordered. - The enemy will find us anyway, because we cannot turn off the path and get lost in the forest. Is that so hard to understand? And if they don't find us on the path, they will wait at the entrance to the hidden part of the sector. As for the informant, it could be someone at the base. - There's probably no one left at the base! The major, of blessed memory, said that it was a bloodbath there, just like Stalingrad. - That's another question. And anyway, we have no choice. Whether strangers are watching us or our own betray us - it doesn't really matter. We'll go further and get there, no matter what it takes. - Just don't make fanatics out of us, okay?! - Scout stood up resolutely. - I won't go into hell voluntarily! - Maybe not into hell, but into someone's mouth, - Olga grinned. - Or are you prophesying? We've passed water, copper pipes, we've passed in the form of bombardment with tractor parts, now fire, right? - Am I prophesying? - Scout stared at Olga in surprise. - In what sense? I just said so. It's a figure of speech. - Well, yes, and you started talking about the snakes in Cherevach just like that, and before that you were afraid that soon we would be covered with a copper basin. Will you say so, or not? What was that, a prophecy or were you giving the enemy hints? The scout was literally speechless from Olga's accusations. He stared indignantly at the girl and silently opened his mouth. - What we fought for, we got, - said the Old Man with a grin. - One - one. That's it, we've finished our rest stop. We've finished throwing mud at each other, too. Rise and march. Olga, help Tatyana. Scout - bring up the rear. We're heading northwest, through the eastern outskirts of Korogod and on to Chistogalovka. Follow me!.

...The mossy stone pier in ruined Pripyat on the shore of the cooling pond was slippery and for the first time in several years seemed uncomfortable to the Engineer. And the surface of the water, covered with rain pockmarks, could not inspire him with peace today. And the gloomy autumn sky, which was oozing with eternal rain, was generally irritating. The only thing the Engineer did not care about was the dank dampness that soaked his clothes and chilled his body, it seemed, to the bones. A black fire raged in his soul, on which fierce hatred boiled in a cast-iron cauldron of depression. Such a powerful internal heating easily coped with any dampness. The Engineer hated everyone and everything, but first of all the people who were guilty of the collapse of such a carefully thought-out and well-organized operation. The Engineer cared little about material losses. Sitting on a pile of gold and gems, it was stupid to think about such things. The scattering of artifacts, which the hidden sector had always been rich in, could be considered not even a pile, but a mountain of jewelry. And yet, neither unlimited funding, nor the enthusiasm of psionics and people who wanted to be like them helped the Engineer and the company to achieve success. The intensive path of expanding the Zone was no longer suitable. And all because of a bunch of people who decided that they were the coolest in the Zone! The engineer stared at the man lying at his feet. This terrified scientist had accidentally survived the flooding of the main laboratory, where people, bribed with money and impressive promises of psionics, tried in vain to transform themselves into the likeness of their masters. Yes, things weren't going great in the lab. What Flash had created in a moment, scientists had to "graft" for a long time and with difficulty. But some things were working out. Another year or two of experiments - and a new kind of people, hidden mutant telepaths, could have spread widely across the world. What benefit would the Engineer and the psionics get from this? Direct. They could gain access to all the secrets of the world, to any technical and military innovations, they would have squeezed the outside world in their fist! And then... everything is simple. All the potential, all the knowledge accumulated by the world would have been thrown into solving the main problem - expanding the Zone. "Now this problem will be solved by the military! Only for them it has the opposite sign! They want to prevent the expansion of the Zone. They are trying with all their might to establish complete control over it. Actually, that is why they reacted to the appearance of the new anomaly quickly and clearly. They threw a smart group into the center of the Zone, helped them get to the Call generator and destroyed it along with our assistants from among the future mutant people. Damn people!" The Engineer squatted down, took the trembling scientist by the chin and pulled him slightly towards himself. Large drops of cold sweat on his forehead, trembling lips and bulging eyes did not give the man any respectability. Just yesterday, the Engineer considered this man one of the best minds among all the workers in the network of underground laboratories. One could say that he respected him, as much as it is possible to respect a person. But now he saw in this trembling creature only a piece of fresh meat. However, the Engineer was in no hurry to kill him. Dinner could wait, business first. He had not yet learned the main thing: who had figured out Kazakevich and his assistants? Who had sent the scouts and special forces here? And, first of all, who had guessed that the Call was an artificial anomaly? In addition to pure curiosity, the Engineer was driven by another consideration. If this unknown enemy was so smart, sooner or later he could understand the main thing - that there was someone more powerful behind the conspirators. This was not to the Engineer's advantage. Especially now, when he had to essentially start everything from scratch. “Go on,” the Engineer croaked, calming his anger a little, and let go of the man’s chin. - That mercenary pulled a machine gun on me! - the scientist whined. - I've known that guy for a long time, he would have shot without thinking! I had to give him the computer that controlled the Call generator. And then he left... to get his friends out of the cages, and we rushed to the tunnel, which was quickly filling with water, but the cart was still working. We got out at the very last second. The tunnel was flooded to the brim as soon as the cart returned to the shore laboratory. - The people have the computer, - the Engineer summed up. - At the MIS headquarters. This mercenary also managed to get out in time. Clever bastard. Who is he? Who hired him? - His nickname is Old, and who he worked for... I don't know! Nobody knows the employers. Contracts are concluded through intermediaries... “Think,” the Engineer interrupted his lamentations. “The computer fell into the hands of the military. Through whom?” - I don't know... but actually... counterintelligence! Through counterintelligence agents! They carried out the operation, they also brought in mercenaries, that happens, that's how they insure themselves! General Ostapenko - that's who you need! He's the head of counterintelligence MIS, he carries out all such operations. A very cunning man, very! “Shut up.” The engineer made a barely noticeable gesture with his hand, and the scientist cut off his fiery speech mid-sentence. The screeching intonations in the scientist's voice irritated the psionicist's fine hearing, and the smell of fear - sour sweat and urine - that wafted from the man was repulsive. The engineer rose from his haunches and stepped aside. To the very edge of the pier. He stood at the edge of the mossy parapet and stared at the dark surface of the water. Whenever the Engineer had a little free time, he always came to this pier. He felt calm here and thought best. In addition, it was safe here. In case of the appearance of superior enemy forces, the Engineer always had a place to hide. First of all, under water. Somewhere there, in the depths, there was an emergency exit from the underwater laboratory, now completely flooded, and a little to the left was a bottom pit in which a giant catfish was fattening itself, until recently faithfully performing the functions of Cerberus. "Until recently. It's unlikely that it has come, "the last time". But today, absolutely nothing makes me happy or calms me down! And what's worst of all is that I absolutely don't want to plan anything even a day ahead. As if this hour really were the last! Damned little people! They will pay me dearly for everything! First of all, for daring to take away so many of my nerve cells!" A huge black shadow moved in the depths of the pond. The Engineer fixed his gaze on the shadow and mentally slapped the fish on the back. The catfish jerked from the impulse that pierced its body, similar to a strong electric discharge, but did not sink to the bottom of the saving pit. It seemed to admit that it was guilty and meekly accepted the punishment. The creature even moved its fins, like a person who guiltily spreads his hands. The Engineer was satisfied with the catfish's reaction, and he limited himself to only one blow. Especially since this blow helped him splash out the accumulated tension and finally curb the anger that was interfering with his mind. Three rather simple but logical conclusions instantly arose in the "cooled" mind. First: nothing is lost until people guess who is really opposing them. Second: if it was not possible to make people allies, then it was necessary to call upon the Zone itself as an ally or something not so inert, but capable of doing the same job. And third: one cannot discount the main value of psionics - the "Key". The Engineer had not yet been able to establish contact with it, but the owner of the artifact felt that he was on the right track. "It's only just beginning, General Ostapenko," the Engineer thought, having regained his composure. "You'll be surprised at how deep the hole is that you've so recklessly jumped into. A real abyss. And there will be no return from it, as from any decent abyss, for you." The Engineer turned around and met the Ghost's gaze. The Master had not yet formulated the thought, but the assistant had already grasped the meaning and carried out the silent order without hesitation. He approached the scientist, who was shaking like an aspen leaf, with one swift movement snapped the man's neck and pushed the body into the pond. To the delight of the giant catfish. The three female psionicists who made up the Engineer's personal guard perceived the fish bait as a very wasteful gesture, but quickly coped with their emotions and drove the regret about the ruined dinner from their thoughts. They were hungry, but the fear of the Engineer was stronger than hunger. After all, there were many other people wandering around the Zone, whose meat was not so saturated with the sour taste of fear... .

...All this happened quite recently. Only at the end of October. But now it seemed like a century ago - so many events had fit in between that autumn evening and today. Different events; both bad and good, but most importantly, not fatal. Balancing on the fine line between victories and defeats, the Engineer had finally reached the final destination and now had an excellent chance to put a bold point in the matter. And thereby erase from memory all the unpleasant memories of the collapse of the first major operation of the psionics. And the second one too. The heavy memories seemed to spur the Engineer's creative thought, and suddenly it dawned on him. He understood what he had to do to break the peculiar limiter that prevented the "Key" from using all the energy accumulated in it to create the maximum number of transitions. For the first time in many years, something resembling emotion stirred in the Engineer's soul. The feeling was contradictory, partly it resembled a long-forgotten feeling of joy, but the other part of it resembled fear. Joy because the stubborn artifact was beginning to reveal its main secret, and fear that this could be just an illusion. The engineer forced himself to tear his gaze away from the crystal and raised his eyes to the ceiling. The glass of light was slowly turning into a flask. The milky white glow, which had not yet reached above the walls, was reluctantly creeping along the vault toward the gaping hole in its center. It was definitely a victory! The Engineer had forced the "Key" to open access to a new energy level. First of all, this meant that the Engineer would be able to create at least two dozen more transitions to the outside world. "And then double this number of exit points! And a little later, make it so that the transitions do not close during the Emission, so that they are always open. And then the long-awaited moment of truth will come!" The engineer rejoiced inwardly. Yes, he rejoiced, now he knew exactly what was going on in his lost soul. Fear and timid joy were gone, giving way to victorious euphoria. “There would be enough creatures for all the transitions!” the Engineer grinned. “And there will be enough. As soon as the transitions become stable, the anomalous radiation of the Flash – the analogue of the Emission in the Thirteenth Sector – will once again begin to reshape the outside world, and along with it, its inhabitants. The notorious Emissions, these pathetic likenesses of Flashes, will turn into what they are supposed to be, their energy will spread far and wide across the world and form new Zones around the transition points. In which new creatures and their masters will be born. Millions and millions of new mutants and psionics.
