This time everything will turn out exactly like this!” “See how cleverly you do it,” a mental voice from the outside world interrupted the Engineer’s thoughts. “You should have taken on the task yourself right away, not been lazy.” “I came to this result by trial and error,” the Engineer thought, holding back his displeasure so as not to spoil the solemnity of the moment. “When the idea of the Call arose, it seemed quite successful to me.” - Yeah, but you chose losers as your assistants. It happens. Although betting on people twice is no longer a random mistake, but a natural stupidity. - You are mistaken. The idea of using the "Black Angel" anomaly to expand the Zone was also correct. People played a supporting role here. If it weren't for them, the psionics would have handled the task. Not as quickly, but... - Haste makes waste, - the irony in the interlocutor's thoughts was obvious. - Well, that's it. - Now everything will be different. - Really? Do you know where I am now? Halfway to Korogod. I'm stomping through the forest, like through a Maidan. At a leisurely pace. Do you know why? Because of all the great and terrible psionics you sent to meet the group, only one survived. And how many were there initially? Five? - Your group is a minor nuisance, nothing more. The fate of the world is being decided here, in my castle. Neither the Old One nor his companions will change anything. - Well, well. - There was distrust in his voice. - And yet I advise you to think of something else like this and entrust its execution to more competent assistants. Or even better - to deal with the matter personally. You do everything so well. - You impudent creature! - the Engineer flared up. - I will decide for myself what to do! And if you dare to give me stupid advice even once more, I will order the psionics to destroy you and the group! - Oh, I'm afraid. Let your psionics practice on cats first. The Old One's group is too tough for your beasts. Not tough. “By the way, the score is four-two,” the Ghost, who had appeared unexpectedly as always, intervened in the tense mental dialogue. “We’re losing, I don’t argue, but not completely.” - Four for sure, and about two - you most likely exaggerated. - The mental voice from the mainland still had a hint of irony. - The Major is no more, a fact, as he liked to say. But about the Mechanic... the question is open. "And why do you think so?" asked the Ghost, parodying the ironic intonations of his interlocutor. "Because you are the Mechanic?" - Tell you everything! Military secret! - Then you are one of the women. - Wow, how did you guess?! - the mental voice was filled with frank joy. - Or maybe I'm a stalker or the Old One himself? - No, the stalker is too weak to be a hidden psionic, and the Old One... he smells of death. He is a man, and he is dangerous. Someone who has been blessed by the Zone with its grace, even a tiny part of it, cannot be so dangerous. So who are you? - The engineer will tell you. If he wants. “It’s not time yet,” said the Engineer. - Really? - the spy's mental voice dripped with sarcasm. - Or maybe the whole point is that you yourself don't really know who I am? And this is after six months of knowing each other! "I know who you are." The engineer tried to think calmly again. "You are an impudent, underdeveloped creature who tries to put himself on the same level as us." - A creature? - The spy seemed satisfied. - You don't even know what gender I am, Engineer. So what is your vaunted telepathy worth? - Shut up, you creature! - The engineer mentally crushed his interlocutor. The spy fluttered a bit, but fell silent. The vaunted telepathy was worth something after all. At least as a means of suppressing the will of rebellious humans, mutants and half-breeds like that. - Ghost, you will personally deal with this group. - The engineer again switched to his assistant. - Take with you as many psionics as you need, and go. - Four brothers and three sisters remained in the castle. All the rest went to the outside world to command the invasion groups. - Then take all the remaining brothers. What you said about the Old One is true. I feel the same, which means it is true. One psionic can be wrong, two cannot. If we both sense trouble and clearly identify its source, then it is not fiction, but a signal from the foggy future. - Everything you do is based on mysticism. - The spy was no longer thinking so boldly, but still allowed himself a distrustful grin. - I will still risk giving you one more piece of advice, don't be offended. Put your psi-abilities somewhere for a while and take up machine guns. The result will be a hundred times better. I tell you for sure. - We'll take that into account. Have you managed to find out what the walkers are bringing to the sector? - Only personal firearms. - Guarantee? No bombs? - Only if one of them is a bomb. But I don't think people have learned to dilute blood with TNT. "The word 'bomb' also has a figurative meaning," Ghost noted. "Someone in the group may have dangerous knowledge or skills." - Then it is the Old One, - the Engineer concluded confidently. - Concentrate your attention on this mercenary, Ghost. And I ask you both: do not distract me with trifles in the near future. Very soon everything will be ready for the main attack. I need to concentrate. The engineer severed the mental connection with the spy, but left his mind open to the Ghost. The assistant knew that the master would do exactly that. And it was not the ability of experienced psionics to see the foggy future. The Ghost simply did not have time to report to the master about the state of other affairs, and the latter, in turn, clearly wanted to give some other instructions regarding the Old One's group. “You first,” the Engineer ordered. - We have occupied the borderland in ten sectors. So far, two bases are resisting. We will destroy one of them no later than in two hours. The last one is more difficult. - D-3? - Yes. The military has barricaded itself in the warehouses and is successfully repelling all our attacks. - Hasn't headquarters ordered them to retreat yet? - Our brothers behind enemy lines are doing everything they can to instill fear and awe in the people at MIS headquarters before our army, but they cannot put concrete thoughts into their heads. As long as the D-3 base resists, the headquarters will have doubts. And hope. The brothers are afraid that the people may throw heavy equipment into the battle. If this happens, a breakthrough to D-3 could be the beginning of a successful enemy counteroffensive. - That won't happen, - the Engineer retorted confidently. - I know human psychology well. They are demoralized and would rather bomb the base than risk breaking through to it. It is easier and safer for them. And they can justify their weakness by their unwillingness to suffer losses. The base garrison versus the breakthrough group - the arithmetic will be on the side of the headquarters. – The bombing could cause significant damage to our army in sector D-3. - There will be no bombing. When people mature to such a decision, they will have new combat missions. More important than clearing the Borderland of our creatures. - Did you manage to stir the crystal? - Yes, Ghost, it worked. In a few hours he'll be ready to open another dozen passages, and then... - Understand. - That's great. Go. "You wanted to clarify the task," the Ghost reminded. "I felt it." - Oh, right. I wanted to make it easy for you. Don't try to figure out which of the walkers is our agent. Kill them all. It'll be better that way..

The settlement of Korogod, abandoned by its residents after the first catastrophe, was famous for the fact that it was still impossible to approach it unnoticed. Almost all of the abandoned settlements of the Zone were drowned in dense thickets of bushes or young trees; of some, for example, Zapolye or Ilovnitsa, not a trace remained, but Korogod still stood, surrounded by swamps, and was clearly visible from any direction. In comparison with the views of thirty years ago, the landscape around the village had nevertheless changed: the forest had reclaimed about a third of the territory from the endless fields, and large islands among the swamps had turned into areas of impenetrable jungle, so much had the bushes grown on them, but in general everything remained the same as before. It was easy to keep heading for the ruins of the settlement, even when there were still several kilometers left to the goal. When the group finally got out of the forest and made a short stop at the edge of the forest, Olga was the most attentive to studying the further route. She went a little to the right, stood at the edge of the swamp spread out in front of the group, studied the distant ruins through the optical sight and returned to her comrades. She did not report the results of her observations to anyone. Nobody asked her. Andrey was sitting by the tree, his eyes closed, and Scout and Tatyana were lying flat out, and all they were interested in now was rest and a can of energy drink. After the start at Lake Krinka, Andrey allowed only two short breaks and categorically forbade the use of any stimulating drinks or pills. The exhausted four were content with water and biscuits from the dry rations. No amount of begging or moaning had any effect on Lunev. The only thing he did to ease the lot of his comrades was to promise that at the next break they would be allowed to open one of the three cans of energy drink each hiker had stocked up with. All the walkers, without exception, understood perfectly well that Lunev was not being savage, but was acting purely rationally. It was not that Andrei foresaw a lot of difficulties ahead and was saving the energy drink for emergencies - no, the Old Man was not acting on fantasies, but on a precise calculation of the journey's duration. The goal was two to six hours away, depending on the situation. The energy drink, the recipe for which had made some Borderland scientists rich, was a special drink, not at all like its analogues on the mainland. After taking it, it began to act in about fifteen minutes, but only after four hours did the effect reach its peak, where it remained for about four more hours, and then slowly began to decline. That is, it made sense to "cheer up," as Scout insistently demanded, only after reaching the outskirts of Korogod. Which, in fact, is what happened now. Lunev was the first to open the can and drain it without stopping, in one gulp. No additional commands were needed. The others followed the leader's example, and drained their cans in one go. - We'll get to the target, drink a second one, - Andrey said. - The effect will be stronger and last longer. It should last almost a day. If we don't get out in that time, we'll drink a third one, there'll be another half day in reserve. “And the consequences?” Tatyana asked apprehensively. - The usual. - Andrey shrugged. - When it lets go - weakness, nervous exhaustion and irresistible drowsiness. A day of sound sleep, and everything will pass. “What a nightmare,” Tatyana looked warily at the jar with the tiger drawn on it. - There are no stimulants without consequences. No, if you don't abuse them and limit yourself to one can a day, there won't be any side effects at all. But that doesn't always work out in a raid. “It never works out that way,” Scout clarified. “And sometimes you have to suck out ten jars a day. Some of them are then locked up in the hospital for a week. In the psychiatric ward.” - But this is... not normal! You can't treat your health like this! - Health, madam, is the condition of a living organism, - Scout said, puffing himself up. - A dead person is unhealthy by definition. One doctor told me so when I woke up in a mental hospital after a week-long raid. Sometimes it is better to slightly spoil your health than to lose it completely. Along with your life. Do you agree? – It’s better not to go on such long raids! - Well, then it would be better not to go to the Zone, but to work as an accountant in Konotop. “Why there?” Olga suddenly asked, holding back her laughter. - Why not there? - Scout's eyes sparkled with joy. - Nice town. Peace, quiet, museums, a NATO military base, where you can exchange chewing gum for milk. - Did you drink an energy drink or something stronger? - Andrey asked, shaking off his drowsiness. - You're awake, I see? - Five more minutes, Commander! - Scout pretended to massage his legs. - The stilts are overheated, let them cool down. No one is catching up with us. Olya, am I right? - I don't feel anyone. - Here you go! “I don’t mind, rest.” Lunev stood up and slowly walked down to the swamp. The trail led straight through the swamp, but there was still room to maneuver. Andrey immediately noticed a path that ran to the right of the camp site and led to the nearest bush island. Lunev remembered well that on that side of the island the swamp was hummocky, but without surprises, and only about two hundred meters wide. Then a gentle climb began all the way to the outskirts of the village. In fact, only the first section of the path, to the island, was dangerous. - We should go to the left. - Olga stood next to him. - Over there. Do you see the hut in the distance at the edge of the forest? - A barn? An old chemical warehouse? - Yes, chemical fertilizers. There is a dry path right opposite it. - But there is no path into the sector. - Maybe not, maybe there is. - Olga shrugged. - The mechanic said that the trail widens again here. Up to one and a half kilometers. It's exactly eight hundred meters to the warehouse. If we're in the middle of the trail now, it's fine. - You can get through here too. - Andrey pointed to the high humps, like camel humps, to the right of the position. - Here. The main thing is not to slip and not break your legs. - Legs are fine, there are leeches here... a sea. And they rush at you like piranhas, in a flash, in a whole shoal. They'll stick around, you'll get tired of trying to tear them off. - You too, - Andrey grinned. - To make up stories. - Seriously, I saw it myself. “Like all the blood was sucked out of a person,” Andrey mockingly encouraged his companion. - Not all of it, but a lot. But that wasn't what killed him. About three hours later he cut himself while opening a can of stew at a rest stop - his hands were shaking from weakness, and the rest of his blood flowed out. Those leeches injected him with some kind of crap, and the bleeding wouldn't stop at all. We bandaged him, and used a tourniquet, and cauterized him... all to no avail. It turned out that the blood wasn't staying in his vessels, it was slowly leaking out. Into his stomach, and into his lungs... - Yeah, it's not funny. - Andrey chuckled. - All the more reason not to fall off the hummocks. We need to cut down poles for support. Take Scout, get busy. - Fine. Olga returned to the camp site, but Andrey did not remain alone for long. Tatyana approached him. She was silent for a while, then sighed and pressed herself against Andrey's shoulder. - So far I'm more trouble than I'm worth, right? “Bye.” Lunev hugged her shoulders. “Thank you for encouraging me,” she said quietly. - And you wanted me to start persuading you? - Andrey moved away a little and tried to look into Tatyana's eyes. “No.” She didn’t want to meet Lunev’s gaze. - Do you blame yourself for Brazhnikov’s death? - What do you think? - Tatyana sighed again. - I think so, and I think it’s in vain. - Are you saying this is fate? - No. It is a war. Local, secret, but as difficult and cruel as any other. “War,” Tatyana repeated thoughtfully. “For what? For what ideals or territories? For this swamp and other sectors of the Zone? Who needs them?” - War is always fought not for something, but with some goal. In ninety percent of cases - for the purpose of enrichment. Everything that happens in the Zone is no exception. People here fight for control over the territories where artifacts are produced. That is, they earn money. - And not people? After all, telepaths, controllers, as you call them, are trying to stop us. What is their interest? - In the same thing. - I don't think they want to make money from trading artifacts. It seems to me that their motives are close to the motivation of any resistance. The Zone is their home, and they are trying to protect it from outsiders, from us. “It’s not that simple,” Andrey shook his head. “I’m sure eight out of ten mutants have no motivation at all. They are driven by instincts and hunger. But the remaining twenty percent, those who have retained the remnants of reason, are not simply resisting our invasion of the Zone. They are preparing a counterattack. What is happening now at the base is convincing proof of that. I don’t know whether this is the beginning of a counteroffensive or reconnaissance in force, but the inhabitants of the Zone do not intend to limit themselves to driving strangers out of their home. They want to go beyond its borders and expand their habitat. - And you think that the headquarters of this army is in the Thirteenth Sector? - I don't know. But the general most likely thinks so. And so far we have only confirmation of his rightness, not a single refutation. The base was attacked from the Thirteenth, there is no doubt that quite reasonable, but still mutants, are trying to detain us, plus the current story has a rich prehistory. If you look at it from the height of the current situation, everything fits together without significant gaps. The facts form a clear picture. Only the last large fragment is missing. Ostapenko believes that we will find him in the Thirteenth sector. And the closer we get to the target, the more I agree with the general. - Let's say everything is as you say, but... - Tatyana moved away a little: - There are only four of us, and even then... you could say three! If the sector is the headquarters and main base of these... intelligent mutants, what can we do? - We'll see. - Andrey shrugged. - I know one thing for sure: Ostapenko never sends people on absolutely hopeless missions. I'm sure we have at least one chance in a thousand. What kind of chance this is, what the general is counting on, I don't know. But this old KGB man definitely knows something that will help us cope with the mission. - What? Know what exactly? - I don’t know, I told you! - And if you don’t find out? - Then we can't cope. - My head is spinning from such inconsistency! - Tatyana was indignant. - Why didn't Ostapenko tell you right away, at least to you, what the final goal of the mission was?! - Most likely, so that no one could squeeze it out of me. - Andrey smiled. - Or fish it out of my memory using telepathy. Remember yourself three months ago. Before sending you to the Zone, Ostapenko pumped you full of chemicals not at all to smooth out the stress that all newcomers experience. He was afraid that telepaths would find out about your spy mission. “I never once thought about the spy mission,” Tatyana admitted. “Until I returned to the base, I thought that I was acting correctly and of my own free will.” - This is what Ostapenko was trying to achieve. So that you would get into the role as much as possible and not even in your sleep remember your close connection with counterintelligence. In my case, chemistry is not required. Ostapenko is confident that I will draw the right conclusions, even without knowing the purpose of our mission. - I understand. - Tatyana finally tore her thoughtful gaze away from the swamp and turned it to the ruins of a village in the distance. - Another dead village in hell. I don't understand how you can love these terrible places. Why are you all drawn to the Zone like a magnet? “I’ll answer when we get back,” Andrey promised. “If you haven’t found the answer yourself by then.” Laughter and stomping could be heard from behind. Lunev turned around and looked at the approaching couple in surprise. It was surprising, but it seemed that there was no trace left of the feud between Scout and Olga. The Old Man could not even imagine the reason for such a sharp change in mood, especially in Olga. It was not the energy drink that had affected her so much. "Maybe she just relaxed? The controllers are far away, there are no other creatures nearby either... Or maybe that's not the issue. The fewer of us there are, the more she understands how petty her whims and nagging are. Anything is possible." The staffs cut by Scout exuded the aroma of pine resin, which, together with the invigorating effect of the energy drink and the emerging improvement in the moral climate in the group, raised the mood and instilled confidence even in the almost depressed Tatyana. When they managed to pass the dangerous section without any problems, where the slush between the slippery hummocks was really teeming with countless leeches, the mood rose to a level bordering on euphoria. For the first time during the entire raid, none of the walkers were injured, did not even stumble on the slippery springy hummocks, and did not quarrel with anyone, which was, in fact, even more important. Once on the island, the walkers dropped their sticks and picked up their knives. The bushes here were not tall, just above waist height, but thick and tenacious. As Scout put it, it would be easier to roll over them than to walk through them. "It's only like that on the edge, it becomes more sparse further away," Olga promised. "And in the center of the island it's not there at all. The ground there is soaked with some kind of crap, nothing grows." “Rubbish?” Scout asked warily. “Anomalous?” - I don't know, kind of white. As if salt had come out. The background seems to be low. The detectors are silent too. - White stuff? - Scout grinned slyly. - Okay, let's smell it. He pretended to pour something on his wrist and sniff it. The pantomime was funny, but for some reason Scout messed up the ending of the sketch. The artistic mask suddenly slipped from his face, giving way to an expression of concern. He lowered his hand and sniffed the air, then sniffed his hand again, and then the air again. - I don't get it... - Scout looked around restlessly. - From somewhere... it's coming... He sniffed again. - What smells like that? - Olga sniffed the air too. - Nothing special... except maybe a swamp. - What, no one feels it? - Scout stepped towards Olga and sniffed again. - Hey, take it easy. - She grinned. - We're not dogs to sniff! - I'm serious. - Scout looked at Lunev. - Old man, you don't feel it either? It smells like smoke. - What kind? - How, how... smoky. - Stalker turned his head again. - It seems like nothing is burning. - Maybe someone is messing with your brain again? - Olga suggested. - Take off your glove, squeeze the "Joker" in your fist, it will help. - No one sat down for me! - The scout pointed to the eastern end of the island: - There's smoke! The bushes are burning! “There too,” said Tatyana, pointing to the west. “And there.” She waved in a southerly direction. - Where is what burning? - Olga turned her head in surprise. - What are you talking about? “You don’t see?” Scout asked in surprise. - No. - Oh, well that's because you're a meter tall in heels. Let me lift you up. - Yeah, right! Just to touch it, I'm ready to come up with anything. “It really is burning there,” said Tatyana. “Okay, I believe you,” Olga said condescendingly. "We need to get moving," Andrey pushed both of his companions forward. "Before the north flares up, too." - The wind is coming from there, it shouldn't, - Scout objected. - No, who set it on fire? We're non-smokers, we didn't throw cigarette butts, and sparks didn't fly out of our eyes. It wasn't the leeches that started it! “Not leeches.” Andrey looked around, trying to spot someone in the place where the walkers had recently been resting, but he saw no one. Continuing to move north, Lunev almost knocked down Olga, who suddenly stood rooted to the spot. - What's wrong? “We can’t go there!” the girl whispered, pointing her gaze at the ruins of Korogod. - Controllers again? - Yes. And a lot. - And who is with them? - No one. Now they don't lead anyone, but that's even worse. They don't bother to control their charges. They think only about us. We'll feel it soon. - And we'll feel it even faster when our butts get set on fire! - Scout said nervously. - Look, it's blazing! The fire is moving faster than a train! - But the wind... - Andrey looked around anxiously. - On the other... side. "I don't know where the wind is coming from, but we better push harder," Scout pulled his mask down. “What smoke... acrid,” Tatyana said, coughing. “And thick.” Andrey and Olga exchanged glances. In Lunev's opinion, there was no particularly acrid smoke around. The bush burned like well-dried brushwood, almost without smoke. Meanwhile, it, like the entire Zone, was drenched daily from the gray skies for several hours in a row. Theoretically, the bush should have really smoked thickly and acridly. - So, so. - Olga narrowed her eyes and resolutely stepped towards the approaching wall of fire. - It's ablaze, you say? - Don't you see? - That's the point. - Olga smiled. - Where is it burning? There? She pointed in the south direction. - Yes, there. - Is it burning badly? - At full height. “Interesting.” Olga moved toward the southern end of the island, following the group’s old tracks. “Put on the mask anyway,” Andrey said. - I'm not burning, you know. - The salamander has been found. Put on the mask, I said! “Okay, okay.” Olga reluctantly pulled on a filter mask and gloves. About ten meters in front of the wall of fire, she stopped and turned around. - Here? “Yes,” Andrey nodded. Olga spread her arms, as if enjoying the wave of heat coming before the fiery shaft, and froze. The sight was not for the faint of heart. Realizing what was about to happen to Olga, Tatyana quietly gasped and grabbed Lunev's sleeve. - You're crazy! Both of you! Call her off immediately! “Calm down.” Andrey patted his companion on the hand. “She’ll manage.” - What can she handle?! Put out a fire with the power of thought?! What nonsense?! - Notice, I only said that she can handle it. - Olya! - Scout's nerves also gave out. - Enough! No need for heroic deeds! Old man, what is she planning? - You don't understand either? - Andrey grinned. - Then just watch and be amazed. The wall of fire swallowed Olga and moved on, straight at the walkers. Tatyana screamed again and backed away. The scout stayed next to Andrey, but became even more nervous than before and tensed up, ready to run as soon as the commander gave the signal. Lunev stood calmly, even relaxed, as if he was looking not at the menacing elements, but only at their image on the big screen. The fiery shaft advanced a few meters more, but suddenly slowed down and froze in place, not quite reaching Andrey and Scout. What's interesting is that when the fire stopped, the heat emanating from it miraculously disappeared. There's no need to even mention the smoke, or even the hint of a burning smell. They had vanished even earlier, when Olga entered the fiery wall. “Damn,” Scout muttered, pulling off his mask. “Bullshit?” “An illusion.” Andrey walked up to the blazing wall and stuck his hand into the very heat. About five seconds later he took two steps back, simultaneously leading Olga out of the flames. - Bow, curtsey, - Scout asked, clapping his hands several times. - The Soviet circus can do wonders. I just don't get it, is this a mass hallucination or something else? “How can you stand here, the heat is unbearable!” Tatyana said, approaching the trio. “And there’s nothing to breathe.” She shielded herself from the wall of fire with her palm and pressed her mask to her face. - There you have your answer, - Olga said. - Each of us is being processed by our own controller. Tatyana got the most talented one... as always. She didn't look at Lunev, but Andrey noticed that it cost her some effort. There was still a certain shade of jealousy in her attitude towards Andrey and Tatyana. In part, it flattered her vanity, but overall it was rather a negative moment. The group had only just begun to forget about petty quarrels. And anyway, now was not the time to sort things out. - And how can I... dehypnotize her? “No way.” Olga shrugged. - What are you talking about, what hypnosis? - Tatyana hid behind Andrey. - Are we going to stand here until we turn into shashlik? - Let's go. - Andrey pointed to the northeast. - Are the controllers there? - They're in Korogod. - Olga nodded her head to the north. - But we won't be able to get around their flank, don't count on it. They'll smell it. - And you? They won't touch you while they're still "figuring it out." - So they don't know yet how many of us are left. They'll press as many as they sense. But there's an open space further on, they'll see me. - We're going there anyway. Let them think we scared them. - Do you hear? - Scout suddenly became alert. A thin, drawn-out whistle reached the ears of the walkers. - Yeah, that's a bummer. - Andrey spread his arms, pushing his comrades back. On the eastern side of the island, a loud splash was heard in the swamp, followed by a dull explosion and numerous slaps of lumps of mud that shot up into the sky and soon fell back into the quagmire. - We missed, - Scout commented. - They fired from an underbarrel grenade launcher. So it turns out that it's not just the controllers who are meeting us? Some shooters are with them. "They themselves are the shooters," Olga objected confidently. "We must go north immediately. It is more difficult to hit an approaching target with a cannon." “But shooting straight at me is a pleasure,” Scout protested. “We can’t shoot forward!” - Do you have a better offer? - asked Andrey. - Everyone forward. At a trot! The second grenade exploded on the island, but still far from the group. The invisible gunners, whoever they were, the controllers or their latest unwitting assistants, were lousy grenade launchers. The third explosion, thundering on the western shore of the island, only confirmed this guess. The group passed the center of the island at top speed - indeed, bare, like a bald spot, and covered with large white crystals reminiscent of salt - and ran, as if into a sea wave, into a new strip of bushes. On the northern end of the island, the bushes were much higher, almost as tall as they were, but not as dense, so the speed of advancement dropped slightly. When a gap appeared ahead, Andrei gave the order to stop and moved on to reconnaissance. It so happened that another overhead shot from an underbarrel grenade launcher slammed at the very moment when Lunev peeked out from the thicket. Andrey easily spotted the point from which they were shooting. The grenade launcher operator was hiding in the ruins on the very outskirts of Korogod, which meant that he was about three hundred meters from Andrey’s position. The distance was ideal for working from the “val” with optics. Let him just stick his head out. But the enemy didn’t seem to be planning on sticking his head out. The bush on the right rustled, and Olga froze next to Andrey. “Do you see them?” she asked in a whisper. - No, and you? - Three are coming down to the left, two are coming in from the right, all with machine guns. Can't you really see? - I don’t see, I told you! "The last thing we needed was night blindness." Olga took aim and fired three shots in a row. The return fire was inaccurate, but one of the short bursts cut off the tops of the bushes quite close to the walkers' position. Andrey backed up and beckoned Olga to follow him. “Did you get it?” he asked when they emerged from the thicket. - No. - Olga shook her head. - Or the bullets don't get them. The left flank one seemed to flinch, but didn't fall. He kept moving as he was walking, didn't even limp. - Sounds familiar, doesn't it? If they have particularly powerful artifacts from the Thirteenth Sector, bullets won't get to them. - Just like us. - But hypnosis "takes" us all. Except you. So it turns out, you are alone against five armed telepaths. - Andrey tried to imitate Olga's intonation: - Do you need it? - Against six. - Olga nodded to the south. - That creature that gave us a bloody shower on the lake will soon join this five, I can feel it. - All the more so. In short, no more amateur performances, got it? - What if you all get squeezed again? - Then act according to the situation. - I am acting. - I'm fine for now. - That's what you think. Listen to me at least once, Old Man, if only out of pure curiosity. “Well, go ahead,” Andrey reluctantly allowed. - They won't let us off this island. - I wonder how they'll manage it? - Lunev smiled indulgently. - Six of us won't be able to control the entire perimeter. - They don't need to control the perimeter. They control us. That is, you. Do you think that after you figured out the trick with the "fire", you let go? Nothing of the sort. The controllers are still sitting on your brain and, rest assured, they are not just sitting there, they are doing a complete disservice. For now, you are relatively adequate, but very soon you will go crazy and stop controlling yourself. Neither the "Joker" nor the amulet will help. - But why did you decide that?! - Because I feel that those three are coming for your soul. And the two who come from the east control Scout and Tatyana. The controllers understand perfectly well that you are the most dangerous, and they act for sure. When the sixth one joins them, I will get it too. - You can't be hypnotized. - I have a heightened resistance to it. But I succumb, like everyone else. Remember how we wandered around Pripyat in the fall, I got it there too. Or today in the labyrinth. The mechanic admitted that it was the first time he wandered around it for a day. I got out in half an hour. But I still got into trouble! So when the sixth one tells his friends that there are four of us, they will redistribute their forces, and the trap will snap shut. - And what do you propose? - Lunev was ready to admit that Olga was right, although he could not find her arguments convincing. It was difficult to believe in his own inadequacy. “I have to leave before I fall under the same pressure as you.” - And who will lead us off the island when... the controllers attack us with the whole gang? - If I succeed, they won't attack. - If! - Andrey smiled sadly. - And if not? What are you planning? - I won't tell! - Are you superstitious or something? Are you afraid of jinxing me? “I’m afraid that the controllers will talk you out of it.” – Did you learn to intrigue from Ostapenko? - Think as you like. Am I going? “God be with you,” Andrey gave in. “How long do we have to wait?” "You'll get there," Olga answered evasively. "In about five minutes, you won't want to leave the island for any amount of money. I bet." - This is for Scout, I'm not a fan of betting. And that was how they parted. And Andrey was convinced that lack of interest in the bet saves money not five minutes later, but only a minute later. The footsteps and rustling of the bare bushes behind Olga had not yet died down, but Lunev already felt how someone else's will was starting to press on his brain. To calm himself, Andrey still squeezed the "Joker" in his fist, and took the faithful amulet in his teeth, but nothing helped. As Olga predicted. How many controllers fell on him at once - three or four - Andrey was no longer interested. The telepaths easily broke through both protective shells and suppressed the will of the man. Lunev had no idea how to get out from under this press. And soon he stopped thinking about it. All he wanted was to sit down in the middle of the salt bald spot in the center of the island in the "lotus" position and immerse himself in meditation. Or just fall asleep. This was also a good option, from the point of view of the new owners of Andrey’s body and mind. Somewhere in the depths of consciousness, the remnants of a meaningful attitude toward oneself and what was happening around still glimmered, but all these grains of self-awareness were capable of was abstract observation and drawing conclusions. They did not contribute to any decisions. Andrey saw how Scout and Tatyana approached him, how they both dropped their weapons, fell to their knees, and then sat down next to the commander, but he couldn’t say anything to them. And they wouldn’t have heard. They were in exactly the same stupor as Lunev. Some time passed - maybe a minute, maybe an hour, Andrey lost track of time - and the bushes to the right began to crackle. It was impossible to turn his head, he had no strength left, and besides, he didn't want to. But with his peripheral vision, Lunev still saw four figures in oversized gray robes. He didn't notice any weapons in their hands, but his hands themselves weren't visible either. Most likely, the "robes" did have machine guns, but they hid the weapons under their clothes. “It’s not about the artifacts,” the adequate part of Lunev’s personality, driven into the basement of his consciousness, realized. “These guys are walking artifacts themselves. But bullets bounce off them for no mystical reason. It’s about the robes. I saw some of those in the very lab where my amulet was made. But back then, they were experimental samples. Here’s more proof that Ostapenko is right. Kazakevich’s labs, the black angels, the Thirteenth Sector – all of these are links in the same chain.” The bushes on the left also began to crackle, but Andrey had no hope of seeing who and in what group approached the bald spot from the eastern direction. All that was left was to orientate himself by the sound. It seemed that two people had approached from there. If they were controllers, it turned out that there was now a full set of them. One thing was strange: all six stopped about twenty meters from the three walkers, as if afraid to move on. "What are they waiting for? They can take us with their bare hands. Or just shoot us like bottles at a shooting range. Even if you're drunk, you won't miss. What are they thinking about? Are they conferring? Are they afraid of a trick?" Andrey never found out what the controllers were afraid of or what they were discussing. In one second the situation changed dramatically, and Lunev had no time for reflection anymore, his reflexes and the combat skills ingrained in his subcortex kicked in. The thought of the bottles at the training ground suddenly materialized, only not quite as Andrey had imagined. Behind the western four enemies, there was the sound of broken glass. Lunev's sense of smell caught the sharp smell of kerosene and some chemicals, and then Andrey was doused with a wave of heat. This time, the heat wave was not imaginary, but real. A second later, two more broken bottles slammed, and almost immediately Lunev let go. It was not easy to come to his senses after such a mental overload, but Andrey did not. He completely relied on reflexes. Three of the four "balakhons" were blazing with blue flames, but apparently did not yet understand how dangerous it was. The fourth was in no hurry to put out his comrades. He turned his back to Andrey and, trying to figure out where the partisan arsonist was hiding, stared at the bushes from which the Molotov cocktails had flown. Lunev, in the controller's place, would have acted more simply - he would have mowed down some of the vegetation with a long burst and only then looked at what was going on there. But the telepath habitually relied primarily on his abnormal sense of smell and kept the barrel of his machine gun pointed at the ground. That is why he did not react when the fourth bottle flew straight into his face. The bottle was flying very fast - the thrower was clearly trained to throw it like that - right at the forehead - and from where, in the controller's opinion, it could not have flown. That is why he did not have time to either dodge or crouch. The sound of broken glass was lost this time in the wild roar of the creature. Trying to put out the flames, the controller threw off his hood and began to slap himself across the cheeks, but in the end he only made things worse. Following his face, his hands caught fire, the flammable liquid flowed into his sleeves and bosom, a moment later the flame burst out from under the cut of his cloak, and the controller blazed like a pioneer fire from the top of his head to his heels. Or rather, the opposite. The attention of the "hot four" finally switched to themselves - the controllers, using the example of the fourth, finally understood what was threatening them if measures were not taken - and Lunev finally let go. Andrei left the creatures alone with their worries and resolutely moved towards the remaining eastern pair of enemies. Andrei ignored the attempt of one of the controllers to attack telepathically. His defense dealt with one telepath easily. Lunev even had a thought that the "Joker" and the amulet were trying to rehabilitate themselves - the psi-blow seemed to pass tangentially, did not even cause dizziness. As if demonstrating resistance to such attacks, Andrei raised his "val" and treated the "hoodie" to a short burst. The bullets, quite expectedly, did not penetrate the controller's special clothing, but discouraged and forced the enemy to take a few steps back. The second telepath didn't wait for the man to give him a couple of lead slaps and quickly dove into the bushes. Andrey took aim at the first one again, but he followed his partner's example and cleared the eastern escape route. Andrey backed up, intending to go back and literally drag his comrades out of the dangerous place by the scruff of the neck, but there was no need for that. Tatyana and Scout were already on their feet and limping toward the commander, looking back every second at the controllers rolling on the ground and setting the bushes on fire. - Run, run, don't slow down! - Andrey waved his hand. - Follow me and don't fall behind! The bushes set ablaze by the combustible mixture crackled, almost drowning out the growling and screaming of the telepaths, and at the same time Lunev's command. And now it really smoked thickly and causticly, for real, without tricks. The wind quickly carried the smoke across the entire island and very soon cut off the walkers from the raging enemy. The group pulled on their masks and moved along the hummocks in the direction of the gentle slope in front of the village. - And Olga? - Tatyana shouted. - Where is Olga? - She'll catch up! - Andrey slowed down, let his friend go ahead and gave her a little push. - Scout, take a step further! - My head... is buzzing, - the stalker admitted. - And it's cold, like in a freezer. What did they do to us? - Nothing special, save your breath, we'll have to walk at this pace for a long time now. - Andrey cast a careful glance at the ruins of Korogod. - And look to the left. The telepaths had machine guns without grenade launchers. They had some in reserve. “Do you think those four burned?” Scout asked again after a minute’s pause for decency’s sake. - I really hope so. I don't know what our pyromaniac lady mixed in the bottles, but the stuff turned out sticky, runny and downright "unquenchable", almost napalm. After such a roast, even if you survive, you'll have a long and difficult recovery. - If a human, and if a mutant? - So what? The point is the same, these mutants aren't aliens. No, if there are any pursuers on our tail, they're the two who didn't get hit by the Molotov cocktails. And one more. The one who remained in the ruins. For some reason, it seems to me that he's the most dangerous of them all. - Why? - Because he didn't run into trouble. A smart move. And a smart enemy is doubly dangerous. Even if he's an absolute zero as a grenade launcher..

After the landing of "batmen" predicted by Bibik was destroyed, a special gleam appeared in the eyes of the defenders of the citadel. It was especially important for the soldiers that stalkers got involved. The forgotten slogan of the Soviet era "The people and the army are united" was not popular in the Zone, on the contrary, in a less difficult situation it would have sounded absurd. But now the stalkers were forced to leave their grievances against the military in the past, and the military, in turn, had to forget about grievances against civilians. The authority of people like Bibik, who had gone through a difficult path from an ordinary stalker, and then a military guide, to a special forces squad commander, but remained an authority for his former civilian comrades, played a significant role in uniting the combined garrison. However, first of all, people were united by the understanding that they were people. Simple, not endowed with any magical talents, but persistent, skillful, and most importantly - born first on the earth, which, by the will of inexplicable reasons, became an anomalous Zone. So, if the defenders of D-3 heard Tatyana Sergeyevna's reasoning that mutants are defending their home, they would probably object: "Initially, this house is ours, we did not invite either the Zone or its spawn into it." It was precisely because of this special enthusiasm that General Ostapenko's order to counterattack the creatures (it was formulated as "Counterattack the creatures") was received with enthusiasm by everyone. The brief preparation for the operation was led by the same Bibik, and therefore everything was thought out thoroughly and calculated down to the minute. The colonel had no shortage of thoroughness. Having carried out a thorough reconnaissance of the area, Bibik found out that the western direction was rather weakly controlled by the mutants, and if the breakthrough group was covered from the north with massive fire, and from the south with a screen of armored vehicles, there would be a chance to organize a narrow corridor through which at least some of the civilians unable to participate in the fighting could be evacuated. There were no children among them, thank God, only a dozen teenagers who had flocked to the D-3 tent camp under the influence of an irresistible craving for adventure, but there were plenty of women and elderly people, mostly scientists from the laboratory sector. When the entire mortar and artillery force of the citadel fell on the northern direction, the controllers commanding the beasts withdrew part of their troops outside the base, but they did not leave the situation unattended. A huge cloud of flying creatures rose into the air again, ready to dive at any moment onto any part of the base and block the path of the rush group. A column of armored vehicles moving out of the southern gate distracted the attention of the "batmen" for some time, but the cunning creatures, realizing that it was pointless to hit the armor, only moved slightly to the south and continued circling at a decent altitude, still controlling the entire area of the base. But when the western and northwestern gates opened, the monsters immediately realized that it was not the distraction group that would emerge from there, but the main one. The entire flock instantly turned around and began to descend over the western sector. Neither the dense fire nor the speed of the column's advance helped the people achieve what they had planned. The vanguard of the breakthrough group, consisting of stalkers and a third of special forces fighters, was instantly cut off from the citadel, surrounded and almost completely destroyed. The people fought desperately, until the last bullet, and when the ammunition ran out - as long as they had enough strength, they worked with knives, shovels and improvised means, but the mutants crushed them with their mass. Only five out of a hundred managed to get out of the cauldron alive: two stalkers and three special forces. Thanks to the competent command of Lieutenant Pashkov, the guys cut their way back to the citadel and literally three seconds before their inevitable death, they found themselves in a dead zone near the walls of the warehouse complex. A barrage of fire threw back the mutants who were catching up with the five and allowed Pashkov and his comrades to return unhindered to where they had started from some quarter of an hour ago. - We just missed it... - Pashkov said, breathing heavily. - If we had broken through to Andreyevka, they would have been unable to stop us. - That's for sure. - Bibik patted the lieutenant on the shoulder encouragingly. - You're a good boy, you worked competently. Rest for half an hour - and then come to me, I'll be at headquarters, under the fifth warehouse. - Yes. - Pashkov exhaled loudly, took off his helmet and wiped the sweat from his forehead. - Do you have any smoking, Colonel? “I don’t smoke and I don’t advise you to either.” Bibik stopped short. - Here. - One of the stalkers handed the lieutenant a pack. - Yeah, thanks. - Pashkov pulled out a cigarette, but couldn't light it - he broke it. His fingers were visibly shaking. - Oh, if only we could make it to Andreyevka... The stalker lit a new cigarette himself and offered it to Pashkov. The lieutenant took a deep drag, then another, and only then took the cigarette from the stalker. The trembling seemed to have subsided. - Although... to hell with advice. - Bibik patted Pashkov on the shoulder again and went to headquarters. Ostapenko greeted the colonel with a heavy look and a short nod towards the leatherette chair. He already knew everything about the results of the breakthrough attempt, albeit without any particularly impressive details. “They simply exchanged a hundred,” the general muttered through his teeth. “Down the drain.” - My fault! - Bibik spread his hands. - What does this have to do with you?! - Ostapenko winced. - Sit down! “Well, it’s not your fault,” Bibik tried to soften the situation. - Whether there is or not is the sixteenth question. I am the boss here, which means I am responsible for everything and everyone. So listen to my command, Colonel. No more breakthroughs. A tight defense until significant reinforcements arrive. Or an evacuation transport. I think if we hold out until the morning, we will have a chance. Kyiv is promising to send a big box. If we squeeze in, we will all fit. - We might fit, but it's not about passenger seats, it's about cross-country ability. To get through here, we'll need tanks. I saw from the tower how the beasts were rioting. They're building a real abatis along the Teterev. The boars and pseudo-giants are cutting down trees, destroying houses and turning equipment upside down. Armored personnel carriers and infantry fighting vehicles might not get through. - There will be tanks too. They've already left. - And the air? – This issue is being resolved right now. - Is it being resolved? - The colonel grinned. - Usually it means "we'll look into your question in a week, and call in two weeks and we'll give you an answer." - Mister General! - A soldier looked out from the adjoining room. - Kyiv on the line! He handed Ostapenko a satellite phone. The general looked meaningfully at Bibik: "See, I told you – it's being decided." Bibik responded with a look that was almost as full of hidden meaning: "First answer the call and listen to what they tell you, and then we'll discuss who was right and how to get out of it now." Judging by the general's expression, which changed ten times in half a minute - from calm and attentive to indignant and even just angry - Bibik was much closer to the ground than Ostapenko, who had slightly soared into the illusory clouds. Having thrown a couple of short "Understood" and one "Yes" into the receiver, the general cut off the connection and literally threw the receiver to the soldier. It's good that he was ready for something like this and caught the valuable device like a seasoned hockey goalie. After a successful "save", the soldier preferred to retire to his control room, since the next thing the general tried on was a free chair, an iron one, by the way. Ostapenko didn't throw furniture either. He managed to pull himself together rather quickly and used the chair for its intended purpose - he sat down on it opposite Bibik. - Let me guess, - asked the colonel. - NATO is afraid to assemble the vehicles into an armored fist, because they suspect us of collusion with Moscow and Minsk. Allegedly, we ourselves started the mess in the Zone in order to roll their base near Kiev with tanks, right? - I don't know who is afraid of what, but the headquarters considers it inappropriate to organize a reconstruction of the Kursk Bulge here. There will be boxes. Only not one strike one, but several small ones, and they will stop at the outer circle of the Borderland. In our sector - on the right bank of the Teterev. We have been asked to break through or infiltrate, as we prefer, towards Gornostaipol. The headquarters does not intend to go deeper into the Zone. - So they recognize the mutants’ self-seizure of the entire Borderland? - Actually yes. - What fat-assed cowards! God sent the United Headquarters! They shouldn't even command a pioneer camp, let alone the Isolation Forces! "You don't know the main thing yet," Ostapenko said wearily. "They're planning to raise attack aircraft and bomb the base." “How is that possible?!” Bibik was amazed. - Into dust. With volumetric explosion ammunition. - No, wait, what about us?! - You heard: we were asked to leave. All at once or in small groups. To Gornostaipol. - But we won't even go a hundred meters, whether all together or in groups! Why didn't you tell us that we already have a sad experience of breaking through?! - Do you think they would listen to me? - The general waved his hand weakly. - The headquarters knows better, don't you know that? And from Brussels everything is visible... just like the palm of your hand. They have satellites, real-time images, such a resolution that every tooth in every mouth is visible. And the headquarters can't feed bread, let them please NATO. In short, Bibik, as one show business figure I know used to say: "If you want success, promote yourself." No one will help us. Only ourselves and the higher powers. - There is still hope for the Old One... - Phantom. In our situation, it is better to rely on more concrete things. - What kind? - Bibik smiled nervously. - There are three specific things in our situation, and all of them are negative. An attempt to break through means death from the beast's teeth, defending the base means we'll be blown to hell along with those same devils, and the third option is to just shoot ourselves. Somehow I don't want to "rely" on any of the options. - Any other suggestions? - Not yet. How much time do we have? - Ten o'clock. At midnight the headquarters will raise the stormtroopers, and... in another half hour there will be a natural lunar landscape here. You could shoot a movie here. - Yeah. - Bibik scratched his head in confusion, sat for a minute in silence, looking at the map of the Zone laid out on the table, then took a satellite phone out of his pocket and stood up. - We need to call Stary and confess the purpose of the mission. “Why?” Ostapenko shrugged. - What do you mean, why? So that he can get his bearings faster. And in general... to stimulate him. - It won't help. And you won't get in touch with him, I tried several times. Zero. - It's worth a try. Nothing smarter comes to mind anyway. But I'm not used to sitting stupidly and waiting for the end. I'd rather just call stupidly..

The forest north of Korogod was almost no different from the forest around Lake Krinka. It even had the same lakes, small and nasty ones. One difference was that these lakes, along with significant areas of the forest, were constantly changing their location. Not much, about two kilometers, but unpredictably. After each Emission, you had to study the area anew, since the path you had taken a week ago could very well have an insurmountable obstacle in the form of a deep lake. There was no specific information about the creatures living in these migrating lakes, they were also talking about water creatures, but no one could describe them. However, after everything that had happened on the banks of Krinka, Andrey was not going to find out how true the rumors were. That is why, when Tatyana declared in a trembling voice that she was being haunted by deja vu, Lunev, without further ado, led the group to the left and went around the lake that loomed in the gap between the trees in a wide arc – as far as the invisible path allowed. Tatyana was right. Lake Kovteb, located (usually) to the north of Korogod, was like two peas in a pod. Well, maybe it was slightly larger than the former. But in all other respects... the same forest around, the same grass on the shore, even the clearing overgrown with young trees, along which the power line once ran, was in place – to the left of the lake. Another question is that Andrey did not expect to find this body of water on the way. Last month, the lake splashed much further east, on the edge of the Kameneshchina tract. But Lunev had stopped being surprised by the Zone’s whims six years ago, after his first raid. If a piece of terrain is migratory, what else can you expect from it? An anomaly is an anomaly. The path ran exactly along the clearing, but the experienced Lunev did not fall for this trick number thirteen. It was much easier to walk through the old forest than to force their way through the young trees. The group simply kept the clearing in sight, but moved west, and at a good pace. And it soon became clear that this way of walking was not only easier, but also safer. Half a kilometer from the lake, the clearing was covered with thick gray strands of "Burning Fuzz." Carried away by the struggle with the jungle, the walkers could very well immediately get into this, now well-known, but therefore even more frightening muck. And, as Olga put it, the group definitely "didn't need it." The thought of Olga stirred up the mud of associations. Andrey turned to Scout and measured him with an attentive look. - What? - Stalker also looked at himself. - I'm okay, what are you looking at? - Didn't even get burned? - Andrey showed a grin on his face. - No, why? - The scout stared at the commander in bewilderment. “Nothing.” Andrey turned away. - No, wait, you wanted to present me with something, right? - Scout was indignant. - Ah, I see, you also think that I am to blame for all the troubles! Like, I jinxed it. First the copper basin, then the water, and now the fire. Right? - I don’t think anything, leave me alone. - If you said "a", say "b"! Do you think I give hints to the enemy?! “Perhaps you have a hidden gift of foresight?” Tatyana tried to defuse the situation. Her attempt only made Scout more excited. - And you think that I am to blame for everything?! - No, I'm telling you... the gift of foresight... - I have no gift! None at all! I don't even have an ear for music! And anyway, why do you trust Olga once, but not me?! Do you think she's a sinless girl? She's killed so many people, I won't be able to beat her record before I retire! By the way, before we were covered in a copper basin, she was hanging around for half an hour! And she only didn't go into the water. And she spat on the fire! “If it weren’t for Olga, you’d be finishing yourself off right now,” Andrey objected sharply. “At the behest of the controllers.” - Ugh. - Tatyana shuddered. - Does this happen? - Anything can happen here. Olga really helped us out on the island, so there's no point in throwing dirt at her. - Sure, let's go ahead! - Scout said irritably. - Let's sling mud at me! I haven't helped anyone out yet. So what if I took out a couple of bandits and dove into a lake with snakes? But tell me, Old Man, where did your little Thumbelina get those Molotov cocktails? And why did you decide that she was the one throwing bottles? - She, - Andrey said confidently. - And she got the chemicals from the warehouse. South of Korogod there is a collective farm yard and a warehouse of chemical fertilizers. You can find gasoline there, and there is something to make a thickener from. Olga is an experienced chemist, in terms of making flammable and explosive mixtures from anything, even nail polish and powdered sugar, she is a great specialist. You know that. - That she's the famous Smoker? I know. But I'm still not sure it was her. You said there was someone in the ruins, maybe he was the one who saved us? - First he threw grenades at you, and then he helped you? - He didn't throw them! Remember, the grenades exploded where the controllers came out later. Maybe he shot at them? “It’s an option,” Andrey admitted. “But Olga was throwing bottles, I’m telling you for sure.” - Even if so! This is no reason to accuse me of God knows what! - Olga will catch up with us, right? - Tatyana asked uncertainly. - She'll tell us herself. - Good. - Lunev nodded. - Let's close the topic. - Where is the apology?! “You’ll manage,” Andrey waved his hand. “That’s it, quiet down, the slope is starting, save your breath.” “So we’re close?” Scout asked after all. - Yes. All that's left is to climb up the hill and go down. Two kilometers in a straight line. Taking into account the terrain, three. “But we’ll wait...” Tatyana began. - No! - Lunev interrupted her. - We will not wait for anyone! We... Suddenly the satellite communicator in Andrey's pocket spoke up, and he grabbed the device without finishing his thought. Theoretically, there should be no interference with this type of communication, but in practice Andrey heard barely half of what the person on the other end of the line was trying to convey. And that was something of a puzzle. Most of the words made no sense, and it seemed as if they were being spoken backwards. After listening to this gibberish for a minute, Andrey turned on the speakerphone and motioned for his companions to join in the deciphering. - "Tidzb batsh?" - Scout repeated in a whisper after the unknown caller. - "Tichuryv mech tibmobzar"? “San eshorp mi otch yarevz oklots mogurk,” Tatyana picked up Scout’s baton. After repeating all this nonsense, she shook her head and winced. - What language is this? - Scout stared at Andrey. - Albanian? He raised his hand, calling the stalker to be silent. Andrey tried not to miss a single word of the strange speech in an incomprehensible language. The communicator, however, raged for only ten more seconds, after which, having repeated twice (it seemed to Lunev that it was questioningly): "yanem shishyls", he fell silent, apparently forever. - Pindyk "tib-mob-zaru", - said Scout, shaking his head following Tatyana's example. - What kind of connection do you have? What operator? New standard? - "There are so many animals around that it would be easier for them to bomb us than to rescue us," Tatyana suddenly said. - And a little earlier... no, I can't repeat that. - "The headquarters is scared to break through," Andrey said, nodding. - That's right, we heard the speech backwards. And it's a pity that I didn't understand it at the very beginning. Because it was at the beginning, that is, in fact, at the end, that Bibik laid out the most interesting thing. - What? - I don't remember. - Oh, shit! - Scout nodded at the communicator. - Maybe we should call him ourselves? - The computer died. - Andrey poked at the buttons of the device and handed it to Tatyana: - Do you understand it? - Like everyone else. - Tatyana repeated her attempt to revive the communicator, but also failed. Scout also suffered a fiasco. The strange connection, even in "Albanian", was resolutely unwilling to be established. In the end, the stalker returned the device to its rightful owner and shrugged his shoulders. - I don't know how to revive him. On the other hand, everything is clear anyway. There are problems at the base, and such that Bibik decided to call us. I just don't understand what he's waiting for? That we'll return and support the garrison with a dashing attack into the enemy rear? "There are so many animals that it's easier to bomb them" and we won't win. “Nobody is asking us to come back,” Andrey answered. “The colonel is asking us to hurry.” - Wait, - Tatyana suddenly became alarmed. - The distortion of his speech... why did this happen? I understand, the Zone and everything else... but why was the connection silent, silent, and then suddenly came to life, but... "turned inside out"? “Because this hill is the very place where the ordinary world smoothly twists and becomes the hidden world of the Thirteenth Sector,” Andrey suggested. “Do you have any other explanations?” - So, it turns out that on the other side of the hill there is already the Thirteenth? - No, wait, - Scout intervened. - The Mechanic said there was a clearing and a ring of fog. Like a gate. You go through it, and you're in the sector. Or am I mistaken? Or did the Mechanic lie? - True, the clearing, the fog and all that, - agreed Andrey. - But I have a suspicion that this foggy ring in the clearing won't be of any use to us yet. - Didn't understand. - A ring like that appeared at the base, too. And they saw these rings in other places, and then especially well-fed mutants started running around those places. Do you get the idea? - No. - Let me explain. Wherever the rings were found, new mutants appeared, which means they got out of the Thirteenth through these "holes". But no one ever got into the sector through these rings. - Blow from there, blow there... nothing. - Scout chuckled. - The nipple principle. - That's right. The mechanic wasn't lying. To leave the sector, you just need to step into the "hole", but to get into it, you have to walk long and hard along the path. So you can mark the place. We are standing where for ordinary walkers it's just a wooded hill, and for those who have walked along the path - the place where the moment of truth comes. “I’ll make a notch.” Scout took out a knife and walked up to one of the pines. “It would be interesting to come here just like that, in ordinary... space,” said Tatyana. “It would be possible to find Scout’s notch, what do you think?” - Scout! - Andrey turned sharply to the stalker. - Are you serious?! Stop it! It's a trace! - So what? - Scout was genuinely surprised. - What's the point of hiding anything? We've already arrived. - Are you really stupid or are you kidding me? - Okay, okay, why are you so mad? - Scout put the knife away. - Got it, realized it. No traces. - One more stunt like that, and I'll decide that you're a saboteur, - Andrey said sternly. - With all the consequences. I'm not your Olga, I won't play interrogation. So think ten times before you do anything. - Well, I said it, I understand! “He understood,” Andrey grumbled and turned to Tatyana again: “What did you ask?” - You already answered. - The romantic notes disappeared from Tatyana's voice. - Everything here is still the same as everywhere else. This is still a relatively normal world. - Border territory. - Andrey nodded. - On the opposite slope is already the Thirteenth. If we leave an idiotic notch there, only the controllers will be able to find it. And if here... it's better not to leave any traces at all! Andrey pronounced the end of the phrase with emphasis and again looked sternly at Scout. “There’s no need to yell at the whole forest either,” Scout muttered resentfully. “Are we moving on or what?” Andrey suddenly raised his hand, calling the stalker to silence. What he suddenly felt was akin to the already familiar sensation of telepathic pressure, but for some reason Lunev was ready to swear that the new controller was not trying to break through the psi-protection and in general – had no intention of subordinating the man to his will. It was as if he simply wanted to indicate his presence. And again – not to demonstrate force, but only to warn: “I’m coming.” “Or ‘They’re coming,’” it suddenly dawned on Andrey. “Someone is trying to help us!” The suggestion was bold, and if Andrei had voiced it, it would have certainly caused a violent reaction from Scout. But Lunev did not voice it. He gestured to Scout to watch the area carefully, while retreating with Tatyana into the depths of the forest. Andrei himself remained in place. Andrey managed to make sure that he had made the right conclusions in just a minute. The rather sparse forest on the hillside allowed him to see the approaching enemy from afar and in all its glory. Probably understanding this no worse than Lunev, the two controllers walked without even trying to hide behind the trees. Andrey carefully examined the enemies with the help of optics and took a couple of steps back. The group had a decent head start, and theoretically these creatures did not pose any particular danger. Even considering that there were two of them. And yet, after taking two steps, Lunev stopped and again caught the controllers' figures in his sights. Something was wrong. The creatures could walk much faster. They simply had to walk much faster if they wanted to catch up with the walkers. However, the enemies walked as if they were thinking about something, like meditating philosophers on a walk in the park. Why? There were two possible answers: either the controllers were trying to establish mental control over the group, or... over the next assistants. Andrey glanced back. The telepaths' assistants could be hiding in ambush, for example, on the top of a hill or on the opposite slope, or they could move behind the group, only going around its flanks. In both cases, in order to disrupt the enemy's plans, it was necessary to urgently change course or attack first. And it was even better to do both at the same time. The scout had gone far enough that calling him back would have been too risky, and the field headset had died at the same time as the communicator. All that was left was to wait for the stalker to deign to look back, but there was no time for that. Andrey raised his weapon again and tried to aim at the controllers, but the cunning creatures seemed to sense danger and hid behind the trees. Lunev had another revelation. There was no ambush ahead. But the enemy's helpers had begun their flanking maneuver long ago and were just now completing it. The controllers had stayed in sight until the last moment only to distract the attention of the "game" from the real hunters. Andrey raised his machine gun barrel up and concentrated on signals from his other senses, in addition to his vision. A small, fly-like, but equally annoying and very unpleasant thought was buzzing in his brain. The enemy's new assistants did not want to give away their presence. Not in any way. Few specialists could work so covertly. Even the best fighters of the Monolith and Duty reconnaissance groups were like bears for the retired mercenary: they could be heard and seen from a kilometer away. In general, Lunev could rarely be beaten in survival games, even by his former comrades. But that was exactly what was happening now. "Well, let's see how you can get around me," Andrey added his intuition to the job. "Rarely, but still, some have managed it. A couple of times, the special forces succeeded, specifically Brazhnikov's people. It's good that they weren't hunting me at the time. And a couple of times, former brothers in arms almost succeeded. For example, the Mechanic..." Today, the special forces were out of the question, Lunev was sure. Andrey also had a hard time believing that the controllers had managed to lasso the Mechanic who had survived the shelling, but they could easily have lassoed a group of mercenaries passing by Opachichi and sent them along the path of the creature. The version was a little lame, because it was almost impossible to run from Korogod to Opachichi and return with reinforcements in the time it took the group to wander in the direction of the hill. But that was if they ran. But if they flew... "They could have flown. Otherwise, what the hell would they need the 'batmen' for? They're clearly no good as attack aircraft, but as transport aircraft, they're still okay. Four of them dragged Tatyana away, so they could have dropped off the controllers at the point. And a forced march in the opposite direction is a piece of cake for a squad of mercenaries. Especially under the cover of the controllers. No anomalies on your way, no animals. Run and enjoy. While we were dragging ourselves from Korogod to Chistogalovka, they arrived. But where is this squad now? They're obviously close, but where?!" The feeling that a person had suddenly appeared behind him, almost right next to him, was unexpected and somewhat disconcerting. Even Brazhnikov's guys had never snuck up on Lunev like that. Although, to be honest, that time Andrei had ended up in the hands of the specialists, also causing them almost no trouble. They had caught him, as they say, while he was still warm. Andrey suppressed the desire to deal with the enemy and froze, waiting for the banquet to continue. His nerves were tense, but his rich experience told him that a pause that lasted for half a second was a good sign. There was one of his own standing behind him. The alien would have tried to cut his throat long ago. "They're on the left," the man behind him whispered, leaning close to his ear. "A full group. The leader is Zonder, you should remember him, the big guy from Lyakh's brigade." Andrey slowly turned to the left and prepared his machine gun to fire. - Don't go to the right, - the man behind my back whispered again. - The path here behaves like a real bastard. It can easily throw you off or lead you in a circle for a day, like in that labyrinth. You can only go straight, through the top. In short, orient yourself on the psychotronic installation. It works, but it won't penetrate the "Joker". “And you?” Andrey asked, also barely audibly. - I'll cover you. You can't just get away from Sonder, you understand. He's stubborn enough as it is, and then there are the controllers sitting on your brains. Lunev nodded. – Was it you who drove away the controllers in the swamp with grenades? - Me. I got hit on the head with a piece of iron in the field, had to lie down and then rush straight to Korogod. I thought I'd catch up with you a little earlier, but you disappeared somewhere. - They slowed down at Krinka's. Unfortunate. - Brazhnikov? - Yes. - Too bad. In short, it's not the time to chat, Old Man, go. - Good luck, Mechanic. Kruger disappeared as silently as he had appeared. Andrey turned around, but only managed to see his comrade's shadow flashing to the left between the trees. Deep down, Lunev was even pleased that the Mechanic had finally reached such a decent professional level. Once upon a time, Andrey himself taught the new recruit Max Kruger the basics of the craft. The student had not yet surpassed the teacher, but had already caught up with him. For someone, this could perhaps be a blow to pride, a reason for jealousy, but for Lunev, this turn of events caused only positive emotions. A worthy student, even if not too docile and not agreeing with the teacher in everything, is better than a simple imitator who mindlessly uses a formal set of skills-tools honed by the mentor. An excellent sequel only emphasizes the quality of the original product, what jealousy can there be? The skirmish behind the group began exactly when Andrey caught up with Scout and Tatyana. The former mercenary Kruger showed himself to be a competent and quite mature specialist here too. The mechanic attacked the enemy using the "pick up and shoot" principle. First, grenades were used, and then a shootout ensued. The mercenaries, under the command of the experienced but somewhat straightforward Zonder, were forced to reveal themselves and change their plan of action, which was clearly not to their liking. The battle started quite far from the walkers, and therefore it was unrealistic to help the Mechanic in any way. Kruger had calculated everything here too. With all his actions, he insistently recommended the walkers to move on, without being distracted by secondary matters. “Forward,” Andrey ordered, gesturing towards the top of the hill. “I don’t understand,” Scout said, looking around warily. “Who’s fighting there?” - The mechanic has risen. - Andrey pushed the stalker forward. - What are you talking about! Survived? Cool! - Don't get distracted. There's a psychotronic emitter ahead. It's aimed right at us. The "Jokers" will help, but you'll still have to demonstrate your willpower. Be prepared. - I'm a Scout. - Stalker grinned. - Always ready. - My legs... - Tatyana complained. - They feel like cotton wool. Like in a dream. You want to run, but your legs won't obey. - In my dreams, in that case, I fly up, - Scout admitted. - Well... it's a dream... you can do that. Sometimes I rise up like that... the ground is below... far, far away! - Scout! - Andrey nudged his comrade again. - I'm coming, I'm coming. "Tanya, concentrate," Lunev said, supporting his companion by the elbow. "There's nothing wrong with your legs, that's just the emitter doing that. We'll get to the top, everything will be fine." - I... try... but I can't! - Notes of panic appeared in Tatyana's voice. She stumbled and fell to her knees. Andrey, without thinking twice, picked her up in his arms and nodded to Scout to bring up the rear. - Watch out, there are mercenaries behind you. - Wow! - Scout shuddered involuntarily. - Now we've definitely got it. “Don’t panic.” Andrey resolutely moved up the slope. It was getting harder to walk with every meter, his legs really did feel like cotton wool, and his arms felt like lead and almost let go of the precious burden, but Lunev stubbornly climbed up. When it became impossible to hold Tatyana in his arms, Andrey put her down and then threw her over his shoulder. Tatyana let out a quiet yelp, but did not protest. His legs eventually gave out, but at that moment Lunev had already crossed the invisible boundary beyond which the effect of the emitter – a dish mounted on a concrete pedestal, similar to a satellite dish – practically ceased to exist. Andrey fell to his knees and then toppled over onto his side. Lunev still had enough strength, thanks to the energy boost, but nervous and mental overstrain had literally paralyzed him. His body was shaking slightly, and he couldn't stop it with an effort of will. Scout, who had crawled up behind him on all fours, couldn't help. He was shaking like a man in a fit. The stalker stretched out next to the commander and moaned something unintelligible, but clearly obscene. Tatyana saved both comrades. She was shaking much less. The girl grabbed Andrey by the collar of his jacket and dragged him away from the emitter. Then she repeated the procedure with Scout. It can't be said that the walkers immediately felt better, but at least they stopped twitching like laboratory frogs under electric current. - Yoklmn! - Scout sat up and with a trembling hand felt for the machine gun. - I'm going to blow this thing to smithereens! - No! - Andrey said, also sitting down on the damp pine needles. - Do you hear, the shooting has stopped? If the Mechanic failed, the mercenaries followed in our footsteps. Let them get what they deserve, too. - If he couldn't handle it? - The stalker lowered his weapon. - And there were many of them? - Raid group.
