Twelve people. “I couldn’t handle it,” Scout concluded confidently and sighed. “So what are we going to do?” “Go down.” Andrey, leaning on his machine gun, stood up and hobbled across a tiny clearing at the top of the hill to the beginning of the descent along the northern slope. - If the winter were normal, we'd ski... bam, and down there. - Scout stood up, groaning. - But we won't get away like this. “We’re down, they’re up, there’s a chance,” Andrey objected. “Move.” - Five seconds! - Scout leaned against a tree and began to pull off his boot. - The toe is knocked off. I'll catch up! Scout caught up even earlier. An explosion that thundered thirty meters away from him made the stalker instantly put on his shoes and in three jumps descend to the level where his comrades had slowed down. - What's the matter? - Andrey looked around anxiously. - The emitter was blown up! - The frightened Scout stared at the commander. - Wow, a pro! The charge from the forest hit the plate perfectly. The first grenade! - Do you think so? - Andrey shook his head doubtfully. - What should I think, I saw it myself! - The explosion was so... it looked more like a hand grenade exploded. - Then it's really bad, - the stalker became even more worried. - That means they're almost at the top. - That's true. Now nothing holds them back, only the opposite - the controllers' suggestion drives them on. - Shall we push harder? - Let's try. The trio gathered all their strength into a fist and rushed down the slope, as if trying to escape from an avalanche rushing at their heels. "Avalanche", as it soon became clear, was still faster than the walkers. Despite all their qualifications, Sonder's men could not run silently, and therefore Andrei very soon heard their clatter literally behind him. Lunev could very well have pressed on, but it was unlikely that Tatyana was capable of this. She ran slightly ahead, and Andrei could clearly see that his companion was starting to give in. A couple of times she almost stretched out to her full height, tripping over pine roots. Scout looked more cheerful, but he, too, was breathing heavily and periodically clutched his left side, trying to calm his spleen, which was throbbing from lack of oxygen. Something had to be done immediately. Andrei took one of the two remaining grenades from his pouch, pulled out the pin and, looking back for a moment, threw it in the direction of the silhouettes flickering between the trees. It's unlikely that the explosion slowed the mercenaries down significantly, but it did force the walkers to find internal reserves. The scout grabbed Tatyana by the hand and seriously increased her speed. Tatyana also pulled herself together and stopped stumbling. Muffled shots rang out behind him, and Andrei mentally prepared himself for a shootout, but, contrary to expectations, bullets did not whistle around. This meant that the mercenaries were not shooting at the fugitives, but either into the air or in the opposite direction. Lunev slowed down and, choosing a more or less decent shelter, three pines growing almost close to each other, stopped. The mercenaries were still running in the footsteps of the walkers, but now their clatter was not as loud. Only two at most continued the pursuit, the rest - at least six more - had clearly stopped. It was clear who had forced Zonder's group to disperse. The mechanic held them back as best he could on the southern slope, let them pass by and attacked again. Everything was correct. Except, perhaps, for one thing. A new attack should have been organized on the flank. And it would have been even better to abandon this tactic altogether and switch to silently cutting off their tails. Andrey would have done so, but Kruger, as always, had his own opinion. Well, it was his fight. Andrey waited in cold blood until the vanguard of the mercenaries passed his shelter, and with an enviable rate of fire, he put two bullets into the backs of the heads of the fighters who were carried away by track and field. The fact that the mercenaries did not notice the Old One who had merged with the trees was pure luck, plus the "merit" of the controllers - no doubt. Controlling the creatures significantly reduced the usual combat capabilities of the wards and seriously suppressed their ability to fully perceive the surrounding reality. People's field of vision narrowed, their hearing weakened, and their intuition was completely switched off. But there was no other way to keep them in check, so the enemies preferred to use the mercenaries' potential halfway, but exactly as the controllers required. Andrey turned again towards the approaching group of Zonder and assessed the changes in the situation. They were serious and not in favor of the walkers. The mercenaries, despite the psi-control, were still dangerous and skilled fighters. They had finally figured out the Mechanic and were now driving him into an improvised trap. Andrey saw that Kruger was being surrounded, but he could not help Max in any way. The mercenaries were moving very quickly, from tree to tree, so Andrey was unable to take down even one on the fly, although he made at least five attempts. It all ended with Lunev also being discovered and forced to retreat from his comfortable position down the slope. Meanwhile, Mechanic was pinned down and forced to lie down by crossfire. As soon as Max lost his ability to maneuver, his fate was sealed. Andrey knew one hundred percent what would happen in the next minute, and so he mentally said goodbye to Kruger. Sonder's men did exactly as Lunev expected. They planted a couple of grenades in the ground near the Mechanic's position, and then, firing on the move, closed the pincers. The sudden silence was something like a statement of fact - Max Kruger, nicknamed the Mechanic, was dead. A single shot - the final one - put a fat period on the "death certificate". Now no one was stopping the mercenaries from concentrating on the walkers. Which is what they did. The short echo of the control shot had not yet died down, and the chase had already resumed. Andrey roughly estimated the distance and speed at which the mercenaries were rushing, and came to the conclusion that there was no point in running. Of course, they could try to break away, relying on the "Joker", but as the mercenaries reduced the distance, counting on the help of the artifact became increasingly risky. The "Joker" did not save from point-blank shots - a conclusion proven in practice. Lunev chose a new position and prepared for fire contact. Andrei did not hear the steps of his companions behind him, and this calmed him down a little. If Major Brazhnikov and Mechanic had been in Scout and Tatyana's place, it would have been a different matter; these two barrels would have been very useful to Lunev now. As for the walkers remaining in the group... it was easier for Andrei alone. Less worries, more space and opportunities for maneuver. Strictly speaking, Andrei had only one concern left - if only his companions would go away and not think of returning. The mercenaries were already at the optimal distance, but Lunev failed to open fire. Instead, someone in the rear of Sonder's group did so. This time, the Mechanic was ruled out, but the mysterious shooter could not have been a random passenger either. Firstly, he was firing from a special weapon, and secondly, such lucky coincidences do not happen on the trail. Of all the possible candidates, Olga was the most realistic. The guess was partly confirmed by the fact that the barely audible gunfire (for the uninitiated, the sounds were no louder than finger snaps) sounded far from the positions of the mercenaries hiding behind the trees and that none of the enemies opened return fire. Andrey almost immediately realized what Olga was doing. The same thing Ostapenko had recruited her for in Stary's group. Shooting off controllers. In response to the claps of the "val", three panicked long bursts from the "Kalashnikov" were heard, then silence reigned for a while, and finally three more shots muffled by a silencer rang out. Andrey was soon convinced that these three shots were indeed the "final" ones. The mercenaries stirred and began to retreat in a westerly direction, trying to simultaneously keep both the top and bottom of the slope under their sights. For a second, Andrey had Zonder himself in his sights. The leader of the raiding party was without a mask, so Lunev could easily see his face. It was, to put it mildly, dissatisfied and puzzled. The mercenary held a machine gun in his right hand, and was rummaging around in his bosom with his left. Having found what he was looking for, Zonder pulled a strap with a leather pouch from behind his collar and squeezed it in his fist. The mercenary's expression did not change much after that. He still looked dissatisfied and puzzled. Maybe even more than before. Andrey could say with a high degree of certainty that he knew what the mercenary was thinking about. Like most mercenaries, Zonder used an amulet, which usually protected well from all types of psi-effects. In any case, ordinary controllers, psychotronic emitters and psi-anomalies are not scary for a person with such an amulet around his neck. However, even Zonder, not the smartest of the mercenaries, understood that his group was still "divorced" by the controllers and the amulets were powerless in this situation. And Zonder also managed to draw the right conclusion from this fact quickly, well done. It would be stupid to sort things out on the spot with such strong controllers. He should have just left while it let up, and only after returning to the base, figure out "what kind of crap is this" and how to fight it. Very soon the group of "blues" disappeared from view, and Andrey relaxed. The mercenaries had left the path, no doubt, and if any of the controllers had survived, they could no longer cause much harm to the walkers. Lunev came out of hiding and stopped, listening. Olga seemed to be hesitating and was in no hurry to rejoin the group. Andrey did not know the reason for this. Perhaps one of the creatures had survived and the girl had decided to first track down the mutant that had escaped, and only then return to the general formation? Lunev stood motionless for several minutes, but he heard nothing and saw no one. Olga seemed to have disappeared. It was unlikely that she had accidentally strayed from the path or died... "So there is a reason. It's better to stop there for now. He'll catch up and tell me." Andrey threw the weapon over his shoulder and resolutely walked down the slope. It was not difficult for Lunev to find his comrades. Scout and Tatyana were sitting in the thickets at the foot of the hill and arguing listlessly, sometimes sticking their heads out of the bushes, sometimes looking warily toward the forest on the hill. - Andrey! - Seeing Lunev, Tatyana noticeably cheered up. - I was so worried! What happened there? “The mechanic died,” Andrey summed up briefly. “But he solved the problem.” “I’m very sorry,” Tatyana sighed. - First Brazhnikov, now the Mechanic, - Scout was saddened for a couple of seconds. - It seems like he just "resurrected" and again... he's gone. Life is a villain. - A controversial issue. What do you have? - We... - Scout didn't immediately switch to a new topic. - We? Oh, well, that's not good either. I mean, the end of the trail, that's good, but... Look at it yourself. Andrey pushed the bushes aside and looked at the field stretching out ahead. It was covered with yellow grass and here and there with islands of bushes. The end of the path that Scout had mentioned was clearly visible. It looked exactly like the "hole" through which, according to Brazhnikov, the creatures from Sector Thirteen had entered the base. Except that this circle was slightly larger. Andrey had no doubt that this was the same exit from the sector as the one that remained at the base. However, the "entrance" path was unlikely to lead further. It simply had nowhere else to go. Along the edge of the clearing, immediately behind the forty-meter diameter ring of greenish fog, stretched a very long and tall, at least three stories, rampart of rusty scrap metal. “But once upon a time this was a strategic military facility, the terror and pride of the armed forces.” Lunev had a good idea of what the "over-the-horizon" radar station in Chernobyl-2 looked like, when it was still a majestic iron wall, more than seven hundred meters long and fifty meters high, a monument to the Iron Curtain and the country's former defensive might. Now, looking at the remains of the gigantic structure curled up like rusty figs, one could only marvel at its scale and how quickly progress was moving. Today, what this radar did - and it tracked any missile launches in the other hemisphere - was handled much faster and more effectively (though not cheaper) by satellites. In general, the entrance to the Thirteenth Sector was located in the same place as the exit, there was no doubt about that. But how to use it really remained a serious question. Around the peculiar gates, forming three more rings in addition to the foggy one, at arm's length from each other, stood a palisade of sentries, with each fighter holding a short chain in his hands, on which a huge dog sat motionless, like a sphinx. Even if Lunev had a whole special forces platoon at his disposal, and not two "conditionally fit for combat" walkers, breaking through this cordon would have been problematic. "No, if the special forces had brought flamethrowers or five boxes of grenades... maybe it would have worked out. Literally. And there's practically nothing to do with machine guns here. It seems like these sentries are no good. Just weaklings. Dogs aren't a problem either. But on the other hand... One hundred and fifty of those and the same number of others. They'll crush us with their mass. Now if someone could break through... a dozen wild boars, for example, to trample a path. But where would we get them?" At the root of Lunev’s doubts lay just one point, which fundamentally distinguished the cordon around the gates of the Thirteenth Sector from any other guard, even one reinforced by a platoon of dog handlers with their pets. All the fighters and dogs were really... dead. Without quotes. One hundred and fifty zombies and the same number of dead dogs. That is why Andrey did not hope to shoot this terrible guard, or to put them to flight or to panic under heavy fire. Bullets were like logs to the dead, and their emotions were exactly the same, that is, nothing. It is generally difficult to find an effective weapon against them. After all, none of the creators of the standard weapons counted on the destruction of an already dead enemy. When stalkers first encountered this problem in the Zone, they had to worry a lot before they understood who zombies were and how to fight them. The only reason why these creatures... not even creatures, but remains, rotted standing, sitting and moving on the surface of the earth, and not lying in it, was the anomalous energy of the Zone, and in this case, also of the nearby hidden sector. Well, these blockheads attacked or built themselves so beautifully at the will of the controllers who controlled them. That is, in order to deal with them, first of all it was necessary to destroy their master, and then consign the zombie bodies immobilized without the will of the controller to fire. At worst - to blow them to pieces with a grenade. Olga's favorite flamethrower was very useful in such cases. Or five boxes of "lemon", as Andrey thought. Lunev backed up, sat down on a mossy stump and looked questioningly at his comrades. “What?” Scout asked. - You tell me. You've been sitting for a long time, discussing something. Do you have any thoughts? "I suggest we find the controller," the scout pointed to the remains of the radar. "He's probably sitting there somewhere." - It wasn't just one. - Andrey shook his head. - There are about twenty creatures hiding somewhere nearby. We won't find them all in a day. Tanya, what did you suggest? - That's it. Wait for Olga. She'll find the controllers... all of them. Probably. And we'll kill them. Probably. With every word, Tatyana's voice became less and less confident. "We have no right to waste time," Andrey firmly objected. "Dozens of people die every minute in the Borderlands. We need to get to the Thirteenth Sector as soon as possible." - Then on a paraglider. - Scout chuckled. - Look, jump from a pile of scrap metal straight into the center of the circle, and hello. - Even if your nonsense weren't nonsense... - Andrey winced. - Remember what the Mechanic said. You can only enter the sector by land. And I'm also sure that the remains of the radar lie beyond the path. Whoever climbs on them will land in an ordinary clearing. "That's why we don't see the controllers!" Scout slapped his forehead. "They really are sitting on bent pieces of iron, only in normal space, right?" - Yes, - agreed Andrey. - For "creative thought" the curved space is not an obstacle. That's probably how the controllers control their invasion army. Several coordinators assess the situation and report to the others, and they command the herd directly from the Thirteenth Sector. Convenient and safe. - Cool! Why did these controllers try to get us personally? - We're not mutants, - Andrey shrugged. - Well, what else can we come up with? Besides paragliders, rocket packs and tanks lying around in the bushes. - Yes! What else can we think of? - Scout seemed to redirect the question to Tatyana. - I don't know. - You are a science! Why were you included in the group? “Scout, leave me alone,” Andrey demanded. “We think that’s all.” - I... - Stalker looked somewhere towards the forest behind Lunev and visibly shuddered. - Wow! You fucking Japanese policeman! The brushwood crackled behind Andrey. Scout choked on the phrase and rolled his eyes, risking losing them altogether. Turning in the same direction, Tatyana quietly gasped and froze, like an ice sculpture. Andrey was the last to turn around. Calmly assessing the situation, Lunev held Scout, who had grabbed his machine gun, slowly rose and took a step toward the forest. The man standing at the edge of the forest, directly opposite the walkers, did not react in any way to either Scout's movements or Lunev's step. The man stood, swaying slightly, and looked straight ahead. Actually, that's how all zombies "look". And it was hard to doubt that the man who came out of the forest was dead. His entire body was covered with numerous shrapnel wounds and bullet holes, and the most noticeable bullet mark gaped right in the middle of his forehead. But the main thing is that Andrey saw with his own eyes how this man was killed by mercenaries a few minutes ago. Lunev slowly approached the dead Mechanic and walked around him. Why his comrade's corpse was moving in space was a mystery that defied a clear explanation, like all cases of zombies appearing in the Zone, and so Andrey assigned it the number one hundred and sixteen. It was much more important now to understand why the Mechanic had come and who had brought him. The questions were tightly connected, and the answer could be completely unified. Andrey stopped in front of the Mechanic again. Kruger smelled of blood and forest mold. The body still retained the remnants of warmth, and the glassy eyes were dry, but not yet clouded. It was difficult for Andrey to look at his dead comrade, but Lunev hoped to understand what the controller who had driven the dead man had "forgotten" here, and so he did not look away. The mechanic suddenly stepped forward, and Lunev barely managed to step out of his way. Kruger broke through the bushes and moved with an even, almost marching step towards the gates of the Thirteenth Sector. The approaching twilight gradually blurred the silhouettes and details of the dismal landscape, but for now visibility remained more or less decent. In any case, it was not difficult to see what was happening at a distance of two hundred meters. Even the suddenly intensifying rain did not interfere. The mechanic walked evenly, at a medium pace, pressing his steps into the mud. High fountains of spray rose from under his feet, but the mercenary never slipped on the slippery ground or tripped over the numerous hummocks. There was plenty of noise from his movements, but none of the guards even turned their heads in the direction of the approaching zombie. The dogs also did not react to his approach. "A fisherman saw another fisherman from afar" and immediately forgot about him. Andrey hadn't expected anything else. It was still a mystery to him why the Mechanic had come, but his instincts told him that there was no reason to panic yet. Kruger had turned into one of the monsters, and theoretically nothing good could be expected from him, but Lunev decided to watch the show to the end and draw conclusions after the curtain. Or legs. When the Mechanic reached the first line of cordon, the second and third began to move - the zombies parted, letting a new tribesman through to the cherished gates to their inside-out paradise. Kruger came right up to the ring of greenish fog and stopped abruptly. The zombies didn't see it. Not a single sentry turned to look at the walker. After he passed the cordon, the zombies simply closed ranks and froze again like rotting idols. The mechanic performed a fairly decent turn around and slowly pulled a small duffel bag off his shoulder. When Kruger pulled three familiar-shaped objects out of the bag one after another, Lunev became worried and raised his machine gun to his shoulder. It was easy to see the objects through the optical sight, despite the suddenly thickening twilight. These were NATO anti-personnel mines, but not simple ones, but ones with a directional explosion. Apparently, the same ones that Mechanic had mentioned back in the forest near Opachichi. When installed correctly, they mowed down a sector of up to sixty degrees along the front and up to fifty meters in length. It was unlikely that three charges would be able to destroy all three cordon lines on half the circumference of the gate, but it was quite possible to briefly scatter at least a sixth of the sentries. "I told you, Max, the mines will still come in handy," Andrey thought. "And so it happened, my intuition did not fail me. It's good that we saved money. Now we just need to break into the circle, and then we'll see. Kill me, I don't understand why, but it seems to me that inside the "ring" these idiots will not be scary. Probably my intuition is telling me again." The mechanic slowly squatted down and stuck the mines into the dirt. He did it in such a way that three explosions would clear a fairly large sector. Andrey estimated the area of destruction by eye and came to the conclusion that if he added a couple of grenades, he could cut a fairly sufficient passage through the zombie formation. There was just one problem - no later than twenty seconds later, the passage would tighten. It turned out that the group had to play along with Kruger and get as close to the cordon as possible so that after a corridor formed in the undead formation, they could use it in time. Having set the mines, the Mechanic took the remote control for the detonation from his bag and rose to his full height. Lunev took out a grenade and also rose, ready to step out of the bushes at the first signal from the former mercenary, but Scout suddenly held him back. - Listen, commander, the radio isn’t working here! - What are you talking about? - Andrey turned around and looked at the stalker with displeasure. - The remote control! It won't work, it'll die, just like your communicator! The mines won't explode! The mechanic... or whoever controls it... shouldn't have started this! “Get the grenades,” Lunev ordered. "It's a dead end, I tell you!" Scout protested. - I see that you are not alive. - Andrey pulled out the ring. - You can stay here if you are a coward. Tatyana, prepare the grenades. - I don't know how to... use them. - No need. I'll hold out my hand, give it to me. Don't fall behind. Follow me! - Listen, Old Man! - Scout tried to grab Andrey by the sleeve, then Tatyana, but both resolutely brushed off the stalker and came out of the thicket into the clearing. The scout swore incoherently, spat in annoyance, pulled a grenade out of his pouch and followed. The zombies and dogs reacted to living people in a completely different way than to the dead Mechanic. The first line of cordon began to stir and slowly moved towards the people. The dogs began to tug at the chains, rush forward, grin angrily, but, as expected, did not make a single sound. However, the background sound over the clearing changed. In addition to the sound of rain, howling wind and cawing of crows in the nearby trees, it now included the sounds of heavy footsteps, splashing water in puddles, squelching sticky mud and the clanking of numerous chains. Such a sepulchral soundtrack was perhaps even more frightening than the sight of zombies mechanically striding towards them. From a distance, it seemed that they were all dressed in brown-green camouflage, this disguise was created by the mud mixed with mold covering their torn clothes. As they approached, details became visible: cloudy whitish eyes, yellow mucus oozing from wounds and mouths and spots of rot on gray faces. It was both scary and disgusting to the extreme. Although, to each his own. Tatyana seemed to be most frightened by the dogs straining at their chains. Not a sound came out of the mouths of the dead creatures, but yellow-green saliva dripped and some scraps were washed away by the rain. Upon closer inspection, it turned out that these were clots of small white worms. When the group had no more than fifty meters to go before the cool embrace of the zombies, Andrey stopped and raised his hand with the grenade. The mechanic, who, thanks to his considerable height, towered in the rear of the dead army like a beacon, repeated Lunev's gesture. It was noticeable that the body was gradually ceasing to obey the "new master", rigor mortis was setting in, but for now the controller was coping with Kruger's body. The mechanic slowly raised the remote control over his shoulder. "Get ready," Andrey quietly ordered his comrades. "I'll throw a grenade, drop to the ground immediately." Lunev lowered his hand and moved it slightly away, but was in no hurry to throw the grenade. “What are you waiting for?!” Scout became nervous. Andrey didn't answer. He waited a few more seconds and, when the zombies were at the optimal distance, he threw the grenade. Already from the "parterre", Andrey threw both grenades given by Tatyana, trying to destroy as many monsters of the first line as possible. The effect of three explosions was minimal. It tore to shreds hardly five or six dogs and three sentries. All the other zombies were simply thrown aside by the blast wave. Considering that their bodies, unlike the Mechanic's body, had long since passed rigor mortis, the monsters should have risen and returned to the line fairly quickly, but still not faster than people could run the coveted hundred meters to the "gates of hell". The only problem was that two more cordon lines were practically undamaged. Andrey raised his head and looked at the Mechanic. He was still standing behind the cordon and holding the remote control in his hand. And that was all. For some reason, he was in no hurry to detonate the mines. "I told you it wouldn't work!" Scout whispered, a hint of panic in his voice. "Crawl away!" He backed away, but Andrey managed to grab him by the hood. - Lie! - What are you lying there for? Look at those dead men getting up and hobbling over here. They'll let their dogs loose and that's it! - Pull closer! - Andrey lowered his head and pressed Scout to the ground. The remote finally worked. Three powerful explosions swept away both remaining cordon lines, and also grabbed the front line fighters who had managed to get up. The clods of earth and pieces of dead bodies falling from the sky had not yet splashed across the puddles, but Lunev had already raised his comrades and literally dragged them to the "gates". The surviving zombies (or rather, their controllers) finally figured out how to unchain the dogs, but the dead dogs ran poorly. Only two of the creatures managed to grab Scout and Tatyana by the legs before the people found themselves at the edge of the foggy ring. The dogs were unable to bite through the Kevlar, and when Scout, trying to shake the creature off, stumbled and stepped into the greenish haze, the dog that had clung to him voluntarily released its grip and backed away, tucking its mangy tail between its legs. Realizing that the foggy frame of the conventional gate was a forbidden place for zombies, Scout abruptly pulled Tatyana onto the narrow strip of land. The creature, which was holding Tatyana tightly by the ankle, also let go and tore away from the ring. "Old man, come here!" Scout called. Andrey stood half a step away from the fog, but was in no hurry to hide in it. The Mechanic was also half a step away from Lunev. He stood there, swaying slightly, completely indifferent, but still supported by the anomalous energy in the unnatural state of a zombie. No one controlled him anymore, Kruger had completed his mission, and now no one needed his services. And this made Lunev even more offended. Max had done so much both before and even after his death that he deserved peace. However, what could Lunev do? Dig a grave and order the Mechanic to lie down in it? But how can you order a zombie if you are not a controller, and there is little sense in it. Any creature, if it wants, can easily force the long-suffering body to crawl out from under the ground and send it wandering around the Zone until it rots and falls apart on the go. What kind of peace is there? All that was left was to hope for the good will of the one who controlled Kruger. After all, someone controlled it. And judging by the fact that this "someone" helped the walkers, he had some notions of humanity. Even though he was a controller. If so, the former "master" was simply obliged to send the body to rest. "If, of course, such a thing is possible. Honestly, I've never seen zombies calm down. Even abandoned by the controller, they still wander or stand like pillars "in waiting mode". And so on until the end, until they rot or until the crows peck them. So... the only real hope is to bomb this place and destroy the Mechanic along with the other zombies." Andrey, as if apologizing, patted the Mechanic on the shoulder and took a step back. Or rather, almost did. Kruger suddenly grabbed Andrey by the hand, slowly turned in his direction and slightly tilted his head to the side. The Mechanic was still "looking" into nowhere, and no thoughts were reflected in his gaze, and could not be reflected, but it seemed to Lunev that he understood the meaning of the gesture and the peculiar nod of his former comrade. “Scout!” Andrey called without turning around. - What? - The stalker's voice was filled with fear. - Why is he clinging to you? - Give me a grenade. - Lunev extended his hand. - And if he doesn't let go? You'll explode along with him! - Let's! The scout put a grenade in Andrey's hand. Lunev carefully removed the amulet and the pouch with the "Joker" from the Mechanic's neck, hung the items on himself, then pulled out the ring and shoved the grenade into Kruger's bosom. The mechanic immediately unclenched his fingers and turned his back to Andrey. So Lunev had done everything correctly. Andrey spread his arms, ordering his companions to step back, and finally took a step back, inside the gate. The walkers no longer heard the thunderous explosion. From the space of the Thirteenth Sector, everything that was happening on the other side of the "ring" looked like a silent game of distorted silhouettes in a shadow theater. Just now, the silhouette of a tall man was visible at the very foggy line, and now it had already scattered into small fragments. Curtain. And the main thing is that for some reason not a single fragment got inside the cherished circle. "Probably for the same reason that dogs and other zombies didn't come here. The only exit here is for the dead. Only the living are allowed in. Goodbye, Mechanic. Thank you and goodbye. This time for sure."

...In a major matter there are no trifles. Every detail can be decisive. The engineer had known this truth for a long time, but, to be honest, he had only recently begun to strictly follow it. In December. After the failure of Operation Black Angel. After a seemingly insignificant mistake led to a new collapse. The blow was no stronger than the one the military had dealt in October, but, like in the fall, it hit the most sensitive spot. And anyway, if Ostapenko's people had been smart enough, they could have ended the confrontation with the psionics back in December. The upstart mercenary, once again attracted by the general to his banners, almost reached the entrance to the Thirteenth Sector, where neither the psionics nor the creatures guarding the entrance were ready for his visit. In December, the entrance path was much shorter, plus it ran through different places, and the Old Man, without knowing it, walked along it from beginning to almost the end. If it had not been for the intervention of people, the Engineer's new assistants, who forced the mercenary to turn north at the last moment, the gates would have turned into an open secret. And what would have followed is hard to imagine. So the destruction of the super-anomaly, and along with it the armed group that helped the psionics subjugate the Zone to the Black Angel devouring it like a cancerous tumor, was a painful blow, but again not fatal. When the previous order of things was restored, the Engineer admitted to himself that the idea was crude. Too much should have been taken into account and calculated in advance, but he limited himself to a general plan and essentially let things slide. Since then, he no longer neglected the little things, although he still focused on the main component - his last hope, the unique artifact "Key". The Engineer struggled for days to create the correct algorithm for controlling this crystal, and his titanic efforts were not in vain. He managed to create the first transition points back when the "Black Angel" operation was in full swing. Through these narrow "holes" the Engineer released only a few dozen mutants from the hidden sector into the outside world, but with each experiment the transitions became better and better: they became wider, more stable and more durable. Looking at his success, the psionics were also inspired. The Ghost, who barely kept his brothers and sisters from reckless actions when the second operation failed, even allowed himself to remark that the death of the Angel could have been the end of the Engineer's career, but now... the brothers and sisters trust their leader again. The engineer himself understood everything perfectly well. That is why he took the risk of leaving the psionics in the care of the Ghost and immersed himself in his work. To be honest, he had no other choice. The second option - the destruction of the dissatisfied, which had worked before, would not work now no matter how hard he tried. Everyone was dissatisfied, including his assistant and his loyal sisters-bodyguards. But the "Key", figuratively speaking, "turned in the lock", and the psionics' dissatisfaction was replaced by a new wave of enthusiasm. The engineer instantly found work for everyone, and most of all for the Ghost. “To make the new project as secure as possible, we must take two important steps,” the Engineer told his assistant. “First: remove Ostapenko and destroy all his archives. People must be sure that the Zone is no longer hiding any major secrets from them. And the second step: send our brothers behind enemy lines. When the decisive moment arrives, they must get close to the MIS headquarters and arrange a mass hypnosis session for the generals. Both tasks are of the highest degree of complexity, and therefore I can only entrust their execution to you.” “The second task is easier,” Ghost noted. “To get several psionics out of the Zone, you won’t need much. Makeup, contact lenses, and money. Or you can do without all that. The brothers will be able to easily convince those around them that they look like people.” "No, they must hide their gift until the last moment," the Engineer objected sternly. "People who have been through the Zone will feel that someone is trying to cloud their minds. You must select the most self-possessed of all psionics, those who will be able to keep their cool even on the brink of failure." - Okay, I'll do as you say. Kyiv is far enough from the Zone, every day in this city will cost the brothers a week of life, but they will easily make this sacrifice, I'm sure. The main thing is that in an emergency the headquarters is not relocated somewhere further from the Zone. If that happens, our brothers will die. - It won't happen. - Let's hope so. As for the first task... it's beyond our capabilities. The General does not go into the Zone, and beyond its borders... I can only repeat: we grow weaker the further we get from the Zone. Until enough passages open in the outside world, through which the life-giving energy of the Zone flows into the wild lands, we are tied to this area like watchdogs. - You are mistaken, Ghost. Outside the Zone, our power remains, but it does not lie in superpowers, but in our wealth. We can easily turn the general's secret weapon against him. And we won't have to leave the Zone. I mean the mercenaries. Contact their intermediaries, pay a fabulous sum, and they will do everything we need. “Yes, that could work,” the Ghost agreed, and immediately outlined a mental outline of the plan: “The first thing we should do is destroy the archive; theft is not such a high-profile crime, it won’t cause much noise.” - That's right. The papers must be destroyed in your presence. - But there shouldn't be much time between the theft and the general's destruction. Within the fork between the actions, the mercenaries will be exposed to great risk. - Pay them extra for the risk. Or hire two teams. - Yes, Engineer, that's what I'll do. I hope Ostapenko doesn't expect such decisiveness from us. I'm just afraid that the reaction to the destruction of the general will be total checks and purges. Our agents will be exposed to serious risk. - What do you suggest? - Divide the first task into two: destroy the archive and kill the general. The papers will burn during an "accidental" fire, and Ostapenko will disappear as soon as our invasion begins. In a combat situation, it will be more difficult for people to get their bearings and understand where the general has gone. In addition, at that moment our brothers will start working behind enemy lines, who will convince the headquarters that there is no time and no point in looking for the general. “You’re a good tactician, Ghost.” The engineer was pleased with the plan. “Otherwise you wouldn’t have kept me as your assistant, Engineer.” In the Ghost’s thoughts the phrase didn’t sound insolent, but rather respectful. This subtle nuance also pleased the Engineer. Now he was absolutely sure that the Ghost was the one who would help take into account all the little things that so strongly influence the success of any major undertaking. "One head is good, but two are better. Especially for psionics"... .

...But it is even better when the minds of the psionics, united in a kind of "network", are assisted by the brute force of the mutants. Now the Engineer knew this for sure. The outside world was on the brink of a new catastrophe - and all thanks to such a successful combination of the abilities of the psionics and the power of the creatures. Satisfied with the news from the outside world, the Engineer felt an emotional lift. The only thing that bothered him was a vague understanding of why the problems of days long past had suddenly come to mind. How were the events of December connected with current affairs? Why had memory pushed these particular facts and episodes to the surface? The appearance of the Ghost nearby helped the Engineer find answers to all the questions at once. Or perhaps the Ghost himself helped find them. For example, if he gave a hint. As unnoticed as he appeared. - Did you say something? - The engineer looked inquisitively at the Ghost. “I couldn’t take into account all the details, I’m sorry,” the assistant lowered his gaze. - You're thinking about two things at once, I don't understand, are you barely asking for forgiveness? - It is not my fault that the general is unreachable. His archive burned down, as we planned, the agents set fire to the counterintelligence building as soon as the operation began. But Ostapenko himself is now in D-3. At the very base that we cannot take. Our brothers are convincing the headquarters that the garrison should be recalled. As soon as that happens, either our creatures or the mercenaries will get to the general. - Yes, the circumstances were beyond us. It is not your fault. But what is it then? - I lost five more brothers. Humans deceived us. Or rather, one of them... one... woman. She is so immune to telepathic pressure that we... practically did not see her. She burned four brothers easily and quickly, as if they were matches. She struck a matchbox and burned them! - Woman? - Yes. Young and... very strange. - Interesting. - The engineer paused for a long time. - I don't understand why the psionics let her get so close? In addition to telepathic gifts, they have keen hearing and smell, and finally, sight. What happened to them before this woman attacked them? - Nothing. That's the point. But none of them had time to react to her attack. And the fifth of the dead brothers was unable to defend himself with either telepathy or a firearm. The woman snuck up on him and killed him a hundred meters from the gate. - Doubly interesting. Where are the people now? - They came in. But the strange woman was not with them. Only another one, older, ordinary. - Plus a stalker, a mercenary and the Old One, right? - We killed the mercenary. The Old Man and the stalker are left. And the second woman. - Are they coming? - Not yet, they're standing still. They're probably waiting for a woman who's fallen behind. - I'll go up and take a look at them. The Engineer took a crystal from the altar stone, placed it in a frame attached to a silver chain, and hung the precious pendant around his neck. Then he walked to the far end of the hall and pushed an inconspicuous door in the wall. Behind the door was a small platform in front of a spiral staircase. In principle, the Engineer could have installed an elevator in the castle long ago or even torn down these ruins and built a normal building in their place, but the owner was not interested in this. Psionics did not need housing. The entire Zone was their home, and the Thirteenth Sector was their office or bedroom, in a word, a secluded place in the house. So the ruins of a medieval castle, somehow ending up in this place, were a purely symbolic refuge for the Engineer. A place for meditation. When the Engineer entered the castle, all the psionics immediately understood that the owner wanted to be alone, and did not bother him with trifles. They did not even look into the castle. No one, except for the Ghost. But even he never went up to the roof of the only surviving room, a hall with a leaky ceiling. To be honest, not because he was afraid. He simply had nothing to do there. If it was necessary to find out what was happening in the remote corners of the sector, the Ghost could come to the place in person. And he was not interested in admiring the surroundings. What could attract or surprise him in the dull landscape? Absolutely nothing. And yet, if the Ghost had risen with the Engineer and observed the owner's reaction to what he saw, the assistant would have been very surprised. And if he had read the Engineer's thoughts at that moment, he would have been even more surprised. Darkness was gathering over the sector, but the poor lighting did not prevent the Engineer from seeing what he wanted. He "looked" not only with his eyes. The abilities granted by the Zone allowed him to perceive much more than the usual senses. The people described by the Ghost did enter the hidden sector, but they were no longer standing at the gate, but were slowly moving forward. Surprisingly, they were moving exactly along the path leading to the castle. The Engineer saw these people for the first time, but their psi-shells were very familiar to him. He had studied the Old One's mental aura back in October, when the stubborn mercenary, having stumbled upon two sources of the Call, accurately calculated which was the false target and which was the real one, and headed for the underwater gates of the bunker. At that moment, the Engineer did not attach any importance to the mercenary's intelligence. He was sure that the catfish would devour the arrogant upstart and close the matter without the intervention of psionics. But everything turned out differently. And when the Old One reappeared in the field of vision, the Engineer had to take a closer look at his aura. The Old One was very strong, but there was nothing unusual about him. However... what was unusual was that after six years in the Zone, this man had not changed one iota. More than enough to take note of the Old One. And since he was already trying to disrupt the operation for the third time - more than enough to destroy him. The companion of the retired mercenary did not seem so interesting, although she also had special potential. Not as much as the Old One, but still. And she also appeared in the Engineer's field of vision not for the first time. As for the stalker, the Engineer had it all figured out. No, not because this Scout was a typical representative of his "shop" or an average example of a Zone person. The woman was also average, but at least she interested the Engineer a little. But this fruit - no. Not at all. The reason why the stalker was of no interest to the Engineer was that this person did not have a single secret. What interest is there in dummies if you are not a baby? The second woman was not yet visible on the horizon, the Engineer did not catch any signs of her approach in the mental ether either, but he did not hurry. He remembered the Ghost's words well. The strange woman knew how to hide her presence from telepaths. "Not from me! I'll wait until she enters the sector, then we'll see what this thing is." There was every reason for such iron confidence. The Engineer was originally the best among psionics, and his abilities were significantly enhanced by the crystal hanging on his neck. "It's a hundred to one that I'll detect this woman's psi-shell in the mental ether before I see her with my eyes! Even a thousand to..." The engineer cut himself off mid-sentence and stared into the twilight in surprise. "One to a hundred" won. The woman came out of the misty circle of the gate, the Engineer saw her, but was unable to telepathically detect her presence. "What a trick!" The engineer involuntarily touched the crystal. "Who is she?" The artifact seemed to take the question personally and kindly gave a hint. A second-long fragment from that very same obsessive episode that kept popping up in his memory flashed before the Engineer's mind's eye. The episode with the first victim. No, this woman was not her. Even mysticism has its limits. This was a different person. "Although she looks very much like my first victim. No, it's still not her. Firstly, it can't be, and secondly, she's much younger. And she doesn't look like her, what nonsense?!" It suddenly dawned on the Engineer... The strange feeling that he recognized this woman still had a right to exist. He had actually seen her before, and it had happened at the very moment that the Engineer could not forget. This mysterious young stranger was the second woman whom the Engineer's first victim had prevented from being devoured by shielding her with herself. Partly everything fell into place. The coincidence was, of course, interesting, but it no longer had a mystical touch. "It's a small world," as people say, and what can we say about the Zone? A tiny patch of land, where a huge but relatively constant number of people and creatures crowd. To meet an old acquaintance at least once every six years - what's surprising about that? The engineer shook his head in displeasure. What he had been doing for the last few seconds was simply called: self-deception. “Meeting an acquaintance... once every six years... a small world... all this is bullshit, delirium! Meeting and getting to know an old friend is not at all the same as meeting and getting to know a person you saw briefly, out of the corner of your eye, through a curtain of dust! To see, to get to know, and to be absolutely sure that you are right. No mystical touch? What is there? Another hint, a foggy clue from the depths of a memory illuminated by a Flash? Why did I then decide that devouring this girl would be too much? Because I knew her in a past life? Because for the former me she was someone close, a relative? So what? There is nothing left in the present me of that person, except for a nickname. I have not been human for a long time, and all these sentiments about feelings and attachments, as they say, are of no use to me! But... I would not gobble her up now either. Why?” The Engineer tried to concentrate and still mentally "get" the strange woman. The result was quite impressive - zero. Neither his own talent, nor pumping the crystal helped the Engineer break into the woman's mind. It was unpleasant to admit, but she turned out to be stronger than the leader of all the psionics of the Zone. The master of all thinking beings and creatures! No, of course, only her defense was stronger, in terms of psi-attacks she would hardly be able to bypass the Engineer. And her defense only held up under the onslaught of one psionic. If three or four attacked, they would crack the nut, no doubt! The Engineer was sure of all this, but such confidence did not cool his curiosity at all. “I want to meet her face to face and talk,” the engineer slapped the stone railing of the roof. “And if she has a new variant of psi-talent, I simply must attract her to my side! Such personnel cannot be thrown away. This is not some hidden psionic who did not even make a good spy! By the way, I never met him, but now it seems too late to regret it. But this woman is not just a psionic, she is a completely different variant. We must definitely meet!” Now the Engineer was not disgusted with himself, since he was thinking relatively clearly, or at least no longer deluding himself. However, in the depths of his consciousness there was still a smoldering ember of doubt. Something was wrong with this situation. There was some kind of catch hidden in it. The Engineer did not understand what it was, but he felt that there was one. "No specific thoughts, no need to get too worked up," the Engineer reined himself in. "We need to go down. In the fresh air, the imagination runs wild too much. And instead of sharpening, the intuition becomes distorted. It won't take long to develop paranoia." Down below, the Engineer was met by the Ghost again. - Did you see them? - Yes. - Any thoughts? - Yes. Don't interfere with them. Let them reach their goal. - Don't interfere? - The ghost was extremely surprised. - What should I do? Help? - Nothing. If the group managed to pass all the tests of the path, it means that the Zone has some considerations about these people. I'm interested to know - what are they? - The Zone? What does the Zone have to do with it? Ostapenko sent the group. And now I'm sure he sent them specifically for your soul. The general realized that he was dealing not with the idiots from the Borderland or with the fat cats from the mainland, but with us. He understood and sent this strange group here. Now that they've entered the sector, I feel that death is not only coming from the mercenary. Everyone is dangerous! The women, even the stalker. Ostapenko is planning some kind of particularly sophisticated provocation, I'm sure! "This is a rare case when General Ostapenko and I can completely coincide in our plans," the Engineer said calmly. "I will give in to his provocation and allow the people to approach the castle. They intrigue me." - Perhaps the general was counting on such a reaction. Otherwise, it is very difficult for me to explain his actions. After all, he sent his best agents straight into hell, to pure destruction. This is not done without a plan thought out many moves ahead. - Perhaps. But then I should take a look at these people all the more. And even talk to them. Otherwise, we won't understand the enemy's plan. - Maybe you need someone specifically from this group? Together, these people are too dangerous. We both felt it. It is difficult to predict how everything will end if they come here. Think again, Engineer, think carefully! Unfortunately, all the brothers died, but there are three more sisters in the castle. We are much stronger on our territory than in the outside world. The four of us will stop these people. - No, there is no point in disturbing the sisters. I have thought very carefully. Let the people try to get there. If they succeed, then there is someone among them for whom it is worth the risk. And I feel that this is so. And therefore, our meeting will definitely end well, I am sure. - What if you're wrong? - Then I will personally destroy them all. Do you doubt that I can do it? - No, I have no doubt. - Then go and take care of the general and that stubborn base in Dityatki. And I will meet the people and lead them to the light of truth. Or to death. As it turns out...

It would be logical to expect that right at the gates the group would be met by a serious barrier of controllers or local especially well-fed mutants, who would easily and casually interrupt the mission of the walkers. In the worst case, some powerful anomaly could have played the role of a filter. But neither the first nor the second happened. The group freely left the ring of greenish fog, stopped and, trying to get their bearings in the new area, turned their heads for about five minutes. It was dangerous to stay in one place for so long even in the regular sectors of the Zone, to say nothing of the enemy's bridgehead, but for now the Thirteenth Sector was only putting moral pressure on the walkers. There were certainly anomalies, mutants and controllers nearby, but people couldn't find them, no matter how hard they tried. Perhaps there was some kind of buffer zone around the gate, the boundaries of which were invisible to the walkers, but well known to the locals. Or perhaps there was a path through the sector, similar to the one the group had walked along, and on which only those who knew where the starting position was could appear. In general, one could guess for a long time, but it would not change the essence in any case. For now, people experienced only quite understandable moral discomfort. After all, the landscape that met the group was seriously different from the usual views of the Zone, and, understandably, it was not different for the better. The landscape had hardly changed. Behind the group rose a wooded hill, and to the left and right stretched a field. But the vegetation had changed in many ways. The same pine trees stuck out on the hill, only with charred trunks and dead red needles, but ahead, where in the outer Zone rose an iron rampart of bent structures of a radar station warped by anomalies, there was an impenetrable forest, in the twilight somewhat resembling the wall in Chernobyl-2. Enormous, gnarled oaks with powerful, gnarled branches grew so close to each other that even small creatures like jerboas or rats would have had difficulty squeezing between their trunks and intertwined branches. The eerie fairytale forest was no higher than the dead pine forest on the hillside, but it seemed to hang over the walkers. And it was at least one hundred and fifty meters away. And it also seemed that the bizarrely curved branches of the mighty oaks were covered with black foliage. It would seem nothing surprising, as is well known, even dried leaves fall off the oak reluctantly, but the crowns of these giants were not dry. Moreover, they moved and periodically suddenly became more lush. Upon closer examination, it turned out that the branches of the fabulous trees were not covered with foliage at all. It was a huge flock of crows. The birds desperately fought for warm spots, every now and then took off and immediately clung to the branches again. Interestingly, the crows made almost no sounds. At first, Andrey thought that these creatures were also dead, like the dogs of the outer gate guard, but soon he understood what was going on. The bird hubbub was drowned out by the howl of a strange wind blowing incessantly from the southwest. The strange thing about the wind was not only that it blew not in gusts but constantly, like an air flow in a wind tunnel, but also that it was not felt at the ground. The movement of the air could only be guessed at by the swaying of the branches, and it could only be felt if you stretched your hand up, and even then it was perceived only by the fingertips. Apparently, this wind made the crows fly up every now and then, but then immediately take up free branches again. Roughly speaking, it blew them away from their habitual places, and at the same time carried away their guttural croaking far away. At first Andrey decided that it was good, there was no need to worry about the attack of the "batmen", who would hardly like such non-flying conditions, but soon Lunev realized that he was wrong. The mutants did not pay attention to such trifles. On the contrary, they used crazy air flows as an accelerator. But this understanding came a little later, and for now the sector continued its psychic attack, without resorting to the services of its inhabitants. The amazing fairytale forest attracted the eye, but the instinct told me that this part of the landscape was not the most interesting and dangerous thing near the main gate of the sector. Andrey made a great effort to tear his gaze away from the gloomy thicket and turn his attention to the field to the right, covered with low mounds. In the twilight, the field resembled a stormy sea. The mounds seemed to be moving like high waves, and in the same direction as the strange wind was blowing. The resemblance to the sea was complemented by wisps of fog or haze, similar to foamy breakers, spreading over the tops of the hills, but absent from the ravines. Smoky whitecaps also seemed to flow along with the mound-waves, lazily swirling and periodically forming strange, swaying compositions. It even seemed to Andrey that several times the haze formed weightless figures of people, or perhaps humanoid monsters - due to the fluidity of the landscape, it was impossible to properly examine these "sculptures". Andrey glanced at the open area to the left – flat as a table – and noticed that the tall grass and bush islands on it leaned in the same direction as the mounds on the right. Since this part of the area ran up against the western horizon and was not framed by forest, the flat field was lighter than anywhere else. He could see even the farthest edge of the field, where some movement was beginning to emerge. Andrey raised his machine gun and used the optics to examine the creatures scurrying in the distance. The four-power optics did not help to discern details, but overall it did the job. Lunev saw the main thing – the creatures had two legs and were gradually approaching. Andrey lowered his weapon and glanced at the field once more. There were many obstacles in the way of the creatures. Despite the seeming harmlessness of the field, it was generously strewn with anomalous areas. This was eloquently evidenced by the piles of bones, rotting remains and scatterings of those very large artifacts that the Mechanic had spoken of. A light slanting rain, which, by the way, did not react to the wind at all, as if there was no wind over the field, clearly indicated the location of the anomalies. Over some areas it scattered as water dust, and in some places it twisted into spirals. In places, not reaching the ground by about a meter and a half, the raindrops suddenly changed direction and, parallel to the ground, rapidly flew away somewhere into the gathering twilight. One could only guess how the bipedal inhabitants of the sector were going to get through this minefield. Perhaps all these gravitational anomalies had no effect on them, or perhaps they knew the secret path. In addition, Andrey was personally concerned with the question of how the creatures were going to maintain the exact direction to the gate. After all, the field with anomalies "flowed" in the same direction as the field with the mounds. And in general it seemed that this entire mysterious sector was slowly flowing somewhere towards the north-eastern horizon. Perhaps this was just an illusion, but so far Lunev had not found any confirmation of the unreality of what was happening. After three minutes of observing this "fluidity" Andrey looked down, and it seemed to him that the ground under his feet was also flowing somewhere, and the walkers were smoothly sliding along with it. The overall picture was slightly spoiled by the "sorcerer oaks", they were leaning wherever they wanted, but if you look closely, all their tops were also facing the north-east. “I’m going crazy,” Scout muttered quietly, indirectly confirming that even if the “routine” was still an illusion, it was created by the sector itself, and not by Lunev’s imagination. “Turn around,” suggested Tatyana. The advice was sound. The wooded hill behind them looked like an unwavering cliff in an illusory stream. Neither it nor the charred pines on its slope flowed or leaned anywhere. Most likely because this hill was also a borderland in Sector Thirteen, partly belonging to the more stable outside world. The view of the hill had a beneficial effect on perception. When the walkers turned to the northeast again, neither the mounds, nor the field covered with bushes, nor the ominous oak grove were floating anywhere. The illusion had dissipated, leaving in the soul of each of the walkers only a feeling of incomprehensible uneasiness. To get rid of the unpleasant feeling, Lunev even pulled out the amulet from his bosom and squeezed it tightly in his fist, and remembering the Mechanic's amulet, he grabbed it in his hand as well. Close contact with the artifacts helped to muffle the anxiety, but Lunev was unable to get rid of it completely. Most likely because the anxiety was not induced from the outside, but formed in the subconscious. The longer the group was in the sector, the stronger this personal anxiety became. Illusions, anomalous radiation and other local factors had nothing to do with it. “I don’t like this,” Andrey involuntarily voiced the conclusion from his doubts. - And you thought there would be paradise here? - Scout grinned. - A normal hellish landscape. - That's not what I'm talking about. “Why isn’t anyone attacking us?” Tatyana muttered, involuntarily clinging to Andrey. - That's exactly it. - Maybe they're waiting for us to move away from the gate? - Scout suggested. - There, on the left, there are so many bones and scraps... it's awful! And here it's clean. “Are we going to stand here?” Tatyana asked apprehensively. “Let’s just go through the mounds,” Andrey said. - Where are we going? - Scout grimaced. - You promised that as soon as we got to Thirteen, it would dawn on you why the hell we came here. Did it dawn on you? - Not yet. But nothing has happened yet. Some stimulus is needed for enlightenment. Something has to happen. - Awesome! - Scout nervously adjusted the belt of his machine gun. - We're waiting for a sign! Or for them to eat us. At the end of the fifth minute of the group's stay in the Thirteenth Sector, the "sign" finally happened. And not just one. Unfortunately, it turned out that the mutants do not have any taboo on mischief near the gates. The local fauna finally decided that the guests had admired the views to their heart's content, and began to crawl out of all the cracks to say hello. Or have a bite to eat. The reason for the five-minute pause remained a mystery to the group. First of all, from the same gray and low sky as in the outside world, an impressive flock of "batmen" suddenly swooped down, and it was pure chance that we managed to dodge their claws. The wind accelerated the flying creatures to a decent speed, and they simply did not calculate the moment when to release those very claws to grab people by the withers. The rapid fire following the flock that whistled over their heads cost the mutants one lost relative, who, instead of soaring upward with everyone else, continued his horizontal flight and crashed with a crash into the intricate branches of the “fairytale” forest. The result, frankly speaking, was nothing. Especially considering that Andrey and Scout spent almost a full magazine on anti-aircraft fire. That is why, when the flock, having performed a dead loop, dived into the air flow again and rushed at the walkers, the trio preferred to simply fall to the ground at the last moment. Once again left with nothing, the creatures were not upset at all, but with the same enthusiasm rushed off on another turn. “We need to get into the forest!” shouted Scout, watching the pack’s maneuvers. - We won't even go a meter into it! - Andrey objected. - We're going to the mounds! “And there, where the pine trees are... closer,” suggested Tatyana. - No. - Andrey waved his hand to the right. - There, run! The companions were soon convinced that Lunev was right. From the pine forest on the hillside came chilling howls, heavy footsteps and the cracking of branches. Whoever was howling there, the walkers had no reason to meet them. Meanwhile, nothing frightening was happening between the mounds. Fog was still swirling on the peaks, darkness was gradually thickening in the hollows and ghostly silhouettes periodically appeared. Andrey admitted that these silhouettes were not just a play of wind and fog, but something like ghosts, but howling and flying material creatures seemed more dangerous to him now than ghostly ones. As the group approached the hills, the imaginary ghosts disappeared, turning into ordinary wisps of fog, and Andrey once again thanked his intuition for the right hint. Scout expressed another gratitude to Lunev's professional intuition when the pack of "batmen" adjusted their course and again tried to attack the targets, who seemed to be running towards their deaths. When the pack was already a few meters away from the fugitives, Andrey literally knocked his comrades off their feet and made them fall in a narrow hollow between two mounds to the left of the route. The feint led to the pack unexpectedly finding itself in the path of a three-meter earthen rampart. Most of the monsters managed to soar into the sky, but a couple still crashed into the obstacle and plowed two deep furrows on its top. Lunev, who jumped to his feet, easily finished off the knocked-out creatures and gestured for his companions to rise. - Next time, don't wait for me to shove you into a trench like this! Fall on your own, preferably in different directions! - Into the mud! - Scout grumbled, shaking himself off somehow. - Thanks! You could have shouted, why push? - You'll survive. Did you hear what I just said? - I heard, I'm not deaf. Falling in different directions. Where are we going? - Directly. - Oh, but I can't, - Tatyana suddenly said in fear. - It's like there are weights on my legs. Andrey! What is this nasty thing?! - Damn it! - the Old Man said, casting a quick glance at his companion's legs. - Scout, get a knife, help! There were no weights hanging on Tatyana's legs. Where would they come from and who would hang them? In fact, she was hobbled by huge worms that had wrapped around her legs up to the knees. The half-meter-long fleshy worms crawled out from under the turf plowed by the "batmen" and crawled in all directions: in search of either new shelter or food. Using their knives skillfully, her companions quickly freed Tatyana from the living chains and, grabbing her under the elbows, carried her away from the dangerous ravine. Tatyana's feet barely touched the ground. When her comrades put her back on the ground, Tatyana involuntarily screamed in pain and almost fell. The close acquaintance with the worms was not in vain. "If you're standing, there are no fractures," Scout concluded. "They just bruised you, maybe dislocated you. Try taking a step." Tatyana took a step and screamed in pain again. - "Joker" will help, - Andrey said confidently. - In half an hour everything will be over. In the meantime, we'll carry him on our backs. Scout, first. “The carriage is here.” The stalker turned his back to Tatyana and sat down slightly. - Maybe I should do it myself? “Sit down, don’t be shy,” Scout encouraged. “You’ll have time to do it yourself.” - Faster! - demanded Andrey. - The "Batmen" are returning! Tatyana sat on Scout's back, but the trick did not lead to what Andrey had expected. From the height of her new position, Tatyana saw what her companions had not seen, and immediately reported it to Stary. - Andrey! You can't go forward! There are some... bugs there! - What other bugs?! - Andrey riskily climbed the nearest mound, assessed the situation and barely managed to jump into the hollow. At the exact moment he landed, a flock of annoying "batmen" flew over the heads of the walkers again. Lunev, to be sure, did not try to keep his balance and fell on all fours. Scout also managed to fall to his knees, and Tatyana ducked, and the mutants' claws again did not find their targets. "Someday they'll figure out that they shouldn't be patrolling, but just hovering," Scout muttered as he stood up. "Then we'll get the full treatment." - They'll get it too. - Andrey turned around uneasily. - There's no way forward. These mounds... they're not really mounds. Here they're almost all dead, but further down they're very much alive. Let's go back. - Back, so be it. - Scout turned around. - Wait, what are you talking about? Who's alive? These mounds? "These aren't mounds, they're shells," Tatyana said in a breaking voice. "Giant beetles! They're so heavy that they can't get over each other, so they're stuck in this field." - Plague! - Scout stared at Lunev in surprise. - Really? Is there a traffic jam on the bug ring road? - Something like that. - Andrey pointed to the clearing with the gate. - Let's go back. "Something attracted them," Tatyana explained. "They came, but the first ones couldn't move on, and the last ones still can't overcome the pull... I don't know what. Maybe the gates? So it turned out that the first ones died, and the last ones are still alive, devouring the corpses of those who got stuck ahead." - So the worms in them are like maggots? - Scout winced. - A shitty place, even worse than the clearing with the dead. "We'll have to go through the field with anomalies," Andrey concluded. "It's too bad we lost time, it'll be completely dark soon." - I told you so! - Scout gave the passenger a little bounce, trying to make her more comfortable. - You were talking about the forest. - Lunev stopped at the edge of the anomalous field and found the "batmen" circling in the sky with his gaze. - I hope we can at least get rid of them. - Oh, we'll get into some kind of "Funnel," Scout muttered discontentedly. - The only joy we'll have is that we've broken away from the "batmen." There won't be any other joys left, that's for sure. - Don't croak. - Lunev pointed his thumb behind his back. - Transfer! - Don't you trust me? - On the contrary, I trust you. You are a stalker, so lead the way. - Lead the way! - Scout helped Tatyana saddle the new pacer and massaged her shoulders. - I'll lead the way, what questions, but I'd like to know where to? At least what to aim for? - We need to go around this dense oak grove, and then... we'll get our bearings. Most likely, we'll have to head towards Pripyat. - For what? What Pripyat? - Well, if we were walking through a normal Zone. - Ah, I see. Okay, we'll do it. So, for now we'll hold on to the oak grove. - But don't go close to her! - Are you afraid that the branches will catch you and drag you into the thicket? - Anything is possible. - No, Old Man, hold your horses! - Scout grinned, but somehow nervously, even a little scared. - Don't make up horror stories, not in a summer camp, I bet. The sector is strange, who argues, but not that strange! - I warned you, and then... The old man didn't have time to finish his sentence. The air around the walkers suddenly crackled like torn parchment, and at the same time filled with a bass buzz. Only Tatyana could have assumed that the group had been attacked by a swarm of anomalous bumblebees, but she didn't assume anything like that. At least, out loud. Her companions especially didn't suspect that the strange sector was to blame for the new problem. The nature of the sounds was perfectly clear to them. It wasn't bumblebees that were buzzing around, but heavy bullets. Andrey turned around abruptly, almost throwing Tatyana off his feet, and raised his machine gun, but did not shoot. The shooter who had emerged from the gate had not been aiming at the walkers. All his bullets had hit their targets, but these targets were not people, but invisible creatures that had surrounded them in a tight ring. In full accordance with the laws of the genre, the blood on the bodies of the wounded monsters and the bodies themselves, if a successful shot killed the creature on the spot, became clearly visible. In appearance, they were typical bloodsuckers, but again, like everything in the Thirteenth Sector, they were larger than their relatives in the outer Zone. However, this did not make them any more invisible. As long as the monster remained whole and unharmed, it was impossible to see it. Even in the rain. But if you managed to mark it, the wound became an excellent target. "Thank you, good man!" Scout shouted, raising his machine gun. "Hold on, you bastards!" - Scout, stand down! - Andrey shouted, pushing Tatyana down. - I'll mark them, you finish them off! Lunev threw the machine gun over his back and took out knives. - What are you thinking of? - Scout was indignant. - With knives?! Are you out of your mind? "Don't bother him!" the shooter shouted from afar. "Do as he says!" The scout barely managed to restrain himself from making another remark, nodded to Tatyana to stand behind him and raised his machine gun. Lunev meanwhile took a couple of steps forward, spread his arms with knives to the sides and... closed his eyes. From the outside, it might seem like pure madness – going out against superior enemy forces with two knives, and closing your eyes. But if you think about it, this was the only way to fight invisible men. You can’t shoot someone you can’t see from afar. But you can feel him, feel his warmth, catch the movement of air and smell his scent by getting right up close to the enemy. And closing your eyes is, of course, not necessary, but still useful. To more fully concentrate on the five senses that remain in the ranks. Andrey did not mark the first bloodsucker, but killed him on the spot just a second after he closed his eyes. Lunev stabbed two more almost simultaneously, making a spectacular throw to the right. As soon as Andrey pulled out his knives, two short bursts cracked dully, and heavy bullets hit the targets Lunev had marked. And the Old One was already on the other side of Scout and Tatyana, who had frozen in the center of the invisible ring. Judging by the sensations, three monsters were attacking him at once, but nothing good came of such an attack, and could not have come of it. The creatures only got in each other's way and immediately paid for it. Andrey ripped open the insatiable wombs of two of them from the navel to the throat, marked the third and sent it with a kick outside the conditional ring. There, Scout calmed the marked creature. The shooter took out two more bloodsuckers. Lunev heard the bullets slapping into their bodies and the monsters falling heavily into the squelching mud. The shooter, like Andrey, was not bothered by the mutants' disguise. Only it turned out that the shooter was even more talented. He could accurately determine the location of the invisible ones not by touch, but remotely. “If he’s a controller, that’s not surprising,” Andrey thought. “It’s just not clear why he’s helping us.” In principle, this shooter could have been the same controller who had brought the Mechanic to the group's aid an hour earlier. Then his participation in the fate of the walkers was partly explainable, but overall the controller's motives for taking the side of the people still seemed unclear. However, Andrey had no time for reflection now. The situation remained very difficult. The bloodsuckers had not retreated yet, moreover, the restless "batmen" were again targeting the walkers. Scout briefly reported this. The battle on two fronts had become too difficult a task even for Lunev. Andrey fought off two more mutants and finally decided to open his eyes. He was quite satisfied with what he saw. A flock of flying creatures was circling above them at a decent altitude and was in no hurry to descend. Most likely, the controller who had come to the rescue was also to blame for this. Lunev turned to the gate. In the ensuing darkness, the greenish fog around the entry point glowed faintly, but did not really illuminate the shooter's figure. Andrey could make out a human silhouette, and that was all. Lunev could not make out the ally's dimensions, much less his special features. He tried to remember the voice, but Andrey could not say for sure who it belonged to. Lunev suspected that Olga was the shooter, but in that case, the version about the telepathic ally would collapse. Olga had some deviations from the norm, Lunev had known this for a long time, but until now the deviations had been limited to immunity to hypnosis, and not to the possession of this talent, and in such a suspicious form - practically, like a controller. That is - a mutant! Andrey returned "to his sheep", but it turned out that there were no bloodsuckers nearby. A dozen ugly corpses and one still alive mutant remained blindly on the battlefield. Lunev did not waste time and energy on the wounded creature. Hiding the knives, he ordered Scout to put Tatyana on his back again and pointed to the distant edge of the dense forest, barely visible in the thick twilight. Scout nodded and, putting on his night vision goggles, carefully moved along the edge of the field, which was completely covered with anomalies, bones, remains, as well as large and especially powerful artifacts. The last moment turned out to be quite important for Scout; his professional stalker reflexes kicked in, and he managed to grab at least a dozen artifacts in passing. “Don’t you have anything to do?” Andrey asked, breathing heavily. - Habit! - Scout spread his hands. - What, I can't?
