I can throw it away. - The main thing is not to miss the anomaly. What's on the left? - Where? Oh, nonsense. "Springboard". It's far away, don't worry. Look, "Night Star" is glowing over there. There's always some distance from the artifact to the parent anomaly. It's about fifty meters from us to "Star", which means it's at least the same distance to "Springboard". Listen, Old Man, wait a second, I'll raise "Star". - Scout, calm down! - Well, the last time! You're doing well, the general pays you as much money as you want, but I only get my salary. I earn my own bonus. Do you feel sorry for him? - That's not why we came here. - I understand, but you should also understand, you won't find such serious artifacts anywhere else. Do you know how much I'll get for them? Ten times more than for ordinary trinkets. Consider it the same as for the "Jokers"! “The last time,” Andrey gave in. “With a bullet.” The stalker chuckled happily and ran towards the greenish glow of the artifact. - Scout, stop! - came from afar. - Get back! Andrey turned towards the voice, but a new sound made him return to his original position. The sound was made by Scout. He screamed, flew up a couple of meters and disappeared into the pitch darkness. The NVG tried with all its might, but Andrey couldn't really see where the treacherous "Springboard" had kicked the hapless stalker. The anomaly's blow was strong enough to throw Scout a hundred meters away. Or to smear him on some obstacle closer than a hundred meters. Lunev set the sensitivity of the night vision device to maximum and still saw something. The scout was rummaging around in the mud almost in the middle of the field, and signs of powerful anomalies could be guessed to the left and right of him. Another anomaly was hiding a little further from where the stalker fell. The scout was clearly not himself, but most likely, he was safe - his arms and legs obeyed him weakly, but did not hang like whips, and his head kept falling onto his chest, but rose again and again. About half a minute later, the stalker recovered from the knockdown and tried to get back on his feet. He managed to do it quite easily, but not because he had finally come to his senses. He was helped. Several figures in oversized robes appeared around the stalker, unnoticed. The creatures easily took the weapon from Scout, grabbed him tightly by his white arms and dragged him, skillfully maneuvering between the anomalies, to the opposite edge of the field. - Damn it! - Andrey grabbed the machine gun and even took a step towards the field, but came to his senses in time. Rushing to help Scout was pointless. Firstly, Andrey would never have managed to pass by the anomalies so deftly, and secondly, he would have been too late anyway. The enemies disappeared into the darkness literally a minute later. Where to look for them there, only God knew. And even then, it was unlikely. “Or maybe the controller, who is still following our tracks, knows.” Andrey lowered Tatyana to the ground, turned to face the south and demonstratively placed the machine gun on the crook of his elbow. Lunev tried to show with his whole appearance that he wanted to sort things out with the uninvited ally here and now. “What are you planning?” Tatyana whispered. - You'll see. - If she doesn't want to come with us, then there are reasons for that. Why force her? - Her? - Andrey looked at his companion in surprise. “Well, yes,” Tatyana responded with the same surprised look, “Olga.” - Do you think it's her? - Didn't you hear her voice? “He heard, but didn’t recognize it,” came from the darkness. “I wanted it that way.” Something seemed to switch on in Lunev's head, and the completely unfamiliar, moreover, genderless voice suddenly acquired familiar intonations. Now Andrei recognized it perfectly. It really was Olga's voice. “I don’t understand.” Lunev frowned at the girl who had appeared from the shadows. “What kind of tricks are you doing?” “I don’t know myself.” Olga sighed. “It just happens that way.” - And for a long time? - As soon as I climbed the hill, it started. Even on the other side. At first I thought I was going crazy, then that I was turning into a controller. Now I just don't think about anything, I do what I can. - Mechanic... is that your job? - Mine. Plus I calmed down another controller who was leading the mercenaries. - And who commanded the dead? - He left for the sector right after you. Didn't you notice? - Not really. You got a clever one. But you went too far with the Mechanic. - What else could I do?! I still don't understand how to put a zombie back down. Honestly, I would have put him down if I could! He's not a soulless creature... even though I now have these control tricks. I honestly tried to calm him down, but I couldn't. “No one can, that’s the problem,” Andrey said, still sullen. “If anything happens, don’t wake me up, okay?” - Yes. - Olga hesitated. - What are you saying?! Bamboozled! - No need for such wishes. - Andrey smiled sadly. - Just in case. - Don't make a witch out of me, okay? - Olga said offendedly. - If we hadn't come to this sector, I would have stayed with my interests. - Exactly! - Andrey said, suddenly snapping his fingers. - Bull's eye! It was a revelation! “What are you talking about?” Olga asked, slightly frightened. - Don't ask yet, I won't be able to answer clearly anyway. - Lunev waved his hand. - Haven't you learned to read minds? - No. - Why did you screw up so badly? - Andrey looked at the girl ironically. - But I can feel the path! - Olga assured him hotly, not catching the irony. - It's here too, only it leads... not like it did with us - from the Zone to the gates of the hidden sector, but from the gates to some special place, like the center of the local anomalous energy, you understand? - No, but I think that's where we need to go, - said Andrey. - One thing confuses me. Why did the controllers suddenly leave us alone? Did you scare them off or did they do it on purpose? “But they took the scout,” Olga objected. - He flew to them himself. - Then I don’t know. - But if you think about it? You should now understand their train of thought. - I don't know, honestly! Maybe they want to lure us into a trap? - It's clear without you. Why - that's the question! Why the controllers need you - it's still understandable: to re-educate you, send you back as a spy... a classic of the genre. And what the hell do they need us for? Me, Tatyana, Scout... After all, from the point of view of the locals, we are mediocrities, walking cutlets, nothing more. Olga just silently spread her hands. “But I’m interested in something else,” said Tatyana. “Did General Ostapenko know or not what would happen when we entered the Thirteenth Sector?” - Let's say he guessed, so what? - Andrey looked at his companion with interest. - Nothing special. - Tatyana showed a tired smile. - Well, except that I personally feel like a worm on a hook. And the most offensive thing is that no matter how much you wriggle, the end has already arrived. “A fish and a bait,” Andrey calmly remarked. - Does that make it any easier for us? Whether the general catches his fish or not, we'll all be wasted anyway. That's what you say, right? - Not us, the military, but that's what they say, it's all true. - Andrey met Olga's gaze and nodded to her to take the guide's place. He turned back to Tatyana. - How are your legs? - They almost don't hurt anymore. - Tatyana sighed wearily. - And even if they did hurt... Why would a worm need legs?

The creeping twilight, thickening clouds and heavy smoke turned the citadel into a glowing island in the middle of a dark sea covered in impenetrable fog. And under the smoky surface of the conventional sea lurked many monsters, to whom any sharks, octopuses and sea snakes were not even fit to be soles, or rather, to be horny growths on dirty heels. On the one hand, the combat mission of the warehouse complex defenders became more complicated, on the other hand, it remained almost unchanged. With full illumination, the work was also carried out mainly in areas, and also almost without pauses; from the continuous roar of explosions, people were almost deafened. And yet, the approaching night seriously worried the fighters and commanders in D-3. In the event of a breakthrough or a new landing on the territory of the citadel, the mutants could crawl along the dark corners and subsequently cause significant damage, organizing sabotage sorties in the rear of the people. The power of autonomous generators could simply not be enough for all the searchlights and "cobras" of the perimeter of the defense, plus for lighting the inner territory, and night vision devices (by the way, not very effective in smoke) were available only to special forces and only every second "cleaner". After an audit of the "special" reserves of the warehouse complex chief, NVGs were found for all the fighters and for the stalker strike force, but they were still not enough. At night, everyone who was able to hold a weapon had to remain sighted. But every cloud has a silver lining. The same audit helped find additional reserves in the form of almost a thousand American phosphorus grenades, as well as two ancient but never fired heavy infantry flamethrowers, ten hundred-liter containers with ready-made napalm and several bags of thickening powder for making a combustible mixture. Officially, all this was considered obsolete, removed from service and prepared for write-off, but in fact it was absolutely serviceable and ready for use for its intended purpose. Bibik, who had initially brandished a pistol in front of the warehouse major's nose, cooled down a bit upon seeing this wealth and even promised the thieving rear serviceman to replace the execution with sending him to the embrasure as a private. - But remember, you spoiled brat, we'll sort out the problems, I'll remember everything! - the colonel finished his fiery speech. - And the fact that you sold the incendiary mixture for the LPO, and most importantly, that you lied that you didn't have anything like that! What, you cretin, don't you understand that lives are at stake now? Money is beside the point! - Well... I'm not a chemist! - the major muttered, turning pale and breaking out in a cold sweat again. - I didn't know that you could use that from flamethrowers... I thought the mixture had a different composition! And these... TPO... I thought they weren't working. They're older than a priest's dog, they were written off back in the Soviet era! And napalm... I thought they filled bombs with it, I thought it wouldn't be useful in barrels. - I'll make you roll one of those barrels out the gate with your hands, open it, tip it over and set it on fire, and then we'll see what this jelly is good for! - Bibik swung his pistol, as if he was going to brand the major on the forehead with the handle. - Is that even possible? - The major pulled his head into his shoulders in fear. - I didn't know, I'm a rear-echelon soldier! - And what about grenades?! You don't understand this subject either? Answer me, you warehouse crook! - I understand. But they were banned... - They're also old, right? The markings say 2005. Do they have an expiration date like kefir? Has the phosphorus inside gone sour? - No, but... I have an order to write it off! The deputy for armament of the MIS personally signed it. "Due to the danger of storage and use..." - Why did you keep them? Why didn't you send them where they belong? Better shut up, you bastard! Get out of here! Privates! - I... - The major hesitated. - I'm an officer... you can't... me like that. - Should I call the general and demote you? Or should I rip your head off and blame it on the mutants? - Yes, privates. The demoted warehouse chief sighed and trotted off to help the soldiers clear the basement of the requisitioned ammunition. Pashkov, who had been present at the dressing down that Bibik had given the "warehouse crook," immediately appointed the major as the crew commander of one of the two almost museum-quality infantry flamethrowers "TPO-50." The lieutenant ordered one weapon to be installed in the most dangerous western direction, and the soldiers dragged the other onto the roof of the northern warehouse. Five light flamethrowers were evenly distributed among the other warehouses, except for the southern one. Bibik did not hold back the grenades either, ordering three to be handed out to each soldier on the walls. “Grenades are good,” Pashkov noted, “but with flamethrowers... it’s just a waste of time. What’s a ton of napalm? The heavy ones use up twenty liters per shot, the light ones only three, but their effect is... so-so. We’ll fight off three attacks, no more. And reloading will be a pain. There’s only one compressor, no spare cylinders, while you’re pumping it in... These flamethrowers are so last century. We need a hundred Shmels here.” - Where can we get them? But we'll give the beast a run for its money with the ancient weapon, Pashkov, don't worry. We'll fill everything around with the prepared mixture, and in the meantime we'll prepare a new one and pump it into the cylinders as the containers empty. We'll have time for everything. And if we don't, we'll prolong the pleasure with grenades. – There will be smoke from napalm and phosphorus grenades... - Isn't there enough smoke around now? You can't breathe without a mask, even in basements. - This one is different. Napalm produces black, thick and acrid smoke, damn, it's just awful! And phosphorus produces poisonous smoke, we ourselves could suffer, get poisoned. - What do we need masks for? Everything will be fine, Pashkov. It's better to walk around in gas masks without taking them off than to shoot at random into the whole wide world without them. Wait, we'll run out of the incendiary mixture with grenades, we'll think of something else like that. We'll sprinkle everything here with berthollet salt and powdered sugar, and sprinkle sulfuric acid on top. It will burn no worse than napalm. We'll maintain the ring of fire as long as our strength allows. It's the most effective tactic. - We ourselves wouldn't get fried in this ring, - Pashkov snorted incredulously. - And where are you going to get so much of this... potassium chlorate? And sulfuric acid. - I'm just saying this as an example. - Bibik patted Pashkov on the shoulder. - Work, lieutenant. And one more thing... don't throw too much at the southwest gate and leave a hundred grenades for emergencies. - Another breakthrough? - The lieutenant looked inquisitively at Bibik. - We'll see then. The colonel grabbed a phosphorus grenade and climbed out of the "secret caches" into the fresh air. The inner courtyard of the citadel was not as smoky as outside the warehouse complex, but it was almost impossible to breathe without a mask. Bibik had a clear idea of what would happen here when the citadel was surrounded by a ring of fire, but he saw no other way out. The question of a breakthrough had not yet been decided. The general had been trying to prove to headquarters for three hours that the bombing plan was no good, but it was unclear how the debate would end. But as for new attempts at an assault, everything was, on the contrary, perfectly clear - they would happen, and they would have to be repelled regardless of the prospects. Even if Ostapenko failed to defend his point of view and the D-3 garrison had to break through, some time would be spent preparing for the operation, which meant that one or two assaults would have to be endured, no matter what. There was a light haze even in the headquarters bunker; the colonel was not exaggerating in his conversation with Pashkov. The exhaust ventilation was working hard, but the entrance door was barely closed, and the smoke was entering the room much faster than it was evaporating. Bibik's appearance on the threshold of the headquarters coincided with a surge of general indignation about this. The colonel suspected that his superiors were irritated not by the smoke tickling their throats, but by the insoluble contradictions with the headquarters, but Ostapenko took out his anger on his subordinates, masking the real reasons with dissatisfaction about the smoke. - Will this walking ever end or not?! Why are you scurrying back and forth like shuttles, letting in smoke? There's nothing to breathe! “I am Shuttle 1, may I land?” Bibik tried to defuse the situation. - Come in and close the door. - Ostapenko waved his hand. - Sergeant! - Me! - the signalman looked out from the adjoining room. - Go out and keep watch on that side. Turn everyone away until the colonel comes out. Is that clear? - Yes sir! “At least it will air out,” the general grumbled, turning on the exhaust fan to maximum. “There’s no draft at all!” - The voltage is weak, - Bibik sympathized. - The searchlights are taking a lot, and the "cobras" have lit up all around the perimeter. I ordered the internal network to be switched to backup power. As a last resort. - Electrician! - Ostapenko looked at the colonel with displeasure. - Don't you have anything better to do? - Why? I also broke into a secret warehouse here, found a ton of napalm, flamethrowers and a thousand phosphorus grenades. So now we're alive. At least we'll hold out until tomorrow evening. I mean... we can hold out. “Good news,” Ostapenko concluded without the slightest hint of joy. “And the NVGs? You seemed to be looking for them.” - Found some too. The major has another warehouse here under the warehouses, it has everything, like in Greece. And everything is written off. Klondike! - Who would have doubted it? - Ostapenko nodded. - The night vision devices will soon be very useful to us. Did you confiscate everything? - Until the very end. But you are counting on them in vain. They don't work well in the smoke. - Now there is smoke, in two hours it is gone... - The general hesitated for a second. - In an open field the wind will quickly disperse it. Nothing is working out, Bibik, with me and the headquarters. You are stubborn as sheep! Break through, and that's all. I tell them about probable losses, they tell me about the general situation. Supposedly I don't know what is happening throughout the Borderland, and I cannot assess the full complexity of our situation. I mean - their, the headquarters' situation. The entire system of isolation of the Zone is under threat. - Is that all? - Bibik shook his head incredulously. - How many breakthrough points were formed, besides ours? - Another eleven, - the general answered gloomily. - And everyone is gushing. For us, the headquarters at least admits it, things are going more or less well. In the sense that we are useful, and if it weren’t for the failures of the defense in other places, no one would bother us. But that’s the problem, no one has been fighting on other fronts for a long time. Enthusiasts like us were found only in Bragin. But an hour ago, everything ended there too. Only we are resisting. In short, the issue is resolved. We will think about how to get out with minimal losses. - Just in case, I've already thought this through, - Bibik reported with a sigh. - There's no point in breaking through to Andreyevka a second time. We need to try the southwestern route. First, move along the canal to Stepanovka, then supposedly to Priborsk, but in fact, immediately after the bridge, turn right, go down the Khocheva to Teterev and rush to the other side through the Khomichev ford. - Difficult route. - But it's reliable. There will always be a forest to the west - we'll set it on fire, the fire will cover it, and to the east there'll be a canal, then a river, a swamp and a highway. Visibility is in full view, we'll see any creature a kilometer away. That way we'll slip between the fire and the water. “I need to think about it.” Ostapenko unbuttoned his collar and rubbed his neck. “My throat is sore from the smoke, I have no strength.” - Well, that can be solved. - Bibik took a flask out of his pocket. - Take a sip. Five stars. Any irritation will disappear. - I don’t drink, Bibik. - I'm not suggesting you drink it, just take a sip. “Go ahead,” the general gave in. “Did you by any chance get through to Stary?” He took a sip, winced, and handed the flask back to the colonel. “I don’t understand it myself,” admitted Bibik. “The connection seemed to be established, he answered something unintelligible, I started dictating instructions, and then everything suddenly cut out – and that was the end of it.” - Where are they now, haven’t you had time to find out? - I'm telling you... - Bibik shook his head. - No, I didn't have time. The colonel, as if by chance, also took a swig from the flask, and more thoroughly than Ostapenko. - So we can't count on Stary, - the general concluded. - So be it. Start preparing for the operation. I'll join you soon. And look, Colonel, everything must be taken into account down to the last detail. We won't have a third attempt, we're not holding a weightlifting championship. - We'll do everything in the best possible way, - Bibik was inspired. - We'll arrange such a fireworks display, the animals will remember it for the rest of their lives. And we'll go through the fiery corridor ourselves. - You're an optimist, Bibik. - What is, is. - The colonel took the flask out of his pocket again, but immediately hid it. - It will come in handy later. A sip for the victory. Ostapenko opened his mouth to make some remark about his subordinate's latest optimistic phrase, but he didn't have time: his remark was interrupted by a phone call. The general pressed the answer button, listened to a short message, and, without answering, turned off the communicator. Seeing the expression on the commander's face, Bibik stood up abruptly and straightened his uniform. - Are we leaving? - Yes, - Ostapenko muttered through his teeth. - Immediately. The headquarters is raising attack aircraft. The pods will carry heavy ammunition. – Are the stakes rising? - The thirteenth penetration point has been formed in Dymer. The first one outside the Borderland. So far, no nasty stuff has come out of it, but the command does not intend to wait for this "happy" moment. Belarusian "Grads" will be used against northern objects, assault helicopters against western ones, and the east and south - the territories most saturated with mutants - will be treated with thermobaric munitions. “We’ve come to this,” Bibik sighed once again. “Do you have at least an hour to spare?” - No more. - Then it's still bearable. We'll get through it, Mr. General. Not the first time..

The hardest thing to get used to was two things: light and darkness. In the sector they were not the same as in the outside world. It would seem, what could be unusual about visible radiation or its absence? The thirteenth sector easily proved - a lot. As in the outside world, the light of the stars here almost did not penetrate the cloud cover, but if a gap suddenly appeared in the clouds, the area under it was filled with a sparkling glow, more like a sparkling fog. At this moment it became light as day, but visibility did not improve. The curtain sparkling with billions of sparkles blinded, not allowing to see even the nearest objects. Once, having got into such a fog, the walkers almost broke their legs. It is good that Olga got her bearings in time and forced her comrades to stop literally on the very edge of a deep ravine. When the clouds again covered the gloomy canopy, the darkness was so thick that night vision devices could not cope with it, even with the backlight turned on. It remained to rely again on the readings of detectors, hearing, smell and inner instinct. The devices, by the way, were of little help either. In the Thirteenth, electronics generally worked poorly. Communicators fell silent, night vision devices almost went blind, and all sorts of motion, anomaly, and heat detectors lied shamelessly. Three times the anomaly detectors raised a false alarm, and the motion sensors went off every ten seconds for no apparent reason. However, with such a play of light and darkness, the expression "apparent reasons" lost all meaning. Perhaps some of the local inhabitants really did cross the walkers' path, but they did not see or hear it. There was something wrong with the sounds in the sector, too. A constant background noise was created by the wind, ruffling the bare branches of young trees and bushes. These sounds were no different from similar ones in the outside world. But in addition to the noise of the wind, Thirteen was rich in many other sounds: from chilling howls, wild laughter and growling of unknown creatures to loud creaking and grinding sounds made by the intertwined branches of old trees. Footsteps, the squelching of dirt and even gunshots were lost in such a sound accompaniment, as if in a muffler. The shots, however, were practically silent anyway, since Andrey was the only one shooting. Neither Olga nor Tatyana used weapons. Olga had enough to do on the trail, after the ravine she concentrated exclusively on her role as a guide. And Tatyana was fiddling with the devices, and it was impossible to convince her that survival in the sector was more important than studying it. And Andrey did not even try. He could cover the shrunken group alone. Tatyana was completely immersed in her favorite business, and this was the most reasonable option. Distracted from the eerie surrounding reality, she did not panic and did not interfere with walking at the pace that the situation allowed. The only thing that did not suit Andrey was that Tatyana constantly tried to get off the trail. But this problem was easily solved - the Old Man tied the lady to his belt with a rope, which is mandatory for every walker. Olga attached another leash to the belt. The ligament could have been a hindrance in an emergency, but the risk was justified; without the ligament it was impossible to stay together. And, to be honest, the further the group moved along the path, the less Andrey believed that an emergency would arise. The path led through strange places, anomalies raged all around and thousands of mutants went berserk, but the walkers were reliably protected from the anomalies by the "Jokers" (in the hidden sector, they seemed to have become even stronger), and for some reason the mutants around only growled and circled. It was unknown whether the artifacts scared them off or whether there were other reasons. Andrey suspected that Olga had some merit in this. It was not that the mutants treated her with respect, but they were clearly afraid of her. Although everything could be explained more complexly. For example, if the relatively calm journey along the inner path was ensured by the one who was in charge of this entire sector. One way or another, the monsters were clearly not kept at a distance by gunshots. When the centuries-old oaks to the right of the path stopped groaning and grumbling, Olga pulled the rope and stopped. Tatyana did not understand the signal and, having flown in, almost knocked the guide off her feet. It was good that Andrey managed to support both of them. "The road goes down," Olga said. "There'll be something like a river down there. Only it looks... like lava. If we're not scared, we'll get through." “I won’t be scared.” Tatyana understood the hint correctly. “Will it be an illusion again?” - No, it will be lava. - Olga pointed ahead. - Look closely, do you see the crimson glow? It is rising right above this river. - A-a... how come... we won't burn? Why? - Because we'll go across the bridge. There's not much left of it, we'll have to jump over holes, but we can get through. The main thing is to gather up courage. Andrey, do you remember what happened in the underground city? This is a reflection of that very crevice in this sector. – It turns out that if we project this place onto the outside world, we are somewhere in Pripyat? “In the area of the bus station,” Olga said confidently. - How do you know all this? - I have no idea. When I entered this sector, it was as if something switched in my head. I know a lot about it now. About it, and about the Zone as a whole. - And why did we come here? - No, I don’t know that. Ask Ostapenko. - I would only ask where to get it? We will have to figure it out ourselves, so help us find the answers. - Ask, I will help if I can. – If only I knew what to ask! - You said it just dawned on you. Didn't you? - So far, everything is at the same level. Solid questions. For example, why did you bring us here, why does your master need our group? - To whom? - Olga stared at the Old Man in surprise. - Are you trying to play Scout? To blame everything on me? It won't work, Old Man. It won't work! Ostapenko sent us here, and the Mechanic brought us here. As for the owner, I have no idea at all. Why did you suddenly decide that there is some kind of owner in the sector? - Isn't that so? - Andrey tried to catch Olga's eye, but in the darkness the staring contest didn't work. - Now you know everything about this sector. So tell me, who's the boss here? - I don’t know anything about anything important! - And Ostapenko believes that all the recent events, starting with the Call, which gathered mutants for modification and people for transformation, and ending with the current attack from the hidden sector, are all parts of the Main Enemy's big plan. A plan for expansion under the cover of the Zone. - Great! - Olga laughed. - But isn't Ostapenko's data too precise? Maybe it's part of HIS plan? - I don’t understand. - Andrey frowned. - What are you hinting at? - For nothing! - Olga grinned vindictively. - Figure it out yourself, you're smart. - Excuse me, - Tatyana intervened, - but could we... leave this topic. For a while. I have a bad feeling about this. - Me too, - Olga agreed. - Let's go to the river. Everything will become clear on the other bank. The path ends half a kilometer from the bridge, in some ruins. - In the center of Pripyat? - Andrey clarified. - Something like that. To the left, where the pool is. - Bad place. - The Old Man thought. - There are plenty of problems there even in the regular Zone. The territory of the "Monolith". “I could be wrong,” Olga warned. - Monolith? - Tatyana asked with interest. - Is it stored there? That same magic crystal that supposedly created the Zone? - Allegedly, - Andrey emphasized. - No one has seen this crystal. Not even the Monolithists themselves. But the energy in that place is crazy, that's true. Maybe there is a connection with what is located here at the end of the path? Olga, what do you think? - We'll see. - The girl shrugged. - There's complete order with the abnormal energy everywhere here. Don't you feel it? - Thanks to the Jokers, not really. - So, everything is ahead. At the end of the path you will definitely feel it, and the "Jokers" will not help. I sense a powerful source of energy. It is there, in the ruins. At the same time, we will see who really sent us here. "Don't distort things," Andrey said sternly. "We won't meet Ostapenko in your ruins. If only because the general is in D-3 now, that's for sure." "It doesn't mean anything," Olga said. "You can get anywhere from the Thirteenth if you want, you've already seen for yourself, you just need to open the transition point correctly. Apparently, the source of anomalous energy is what opens such points." - No, you're still wrong. In the Borderland, only exit points from the sector are opened, not entry points, - Andrey objected, not too confidently. - That's right, it's done so that the enemy can't strike back. But for himself, your beloved, your Main Enemy could easily leave a loophole. What reason would he have to stay in the outside world if something suddenly goes wrong? Logical? “We’ll see,” Andrey said in the same tone as Olga a minute ago. “Lead the way.” The fiery river that Olga promised was not as scary as Lunev imagined. The blazing hot barrier was quite narrow, and the dark red lava in its bed was barely smoldering, heating the air to about seventy degrees, no more. Of course, it was not very pleasant, but even Tatyana, who was poorly prepared for such tests, could have endured such a "roasting". But what Olga called a bridge, in fact, turned out to be only the skeleton of such a structure. It was possible to walk along the narrow stone beams thrown across the lava river only on the condition that the person walking would use nerves of steel as imaginary rope-railings. Or use mental balance as a balance pole. - Will you pass? - Andrey turned to Tatiana first. - Weak link, huh? - Tatyana smiled tiredly. - I'll pass. I did acrobatics as a child, I keep my balance well. - I thought so too. - Olga glanced sideways at the Old Man. - But for some time now I prefer not to hang over fiery rivers. Even though I love fire. - It's all in the past, - said Andrey. - I promise, if you break now, I'll pull you out right away. We're roped together, after all. Do you want me to go first? - No way! - Olga shook her finger at the Old Man. - I can handle it! She really did it. The sight of the lazy crimson river did not frighten Olga, the heat and stench did not affect her peace of mind in any way, and the guide's confidence seemed to be transmitted to the entire group. Only in the middle of the narrow bridge, when a bright flash suddenly flared up near the surface of the river, Olga froze for a moment, but, having made sure that there was no danger, she moved on. Several more flashes and low fountains of sparks tried to confuse the walkers when the group had almost reached the shore. Andrey suspected that if the river were hotter, the spit of red-hot lava would have flown much higher. Then the walkers would have gotten it in full. But now the fiery river looked sleepy and was in no hurry to burn people. “What a nice little stream,” Andrey grinned as he stepped onto the bank. “If only it didn’t stink so much of hydrogen sulfide that filters don’t help, we could have a picnic on the bank.” “I don’t like this.” Olga rubbed her temple and looked around. “Something’s going to happen, I can feel it with my ass.” - Miss, what kind of expressions are you using? - The old man chuckled again. - You are in decent company. Now. - Seriously, Old Man. - Olga turned her head uneasily. - Someone is watching us. And I don't like it. It's not an animal, it's someone else. - Controller? - Stronger. He looks from afar, sees only our silhouettes, but I still feel his gaze. - And after this you will say that they are not waiting for us? Olga answered something, but her words were drowned out by the roar of collapsing beams and bridge supports. None of the walkers understood why the structure suddenly collapsed. It just stood there, stood there, and suddenly fell apart. “Well, well,” Andrey muttered in surprise, looking at the stone fragments slowly sinking into the lava. “What if we were on the bridge?” “There you have it, two,” Olga said in the same tone as the Old Man and pointed to the left, upstream along the fiery river. “Did a dam break somewhere?” Above, the river was boiling and spreading along both banks in a wide fiery stream. And all this flaming ugliness was rushing towards the walkers. - Legs! - Andrey shouted quietly. - As high as possible! - Right along the path. - Olga waved her hand in the northern direction. - Further on, there's a hill. There are ruins on it. In the ruins... a spring. - "On a black-black hill stood a black-black house..." - Andrey recalled a childhood horror story. - It's not a raid, but a journey through the unconscious. Right, Tatyana Sergeyevna? - What do you mean? - Tatyana tore her frightened gaze away from the rushing wave of fire and looked at Andrey with confusion. - I missed it. - Run, I say, don't slow down! - The old man took her hand and pulled her along with him. The light from the spreading river of fire finally provided relatively normal visibility, and soon the walkers were able to discern the ruins Olga had promised. The sight was quite something. On top of a low hill stood the ruins of a real medieval castle; with half-ruined towers and walls made of huge stones, the remains of tiled roofs over the buildings in the courtyard and a crooked gate sunk into the ground. It lacked a moat around it, but the fiery river played its role well, so in this regard everything was in order. And instead of lightning tearing apart black clouds (they could have given the picture a completely fairy-tale look), there was a soft white glow above the ruins. Not too intense, but enough to create normal illumination of the upper floors and the roof of the castle. - There he stands, waiting. - The old man pointed to a human figure on the roof. - Him? - How should I know? - Olga tried to see the figure through the scope, but didn't have time. - He disappeared somewhere. “We’ll figure it out,” Andrey promised. “Enter through the gate? What do you feel?” - They're locked, can't you see? There's the entrance, through the hole in the wall to the right of the gate. - Where are the dogs hiding? - Andrey, following Olga's example, also assessed the situation with the help of the scope. - Toothy ones. - Cerberus. - Olga checked the magazine. - You can't take them out with simple bullets. - Cerberus? - Tatyana didn't understand. - What do you mean? With two heads? And what kind of bullets do they need, silver ones? - Armor-piercing! - Olga explained with a snort. - And they have one head. I mean, one for each. You don't take it so literally. - Sorry, it's from excitement. Everything looks very unusual, like in some fairy tale. In a scary fairy tale. This is my first time in such a place. - It's the first time for everyone. Now be quiet, please. Old man, I'll go ahead, cover me. - No problem. But I don't think they'll touch you. - I know. I'm worried about you. I'll see if I can lock them up somewhere. “No need to worry,” suddenly sounded, it seemed, two meters above the heads of the walkers. Andrey and Olga, as if on command, raised their weapons, but did not shoot. There was no one to shoot at. Theoretically, people should have seen a particularly intelligent "batman" or a controller in the sky, sitting in some exotic carriage harnessed by flying creatures. But they saw no one, although there was plenty of light on the slope. A fiery river illuminated (and thoroughly baked) from below, and a mysterious white glow shone from above. Andrey was inclined to probe the zenith with a couple of bursts, but Olga stopped him. She put her hand on the barrel of his "val" and pointed with her eyes at the roof of the building, which was visible through the gap in the wall. A dark figure towered on the roof again. It was hard to believe that the voice belonged to this person, but Andrey decided to trust his companion's instincts and lowered the weapon. – Is he broadcasting through a megaphone? - I don't know. I doubt he even uses a mobile phone. - Seriously speaking? - How should I know? Ask him! "I will answer any questions," came again, as if from the sky. "Go. The dogs will not touch you." - Very kind of you. - The old man changed the magazine and handed another one to Olga. - "But it's better to hide the lard." These are armor-piercing. The last ones, mind you. Shall we go? “Let’s go!” Olga exhaled. Andrey grinned and nodded to Tatyana. As usual, she didn't even touch the weapon, only clenched her fists resolutely. Andrey pointed to the holster, but the lady shook her head. - I'm a bad shot. I'll just waste the bullets. And I might even hit you. I'd rather give it to you. If necessary. - Then stay between us. Olga, go ahead! The Cerberuses, indeed, did not touch the people. They only growled menacingly and lined up in a living corridor on both sides of the breach in the wall. Two creatures met the group outside the castle, eight more inside. The dogs were like Rottweilers, only the size of a yearling bull and with teeth like sharks, except that they were not in several rows. The Cerberuses bared their teeth and spat saliva, a couple of times the right-flank ones even twitched, as if they were going to pounce, but they did not do anything to the people. Moreover, when the group reached the last pair of beasts, the dogs suddenly calmed down and even sat on their short tails, as if embarrassed to wag them. Or afraid to do so in front of their master. Olga suddenly stopped and fearlessly extended her hand to one of the dogs. Andrey froze, fully prepared to pierce the beast's hard skull with an armor-piercing burst, but nothing of the sort was needed. The dog lay down and condescendingly allowed the girl to stroke his ears. “I wonder if you can tickle the bloodsuckers between their tentacles?” Andrey muttered through his teeth. "There are no other creatures here," Olga answered calmly. "Some controllers are hiding somewhere and... that one... with the megaphone." - Ah, well then of course. - The old man chuckled. - Then it's a different matter. We can consider the castle clean. “We’re going this way.” Olga stopped at the entrance to the building. A milky white light was pouring out of the doorway. Andrey knew without a hint that the walkers were heading this way. In addition to the light, an invigorating wave of energy was coming from the depths of the building. The air seemed electrified. The electricity poured into it made every hair stand on end and prickled the skin with millions of invisible needles. The walkers carefully entered the building and stopped at the threshold. The interior of the room they found themselves in was extremely laconic. In the middle of the spacious hall there was a stone, resembling a squat barrel from a distance, and some kitchen utensils, covered in a thick layer of dust and ash, were lying by the far wall. Between the stone and the pile of junk, with his back to the guests, stood the owner. Judging by the general outlines, this was the same person the walkers had seen on the wall. However, it was difficult to say with complete certainty. The outline was distorted by the white glow enveloping the person. It blurred the contours and made the owner's figure look like a large tongue of wavering flame, black in the center and white at the edge. The spectacle was captivating and even hypnotizing. Andrey had to make a serious moral effort to overcome the obsession and, raising his machine gun to his shoulder for insurance, take at least another step forward. Feeling or hearing that the Old One had come out of his stupor, the owner of the castle grinned and placed his hand on his chest. The glow became less intense. The owner slowly turned around and looked at the walkers. The creature's pale face reflected practically nothing, and it was impossible to read either thoughts or emotions in his black, white-less eyes, but still the owner gave himself away. Andrey almost immediately realized which of the three guests interested this special controller more than the others. The enemy glanced at Tatyana's face, lingered on Stary for a few long seconds, and fixed his gaze on Olga. Andrey slowly lowered his weapon and glanced briefly at Olga. She stood like a statue, motionless, not even breathing, and stared at the controller. No, she had not succumbed to his hypnosis. She looked with wide-open eyes, her gaze was motionless, but a thought shone in it. It was as if she recognized this creature and was now trying to understand whether this was a figment of her imagination or a real hint from her memory. Tatyana touched Andrey's shoulder. She quietly moved closer to Stary, and now Andrey could see her out of the corner of his eye. With a barely noticeable movement of her head, Tatyana pointed at Olga, and then at the controller. At first, Andrey did not understand what his companion was trying to tell him. And then suddenly it was as if he had been doused with cold water. Despite the unhealthy complexion and creepy look, this creature bore a clear portrait resemblance to Olga. Well, just like a brother. Or a father. “Well done Ostapenko, well done son of a bitch!” Andrey thought. “He figured out the enemy after all! And he said that he didn’t have anything except bare theory. And he does! Not only does he have control over the entire situation, he can also twist it around as he pleases. I’ll take my hat off to him if I get out. But first I’ll give him a whiff, so that Mr. General will finally understand that people are not pawns or creatures from the Zone. That you can’t play with them like that, harshly and blindly. Even if they have dubious relatives!” - Hello, daughter, - the controller said quietly. - Glad to see you. I had no doubt that you would make it. After all, you have such good genes..

To put it mildly, Olga felt lousy. As if she had been stuffed alive into a coffin. As long as the monster remained silent, there was still a chance to explain to the unknown tormentors that a mistake had occurred. But the controller's words became thick nails hammered into the lid of the coffin. Now there were no chances left. None. It was terribly offensive. Offensive and bitter. Such a powerful shock immobilized Olga more effectively than any suggestion. Everything that suddenly fell on her turned out to be unliftable, like a construction slab. It fell right on Olga, crushed her, and now there was no point in struggling. For Olga herself, and for anyone else. She had no chance of getting out from under this slab on her own, and the others should think carefully about whether they should pull her out, as in the cases with those zombies. Maybe it would be more logical to just scratch the years of life and the last name right on the slab? The burning resentment that had been spreading inside gradually went away somewhere, giving way to an icy emptiness that froze her soul and thoughts. Now Olga felt like a mammoth in the permafrost. Or a creature in a grotto of an underground city, one of those whose contours showed through the icy walls. Olga saw everything that was happening around her, heard words, but did not understand their meaning. The world around her became something distant and unreal for her. Neither touches nor remarks addressed to her were able to tear her from the icy captivity. Both the controller and the Old Man tried in vain to rouse her. From now on, Olga did not care how this damned raid would end, who would win the unprecedented war, who would get the outer Zone and the whole world. For Olga, nothing existed anymore except the icy emptiness in which she was destined to remain forever...
...The pause had clearly dragged on. Olga didn't answer the controller, as she had gone deep into herself. Andrey waited as long as he could, and finally decided to take the initiative into his own hands. - You, dad, didn’t make a mistake by any chance? - You can see that it is not. - The controller turned his otherworldly gaze to Lunev. - It is not easy for Olya now, but it will pass. Do you have any objections? - Against what? - Andrey grinned. - Against the reunification of your family? No, I have no objections. Just one question: are you sure she needs it? – Otherwise she wouldn’t have come here. - You, daddy, don’t seem to quite understand why she came here. - I know everything, mercenary. Ostapenko sent you. But he didn't tell Olga why. And he didn't tell you either, right? - I figured it out myself. - Andrey pointed the machine gun at the controller's stomach. - Give you an ultimatum. After all, you're the one in charge of everything? - Depends on what. - The controller's face showed no emotion, but his voice sounded mocking. - What do you think? “I’m thinking of killing you as a precaution, and then finding out what you were doing here.” "Are you sure the weapon will help you?" The controller turned his gaze to the machine gun, and the magazine suddenly "by itself" came off the weapon. The trick impressed, but did not frighten. True, only the Old Man was not frightened. Andrey felt Tatyana pressed against his back. He lowered his machine gun and slightly tilted his head to the side, examining the controller. “Well, you’re not too confident in yourself either, dad,” Lunev said. “Why?” “You’re wrong, mercenary,” the controller replied calmly. “And stop calling me daddy. I’m an Engineer.” - I'm the Old One. - Andrey nodded. - So we've met. Let's talk, since it's happened this way? You can guess the text of my ultimatum. I won't voice it. - Then what do you want to talk about? - About anything. About the vicissitudes of fate, about the meaning of life... yes, about many things. I am very inquisitive. And you are too. Otherwise, you would not have let us in here. - I'm only interested in her. - The controller pointed at Olga. - You can go, I'm not holding you back. - Oh, no, that won't do. - Andrey, without looking, held Tatyana back as she quickly stepped back. - Three of us came, three of us will leave. But until Olga is ready, we will have to fill the gap. - She won't leave with you. Her place is here. - The dialogue isn't going well. - Andrey grinned. - Let's have a duel? - You will lose. - Oh, I doubt it. If you could kill me, you would have done it long ago. But you waste time talking, so something is bothering you. Maybe it's this? Andrey pulled the Joker out from his bosom. The engineer barely reacted. Almost. There was a millimeter of movement backwards, and this fact did not escape Lunev's attention. He stepped toward the mutant and demonstratively threw the machine gun aside. The engineer lowered his hand, which was pressed to his chest, and the hall was once again illuminated by a milky-white glow. In its radiance, the Joker suddenly began to glow, but crimson-red. This glow enveloped Andrey and began to spread across the dirt floor, gradually pushing the moon milk to the periphery of the hall. Lunev did not see it, but a crimson glow enveloped both Tatyana and Olga. Three red spots around the walkers grew slowly but steadily. A minute or two, and the red glow could win back a significant part of the room from the white one, no less than a third of it. "Get back!" the Engineer ordered. "If our artifacts touch, it could be a disaster." - Is it the first time? - Andrey chuckled again. - For the Disaster Zone, it's like a spring flood, a familiar situation. - We will die! - It's not the first time either. What are you afraid of? You died a long time ago. When you turned into a beast, you died as a human. Now you'll die as a beast. Who knows, maybe you'll turn into something more decent? A rat, for example, or a toad. - Don't talk nonsense, Old Man, stop. - The controller raised his hand and tried to stop Andrey with telekinesis. Lunev was prepared for such a turn of events. He certainly felt the pressure, as if there were pood weights hanging on his legs, but such an obstacle could not stop him. Andrey gathered his strength and took a couple more steps forward. - This is not a machine gun for you to take apart. - Lunev pulled a Finnish knife from its sheath. - Back off, or I'll hurt you. - Good. - The engineer lowered his hand. - What do you want? You won't just leave even with Olga. Am I right? - Right. I need answers. You promised. So keep your promise. Why were Kazakevich's laboratories needed? - Started from afar. - The engineer grinned and was silent for a moment. - Okay. I'll answer. They implanted anomalous abilities into people. Without changing their appearance. Does that mean anything to you? - No dumber than creatures like you. That is, hidden mutants were created there. Like a fifth column. And for what? For a full-scale expansion to the mainland? But why hidden, and not like you? “People with mutant traits can live outside the Zone, but people like him cannot,” Tatyana whispered. "She's right," the Engineer agreed. "A smart woman. You're lucky, mercenary." "Your mouth doesn't give compliments," the Old Man snapped. "Why do you need the Black Angel?" - It's even simpler with that. The Zone is too inert to expand at the pace I need. I found an alternative. The Black Angel could stretch its tentacles hundreds of kilometers around in a matter of weeks. - The Black Angel was the Zone's disease, it was killing it. - Now I know that, but back then it seemed like a lucky find. Besides, it couldn't have extended its tentacles here, into the hidden sector. So, with the destruction of the outer territories, the Zone wouldn't have died and would have gradually regained its possessions. “You are too calm, Engineer,” Andrey abruptly changed the subject. He took another step towards the monster. - Stay put, Old Man, we're just talking. Why should I worry? - Your horde is fighting with people in the Borderlands, and you are talking to me. Are you so sure that your new campaign is already won? Why? What is the main focus of your new discovery? What are "contact spots"? - "Contact spots"? You could call them that. Through them, my troops will be able to penetrate into the outside world, straight to the mainland. - And what if there is a counterattack? - It won't. Do you know what a diode is? The corridors work on the same principle, you can leave this sector through them, but you can't enter it. This is a precaution. I've been experimenting with them for a long time. Previously, I had little success: unstable corridors could be opened either inside the Zone or not far from it. But gradually I found a way to open them anywhere. You may have heard of rings of green fog in Termakhovka, Dymer, Polesky and even on the outskirts of Kyiv. These were the auras of the exit points from the Thirteenth Sector. - Through which new mutants, for example, flying ones, penetrated into the outside world? But until now you have been testing new fighters of the future army only inside the Zone. - That's right. - And you weren't afraid that people would figure out your plan and come up with a weapon against the new species? What were you counting on? To crush them with mass? - That's what happened. At this very moment, the creatures obedient to me are finishing off your friends in the Borderland and will very soon go beyond its borders. - Where they'll die. - Andrey snapped his fingers. - You're lying, Engineer. The creatures can't live outside the Zone, you confirmed it yourself. But you're still calm. That means there's another reason. What is it? - And you are quite smart for a human. - The engineer slowly moved to the right. - Well, I will tell you the whole truth, you will have no one to report this to anyway. The opening of "contact spots" between this sector and the outside world will lead to the fact that at the moment of the next Emission, the anomalous energy of the Zone will gush from the spots, essentially mini-zones, into the outside world. It will turn those who survive the metamorphosis into mutants. The rest will either die immediately or a little later, wandering around for some time as living corpses and rotting on the go. - A cheerful prospect. - That's how it was during the first Flash. - The engineer shrugged. - That's how it will be now. Due to the serious curvature of space in this sector, a hundred transitions to any point on the planet can be created on a hundred hectares. You just have to calculate everything correctly so that the contact spot is formed not in some Uryupinsk, but in the center of Kyiv, Moscow, London, and so on. - And you can calculate it? - Yes. Termakhovka, Dymer and Poleskoye were a kind of triangulation, and the outskirts of Kyiv were a test shot, a check of the calculations made using the system I developed. - He swung with all his heart, nothing to say. - Andrey measured the Engineer with a skeptical look. - Do you have enough strength? - Don't worry, Old Man, I can handle it. - I'm not worried. - Andrey grabbed the knife more comfortably. - Because you can't handle it. I won't allow it. - Who do you think you are, little human?! - the mutant suddenly flared up. - Do you really think that you can stop me?! - Otherwise, why are you pulling my leg, Engineer? You're afraid to fight until help arrives! The Old Man jumped to the left and blocked the Engineer's path to the secret door in the wall. The monster slowed down and again tried to stop the man with a telekinetic blow. Andrey lost his balance for a moment, but did not fall. Choosing the moment, he stepped forward sharply and stabbed with a knife. The blade slashed the mutant's cheekbone, but not a drop of blood flowed from the thin cut. The Engineer jumped to the left, about three meters, and raised his hand again. - I suggest you stop for the last time, mercenary. I have the gift of foresight. Our fight will end in disaster. Everyone in this room will die! If you don’t value your own life, think about the life of your woman! - I thought! We won't be able to part ways peacefully, Engineer, even if you promise to forget about the outside world, seal all the corridors tightly and swear to live here as quietly as a mouse until the end of time. I won't leave here without your head! - No, you are still an idiot, I thought too well of you. - The engineer suddenly raised his hand to the ceiling and abruptly lowered it. A large stone fragment that fell out of the vault crashed to the floor a step away from Andrey. If the floor had not been dirt, the stone fragments could have flown in all directions as sharp shrapnel and seriously harmed people, but nothing of the sort happened. The stone block sank halfway into the ground, only shaking it noticeably. Andrey managed to keep his balance again and attacked again. What sets jiu-jitsu apart from some other popular martial arts is that the arsenal of the fight includes not only techniques for countering attacks with cold weapons, but also techniques for working with them. Andrey mastered both sections equally well, and therefore his second attack reached its target. The knife went deep into the Engineer's neck, but again did not cause any noticeable harm. The monster did not pay any attention to the wound at all. He jumped back, this time back, to the wall itself and made a new pass with his hand. The kitchen utensils that had been gathering dust on the floor rose into the air. Andrey quickly realized what was about to happen and managed to shout, without turning around, but addressing his companions: - On the floor! He himself not only fell, but also rolled under the cover of the stone in the center of the hall. The charge of many knives, forks, copper plates, jugs and heavy cups whistled literally half a meter from the floor and crashed down with a roar on the wall with the entrance breach. Without waiting for the enemy to come up with a new nastiness, Andrey jumped out of cover, like a jack-in-the-box, and rushed at the Engineer. This time, in addition to the knife, he prepared the "Joker" for the fight. He clutched the artifact in his fist and prepared to carry out a "two", first with the knife, then with the fist with the thing clutched in it. The engineer clearly didn't like this option. He suddenly tore off his cloak and used it, like a medieval swordsman, as a flexible shield and then as a weapon. The fabric twisted into a bundle suddenly became unusually heavy and elastic and lashed his hand with such force that Andrey dropped the knife. It felt like a powerful blow from a rubber truncheon with a steel core. The Old Man did not refuse to continue the attack and still pinned the Engineer to the wall, but this time with a direct kick. He had only to deliver a decisive blow with his fist to the enemy's chest - a large milky-white crystal was hanging on the Engineer's chest - but at that moment a loud scuffle was heard behind the Old Man, and then an unfamiliar voice called out to Andrey: - Leave him alone, Old Man! The first thought was: Olga came out of her stupor and made a choice in favor of her newly found daddy. The second: no, Olga is not so stupid and sentimental as to recognize this monster as her parent, she understands perfectly well that this is no longer a person, but a creature in his skin. The third: the voice was high-pitched, but it did not belong to Olga. However, it did not belong to Tatyana either. Andrey turned around abruptly and assessed the situation. Olga was being held tightly by three furies. In appearance and equipment, they resembled the Engineer, only instead of a white hedgehog, they had fashionably tousled hairdos on their heads. In all other respects – pale skin and black eye sockets – the ladies resembled the Engineer, like sisters. At least from a distance. It was clear that the furies understood which of the guests was the most dangerous after Andrey, which is why they took control of Olga, although there was no real need for this. The girl was still in a daze and showed no interest in what was happening. At first, the choice of the furies even surprised the Old Man. Logically, in order to blackmail the Old Man, these sisters should have blocked Tatyana, but they did not do this. Andrey understood why a split second later. The Furies assigned this mission to another character. The character grinned and held Tatyana by the waist with one hand, and held a knife to her throat with the other. He looked at the Old Man and then at the Engineer, with the same grin. As if hinting that he had outplayed them both in some way. - Well, Mr. Engineer, are you convinced that I was right? We should have wiped out that team right away, back at Opachichi! With machine guns. And you are all telepathy, telekinesis... that's how you played. - Scout? - Andrey felt the Engineer slipping away from the danger zone, but he didn't stop him. - What kind of tricks are you doing? - Throw away the Joker. - Scout shook his head, pointing to the far end of the room. - Over there. The amulet too. As if setting an example, Scout cut the strap with the artifact from Tatyana's neck and threw it to the far wall. A second later, the "Joker" that the Furies had removed from Olga's neck flew there too. - I don’t understand, stalker, have you gone over to the creatures? - And I was there. - Scout grinned. - What, you don't believe me? Ask Olga. She almost figured me out. Bitch. - Shut your mouth, goat. Olga woke up just in time. The Old Man assessed the new balance of power and the situation. The Engineer had prudently taken up a position near the secret door. Scout was holding Tatyana hostage, but it was unclear whether he would have the courage to kill his former companion in case of complications. The three furies surrounding Olga were as dangerous as controllers, but they were not armed. As for Olga herself, the question remained open. Logic suggested that she should stay on Andrey's side, but the same logic protested. Olga understood from vivid and clear examples that in her inner essence she was closer to the controllers than to people, which meant that she could also take the enemy's side. Such was the paradox. The notorious logic did not know how the internal conflict would end. Or rather, it also suggested, but uncertainly. Fifty-fifty. Needless to say, the situation was not simple. Andrey, of course, could have tried to somehow drag things out and try to transfer the problem to a new plane. But it was impossible to drag out the pause indefinitely, and there were no other ways of transferring it in sight. Olga, the only variable that could change the balance of power, stubbornly maintained neutrality. Andrey threw the Joker, and then the amulet. “They’re all yours now, Mr. Engineer,” Scout chuckled. “You’re such a bastard,” said Tatyana. “Get that piece of iron away from my neck!” - Oh, shut up, Tatyana Sergeyevna. - Scout winced. - I won't remove it, you understand. Your faithful is too well-known a killer in the Zone to remain against him without a human shield. - He's not armed! - That's what you think. Listen, Engineer, he's yours, why are you holding up? - So it's you? - The engineer took a couple of steps, but not towards Lunev, but towards the center of the hall. - I admit, you turned out to be a good psionic. Almost like my daughter. - Who?! - Scout was amazed. - Olga? Damn it, what a movie! And how did you find each other? Oh, well, I mean... I see... I wanted to ask - how did you get lost. When? “You’ll remember yourself if you try,” Olga suddenly said, “Sasha...” - Right, a movie. - Scout snorted incredulously. - I don't think I introduced myself to you. How do you know my name? - It was a few minutes before the Flash. Remember. A tour, a bus, the three of us. Mom, me and... Dad. You promised us it would be a cool trip. - Exactly! - Scout looked at the Engineer, then at Olga and back at the Engineer. - And I was tormented the whole way, how do I know you? That's life! Sometimes it turns out so that you can't make it up. It turns out that the Flash has spared you too. - It depends on how you look at it. - Olga put her hand on the machine gun, but the furies immediately took the weapon away from her. "Calm down, sisters," said the Engineer. "She is not dangerous. Yes, daughter?" - Don't call me that. - Olga flashed a cold look. - You are not my father. You are just a creature in his guise. And even that... a pathetic parody. Yes, Scout, we both got caught in the Flash then, but we got off with "only" a couple of internal deviations. We became hidden controllers, and we cannot go further than fifty kilometers from the Zone. We are alive because the Zone supports us. Almost like zombies, only in a lighter version. - I know, I know. - Scout winced. - It's not good, you have to admit. Being a hidden mutant is okay, bearable, but sitting in the Zone for the rest of your life is not so good. That's why I decided to help your... this Engineer. Contact spots all over the world are our chance to always be in touch with the Zone, no matter where we go. And that means this is our real chance to live a full life wherever we want. Isn't that right? - No, Scout, that's not it. We won't be able to live fully, that's self-deception. “He’s right, daughter, think about it carefully,” said the Engineer. - Stop calling me daughter! - Olga flared up. - My father died six years ago! Throughout this tense conversation, Andrey was not so much listening as assessing the changes in the situation. Olga found it unpleasant to talk to these creatures, but she overcame herself, trying to stall for time. Why, if not to choose the moment to attack? Surrounded by three controllers, resistance seemed pointless, but that was if you did not take into account Olga's abilities. For her, these half-dead ladies were no obstacle. She did not succumb to hypnosis, and her "daughter" was clearly better physically and in combat training than the Engineer's "sisters". The owner of the castle himself was far away and could not interfere with Olga or Andrey. It turned out that Tatyana remained the weak link, as before. “No problem,” Andrey thought. “And Scout opened up. Very successfully.” Scout stared at Olga, his vigilance seriously weakened. There was no point in dragging it out any longer. Andrey quietly pulled out a second knife he had hidden in advance from his sleeve and threw it without a swing, aiming at Scout's temple. Everything could have easily been decided at that very second, but the Thirteenth Sector wished to postpone the outcome. As if reading Andrey's thoughts, the "weak link" decided to prove that it was in fact no different from the other "links". The perfectly balanced Finnish knife with the "NKVD" engraving had barely started to fly when Tatyana suddenly threw Scout's hand with the knife aside and hit the stalker hard in the groin. Scout let out a muffled cry and bent in half. At the same moment, the knife whistled over the stalker's head, but, contrary to the Old One's fears, did not ring out against the stone wall, but found another target. "Such a weapon cannot return to its sheath clean," the thought flashed. "Only it looks like it won't drink hot blood today." The knife crunched through the bones and went deep into the eye of another controller who suddenly appeared behind Scout. Unlike the Engineer, this creature was quite vulnerable. Dark, almost black blood covered the controller's entire face. The mutant froze for a second, and then collapsed as if mown down. "Ghost!" one of the Furies shrieked. "A man has killed a Ghost!" The Engineer and all three sisters turned sharply to Andrey and delivered a powerful psi-blow. Lunev staggered, took a couple of steps back, but again did not fall. On the contrary, he even grouped himself and, straightening up like a large powerful spring, rushed straight at the Engineer. The controller, who absolutely did not expect such agility from a person crushed by psi-impact, did not have time to step aside and immediately paid for his sluggishness and arrogance. Andrey knocked him down and threw him onto the altar. The Engineer hit his head hard on the edge of the stone and floated for a moment. Lunev tried to slam his head to the stone with a kick, but missed. The mutant quickly came to his senses and managed to dodge. - Yes, mercenary, you are strong! - The engineer jumped up and waved his cloak again, preventing a new attack. - Even without the artifact, you are strong. It was not for nothing that Ostapenko relied on you. Not the artifacts, but you are the main "Joker" in the general's deck. But you are only a man! The Monster raised his hand again and brought down a new psi-blow on the Old One, much stronger than all the previous ones. Andrey understood perfectly well what the Engineer was hoping for. Without the "Joker", any, even the strongest fighter, became truly just a man. But there was one small but significant mistake in the Engineer's plan. Apparently, he had never heard the joke about appendicitis. "Doctor, my wife has appendicitis!" - I removed your wife's appendix last summer, a person cannot get a new one. - But a person can get a new wife! After Andrey spat on the psi-effect and rushed into a new attack, the Engineer suspected something, but it was too late. The Old Man gave him a real meat grinder. Mercenaries call it "beating him up to the nines." If the Engineer had been a human or a mutant, not protected by a powerful artifact, he would have been guaranteed death on the spot. But the Engineer, thanks to the support of the white crystal hidden in his bosom, again got off with only bruises and a slight groggy state. Scout suddenly wheezed from the floor. He was clearly trying to reveal the Old One's secret, to hint that Andrey had the Joker and the Mechanic's amulet hidden in his bosom. But Tatyana intervened again. She finally decided to open her holster and take out her pistol, but she didn't fire, she just hit Scout hard on the back of the head with the butt. For her zeal, she immediately received a psi-blow from the sisters and fell unconscious on the stalker. But Scout didn't feel any better. From the blow to the back of the head, he also passed out thoroughly and for a long time, like a mere mortal, despite being a psionic. Unfortunately, Olga still did not participate in all these dances, although it was clear that the struggle of motives in her soul had long since ended, and in favor of the people. Why she did not at least rein in the furies, Andrey did not understand. Something was holding Olga back. It was as if she was waiting to see how the confrontation between the Engineer and the Old One would end. As if she had decided for herself that the outcome of their duel would confirm or refute her conclusions. Meanwhile, Andrey tried to confiscate the crystal from the Engineer, but he didn't have time. The monster came to his senses and began to resist desperately. It turned out that he could not only juggle objects with his talents, but also understood something about martial arts. The discovery was a complete surprise for Andrey, but very soon it dawned on the Old One that the enemy's transformation was explained simply. The Engineer focused not on psi-attacks, but on active defense. Including predicting events. The new tactics were clearly successful. Andrey made three attacks and never hit the target. The enemy managed to dodge the blow a split second before contact. At this rate, Andrey could have wasted all his strength and given the victory to the enemy, losing "on points". Besides, the "sisters" finally believed that Olga had decided to remain neutral, and began to approach Lunev from behind. This was completely unnecessary. It was unknown what they were armed with, it was not visible under their robes, but even if they simply grabbed him with their claws, the pleasant result was minimal. And if they managed to rip off the second set of protective artifacts... "They can! I don't understand yet why they can't pin me down with their hypnosis - after all, in the swamp, the four creatures were doing just fine, despite the Joker - but someday this damn luck will run out, and I'll find myself in a very ticklish situation. One that I might die from tickling. It is unlikely that Olga caught Andrey's thoughts, but then events suddenly began to develop much more rapidly than before, and clearly according to her scenario. At first Andrey did not understand this, hesitated for a moment and again let the nimble Engineer out of his sight. However, he quickly got his bearings in the changed situation and focused on the main goal....

...The Furies moved towards the Old One, and Olga finally made a decision. Whatever the threat, she had to complete the mission. And complete it in the same order in which she had begun. Who she was now, a person or a creature, did not matter to her. As long as there was at least something human in her soul, she had to remain human. "We are who we feel ourselves to be, even if the facts stubbornly insist on the opposite." Clinging to this thought, Olga emerged from the icy lake of indifference and stepped onto solid ground. The same ground from which she had been pushed into the cold water by a hurricane of emotions a few minutes ago. Olga glanced at the machine gun discarded by her sisters, but decided against picking up the weapon. Some deserve a bullet, some deserve to die from poison, and some are too few to even spend a piece of hemp on. "The beasts... a dog's death!" Olga, without knowing why, suddenly gained complete confidence in her new talents and powers. She snapped her fingers, and a second later all ten Cerberuses guarding the lair waddled into the hall. The dogs lined up in a neat line, the envy of any soldier, and growled softly. Olga somehow knew that the dogs knew what controllers tasted like, and they liked that taste. “Get him,” Olga ordered quietly, at the same time imagining who exactly she was setting the dogs on. Of the four targets that Olga had mentally designated, the dogs attacked only three. No, they would not have minded tearing their former master to shreds, but the artifact protecting the Engineer did not allow them to even approach the fourth victim. The hall was filled with the guttural growls of dogs and the cries of dying sisters... ...Andrey didn't have to turn around to understand what was happening behind him. Olga's train of thought was clear to him. Weapons in the castle meant little. It was much more important to seize the initiative and attract as many allies as possible to his side. Subduing the Cerberuses was an excellent idea. Now the people could fully concentrate on the main task without worrying about the rear. Even if the Engineer called for help from some other creatures, the dogs would deal with them in an instant. The Engineer also realized that he was losing, and therefore decided not to take any risks. He tore the chain with the crystal from his neck and clutched his most valuable asset tightly in his fist. The milky white light went out for a moment, then seeped through his fingers and flowed down to the Engineer's feet. Upon touching the ground, the moon milk seemed to boil and turn into a glowing fog. A low wave of fog quickly spread to the sides and froze, limiting a circle twenty meters in diameter. As soon as the glowing fog stopped, the Engineer's figure became blurry and very soon disappeared. Hardly had this happened when the nature of the glow changed. The fog acquired a greenish tint. As far as the Old Man understood, this meant that the passage from the Thirteenth Sector to the outside world was open. Andrei turned around abruptly and vigorously waved his hand at Olga. She rushed to Tatyana, lifted her to her feet and pushed her into the circle. Tatyana was clearly not herself yet and could barely keep her balance. Andrey grabbed his friend's hand tightly and nodded towards the center of the circle. “Yes, yes, I understand,” Tatyana whispered. “And Olga?” “Go, I’ll catch up!” Olga shouted, leaning over Scout. - He will close the crossing! - No. - Olga waved her hand. - The corridors close themselves, not earlier than in a minute! Go!.. ...The room the walkers found themselves in, stepping into the outside world, looked even stranger than the hall in the castle ruins. It had once been a two-story building, but now nothing remained of the second floor and the roof except for a dozen sharp teeth where the windows had been. It seemed as if the walkers had found themselves inside the jaw of a long-rotted dinosaur that had grown into the ground. Concrete debris and brick rubble in the building formed several almost identical hills, covered with wind-blown rotten leaves and the ubiquitous dirt. Overall, the picture was typical of the Zone, especially of thoroughly destroyed Pripyat. And Andrey had no doubt that the passage had led to Pripyat. He knew what kind of building it was. The contact spot opened up inside the ruins of the pier. Somewhere behind the right-hand battlement, a cooling pond splashed, tall trees peered into the ruins to the left, and straight ahead, if you climbed one of the piles of rubble, you could see the roof of a cafe that had miraculously withstood the blows of both Catastrophes. Andrey's conviction was indirectly confirmed by the reaction of the instruments. The dosimeter in Tatyana's pocket began to beep. The radiation background around the pier was always quite high. The familiar surroundings gave him additional confidence in his own abilities and helped him concentrate. Even the alarming buzzer of the dosimeter failed to distract Andrey from searching for the enemy hiding somewhere among the rubble. For some reason, the Old Man was sure that the Engineer had not left the building. In the short time of confrontation in the castle, he seemed to have learned to sense the enemy. Although everything could be explained more simply - the clues came from Olga. Andrey gestured to Tatyana to stay put. She tried to hand him the gun, but Lunev shook his head. Andrey now knew exactly how to kill the Engineer. He didn't need any weapon for that. None. Only the "Joker". The enemy was bluffing, claiming that the contact of the white and red artifacts would cause a catastrophe. They could destroy each other, but not their owners, much less their immediate surroundings. But the Engineer was afraid of losing the crystal, since it would truly be a catastrophe for all his plans. And for him personally, too. After all, Andrey would then have a serious advantage. In addition to the "Joker", the former mercenary still had an amulet blocking psi-effects, which meant that the chances of the controller and the human were equal. Almost. The chances of the Old One looked even more preferable. Whether the Engineer reasoned so or not, Andrey did not know, but the enemy was in no hurry to get out of the ruins, that was a fact. The Old Man looked closely at the surroundings and noticed a weak white glow in the left corner of the destroyed ticket hall of the pier.
