Lunev carefully moved in the direction of the glow, but managed to go only a couple of meters. The pile of debris separating him from the enemy suddenly rose into the air, as if a powerful whirlwind had lifted it, and, after hanging for a second, fell to the ground. In such a situation there was no time for beautiful acrobatics, and Andrei simply jumped back and awkwardly fell on his back two steps away from Tatyana. “I thought he’d be weaker here,” Andrey admitted, standing up. “He almost knocked me down, the bastard!” - Andrey, don't! - Tatyana begged, naively trying to disperse the dust swirling around her with her hand. - Let him go! He won't let you get closer! - No! Lunev's answer seemed to echo through the ruins and make them tremble. The effect was strange, but explainable. It was just that, at the same time as the Old One's answer, the Engineer, hiding in the ruins, struck again. The battlements on the walls began to crumble and fall in large fragments, exclusively into the building. A minute of such a collapse - and people could be buried in debris up to their necks. Andrey pushed Tatyana towards the nearest gap and pointed to the dark bushes behind it. - Leave! - I won't go without you! - At least just get out of this trap. Wait for me in the bushes! - I'm afraid! - Don't argue! If you hear footsteps or rustling in the bushes, shoot without thinking. If I don't return in five minutes, go south, to the old pier! This is a checkpoint. They'll take you from there. - Andrey! - That's it! The question is closed! Lunev pushed Tatyana out of the danger zone, then turned and dove into the dust curtain. He had a hard time imagining how he would make his way through the stone rain to the enemy's position, but he hoped that the "Joker" would help him in this difficult task. "Joker" really helped, but in a different way. As soon as Andrey passed the glowing ring of the exit point, clearly visible even in the dust, the Engineer struck again, and this time with full contact. He suddenly appeared right in front of the Old Man and with a lightning-fast movement plunged a long, translucent, glowing stiletto into Andrey’s chest. Lunev managed to figure out that it was not a stiletto at all, but a crystal transformed into a strange weapon, but for some reason Andrey did not have time to dodge the blow or block it. Or did not want to. But in that case he did it, instantly reacting to a new hint from outside. In a thousandth of a second, a good dozen disturbing thoughts flashed through my brain, but only one clung to my consciousness: “Everything is right.” What could be right when a knife is being plunged into your chest, Andrey did not understand, and yet he did not challenge the dubious thought. As it turned out in the next thousandth of a second, he acted wisely. The sparkling stiletto seemed to have hit the armor. And it hit it so hard that it struck a sheaf of bright red-white sparks that almost scorched Lunev's face and clothes. In fact, the crystal had hit the "Joker", but that didn't change the essence of the matter. Except that it refuted all the assumptions of both the Old Man and the Engineer. The artifacts didn't destroy each other. They just hit each other no weaker than their owners. The force with which the artifacts repulsed each other was so great that the weapon fell out of the Engineer's hand and flew off to the side. Lunev was thrown back two meters again and fell on his back. "This is the end for you, creature!" a new thought came to Andrey. "Were you afraid of a catastrophe? Get a catastrophe!" Andrey jumped to his feet, rushed forward, grabbed his opponent by the throat and squeezed his fingers tightly... ...The first thing Olga saw when she found herself in the outside world was a fountain of red and white sparks that flared up somewhere in the depths of the dust curtain just beyond the greenish glow. And the first thing she felt was a not very strong but painful blow to the shoulder. As if some hooligan had thrown a hefty piece of gravel from a slingshot. Olga looked down and was surprised to discover that at her feet lay not a stone, but a milky-white, slightly glowing crystal. Moreover, it was in a frame and on a silver chain. In the outside world, the crystal did not glow as brightly as in the Thirteenth Sector, but it was still impossible to confuse it with anything else. Olga sat down and carefully touched the artifact. Nothing terrible happened. Then she picked up the thing and turned it over, trying to examine it from all sides. She didn't like what she saw. No, there were no defects or anything like that on the crystal. The thing itself was perfect, but the way it affected consciousness slightly scared Olga. The girl did not begin to turn into the same soulless creature that the previous owner of the crystal turned out to be. Olga remained herself, only her ability to look into the thoughts and feelings of others sharply increased and, it seemed, got out of control. Olga did not want this at all, but the "Key" made her a very strong psionic, it literally unlocked all the most secret storerooms of any consciousness. More precisely, not any, but the one that is closest or most disposed to telepathic contact. Olga felt four minds nearby. She had little interest in one. No, she wouldn't mind getting a couple of intimate secrets out of it, but the situation didn't lend itself to such entertainment. So Olga left Tatyana alone. The second mind was clouded. And the third, Andrey's mind, was completely subordinated to the fight with the enemy, and was also protected from external interference by the "Joker". The cunning artifact perfectly filtered any signals from the outside. It let in business advice, but instantly cut off stupidity, and especially attempts to get into a person's consciousness for the purpose of reconnaissance. The Engineer's mind remained. Olga desperately did not want to get into it, but the crystal demanded action, and it would not hurt to distract the enemy. She plunged into the enemy's consciousness, but saw only one picture, which had nothing to do with what was happening now in the ruins, but for some reason occupied all the Engineer's thoughts. Olga saw a frozen frame from six years ago, in which several monsters were killing a woman. Olga felt as if she had been burned. She suddenly remembered her endless dream and clearly understood what fragment had forever fallen out of her memories of the events of that six years ago....

...It was the same frame as the one that stood before the Engineer's inner eye now. With the same characters, in the same place, only from a different angle: from the point of view of a man sitting on the ground five meters from the mad feast of monsters. The frame was so hard on the psyche that Olga's mind simply crossed it out of her memories, replacing it with a dark gap between the catastrophe and the soldier's appearance. However, now Olga slowly but surely remembered everything that had happened before and after. She remembered how she hid in the ruins with her mother, who was wounded during the disaster, how they were attacked by monsters, and how her mother tried to save Olga, taking the blow herself. How someone appeared, who looked like her father from afar, and how terrifying it was to discover that it was not her father, but another monster... Now Olga remembered everything and understood the main thing: she was not completely mistaken in those nightmarish minutes. The Engineer who killed her father really was one of those monsters. He killed her father, and then her mother... Moreover, in the depths of the Engineer's consciousness at that moment there remained the understanding that he was killing and devouring his own wife. That is why the first victim remained in the monster's memory...
...Olga, with an incredible effort of will, forced herself to emerge from the new lake of icy fear and, swinging with all her might, threw the crystal where her intuition told her to. As it turned out a second later, intuition suggested the only correct direction... ...Strangling the monster was not easy. Firstly, the mutant's cold skin suddenly became slippery as ice under Andrey's fingers, and secondly, the Engineer unexpectedly began to transform into something even more obscene than an advanced version of the controller. The monster's body stretched out so much that Andrey simply could not reach its neck, and instead of a face, a huge toothy muzzle began to form, most reminiscent of the muzzle of an ancient reptile. Long claws dug into the Old One's shoulders, and the hems of his cloak wrapped around his legs, turning, as it seemed to Andrey, into two scaly tails. It is unlikely that all this was really happening. Most likely, the dying creature gathered the rest of its strength and tried in a last desperate attempt to attack the enemy with the help of its gift of suggestion. A cornered beast is always especially dangerous. But whatever it was – illusion or reality – the Engineer once again had a chance to get out of it, and Andrey could do nothing to prevent it. The creature, transformed into a slippery scaly monster, opened its huge mouth and hung over the man, clearly intending to bite his head off. Andrey involuntarily pulled his head into his shoulders and also made a desperate attempt to break free from the monster's claws. The sharp pain in his body, slashed by the monster's claws, literally paralyzed the Old One, and all that was left for him was to watch as the mouth, studded with sharp teeth, approached. To be honest, Andrey couldn't even imagine such an ending in his worst nightmare. In theory, it would have been better to close his eyes, but the Old Man couldn't even do that. He was forced to accept death with an open visor, like a medieval knight in the clutches of a dragon. Although he would have preferred to do without such dubious romance. Andrey imagined for a moment how the monstrous jaws would close and how he would see the notorious light at the end of the tunnel. The Old Man chuckled to himself. In the rather boring gloom of the Zone, it would be nice to see the light, at least in this version. "And to hell with it! I did what I could! Don't think ill of me!" Suddenly, a soft white light flashed before Andrey's eyes, but it was definitely not the light Andrey had been prepared to see. The unknown firefly flew swiftly into the monster's mouth and disappeared somewhere in its throat. For a couple of seconds nothing happened at all. The monster froze with its mouth open, neither tightening nor loosening its grip. Andrey stopped thrashing and also froze in anticipation. By the third second of the strange pause, something had changed, but Stary only realized in what direction three seconds later. A bright white glow illuminated the monster's mouth and burst out. The monster's fingers immediately unclenched, and its scaly tails hung limply. Andrey fell backwards for the third time and, still unable to move, stared at the monster glowing from within. The engineer no longer looked like a monster, he had once again taken on his former appearance, with the only difference being that white light was coming from his mouth and eyes, and his previously grayish skin had a blue tint. The Engineer glowed like this "bulb" for a short time. The glow suddenly intensified and burst out in a hundred places at once. The Engineer's body became covered with a network of glowing cracks, like that lava in a fiery river, and then exploded with the sound of a burst tire. Thousands of cold shreds flew in different directions, generously decorating the walls and piles of debris with thousands of black and red blots. A second later, the ruins were illuminated by a new flash, this time greenish. The outline of the misty ring of the access point flared and disappeared. The exit from the Thirteenth Sector closed. But Lunev did not see it. He did not want to look. Andrey pressed the back of his head against the cold rubble of the brick rubble, exhaled loudly and closed his eyes. He didn't want to do anything else. First of all, to look, and secondly, to be surprised. Even to wipe the black blood of the enemy from his face and brush away pieces of his flesh. He was in control of his body again, the paralyzing pain had disappeared, as if it had never been there, but he didn't want to move, even if it killed him. Andrey didn't want anything at all. Not even to find out what had really happened. "Don't give up on everything! There is no me. For no one! Forever!" - Good throw. You should play that, what do you call it... baseball, Olya, - a familiar voice creaked from the corner. “Shut your mouth,” Olga answered dully. - The commander needs to be dragged to the water and washed, otherwise he won't come to his senses until the morning. Untie him, I'll help. - Calm down. - Listen, what's that glowing thing? That crystal, right? Over there, among the scraps. - Scout, do you feel like chewing on a gag? - I'm silent! Unfortunately, even a sleeping person always has at least one of the six senses working. No, not intuition. It's hearing. And there's no getting away from this fact. There's you, there's no you, for everyone or for someone in particular - you can't fool physiology. You'll hear everything and everyone. Andrey gritted his teeth and opened his eyes. "Whether you want to or not, you have to. And think, and move, and do everything else. Damn work!" Andrey sat up slowly and wiped his face with his sleeve. - Andrey! - Hurried steps were heard from behind. - Are you okay?! Lunev looked up. Tatyana was squatting next to him. Olga was standing slightly behind her. Again indifferent, as in the castle, but now also clearly depressed. “Okay.” Andrey gestured for Tatyana to hold off on the rest of her questions. He put his hand into his bosom and pulled out a rawhide strap. As Andrey expected, the leather pouch with the "Joker" was missing from the strap. When the Engineer managed to tear the main protection off his opponent's neck, Andrey could only guess, but now he knew exactly why he almost lost the decisive stage of the fight. The amulet alone could not cope with the suggestion of such a strong controller. So, if not for Olga's throw... Andrey looked at Olga. - Olya, what did you quit? “The Crystal, the Key,” Olga answered without intonation and slid an indifferent glance over the place where a man and a monster had fought a minute earlier. - Thank you. Just in time. Another second and he could have defeated me. - You're welcome. It just happened by chance. Reflexively. - And he was great - bam, and in pieces! - Scout couldn't help but remark. - And why? Did you pick the wrong sauce for this crystal? Did he choke? “He burst from anger,” Olga muttered. “Shut up, I warned you.” Andrey turned around and measured the stalker with his gaze. He was tied hand and foot and sitting by the wall, to the left of the gap in which Tatyana was hiding. - Why did you bring this one? - Andrey grinned. - Let him stay in the Thirteenth, that's where he belongs. - You said it yourself, as many of us came, as many will leave. - Olga shrugged. - The crystal is over there, you can send it back. "It's unlikely to work," Tatyana noted. "Only the Engineer knew how to open passages with this artifact. As far as I understand, you need to know something special to do this." - A spell, or what? - snorted Scout. - Don't mess around! What am I supposed to do in this Thirteenth? “And in prison?” Olga asked calmly. - There's nothing there! Untie me, I beg you, I'll go far away, I'll never come back, I swear! - Olya, gag him, - Andrey suggested wearily. - Or shoot him. I'm tired of him. - You're evil! - Scout muttered resentfully. - The man made a mistake, it happens to everyone! And you shoot right away. I'll shut up, you won't get another word out! Andrey stood up and walked over to Olga. - I'm sorry you had to go through so much. - No more than you. - Olga looked away. - That creature wasn't my father, I already told you back there, in the sector. It was just a monster that took his form. I lost both my parents in Chernobyl during the Second Catastrophe. - It was still harder for you than for us. - Andrey took her hand. - I saw it. - No need, Old Man. - Olga freed her hand. - The fact that I was hanging there means nothing. Yes, at first I was slightly shocked, but common sense kept me from making the wrong choice. When I had the crystal in my hands, I was able to look into the enemy's hidden thoughts and saw in them what finally convinced me. Now I know for sure that I did the right thing. - What did you see? - I don't want to talk about it. It's too personal. And it doesn't matter now. Let's think about how to get out of here. We've ended up almost in the very center again. - Just not an hour before the Emission. There's time, we'll break through. Untie Scout's legs, I'm not going to drag him on my back. Andrey approached the place where the Engineer had died and examined what was left of him. The monster had been torn to shreds, only he hadn't "burst with anger," but rather turned inside out. Or, to be more precise, he had been unable to live in two dimensions at once. The crystal he had swallowed had provided such an opportunity, but the Engineer had used it incorrectly. Andrey pulled the chain with the crystal out of the pool of black blood and wiped the "Key" with a scrap of cloth he had found nearby, probably a piece of the Engineer's cloak. The crystal glowed, as before, not too brightly, but evenly, one might say, solidly. Andrey clutched the crystal in his fist and looked around the battlefield, hoping to find the "Joker" as well. This turned out to be more difficult. The artifact didn't glow in the outside world, and it was also packed in a leather pouch. In the semi-darkness, among the concrete rubble, it was unrealistic to find the thing. Andrey resigned himself and stood up. “The crystal is good compensation for all the lost artifacts,” said Tatyana. - What? - Andrey turned around. - Oh, yes, good. But we don't need such compensation. This artifact is too dangerous. - From a scientific point of view, it is very interesting and... - No. - Andrey pointed to the gap. - Let's go..

“As you wish.” Tatyana sighed. “I hope we’ll get some rest before we head back?” - Let's rest. At the pier. Remember, I told you? It's not far from here. It really wasn't far to the old pier. When the group settled down on the mossy rocks, Andrey stepped aside and took out his communicator. To be honest, he didn't expect the connection to be restored, but the communicator suddenly came to life and even allowed him to contact the base on the first try. Or rather, not the base, but Bibik. - Old man! - The colonel was clearly delighted. - I'm listening to you! - Hello, Bibik, can you hear me? - There was static crackling in the receiver, and Andrey was forced to raise his voice. - I hear, I hear! - Do you hear that? - Andrey pressed the receiver closer to his ear. - Damn it, interference! Okay, screw it, I'm reporting! The main enemy has been destroyed along with the main artifact. Do you hear that? - I'm not deaf! - The answer came through the crackling and hissing. - Excellent! Well done! Where are you now? - What?! Oh, I'm in Pripyat! Remember where you and I surfaced in the fall? Right on this pier. - Don't move anywhere, we'll send a helicopter. - Got it! When?! - Soon! - Got it! End of communication. Andrey turned off the communicator, put it in his pocket and turned to his companions. All three were looking at Lunev, but each of them had completely different expectations in their eyes. Tatyana looked with hidden hope. She definitely did not want to trudge back through the entire Zone. Scout did not want to go back either on foot or in a helicopter. He clearly hoped that his former comrades would show leniency and let him go his way. Olga looked... not at all. Just looked. It seemed that she did not care whether to go back or not. Andrey understood her. Where to return to if your home is now the entire Zone? From the bedroom to the hallway? What's the point? After all, in a day or two you'll have to go deeper into this house anyway. Andrey nodded to all three at once, but said nothing. Each of his companions was free to understand this as he wished. And everyone understood it correctly. Tatyana sighed with relief, Scout morose, and Olga lowered her gaze and stared at the rough stones. Andrey turned away again and walked to the very edge of the pier. He remembered well how in October he had dived into the pond a little to the right of this place. And how he had almost become dinner for a giant catfish living in a deep bottom hole not far from the pier. Lunev again put his hand in his pocket, but this time he took out not a communicator, but a crystal. The thing still glowed faintly, radiating calm, as if trying to convince its new owner that with it he would know no worries and would be able to defeat any enemy. "You're lying, glass. With you, there'll be nothing but worries and problems. For me and for the entire Zone. It's not a given that a new Engineer will be found who can use you as a weapon, but I can't allow even the slightest possibility of such an option. Don't hold it against me." Andrey swung briefly and threw the artifact into the pond, approximately where the very center of the bottom pit was hidden under the thickness of the water. - What a weirdo! - Scout said quietly. - What a rip-off! Consider that you've sunk a wagonload of money. Bibik was right when he said you were nuts. - Do you think he will save it? - Olga stood next to Andrey. - Who, a catfish? I think so. Under the protection of this fish, the thing will be safe. And that means the Zone, too. Are you against it? - No, I would have done the same. - Olga extended her hand. - It's time for me to go, Old Man. Thank you for everything. - Are you sure you don't want to go back? There's plenty to do in the Borderlands for you. - I'm sure. Now my whole life is only here. With my heredity. And now excuse me, Old Man, I'm going. I need to be alone. - I understand. - Andrey shook her hand. - Remember, you can always turn to me. I will definitely help. - Okay. - Olga smiled sadly and pointed at Tatyana. - I wish you great personal happiness. - You too. - Who should I go with? - She turned her gaze to Scout. - With him, perhaps? He won't get out of jail until he retires. And I didn't really want to. Goodbye. Andrey watched her go for a long time and, when Olga disappeared into the thickets, he nodded. - Goodbye. Although, I'm sure we'll meet again. The Zone is such a tiny patch... sooner or later you can meet anyone here. You've seen it for yourself..

The blazing corridor, created with flamethrowers and phosphorus grenades, allowed the strike group to advance only two hundred meters. Barricades prevented them from going any further. The APCs tried to take the barrier on the move, but the first vehicle failed to cope with the task and got stuck, and the second one caught fire for unknown reasons. The loss of the armored personnel carriers was a significant blow, but the fighters coped with the problem. They created a corridor around the blockage, and the infantry fighting vehicles that followed the APCs finally broke through. And the main column followed them. As Bibik predicted, the toxic smoke that enveloped the battlefield reduced visibility to zero, and the fighters had to literally make their way by touch. It was impossible to count how many soldiers perished in the smoky hell, but overall the strike group accomplished its task. The corridor allowed the garrison to leave the citadel and break out into operational space. At first, things were no better in the field than at the walls of the warehouse complex. The mutants were constantly attacking from all sides, and even from the air. But when the people managed to break through to the old canal, Bibik's plan worked as the colonel had hoped. Two of the four BMPs remaining at the garrison's disposal took a special forces group and three flamethrowers onto their armor and moved as the lead patrol. The flamethrowers set fire to the forest to the right of the column directly from the sides of the boxes, and the rest of the fighters covered them from the monsters and helped with the fire work with grenades. As a result, when the column was pulled into a new corridor, the picture of the route was painted, as they say, in oil. Fifty meters to the right, a forest was ablaze, two steps to the left, a dirty canal was splashing, thick clouds of smoke covered the column from above, and from the rear, there were two more BMPs and a screen of “cleaners” cutting off the pursuing mutants with intense machine gun fire and those same grenades. It cannot be said that the plan was ideal. The heat of the nearby fire was trying to kill not only the monsters, but also the initiators of this apocalypse. Every half minute, people scooped up muddy water from the canal with their helmets and poured it on themselves to somehow protect themselves from the heat. From the swirling clouds of the curtain hanging over the column, "batmen" completely maddened by the poisonous phosphorus smoke fell every now and then. Fearless pseudo-giants tried to force the canal. And the bloodsuckers persistently crawled under the tracks of the lead vehicles. However, all these problems were no longer as serious as before. The mutants were clearly not as well prepared for a maneuverable battle as they were for a siege. True, it soon became clear that the enemy had at least one more trump card up his sleeve. Even two: a psi-attack and bribery. And if people somehow coped with the first problem: the special forces were pumped with special drugs, the "cleaners" also swallowed some pills, and the stalkers protected themselves with the help of artifacts, then the problem of bribery turned out to be much more difficult to cope with. However, it later turned out that it was not entirely about bribery. It all started at the gate. Several stalkers suddenly tried to attack the general. Lieutenant Pashkov's men, who were acting as headquarters guards, as well as Bibik personally, had to work hard to drive away the gang that had attacked. In the smoke screen and confusion, the aggressive stalkers were lost for a while, but when the column set out on the route, they showed up again. This time, Bibik almost missed the moment of the attack, but everything worked out. The colonel personally gave three saboteurs a good talking to, and three more were calmed down by special forces. There was no third attack, although it turned out that the sabotage group had not yet been completely destroyed. Two strangers were captured and dragged to Bibik for interrogation by the stalkers themselves. - They weren't with us in the warehouses! - one of the militiamen confidently declared. - They joined us outside the gates! And they're dressed, look for yourself, ladle, like blue ones! - I see, you're not blind. - Bibik pointed his machine gun at one of the saboteurs. - Who hired him? Who were you hunting? “Fuck you!” the mercenary muttered through his teeth. - The answer is incorrect. - Bibik shot a short burst at the enemy and pushed the body into the canal. - I repeat the question. He pointed the gun at the second one. “The middlemen don’t tell us who the employer is!” the second mercenary panicked. - Well, they do designate the target. - Bibik stuck the barrel of the machine gun under his nose. - Who was the contract for?! - On the general! - How did you get through the barriers, who led you? - I don't know! - The answer is incorrect! - He was in a robe! He was putting pressure on my brain like a controller! He didn't let me look at him! - I see. - Bibik spat angrily. - Well, you, bastard, are completely without conscience, huh? You hired yourself to serve mutants! Aren't you disgusted yourself? - It's just work! Nothing personal! - Have you watched too many movies? "Nothing personal"! Well, go to your buddies, if you don't care who you serve! Get up! Go! Bibik grabbed the mercenary by the collar, abruptly stood him up and pushed him hard towards the canal. - I... where am I... going to go? - the mercenary looked around in fear. - Forward! Swim to the other shore! - There?! There are animals there! Better hit here! - That's a shitty question. - Bibik raised his machine gun. - No! - The mercenary jumped into the water and swam to the other bank. He reached exactly the middle of the canal. In the light of the nearby forest fire, it was clearly visible how the water around the mercenary suddenly began to bubble, as if boiling, and the man screamed piercingly. Hundreds of some small but toothy fish pounced on him from all sides and in a matter of seconds had gnawed the mercenary to the bones. The echo of his wild screams was still flying over the water, but it was already over. - Mercenaries used to be tougher, - Bibik spat again and pulled on his mask. - Now they recruit anyone. Pashkov, get the stragglers moving! Don't get too spread out! I'm going to see the general. - Eat! Bibik managed to take only ten steps towards the head of the column when the lieutenant called out to him. - Mister Colonel! - What else do you want? - Bibik looked around. - The beasts are falling behind! - Pashkov pushed the "cleaners" fighter forward. - Here, the messenger has arrived. “Speak.” Bibik stopped and waited for the messenger. - There, that is... we ran out of grenades, and we saw it. There are no animals in the rear! They flicker in the distance, but they don't follow us. “They really need to walk on burning ground,” Bibik grinned. - No, well... there's not much of it on that bank either. - The soldier pointed to the left. - And they don't fall from the sky anymore. And the controllers don't mess with your brains. The sergeant sent you to tell you that... that's it, they've left you alone. “You’re free,” Bibik nodded and glanced around the area. The sergeant of the "cleaners" commanding the rearguard seemed to be right on all counts. The mutants had clearly left the column alone, moreover, they had begun to retreat in the direction of the Zone. The reason for this could only be guessed at. Perhaps the mutant command had somehow gotten wind that stormtroopers would be attacking the hellish army any minute now, or perhaps something had happened in the "headquarters" of the beast horde... in principle, it didn't matter. The fact remained a fact in any case. The monsters were retreating. Somewhere high above the continuous smoke screen, multiple peals of thunder rolled, and Bibik involuntarily raised his gaze upward. - What is this? Airplanes? - Pashkov was surprised. - Stormtroopers. - The colonel nodded. - Goodbye, base. - Stormtroopers?! - the lieutenant rejoiced. - Counteroffensive? - Something like that. - Bibik looked down. - Why are you still here? Where did I send you? - Well, now... - Now we need to pull ourselves together even more. So that in five minutes the column will be more compact than a pack of biscuits! Run, march! - Eat! This time Pashkov reached the tail of the column without any obstacles. But Bibik never reached the general's Hummer. He was intercepted by a new messenger, this time from the lead patrol. - Tanks, Colonel! They're at Stepanovka! There's no connection, but they're signaling, they can see us. - Why don’t they come to meet us? - They're probably afraid we'll set them on fire. No one here understands their signals. The commander sent to ask if maybe someone in the column understands this? - In Morse code, or what? - Bibik turned the fighter around. - Come on, I'll figure it out. Soldiers, damn it! You've gotten used to chatting on your cell phones even in the field! They're called special forces! What's going on anyway? The mercenaries have lost their nerve, the Morse code specialists don't know... where is this all heading?! - Stop grumbling. - Ostapenko, accompanied by three fighters, taxied out of the smoke screen to intercept Bibik. - Tanks straight ahead, Mr. General. - I heard. Let's go. What kind of shooting did you have here? - Ah, so... the saboteurs were caught. - Whom? “Mercenaries,” Bibik clarified. “They came for your soul, by the way.” - Interrogated? - I'm telling you... - What about my soul, I understand, what else did they say? - Nothing more. They couldn't say anything. They were given a target, and they worked. - Who hired you? - It’s unknown, but I think... mutants hired them. - Are you kidding? - No, absolutely not. Judge for yourself: the mercenaries did not hide among the citadel's defenders, but came from outside. The stalkers confirm this. And the controllers led them through the menagerie, the prisoner confirmed this. So, they were also hired by the controllers. - Hired or forced? – That’s the whole point, they hired him. “This is taking an interesting turn,” Ostapenko said thoughtfully. - Not anymore. - Bibik pointed his thumb behind his back. - The animals are retreating. I think there's a reason for that. - Are you hinting that the reason for this retreat lies in the Thirteenth Sector? - I don't know, but the timing matches. Oh, if only we could establish communication! But because of these nasty transition points, there's total chaos on the air. Look, you can't even contact the tanks. - We have a connection! - suddenly shouted one of the soldiers sitting on the armor of the lead BMP. - Yurka, to the general, like a bullet! - They're already here! - shouted the messenger accompanying the boss. - Mister General, the box is online! “I’m coming.” Ostapenko jumped onto the armor and took the headset from the fighter’s hands. At that very moment, a deafening roar rolled over the field, as if a whole squad of drummers had started beating out of tune nearby. With sledgehammers on the railway tanks. It was impossible to hear anything in such a rumble, and Ostapenko was forced to join the crowd of onlookers that the entire column had turned into for a while. Just three kilometers to the northeast, the twilight first thickened for a moment, as if an invisible giant had blown out all the napalm fires left in D-3, and then was illuminated by bright flashes. The attack aircraft began to work on the Dityatki-3 base and the adjacent territories. This bacchanalia lasted only a couple of minutes, but as soon as the planes went into a turn, the flames over the base, knocked down by explosions, again soared into the smoky skies. - Forgive us, Lord, for our sins. - Bibik crossed himself and sighed. - There is no more base. - Hello, box, General Ostapenko is on the line! - the general took advantage of the pause. - We're moving out to meet you, Mr. General! - the tank commander replied. - Try to go at least another five hundred meters! Don't burn the forest, it's clear there! - Got it, let's go. - Ostapenko looked at the commander of the lead vehicle and waved his hand: "Forward!" “And the ‘combs’ seem to have left,” Bibik said, jumping onto the armor. “Do you hear the silence?” Ostapenko turned his head and nodded. The silence was relative. The shooting, explosions, roar of engines and other sounds of battle in the field had not yet disappeared, although they had become less intense, but the large caliber had indeed fallen silent. And the jet turbines overhead no longer roared. - Now we'll find out from the tank crews what's going on. They probably have better communications than we do. The meeting with the tank battalion took place five minutes later. The commander of the "box", a mustachioed, stocky major, immediately flew up to the general. - What's the news? - Ostapenko asked after listening to the report. - Is there contact with headquarters? - Now we have it, Mr. General! As soon as the "hole" closed, the connection was immediately established. - "The Nora" is closed? - Ostapenko looked meaningfully at Bibik. - Yes sir, the stormtroopers reported it. They were approaching the target, and suddenly it went out. “Then why the hell did they bomb?” Bibik muttered. - That's the order, - the tanker shrugged. - But there won't be a second run. The "combs" have gone to base. - They didn’t say where the mutants were going? - It's hard to see. But it seems they're heading towards the center of the Zone. An order has come from headquarters for you, Mr. General. The D-3 garrison has been ordered to return to their deployment site. Put out the fires. Firefighters have been sent from Dymer, and they'll be arriving from Kyiv by morning. But for now, you'll have to do it on your own. - Why did you come here? - And we'll move on, to the first line of checkpoints. We'll stop there. - Is there a chance that the beast will return? Is the headquarters expecting a counterattack? - No, sir, it’s just insurance. - Where were you before, insurance agents, damn it! - Bibik said angrily. - Now of course! Why not ride tanks in clean terrain! - There was no order. - The major stared at Bibik with honest eyes. - Do you think we weren't bothered? We're not animals, we understood everything. As soon as the order came, we rushed here at maximum speed. “Okay, let’s move on,” the colonel waved his hand. - Here's the thing. - The tanker lowered his voice conspiratorially and leaned forward a little. - There was disorder at headquarters. What kind of... chaos! A comrade from Kyiv just called me. He says that controllers who were putting pressure on the generals' brains and organizing sabotage were caught not far from headquarters! Can you imagine?! They say that they set fire to the counterintelligence headquarters and the building of the Main Military Scientific Laboratory. - Hello, Ostapenko grimaced with displeasure. - Put it out? – It seems like they put out the fire, but I don’t know what the damage was. - However, this time the animals approached the matter seriously, - Bibik noted. - They sent agents to the headquarters, exposed the counterintelligence, almost finished you off. They are growing. “Let’s hope they don’t grow any higher,” Ostapenko said, finishing the conversation with his intonation. “Major, give my people a lift to the base.” - Got it! - The major waited until the general and the colonel returned to the Hummer and shouted: - Hey, screws, all on the armor! “Will we get covered in fuel oil?” someone from the column shouted. - Let's chat! Get in while we're still nice, we'll take you straight to the location. - Are you crazy?! Everything is on fire there! Guys, they want to take us back! - Die, you bastard! - someone from the stalker group shouted. - If you have to, then you have to! You're in the Zone, not at a resort! The continuation of the verbal altercation was drowned out by the roar of the tank turbines. Ostapenko sat down in the Hummer next to the driver and thoughtfully stared at the windshield. Bibik was eager to discuss the news, but he still paused for a minute for the sake of decency and only then asked: – Do you think the assassination attempt and arson were planned in the Thirteenth? - And you think so, don't you? - Yes, but... Bibik didn't finish his sentence. The communicator in his pocket suddenly buzzed. - He's alive! - the colonel rejoiced. - Mister General, the Old One is calling! “Give me the loud one,” Ostapenko ordered. Bibik turned on the speakerphone and answered the call. - Old man, I'm listening to you! - Hello, Bibik, do you hear?! - I hear, I hear! - Do you hear?! Damn it, interference! Okay, screw it, I'm reporting! The main enemy has been destroyed along with the main artifact. Do you hear?! - No, I'm not deaf! Excellent! Well done! Where are you now? - What?! Oh, I'm in Pripyat! Remember where you and I surfaced in the fall? Right on this pier. - Don't move anywhere, we'll send a helicopter. - Got it! When?! - Soon! - Got it! End of communication. "Did I blurt out anything unnecessary?" Bibik asked Ostapenko after hanging up. - Do you hear? - The general raised his index finger up. - I hear. - The colonel pressed his face to the window. - Everything is in smoke. But judging by the sound, it's helicopters. Only those are probably firefighters. - So, we'll send the firefighters, and at the same time they'll get some water from the pond. Is there a connection with the duty officer? - We'll fix it now. When the connection was established, Ostapenko had a short but productive talk with headquarters. On the one hand, the head of counterintelligence was indirectly to blame for the fact that the enemy agents had brainwashed the generals, but on the other hand, it turned out that not all of the headquarters staff had fallen under psi-control. And why they, being of sound mind, had almost destroyed the entire Zone isolation system was a much more sensitive issue than all the questions to counterintelligence put together. In general, “just in case,” headquarters was very glad that Ostapenko was alive, and they emphasized their joy in every possible way. Including their readiness to carry out any order of the general. Instantly and in the best possible way. - They're doing the right thing, - Bibik commented on what he heard. - You're the one who'll be twisting and turning them all. I just don't get it, will they send a helicopter? - They've already sent it. It'll be there in half an hour. “Okay,” Bibik nodded. “What?” Ostapenko asked thoughtfully. - That's all! And that the Old One managed, and that the mutants were "released" in time. Otherwise, sooner or later, you would have been killed for sure. If not them, then the mercenaries. By the way, if the mutants came to their senses, and the "holes" disappeared thanks to the Old One, you are in his debt. - He'll come back, we'll figure out who owes whom. - Ostapenko took off his cap and wiped away the sweat. - What did he say? "The main enemy has been destroyed along with the main artifact"? - So what? - Nothing. - And still? - Why did he mention some "main artifact"? At one time I only talked to him about the main enemy. There was no talk of any artifact. - Strange. - That's what I'm talking about. The old man is being shady, no doubt about it. And if he's being shady, that means he screwed up with something. Either with this mysterious "main artifact" or with Olga. So we'll see who owes whom and how much. - Even if that's the case. - Bibik smiled indulgently. - The enemy is running. We're returning to base. Your secret plan worked. As they say, I see no reason not to have a drink!

It was nothing new for Andrey to fly a helicopter to D-3 in the middle of the night, but Lunev had never seen the base in such a deplorable state. From above, the drama of the situation could be fully appreciated, despite the thick smoke and darkness. In fact, there was no particular darkness, and the smoke was blown away by the wind and was almost not a hindrance. The smoldering embers all over the area, the puddles of napalm and gasoline that had not been extinguished by the firefighters who had run off their feet, the blazing cars and tents in the shopping area created quite a bright backlight. In addition to this backlight, three searchlights on the roofs of the three surviving buildings of the warehouse complex - the legendary citadel - were doing their best. It was in the courtyard of the only relatively intact facility that the helicopter landed. Before that, however, it had to make a couple of circles over the ruins of the base - the soldiers did not have time to clear the landing pad of large debris, corpses and empty boxes from under mines. It was during an unscheduled flight over the territory that Andrei saw the scale of the destruction. Strictly speaking, the D-3 base was wiped off the face of the earth. The three surviving buildings of the warehouse complex could only be considered "the saved regimental banner." But even if all eight two-story warehouse buildings had survived the attack, the state of the base would not have looked less horrific. The warehouses made up barely one twentieth of all the buildings on the base, and they occupied even less area. And that's not counting the trade sector, the tent city, and the airfield. The vast territory had turned into a smoldering dump, cemetery, and cattle burial ground all at once. The only thing that was almost absent here was radioactive waste. But that seemed little consolation. The helicopter finally got the go-ahead to land and carefully squeezed its grey carcass onto a tiny patch in the center of the cleared parade ground. At the edge of the improvised landing pad, the arrivals were met by a very representative delegation: a dozen special forces soldiers led by Colonel Bibik. As soon as the helicopter touched the pad, the soldiers formed a living corridor, preparing to receive the especially dangerous prisoner promised during the communication session with the approaching aircraft. Not seeing anyone except their own: Stary himself, Tatyana and Scout, the soldiers began to look around at Bibik in surprise, and upon discovering that Scout was unarmed and tied up, the special forces were completely at a loss. Bibik gave them a sign to remain calm and nodded to Andrei to step aside. - Well, what are we going to do with him? - It's up to you. - Andrey shrugged. - He got everything from me. - Well, you understand, there will be an investigation and all that. You will be called as a witness... - Do I need it, you wanted to ask? - Andrey grinned. - No, no need. You don't have to bring it to an investigation, you know how it's done. - I know, but for this I need your consent. "Let's not air our dirty laundry in public," Lunev nodded. "After what we've been through, anyone could go nuts." - Well. - Bibik nodded with satisfaction. - And he also came back from the hospital. Rehabilitation wasn't over. And he's already had nervous breakdowns. He's been in a mental hospital twice. - Here comes the third breakdown. The most severe one. Voices started to sound in the guy's head, and then he started seeing devils. The poor guy will have to be treated for at least a year. Just keep in mind, in a local hospital. In Kyiv he will kick the bucket right away. - Shake on it. - Bibik gave a sign to the special forces. The fighters took Scout by the arms, bent him down and dragged him to the ambulance. One of the remaining specialists helped Tatyana, barely alive from fatigue, cope with the wind raised by the helicopter blades and approach Stary with Bibik. - Welcome back, madam. - The colonel smiled. - How are you? - Shit. - Tatyana looked at Bibik indifferently. - Wow, how scientists have started talking! - The colonel laughed. - Where is your academic style? "You can't describe all this to them." Tatyana pointed with her gaze at the mutant corpses piled up against the wall of the southern warehouse. "Is it possible to fall down and die somewhere here? Just not next to the mutants." - Why die? - Bibik gestured towards the nearest warehouse. - Please! A shower, a clean uniform, a modest dinner and a leather sofa. Everything is at your service. - And the sofa? - Tatyana grabbed the Old Man tightly by the shoulder. - Andrey, tell me this isn't another stupid joke! - Not a joke. - The old man looked condescendingly at Bibik. - And if it is a joke, then it's a really stupid one. - So do good to people! - The colonel sighed with feigned disappointment. - You treat them with kindness, and they try to offend you! Go! It's all true. Get yourself in order, go up to the second floor, to the chief's office, have coffee and dessert. Third door on the left... or fourth... in short, you'll find it... ...It took two hours to get themselves in order and have a modest dinner. However, neither Bibik nor Ostapenko began to scold the walkers for their sluggishness. The general was beaming with an uncharacteristic kindness for him, and Bibik brewed real coffee and kindly poured thirty drops of "five-star" into it. Considering the military field conditions and the extreme exhaustion of both sides, such a reception could well be considered chic. As a return gesture, Andrei did not crumple his report and described the raid and all the difficulties that fell on the heads of the walkers in the brightest colors. Of course, as best he could. As Andrei finished laying out his version of events, the ancient wall clock in the former office of the warehouse complex chief, now Ostapenko's temporary office, struck midnight. The pedantic general glanced briefly at his wristwatch and nodded with satisfaction. It was unclear what he was satisfied with: the accuracy of the antique clock or the Stary's story, but the satisfied grimace was firmly glued to Ostapenko's face. Even when the general said that he felt sorry for Brazhnikov and the Mechanic, his expression did not change. - And I feel sorry for Scout, - Bibik boomed in the corner. - Or maybe I'm just offended... I don't know. We're not blind, but we overlooked! For so many years the traitor hung around, pretended to be one of us, even admitted to being a coward, I sympathized with him... and look how it turned out. “It’s not the scout’s fault,” Tatyana quietly intervened. “Andrey, tell them.” The old man glanced briefly at his friend, who was warming herself in the leather chair, and reluctantly nodded. - In general, yes, it is not his fault that six years ago he found himself almost at the epicenter of the Second Catastrophe. The outbreak changed him, like Olga, not externally, but only internally. To put it mildly, they both became mutants, but only latent ones. And roughly speaking, both of them became the same zombies, but only, so to speak, in a lighter version and with the beginnings of telepathic abilities. They both lived at the expense of the Zone. If they had gone further into the outside world, they would have died immediately from causes unknown to normal medicine. That is what Scout was afraid of. Not the Zone, but excommunication from it. And exposure. Any stalker would have killed him on the spot, having learned that Scout was a latent mutant. The enemy promised him to find a way, if not to get rid of the addiction, then at least to increase his vitality outside the Zone. And Scout believed the Engineer, but did not understand that it was about expansion and turning the whole world into the Zone. And when he understood, it was too late. - Well, well. - Bibik sighed. - So he was a dead man? But he drank vodka like he was alive. And when they wounded him, he almost really kicked the bucket. “He was alive in the Zone, just like the rest of us,” Tatyana objected wearily. “And he betrayed us because the Engineer deceived him.” - Well, well, what a naive lamb! - Bibik slammed his fist on the armrest of his chair. - I won't forgive you! - And it is not necessary, - Ostapenko said calmly. - It turns out that Olga is also a latent mutant? Why did you let her go? - Why not? - The Old Man shrugged. - Olga made the right choice and helped destroy the Engineer, although it cost her a lot of moral effort. To kill a monster so similar to her father... personally, I take my hat off. After all, her generation doesn't know that Pavlik Morozov is a hero. By all accounts, she should have made a choice in favor of the enemy. But she didn't do it - which means she thinks correctly. "A mutant, let him think correctly for a while, sooner or later he will reveal his essence," the general objected, frowning. "If Olga finds the entrance to the Thirteenth Sector again, the metamorphosis will definitely happen." - He won't find it, - muttered Bibik. - The entrance to Chernobyl-2 was closed, and in other places too. There were no entrances there, only exits, you said it yourself. My scouts checked everything. Pashkov reported five minutes ago. The sector is in complete isolation. - Good, if so. - Ostapenko looked with displeasure at the colonel who had interrupted at the wrong time. - But at headquarters they will definitely reprimand me for the fact that the Old Man let a potential enemy go, and that the crystal disappeared to an unknown place. - Are you checking? - Andrey grinned. - I can repeat: the crystal, also known as the "Key" artifact, remained in the Thirteenth Sector. - Well, yes. - Ostapenko grinned crookedly. - First you stuffed it into the monster's throat to turn it inside out, and then you picked up the crystal and threw it into the "contact spot", did I understand correctly? He suddenly turned to Tatiana and stared at her with extreme suspicion. “Yes,” she blurted out in surprise. “No,” Andrey rushed to her rescue. “And don’t take us with a cannon, Mr. General.” - Who's taking whom? - Ostapenko said in false surprise. - I just want to hear - "yes" or "no". - No. We didn't throw anything into the "contact spot". And we couldn't have done it even if we wanted to. Firstly, it was a clear exit from the sector; you can't go back through that "hole". Secondly, even if this passage had been two-way, it closed the moment the monster died. The crystal simply disappeared at the same time as the Engineer died. That's why I decided that it fell into where it belonged, or evaporated... In short, I don't know what happened to it, but the "Key" disappeared. I didn't dig around in the monster's remains particularly carefully, but I'm still one hundred percent sure: the crystal is gone. "My scouts were poking around," Bibik muttered again. "There's nothing there. Just stinking guts." Andrey silently pointed at the colonel. Ostapenko grinned mysteriously again, but pretended to finally believe him. - It was even more dangerous to let Olga go. She would accidentally end up in the sector, and everything would be ready for the new coming. And the "failed" crystal would be there, and the monsters, and the anomalous energy would accumulate... enough for the Third Catastrophe. This sector is a damned mine, a real mine! - Stop grumbling, Mr. General. - The old man tiredly ran his hand over his face. - A new brawl requires two indispensable conditions - a crystal and an Engineer. Both things are unique, and both are out of the game. Even if Olga decides to follow in her father's footsteps... she won't get into the sector. Relax. - Well, as for relaxing, that's more like what you need. - Ostapenko looked condescendingly at Tatyana, and then at Andrey. - It would be nice, if you don't mind. - Lunev shook his head, as if in response to his thoughts. - But I have one question left. Until I get an answer, I won't be able to relax. - Ask. - How did you know? - About what? – About the Engineer and Olga. - Oh, that. - Ostapenko grinned. - It's a company secret, Andrey. I'm a counterintelligence officer, remember? - I remember. But I have little faith in the mystical abilities of your company. If you were what you want to seem, the Engineer would have been left high and dry long ago. And Olga wouldn't have had to be exchanged like a pawn. - I would have had to, Andrey, I would have had to. Otherwise, it would have been impossible to get close to the Engineer. If Olga hadn't been in the group, he would have destroyed you before you even entered the Thirteenth Sector. And with her, you began to be of interest to the enemy. He even let you into his lair. Will you say that I miscalculated the situation? - They calculated it like a computer, correctly. And just as heartlessly. Olga, by the way, is a living person. And you fed her to this monster as if she were a poisoned piece of meat! - She's a mutant. - A human being! - Andrey shook his head. - Not like us physically, but in his soul... maybe even more human than you! - Words, words... - Ostapenko winced. - Let's have a rest, and then we'll continue this argument. If you want to. As for our company's capabilities, I'll definitely let you in on all the secrets, Andrey, I promise. - I don't give a damn about your secrets, - Andrey waved his hand wearily. - And about your company too. - That's good. - Ostapenko nodded. - You're free. I won't bother you for a day for sure. And the day after tomorrow... Andrey, be so kind as to come by, I have something to talk about. - Stop! - Lunev, despite his fatigue, raised his hand sharply. - One more word and you'll be looking for me in Australia! No more contracts! I'm fed up, sorry. - I'm not insisting, - the general made a sympathetic face. - None, then, none! Just come in, we'll talk. If we come to an agreement, I promise I won't bother you for at least a month. Honey. - And if not? - The old man looked at the general from under his brows. - So, no. - Ostapenko met his gaze. - But I think you'll come in. Without me, you'll be out of work, and without work, like a Scout without a Zone, you won't live long. You don't have that kind of character. - I don't promise anything. - Andrey stood up and offered his hand to Tatyana. - Nothing! When the door closed behind the couple, Ostapenko and Bibik exchanged glances. “Will he come back?” Bibik asked with hidden hope. “One hundred percent,” Ostapenko answered confidently. “I think he’s had enough exams, it’s time to move on to real business.” - And I told you right away that the guy would pull it off! - The colonel rubbed his hands. - Well, now let's get going! - Easy, Bibik, easy. - The general patted the table. - No one is going anywhere yet. We're resting and preparing the operation. As I said - a month. God willing, everything will work out, then we'll go. You're free for now, too. - Got it! - Bibik energetically rose from his chair and saluted. - I have the honor! And may the Zone help us... Lately, the ancient mossy pier and the half-ruined landing stage had become something of a must-see place for Olga if she wandered into Pripyat. She didn't understand why this was happening, and she didn't try to understand it. She just liked it here. To sit on the greenish rough stones, look at the water and not think about anything. Nirvana. No, she couldn't help but think. Literally two steps behind her, on the other side of the dense thickets, a ruined city was teeming with abnormal life, she had to keep her ears open, but still Olga felt calmer and more comfortable here than in the most reliable shelter. Perhaps the calm was inspired by the measured splash of the waves, or perhaps by the realization that she could simply swim away from mutants or evil people at any moment... Olga couldn't find a clear explanation for the serenity that enveloped her in this place. In the depths, at the bottom of a familiar underwater pit, a huge shadow stirred, and Olga shook off her sleepy stupor for a while. Olga's old acquaintance, the giant catfish, was still alive and well, although it had many competitors in the cooling pond and Pripyat. Rumors about several new species of aquatic mutants had spread throughout the Zone three months ago, and Olga had seen for herself that it was true. Overgrown newts, leeches with the habits of piranhas, five-meter snakes with a mouth like a crocodile - all these evil spirits were mastering the zonal waters quite successfully. However, the giant catfish remained in its rightful place for now, and for some reason this fact pleased Olga. Probably because she was pretty tired of the rapid changes in the Zone, and she desperately wanted to cling to at least something stable, unchanging, despite all the abnormal laws of the local crazy nature. She wanted to cling to something that vaguely resembled Olga herself... as she was before the raid in the Thirteenth Sector. The catfish was quite suitable. He had changed only once, probably during the Second Catastrophe, and since then he had stubbornly ignored all new "tempting offers" from the Zone. Although it was unlikely that it could offer him anything more. He had already found something that was beyond the wildest fish dreams. He was the largest fish in a small pond and had no problems with feeding. There was plenty of biomass in the Zone, and a significant part of it settled on the bottom of the cooling pond. What else did a catfish need to be happy? Olga smiled sadly. "Fish dreams... what stupidity! What dreams can these brainless creatures have? On the other hand, if in a figurative sense, my dreams can easily be called "fish dreams" too. I am forever stuck in the Zone and now I need no more to be happy than this catfish. Food, water and oxygen. No prospects, and therefore no aspirations. Well, who am I after this, not a fish, or what? No, of course, I can come up with a plot for myself, like the stalkers do, or start weaving intrigues, like my father did... no, not my father, a monster in his guise. But why? Unlike him, I do not want the Zone to be everywhere. And I can’t find any other motivation for weaving intrigues. So that leaves..." A loud splash did not allow Olga to finish her thought. A high wave hit the parapet. The shadow of the catfish suddenly grew larger, and Olga could clearly see the huge smooth back of the monster that had surfaced. The girl pulled her legs up, preparing to jump back, but for some reason she changed her mind. The catfish hung a meter from the surface, barely moving its fins. Its long and thick, like the tentacles of a giant octopus, whiskers slowly moved, as if stirring the water under the belly of the fish. The movements of the monster's whiskers were mesmerizing. Olga even held her breath. The peculiar "freeze frame" lasted about a minute. Then the catfish suddenly lay on its left side and began to dive quickly. Olga exhaled, and at that very moment the fish's huge whisker rose above the surface of the water. Like a whip, it loudly slapped the pier to Olga's left and quickly slid into the water. If the blow had landed a little to the right, there would undoubtedly have been only a wet spot left of the thoughtful young lady. The most reasonable thing would be to dash away from the shore before the catfish tried to get its lunch again, but Olga didn't move. The catfish wasn't hunting, she knew that for sure. The girl wiped the cold splashes from her face with her sleeve and slowly turned her head to the left. The catfish did not hunt, one hundred percent. And Olga was now ready to argue about fish being brainless creatures. Maybe in the reservoirs of the mainland they are primitive creatures, but in the Zone... Olga hesitantly reached out to a large piece of mud and with two fingers pulled out a silver chain with a large crystal hanging from it. The milky-white glow of the artifact was weak, but she could not help but recognize it. It was the very artifact with which the Engineer turned the Zone space inside out. The key to the Thirteenth Sector and at the same time... to the entire world. Olga's first desire was to spin the crystal on the chain above her head and throw it back into the pond, but she imagined what would happen next and changed her mind. Sooner or later she would return to this pier, and the catfish would repeat its trick. The inevitable will happen, what is predestined cannot be erased. Olga thoughtfully fingered the chain, then removed the remaining mud, hung the crystal around her neck and stood up. Olga didn't know where she would go or what she would do. And it didn't matter yet. For starters, forward. And then we'll see. The Zone will definitely tell you the right decision. Definitely. As always.

Wanderer from the Military. Recruitment date 10/23/2012. Ultimate loyalty remains in doubt. Did not carry out special assignments. Not listed in "Zetta". The data on the equipment supply channels obtained last time, use according to the Apostle's requirements. The meeting is scheduled for the 17th. Transfer with the permission of the Envoy. The object must be destroyed after the transaction is completed.

Biological clone of subject number seventeen. Recruitment date 11/20/2011. Ultimate loyalty is beyond doubt. Chief intermediary of Operation Passage. In the list of "Zetta" goes by the name Lukon. Contact after the main order is fulfilled.

It is enough for you to know that he constantly monitors all your work. So do not even try to deceive anyone. In case of disobedience, or disclosure of identity, you will have to be eliminated. If the Mediator also falls under suspicion, then he too...

The entrance to the object is disguised as sewer communications. And when the enlightened one brought knowledge, light descended from the heavens, which covered their abode with a glow pointing to the northeast. To the most extreme point.

The pentagram of these angels will cover the city and punish the fanatics. They are always born according to the same pattern, one after another. Finding the past place by the existing ones will not be difficult.
