The main (and actually the only) difference between the AK-103 and the AK-74M is the caliber - this "hundredth" series assault rifle uses 7.62mm (7.62x39 M43) cartridges and corresponding magazines (compatible with magazines of older AK / AKM assault rifles), otherwise the assault rifles are identical. In 2006, a significant number of AK-103 assault rifles (100,000 units) were purchased in Izhevsk for the Armed Forces of Venezuela.

Compact Kalashnikov assault rifle of the "hundredth series" of 5.45 caliber. It is based on full-size assault rifles of the same series, based on the standard assault rifle of the Russian Army AK-74M. The main differences from the "full-size" brothers were a shortened barrel (314 mm) with a special muzzle-flame suppressor and a modified sighting bar, which has markings only up to 500 meters (1000 for full-size models). The assault rifle has a side-folding plastic buttstock and a standard bar located on the left side of the receiver for installing optical or night sights. Structurally, it is completely similar to the AK-74M assault rifle.

The AK-107 and AK-108 assault rifles were developed at the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant (IZHMASH) based on earlier models created for participation in the Abakan competition program. The main creators of the new assault rifles are Yu. K. Aleksandrov and V. N. Paranin, and the design of these assault rifles was based on the experimental Izhevsk assault rifles AL-4 (developed in the late 1960s) and AL-7 (a later development). Both of these assault rifles were distinguished by the so-called "balanced" automation, similar in concept to that used in the AEK-971 assault rifle from the Kovrov Mechanical Plant. Such automation allows for a significant improvement in the weapon's performance when firing bursts of any length, compared to traditional Kalashnikov assault rifles, on the basis of which the AL-4 and AL-7, as well as the AK-107/108, were developed. \n In essence, the main difference between the AK-107 5.45mm and AK-108 5.56mm NATO assault rifles and the Kalashnikov AK-74M and AK-101 assault rifles, respectively, is the modified design of the automatics, which received a second gas piston with a counterweight installed on its rod, and a synchronizing device located between the bolt carrier and the balance beam. There are a number of other differences, in particular, a modified design of the receiver cover mount, but they are less significant. The AK-107 and AK-108 assault rifles differ from each other only in the cartridges used. The automatics are built according to a gas-operated scheme with a long stroke of the gas piston and an additional balance beam, which has its own, second gas piston, moving in the opposite direction to the main one. Synchronization of the bolt carrier and the balance beam is carried out using a gear located vertically between them. In addition, an original part has been introduced into the design, eliminating the rebound of moving parts when they reach the extreme position corresponding to the forward position of the bolt carrier (at the end of the reloading cycle). The barrel is locked by a rotary bolt, similar in design to the bolt of the Kalashnikov AK-74 assault rifle.

AKM - Kalashnikov assault rifle modernized. The assault rifle that replaced the AK in service with the Soviet army in 1959 and is its further development. The main differences between the AKM and its predecessor. Increased sighting range (from 800 m to 1000 m). New stamped receiver, which made it possible to reduce the weight of the assault rifle. Raised buttstock, which brought the point of emphasis closer to the firing line. A trigger delay device has been added, allowing the bolt carrier to stabilize in the extreme forward position before the next shot to increase the stability of the assault rifle and improve the accuracy of fire. Stability in the horizontal plane has been increased by moving the point of impact of the bolt carrier in the forward position from the right side to the left. Muzzle compensator, which increased the accuracy of shooting from unstable positions (on the move, standing, kneeling). Instead, a PBS or a blank firing attachment can be installed on the thread. A short (150 mm blade) detachable bayonet-knife was introduced, which had more of a household purpose than a combat one. Instead of a second blade, it received a saw, and in combination with a sheath, it could be used to cut barbed wire fences.

AKMS is a variant of the AKM with a folding stock. The stock attachment system was changed relative to the AKS (it folded down and forward, under the receiver). The modification was developed specifically for paratroopers.

In 1978, the USSR Ministry of Defense announced a competition to create an automatic weapon that would surpass the AK-74 in combat qualities. The main requirement was to increase the effectiveness of shooting, including from awkward positions: on the move, kneeling, without support. As a result of preliminary studies, a scheme with a recoil momentum shift was adopted for further development.\n In 1981, the Abakan competition was announced, with twelve models participating. According to the results of the competition, the AS assault rifle (with a shifted recoil impulse), developed by Gennady Nikonov, was recognized as the most promising. By 1986, Nikonov's assault rifle had been improved and received the name "ASM" (with a shifted impulse, modernized). In 1991, the ASM passed state tests in the Taman Division, showing, compared to the AK-74, a significant increase in the accuracy of firing fixed bursts, even from awkward positions. Based on the test results, it was recommended for adoption. However, the process dragged on. In 1997, the ASM, under the official name "5.45-mm Nikonov assault rifle mod. 1994 (AN-94)", was adopted by the Russian Army. The assault rifle was named after the competition it won - "Abakan". Although the original plans (1980s) assumed a complete rearmament of the army with new assault rifles, modifications of the Kalashnikov assault rifle remained in service. Both the complexity of the assault rifle and economic reasons played a role. The machine gun is characterized by high accuracy and shooting precision: when firing in bursts of two rounds at a distance of 100 m, a good shooter hits one point on the target with two bullets [3]. The high rate of the first two shots has a positive effect on both the accuracy and the effectiveness of shooting (compared to single shots, the lethal, stopping effect and armor penetration are significantly increased, and the probability of hitting protected targets increases). Recoil when firing fixed bursts has virtually no effect on shooting accuracy; aimed fire is possible without resting the machine gun on the shoulder. However, it should be taken into account that the shift in the recoil impulse allows you to compensate for the recoil only from the first two shots. When firing long bursts, the effect of recoil compensation is practically absent, and the AN-94 is on the same level as the AK-74 in terms of accuracy and bullet spread.

The compact assault rifle OTs-12 "Tiss" was developed in the early 1990s at the Tula Central Design Bureau of Sporting and Hunting Weapons (TsKIB SOO). This assault rifle was intended to arm the personnel of the Ministry of Internal Affairs. A small batch of OTs-12 assault rifles was released in 1993 at TsKIB SOO, but serial production was never launched. Currently, a small number of OTs-12 assault rifles are still in service with a number of units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Russia. In terms of design, the compact assault rifle OTs-12 "Tiss" is completely identical to the Kalashnikov assault rifle AKS-74U, differing from it only in the barrel with the muzzle device (muzzle brake-compensator), the dimensions of the bolt face and the magazine (for 9x39 SP-5 and SP-6 cartridges).

Silent grenade launcher system "Kanareyka": BS-1M grenade launcher on AKS-74UB assault rifle with PBS-4 silencer. Leather pad on the assault rifle fore-end serves to cover the bracket for mounting the grenade launcher on the fore-end when the grenade launcher is removed from the weapon. The assault rifle is equipped with a rare 20-round magazine, the grenade launcher sight is folded. \n In connection with the adoption and mass delivery to the Soviet troops of a new small arms system chambered for 5.45 mm caliber cartridge, it became necessary to adapt a silent underbarrel grenade launcher to the new weapon. Since acceptable results when firing 5.45 US cartridges with a silencer were achieved only on shortened AKS-74U (UB) assault rifles, a new version of the system, designated 6S1 "Kanareyka", was developed specifically for this assault rifle. While maintaining the general design of the grenade launcher and grenade and their general appearance, the designers of TsNII TOCHMASH managed to increase both the effectiveness of the grenade's warhead and the firing range of the grenade launcher, while instead of a blank cartridge of caliber 7.62x39mm, special blank cartridges of caliber 5.45x39 began to be used. \n Around the beginning of the eighties, the silent 30mm grenade launcher system 6S1 "Kanareyka" consisting of: grenade launcher BS-1M, assault rifle AKS-74UB and silencer PBS-4 began to be supplied to special forces units of the Soviet Army.

The AEK 971 assault rifle was developed in the 1980s at the Kovrov Machine-Building Plant (KMZ) under the supervision of S.I. Koksharov as part of the Abakan competition program. The design feature of the AEK971 is a balanced automation scheme based on a gas engine (similar to the AK-107/108 assault rifles). \n With this scheme, an additional gas piston connected to a counterweight moves synchronously with the main one, moving the bolt carrier, but towards it, thereby compensating for the impulses that occur during the movement of the bolt group and during its impacts in the rear and forward positions (it is no secret that one of the design features of Kalashnikov assault rifles, which ensured the high reliability of the weapon, is the arrival of the moving parts of the automation in extreme positions at a significant speed, and therefore, at the moments of movement of the bolt group, the assault rifle receives significant and multidirectional impulses of movement, negatively affecting the accuracy of automatic fire). As a result, the shooter feels only the impulse from the recoil when firing, and the machine gun does not twitch when firing bursts, but rather sticks to the shoulder. Thus, in the AEK971 machine gun it was possibleachieve automatic fire accuracy 2 or more times better than that of the AKM or AK-74 assault rifles (when firing from the AEK973 7.62 mm caliber and the AEK 971 5.45 mm caliber, respectively).

AEK-973S - a variant of the AEK-973, with a retractable telescopic buttstock, a modified shape and angle of the pistol grip. The cartridge is 7.62x39 mm, the frame buttstock does not fold by turning, but slides into the receiver, the safety catch - fire selector on the right side of the receiver. The shape of the pistol grip has been changed. for comfortable holding of the machine gun with the buttstock retracted.

The Groza OTs-14 automatic grenade launcher system was developed in Tula, at the TsKIB SOO, and was produced in small batches at the Tula Arms Plant in the mid-1990s. The development of the automatic grenade launcher system for close combat was started in 1992 by designers Valery Telesh (developer of the 40mm GP-25 and GP-30 underbarrel grenade launchers) and Yuri Lebedev, and the first prototypes were ready in 1994. The main idea behind the creation of a specialized system was that the traditional installation of an underbarrel grenade launcher on a standard assault rifle (be it AK-74 or M16A2) greatly worsens the balance of the weapon, and therefore it is necessary to initially design the weapon taking into account the installation of a grenade launcher on it. In addition, due to the modular design of the weapon, it was supposed to achieve great flexibility in its use. \n Initially, this assault rifle-grenade launcher system was created for the special forces of the Ministry of Internal Affairs for special 9mm cartridges SP-5 and SP-6. The "Groza-1" variant (another designation is TKB-0239) was created for the army special forces for the widely used 7.62x39 cartridge. The OTs-14 "Groza" assault rifle is based on the receiver and mechanisms of the AKS-74U assault rifle - the main changes concerned the adaptation of the bolt to a different cartridge with a larger diameter of the base of the cartridge case, and the firing mechanism. In addition, the OTs-14 is designed according to the bullpup scheme, so that the pistol grip for controlling fire is moved forward, in front of the magazine, and the butt plate is attached directly to the rear of the receiver. The highlight of the OS-14 is its variable configuration: the basic machine gun can be used as a carbine, an assault rifle (with an extended muzzle and an additional front handle for holding), or a silent machine gun (with a silencer).machine gun-grenade launcher complex (the standard fire control handle and forend are replaced with a fire control handle with a trigger switch "machine gun-grenade launcher" and an under-barrel 40mm grenade launcher). The OTs-14 assault rifle underwent military trials during the counter-terrorist operation in Chechnya, but did not gain much popularity and never entered mass production.

AS "Val" (Special Assault Rifle) is a silent assault rifle developed at the Klimovsk TsNIITochMash by designers P. Serdyukov and V. Krasnikov in the second half of the 1980s along with the VSS silent sniper rifle and is in service with special forces units of Russia. "Val" and "Vintorez" are 70% unified, which has a positive effect on production and operation. The assault rifle is intended for special units of state security and internal affairs agencies.\n The machine gun is based on a gas-operated automatic system with a long working stroke of the gas piston. The buttstock is a tubular metal folding to the left, strong enough for use in close combat, when folded it does not interfere with shooting. The pistol grip, forend and double-row magazines are plastic. The built-in silencer also acts as a flame arrester. Thanks to it and cartridges with a subsonic speed, the sound pressure level during a shot is 130 dB. The fire mode selector is located at the rear of the trigger guard, and the safety catch and reloading handle are on the right. It uses special 9-mm subsonic cartridges SP-5 and SP-6. The bullet of the sniper cartridge SP-5 (with a steel core) penetrates class 2 body armor or a 6-mm steel sheet at a range of 100 m, and 2-mm - at the entire range of aimed fire, while maintaining energy sufficient to defeat a hidden enemy. The armor-piercing cartridge SP-6 with an enlarged core made of hardened steel is capable of disabling a car and even lightly armored vehicles. It penetrates an 8-mm steel sheet at a range of 100 m, and a 5-mm steel sheet or class 3 body armor - at the entire range of aimed fire. These indicators are comparable to those of standard cartridges of caliber 5.56 mm, 5.45 mm, which have several times greater muzzle energy. Disassembled into its main components (receiver with barrel, automatics, trigger and fore-end, silencer with sights, buttstock) the machine gun is transported in a special case together with sights and magazines. Assembly takes from 30 to 60 seconds depending on the shooter's training.

The design and operating principle of the Kbs wz.96 Beryl assault rifle are similar to those of the wz.88 Tantal assault rifle, the main changes were made to the design of a longer barrel (457 mm long, the barrel had 6 right-hand grooves with a pitch of 228 mm), a new slotted muzzle brake-flame suppressor, receiver, fore-end, sights, magazine, and butt. The metal butt, folding to the right of the receiver, received a more durable frame-type design, borrowed from the Belgian FNC assault rifle. The back of the butt was equipped with a rubber pad, which helped to partially dampen the powerful recoil when firing from an under-barrel grenade launcher. The trigger mechanism with two-sided controls remained the same, designed for single, fixed three-shot and automatic fire. To stabilize the weapon when firing automatic fire, a light metal bipod is used, which is put on the barrel of the machine gun. "BERIL" can be equipped with a 40-mm underbarrel grenade launcher Kbk-g wz.1974 Pallad and its sight. The flash suppressor is adapted for firing NATO standard rifle grenades. \n The receiver differed slightly from the receiver of the Kbk wz.88 assault rifle. The weapon received a new plastic fore-end and gas chamber barrel pad. However, the shape and execution of the fore-end changed. Unlike the fore-end used in the Kbk wz.88 Tantal assault rifle, it acquired a different shape and received diagonal ribs for better grip of the weapon.\n The Kbs wz.96 Beryl receiver cover can be equipped with a special quick-release bar for mounting various sights: PCS-6 night IR, SK-3 collimator, LKA-4 optical or CWL-1 laser target designator. The weapon is fed from 30-round modernized magazines from the KA wz.90. The 5.56 mm Kbs wz.96 Beryl assault rifles come with a blade bayonet-knife.

Steyr AUG (Armee Universal Gewehr - army universal rifle) is a small arms complex, released in 1977 by the Austrian company Steyr-Daimler-Puch. Adopted by such countries as Austria, New Zealand, Luxembourg and Ireland. In Australia, the rifle is produced under license under the brandF88. It has interchangeable barrels of different lengths: the main 508 mm, as well as shortened barrels of 350 mm and 407 mm and a heavy barrel of 621 mm. This rifle uses the "Bullpup" layout, in which the magazine and bolt assembly are located behind the fire control handle and trigger. There are two fire modes: single shot fire and automatic fire. It is also possible to upgrade to fire single shots and with a cutoff of 3 shots. There is no fire mode translator. The modes are selected by the depth of pressing the trigger. Light pressing results in a single shot. Pressing it all the way causes automatic fire in a random burst. The rifle can be equipped with an American 40-mm underbarrel grenade launcher M203 \n The Steyr AUG A2 differs from the basic model by the presence of a universal mount for NATO standard sights (Weaver rail) and a folding front handle.

M16A2 is a variant of the M16A1 rifle, adapted for the SS109/M855 cartridge. Differences from the M16A1: a reinforced barrel with a 1:7 twist rate, improved sights marked up to 800 m, a new fore-end and butt made of impact-resistant polyamide, replacement of the continuous fire mode with a mode with a cut-off of a burst after 3 shots (the Marine Corps received a variant of the M16A2 with the ability to conduct continuous fire), a new flash suppressor with slots only in the upper part (due to which the upward drift of the weapon was compensated). The new rifle began to be equipped with lightweight plastic magazines for 30 rounds. In the M16A2 modification, a reflector was added, which allows the shooter to fire from the left shoulder without fear of shell casings hitting the face. The rifle can be fitted with a 40mm M203 underbarrel grenade launcher.

The Colt M16A4 assault rifle was adopted by the United States Armed Forces in 1994, along with the M16A3. The M16A4 rifle differs from the A2 in that it has a Picatinny rail on the receiver for mounting any type of sighting devices.

In 1996, at the request of special operations forces, a similar carbine, the M4A1, was developed based on the M16A3 rifle, which was distinguished by a return to fully automatic fire instead of a fire mode with a cutoff of 3 shots. The M4A1 modification, in addition to a short barrel and a telescopic buttstock, received a replaceable carrying handle and, accordingly, a replaceable sight (optical, collimator or night sights can be installed).\n At the same time, the basic version for the M4 and M4A1 carbines was adopted with a removable carrying handle installed on an integral Picatinny rail. Based on the M4A1 carbine, the US Naval Land Operations Center developed a special set of components called the SOPMOD M4 kit. The kit has as its core the M4A1 carbine with a RIS (Rail Interface System) handguard. Various elements of the kit can be attached to the Picatinny rail on the carbine's receiver and to the guides on the RIS handguard: sights - optical 4x ACOG, collimator ("red dot") ACOG, open removable diopter sight; visible and infrared laser target designators; night sights; flashlights; front grip; modified 40-mm M203 grenade launcher with a shortened barrel and an improved sight. In addition, the kit includes a quick-release silencer that fits directly onto the carbine's standard flash suppressor.\n The new carbine model was also adopted by the US Special Operations Forces. From a technical point of view, the M4 and M4A1 models are almost no different from their corresponding versions of the M16A2 and M16A3 rifles.

The FAMAS rifle in all modifications has a bullpup configuration (the magazine and chamber are located behind the trigger and pistol grip fire control). FAMAS uses a rather rare automatic scheme with a free blowback, which has a retarder in the form of a lever. The FAMAS bolt group consists of two parts - a lighter bolt itself and a heavier bolt carrier. A double-arm lever is installed on the bolt, with its lower arm, when the bolt is in the forward (closed) position, resting against a special transverse pin in the receiver, and the upper arm - against the bolt carrier. When a shot is fired, powder gases press on the bottom of the cartridge case, forcing it to move back in the chamber and press on the bolt face. In this case, the lower arm of the lever rests against a fixed pin, and the upper one transmits movement to the massive bolt carrier. Due to the difference in the shoulders, at the first moment after the shot, the bolt itself moves slowly, and it is mainly the bolt carrier that accelerates. By the time the pressure in the chamber drops to a safe level, the retarder lever disengages from the pin, and then the bolt and bolt carrier move together, at the same speed, compressing the return spring. At the same time, the spent cartridge case is extracted and ejected, and a new cartridge is fed on the return stroke of the bolt group. The retarder lever also serves as a safety device against firing when the bolt is not closed, since when turning from the initial position, it pulls back the striker and blocks it until the bolt group is set to the closed position. 
 Another feature of the FAMAS bolt is that it has two symmetrical extractor sockets, and the extractor itself can be moved to the right or left side after partial disassembly. In combination with a spring-loaded plunger ejector located along the bolt axis below the striker, and two ejection ports on both sides of the weapon, this allows the FAMAS to be quickly converted to extract spent cartridges on either side. The ejection port that is not currently in use is covered with a removable plastic cover, which also serves as a cheek rest on the butt. The charging handle has the form of a hook and is located above the barrel, in the opening of the carrying handle. When firing, the loading handle is stationary. The FAMAS barrel is unusual in that it has only 3 right-hand grooves with a pitch of one turn per 228 millimeters, which allows the use of almost any 5.56 mm caliber cartridges - both old ones with a light bullet, such as the American M193, and new ones with a heavier bullet, such as the 5.56 mm NATO / M855 / SS109. 
 The flash suppressor can be used to throw rifle grenades directly from the barrel. The chamber has longitudinal grooves (the so-called Revelli grooves), facilitating the extraction of cartridges in systems with a semi-free breech. The FAMAS trigger mechanism is a hammer, made in the form of a separate quick-detachable module. The flag safety-fire selector is located inside the trigger guard, in front of the trigger. The main modes are "safety", "single shots", "automatic fire". Additionally, on the trigger body behind the magazine, there is a switch that turns on or off an additional mechanism for firing bursts with a cutoff of 3 rounds. The FAMAS receiver is quite compact, most of the parts of the weapon body, including the forend, butt, trigger body and carrying handle, are made of plastic. Sighting devices in the form of a front sight and a diopter rear sight are mounted on the barrel on special stands hidden in the carrying handle. A guide for optical sights can be attached to the carrying handle, and there are folding lightweight aluminum bipods on the fore-end in front. FAMAS rifles are equipped with a carrying strap and a bayonet knife as standard, and they can also be equipped with a 40mm M203 under-barrel grenade launcher.

The HK416 is an automatic rifle created by the German company Heckler Koch based on the M4 carbine. Initially, the new weapon was supposed to be a replaceable module for installation on any lower part of the receiver from the M4 or M16 rifles, but later full-fledged automatic weapons also appeared. The name HK416 symbolizes the continuity from the M4 and M16. The HK416 is available in four versions that differ only in barrel length and, accordingly, weight: - HK416 D20RS - with a barrel length of 505 mm. - HK416 D16.5RS - with a barrel length of 419 mm. - HK416 D14.5RS - with a barrel length of 368 mm. - HK416 D10RS - with a barrel length of 264 mm. The M16's direct gas system has been replaced by the HK G36's short-stroke gas piston design, which is more reliable and resistant to contamination. Improvements to the gas engine have also eliminated the M4's problems with overheating when firing in bursts. The bolt and return mechanism have also been improved. The bolt catch mechanism and traditional T-shaped cocking handle have been retained. The three-position safety/fire mode selector allows for single shots and continuous fire. The long-life barrel is cold-forged and can withstand over 20,000 rounds. The receiver is made of aluminum alloy, and the magazines are made of steel (standard M16 magazines can also be used). The barrel is suspended in the fore-end as a cantilever. The assault rifle has Picatinny rails on the upper surface of the receiver, as well as on four sides of the fore-end, thus providing the ability to use a wide range of accessories. The multi-position telescopic stock is similar in design to that of the M4.

Based on the experience gained by the international coalition forces in Afghanistan and Iraq, where under certain conditions the 5.56x45mm caliber weapon demonstrated insufficient effective firing range and insufficient penetration and stopping power, Heckler-Koch began developing a new 7.62x51mm caliber weapon in early 2005.
 The new 7.62mm automatic rifle developed by Heckler-Koch, designated HK417, is based on the 5.56mm HK416 automatic rifle from the same company.
 In 2007, the NK 417 series rifles were launched into serial production and are offered for arming the army and police forces.
 The HK417 rifle, like the HK416, has a modular design that largely replicates the design of the American M16 rifle, with one important difference - the German HK417 rifle has a modified gas-operated system that uses a conventional gas piston with a short working stroke. There are a number of other differences, but all the main controls and the method of disassembling and assembling the weapon are inherited from the M16, which is primarily due to the fact that one of the main markets for the HK417 was originally supposed to be the United States.

After the M16A3 and M16A4 assault rifles were adopted by the US in the 1990s, a variant of the Colt Commando Model 933 was developed based on their principle, which received a new receiver with a universal guide instead of an integral carrying handle, as well as the ability to install additional guides instead of a fore-end.
 From a technical point of view, the compact Colt Commando model submachine gun is almost no different from the corresponding versions of the M16 rifle.
 The machine gun has the same receiver made of aluminum alloy, consisting of two parts, the same automation with direct removal of powder gases into the bolt carrier and a rotary bolt with 8 lugs.
 The recoil spring is located in the buttstock, and therefore all Commando variants are equipped with telescopic butts rather than side-folding or downward-folding ones.
 Depending on the model, the Colt Commando may have a fully automatic or 3-round burst fire mode (in addition to the mandatory single-shot mode).
 Various variants of the Colt Commando have been adopted by US Special Forces and have also been supplied to other countries, including Israel and the Philippines.

In 2010, the American division of the international arms concern SIG-Sauer offered a whole family of self-loading rifles SIG 716 in 7.62 mm NATO caliber.
 In addition to the SIG 716 Tactical Patrol and SIG 716 Patrol assault rifles, the SIG 716 Precision Sniper rifle is now part of the SIG 716 model range.
 The entire SIG 716 rifle family is based on the same design and construction as the SIG 516 in 5.56 NATO, with the exception of changes related to the use of the 7.62x51 mm cartridge. The sniper rifle differs from the assault rifles only in its longer barrels, manufactured with greater precision, and an adjustable buttstock.
 In essence, this weapon, like the SIG 516, is an attempt to improve the AR-15/M16 rifle system that has become popular in the US by eliminating the most controversial element of this design - direct gas bleed into the bolt carrier, and also to offer a weapon in the 7.62 mm NATO caliber, which is once again gaining popularity.
 The SIG 716 rifle is similar in design to the SIG 516 and follows the AR-15/M16 system.
 The SIG 716 is a gas operated weapon, however, instead of a long gas tube, a separate gas piston with a short stroke is installed above the barrel, transmitting the impulse to the bolt carrier through a special rod. The gas operated unit design includes a three-position manual gas regulator. The bolt is rotary, made in the standard manner for AR-15/M16 type systems, but taking into account the increased caliber.
 The upper and lower halves of the receiver are similar to the base platform and are made of aluminum alloy with an adjustment for the increased dimensions.

The Magpul Masada assault rifle is one of the variants of the Magpul Masada ACWS (Adaptive Combat Weapon System), which allows changing the caliber and class of the weapon in a minimum amount of time and without using tools. Thus, the compact carbine can, at the shooter's discretion, become a "full-size" assault rifle or a tactical sniper rifle.
 The Magpul Masada ACWS system consists of the following main weapon variants:
 - Carbine – carbine, with a barrel length of 14.5 inches (368 mm). Buttstock – combinable, folding and adjustable in length. The buttstock is equipped with a two-position adjustable cheek.
 - CQB – a close combat carbine with a 10.5-inch (267 mm) barrel. Equipped with a shortened version of the fore-end, with additional guides.
 - SPR – sniper version, with 18-inch (457 mm) barrel. Equipped with PRS buttstock (manufactured by Magpul). Sniper buttstock – adjustable in length and height.
 - AK – Masada rifle, but for 7.62x39 mm caliber (the cartridge used in the Kalashnikov AK-47 assault rifle). In addition to the barrel and bolt group, the lower receiver in the AK also changes.
 The rifle consists of 6 main modules: receiver (2 parts, upper and lower receiver), bolt carrier with bolt, barrel with gas-operated mechanism, buttstock and barrel pad.
 The upper part of the receiver (the so-called upper receiver) is stamped from aluminum. Inside it are installed guides for the bolt carrier, made of steel in the form of a single piece. On the upper part of the receiver, along its entire length, there is a Picatinny guide, allowing the installation of various sighting devices. The lower receiver (the lower part of the receiver) is made of high-strength polymer as a single piece with the pistol grip and magazine receiver.

F2000 Tactical - similar to the standard model, but lacks an optical sight, instead of which an additional Picatinny rail is installed, as well as open sights.\n The F2000 is a gas operated weapon, with the barrel locked by rotating the bolt. The design is bullpup. The patented frontal extraction of spent cartridges (the cartridges fall out almost at the muzzle) solves the main problem of weapons with this design - the inability of a left-handed shooter to use the weapon due to spent cartridges hitting the face. At the same time, the weapon can be used by both left-handed and right-handed shooters without any modifications, unlike the Steyr AUG or FAMAS. The location of the two-way safety/fire mode selector under the trigger guard also ensures "two-way" operation of the system. The body is made of polymers, the handguard is plastic, easily removable, in place of which various additional modules can be installed: a laser target designator, a flashlight, a 40-mm grenade launcher, a "non-lethal" module M303, designed for firing capsules containing paint or tear gas. In the future, other modules can be installed at the customer's request. The standard sight is an optical 1.6X with a wide field of view. It can be quickly replaced with any other sight that has the appropriate mounts, for example, a special computerized fire control module. This module includes a laser rangefinder and a ballistic computer that sets the aiming mark of the sight for both the machine gun itself and the underbarrel grenade launcher (if installed), based on the range data to the target. With this module, the F2000 can be considered a cheaper analogue of the American OICW system.\n

In terms of the automatic device, the XM8 modular rifle is practically no different from the German Heckler-Koch G36 rifle, on the basis of which it was created. The XM8 has the same automatic scheme with a short stroke gas piston located above the barrel, and locking behind the barrel by turning the bolt, which has 7 lugs. The receiver, forend, and butt are made of high-strength plastic. The XM8 barrels are quick-changeable, it is assumed that depending on the desired configuration, the following types of barrels will be used: 318 mm long in the basic version; 229 mm long in the Compact / PDW version; 508 mm in the sniper rifle version and 508 mm weighted - in the Automatic Rifle version, that is, a light machine gun. \n The butts are also easily replaceable, the main one is considered to be a five-position telescopic butt adjustable in length. Instead of the standard fore-end, it is possible to install a 40mm single-shot XM320 grenade launcher (modified Heckler 'n Koch AG36 grenade launcher) with a standard barrel, or a fore-end with an integrated bipod with a machine gun barrel. The carrying handle is also removable, has mounts for various sights. \n The main sight is supposed to be a red dot collimator sight with battery-powered illumination, and various other sights can also be installed. The two-way selector - safety is located above the pistol grip and provides for firing single shots and bursts, fire with a burst cutoff of 2 or 3 shots is not planned. The bolt handle is located above the receiver, like the G36, and is accessible to both hands. The ammunition is fed from detachable box magazines for 30 rounds or, in the machine gun version, also from 100-round drum magazines.

Fabrique Nationale Mk.16 FN SCAR-L (L stands for Light) is a 5.56 NATO caliber assault rifle. The SCAR's automation is based on a short-stroke gas engine separate from the bolt carrier. The upper half of the receiver is made of aluminum, the lower half is made of polymer. Interchangeable barrels are attached to the upper half with two transverse bolts. The safety catch/fire mode selector is located on both sides of the weapon, with single and continuous fire modes. There are cutouts on both sides of the upper part of the receiver for installing the bolt cocking handle. The magazine release button is also duplicated. The plastic buttstock folds to the side and is also adjustable in length. A 40mm underbarrel grenade launcher or a front handle can be additionally installed.

G36K (Kurz) — shortened version, assault rifle with 318 mm barrel. G-36 is based on automatic system with gas engine and short stroke of gas piston. Rotating bolt, with 7 radial lugs, is located in bolt carrier, which moves along one guide rod, on which return spring is put. On the upper surface of bolt carrier there is cocking handle, which protrudes above the upper surface of receiver. In stowed position, cocking handle is located along the axis of weapon and is held in this position by spring, and for cocking it can be bent in any direction by about 90 degrees. When firing, handle moves together with bolt carrier. Receiver is made of plastic with steel inserts, trigger mechanism is made as single block together with pistol grip and trigger guard, and is attached to receiver by means of transverse pins. The trigger mechanism can be supplied in several versions - with or without a firing mode with a cut-off of 2 or 3 rounds. The plastic fore-end is also attached to the receiver with pins, so that for partial disassembly only a cartridge or other object suitable for pushing the pins out of the holes is required. \n The G36 rifle has a side-folding buttstock made of plastic. On the upper surface of the receiver there is a large carrying handle, in the rear part of which there are sighting devices. The standard G36 rifle for the Bundeswehr has two sights - an optical sight with a magnification of 3.5X, and a collimator sight ("red dot") located above it, designed for use at close ranges. The export version of the G36E rifle and the shortened "carbine" G-36K have only one optical sight with a magnification of 1.5X. An even shorter version of the G36C (C stands for Compact or Commando) instead of a carrying handle has universal Picatinny-type rails for mounting sights of any type. The G36 is fed from clear plastic 30-round magazines that have special mounts to allow magazines to be stacked together for quick reloading. Since the G-36's magazine well is designed to NATO standards, the G-36 can use any standard magazine, including the Beta-C 100-round double drum magazine.\n

FN FAL 50.63 (FN "Paratrooper") - a variant for airborne troops (shortened barrel and folding metal butt). The FAL rifle is an automatic weapon built on the basis of gas-operated automation. The rifle uses a scheme with a short working stroke of the gas piston, similar to those adopted in the SAFN-49 and SVT-40 rifles. The gas piston is located above the barrel and has its own return spring. The gas chamber is equipped with a gas regulator, which allows you to adjust the automation to the existing operating conditions of the weapon or completely block the gas tract, which is necessary for launching rifle grenades from the barrel. The barrel is locked by tilting the rear part of the bolt downwards, behind a special ledge in the bottom of the receiver. The bolt is installed in a massive bolt carrier, after the shot receives a short powerful push from the gas piston and then moves by inertia, compressing the return spring. In the fixed-stock variants, the return spring is located in the stock and the bolt carrier interacts with the spring using a special long narrow shank. In the folding-stock variant, the return spring is located in the receiver cover and acts directly on a specially modified bolt carrier. The trigger mechanism (TM) together with the pistol grip and the butt plate of the receiver is made as a separate unit, hinged to the receiver from below, behind the magazine well. To access the rifle's components and mechanisms, this block is folded down and forward after releasing a special latch located at the rear on the left side of the receiver. The safety catch - fire selector is located on the left side of the trigger mechanism, above the trigger, and interacts with the sear of the hammer mechanism. Sighting devices usually include a front sight in the gas chamber and a diopter adjustable rear sight at the rear of the receiver. Depending on the country of manufacture, the design details and rear sight markings may differ. In England, some L1A1 rifles were equipped with a 4X optical sight SUIT (Sighting Unit, Infantry, Trilux) with a luminous aiming mark.Almost all FAL rifle variants are equipped with a combined muzzle device - flash suppressor, which is also used as a guide for throwing rifle grenades. FAL rifles use 20-round magazines; in a number of countries (for example, Canada), for variants with a heavy barrel (C2) used as light machine guns, modified 30-round magazines from Bren L4 machine guns of 7.62 mm NATO caliber are used. It should be noted that magazines from rifles manufactured in countries with the metric system can be used in rifles from countries with the imperial (inch) system, but the opposite is not true (that is, the British L1A1 or Canadian C1 rifle can use magazines from the Belgian FAL or Austrian Stg.58, but the opposite is not possible). The fittings (stock material, forend, pistol grip) of FAL rifles of different countries and years of manufacture may also differ - they can be made of wood, plastic, metal. Rifles may have bipods of various designs, sometimes replacing the missing fore-end when folded (as on the Canadian C2 light machine gun). Almost all rifle variants have sling mounts, and most also have a bayonet mount under the barrel.

VSS (Special Sniper Rifle) "Vintorez" was created for special operations requiring low-noise weapons. The rifle was developed at the Central Research Institute of Precision Engineering (TsNIITOCHMASH) in the city of Klimovsk under the leadership of Petr Serdyukov. Along with the rifle, special ammunition with a subsonic bullet speed was also developed, capable of reliably hitting targets at ranges of up to 400 meters. Such ammunition became the 9x39mm SP-5 cartridges (with a conventional bullet) and SP-6 (with an armor-piercing bullet), created on the basis of the 7.62x39 cartridge case mod. 1943. The muzzle velocity of the SP-5 and SP-6 bullets is about 280 m / s, the weight of the bullets is about 16 grams. \n In service with the VSS since the late 1980s. The VSS rifle is widely and successfully used by various special forces units in the Russian security forces, including in Chechnya. The VSS rifle is based on a gas-powered, rotary bolt mechanism. The gas piston is located above the barrel and is rigidly fixed to the bolt carrier. The rotary bolt has 6 lugs. The receiver is milled from steel to increase the strength of the structure. The bolt handle and safety are made like those of the Kalashnikov assault rifle. However, the fire mode selector is made in the form of a transverse button located behind the trigger. The firing mechanism is also very different from the AK trigger mechanism and has a striker design. The barrel in the front part (after the gas chamber) has several rows of holes that remove part of the powdergases from the bottom of the rifling to the rear of the integrated silencer. In the front part, in front of the muzzle of the barrel, the silencer has a number of steel diaphragms with a hole for the bullet, slowing down the powder gases inside the silencer. \n For cleaning and compact storage, the silencer can be removed from the weapon, but firing without a silencer is prohibited. The VSS sighting devices include a side bar for mounting optical or night sights, as well as a backup open sight mounted on the silencer casing and consisting of a rear sight, adjustable for range up to 400 meters, and a front sight. The standard optical sight for the VSS is the 4X PSO-1, remarked for the ballistics of the 9x39 SP-5 cartridge. The butt of the VSS rifle is wooden, skeletal design. If necessary, it can be easily removed from the weapon for storage in minimal dimensions.

The OTs-03 rifle was proposed to the Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs as a weapon for combat operations in urban conditions, where the increased maneuverability of a short rifle is an absolute plus. The rifle was adopted by the Ministry of Internal Affairs under the designation SVU (Sniper Rifle Shortened), but at the request of the same Ministry of Internal Affairs, its OTs-03A (SVU-A after adoption into service) variant was developed in Tula, which was distinguished by the ability to conduct automatic fire. The latest variant of the OTs-03 line was the OTs-03AS (SVU-AS) variant, which differs from the SVU-A only in the presence of a folding bipod mounted under the barrel on a special bracket. It should be noted that automatic fire from the SVU-A and SVU-AS rifles should be conducted only in emergency cases, since the light barrel and small magazine capacity do not allow for any intensive burst firing. In terms of single-shot accuracy, according to available data, at short and medium ranges, the SVU series rifles are approximately similar in characteristics to the Dragunov SVD rifle. The SVU sniper rifle uses the basic mechanisms and a modified receiver from the SVD sniper rifle. The SVU retains the gas-operated mechanism with a regulator and a short stroke of the gas piston, as well as the locking by rotating the bolt from the SVD. The changes affected the trigger mechanism, which received a long rod connecting it to the forward trigger. In the SVU-A and SVU-AS rifles, the trigger mechanism is modified to ensure automatic fire. The fire mode is selected by the degree of pressure on the trigger - a short press causes single shots, a long press (all the way) - automatic fire. To ensure only automatic fire, there is a special translator, when turned on and limiting the working stroke of the trigger. The rifle barrel is equipped with a special muzzle device that combines the functions of a flame suppressor and a muzzle brake. The sighting devices include a front sight and a diopter rear sight, located on folding bases. The rear sight has range adjustments from 100 to 1300 meters. On the left side of the rifle there is a bar for installing brackets for optical sights. Usually the SVU is used with a PSO- sight.1 fixed magnification 4X. The SVU-AS rifle is distinguished by the fact that it is equipped with a folding bipod, placed on a special bracket under the barrel. The bracket is attached to the receiver to relieve the barrel from the influence of the weight of the bipod and the entire weapon (when shooting from a rest).

In 1958, the GRAU (Main Missile and Artillery Directorate) of the General Staff of the Soviet Army announced a competition to create a self-loading sniper rifle for the Soviet Army. The team headed by E. Dragunov won the competition, and in 1963 the SVD (Dragunov Sniper Rifle) was adopted by the SA. A "sniper" cartridge with a steel-core bullet was created specifically for the SVD, but the rifle can use the entire range of domestic 7.62x54R cartridges. The SVD rifle is a self-loading weapon with gas-operated automation, with a short stroke of the gas piston that is not rigidly connected to the bolt carrier (to reduce the weight of the moving parts of the automation). The design of the gas-operated unit includes a two-position gas regulator. The barrel is locked by rotating the bolt, which has 3 lugs. The receiver is milled from steel. The trigger is non-adjustable, made on a separate base. All versions of the rifle are equipped with removable open sights in the form of a front sight in the front sight and an adjustable rear sight located in front of the receiver cover. The bracket for the optical sight is attached to the receiver on the left. In addition to the main optical sight PSO-1 (fixed magnification 4X), the SVD can be equipped with night sights without illumination NSPU-3 or NSPUM. On early versions of the rifle, the forend and butt of the frame design were made of wood, on more modern versions the forend is made of plastic, the frame butt can be either wooden or plastic. The SVD-S rifles have a separate plastic pistol grip and a side-folding metal butt. The rifle is standardly equipped with a gun belt for carrying. One of the characteristic features of the SVD is the presence of a lug on the barrel for installing a bayonet knife.

The KSVK rifle was developed by the Degtyarev Plant (ZID), located in Kovrov, Russia, based on the earlier SVN-98 rifle. The rifle was originally called ASVK. The rifle is designed to destroy unarmored and lightly armored enemy vehicles and equipment onranges of up to 1000 meters, as well as enemy manpower at ranges of up to 1500 meters. The latter, however, is actually difficult due to the lack of special sniper cartridges of 12.7x108 mm caliber, since standard machine gun cartridges are specially made in such a way as to provide some dispersion when firing. The manufacturer claims an average accuracy of about 16 cm at a range of 300 meters for the KSVK, with the use of special sniper cartridges this characteristic will certainly improve. According to some reports, KSVK rifles are successfully used in the counter-terrorist campaign in Chechnya. \n The KSVK rifle is a non-self-loading large-caliber magazine weapon, designed according to the bullpup scheme. The barrel is locked by a longitudinally sliding rotary bolt, and is fed from a detachable box magazine for 5 rounds. The rifle is equipped with a standard side rail for mounting sights and can be equipped with various day and night sights. In addition, the rifle has open sights as a reserve. Folding bipods are located under the barrel on a special bracket, a powerful muzzle brake-compensator is located on the barrel, the butt is equipped with a wooden pad under the cheek and a rubber butt pad-shock absorber.

The M99 large-caliber sniper / anti-material rifle was developed in China and is offered for export. It is also assumed that it is supplied to the PLA or special police forces of the PRC. The rifle is available in two externally and structurally identical versions, differing only in the cartridge used - the Soviet 12.7x108 (M99-I) or the "Western" 12.7x99 / .50BMG (M99-II). There is no reliable data on the accuracy of this rifle, but the available information allows us to assume an accuracy of about 2 MOA (angular minutes), which is more or less enough to defeat light vehicles and to combat lightly fortified firing points at medium ranges (sniper positions, machine gun nests, etc.). The rifle's automation uses a gas-operated mechanism and a locking mechanism by tilting the bolt. To reduce the felt recoil, a muzzle brake is installed on the barrel, and the buttstock is equipped with a rubber butt pad-shock absorber. The rifle is equipped with a folding bipod and an additional adjustable support under the buttstock. A Picatinny rail is used to install sighting devices. The rifle can be equipped with various optical (day) and electronic-optical (night) sights.

In the early 1980s, the British Army announced a competition to replace the aging Enfield L42 sniper rifles. The main participants in the competition were the British companies Parker-Hale with the Model 82 rifle, and Accuracy International with the Model PM rifle. The PM rifle won the competition, and in the mid-1980s it was adopted by the British Army under the designation L96. The main distinguishing feature of this rifle was the stock of an unusual appearance and design: the basis of the stock is an aluminum beam running along the entire length of the stock, to which the barrel with the receiver, the trigger mechanism and all other parts of the rifle are attached, including the stock itself, consisting of 2 plastic halves - left and right. In addition, L96 rifles are equipped with open sights in addition to the mandatory optical sight. In the mid-1980s, the Swedish Army also began searching for a new sniper rifle suitable for use in harsh northern weather conditions. Accuracy International offered the Swedes a modified version of the L96 rifle called the Arctic Warfare, and in 1988 the Swedish Army adopted it under the designation PSG.90. The British Army, in turn, also adopted Arctic Warfare rifles (new designation L96A1). The basic model of the series - AW is designed as an army weapon, in addition to it, four more basic models are produced: Police (AWP), Suppressed (AWS), Folding (AWF) and Super Magnum (AW SM). The name of the series (Arctic Warfare = Arctic combat operations) comes from the fact that the rifles have special design features that allow them to be used in Arctic conditions (at temperatures down to -40 degrees Celsius). The AW, AWP and AWS models are chambered for 7.62mm NATO only, while the SM model is chambered for .338 Lapua Magnum, .300 Winchester Magnum and 7mm Remington Magnum. The AW model has a 660mm barrel, while the AWP has a 609mm barrel. The AW SM model barrels can be from 609mm to 686mm long. The AWS model is equipped for use with a suppressor and subsonic ammunition. The accuracy of the basic AW model is such that at a distance of 550 meters, a series of 5 shots fits into a circle less than 50mm in diameter! The rifles are equipped with Smidt 3-12X variable magnification or Leupold Mark 4 fixed 10X scopes, as well as a folding removable bipod.

In November 2007, it was accepted into service as the L115A3 Long Range Rifle. Soon after, the first rifles were transferred to the British contingents in Iraq and Afghanistan. On November 15, 2007, the British Ministry of Defense published information about the conclusion of a contract with Accracy International Limited for the supply of 580 L115A3 rifles worth 4 million British pounds (5.04 million euros). The L115A3 rifle is a completely traditional design with manual reloading by means of a longitudinally sliding bolt with a rotating bolt. The basis of the design is a system made of high-quality steel and firmly connected to thealuminum frame. The bolt stem is made by die casting, it has a diameter of 20 mm. The integral bolt cylinder is equipped with six combat stops arranged in two rows, the bolt opening angle is only 60 degrees.

In the early 90s, they tried to improve the quality of the PM, primarily by introducing a new, reinforced (high-impulse) 9x18 PMM (57N181SM) cartridge. The modernized Makarov pistol (PMM, GRAU index - 56-A-125M) was developed on the basis of the PM (Makarov pistol) for a more powerful cartridge. The pistol was modernized by designers B. M. Pletsky and R. G. Shigapov, and has been mass-produced since 1994 by the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant. Externally, it is distinguished by an enlarged plastic grip of a more comfortable shape. The rear end of the grip is notched for a more secure hold. The PMM can be equipped with a single-row magazine for 8 or a double-row magazine for 12 rounds. The smooth transition of the double-row magazine at the top to the single-row neck made it possible not to redesign the magazine well and the bolt. Three helical grooves are made on the walls of the chamber. The gas pressure presses the walls of the cartridge case into the grooves, increasing the resistance force to its backward movement. Since the standard cartridge develops less pressure, the grooves do not interfere with the use of standard cartridges. The maximum pressure in the barrel of the pistol with the new cartridge has increased by 15%. Thus, the cartridge in terms of power and damaging effect has come close to the 9x19 Parabellum. Due to the higher gas pressure at the muzzle, the acoustic load when firing the new cartridge has also increased by 20%. The magazine cover protrudes slightly forward, which provides support for the palm and can somewhat speed up reloading. Changing the holding devices can significantly improve the accuracy of shooting from the pistol.

In January 1989, FN introduced another modification, designated the High Power Mk III. This model was developed specifically to replace the unsuccessful Mk II. The High Power Mk III pistol was manufactured to higher standards. Electronic software-controlled equipment allowed for a significant increase in the quality of parts processing. To make the design more robust, the frame and slide were enlarged. The ejection port and grip design were changed. The sighting device was now attached to the slide using"dovetail", which allowed it to be replaced with a target sight if necessary and to use not only a replaceable rear sight, but also a front sight. The HP Mk III pistol was initially intended for use by troops, and was soon joined by the High Power Mk III S modification. The High Power Mk III S pistol, equipped with an automatic striker lock, which did not allow the latter to move until the trigger was fully depressed, was intended for police and commercial sale.

Beretta 92FS (1989) – a modification of the Beretta 92F pistol, which has an enlarged hammer head, which prevents the rear part of the slide from falling off the guides when it is destroyed. It differs from the 92F model by a small disk on the hammer head and a groove along the lower left edge of the slide. The disk serves as an additional guide when the slide moves and prevents the rear part of the slide from falling off the guides when the slide is destroyed.\n The slide failure occurred on early Model 92F pistols after 4,000 rounds, falling short of the 5,000-round guarantee when firing heavy-duty cartridges (9x19mm +P+ or 9x19mm NATO). In 1987, after the contract with the US was suspended, the strength of the metal used to manufacture the slide was increased and no slide failures were detected, but additions were made and the Model 92FS was born. It differs from commercial pistols in its markings and external finish. It is one of the most common and recognizable pistols in the world. It is used by armies, police and special forces of many countries, and is very popular on the civilian weapons market. The annual production of pistols of the 92 series of various modifications in Italy and licensed production in other countries is more than 100,000 units.

The pistol, best known by the designation M1911 (Colt's commercial designation - Government model = Government Model), was developed in the United States by John Moses Browning in the early 20th century, and was adopted by the US Army in 1911.
 In 1926, the pistol underwent a partial modernization, and from then until the mid-1980s, it remained in active military service under the designation M1911A1, thus becoming the longest-serving military pistol. In the US Army, it was replaced by the Beretta 92 pistol (military designation M9), but to this day, the M1911 remains one of the most popular pistols in the United States. Key design features of the M1911: 
 - automatic system with a short barrel stroke; locking by two lugs on the top of the barrel and on the inner surface of the bolt in front of the window for extracting the cartridges; the barrel and bolt are disengaged by lowering the barrel using a steel shackle connecting the breech of the barrel and the frame of the pistol 
 - single action trigger with open trigger 
 - inertial striker, held in the rear position by its own spring 
 - non-automatic safety on the frame, blocking the bolt and trigger (both in the cocked and released positions) 
 - automatic safety - a button on the back of the handle, which is switched off when the handle is tightly grasped by the hand 
 - single-row magazine in the handle; magazine release in the form of a button located next to the trigger guard

The P220 pistol uses the recoil energy of a short barrel stroke for reloading. The barrel is locked using a modified Browning locking system, with one massive protrusion on the breech of the barrel beyond the window for ejecting the cartridges. The barrel is lowered for unlocking by the interaction of a shaped cutout in the lug under the breech of the barrel with a steel frame insert. The pistol frame itself is made of aluminum alloy; the latest pistols may also have a heavier stainless steel frame. The bolt casing is stamped from sheet steel; in its rear part there is a massive steel insert - the bolt, rigidly fixed with a transverse pin. The firing mechanism is self-cocking, with an open hammer and automatic striker locking when the trigger is not pressed. There are also versions with only a self-cocking firing mechanism (self-cocking for each shot). Since 2006, versions of the P220SAO pistol with a non-self-cocking trigger (single action) have also been produced specifically for the US market. These pistols have a two-sided manual safety on the frame instead of a trigger release lever, which locks the trigger when the hammer is cocked. The pistol does not have non-automatic safeties; on the left side of the frame behind the trigger on pistols with a basic trigger, there is a trigger release lever from the combat platoon. Sightsopen devices, on service versions - non-adjustable, with white or luminous inserts. The rear sight is mounted on the bolt in a transverse groove of the "dovetail" type. The magazine catch on "European" models was located at the base of the handle, on models for the American market the magazine catch has the form of a button located at the base of the trigger guard.

The Desert Eagle pistol uses a very rare for pistols automatic scheme with a barrel locking by a rotary bolt and using the energy of powder gases discharged from the barrel for reloading. The bolt has 4 lugs that engage with the breech of the barrel. Powder gases are discharged through an opening next to the chamber, after which the gases are directed through a channel under the barrel to a gas piston with a short working stroke located in the front part of the weapon under the barrel. The firing mechanism is hammer, single action (not self-cocking). The safety is ambidextrous, located on the bolt. Magazines are single-row, with a capacity of 7 to 9 rounds depending on the caliber, non-interchangeable between calibers. Sights are usually open and non-adjustable, although adjustable micrometer rear sights can be installed. The upper surface of the barrel of modern pistols has a special shape that allows direct installation of rings for optical sights on the barrel.

The Five-Seven pistol (that's right, not Five-Seven!) was developed by the Belgian company Fabrique Nationale, Gerstal, as a companion weapon to the P90 submachine gun of the same company. The key features of both the Five-Seven and the P90 are the new, specially developed SS190 cartridge of 5.7 mm caliber with a pointed bullet that develops a muzzle velocity of over 650 m/s when fired from the Five-Seven and about 700 m/s when fired from the P90. The main task of such a weapon is to fight an enemy protected by body armor. The Five-Seven pistol is built according to the scheme with a semi-free breech, and has a polymer frame with guides located under the barrel for attaching a tactical flashlight or laser designator. The trigger mechanism is striker-type, with an integrated striker lock until the trigger is fully pressed. Currently, the Five-seveN is available in two versions: standard, with only a double-action trigger mechanism.action, without a non-automatic safety, and Tactical - with a single-action trigger mechanism, with an ambidextrous manual safety located on the frame above the trigger guard. The standard Five-seveN version is intended primarily for the military as a last-chance weapon, and the Tactical version is for the police, where the pistol is often the main weapon. A shorter trigger stroke with less trigger pull ensures more accurate shooting.

The development of a new promising pistol, primarily aimed at the American market (both civilian and police) was started at Heckler und Koch in mid-1989. The concept envisaged the creation of a fairly universal weapon with various trigger options in accordance with the requirements of various customers, as well as high performance. The name of the new project - USP, stands for Universal Selbstlade Pistole, that is, a universal self-loading pistol. Serial production of the first version of the USP began in 1993, and already in 1995, a 9-mm version of the USP, slightly modified to meet the requirements of the Bundeswehr, was adopted by the Armed Forces of the FRG under the designation P8 (Pistole 8). In the same year, a version of the USP pistol for the American caliber .45ACP appeared. Unlike pistols of 9mm and .40 caliber, the .45 caliber version had a barrel and slide extended by 5 millimeters. In 1996, a compact version of the USP Compact was released, which had a shortened barrel, slide and grip, and in 1997, the 9 mm version of the USP Compact was approved as a police weapon in Germany and received the official designation P10. Based on the USP series pistols, civilian versions of the pistol were also created, such as the USP Match, USP Expert, USP Elite and others, as well as special versions with an extended barrel and adjustable sight, designed for use with a silencer - the USP Tactical in .45ACP and the USP 9SD in 9 mm.

The OTs-33 pistol was developed in the mid-1990s at the TsKIB SOO (Central Design Bureau of Sporting and Hunting Weapons) under the leadership of I. Ya. Stechkin to replace the outdated and long-discontinued APS pistol designed by the same author. Currently, due to the inclusion of TsKIB SOO in the Tula Instrument-making Design Bureau, the OTs-33 is being produced and promoted by the KBP. In minorThe OTs-33 is in service with various units of the Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs. Compared to the Stechkin APS pistol, the OTs-33 has a much simpler design and a lower rate of fire, and is easier to handle.\n The OTs-33 pistol is an automatic weapon based on a free-bolt mechanism. To reduce the felt recoil, the pistol barrel is movable. At the end of the recoil cycle, the bolt catches the massive barrel, and due to its inertia, the bolt is pre-braked before it hits the frame in the extreme rear position. This solution not only reduces recoil, but also reduces the rate of automatic fire, which made it possible to abandon the mechanism for forced reduction of the rate of fire, which was present in the APS pistol. The return spring is located under the barrel. The weapon is almost entirely made of steel, the grip cheeks are plastic. The safety catch - fire mode translator is located on both sides of the bolt, and provides single (middle position) and automatic fire (lower position). \n In the upper fixed position, the safety locks the firing pin, hammer and bolt. Moving the safety-translator lever to a position above the "safety" position causes the hammer to be safely released from the combat platoon, after which the safety automatically returns to the upper fixed position. The pistol is fed from standard double-row magazines with a capacity of 18 rounds or from extended magazines with a capacity of 27 rounds. For aimed automatic fire, the pistol is equipped with a removable folding shoulder rest (butt), made of steel and worn in a separate case on the belt. Under the barrel on the weapon frame there are guides for attaching a combat flashlight or laser designator. The pistol is also equipped with a soft holster for carrying and pouches for spare magazines.

The Remington 870 "Wingmaster" shotgun was introduced by Remington in 1950 and has been the best-selling pump-action shotgun in the United States ever since. Originally designed as a general-purpose hunting weapon, the Remington 870 has been and is produced in dozens of modifications in various calibers. Technically, the Remington 870 is a magazine-fed shotgun with manual reloading using a fore-end that moves back and forth ("pump-action" shotgun). Locking is accomplished by a swinging bolt, located in the bolt, behind the breech of the barrel. The receiver is steel. The bolt carrier drive is two rods for reliability. The barrels are replaceable, of various lengths and drilling. The tubular magazine is loaded through a special window in the bottom of the receiver, one cartridge at a time. Depending on the model and caliber, the capacity of the underbarrel magazine mayvary from 3 to 8 rounds. Sights may also vary depending on the modification of the gun and its purpose.

Technically, the Benelli M4 Super90 is a self-loading magazine shotgun. The automatic drive is by gas discharge into two symmetrically located gas cylinders under the barrel, with two gas pistons and rods, for greater reliability. The barrel is locked by turning the bolt, on 2 lugs. The chamber is made for 3-inch (76 mm) cartridges, but the gun is designed to work reliably with 2.75-inch (70 mm) cartridges without any adjustment.\n Partial disassembly and assembly of the gun is carried out without additional tools. The gun is standardly equipped with a telescopic sliding stock, which, if necessary, can be replaced with a pistol grip or a regular non-folding stock. The sight is with a dioptric rear sight and a regular rifle-type front sight. In addition, a universal guide (weaver rail) is mounted on top of the receiver for installing various sights (night, collimator) and other devices. The coatings of all metal surfaces, both external and internal, are matte, do not reflect light, and have increased resistance to corrosion and wear. The barrel is replaceable, can be replaced with a shortened one, 355 mm long. The barrel is equipped with screw-in chokes for greater versatility.

The Mossberg 590 shotgun was created by the American company O.F. Mossberg 'n Sons in the mid-1970s as a "militarized" modification of their popular Mossberg 500 shotgun. Militarization included strengthening a number of parts of the weapon, installing a barrel pad-casing to protect hands from burns, installing a mount for a standard M9 bayonet-knife, a more durable matte coating of the outer surfaces to protect against wear and corrosion, a metal trigger guard - the base of the trigger unit. The Mossberg 590 shotgun is in service with the US Navy and Marine Corps, many US police departments; it is also sold on the civilian market. The Mossberg 590 is a magazinesmoothbore gun. Reloading with a movable fore-end (pump-action), non-removable tubular magazine, located under the barrel. Barrel locking - installed on the breech by a swinging larva for the breech of the barrel. The receiver is made of aluminum alloy. The safety is located on top of the rear part of the receiver and is equally convenient for both right-handed and left-handed people.

The Saiga-12 is a self-loading shotgun developed at the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant based on the Kalashnikov assault rifle and intended for commercial and amateur hunting of small and medium-sized game and birds in areas with any climatic conditions. The family of weapons called "Saiga" was developed during the collapse of the USSR at the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant. "Saiga-12K", a shortened self-loading shotgun - "smoothbore carbine". The buttstock and pistol grip (and in some versions, the forend) are borrowed from the Kalashnikov assault rifle. The design includes a locking mechanism that does not allow the weapon to be removed from the safety until the buttstock is folded back. Barrel length is 430 mm. Sights are of the hunting or "machine gun" type.

The Vepr-12 shotgun is based on the time-tested design of the Kalashnikov RPK light machine gun, but when it was created, the wishes of sports shooters were taken into account, and additional elements were introduced into the design of the shotgun, making it more convenient to handle - a two-sided safety catch, a magazine well, a bolt catch, etc. Currently, the Vepr-12 series shotguns are available in three versions, differing in barrel length - in the basic version, the barrel is the shortest, in the versions version 01 and version 02, the barrels are longer. The Vepr-12 smoothbore shotguns inherited the general layout and design of the Kalashnikov assault rifle (light machine gun), with a gas-operated mechanism and locking by turning the bolt. Naturally, the bolt group and receiver were redesigned taking into account the use of hunting cartridges, the trigger mechanism lost its automatic release, the gas mechanism is self-adjusting and allows shooting cartridges with both 70mm and 76mm (Magnum) cases without additional adjustments. Sights are of the Kalashnikov type, with an adjustable rear sight and a front sight mounted on the gas chamber.The receiver cover is not detachable, but folds up and forward, like the AKS-74U assault rifle. Additionally, a Picatinny rail is made on the receiver cover, allowing for quick and convenient installation of various additional sights on the corresponding brackets. The ammunition is fed from plastic single-row magazines with a capacity of 8 rounds, a bolt catch is introduced into the design of the weapon, blocking the bolt in the open position after all the cartridges in the magazine are used up (to speed up reloading). The butt is metal, skeletal design, folding to the side. The outside of the butt is covered with plastic to increase the comfort of shooting in frost or heat. Additional Picatinny rail guides are made on the fore-end and under the gas chamber for installing laser designators, tactical flashlights or other accessories.

The development of a new submachine gun (PP) was started by the German company Heckler und Koch around 1964. The first prototypes, designated as NK MP-54 (sometimes they are designated simply NK-54), appeared at the same time, and already in 1966 the police and border guard of the FRG adopted this PP under the designation MP-5 in two versions: MP-5 - with a fixed butt, and MP-5A1 - with a sliding telescopic butt. Later, the MP-5 was purchased by a variety of police and military forces both in Europe and on other continents.\n Structurally, the MP-5 is nothing more than a scaled-down copy of the German Heckler 'n Koch G3 rifle chambered for a pistol cartridge. It inherited from the G3 both a stamped steel receiver with a downward-folding and detachable trigger module (USM), and a semi-free bolt with a recoil delay using a pair of rollers. Fire from the MP-5 is always conducted from a closed bolt, which increases the accuracy and grouping of fire, but increases the likelihood of spontaneous ignition of the cartridge in the barrel when the weapon overheats. HK MP-5N. A modern version developed specifically for the US Navy. It features a folding buttstock of the A3 configuration, an all-plastic trigger housing with a two-way fire mode selector and "icon" markings. In addition, the muzzle of the barrel is threaded for installing removable American-designed silencers.

The AEK-919K "Kashtan" submachine gun was developed at the Korovsky Mechanical Plant in the mid-1990s. Initially, the Austrian Steyr MPi-69 submachine gun was taken as a model, but after the release of a small trial batch, a number of shortcomings were discovered, and the "Kashtan" was redesigned. The updated model received the index AEK-919K, and is currently actively advertised for arming the crews of combat vehicles and aircraft, as well as for special forces of the Ministry of Internal Affairs and the army. "Kashtan" is based on an automatic rifle with a free breech. Fire is conducted from an open bolt, the fire mode selector (also known as the safety catch) is located on the left above the trigger guard. The receiver is stamped from steel, the pistol grip with the trigger guard and forend are cast from plastic. The barrel of the "Kashtan" has polygonal rifling and can be equipped with a removable silencer. The standard sighting devices consist of a front sight and a flip-up rear sight for two distances - 50 and 100 meters, in addition, the latest versions of the "Kashtan" can be equipped with removable collimator sights ("red dot"). The butt of the AEK-919K is retractable, made of metal, the butt plate when extended can be turned down by 180 degrees for more convenient butt. In general, the "Kashtan" is characterized as an accurate and reliable weapon of small dimensions. In my opinion, the main drawback of the AEK-919K PP is the location of the translator / safety - removing the weapon from the safety with the right hand without rotating the weapon in the palm is almost impossible even for a person with long fingers. If it is impossible to use the second hand, this disadvantage can cost the shooter extra seconds of time, and maybe even his life. The collimator sight, advertised together with the "Kashtan" at the present time, is quite large, and is intended rather for use in police operations, as it significantly facilitates aiming at the expense of increasing the inconvenience of carrying the weapon. However, it is possible to install more modern and compact sights.

The FN P90 is a Belgian submachine gun (personal self-defense weapon) developed in 1986-1987 by FN Herstal. It was developed primarily for tank crews and drivers of combat vehicles. It has a progressive and convenient ergonomic design. A 5.7?28 mm SS190 cartridge was developed specifically for the P90, which has high penetration power and a low ricochet rate. The bullet of this cartridge develops a muzzle velocity of up to 715 m/s and is capable of penetrating titanium/kevlar body armor that meets NATO CRISAT requirements from 200 meters. The magazine is installed on top of the receiver and its capacity is 50 rounds, while the rounds are located horizontally, perpendicular to the barrel and rotate 90 degrees before feeding into the chamber. It is made of transparent plastic, which allows the shooter to find out the number of rounds in it at any time. Filling the magazine is done in the usual way.The spent cartridges are ejected downwards through the hollow fire control handle. The FN P90 is equipped with a built-in 1X collimator sight with a T-shaped reticle. The reticle has an automatically adjustable illumination depending on the external illumination; the weapon is also equipped with a laser target designator located below. In addition, it comes with a silencer, but its use is only possible if subsonic cartridges are used. At the same time, one of the main tasks was to minimize the weight of the weapon and ammunition. Belgian designers took the path of creating a small-caliber cartridge with a high initial bullet velocity and developed ammunition that met the requirements, also had a flat trajectory of the bullet and gave a small recoil force, which made it easier for inexperienced soldiers with poor fire training to fire and defeat the enemy. This weapon-cartridge complex was intended to replace pistols and submachine guns in the active armies of NATO countries using the traditional 9 mm Parabellum cartridge.

The Heckler - Koch HK MP7 submachine gun, developed in the late 1990s by the German company Heckler 'n Koch, according to the classification adopted in the West, belongs to a new class of small arms - Personal Defense Weapon (PDW), and is intended to arm military personnel who are not entitled to a full-fledged machine gun (assault rifle), that is, crews of military vehicles, gun crews, etc. From a technical point of view, the HK MP7A1 is an automatic weapon chambered for a specially developed 4.6mm cartridge (cartridge designation 4.6x30mm, externally the cartridge resembles a reduced 5.56mm NATO cartridge). The HK MP7A1 is designed as a compact submachine gun, the magazine is inserted into the pistol grip, the butt is folding, telescopic, there is an additional folding handle in front for holding the weapon with two hands.
